posts.summary: Julia Evans briefly documents her decision to switch from Jekyll to Octopress for her blog. She found Jekyll's HTML, CSS, and layout configuration tedious, and Octopress offered an easier setup experience. The post is essentially a first impression noting that things look okay so far. Her main remaining concern is customizing the color scheme to be more orange.
key_points: Jekyll's manual HTML/CSS/layout setup was frustrating enough to motivate switching tools
Octopress offered a significantly easier setup experience out of the box
Customization (making it 'more orange') remains a priority even with the simpler tool
money_quotes: Decided that I was tired of messing with HTML and CSS and layouts to set up Jekyll, so trying out Octopress here instead.
Still need to figure how to make it more orange.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best tool is the one that gets out of your way and lets you focus on what you actually care about.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a talk she gave at Montreal Python about graphing bike path usage data from Montreal's open data portal. She combined bike counter sensor data with weather data from Canada's National Climate Archive to explore relationships between cycling and weather conditions. The analysis was done using pandas and IPython Notebook, and she links to the viewable notebook. She invites readers to download the notebook and do their own analysis at an upcoming Python Night event.
key_points: Montreal collects bike path usage data via sensors, available as open data
Combining datasets (bike counts + weather) reveals patterns between cycling and conditions like temperature and rain
IPython Notebook and pandas make it easy to share reproducible data analysis that others can run themselves
money_quotes: I showed some graphs of # bikes vs temperature and # bikes vs raininess, using data downloaded from the Canadian National Climate Archive.
If you download it you can run it yourself and do some more complicated analysis.
posts.key_insight: Combining publicly available open datasets with tools like pandas and IPython Notebook makes exploratory data analysis accessible and shareable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps the Open Data Exchange 2013 conference in Montreal, highlighting talks and panels about making open data useful to real people. A recurring theme was the importance of humanizing data and connecting technologists with citizens, business owners, and domain experts. She highlights lightning talks covering topics from historical aerial photo overlays to journalist-programmer collaboration, and panels discussing how open data has saved Canada billions and how in-person engagement beats online outreach. The post reflects her enthusiasm for the community and the practical, people-centered approach to open data advocacy.
key_points: Open data apps are only valuable when they solve real problems people actually have, not just problems that are fun for programmers to work on
Connecting programmers with journalists, citizens, and domain experts produces better outcomes than technologists working in isolation
In-person meetings and conversations yield far more insight than online engagement when trying to understand community needs
Geospatial data communities have decades of experience sharing data across boundaries that the broader open data movement can learn from
money_quotes: Open data apps are only useful if they're solving problems that people are really having, and answering questions that people really need to know the answers to.
This really made me think about how to make sure that things I work on are actually useful to people, and not just fun to work on.
Technology is less important than asking the right questions
Geographers have always had to share data, because mountains don't respect country boundaries. They've been doing this for decades.
His main goal with Eco Hack MTL was not to work on any specific project, but to connect people from different areas and get them to talk to each other.
posts.key_insight: Open data work is most impactful when technologists prioritize connecting with real people and their actual problems over building technically interesting projects.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps PyData Boston 2013, highlighting talks she enjoyed including Travis Oliphant's keynote about Continuum Analytics, Lynn Cherny's talk on detecting sex scenes in Fifty Shades of Grey using machine learning, and Jeff Bezanson's introduction to the Julia programming language. She shares enthusiasm about the people she met and the tools she discovered. The post is a brief, energetic conference recap focused on the people and ideas that excited her most.
key_points: Travis Oliphant claimed there's no reason to write C++ anymore for scientific computing thanks to Numba's @autojit decorator
Lynn Cherny used Mechanical Turk to build a classifier for detecting sex scenes in fiction, doing analysis in Python and frontends in JavaScript with D3
Jeff Bezanson's Julia talk impressed Evans not just technically but personally, noting most of Julia's standard library is written in Julia itself
The post emphasizes community connections — meeting people like Kat Chuang from PyLadies NYC and discovering Lynn Cherny's work
money_quotes: In my mind, there's no reason to write C++ anymore
She said that she does all her data analysis in Python, and then writes frontends in Javascript (using D3).
What stood out to me the most was not how cool Julia looks (though it does!) but he just seemed like such a lovely guy.
Apparently most of the standard library for Julia is written in Julia!
He said there's lots of work to be done on Julia, and now I really want to contribute.
posts.key_insight: Conference value comes as much from discovering inspiring people and communities as from the technical content of talks.

posts.summary: Evans reviews Julia Serano's book 'Excluded: Making Feminist and Queer Movements More Inclusive,' calling it one of the best purchases she's made. She highlights Serano's argument against the myth that all women share a common bond, noting that placing people in boxes suffocates them. Evans connects this to her own realization that everything she does is inherently 'a thing women do' and 'a thing programmers do.' The post touches on allyship, inclusivity, and how communities sometimes exclude people by demanding they be radical enough.
key_points: Serano argues against the myth of a universal common bond among women, emphasizing that difference should be acknowledged rather than suppressed
Labels should be used to talk about commonality and bring people together, not as boxes to limit identity
Making communities more inclusive isn't about making them more radical, which can just create new forms of exclusion
Allyship requires deliberate work and doesn't come naturally
money_quotes: I was really happy and surprised not too long ago when I realized that everything I do is a 'thing women do' (and a 'thing programmers do'), because I do them.
I love the idea of using labels as a way to talk about what we have in common and bring us together, instead of boxes to limit ourselves with.
The way to make our communities more inclusive is not to make them more radical – that's often just another way to exclude people in different ways.
This is so obvious ('all women are different, and do tons of different things! And all of those things are okay!'), but so easy to forget.
posts.key_insight: Labels and identities should be tools for finding common ground, not boxes that constrain who gets to belong.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her pre-Hacker School preparation, attempting to implement Snake in C using ncurses as a way to improve her low-level programming skills. The Snake implementation went smoothly, but unit testing in C proved to be the real challenge. After struggling with autotools for hours, she discovered checkmk, a simpler awk-based tool that made writing tests for the check framework painless. She concludes that Snake was too easy and considers writing a shell next.
key_points: Getting started with C unit testing is harder than it should be, with autotools being a major barrier to entry
checkmk simplifies the check unit testing framework by letting you write test snippets that get transformed into proper test code
ncurses is more approachable than expected for building terminal-based games in C
Sometimes the tooling around a task (testing, build systems) is harder than the task itself
money_quotes: I spent an hour or two trying to understand how to autotools and it made me want to kill someone.
The hardest part actually turned out to be unit testing.
One of my goals for this batch is to get better at low-level programming and managing my own memory, so I'm planning to learn C a bit, as well as maybe Go or Rust.
I am also thinking of starting to write about coding on the internet, so here this is.
Conclusion: snake is too easy of a thing to do, so I probably won't take this much further. Next coding day maybe I'll start trying to write a shell?
posts.key_insight: The tooling and infrastructure around C development (build systems, testing frameworks) can be a bigger obstacle than the actual programming, but simpler alternatives exist if you look for them.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recounts her first day at Hacker School, where she paired with another student to build a shell in C. They encountered a subtle bug where a local array allocated on the stack was accessed after the function returned, causing corrupted output. The post walks through a minimal code example showing how a pointer to stack-allocated data becomes invalid once the function scope ends. Evans expresses excitement about encountering low-level bugs that would be impossible in higher-level languages like Python.
key_points: Local arrays in C are allocated on the stack and become invalid after the function returns, leading to dangling pointer bugs
The bug manifested as correct output on the first print but garbage on the second, because an intervening variable declaration overwrote the stack memory
This class of bug is impossible in garbage-collected languages like Python, making it a novel learning experience
Pairing with another programmer was an effective way to debug and learn C fundamentals
money_quotes: So this looks like normal code that would print 'banana' twice.
the pointer in strings points to the stack and when you do something like setting a variable, it becomes something weird.
It's sort of exciting to get bugs that are (as far as I know) totally impossible in Python.
posts.key_insight: Returning a pointer to stack-allocated data in C creates a dangling pointer that silently corrupts when the stack is reused, a class of bug that simply cannot exist in memory-managed languages.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her early exploration of writing a shell in C during Hacker School, breaking down what a shell actually needs to do. She lists the core responsibilities: parsing commands, glob expansion, pipes and redirection, signal handling, process management, and shell scripting. She also notes what the shell doesn't handle — PATH resolution is done by exec. The post captures the moment of demystifying something fundamental by trying to build it from scratch.
key_points: A shell's core jobs include parsing, glob expansion, pipes/redirection, signal handling, process management, and scripting
PATH resolution is not the shell's job — exec handles that
Building something from scratch forces you to understand what it actually does
Honest acknowledgment of gaps ('I don't really understand this yet') is part of the learning process
money_quotes: Before yesterday, I didn't have a very clear idea of what writing a shell even *meant*.
If you press `Ctrl+C`, it needs to send that signal to whatever process you're running. Or something. I don't really understand this yet.
Figure out which command to execute using the `$PATH` environment variable. `exec` does that, apparently.
posts.key_insight: You don't truly understand a tool until you try to build it yourself, and listing what it must do is the first step toward that understanding.

posts.summary: A short post from Hacker School about learning to transfer files between computers on a local network using netcat. Alan showed the author how netcat can act as a simple file transfer tool with just two commands. The post also notes that Wireshark makes it fun to watch the transfer happen in real time, and acknowledges the transfer is completely insecure.
key_points: Netcat can transfer files between machines with a simple listener/sender pattern
The command syntax differs between BSD and non-BSD versions of netcat
Wireshark lets you observe the raw transfer happening on the network
The transfer is completely unencrypted and visible to anyone on the network
money_quotes: IN PARTICULAR that you can transfer a file to your friend on your local network with netcat.
Then you wait a while until you think it's probably done and stop it. And I have the file!
Since you can see the whole thing in Wireshark, it is not secure or anything and anyone in the middle could also get the file. FUN.
posts.key_insight: Netcat is a surprisingly simple way to transfer files between machines on a local network, requiring just two commands and no setup.

posts.summary: Evans documents a collaborative session at Hacker School where participants brainstormed the different functions handled by the Linux kernel. The post presents a comprehensive list covering memory management, device drivers, process scheduling, filesystems, networking, and more. It serves as a beginner-friendly inventory of kernel responsibilities, written during her early days of kernel exploration. The post reflects her learning-in-public approach, inviting corrections from readers.
key_points: The Linux kernel handles a vast range of responsibilities from memory management and device drivers to networking and power management
The VFS (Virtual Filesystem Switch) provides a unified interface regardless of underlying filesystem type
Interrupt handlers are a key kernel function, managing hardware events like keypresses, packet arrivals, and timer expirations
The kernel provides UNIX/POSIX APIs (system calls) that serve as the interface between user programs and hardware
money_quotes: We had a really fun session this morning where we got together and made a list of the different functions that Linux kernel takes care of.
VFS: interface that lets you get files no matter what filesystem you're using
interrupt handlers – handles events from the hardware (packet received, keypress, timers, graphics card ready, data ready, hard drive finished reading)
posts.key_insight: The Linux kernel's responsibilities span an enormous range of functions from hardware management to process scheduling to networking, and mapping them all out is a useful first step toward understanding how operating systems work.

posts.summary: Evans recounts a Linux Kernel Club session at Hacker School where she learned about the difference between processes and threads. The post explains that processes have their own PID and address space while threads share both. She breaks down what address space includes (code, data, heap, stack, environment variables) and lists the tradeoffs of threads: easier communication but risk of race conditions. The group also discussed kernel modules as a good entry point for kernel development.
key_points: Processes have their own PID and address space; threads share both with their parent process
Address space includes code/text, data, heap, stack, environment variables, and command line arguments
Threads trade easier inter-thread communication for the complexity of race conditions and mutexes
Writing a kernel module is considered a good entry point for Linux kernel development
money_quotes: Processes each have their own PID and address space, threads share the PID and address space of a single process.
Each process has a piece of memory that it's allowed to use, so that processes can't step on each others' toes.
Because they write to the same memory, you can have all kinds of race conditions. So you need to use mutexes and things.
The consensus was that writing a kernel module is a really good way to get started with Linux kernel development.
We talked a bit about exploits and rootkits and dastardly things that kernel modules can do.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental distinction between processes and threads comes down to address space isolation: processes get their own memory for safety, while threads share memory for efficiency at the cost of concurrency complexity.

posts.summary: This short reference post collects the data feed URLs for bike sharing systems across North America, London, and Melbourne. The feeds, provided by Bixi-powered systems and others, publish real-time station availability data in XML or JSON format, updated roughly every minute. Evans notes that these feeds aren't always easy to find on official websites, motivating her to compile them in one place. She also mentions occasional irregularities in feed formats.
key_points: Bike sharing systems publish real-time availability data as XML or JSON feeds updated approximately every minute
These data feeds are often not advertised on official websites, making them hard to discover
The post catalogs feeds for systems across multiple cities in North America, London, and Melbourne
Feed formats have historically had irregularities and could change without notice
money_quotes: People sometimes ask me where the feed is for this data, as it's not always advertised on their website.
Washington State University's Green Bike system has an unfortunate situation where the availability data is embedded in the source of their homepage (near the end).
I've found that there are some irregularities in the feed format, and sometimes it changes without notice.
posts.key_insight: Publicly available real-time data feeds for bike sharing systems exist but are often undocumented, making a curated reference list valuable for developers who want to hack on transit data.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her excitement about writing her first Linux kernel module during Hacker School. The module uses the Netfilter framework to intercept incoming packets and print 'Hello packet' to the kernel log. While it doesn't do anything useful, the exercise taught her fundamental differences between kernel-space and user-space programming, like the inability to use glibc functions. She shares resources for others interested in kernel module development.
key_points: Writing a basic kernel module that hooks into Netfilter to log incoming packets is surprisingly accessible
Kernel code can't use glibc functions like malloc — the kernel has its own memory allocation with kmalloc and vmalloc
Building a firewall that blocks all traffic is trivially easy once you have a Netfilter hook — just change NF_ACCEPT to NF_DROP
kernelnewbies.org is highlighted as a valuable resource for learning kernel development
money_quotes: I WROTE A KERNEL MODULE. It doesn't do anything useful or anything, but still!
You can't use malloc inside the kernel (?!!?). This is because anything that's used in the kernel needs to be defined in the kernel, and malloc is in glibc.
This seems obvious in retrospect, but kind of blew my mind
It is really easy to write a firewall that doesn't let any packets in or out – just replace NF_ACCEPT with NF_DROP in my kernel module.
I think I'm going to work on writing a rootkit tomorrow. eee.
posts.key_insight: Kernel module development is more accessible than it seems, and even a simple packet-intercepting module reveals fundamental differences between kernel-space and user-space programming.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents writing a simple rootkit as a kernel module during her Hacker School experience. The rootkit allows any unprivileged user to become root by writing a PID to /proc/buddyinfo. She explains how it works by hijacking file_operations structs to add a write handler to a read-only proc file, which then changes process ownership to root. She credits the code to an existing rootkit she studied and simplified.
key_points: Kernel modules can modify file_operations structs to change how files behave, which is the same mechanism used for device drivers
A rootkit can be surprisingly simple - just hijack a proc file's write handler and change process ownership
Every file in Linux has a struct file_operations controlling read/write behavior, giving the kernel complete control
Process ownership can be changed by copying the owner credentials from PID 1 (init, always root)
money_quotes: I made some small improvements on my kernel module from yesterday – I made it into a rootkit!
What I mean by a "rootkit" is a kernel module that once I put it in my kernel, any unprivileged user who knows the right incantation can become root.
THEN I AM ROOT.
Since the kernel can do ANYTHING, it can change those file handlers and do nefarious things.
Print "YOU HAVE BEEN HACKED: Making PID $PID root" to the kernel log. Bwahaha.
Maybe tomorrow I will improve the rootkit so that people can exploit my computer over the network, not just when they're logged in.
posts.key_insight: Building a rootkit is a surprisingly effective way to learn how Linux kernel modules and file operations work, because it demonstrates the kernel's unrestricted power over process ownership and file handlers.

posts.summary: Julia documents her seventh day at Hacker School where she built a simple UDP echo server in Clojure. While also working on a kernel module that isn't ready yet, she shares the echo server as a quick win. She notes the code is essentially procedural code written in Clojure and isn't sure if that's idiomatic. The project is a first step toward potentially writing a BitTorrent client in Clojure, inspired by other Hacker School participants.
key_points: Built a working UDP echo server in Clojure as a quick project alongside unfinished kernel module work
Acknowledges the code is procedural rather than idiomatic Clojure and wants code review
Frames the echo server as a stepping stone toward a potential BitTorrent client project
Demonstrates using netcat to interact with the server, connecting back to earlier learning
money_quotes: This is really just procedural code written in Clojure. I can't tell yet if this is an appropriate way to write a small Clojure program.
This is the first step towards maybe writing a BitTorrent client in Clojure – many other people are writing BitTorrent client and really enjoying it, and I'm jealous.
posts.key_insight: Small, working projects serve as both learning exercises and stepping stones toward more ambitious goals.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her excitement about contributing to the Julia programming language during Hacker School's maintainers program, where open source maintainers help students contribute to projects. She lists several reasons Julia appeals to her, including that much of Julia is written in Julia itself and its focus on scientific computing. She then honestly confronts the emotional difficulty of open source contribution—the fear of public discussion, the frustration of spending hours on tiny bugs, and the vulnerability of being visibly new. She resolves that facing this discomfort is exactly why she's at Hacker School and celebrates making pull requests that day.
key_points: Julia the language is compelling because much of its code is written in itself, unlike Python which requires C for performance-critical code
Open source contribution is emotionally difficult—public discussion of bugs and submitting pull requests can be terrifying even with supportive communities
Being new to a project means spending disproportionate time on small bugs and publicly demonstrating confusion
Pairing with others and deliberately practicing the scary parts makes open source contribution more manageable
money_quotes: The thing I forgot about open source is that open source (for me!) is *scary* and *hard*.
Discussing bugs on mailing lists and issue trackers and submitting pull requests is *terrifying*.
I have gone to awesome code sprints with really lovely supportive people and gone home and cried because being new to a project and trying to get work done is sometimes *so frustrating*.
Like you can spend 12 hours trying to fix a tiny bug and get *nowhere*.
And say ALL OF THE THINGS THAT ARE CONFUSED AND WRONG. IN PUBLIC.
I'm going to work on lots of open source anyway and write code and make pull requests and then it will not be terrifying.
posts.key_insight: Open source contribution is genuinely scary and emotionally hard, but deliberately leaning into that discomfort—especially with support from others—is how you get past the fear.

posts.summary: Evans documents her experience pairing with Hacker School facilitator Allison on a Python bytecode interpreter, discovering that CPython's internals are surprisingly approachable. She explores ceval.c, the 3,500-line file containing CPython's main interpreter loop, and finds that individual opcodes like BINARY_SUBTRACT are straightforward to understand. The post walks through what a single opcode does step by step, demystifying the C code behind Python execution. Her core realization is that bytecode is literally made of bytes—fewer than 256 instructions, each represented by a single byte.
key_points: CPython's interpreter is mostly a single 3,500-line file with a 2,000-line switch statement, which sounds intimidating but is surprisingly readable
Individual bytecode operations like BINARY_SUBTRACT follow a simple pattern: pop arguments, perform operation, manage references, push result, dispatch next instruction
Pairing with an experienced guide can transform something that seems scary into something approachable
Python bytecode literally uses single bytes for instructions, with fewer than 256 possible opcodes
money_quotes: The CPython interpreter is mostly in one 3,500 file called ceval.c. The main part of this file is a 2,000-line switch statement – switch(opcode) {.... Ack.
But! This file is surprisingly not-scary.
I could TOTALLY WRITE THAT.
It's actually possible to read the C code that runs Python!
Bytecode is made of bytes. Like, there are less than 256 instructions and each one is a byte. I did not realize this until today. Laugh all you want =D
posts.key_insight: The C code that implements Python's interpreter is far more approachable than it sounds—individual bytecode operations are simple, readable functions, and demystifying them just requires looking at the source.

posts.summary: Julia reflects on her goals midway through Hacker School, realizing that while she's contributing to open source, she hasn't made progress on writing non-trivial software from scratch or improving her code design skills. She identifies five goals including learning concurrency and functional programming, and considers that reading more well-written code might help. She floats the idea of writing a JavaScript interpreter as a project that could address multiple goals at once.
key_points: Having goals is important but honestly assessing progress against them is harder — most of her goals are 'not happening yet'
Reading well-written code may be as important as writing code for learning good design
Finding a project that's the right size and personally motivating is a real challenge
Ambitious multi-goal projects like writing an interpreter can be a way to address several learning objectives simultaneously
money_quotes: Still hunting for something which is the right size and which I can care about enough to do it.
To address #3, I think I need to spend more time reading well-written code.
Learn about concurrency, because I don't even.
Write a non-trivial piece of software from scratch that I feel good about the design of. This is not happening at all.
posts.key_insight: Improving at code design may require spending more time reading well-written code, not just writing more code.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her early exploration of how gzip compression works, undertaken as a project at Hacker School. She's writing a parallel version of gzip in the Julia programming language. The post covers the basic building blocks of gzip (Huffman coding, LZ77 compression) and notes that the real complexity isn't in the algorithms themselves but in the bit-level fiddling required for efficiency. She shares a small Julia code snippet for converting bits to bytes and admits she's only just started decoding the gzip header.
key_points: Gzip relies on Huffman coding and LZ77 compression, which are conceptually straightforward but require complex bit manipulation to implement efficiently
Julia is a good fit for this project because it combines low-level bit-fiddling capabilities with high-level language features similar to Python
The real complexity of gzip lies not in the algorithms but in variable-length encoding and bit-level details like obscure offset conventions
money_quotes: Spoiler: I don't really know yet, but there is a lot of mucking with bits.
These things *sound* like they would be the complicated part, but so far they don't seem to be too bad. Conceptually. I think.
Everything is variable-length encoded, so instead of just having bytes, you have bit sequences of various lengths that you have to extract. Because efficiency.
You can see that this kind of just looks like Python, except it is fast!
Every gzip file begins with the "magic bytes" 1F8B
posts.key_insight: The conceptual algorithms behind gzip (Huffman coding, LZ77) aren't the hard part — the real complexity lives in the bit-level fiddling needed to make the encoding efficient.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her impressions of the Julia programming language after a week of use during Hacker School. She highlights its appealing combination of Python-like ease and C-like performance, its IPython notebook integration, and the ability to inspect LLVM code for functions. She notes that much of Julia is written in Julia itself, making it accessible for contributors. Her complaints are minor, mostly around REPL startup time and session instability when redefining functions.
key_points: Julia combines Python's friendly syntax with C-like memory layout and performance characteristics
IPython notebook integration via IJulia was a major draw for her interactive workflow
The ability to inspect LLVM intermediate code for any function provides unique transparency into performance
Julia being largely self-hosted means contributors can improve the language without switching to C
The main frustrations are minor REPL issues rather than fundamental language problems
money_quotes: I will never get tired of the fact that Julia is called Julia.
I've been programming in Julia for about a week now. So now is a good time to share my Extreme Julia Expertise.
It's like C. This is kind of the opposite of 'it's like Python'.
So if you want to add to the standard library or improve Julia's type inference, you can do it in Julia!
Having to switch to a different language to make your fast is no fun.
Multiple dispatch is also pretty neat. I may write more about it later, because I'm not able to articulate yet why it gives me nice programming patterns.
posts.key_insight: Julia's power lies in combining the ease of a dynamic scripting language with the performance transparency of a systems language, all while being largely self-hosted.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recounts spending most of a day debugging a single off-by-one error in her gzip decompression implementation. The fix was changing 257 to 258 in a calculation, though she still doesn't fully understand why. Despite the frustration, she's nearly able to decompress gzipped files. The post captures the unglamorous reality of low-level programming work.
key_points: A single off-by-one error (257 vs 258) consumed an entire day of debugging
Even after fixing the bug, the underlying reason wasn't fully clear
The gzip decompression project is making progress despite painful debugging
money_quotes: Today I spent most of the day figuring out that n_to_read = head.hlit + head.hdist + 257 should be n_to_read = head.hlit + head.hdist + 258
And I still don't know why, exactly.
I think the life lesson here is "sometimes it takes forever to figure things out and it is no fun" :)
posts.key_insight: Sometimes debugging means spending an entire day finding a single off-by-one error, and that's just part of the process.

posts.summary: Evans reflects on struggling with an off-by-one error in her gzip implementation and realizing that the situations where testing feels hardest are exactly when tests are most valuable. After repeatedly telling people she couldn't write tests because she didn't know what intermediate results should look like, a colleague suggested using a reference C implementation to generate expected test data. She acknowledges that writing more tests would help her identify which parts of her code she's confident about versus which parts likely contain bugs.
key_points: The difficulty of writing tests is a signal that tests are most needed, not an excuse to skip them
Reference implementations can be used to generate expected test data when you don't know what intermediate results should look like
Tests help you map your confidence: identifying which parts of code you trust versus which parts are suspect
The excuses we make for not testing ('it's complicated', 'I don't know the expected output') are often solvable with creative approaches
money_quotes: When you're not sure exactly what your code is supposed to be doing or how to verify whether it's correct is exactly when it's the most helpful to write unit tests.
I'm thinking, if I write more tests, maybe I'll have a better handle on which parts of the code I'm confident and which parts are more likely to be broken.
Do you have unit tests? Some! But it's complicated! I don't really know what my output is supposed to be in the middle of the calculation, just the final result!
Pretty much all the situations it describes are less tractable than my gzip situation, so I pretty much have no excuses.
There was all this code! And I had no idea where the problem was!
posts.key_insight: The moments when writing tests feels hardest—when you don't fully understand your code's expected behavior—are precisely when tests provide the most value.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents the internal structure of gzip files after implementing gunzip in Julia. She walks through the DEFLATE algorithm's two passes—LZ77 for replacing repeated sequences with pointers, and Huffman coding for efficient representation. The post details the file format layer by layer: headers, metadata, block structure, the recursive Huffman tree encoding scheme, and the actual decompression logic. She shares her Julia data structures and code, noting the surprising complexity of details like off-by-one constants and the non-obvious split between literal and distance trees.
key_points: Gzip uses two passes: LZ77 to replace repeated sequences with back-references, then Huffman coding to compress the result efficiently
The format has a recursive structure where one Huffman tree is encoded using another Huffman tree, adding significant implementation complexity
Gzip files cannot be decompressed in parallel because block boundaries are only discoverable through full decompression
Small format details like 'minus four' and 'minus one' offsets in the header fields caused disproportionate debugging difficulty
money_quotes: The basic idea behind gzip (aka the DEFLATE algorithm) is that there are 2 passes.
There is a recursive thing going on where there is a Huffman tree encoded with another Huffman tree.
This minus four and minus one business basically drove me insane.
As far as I can tell it is impossible to decompress gzip files in parallel – there's no way to know when a block has ended without fully decompressing it.
You actually end up with two Huffman trees here – they're called literal_tree and distance_tree. This was really not obvious and took me forever to figure out.
It modifies decoded_text in place because it turns out that blocks can refer to text in previous blocks.
posts.key_insight: The gzip file format's apparent simplicity conceals recursive complexity—Huffman trees encoded with other Huffman trees, off-by-one constants, and interdependent blocks—that only becomes visible when you implement a decompressor from scratch.

posts.summary: Julia Evans created a visualization of gzip decompression applied to Edgar Allan Poe's 'The Raven,' showing how the LZ77 algorithm replaces text with pointers to earlier parts of the document. The visualization highlights copied text segments during decompression, revealing how poetic structures like rhyming naturally emerge in the compression patterns. She built this by implementing gunzip from scratch and adding print/sleep statements to show the process. The post includes links to visualizations of the full poem and Hamlet.
key_points: Gzip's LZ77 compression works by replacing text with pointers to earlier occurrences of the same text
Applying gzip visualization to poetry reveals how rhyming and repeated structures naturally show up in compression patterns
She implemented gunzip from scratch in Julia, then hacked on a visualization layer with print and sleep statements
Gzip/DEFLATE has two steps: LZ77 compression (shown) and Huffman coding (not shown)
money_quotes: Gzip compresses by replacing text with pointers to earlier parts of the text.
I really like how you can see the rhyming inside the poem like (rapping… tapping) come out in the compression algorithm.
I implemented gunzip from scratch to learn how it works.
This visualization is a small hack on top of that, just adding some print and sleep statements.
This in fact shows how LZ77 compression works, which is the first step of gzip (or DEFLATE) compression.
posts.key_insight: Visualizing gzip decompression on poetry reveals how compression algorithms naturally detect and exploit the repeated structures that make poems poetic, like rhyme and refrain.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a visualization she built from a Hacker News survey of 1,500 responses about Unix command line utility usage. She was curious about which commands are most popular and how they correlate with each other. The result is an interactive graph showing which commands are strongly correlated in usage, such as ruby and rails. She highlights the sparse area around gcc as particularly interesting.
key_points: A Hacker News survey collected 1,500 responses about Unix command line utility usage
The visualization shows correlation graphs between commands, revealing which tools tend to be used by the same people
Commands that are strongly correlated are linked together in the graph, revealing ecosystem clusters
money_quotes: I got curious about which unix command line utilities were the most popular, so I ran a survey on Hacker News.
I wanted to know how commands are related to each other: do people who use gcc use python? What about scala and clojure?
I enjoy it a lot, especially the part of the graph around gcc, because it's pretty sparse.
posts.key_insight: Surveying real-world command usage patterns and visualizing their correlations reveals distinct ecosystem clusters among Unix users.

posts.summary: Evans walks through creating a basic buffer overflow exploit as part of her Hacker School security week, inspired by Jon Erickson's 'Hacking: The Art of Exploitation.' She demonstrates a vulnerable C program that uses gets() to read into a fixed-size buffer, then shows how to overwrite the return address to redirect execution to an arbitrary function. The post covers practical details like disabling gcc's stack protector, dealing with little-endian byte ordering, and the difference between 32-bit and 64-bit exploitation. She notes the existence of more sophisticated techniques like NOP sleds but sticks with the simpler brute-force approach.
key_points: Buffer overflows work by writing past a buffer's bounds to overwrite the return address on the stack, redirecting program execution
Modern gcc includes stack smashing protection by default, which must be explicitly disabled with -fno-stack-protector for the exploit to work
Little-endian memory layout on Intel architectures means addresses must be written in reverse byte order in the exploit payload
The gets() function is inherently dangerous because it has no way to limit how many characters it reads into a buffer
When you don't know the exact offset of the return address, repeating the target address multiple times can brute-force the overwrite
money_quotes: Oh look! It turns out that gcc already knows about this exploit and has special built-in protections! We can disable those, though…
BOOM. We win!
Since gets doesn't know how many characters to read into buf, it keeps reading into the stack until the string is over and writes over that address.
We don't know exactly where the address is, so we keep writing it over and over and over again until it works.
"Little-endian" basically means "backwards". Intel architectures are always like this.
posts.key_insight: Buffer overflows exploit the fact that functions like gets() write past buffer boundaries, allowing an attacker to overwrite the return address on the stack and redirect program execution.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experiment with ARP cache poisoning, a technique for intercepting network packets on a wireless network. She explains how ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) works by translating IP addresses to MAC addresses, and how the protocol's trust-based design makes it trivially exploitable. Using the arpspoof tool, she demonstrates how you can trick a device into sending its packets to your machine instead of the router. She notes some devices are more susceptible than others, with her Linux machine resisting the attack while a Macbook Pro was vulnerable.
key_points: ARP cache poisoning works because devices blindly trust unsolicited ARP replies, accepting MAC address updates from anyone on the network
The attack requires only a single command (arpspoof) to redirect traffic from a target device through the attacker's machine
MAC addresses belong to network interface cards (NICs), not machines — a computer's ethernet and wireless cards have different MAC addresses
Different operating systems and devices handle ARP poisoning differently — routers and Linux machines may resist while others like macOS accept the spoofed addresses
money_quotes: What this line basically does is "Hey phone! You want to send a packet to the router? Send that to me instead. Thanks!"
It's not just that anyone can reply, they can reply even if the phone didn't ask for a MAC address.
And my phone will just think "Sweet. Thanks for the update!"
I think the router doesn't take as much bullshit so it doesn't work.
It'll eventually fix itself up, so you have to keep sending it these messages over and over again to keep the cache poisoned.
posts.key_insight: ARP's fundamental design flaw is that devices trust unsolicited MAC address updates from anyone on the network, making packet interception trivially easy with a single command.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience trying to learn networking by attempting a specific exploit (replacing webpages with ponies on her phone). She discovers several concrete reasons why networking is fundamentally complicated: protocols evolve but implementations lag behind, and the abstraction layers that are supposed to simplify things end up leaking. The post uses iptables and TCP delayed ACK as specific examples of these broader problems.
key_points: Networking protocols change over time but implementations don't always keep up, leading to real-world incompatibilities like old firewalls rejecting valid packets
The multiple layers of networking (Ethernet, IP, TCP) are supposed to abstract away lower-level details, but those details leak through and cause unexpected problems
TCP's delayed ACK mechanism can cause a 1/5 second delay per client, meaning web server authors sometimes need to understand TCP internals they 'shouldn't' have to worry about
The sheer volume of information needed to understand even one tool like iptables is itself a reason networking is hard
money_quotes: For a long time I've had the notion that networking is pretty complicated, but I didn't really know why that was.
It is an avalanche of information and way too much to actually absorb, but it is very useful to know that there is that much stuff that exists to know.
So one part of 'networking is complicated' is 'The protocols change over time and sometimes implementations don't keep up'.
A webserver 'shouldn't' have to worry about the details of how TCP works. But then it turns out that the details of how TCP works do matter sometimes.
And there are a lot of levels of networking that could be causing problems, so when you're doing high-performance networking stuff, well… it's complicated.
posts.key_insight: Networking is hard not just because of its complexity, but because its abstraction layers leak — higher-level code that shouldn't need to care about lower-level protocols often does.

posts.summary: Julia Evans implements traceroute in just 16 lines of Python using the Scapy library. She explains how IP packets have a TTL (Time-To-Live) attribute that decrements at each hop, and how traceroute exploits this by sending packets with incrementally increasing TTLs. When a packet's TTL expires, the intermediate router sends back an ICMP error, revealing its address. The implementation sends UDP packets with increasing TTLs and checks whether the reply indicates a timeout (keep going) or a destination reached (done).
key_points: Scapy lets you construct and send custom network packets in Python with minimal code, making protocol experimentation accessible
Traceroute works by exploiting the TTL field in IP packets — each router decrements it, and when it hits zero, the router sends back an ICMP error revealing its address
A basic traceroute implementation only needs to send UDP packets with incrementing TTLs and check the ICMP reply type
Unix traceroute typically sends UDP packets while Windows traceroute sends ICMP packets; tcptraceroute sends TCP packets
money_quotes: IP packets have a ttl attribute, which stands for "Time-To-Live". Every time a machine receives an IP packet, it decreases the ttl by 1 and passes it on. Basically this is a super smart way to make sure that packets don't get into infinite loops.
If a packet's ttl runs out before it replies, the last machine sends back an ICMP packet saying "sorry, failed!".
I thought it would take a long time, but turns out that (a basic version) is really easy.
To implement traceroute, we send out a UDP packet with ttl=i for i = 1,2,3,... Then we look at the reply packet and see if it's a "Time ran out" or "That port doesn't exist" error message.
So it turns out traceroute is kind of easy!
posts.key_insight: Traceroute is a surprisingly simple concept — just send packets with incrementing TTLs and read the ICMP errors back — and can be implemented in about 15 lines of Python with the right library.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her first attempt at writing a TCP stack in Python using scapy. She gets a TCP three-way handshake working but runs into a problem: her own machine's existing network stack sends RST packets in response to unexpected SYN-ACK replies. She solves this by using a fake IP address and ARP broadcasting to bypass her OS's interference. The post captures the messy, iterative reality of learning networking by building things from scratch.
key_points: Writing a TCP stack from userspace conflicts with the OS's own network stack, which resets connections it didn't initiate
Using a fake IP address with ARP spoofing is a clever workaround to prevent the kernel from interfering with raw packet experiments
The TCP three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) can be implemented in surprisingly few lines of Python with scapy
Reading historical source code like the 4.4BSD network stack provides valuable context for understanding networking internals
money_quotes: Today I started trying to write a TCP stack. In Python. We will see if this is a good idea.
I already have a network stack on my machine, and it was like "what's this SYN-ACK packet? I didn't ask for this!". So it would just reset the connection.
I think I could also use iptables here to tell the kernel to ignore those packets. But I'm currently afraid of iptables.
Upon inspecting Wireshark, it turned out that *my* machine was the problem: it was sending out a RST (reset) packet after I got a SYN-ACK packet back from Google.
That is my small amount of code for the day.
posts.key_insight: Building a TCP stack from userspace forces you to confront the reality that your OS already has one, and it will fight you for control of the network unless you find creative workarounds.

posts.summary: Julia describes her progress building a TCP stack from scratch, where she and Jessica managed to successfully receive HTTP responses by sending properly formatted TCP packets to example.com. The main breakthrough was realizing the problem was at the HTTP level (needing proper headers with CRLF line endings), not TCP. She successfully reassembled TCP packets into a webpage but notes several remaining tasks like handling FIN-ACK and deduplicating packets. She reflects on a less productive day due to giving a talk at NYC Python, and expresses eagerness to move toward building something concrete like a bittorrent client.
key_points: The debugging breakthrough was recognizing the issue was HTTP-level formatting (proper CRLF headers), not a TCP-level problem
Successfully reassembled raw TCP packets into a readable webpage, a key milestone in her from-scratch networking project
Remaining TCP implementation work includes ACK responses, FIN-ACK handling, and packet deduplication
Tension between exploratory learning and desire to write 'real' production-style code
money_quotes: It turned out that the problem we were having was not at the TCP level, but actually at HTTP level
I then successfully reassembled a bunch of TCP packets into a webpage!
This exploratory networking stuff is super fun and I'm learning a lot, but I feel like I haven't written much code in a while and it is bothering me.
Maybe a bittorrent client will come soon =)
BUT WHICH LANGUAGE. Clojure? Hmm.
posts.key_insight: When debugging networking code, the problem often lives at a different layer of the stack than you expect — here an HTTP formatting issue masqueraded as a TCP problem.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes starting to build a minimal TCP stack in Python as a way to learn networking through hands-on coding. She's using scapy to handle low-level packet construction while implementing the TCP state machine herself, including socket management and a listener thread. So far she can establish connections and send data, but can't yet parse responses or properly tear down connections. The project is revealing practical challenges like managing half-dead connections and servers treating her implementation as unreliable.
key_points: Building a TCP stack from scratch is an effective way to understand networking beyond just reading about it
Using scapy as a foundation lets her focus on TCP state machine logic without worrying about raw packet bit layout
The implementation includes a TCPSocket class tracking connection states and a TCPListener dispatching packets to sockets via threads
Practical challenges like zombie connections and servers throttling responses reveal real-world TCP behavior that reading alone wouldn't surface
money_quotes: Yesterday I realized that I really hadn't been writing as much code as I want to be, though.
So I've started working on a mini TCP stack! I'm not sure yet how far this will go, but so far I'm running into tons of problems, so I think this might be a good choice.
This is my first time using threads pretty much ever. Expecting all the problems.
I keep starting and stopping the library, so there are all kinds of connections that just die.
Also I think the servers I'm interacting with think I'm unreliable, so sometimes they send me packets slowly.
posts.key_insight: Building a TCP stack from scratch, even an incomplete one, teaches you more about networking than reading about it because the real-world problems you hit — zombie connections, threading issues, server behavior — are the actual substance of how TCP works.

posts.summary: Julia Evans gave a talk at NYC Python about using IPython Notebook for data analysis, using NYC's 311 calls dataset as a practical example. The talk explored real urban data questions like which borough complains most about rats (the Bronx) and which days have the most noise complaints (Saturday and Sunday). She shared the notebook for others to try themselves.
key_points: IPython Notebook is an effective tool for interactive data exploration and presentation
NYC Open Data's 311 calls dataset provides rich, accessible data for learning data analysis
Sharing notebooks enables others to reproduce and extend your analysis
money_quotes: We talked about which borough complains the most about rats (the Bronx) and which days of the week have the most noise complaints (Saturday, Sunday).
posts.key_insight: Real-world public datasets like NYC 311 calls make data analysis tools like IPython Notebook immediately engaging and accessible to learn.

posts.summary: Julia describes improving her TCP library's test suite by replacing network-dependent tests with mock-based unit tests. The tests went from taking 5-10 seconds to 0.009 seconds and became deterministic. She acknowledges the implementation is still messy and she's stuck on implementing the TCP state machine, so she plans to study a minimal C implementation of TCP for guidance.
key_points: Replacing network-dependent tests with a MockListener class made tests faster and deterministic
Good unit tests should not depend on external resources like the network or arbitrary sleeps
Sometimes you need to study existing implementations to understand how to build something complex like a TCP state machine
Having only 100 lines of Python (with scapy handling packet unpacking) versus 1000 lines of C shows the power of high-level abstractions
money_quotes: I realized I'd been having a lot of trouble testing this TCP library yesterday, because all of the tests needed to use the network.
I did this by adding a MockListener class which takes packets.
The tests are faster (0.009 seconds instead of 5 or 10), and deterministic (they do the same thing every time).
The implemention is still pretty much a scattered mess.
I'm at the point where I have no idea of how to implement this TCP state machine.
Right now I have about 100 lines of Python, and I don't even have to unpack the packets or anything because scapy does that.
posts.key_insight: Replacing network-dependent tests with mocks makes tests dramatically faster and deterministic, which is essential for making progress on complex networking code.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares progress on her TCP stack implementation, describing a key insight from TCP/IP Illustrated: that every TCP packet except the initial SYN can have the ACK flag set. This realization significantly simplified her packet-sending code. She's taking a correctness-first approach by ACKing every received packet, and has started thinking about implementing bind() for server-side TCP, which turns out to be surprisingly complex. She also celebrates how tests enabled a major refactoring.
key_points: Every TCP packet except the initial SYN can have the ACK flag set, which dramatically simplifies packet construction code
A correctness-first approach (ACKing every packet) is a valid strategy even if it's not efficient
The bind() syscall is deceptively complex—it must manage multiple connections and queuing simultaneously
Tests enabled a confident large-scale refactoring of the TCP stack code
money_quotes: I realized I can construct basically every packet the same way, except for the flags and the payload.
Right now the way I'm handling ACKs is basically to send an ACK for every packet I receive. This isn't really efficient... But it is not incorrect, which is all that I'm going for.
Seriously I did not ever think about how bind() works before. It has to manage multiple connections! And keep a queue!
What am I supposed to do when people send me packets all at once? Just ignore them until I have time? I don't get it.
The code still works, because tests. =D
posts.key_insight: A correctness-first approach—doing the simple, technically valid thing before optimizing—is a powerful strategy when building complex protocol implementations from scratch.

posts.summary: Evans shares her struggle to implement a readable TCP state machine handler function in Python as part of building a TCP stack. She shows two iterations of the code: the first naively sends an ACK after every packet (which turns out to be wrong), and the second handles flags more explicitly but introduces ordering dependencies. She candidly admits that the messiness of her code reflects her incomplete understanding of TCP itself, and that the combinatorial nature of states and flags makes it hard to verify correctness without extensive testing.
key_points: A naive assumption that you always send an ACK after receiving a packet turns out to be wrong in several cases (ACK packets and RST packets)
Restructuring the handler to dispatch on TCP flags rather than connection state made the code more correct but introduced ordering dependencies between cases
The combinatorial explosion of TCP states and flags makes the code hard to reason about and verify without extensive unit testing
Messy code often reflects incomplete understanding of the underlying problem domain
money_quotes: In particular, I thought this _send_ack() call at the end was a great idea, because you always want to send an ACK! Except when you don't!
My confusion about this function largely reflects my confusion about TCP at this point, I think.
The cases aren't all mutually exclusive, so the order matters. :[

Each one is a seemingly random combination of self.seq += 1, self._send_ack(), self._close(), and a change to self.state. It's hard to make sure the whole thing is right without a ton of unit testing.
Today I made a bunch of progress (I can now be a TCP server, kinda!), but I want to talk about problems instead.
posts.key_insight: The messiness of code implementing a protocol often directly mirrors incomplete understanding of that protocol, and iterating on the code is itself a way of deepening that understanding.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recounts running Python workshops for high school girls at PyData NYC, organized by NumFOCUS with Hacker School volunteers. The beginner group learned programming concepts through Ren'Py story games, while the advanced group explored NYC 311 complaint data using Python. Evans emphasizes prioritizing fun over maximizing learning content, noting the students were genuinely engaged with exploring data from their own neighborhoods. The event also featured demos, talks, and a panel where women in tech shared their diverse paths into programming.
key_points: Teaching programming through personally relevant data (students' own ZIP codes and neighborhoods) created genuine engagement and curiosity
Prioritizing enjoyment over cramming maximum content proved to be an effective workshop strategy
The workshops used creative entry points for programming: story games for beginners and data analysis for advanced students
The panel highlighted diverse paths into programming, including people who came from music and other non-technical backgrounds
money_quotes: I tried to work more on making sure they had a good time than making them Learn the Maximum Amount of Things, and I think that went pretty well.
One person found that someone had been complaining about "Unsanitary Pigeon Conditions" near their house.
The colour-changing was more exciting than I expected – they got pretty into investigating which colours matplotlib knew about, and were really impressed.

One person said she studied music and then started programming later, and someone else said she wanted to study music, but her parents made her do something more practical and she ended up enjoying programming.
I am always amazed by how cool the programmer women I meet are.
posts.key_insight: Making programming fun and personally relevant through local data exploration is more effective than trying to maximize the amount of technical content taught.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recounts a productive day at Hacker School covering three topics: using Python's __setattr__ to automatically test TCP state transitions, learning about floating point number representation, and exploring the idea of visualizing Git workflows from shell history. She worked with Allison Kaptur on a clever testing approach for her TCP client, attended a lecture on floats by Stefan Karpinski, and brainstormed with Philip Guo about using command history files to understand how people actually use tools. The post concludes with her realization that social interaction is her most effective learning channel.
key_points: Overriding Python's __setattr__ enables elegant automatic logging of state transitions for testing stateful code like TCP connections
There are exactly 2^64 possible 64-bit floats, and the distribution between powers of two has useful mathematical properties
Shell history files could be valuable for teaching novices, understanding tool usage patterns, and studying expert workflows
Learning about command line tools happens primarily through social interactions and folklore rather than documentation
Visualizing transitions between Git commands from history data reveals personal workflow patterns
money_quotes: Allison had the amazing idea of subclassing my socket class to log every time I set the state attribute.
The key thing I learned there is that there are a finite amount of 64-bit floats (2^64 of them!). Which is obvious in retrospect, but I hadn't thought about it before.
There are always 2^52 floats between 2^n and 2^(n+1).
I find it really interesting how I learn about command line tools largely through social interactions and folklore, and rarely by reading documentation or man pages.
I need to talk to more people. I learn more when I talk to more people.
posts.key_insight: The most effective way to learn technical tools and concepts is through social interaction and collaboration rather than solo documentation reading.

posts.summary: Julia built a web application that visualizes Git workflows based on the sequence of commands a user has executed. The tool generates visual diagrams showing how different Git commands relate to each other. She demonstrates it with Selena Deckelmann's workflow, which clearly illustrates when to use rebase and stash in a typical workflow.
key_points: Built a webapp that turns Git command histories into visual diagrams
Visualizations can reveal workflow patterns like when to use rebase vs stash
The tool makes implicit Git knowledge explicit through pictures
money_quotes: You tell it the sequence of git commands you've ever executed. It gives you back pretty pictures.
I really like how this one makes it clear when you'd want to use rebase (in between commit and push), or stash (before checkout and pull). It is a thing of beauty.
posts.key_insight: Visualizing the sequence of Git commands you actually use can reveal workflow patterns and clarify when different commands are most useful.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her decision to try Emacs after watching Lisp programmers at Hacker School use it with live-reloading capabilities. She's motivated by wanting to use Overtone for music generation in Clojure and was inspired by a talk on logic programming and automatic quine generation. She plans to explore Clojure's core.logic library. She notes she'll use evil mode to keep her vim keybindings.
key_points: Seeing Lisp programmers interact with running processes through Emacs motivated her to try it
Plans to combine Emacs with Clojure and Overtone for music generation
A talk on relational programming and automatic quine generation sparked interest in core.logic
Pragmatically choosing evil mode to preserve vim muscle memory while gaining Emacs capabilities
money_quotes: I've been watching all the Lisp wizards with their emacs attached to magical running Lisp processes where they can change and reload the code in real time. And I am officially jealous.
Definitely using evil mode, though, because I haven't spent years practicing vim keybindings for nothing.
posts.key_insight: Seeing the live-coding workflow of Lisp programmers using Emacs can be compelling enough to make even a committed vim user switch editors.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her first day using Overtone, a Clojure library for generating music through code. She successfully made sounds by copying examples despite not knowing Clojure, music theory, or Supercollider. She encountered her first Clojure bug involving lazy evaluation, where map wouldn't execute side effects inside a recursive function. The fix was using doseq instead of map for side-effecty operations. She also shares her growing comfort with Emacs and paredit for Clojure development.
key_points: Overtone lets you make music in Clojure, and you can get started by copying examples even without knowing the prerequisites
Clojure's lazy evaluation with map means side effects won't execute unless the result is consumed, which is a common stumbling block for beginners
doseq is the correct tool for executing side effects in sequence when you don't care about the return value
Learning by doing and hitting real bugs teaches language semantics more effectively than reading documentation
money_quotes: On the box it says that you need to be "comfortable with" Clojure, the basics of music theory, and the Supercollider audio synthesis environment. I do not know any of these things and I successfully made sounds by copying and changing examples.
And I was TOTALLY CONFUSED, because, it says (map side-effecty-thing) in it! It should make sounds!
But then Travis explained that map is lazy and not actually appropriate if you want the function you are running to happen right away.
I no longer know how to write if statements without looking it up! Very Exciting Times!
posts.key_insight: You don't need to meet all the stated prerequisites to start making things — copying examples and hitting real bugs is a valid and effective way to learn.

posts.summary: Julia describes pairing with Will Byrd to implement binary search trees using core.logic in Clojure. The key revelation is that logic programming lets you run functions 'backwards' — instead of just checking if a tree contains a value, you can ask for trees that contain a given value. She shows the code for a containso function and demonstrates both forward and backward queries, generating 10 different binary trees that all contain the number 3.
key_points: Logic programming with core.logic allows bidirectional queries — you can check membership or generate structures that satisfy constraints
Running a logic program 'backwards' to generate valid data structures from constraints is the compelling feature of relational programming
The code is remarkably concise — a few lines of core.logic define a relational binary search tree
money_quotes: My favorite thing about logic programming is that all the functions end with 'o'.
But the really fun part is that you can run it "backwards" and ask for 10 trees that contain 3.
If you inspect carefully, you will see that those are all binary trees and they all contain 3!
posts.key_insight: Logic programming's power lies in running programs backwards — instead of just verifying properties, you can generate all structures that satisfy a given constraint.

posts.summary: Julia describes building a 'Magical Orchestra' project in Clojure with collaborator Lyndsey during Hacker School. The web app lets people visit a URL on their phones and trigger sounds (like a crow or cowbell) that play on the host computer. They demonstrated it at presentations by plugging into a speaker and letting everyone make noise together. It's a short, playful post celebrating a fun collaborative hack.
key_points: Built a collaborative web-based music toy in Clojure where visitors trigger sounds on the host computer
The project turned presentations into interactive experiences by letting the audience participate
Demonstrates the joy of building small, fun, shareable projects during Hacker School
money_quotes: Then a sound plays on my computer! Like a crow, or a cowbell.
So at presentations we plugged my computer into a speaker and let everyone make noise. It was delightful.
posts.key_insight: Some of the most delightful programming projects are simple, playful tools that let people interact together in unexpected ways.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a curated collection of resources for making music with ClojureScript, compiled during her Hacker School project to practice change ringing. The post covers three main areas: getting started with ClojureScript, using the Web Audio API for sound synthesis in the browser, and working with sound samples from freesound.org. Rather than a tutorial, it's a practical reference list born from the frustration of repeatedly searching for the same resources. The post reflects her learning-in-public approach during her Hacker School batch.
key_points: ClojureScript can be used with the Web Audio API to synthesize and play music directly in the browser
The Web Audio API was relatively new at the time and enabled sound synthesis without plugins
When starting new projects, collecting and organizing scattered resources in one place saves repeated searching
The Hacker School community provided many of the key resources, showing the value of peer learning
money_quotes: As always when starting a new project I have been consulting approximately a million resources to get started.
So I thought I'd collect them all together in one place instead of searching for them over and over forever.
It says "Apply", but it gives it to you automatically.
It should make an annoying noise when you run it.
posts.key_insight: When learning a new technology, collecting and sharing the scattered resources you find is itself a valuable contribution.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her first steps toward writing a tiny operating system during Hacker School. She decides to target x86 so she can run it on real hardware, finds the MikeOS tutorial as a starting resource, and gets a minimal OS running in an emulator. The OS can print characters to the screen and change the background color using BIOS interrupt handlers. It's a brief, enthusiastic update documenting the very beginning of an OS project.
key_points: Writing an OS from scratch is approachable with the right resources like MikeOS tutorials
Starting with BIOS interrupt handlers (INT 10H) provides immediate visual feedback when learning OS development
Using emulators like qemu/kvm lets you test bare-metal code without dedicated hardware
The barrier to entry for OS development is lower than expected: just nasm, qemu, and build-essential
money_quotes: One of my as-yet-unrealized goals for Hacker School is to write an operating system, at least a tiny one.
Today I decided to actually start in earnest, because this operating system isn't going to write itself!
If you install build-essential, nasm, and qemu, and clone this gist, you too will have the tiniest operating system!
posts.key_insight: Getting started with OS development is more accessible than it seems — a few tools and a good tutorial can get you from zero to printing characters on screen in a day.

posts.summary: Julia Evans wraps up her TCP stack project at Hacker School by writing a minimal curl implementation on top of it. The tool uses ARP spoofing to bypass the kernel's built-in TCP stack, listening on a fake IP address to avoid the kernel resetting connections. She candidly documents that the implementation is finicky and unreliable, often requiring multiple runs to succeed. The post catalogs both what works (connecting, sending packets, reassembling replies) and what doesn't (multi-packet sends, retransmission, server functionality).
key_points: Building a userspace TCP stack requires bypassing the kernel's own stack via ARP spoofing, since the kernel would otherwise intercept and reset connections
The implementation works as a barely functional client but is essentially useless as a server, highlighting how much complexity a real TCP stack handles
TCP stacks aren't designed to start and stop — they're meant to run as daemons, which creates fundamental design friction for a project like this
Python's performance creates visible problems: ACKs pile up and the connection closing process becomes comically slow when watched in Wireshark
money_quotes: I discovered that it was impossible to explain my TCP fun project because it was, er, mostly not working.
I found that if I ran it 5 times then it would work. Mostly.
This IP address needs to be in my subnet and should not belong to anyone else, because it would do bad things to them.
Basically it is a marginally acceptable client and a totally ineffective server
TCP stacks aren't really *supposed* to start and stop.

It's slow, because Python. If you watch it in Wireshark, it does a hilarious thing where it gets backed up sending ACKs and then sends a load of ACKs at the end and takes forever to close the connection.
posts.key_insight: Building a TCP stack from scratch reveals how much invisible complexity the kernel handles, even when the result barely works.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her attempt to understand what happens at the OS level when running a simple 'Hello, world' program. She posted her current understanding as a GitHub gist and asked Twitter for corrections and additions, receiving many helpful comments. The post walks through the key steps: loading code and data segments into memory, setting up the heap and stack, and handling system calls. She simplifies the program by replacing printf with a direct write() call and uses strace to observe the actual system calls made. Throughout, she's honest about gaps in her understanding, particularly around virtual memory and certain system calls like arch_prctl.
key_points: A statically compiled hello world program still requires the OS to load code and data segments, set up heap and stack, and handle multiple system calls
Replacing printf() with write() dramatically simplifies the program for learning purposes, removing layers of abstraction
strace reveals the actual system calls made by a simple program, including memory allocation via brk before the write can happen
Posting your incomplete understanding publicly and asking for corrections is an effective learning technique
money_quotes: The question: If I were an OS, what would I need to do to run 'Hello, world?'
printf() is a pretty complicated function, so it's better to not use it.

I'm still really not sure about the details of what this set up looks like – people talk a lot about virtual memory and I don't know how I would implement that at all or if I would have to implement it.
I wanted to understand what happens when I run a 'hello world' program, but I wasn't at Hacker School. So I wrote down my current understanding and a bunch of questions and asked Twitter!
I don't think I have to worry about the first two system calls, since the first one is definitely called by my shell.
posts.key_insight: Even the simplest program involves a complex chain of OS operations — loading segments, setting up memory, handling system calls — and the best way to learn this is to strip away abstractions and examine what actually happens.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience starting to write a kernel in Rust using the rustboot project and rust-core library. After being encouraged by Lindsey Kuper to try Rust for her OS project, she progressed from turning the screen green to writing functions that print characters to the VGA buffer. She shares the actual code for writing to VGA memory and iterating over string bytes, along with the cryptic import requirements needed to make basic features work in freestanding Rust. The post highlights both the satisfaction of low-level programming without an OS and the challenges of working with an early-stage systems language.
key_points: Rustboot provides a minimal 32-bit kernel starting point, and she extended it from displaying a colored screen to printing text via the VGA buffer
Writing freestanding Rust requires importing seemingly unrelated modules to enable basic features like iterators and loops
Rust stores string length separately rather than using null-terminated strings, unlike C
The Rust IRC community and rust-core maintainer were essential to making progress past obscure error messages
There's a mysterious bug where printing 10 characters fails despite 9 working correctly
money_quotes: So far I've made some minor changes – it now turns the screen *green* and hangs.
Rust is for systems programming, and right now I *am* trying to write an operating system, and this would be the perfect time to pick it up again!
It's nice that I can write code like this without having an OS in the background.
I got the cryptic error message `error: unreachable pattern`, which meant that I needed to add `use core::option::None`.
This small amount of accomplishment wouldn't have been remotely possible without the help of the lovely people on the #rust IRC channel.
That code prints 9 characters to the VGA buffer correctly, but if I try printing 10 characters, it doesn't work. I have no idea why. That will be for tomorrow.
posts.key_insight: Writing a kernel in Rust is possible with minimal scaffolding, but freestanding Rust development requires navigating cryptic import dependencies and relying heavily on community support to get even basic features working.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares that videos from PyData NYC 2013 are now available, including her own IPython Notebook and pandas tutorial. She highlights several talks she enjoyed, particularly one on probabilistic data structures and Bloom filters, and another on Python at Datadog that demonstrated Cython's code optimization visualization. The post serves as a brief conference recap with links to resources and recommendations for talks worth watching.
key_points: PyData NYC 2013 videos are available, including Evans's interactive pandas tutorial
Bloom filters can describe huge amounts of data but require sizing based on expected data volume, and can be configured with expiring elements
Cython has a tool that automatically color-codes your code to show optimization levels, which Evans found particularly impressive
Interactive tutorials don't always translate well to video format
money_quotes: I'm not sure how well it translates to video, since it was a pretty interactive tutorial and I spent a lot of the time running around in the audience answering questions.
My main takeaway from this talk was that you can use Bloom filters to describe a huge amount of data, but not an unlimited amount of data.
At 25:27, he demos how Cython has a tool to automatically colour your Cython code and show how optimized it is. Whoa. That is the kind of stuff I go to conferences to learn :)
