I wasn't able to make it to this talk, but everyone was abuzz about it on Twitter afterwards, so I'm definitely going to watch the video.
I didn't make it to very many talks at the conference, so I'd love to hear about what else I missed.
posts.key_insight: Conferences are valuable for discovering unexpected tools and techniques — like Cython's optimization visualization — that you wouldn't encounter in your normal workflow.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains the mechanics of calling Rust functions from assembly and vice versa, as part of her project building a kernel in Rust. She walks through the key concepts: calling conventions, object files, symbols, and linkers. The post demonstrates how the `extern` keyword works in both assembly (NASM) and Rust to declare functions defined elsewhere. She shows how a linker script combines the object files into a single binary by matching symbol tables. The post demystifies linker errors by explaining they simply mean a symbol wasn't found in any linked object file, and shows how to inspect symbol tables with `objdump`.
key_points: A function call is fundamentally just jumping to a memory address with arguments on the stack/registers — the code doesn't care if that address comes from Rust, C, or assembly
Both assembly (NASM) and Rust use the `extern` keyword to declare functions defined in other object files, though Rust requires type annotations
The linker combines object files by matching symbol names across their symbol tables, and linker errors just mean a declared symbol wasn't found
You can inspect object file symbol tables with `objdump -t` to see which symbols are defined and which are undefined
money_quotes: As far as I understand it, a function call is just jumping to an address in memory and putting some stuff on the stack and in registers, and the code doesn't care at all if that address in memory comes from Rust or C or assembly or what.
This is like a function declaration in C, except without the types.
I haven't tested this yet so there's probably something wrong with it. But it compiles.
But this kind of linker error isn't scary any more! It just means that _interrupt_handler_kbd_wrapper isn't in the symbol table of any of the other object files we're linking.
We have no idea what the function could be doing, so it's unsafe.
Disclaimer: even more than usual, some of this is probably wrong.
posts.key_insight: Cross-language function calls between Rust and assembly are demystified once you understand that linkers simply match symbol names across object files, regardless of the source language.

posts.summary: Julia Evans wrote a beginner-friendly tutorial on Rust's type system to help herself understand it. She covers primitive types, vectors, strings, functions, closures, and raw pointers with practical examples. The post acknowledges that Rust's type system is confusing and that the official documentation lacks sufficient examples. She provides workarounds for common frustrations like the lack of a REPL and difficulty discovering variable types. The tutorial reflects early Rust (pre-1.0) when the language was rapidly changing, with syntax like owned pointers (~) and managed pointers (@) that have since been removed.
key_points: Rust's type system is confusing for beginners partly because the language was constantly changing and documentation lacked examples
Practical workarounds exist for Rust's missing REPL, including a compile-and-print script and deliberate type mismatch errors to reveal types
Vectors and strings in Rust have multiple type variants (owned, borrowed, fixed-size) which is a major source of confusion
The distinction between owned (~) and borrowed (&) pointers is fundamental to understanding Rust's type system
Writing tutorials for yourself is an effective way to learn confusing material
money_quotes: I found understanding Rust types really confusing, so I wrote up a small tutorial for myself in an attempt to understand some of them.
There is a types section in the manual, but it has nowhere near enough examples.
A lot of the difficulty with Rust types is that the language is constantly changing, so this will likely be out of date soon.
It's easier to play with types if you have a REPL and can interactively check the types of objects. This isn't really possible in Rust, but there are workarounds.
Indexing into a string gives you a byte, not a character.
posts.key_insight: Writing a tutorial for yourself, complete with practical examples and workarounds, is an effective way to learn a confusing type system when the official documentation falls short.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes the experience of writing a kernel in Rust without access to malloc, standard libraries, or basic OS facilities. She catalogs what's unavailable—memory allocation, printing, sleep, files, stdin—and finds it surprisingly manageable. The constraint forces all memory to be compile-time or stack-allocated, which gives her new appreciation for why the Linux kernel uses so many global variables. She shares VGA buffer code that writes characters directly to screen memory.
key_points: Writing an OS kernel means losing nearly every convenience programmers take for granted: memory allocation, printing, files, sleep, and other processes
Programming without malloc is less difficult than expected—memory just needs to be compile-time or stack-allocated
Global variables in the Linux kernel make more sense when you realize malloc/free overhead is pointless for singleton structs
The shift from high-level Python to bare-metal Rust inverts expectations: fewer resources but code runs fast with no excuse not to
money_quotes: My OS literally can't allocate memory, and there's no standard way to print (I have to write to the VGA buffer manually).
I expect my code to run super fast, because it has no excuse not to.
Right now I definitely don't have timers or anything, so I'm looping 80,000,000 times to sleep.
read from stdin (I don't have a keyboard driver yet. There is no stdin.)
open files (there are no files)
This is less difficult than I expected it to be!

It's given me an appreciation for why there is so much use of global variables in the Linux kernel – if just need 1 struct, it makes so much more sense to just have 1 global struct than to keep malloc'ing and free'ing it all the time.
posts.key_insight: Programming without malloc on bare metal is surprisingly tractable and reveals why kernel code relies on global variables—when you only need one of something, dynamic allocation is pointless overhead.

posts.summary: Julia documents her multi-day struggle to get keyboard interrupts working in her hobby operating system written in Rust. She walks through 16 steps of debugging, from creating descriptor tables to remapping the PIC, each step revealing a new way the OS could crash. The post captures the grueling iterative nature of OS development, where each fix reveals the next obscure problem. After five days, she achieved the milestone of pressing a key without crashing, though initially only for the first keypress.
key_points: Getting keyboard input working in a hobby OS requires a chain of low-level steps: GDT, IDT, interrupt handlers, PIC remapping, and each one can silently break everything
The OSDev wiki page 'I Can't Get Interrupts Working' was essential — she recommends reading it three times every time you have a problem
An Intel design bug requires remapping the PIC so hardware interrupts don't collide with CPU exceptions, and forgetting to update the IDT after remapping cost two days of debugging
Testing interrupts manually before enabling hardware interrupts is a key debugging technique for OS development
money_quotes: Right now I'm working towards being able to: 1. press keys on my keyboard 2. having the OS not crash 3. and have the key I pressed be echoed back
Read this three times every time you have a problem. For real.
Press a key. The OS crashes. Continue experimenting in this vein for some time. Still crashing.
Remap the PIC so that interrupt i gets mapped to i + 32, because of an Intel design bug. This basically looks like just typing in a bunch of random numbers, but it works.
THE OS IS STILL CRASHING WHEN I PRESS A KEY. This continues for 2 days.
Press a key. My interrupt handler runs. Practically faint with joy.
As far as I can tell this is all totally normal and just how OS programming is.
I'm seriously amazed that operating systems exist and are available for free.
posts.key_insight: OS development is a chain of obscure, interdependent low-level details where each fix reveals the next crash, making every small milestone — like pressing a key without crashing — a genuine triumph.

posts.summary: Evans documents a baffling array indexing bug in her Rust-based OS kernel where NUMS[2] and NUMS[keycode] produce different results even when keycode provably equals 2. The bug only manifests when the conditional checks for multiple keycodes, suggesting compiler optimization interactions. She methodically demonstrates the contradiction through output analysis and rules out obvious explanations. The fix turned out to be switching from raw binary linking to ELF format with objcopy, though she doesn't fully understand why that resolved it.
key_points: A static array index with a literal constant produced different results than indexing with a variable holding the same value, which should be impossible in normal circumstances
The bug disappeared when the conditional was simplified, suggesting compiler optimizations were masking or causing the issue
The root cause was related to the binary linking format — switching to ELF linking with objcopy fixed it
Debugging was hampered by symbols being stripped during linking, preventing breakpoints in gdb
money_quotes: After 6 days, I have problems that I can't understand at all
I expect NUMS[2] to equal NUMS[keycode] when keycode == 2. This doesn't appear to be the case, and I don't understand how this is possible.
should be '2', BUT IT ISN'T. IT IS SOMETHING ELSE. HOW IS THIS HAPPENING.
I think this is because of a compiler optimization replacing keycode with 2.
Some hypotheses: There's something wrong with the Rust compiler. There's something wrong with the stack and how I'm calling _interrupt_handler_kbd. ?????????
posts.key_insight: When writing OS-level code, the most bewildering bugs often stem not from logic errors but from toolchain issues like linking format, where the abstraction layers between your code and the hardware silently break assumptions.

posts.summary: Julia describes writing a deliberately broken malloc implementation in Rust as part of her OS project at Hacker School. Her first version always returns the same memory address, causing every variable to share the same value. She then shows the working version that properly increments a base pointer. She notes that writing malloc is easy if you never need to free memory, and the hardest part was actually getting the Rust type signatures right.
key_points: A malloc that always returns the same pointer makes every variable share one value, producing entertaining wrong behavior
Writing a basic malloc is straightforward — just maintain a counter and keep incrementing it, as long as you never need to free
Getting Rust's type system to accept the function signature was harder than the actual memory allocation logic
The free implementation is literally empty, which is fine for a toy OS context
money_quotes: My major achievement for today is writing the following five lines of code and having them do the wrong thing.
This means that every time I allocate memory, I get the same pointer back, and so a and b will always be equal no matter what.
This is of course a terrible idea in real life, but it is really fun.
Writing malloc is easy, as long as you never need to free memory.
The hard part was getting the type of the function right, because Rust :).
posts.key_insight: Writing a basic malloc is surprisingly simple — the real complexity lives in freeing memory and satisfying the type system, not in allocation itself.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents 12 things she learned about linkers from reading an 11-part series on LWN. The post covers ELF file structure, including segments vs sections, symbol types and visibility, relocation tables, and shared library linking. She explains practical tools like objdump, readelf, and nm for inspecting object files. The post ends with a curiosity about why symbol name mangling is used for polymorphism instead of ELF symbol versions.
key_points: ELF executables have both segments and sections, but the OS only looks at segments for read/write/execute permissions
Linkers do surprisingly complex work to enable shared libraries to save memory across programs, using mechanisms like PLT/GOT
Every .o file contains a relocation table listing every symbol reference the linker needs to update
.a archive files are simply collections of .o object files
The speed benchmark for a linker is comparing it to cat, suggesting the goal is to be I/O-bound rather than compute-bound
money_quotes: Those `.a` files? Those are just collections of `.o` object files, and they're called "archives"!
Linkers actually have to do fairly complicated stuff to allow the code in a shared library to be shared between different programs and save memory.
If you're making a speed comparison for a linker, you might compare it to `cat`.
In the Mach-O executable format you can have assembly code for *different architectures* in the same executable. Nuts.
Every `.o` file has a "relocation table" listing every single reference to a symbol that the linker will need to update, and how it will need to update it.
posts.key_insight: Linkers are far more complex than they appear, handling symbol resolution, memory optimization for shared libraries, threading awareness, and relocation — all while trying to run as fast as cat.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her deep dive into how linkers work, motivated by a linker bug in her kernel project. She summarizes the first 11 parts of Ian Lance Taylor's 20-part series on linkers, covering core concepts like relocations, ELF sections and segments, shared libraries, position-independent code, thread local storage, and symbol versioning. Throughout, she uses hands-on exploration with tools like objdump and readelf to make abstract concepts concrete. The post serves as both personal reference notes and an accessible introduction to linker internals.
key_points: Linkers fundamentally don't care about your code's semantics - they just combine bytes and make address replacements, making 'cat' a reasonable performance benchmark
Position-independent code trades runtime speed for faster library loading, using the Global Offset Table (GOT) for variables and Procedure Lookup Table (PLT) for functions
ELF segments are collections of sections that the OS actually uses to run programs - sections like .text and .data are separated into different segments based on read/write/execute permissions
Linkers are architecture-dependent because different CPU architectures generate different relocation patterns for the same C code
Symbol versioning in ELF allows multiple versions of the same symbol to coexist, enabling backward compatibility when function signatures change
money_quotes: Linkers don't care about your functions and variables! They're just bytes.
A benchmark you could compare a linker to is cat, because it's just combining the bytes together, and making some replacements along the way.
The compiler and linker will do a bunch of stuff so that every thread has its own copy of the variable.
Apparently the operating system doesn't use the sections at all to run programs, just the segments.
This indicates the address and size of the segment table. This is not too useful in practice as you have to have already found the segment table before you can find this segment.
Could you abuse this to have polymorphism in object files without doing name mangling?
I had no idea this was a thing the linker did. Linkers are crazy.
posts.key_insight: Linkers are surprisingly complex systems that handle relocations, position independence, thread-local storage, and symbol versioning — but at their core they're just combining bytes and filling in addresses.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents a frustrating day spent debugging a linker problem in her OS project. She and a pair programming partner discovered that ELF files only work when sections are ordered in a specific way (.text .rodata .data .bss), but she can't figure out why. Despite fixing the issue, the lack of understanding is clearly bothering her. She posted a StackOverflow question that went unanswered and began writing a tutorial on binary formats as a consolation project.
key_points: ELF section ordering (.text .rodata .data .bss) matters for correctness, but the reason why is not obvious even after fixing the bug
Pair programming helped discover the fix through experimentation, even without full understanding of the root cause
Some low-level systems topics like linker scripts have sparse community support, even on StackOverflow
Writing tutorials and explanations can be a productive response to frustration with a problem
money_quotes: I've fixed it, but I don't understand *why* it's fixed, and I am having no luck.
Today I spent pretty much the whole day trying to figure out what's going on with a linker problem I'm having.
I also created a StackOverflow question but it is not getting any love.
posts.key_insight: Fixing a bug without understanding why it's fixed is deeply unsatisfying, and linker internals are one of those areas where even community resources fall short.

posts.summary: Julia documents her attempt to figure out how to run a simple ELF executable inside her hobby operating system (called 'puddle'). She breaks down the problem into concrete steps: parsing ELF headers, implementing system call handlers, finding the entry point, and jumping to it. She explicitly lists what she's skipping for now (virtual memory, security, scheduling) and what questions remain unanswered, particularly around position-independent code and address resolution. The post captures the messy, exploratory middle of a learning project where the path forward is only partially clear.
key_points: Running an ELF binary requires writing a 'loader' that parses headers, sets up system call handlers, and jumps to the entry point
She deliberately skips virtual memory, security, scheduling, and proper memory management to get a minimal version working first
Static linking simplifies the problem significantly by removing the need for dynamic linking infrastructure like PLT/GOT
The post honestly catalogs unresolved questions alongside the known steps, showing learning as a process of narrowing unknowns
money_quotes: I've discovered that this "set stuff up so that a program can run" business is called loading, and what I'm doing is writing a loader. Sweet.
Right now I'm doing this before implementing paging and virtual memory and not after, because this seems more fun than virtual memory for now. If this is a very bad idea, I would like to know.
I'm just going to allocate everyone's memory in the same part of physical memory for now. And never free. Yeah.
The goal is to get everything just barely working, so that it will print the string to the screen and not crash the whole system.
Is there even a PLT in a static executable, or is that just for dynamic linking? Eep. Hmm.
posts.key_insight: Breaking an intimidating systems problem into a checklist of concrete steps and explicit unknowns transforms it from overwhelming to tractable.

posts.summary: Julia recounts a frustrating day debugging her hobby OS project, where a mysterious bug kept zeroing out her array contents at runtime. After upgrading Rust and rust-core (which broke her build), she worked through the compilation issues only to hit the same recurring mystery bug. With help from Allison Kaptur and GDB debugging, she discovered that half her binary was being overwritten with zeros at runtime. The root cause turned out to be embarrassingly simple: her bootloader was only loading 12KB of the binary, so everything beyond that was missing.
key_points: Upgrading dependencies in a chain (Rust → rust-core → build system) can consume an entire day just to get back to where you started
GDB proved invaluable for OS debugging by allowing memory inspection, searching for strings, and comparing core dumps against the original binary
The bug that seemed mysterious and complex had a mundane root cause: the bootloader's hardcoded size limit was too small for the growing binary
Having a debugging partner (Allison) who asks the right questions — like 'what debugging strategies do you have?' — can unstick you faster than working alone
money_quotes: SURPRISE MY CODE IS NOT WORKING BECAUSE SOMETHING IS ERASING IT.
Half of my binary gets overwritten with 0s at runtime. Why. What did I do to deserve this?
It is a wonder that this code even runs, man. Man.
Allison asks: "What linker debugging strategies do you have?"
Change the linker script randomly (actual thing that has worked)
I found why my binary has lots of 0s in it at runtime. It is because I was only loading 12k of it in loader.asm.
posts.key_insight: The most baffling bugs often have the most mundane root causes — in this case, days of mysterious runtime corruption traced back to a hardcoded size constant in a bootloader.

posts.summary: Julia describes a day spent debugging her OS's interrupt descriptor table by switching the implementation from assembly to Rust. She found two bugs—an operator precedence error and a wrong function name—by using GDB to compare memory contents between working and non-working code. Despite taking all day, the debugging felt manageable thanks to GDB's memory examination tools. The fix lets her set up interrupt handlers in Rust, moving her closer to being able to run programs on her OS.
key_points: Operator precedence bugs (like `1 << 16 - 1` vs `(1 << 16) - 1`) can be subtle and hard to spot without examining actual memory contents
GDB's memory examination commands (`x/`) make it straightforward to compare expected vs actual memory layouts for debugging low-level code
Replacing assembly with a higher-level language like Rust reduces liability when you don't fully understand the lower-level language
Debugging can feel manageable even when it takes all day, as long as you have the right tools and a systematic approach
money_quotes: This actually ended up being really easy to debug! "Really easy" as in "it took all day, but I did not feel like hiding under the table at any point".
Comparing sections of memory and figuring out why they're wrong is tedious, but pretty straightforward – I had a good handle on what all my code was doing.
I don't really know assembly and the less of it I have in my OS, the less of a liability it is.
gdb isn't totally the best interface – I can certainly imagine having better ones. But it is Very Useful.
These are pretty awesome superpowers already, and I'm sure there are tons more.
posts.key_insight: GDB's ability to examine and compare raw memory contents makes debugging low-level OS code systematic rather than terrifying, even when bugs take all day to find.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her progress writing a kernel in Rust, specifically getting to the point where she can parse ELF headers and execute simple programs. After receiving help from Graydon Hoare (Rust's creator), she got a minimal 897-byte test program to work with instead of an 800k Hello World. She walks through the steps needed to load and run an ELF executable: reading headers, finding segments, copying them into memory, and jumping to the entry point. The post shows her actual Rust code for parsing ELF headers and executing programs, noting the heavy use of unsafe code.
key_points: A statically compiled Hello World is 800k, but a minimal program using inline assembly for a single syscall compiles to just 897 bytes — about 1000x smaller
Running an ELF executable requires reading the header, finding loadable segments, copying them to the right memory address, and jumping to the entry point
Writing OS-level Rust code requires extensive use of unsafe blocks, since operations like casting raw pointers and jumping to arbitrary addresses can't be verified by the compiler
Community engagement through blogging led directly to expert help — Graydon Hoare emailed answers to her questions after reading her previous post
money_quotes: I wanted to be able to run executables because THAT WOULD BE COOL.
One of my favourite things about this blog is when delightful people comment or email and answer my questions
This is super great, because it means that I can understand the whole program and it doesn't have a bunch of glibc/Linux stuff compiled into it.
I could also make up my own convention for system calls, but that seems unnecessary.
This is typical of a lot of Rust code I'm writing – I need to write a lot of `unsafe` code.
What could be more unsafe than jumping to a possibly arbitrary address in memory?
posts.key_insight: Running executables from a hobby kernel boils down to parsing ELF headers and jumping to the right memory address, and working with a minimal test program makes each step understandable.

posts.summary: Julia reflects on her last day at Hacker School (now Recurse Center), expressing sadness about leaving and gratitude for the community. She shares a conversation with Sumana Harihareswara about continuing to blog after Hacker School ends. The exchange captures the 'never graduate' ethos — that learning and creating don't stop when a program ends. She resolves to keep blogging, coding, and learning beyond the structured environment.
key_points: The end of Hacker School brings mixed emotions — sadness about leaving but excitement about returning home
A conversation with Sumana reframes the anxiety of 'what will I write about' into a commitment to keep going
The 'never graduate' philosophy means learning and creating are lifelong pursuits, not bounded by programs
Community and relationships formed during intensive learning experiences are deeply valued
money_quotes: I WILL WRITE 4000 BLOG POSTS TOO.
Oh, but what will I write about when Hacker School is over? I will have nothing to say!
Never graduate!
So hopefully I'll keep blogging and seeing all the friends I've made here. And coding. And learning.
brb hugging all the people
posts.key_insight: The end of a structured learning program isn't the end of learning — the 'never graduate' mindset turns a bittersweet goodbye into a commitment to lifelong curiosity.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her pandas-cookbook, a GitHub repository of worked examples for learning the Python pandas library. The cookbook grew out of slides from her talks that people found helpful but which kept going out of date. Each chapter uses real datasets to teach pandas concepts progressively, from reading CSVs to cleaning messy data and parsing timestamps. The approach emphasizes learning by doing with real-world data rather than abstract documentation.
key_points: Talk slides are useful for getting started but go stale quickly, motivating a more maintainable cookbook format
The cookbook teaches pandas through real datasets and concrete questions rather than abstract API documentation
Topics progress from basics (reading CSVs, selecting data) to practical skills (groupby, string operations, cleaning messy data, timestamps)
money_quotes: A lot of them are taken from talks I've given, but I also want to give some new examples, like how to deal with timestamps, what is a pivot table and why would you ever want one?
It's along the same lines as the pandas talks I've given – take a real dataset or three, play around with it, and learn how to use pandas along the way.
posts.key_insight: The best way to learn a data tool like pandas is through worked examples on real datasets that answer concrete questions, not through abstract documentation.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores how the killall command works internally by tracing its system calls with strace. She discovers that killall performs an exhaustive search through every PID in /proc, reading each process's stat file to check its name against the target. When it finds a match, it sends the appropriate signal (SIGTERM by default, SIGKILL with -9). The post also introduces ltrace for seeing library-level calls like strcmp, and mentions attaching strace to running processes.
key_points: killall works by iterating through every PID directory in /proc and reading each process's /proc/$PID/stat file to find matching process names
strace captures system calls (the kernel interface) while ltrace captures library calls, showing different levels of abstraction for the same operation
The string comparison to match process names happens in userspace and is invisible to strace, but visible with ltrace
You can attach strace or ptrace to already-running processes to observe their behavior in real time
money_quotes: What strace does is capture every single system call that gets called when executing a program.

System calls are the interface between userspace programs and the kernel, so looking at the output from strace is a fun way to understand how Linux works, and what's really involved in running a program.
I never thought of killall as having to do an exhaustive search through all PIDs before, but it makes sense.
The kernel isn't involved in seeing whether or not the process has the right name, so we don't see that in the strace output.
SIGTERM isn't a very serious killing-y signal – it's signal 15, and processes can ignore it or save their state before they stop.
posts.key_insight: Using strace to trace system calls reveals that killall performs a brute-force search through every process in /proc, showing how simple tools can teach you about Linux internals.

posts.summary: Julia Evans conducted an informal survey of about 2,300 people to investigate whether 'guys' is perceived as a gender-neutral term, after a disagreement where different friend groups had opposing views. She found a significant gender split in perception: about 50% of men considered terms like 'Java guys' gender neutral, while only 25% of women did. She acknowledges the survey's limitations including selection bias and framing issues, but considers it a successful way to generate productive, non-heated discussion about language use.
key_points: Men and women perceive the gender-neutrality of 'guys' very differently, with men twice as likely to consider it neutral
Running a survey turned an anecdotal disagreement into a data-driven conversation that engaged 2,300 people
The survey format helped people examine their own assumptions—some were surprised by their own results
Evans openly acknowledges methodological limitations including selection bias and biased framing
money_quotes: This person said 'my friends totally think it is!', and I verified with my friends, who totally said it wasn't. Not the best grounds for a discussion.
About 50% of men (+ other) think that 'Java guys' is gender neutral, while only 25% of women do.
I think presenting this as a survey was a great way to get people to engage in a discussion in a mostly non-heated way and think about the way they use language.
Several people told me that they thought that 'guys' was a totally gender-neutral term and were surprised by their results when they took the survey.
This really isn't meant to be a scientific study or anything – think of it as informally polling a few of your friends, except if you had a lot of friends.
posts.key_insight: Whether 'guys' feels gender-neutral depends heavily on who you ask—men are roughly twice as likely as women to perceive it as neutral, revealing a blind spot that a simple survey can surface.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a collection of unconventional coding strategies, presented as a series of tweets and brief reflections. The strategies emphasize deliberate practice techniques like writing code and then deleting it, writing the README before the code, and coding without internet access. She also includes contributions from Erik Åsheim, who adds strategies around self-conscious interrogation of decisions and physical exercise as a coding tool. The post frames programming improvement as something that comes from intentional constraints and varied approaches rather than just writing more code.
key_points: Deliberate constraints like time limits and forced deletion can improve coding skills
Writing documentation or tests before code helps clarify thinking and design
Explaining code to others (programmers or not) is a valuable development practice
Avoiding dogma and trying varied approaches leads to growth
Physical and mental breaks from coding are part of the process
money_quotes: One of the hardest and most memorable coding exercises that I've done was to spend 30 minutes writing code that I really thought was good, and then delete it.
Coding strategies: write code for 25 minutes, then git reset --hard no matter what happens
Coding strategies: pretend you have the code already, and write a program using it
Coding strategies: code without using the internet for an hour
Coding strategies: code for 30 minutes without thinking about whether it's good or not
To sum up my coding strategies: avoid dogma, question conventional wisdom, try new things, be idealistic, improve, and have fun.
Coding strategies: ride a bicycle until you are out-of-breath on the other side of town.
posts.key_insight: Imposing deliberate constraints and varied exercises on your coding practice builds skills more effectively than just writing code the usual way.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her comprehensive list of questions to ask when interviewing at companies, organized into categories like engineering practices, management style, quality of life, and culture. She advocates spending 20 minutes before each interview selecting relevant questions from the list. The post emphasizes that asking many questions signals self-respect and careful decision-making. She highlights the importance of 'statistical' questions that reveal actual company behavior rather than stated policies, like asking what time people leave rather than whether there are set hours.
key_points: Asking many thoughtful questions in interviews shows you value yourself and make careful decisions, not that you're being difficult
Statistical questions that probe actual behavior (what time people leave, how much vacation people actually take) reveal more than policy questions
Retention and diversity questions should be specific and measurable, like asking about women's retention rate over 1.5 years
Questions about how failure and disagreement are handled reveal more about culture than questions about success
The list covers often-neglected areas like financial transparency, ethical concerns, and whether catered suppers are a red flag for overwork
money_quotes: Asking a lot of questions shows that you value yourself and that you're careful when making decisions. It's a good thing.
A company may say that they 'don't have hours', but if everyone leaves at 9pm that's not a good sign.
If there's 'unlimited' vacation, how much vacation do people normally take?
Has there been a situation where someone raised an ethical concern? If so, how was it handled? If not, have there really not been any?
When something goes wrong, how do you handle it? Do devs get shamed for breaking the build?
What's your retention rate of women over 1.5 years? Do you think you could have done anything differently to keep people who left?
I often explain to my parents or non-technical friends companies' business models to test if they really make sense.
posts.key_insight: The best interview questions probe actual company behavior through statistical and situational evidence rather than accepting stated policies at face value.

posts.summary: Evans describes the kernel she wrote at Hacker School by listing everything it can't do, using that as a framework for explaining what kernels actually handle. Her kernel can only print to the screen, understand keyboard inputs, and almost run programs. By enumerating missing capabilities like filesystem support, networking, scheduling, and memory protection, she makes the scope of kernel work concrete and approachable. She's preparing a conference talk arguing that kernel programming is something normal humans can do, and frames the learning process as picking a specific subsystem and diving in.
key_points: A toy kernel that can only print to screen and read keyboard input reveals how much work production kernels do
Listing what's missing (filesystems, TCP/IP, scheduling, memory protection) makes kernel concepts concrete rather than abstract
Understanding what a kernel does lets you pick any subsystem and dive into real kernel source code productively
Kernel programming has become concrete enough that she feels she could write a production OS given enough time and help
money_quotes: I now totally feel like I could write a production OS if I were given some hardware, 20 years and an army of volunteers.
The idea is that once you know what a kernel does, you can pick a Thing and a Kernel, and then dive into it.
These are all pretty approachable concepts (I think).
So not much :)
I'm working on a talk for CUSEC about how kernel programming is something that normal humans can do (albeit with some pain and suffering).
posts.key_insight: Building a minimal kernel that can barely do anything is a powerful way to demystify what production kernels actually handle, making the whole domain feel approachable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans pushes back against gatekeeping advice she received about learning kernel programming and offers four accessible paths for getting started. She was told that without a professional need, there's no reason to learn about the kernel, and that subscribing to LKML was the way in. Instead, she proposes writing your own OS (starting from existing projects), writing kernel modules, doing a Linux kernel internship through outreach programs, and reading kernel source code with cross-referencing tools. Each path is presented as genuinely fun and achievable without deep expertise.
key_points: Traditional advice about kernel learning is gatekeeping and unhelpful — telling people to subscribe to LKML or only work on mainline code discourages beginners
Writing your own OS is less scary than it sounds when you start from something that already works and make small incremental changes
Kernel modules can be as simple as 18 lines of code, making them an accessible entry point to kernel development
Reading kernel source code is surprisingly approachable — you won't understand everything, but tools like LXR make it fun to explore familiar system calls like chmod
Outreach programs like OPW provide structured paths into kernel development with mentorship and real patch acceptance
money_quotes: If you don't *need* to understand the kernel for your work, why would you try?
This was really, really, really not helpful to me.
Remember that your operating system doesn't have to be big and professional – if you make it turn the screen purple instead of red and then maybe make it print it a limerick, you've already won.
I don't really understand what the `__init` AND `__exit` macros do, but I can use them!
I felt kind of dumb for not understanding things, but then every single person I talked to was like 'yeah, it's the Linux kernel, of course!'
Most of the code I read I find pretty opaque, but some of it (like this chmod code) is a little bit understandable.
posts.key_insight: The Linux kernel isn't scary if you ignore the gatekeepers and find your own fun, incremental path in — whether that's turning a screen purple, writing an 18-line module, or reading chmod's source code.

posts.summary: Julia Evans makes the case that more people should give conference talks, drawing on her early experiences speaking at PyCon Canada, PyData NYC, and local meetups. She addresses the fear of public speaking by referencing Hilary Mason's argument that talks are actually efficient for introverts since you introduce yourself to everyone at once. She counters perfectionism by suggesting you compare yourself to mediocre talks rather than the best ones. The core preparation advice, drawn from the Serious Pony blog, is that audiences care about becoming more awesome themselves, not about your polish or presentation skills.
key_points: For introverts, public speaking is efficient because you introduce yourself to everyone at once instead of one-by-one
Comparing yourself to mediocre talks you've seen is more motivating than comparing yourself to the best talks
Audiences don't care about you or your polish — they care about what they can take away and use
Conference proposals should frame the talk as 'how will this make attendees better' rather than 'why is this a good talk'
money_quotes: The first order of business when preparing a talk is to convince yourself that public speaking is a good idea and better than hiding under the bed.
If you're an introvert, public speaking is an excellent idea because introducing yourself is a lot of work. If you give a talk, you only have to introduce yourself to everyone once.
I often worry that my talks won't be as well-put-together and inspiring as the best talks I've ever seen. And they probably won't be!
Nobody has ever said anything mean, which I take to mean that I am amazing and can do no wrong.
People don't care about you, they care about themselves.
All that matters is that you get across information that makes the people you're talking to more awesome.
I try to explain not 'why is this a good talk?', but 'if you come to this talk, how will it make you better?'
posts.key_insight: The secret to good talks isn't polish or presentation skills — it's giving your audience something they can actually use to become better at what they do.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience from 15-30 technical interviews during a job search, breaking down the types of questions she encountered. She categorizes them into background questions and technical questions, offering practical advice on how to approach each. She notes that the industry seems to be moving away from brainteaser questions toward more practical coding and system design questions. She finds that having unusual technologies on her resume sparks interesting conversations, and that interview preparation doubles as useful self-reflection.
key_points: Background questions like 'tell me about yourself' are actually a gift because they let you control the narrative of your career
The tech industry appears to be replacing brainteaser questions with more practical questions about estimating program runtime and space requirements
Having unusual or niche technologies on your resume (like Rust or Julia) sparks good conversations and makes interviews more interesting
Preparing answers to 'what are you looking for in a job' is valuable self-reflection beyond just interview prep
Most interviews focused on practical coding, discussing past projects, and system design rather than whiteboard tricks
money_quotes: In the last month I've done what feels like 100 interviews. It's easier than I thought it would be.
I was originally pretty intimidated by all these questions, but I think they're actually a gift (if initially a difficult gift).
I've been asked exactly zero tricky math questions or questions like 'How many bingo balls could fit in the Empire State Building'. Hopefully the tech industry has gotten over these.

I think people are replacing 'how many golf balls can fit in the Empire State Building' with more concrete questions about estimating program runtime and space requirements. I am very happy about this.
One takeaway from all this is that having weird things like Rust and Julia on my resume has been a fun time – people ask about Rust a lot, and I get to have good conversations about it.
I'm getting much better at talking about myself to strangers on the phone, which is an awesome thing to be able to practice.
posts.key_insight: Technical interviews are less intimidating than expected, and the industry is shifting toward practical, project-based questions that reward genuine curiosity and diverse experience over rote problem-solving tricks.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience attending and speaking at CUSEC, an undergraduate software engineering conference. She highlights favorite talks on contributing to Firefox, functional programming, and probabilistic data structures. She describes organizing CUSECLadies, an informal meetup connecting women students with experienced women in tech. She also shares her excitement about giving her 'You can be a kernel hacker!' talk and a useful presentation technique of graying out irrelevant code.
key_points: Conference talks that make intimidating topics accessible — Firefox contribution, functional programming, HyperLogLog — were the standouts
An impromptu women's meetup at the conference covered salary negotiation, staying in tech, and career advice, showing the value of informal mentorship
Graying out irrelevant parts of code samples reduces cognitive load and makes technical content less intimidating for audiences
money_quotes: She pointed out that people who write imperative programs spend a lot of time learning how to read for loops, and learning how to read map is similar.
A whole bunch of delightful people came up to me after and told me that they now feel like they know what a kernel is and how to get started with kernel programming!
I found that this really helped with the cognitive load of code samples and made showing kernel code much less intimidating.
It was totally unplanned, but I think/hope people got something out of it.

We talked about salary negotiation, whether or not women stay in tech, how we all got into programming, people's experiences in school, whether we identify as feminists, advice for talking to companies at job fairs, the role of women-only groups, and a whole bunch of other things.
posts.key_insight: Making technical topics less intimidating — whether through talk design, community building, or simple presentation tricks like graying out irrelevant code — is what turns curiosity into confidence.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her struggle with underselling herself in technical interviews and offers practical strategies for sounding more confident. She identifies a common pattern of defaulting to 'I don't know anything' when she actually has substantial knowledge. Her three strategies are: stating factual experience instead of hedging, treating interview questions as genuine knowledge-sharing opportunities, and leading with what you do know rather than what you don't. The post is a concise, personal reflection on interview communication.
key_points: Many technically skilled people default to underselling themselves by equating 'not expert-level' with 'knowing nothing'
Stating factual experience (years of use, specific projects) is more helpful to interviewers than vague self-assessments
Answering as if the interviewer genuinely wants to learn reframes the dynamic from evaluation to explanation
Leading with what you know instead of what you don't turns partial knowledge into a positive signal
money_quotes: I have been known to answer questions like 'do you know statistics?' with 'no, I don't know anything about statistics', followed by an eventual admission of 'well except that I have a math degree'.
I struggle with underselling myself, and I forget sometimes that 'I don't have a PhD in this' isn't the same as 'I don't know anything'.
Instead now I say 'I've been using it every day for 10 years.', which is not an exaggeration, and doesn't undersell my experience.

I find saying how long I've been using a technology and talking briefly about what kinds of projects I've done with it easier (and more helpful to the interviewer) than giving any kind of value judgment on my experience.
Pretend that they don't know the answer, but that they're really really smart.
posts.key_insight: Replacing vague self-deprecation with factual statements about your experience is a simple but powerful way to communicate confidence in interviews.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a beginner-friendly introduction to GDB, the C debugger, motivated by her experience debugging her own operating system. She walks through three fundamental GDB operations: setting breakpoints, viewing disassembled code, and examining memory contents. The post uses a minimal C program to demonstrate each concept with concrete examples. She emphasizes that just knowing breakpoints and memory examination got her surprisingly far in real debugging work.
key_points: GDB can be learned incrementally - just three commands (break, disassemble, x) provide significant debugging capability
Setting breakpoints and using 'where' to inspect the call stack are the most basic but essential GDB operations
The 'examine' (x) command lets you inspect raw memory with different format specifiers like strings or individual characters
Compiling with -g flag is necessary to get useful debugging symbols in GDB
money_quotes: Debugging C programs used to scare me a lot.
The main thing I used gdb for when I was debugging my kernel was to examine regions of memory to make sure they were what I thought they were.
I know that gdb does lots of other stuff, but I still don't know it very well and x and break got me pretty far.
posts.key_insight: You don't need to master GDB to get real value from it - just learning breakpoints and memory examination can carry you surprisingly far in debugging C programs.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a curated list of blogs she enjoys reading, spanning topics from distributed systems and Postgres to game development and finance. She emphasizes her deliberate strategy of reading outside her comfort zone to expand her knowledge. The list highlights mostly women and underrepresented voices in tech, covering both technical and non-technical subjects. Each recommendation comes with specific post suggestions and genuine enthusiasm for what she's learned from each writer.
key_points: Deliberately reading blogs outside her area of expertise to broaden her knowledge
Highlights a diverse range of voices including many women in tech writing about systems programming, databases, data science, and community management
Values blogs that mix technical depth with personal reflection, like Camille Fournier's transition from systems programming to engineering management
Reads non-technical blogs (marketing, finance, game development) because insights often transfer across domains
money_quotes: I'm trying to read more blogs about things I don't know much about yet, to expandify my mind.
I basically want to be her when I grow up.
I have almost no experience with this stuff, and I've learned a ton from just following her on Twitter.
I don't understand everything, but I understand 10x more about her research than I would if I didn't read her blog.
This is on my list of 'things I read that don't really use right now but I am reading them just in case!'
posts.key_insight: Deliberately reading blogs outside your expertise — even when you don't fully understand them — is a powerful way to expand your thinking and discover unexpected connections.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her three-month experience at Hacker School (now Recurse Center), describing it as one of the best experiences of her life. She highlights the welcoming, positive atmosphere where nobody is told they can't do something. The environment's energy was so strong that her worry about lacking motivation proved laughably wrong. She credits the carefully chosen residents, skilled facilitators, and curated admissions process for creating a community of curious, friendly people. The piece serves as both a personal reflection and an enthusiastic recommendation for others to apply.
key_points: Hacker School's culture of 'yes, and!' meant nobody was told they couldn't do something because they didn't know enough, creating a fearless learning environment
The facilitators excelled not through domain expertise alone but through relentless curiosity, egolessness, and the ability to diagnose why someone doesn't understand something
Pairing was a powerful learning mechanism because it forced focus and enabled accidental learning from watching how others think and code
The admissions process was critical to the experience—curating for curiosity and friendliness created the community that made everything else work
The environment generated so much energy and ideas that the challenge was saying no, not finding motivation
money_quotes: The attitude was 'Oh, is something not working? How delightful! A learning opportunity!'.
I don't remember anyone ever being told that they couldn't do something because they didn't know enough.
I did more interesting projects in the 3 months of Hacker School than I did in the rest of my life combined.
They're very quick to admit if they're wrong and care about understanding, not about being right.
They have tons of practice at understanding why you don't understand.
She'd ask me super helpful leading questions and we'd fix my bugs! They are magicians.
Always keep learning, keep being curious, keep building.
posts.key_insight: The best learning environments are built not from curriculum or credentials but from a deliberately curated culture of curiosity, positivity, and mutual respect.

posts.summary: Julia Evans demonstrates how strace can be used to spy on SSH passwords by attaching to the sshd process and reading system call arguments. She walks through the process of finding the child sshd process, attaching strace to it, and observing the password appear in plaintext in the read() system call output. The key insight is that strace doesn't just show which system calls are running—it shows their arguments, including sensitive data like passwords. She notes this requires root access so it's not a vulnerability per se, but it illustrates how thoroughly compromised a machine becomes once root is obtained.
key_points: strace reveals not just which system calls a process makes, but also the arguments passed to them, which can include sensitive data like passwords
By attaching strace to the sshd child process handling an incoming connection, you can see SSH passwords in plaintext in the read() syscall output
This requires root access, so it's not a new vulnerability—but it demonstrates the depth of compromise that root access enables
When sshd receives a connection it forks child processes, and the password is passed from the child to the main process via a readable system call
Root access means far more than just reading sensitive files—it means real-time surveillance of any running process
money_quotes: Hey! If you can look at the system calls with strace and the library calls with ltrace, can you spy on people's ssh passwords?!
The reason this is possible is that strace doesn't just tell you which system calls a given program is running. It also tells you what the arguments are!
So if a program ever calls a function with a password the odds are pretty good that you can find out the password this way.
This just means that if your machine is already compromised, it's really, really, compromised.
But you can also spy on running processes and learn basically anything you want around them!
So if the NSA has root on your server, it can easily find out everyone's password who logs in via SSH. Whoa.
posts.key_insight: strace exposes not just system call names but their full arguments, meaning any process handling sensitive data like passwords can be trivially surveilled by anyone with root access.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes using strace to quickly figure out what SSH command an internal Ruby tool was running, instead of reading through the Ruby source code. By running strace with the -f flag to follow child processes and filtering for execve system calls, she and a colleague were able to see the exact SSH command being executed. The post advocates for using system-level debugging tools as a faster alternative to reading unfamiliar code, and frames it as an example of how understanding system calls has practical everyday value.
key_points: strace with -f and -e trace=execve can reveal exactly what commands a program runs without reading its source code
Following child processes (-f flag) is essential when the program spawns subprocesses
Understanding system calls is practically useful even for non-kernel programmers
Learning from colleagues who know different things accelerates skill acquisition
money_quotes: The normal way I'd think about doing this is by, well, reading the code. But that takes time!
This is super fun because what I'd usually do is go read the code to try and figure out what it's doing, and reading code is hard! strace is easy!
Also it's a great example of incidental/accidental learning. I like working with people who know more (and different!) things than I do :)
posts.key_insight: System-level tools like strace can answer questions about program behavior faster than reading source code, making them practical debugging shortcuts for everyday programming.

posts.summary: Evans shares community suggestions for practical uses of strace, collected after her previous post on using strace to avoid reading Ruby code. The suggestions range from debugging running Ruby processes with strace/gdb/lsof, to finding shared library loading paths, analyzing git performance with strace -c, and using related tools like ltrace and system-wide syscall tracing. She highlights several external resources including blog posts and slides that demonstrate strace as a versatile debugging tool.
key_points: strace's 'open' system call tracking is invaluable for sandboxing and finding where programs load shared libraries
You can combine strace with gdb and lsof for open-heart surgery on running Ruby processes, though you risk killing the process
ltrace (library call tracing) and system-wide syscall tracing are useful complements to strace that many people overlook
strace -c provides useful performance profiling, such as analyzing git performance
money_quotes: There were so many excellent suggestions that I couldn't not share!
He remarks that some of the things are like open-heart surgery – you can go into a running Ruby process and execute code using gdb, but you might kill the process.
all I know is that I usually start with strace, get annoyed with it, then remember to use ltrace instead.
check out syscall tracing on Linux, it's like strace for the whole system, handy if you want to know which process is doing something.
I didn't know syscall tracing was a thing! This seems very worthy of investigation.
posts.key_insight: strace is just the starting point of a rich ecosystem of tracing tools — ltrace, system-wide syscall tracing, and dtruss — that together form a powerful debugging toolkit for understanding what programs are actually doing.

posts.summary: Julia Evans advocates for pair programming based on her experience at Hacker School and her new job at Stripe. She defines it simply as two people working at one computer together, and frames it as an extended version of something developers already do naturally—asking someone to look at code with them. She lists practical benefits including timeboxing, knowledge sharing, and making boring tasks enjoyable, while acknowledging that misaligned goals can cause sessions to go badly. Her core message is that even occasional, casual pairing produces better code and more learning.
key_points: Pair programming is simply an extended version of asking a colleague to look at code with you, not a radical methodology
Pairing has social benefits beyond code quality: it makes boring tasks fun, keeps you focused, and creates incidental learning opportunities
Misaligned goals between pairing partners are the main source of friction
You don't need to pair all day like some shops do—even occasional pairing sessions are valuable
money_quotes: Two humans sit down in front of the same computer, with one keyboard, and work on a Thing together.
Explaining your code to someone makes you understand it better.
Pairing on a boring task makes it way more fun.
Pairing is a good way to share knowledge. I get a lot of incidental learning out of pairing with people, seeing things they do that are new to me, and asking them questions.
It keeps you honest. You can't just stop coding and go look at cats when you're pairing.
I usually end up writing better code than I would have on my own, and I learn a lot.
posts.key_insight: Pair programming doesn't require a formal methodology or full-time commitment—even casual, timeboxed sessions produce better code, more learning, and more fun.

posts.summary: Julia Evans demonstrates how strace can solve shared library version conflicts by tracing which files a program actually opens. She was trying to install IRuby notebook but kept getting the wrong version of libzmq loaded despite upgrading it. By using strace to trace system calls and grepping for the library name, she discovered two different versions of libzmq were being loaded from different paths. Removing the outdated version fixed the problem.
key_points: strace reveals which shared libraries a program actually loads at runtime by showing open() system calls
Library version conflicts can be invisible without tracing tools because multiple copies can exist in different paths
Filtering strace output with grep (excluding ENOENT failures) quickly isolates the relevant library loads
When debugging feels impossible, dropping down to the system call level removes all ambiguity
money_quotes: It's official. I have a love affair with strace.
This doesn't sound so useful until you find out that it is useful FOR EVERYTHING.
strace is like an immersion blender. I use strace more than my immersion blender.
I will get strace to tell me which shared libraries are being loaded! strace will never lie to me.
AH HA. The first libzmq is the right version (libzmq.so.3), but the second one is all wrong! It is libzmq1 and it is a disaster and a disgrace.
posts.key_insight: When shared library conflicts defy normal debugging, strace reveals exactly which files a program loads at runtime, cutting through all the confusion about library paths and versions.

posts.summary: Evans argues that Hacker School's secret to productivity is a culture where everyone takes responsibility for helping each other. She breaks down why help-seeking is efficient (saving hours of frustration), why it's different from handholding, and why creating a psychologically safe environment for questions is essential. She challenges the notion that self-directed learners don't need help, arguing that support turns autonomous people into superheroes. The post reframes helping as core work rather than a distraction, and proposes that answering others' questions should replace typical procrastination habits.
key_points: Asking for help is an efficiency multiplier — a 30-minute answer that saves 6 hours is a responsible use of everyone's time
There's a crucial middle ground between handholding and throwing documentation at someone — open source culture often gets this wrong
Acting surprised at 'basic' questions creates a chilling effect that makes people ask fewer questions and get less done
Self-directed learners aren't people who don't need help — they're people who become dramatically more effective when help is available
Helping others should be treated as real work, not as a distraction from 'actual' work
money_quotes: You can always get help. Everyone takes responsibility for helping everyone else.
Note that this says 'teach them to fish', not 'give them a disassembled fishing rod and a manual and a map and tell them that it's all there'.
I think of it as my responsibility to ask questions like this.
If somebody asks you a basic question and you make fun of them or act super surprised that they don't already, they're going to ask less questions. And then they're going to get less stuff done.
The amazing support that was available turned self-directed and autonomous people into superheroes.
It's important to think of supporting people and answering questions as a core part of your work, not something tangential.
What if helping people was everyone's default procrastination method?
posts.key_insight: A culture where everyone takes responsibility for helping others doesn't create dependency — it turns already-capable people into superheroes by removing the friction that makes learning slow and painful.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience writing a tiny operating system in Rust, breaking it down into small incremental steps starting from copying an existing project called rustboot. She walks through turning the screen blue, writing I/O functions, handling keyboard interrupts, and implementing malloc, explaining the challenges at each stage. A key digression explains why you can't write a kernel in Python (because print ultimately calls the write system call, which is implemented in the kernel you're trying to write) but can in Rust using #[no_std] mode. The post emphasizes that even seemingly simple tasks like printing a string required days of learning about freestanding mode, Rust's type system, and rust-core.
key_points: Starting from an existing project (rustboot) and making a tiny change (red to blue) is psychologically important for building momentum when tackling something intimidating

You can't write a kernel in Python because all I/O ultimately depends on system calls implemented by the kernel you're trying to write — Rust's #[no_std] mode solves this by letting you opt out of the standard library entirely
Rust's language feature gating lets you selectively enable capabilities like memory allocation by implementing the underlying primitives yourself, which is powerful for OS development
Simple-looking code like iterating over a string's bytes required wrestling with Rust's type system, freestanding mode limitations, and rust-core's unfamiliar APIs
You can get surprisingly far without malloc — Rust automatically puts local variables on the stack and global variables are laid out at compile time
money_quotes: My experience of writing a kernel that it was like jumping in puddles: it's a lot of fun, and there are a lot of mishaps.
This sounds silly, but psychologically it's an important step! It forced me to look at the code and understand how it worked, and it was really exciting that it worked right away.
This code looks simple! It is a lie.
OH WAIT YOU DON'T HAVE A KERNEL YET. YOU ARE WRITING ONE.
Wrapping it in an unsafe block tells Rust 'okay, I checked and I promise this code is actually doing the right thing and won't blow anything up'.
It also needs implements of realloc and free, but I left those blank :)
The hardest parts of this were not writing the function, but getting the type right and understanding how Rust's language features can be turned on and off.
posts.key_insight: Writing an operating system becomes approachable when you break it into tiny steps and embrace the messiness of learning, starting from copying existing code and changing one color.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains why her toy Rust operating system will never be finished, and why that's perfectly fine. She distinguishes between programming projects meant to produce useful code versus projects meant for learning. Her OS project was firmly in the learning category, and by that measure it was a huge success—she learned about linkers, virtual memory, executables, system calls, and much more. She argues that the blog posts documenting what she learned are far more valuable than the code itself. She points to another developer's similar learning project as further evidence that the process and documentation matter more than the final product.
key_points: Programming projects can have different goals: producing useful code, learning something, or exploring a new concept
The blog posts documenting what you learned are often more valuable than the code you produced
Writing a toy OS taught her about linkers, virtual memory, executables, system calls, the x86 boot process, and interrupt handlers
Not finishing a project doesn't mean failure—success depends on what your goals were
money_quotes: The reason I wrote an operating system in Rust wasn't so that I could have an operating system written in Rust.
Any code that I write in 3 weeks is at best a duplication of someone else's work, and mimicking the state of the art 20 years ago.
So when people ask me why my code doesn't compile, it's because the code is basically a trivial output of the process.
The blog posts I wrote are much more important, because they talk about what I learned.
Not finishing your project doesn't mean it's not a success. It depends what your goals are for the project!
I hope to not ever finish learning.
posts.key_insight: A programming project's success should be measured against its actual goals—and for learning projects, the knowledge gained and documented matters far more than whether the code compiles or ships.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her discovery that /proc can be used to recover accidentally deleted files that are still held open by a process, via /proc/$pid/fd/. She explores file descriptors, showing how they're symbolic links that reveal where processes direct their stdin, stdout, and stderr. She demonstrates practical uses like tracking which file a backup program is currently processing, and shows that even standard tools like ps get their information from /proc. The post ends with a list of related topics to investigate further.
key_points: Deleted files can be recovered through /proc/$pid/fd/ if a process still has them open, because file descriptors maintain a reference to the underlying file
File descriptors 0, 1, and 2 are symbolic links to stdin, stdout, and stderr, letting you discover where any process redirects its output
Standard Unix tools like ps actually get their information by reading files in /proc, as revealed by strace
The /proc filesystem turns kernel internals into readable files, making process inspection accessible without special tools
money_quotes: If you accidentally delete a file that a process still has open, you can recover it with cat /proc/$pid/fd/$file_descriptor.
Suddenly it was like /proc was turned into a magical unicorn! I can use it to recover my files?!
I didn't know that! It turns out this is also a great way to find out where a process that you didn't start is redirecting its output.
So ps gets its information from /proc! Neat.
I thought they were trolling me, but then I clicked on it and it was actually useful!
I still feel like there are concrete uses for /proc/ that I don't know about, though. What are they?
posts.key_insight: The /proc filesystem exposes kernel internals as ordinary files, enabling powerful capabilities like recovering deleted files and inspecting any process's state with basic shell commands.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience with remote pair programming after two weeks of working remotely. She was pleasantly surprised to find that remote pairing works nearly as well as in-person pairing, contrary to her expectations. She describes her toolset including tmate for terminal sharing, Google Hangouts/Skype for communication, and screensharing as an alternative. The main pain point is internet connectivity issues disrupting flow, but when the technology works, she finds remote pairing just as productive as in-person pairing.
key_points: Remote pairing can feel just as productive as in-person pairing when the technology cooperates
tmate for terminal sharing and Google Hangouts/Skype for video are a practical remote pairing toolkit, though none are perfectly reliable
Screensharing is a good alternative because it's editor-agnostic and requires no special software, but retina display resolution mismatches cause problems
The biggest downside of remote pairing is that internet drops and tool freezes disrupt flow in a way that never happens in person
money_quotes: When I got a remote job, I thought it would be impossible to do pairing, or at the very least it would be a terrible experience.
I find that it feels just as productive than in-person pairing, which surprised me a lot.

The worst thing about remote pairing is that it's really disruptive to the flow of the pairing session when your internet connection keeps dropping or if one of your tools freezes, and that never happens with in-person pairing.
The bigger you make the text, the less you'll have trouble when the internet connection wavers.
If you're screensharing, be proactive about asking your partner to zoom in :)
posts.key_insight: Remote pair programming can be surprisingly effective and feel as productive as in-person pairing, with internet reliability being the main bottleneck rather than the remote format itself.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that simply telling beginners to 'ask if you have questions' at programming workshops is insufficient because people get stuck but won't speak up due to fear or self-blame. She shares specific tactics she uses as a workshop helper: actively circulating, repeatedly checking in with people, watching for confused faces, and responding to questions with genuine enthusiasm. The core message is that helpers need to be proactive rather than passive, going to the people rather than waiting to be flagged down.
key_points: People at workshops won't ask questions even when told to because they're scared or blame themselves for being stuck
Proactive circulation and repeated check-ins are far more effective than standing in the corner waiting to be flagged down
When everyone says they're fine, some of them aren't — asking follow-up questions draws out hidden problems
Responding to questions with genuine enthusiasm lowers the barrier for future questions
money_quotes: And it's not enough. Inevitably people will not ask because they're scared or they think it's their fault and then they'll get stuck.
If everybody's saying yes, some of them are not okay and just not telling you.
Keep asking the same people if they're okay over and over again. If you keep talking to them, they'll be much more likely to ask you when they do have a problem.
Hey I have a ques- GREAT. I LOVE QUESTIONS. WHAT IS YOUR QUESTION
That's definitely something we can solve! Oh that's actually a mistake in the directions! That's totally our fault!
It feels weird to ask the same people if they're doing okay over and over and over and over and over again, but I think it really does help.
This feels more effective and like a better use of my time as a helper than standing in the corner waiting for someone to flag me down.
posts.key_insight: Effective teaching help requires proactive outreach and repeated check-ins, not passive availability, because people who are stuck rarely ask for help on their own.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the question of how to grow from a developer into a senior engineer, prompted by conversations with friends and a Twitter discussion. She highlights John Allspaw's influential post 'On Being a Senior Engineer' as the best resource she found, emphasizing that seniority involves far more than technical skill. The post surfaces key non-technical qualities like empathy, seeking criticism, and raising up others. She also references @seriouspony's insight that deliberate practice matters more than volume, warning against the comfort of doing only what you already know.
key_points: Becoming a senior developer requires cultivating non-technical skills like empathy, handling criticism, and lifting up colleagues
Simply writing more code isn't enough to grow — what and how you practice matters far more than volume
It's easy to get stuck in a rut doing comfortable things rather than deliberately pushing into growth areas
John Allspaw's 'On Being a Senior Engineer' is highlighted as essential reading on engineering maturity
money_quotes: How does someone become a senior developer? How do I know what I should be practicing? What qualities should I be looking for in mentors?
Myth: to get better at x, just do a lot more of it (writing/skating/coding, etc.) What/how you "do" X matters far more than how much/often.
It's easy to get stuck in a rut and keep doing the things you're comfortable with. I'd like to not do that.
posts.key_insight: Growing as a developer is less about writing more code and more about cultivating non-technical skills like empathy, seeking criticism, and deliberately practicing outside your comfort zone.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps her first PyCon experience in 2014, where she gave a talk and was overwhelmed by the quality of the conference. She highlights roughly a dozen talks that stood out, spanning topics from computational biology and imposter syndrome to compiling Lisp to Python bytecode and analyzing rap lyrics with NLP. She notes that a third of talks were by women and emphasizes the practical, actionable nature of the best presentations. The post conveys genuine excitement about the Python community's inclusivity and intellectual energy.
key_points: PyCon 2014 had exceptional diversity, with one-third of talks given by women
The best talks offered concrete, practical advice and specific calls to action rather than abstract encouragement
The conference showcased Python's range from scientific computing and data analysis to creative applications like rap lyric analysis
Community and inclusivity were central themes, with talks on imposter syndrome, trans visibility, and CS education reform
money_quotes: 1/3 of the talks were by women! It was wonderful.
We need better advice than 'you shouldn't have imposter syndrome!', and this did well at that.
I have harder data problems than your tech job does! Come do a PhD with me and I'll pay you a fraction as much.
I never stop being impressed with people I meet at PyCon.
Conferences are so hard! I want to meet all the people and do all the things and be in 3 places at things.
posts.key_insight: The best tech conferences combine intellectual rigor with genuine inclusivity, offering concrete calls to action rather than empty encouragement.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that system calls provide a powerful debugging approach that doesn't require source code, a debugger, or print statements. By using tools like strace and ltrace, developers can observe exactly what any program is doing at the OS level — which files it opens, which commands it runs, and what data it writes. She walks through specific system calls (open, execve, write) and the /proc filesystem, showing practical examples with ssh, Chrome, and killall. Her core argument is that learning your operating system is a better investment than learning language-specific debuggers, since OS-level tools work across all languages and even on closed-source binaries.
key_points: System calls are the only way programs can interact with the OS, making them a universal and reliable debugging interface
strace and ltrace let you observe any program's behavior without source code access, by tracing system calls and library calls respectively
The /proc filesystem exposes every file a process has open, providing ground-truth information that 'won't lie to you'
Learning OS-level debugging tools is a better long-term investment than language-specific debuggers because you switch languages more often than operating systems
