These tools are most useful for I/O-related debugging (files, network, processes) rather than logical errors in algorithms
money_quotes: I can look at the internal behavior of closed-source programs.
System calls are the API for your computer, so you don't have to know how a network card works to send a HTTP request.
At first debugging this way is confusing, but once you're familiar with the tools it can actually be faster, because you don't have to worry about getting the wrong information!

You probably switch languages more often than you switch OSes. So, learning your OS in depth and then using it as a debugging tool is likely a better investment of your time than learning a language-specific debugging tool.
If you learn your operating system, you acquire superpowers. You can debug programs that are binary-only and closed source.
My favorite thing about these methods is that your OS won't lie to you. The only way to run a program is with the execve system call.
The only way for a program to open a file on Linux is with the open system call.
posts.key_insight: Learning to debug through OS-level tools like strace gives you universal, language-agnostic superpowers because system calls are the only interface between any program and the operating system.

posts.summary: Julia Evans pushes back against the vague guilt many developers feel about not contributing to open source. She uses Mel Chua's motivation framework of competence, autonomy, and relatedness to analyze why she does or doesn't contribute to specific projects. She argues that most open source projects fail the relatedness test—if you don't use it, don't want to learn about it, or don't believe in its mission, there's no reason to feel bad about not contributing. She shares examples of projects she has contributed to where motivation was genuine, and concludes that open source should only be pursued when it makes personal sense.
key_points: Motivation for open source requires competence, autonomy, and relatedness—autonomy is usually present, but relatedness and competence are often missing
Most projects fail the relatedness test: without personal use, learning interest, community connection, or mission alignment, there's no authentic motivation to contribute
The competence barrier (unfamiliar languages, complex codebases, unwelcoming communities) can make even desired contributions impractical
Successful contributions come from genuine personal motivation—wanting a feature, wanting to learn, or believing in the project's mission
Time spent not contributing to open source can be equally or more valuable when spent on family, learning, or other pursuits
money_quotes: For a long time I had a vague feeling that I *should* be contributing to open source more, and that it was in some way a Good Thing. And I felt a little bit guilty about not doing it enough.
Now if I'm going to contribute to a project, it needs to be something that I'm motivated to do.
Motivation is made up of competence (I know how to do this!), autonomy (I can make my own decisions!), and relatedness (I know why I'm doing this!).

Most open source projects fails relatedness – if I don't use it, don't want to learn about it right now, don't know anyone who works on it, and/or don't believe strongly in its mission, I probably don't want to contribute!
This is TOTALLY OKAY. There are tons of people who are contributing to those projects! They will be okay without me =)
One of my PRs got merged into IPython and it is SUPER COOL.
I sometimes hear people say 'you should write open source software!' like it's a universal good. Don't. Do it if it makes sense for you!
posts.key_insight: Contributing to open source should be driven by genuine personal motivation—competence, autonomy, and relatedness—not by vague guilt or a sense of universal obligation.

posts.summary: Evans recounts a debugging experience where she was stuck on a bug and frustrated by her inability to get more information through a debugger. After stepping away and thinking about it on her commute, she realized the error message she already had contained enough information to fix the problem. The post advocates for pausing to think before reaching for more tools or data.
key_points: Sometimes the information you already have is sufficient to solve a problem
Stepping away from a problem can provide the mental clarity needed to see a solution
The instinct to gather more data can be a distraction from actually thinking through what you already know
money_quotes: I realized I didn't need more information! What I had was enough, and I knew how to fix it.
I'd like to remember to step back and think more often and more quickly.
posts.key_insight: Before reaching for more tools or data, stop and think about whether you already have enough information to solve the problem.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores how fast her computer can process a 1GB file by writing progressively optimized C programs to sum all bytes. Starting with a simple implementation that runs in 0.6 seconds (after caching), she discovers that vector intrinsics (SIMD) can cut that to 0.25 seconds, while deliberately engineering cache misses makes it 40x slower. She uses perf to analyze where the remaining time is spent, finding roughly 32% on memory access and 68% on computation. The post demonstrates a hands-on, experimental approach to understanding hardware performance.
key_points: A simple C program can process a 1GB file in 0.6 seconds once it's cached in RAM, with SSD reads adding about 2 seconds on the first run
SIMD vector intrinsics doubled the program's speed from 0.6 to 0.25 seconds by doing multiple additions simultaneously
Deliberately engineering cache misses made the program 40x slower, demonstrating how much CPU caches matter for performance
Linux's perf tool can annotate assembly code with performance statistics to show where time is actually spent
The gap between dynamic languages and optimized C for raw data processing is enormous
money_quotes: So today I paired with Sasha Laundy, and we ran Serious Investigations into how fast my computer can process a 1GB file.
So our program now runs twice as fast, and we know a whole bunch of new words (SSE! SIMD! vector intrinsics!)
We've learned that cache misses can make your code 40 times slower.
I used perf, which is a totally magical performance measurement tool for Linux.
I'm also kind of amazed by how fast C is. I'm used to writing in dynamic programming languages, which definitely do not process 1GB files in 0.25 seconds.
posts.key_insight: By progressively optimizing a simple byte-summing program and deliberately breaking it, you can build concrete intuition about how CPU caches, SIMD instructions, and memory access patterns determine real-world performance.

posts.summary: Julia Evans introduces perf, a Linux profiling tool that goes beyond strace by revealing CPU-level performance details like instruction counts, cache misses, and branch predictions. She demonstrates three use cases: basic performance counters with perf stat, generating flame graphs to visualize where execution time is spent, and measuring L1 cache misses to confirm a hypothesis about why a program with bad memory access patterns was slow. The post builds on her previous exploration of why computers are fast, using perf to verify that a stride-based memory access pattern caused 60x more L1 cache misses. She references Brendan Gregg's perf resources as essential reading.
key_points: perf fills a gap that strace can't: it measures CPU instructions, cache misses, branch predictions, and other hardware performance counters
Flame graphs generated from perf output provide an intuitive visualization of where a program spends its execution time across the call stack
perf can empirically verify performance hypotheses — the stride-based program had 60x more L1 cache misses than the mmap version, confirming the suspected cause of slowness
Hardware performance counters make invisible bottlenecks visible, turning speculation about cache behavior into measurable data
money_quotes: strace only does system calls, and none of those things are system calls. So it can't tell you any of those things!
Basically perf periodically interrupts the program and finds out where in the stack it is.
Apparently stopping my program takes a long time! Neat!
So, uh, that's really bad. We now have 60 times more L1 cache misses, and also 3 times more hits.
There are still so many things I don't understand at all!
posts.key_insight: perf transforms performance debugging from guesswork into empirical measurement by exposing CPU-level details like cache misses and instruction counts that are invisible to higher-level tools like strace.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her hands-on exploration of HDFS internals by setting up an Amazon Elastic MapReduce cluster and using Spotify's snakebite Python library to understand how distributed file storage actually works. She walks through the process of discovering how files are split into blocks, how the namenode tracks block locations, and how data is read from data nodes. Along the way she uses strace to observe the actual network traffic, revealing the protocol buffer messages exchanged between client and cluster. The post captures the satisfaction of peeling back layers of abstraction to see raw Wikipedia text coming back from a distributed filesystem.
key_points: HDFS files are split into blocks, and the namenode serves as a directory telling clients which data nodes hold which blocks
Using strace to observe network calls reveals the actual protocol buffer messages sent between HDFS clients and the namenode/data nodes
Spotify's snakebite Python library provides a readable, hackable way to understand HDFS internals by reading its source code
Hadoop versioning is confusing — getting the right combination of AMI version, Hadoop version, and client library required significant trial and error
money_quotes: I knew how to navigate the filesystem from the command line, but not how to figure out how it works internally.
Hadoop versions are confusing.
We needed to edit snakebite/config.py to say 'fs.default.name' instead of 'fs.defaultFS'. Who knows. It worked.
One of the first things I did was use strace to find out what data actually gets sent over the wire when I call this function.
So we have two blocks! The two numBytes add up to the total size of the file! Cool!
AMAZING. We have conquered HDFS.
posts.key_insight: You can demystify distributed systems by using tools like strace and readable client libraries to observe what actually happens on the wire when you read a file.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes how anonymous talk review worked surprisingly well for !!Con, a conference she helped organize. Despite initial fears about not knowing speakers' identities, the process produced a diverse range of high-quality talks from both experienced and new speakers. The anonymization required significant effort from a dedicated team member who personally scrubbed all proposals. The result was a process where speakers felt confident they were judged on merit, and organizers couldn't give preferential treatment to famous speakers or friends.
key_points: Anonymous review required significant manual effort — one person anonymized all proposals and then recused himself from the review process entirely
The letter grade system (A-D) with advocate requirements created a structured way to winnow 168 proposals down to 24 accepted talks
Anonymous review prevented both positive bias (accepting talks from famous people or friends) and negative bias (judging on demographics rather than proposal quality)
Speakers specifically expressed relief at knowing they were selected on merit rather than identity characteristics
The organizers sent personalized rejection emails highlighting what they liked about each rejected proposal
money_quotes: I was honestly a bit scared by this – how were we going to make sure people were good at public speaking if we didn't even know who they were?! But it turned out my fears were totally unfounded.
It's a relief to know that I wasn't picked for gender, race, age, or anything like that.
It was impossible to accept less-interesting-sounding talks just because they were 'by someone famous'.
It was impossible to give preferential treatment to our friends! This was a bit sad, but it made the conference better.
Each of us assigned a proposal a letter grade (A-D), from 'I will fight for this talk', to 'I will fight for this talk to be rejected'.
None of this is to say that anonymous review is the best way to do talk review. But it worked very well for us.
posts.key_insight: Anonymous talk review, while labor-intensive, produces fairer outcomes by forcing organizers to judge proposals purely on merit and giving speakers confidence that their selection was earned.

posts.summary: Evans examines whether conferences should adopt anonymous review processes for talk submissions, weighing the benefits against limitations. She confirms that anonymous review reduces bias, citing both speaker testimonials and academic research showing systemic gender bias in non-blind reviewing. However, she notes it won't automatically improve diversity since it can't fix low application rates from underrepresented groups. She presents a nuanced debate about whether anonymous review helps or hurts new speakers, with arguments on both sides. Her conclusion is that anonymous review is worthwhile but should be combined with active outreach, mentorship for new speakers, and strong calls for proposals.
key_points: Anonymous review is demonstrably less biased — research shows women had to be 2.5 times more productive to be judged equal to men in non-blind review
Anonymous review alone won't improve diversity because it can't increase application rates from underrepresented groups — active outreach is needed
There's a real tension between anonymous review and supporting new speakers: anonymity may favor polished proposals, but it also weeds out mediocre proposals coasting on big names
Conference organizers should think carefully about what specific benefits they want from anonymous review rather than treating it as a universal solution
The eurucamp experience suggests newcomers don't actually write worse proposals — many seasoned speakers submit unspecific, recycled proposals that only fly on reputation
money_quotes: It's a relief to know that I wasn't picked for gender, race, age, or anything like that.
It gave me courage that I wasn't going up against big names.
To be judged as good as their male counter parts, female applicants had to be 2.5 times more productive.
You can't accept talks that don't exist!
We found that many proposals that are submitted to many conferences are unspecific and dull and would only fly by having a big name attached.
Anonymous CFPs are very good at weeding out copy-pasta.
Every year, we have at least one person we take huge bets on and get very good talks out of that.
Nepotism and accepting your friends' talks are really hard to fight against.
posts.key_insight: Anonymous review provably reduces bias and nepotism in conference talk selection, but it's not a silver bullet for diversity — it must be paired with active outreach and support for new speakers.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her first three months working remotely for Stripe from the east coast while her team is in San Francisco. She honestly catalogs the difficulties, including timezone challenges, missing synchronous communication, and not knowing coworkers beyond IRC names. She also identifies genuine upsides like flexibility, working in the sun, and learning to optimize for happiness. The post frames remote work as a tradeoff worth making rather than an ideal arrangement, with practical strategies for coping.
key_points: Timezone differences create both problems (no one to talk to early morning) and opportunities (uninterrupted focus time, handoff advantage)
Losing synchronous communication was harder than expected, especially for building technical consensus
Having other remote teammates is critical — being the only remote person would likely be unsustainable
Happiness while remote is measurable and optimizable, correlating directly with time spent talking to people
Reframing remote work as 'a cool possibility with ups and downs' rather than a loss makes a meaningful difference
money_quotes: I didn't realize how much I depended on synchronous communication (talking face-to-face!) to do things until it was taken away from me.
My happiness seems to be proportional to the amount of time I spend talking to people. This is something I can measure and optimize!
Thinking about working remote as 'a cool possibility with some ups and downs' instead of 'this enemy that means I HAVE TO SEE LESS PEOPLE OH NO' helps me be happy instead of grumpy.
A lot of people are just names on IRC to me. I do not know of any upside to this, or how to fix it.
If I were the only one it would probably be a disaster and I would be way more sad.
I changed my work computer's clock to be the time in San Francisco. This helps more than I expected.
posts.key_insight: Remote work is sustainable when you treat happiness as a measurable variable, have other remote teammates, and reframe the tradeoffs as choices rather than losses.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that asking questions, even about things you feel you 'should' already know, is one of the most powerful tools for learning. She shares how admitting she didn't understand what a Linux kernel does—despite using Linux for 10 years—led to deep learning and eventually giving talks on the topic. She highlights how even experts often don't know the answers to basic questions, citing Aphyr's discovery that RabbitMQ could lose 40% of data. The post advocates for saying 'I don't understand' openly, while being specific about what you do and don't know to avoid wasting anyone's time.
key_points: Asking questions instead of worrying about looking dumb unlocks actual learning
Even respected experts often don't know the answers to basic questions when you dig into specifics
Saying 'I don't understand' requires specifying exactly which part you don't understand to be effective
Being specific about your knowledge level prevents people from explaining things you already know
Understanding and learning are more important than feeling smart
money_quotes: I've started trying to ask questions any time I don't understand something, instead of worrying about whether people will think I'm dumb for not knowing it. This is magical, because it means I can then learn those things!
I'd been using Linux for 10 years, and I didn't really understand at all what the basic responsibilities of the Linux kernel were.
One surprising thing about asking questions is that when I start digging into a problem, people who I respect and who know a lot will sometimes not know the answers at all!
If you don't start asking questions about how RabbitMQ works from the beginning, then you'll never find that out.
Be skeptical! Don't believe what people say even if they're using fancy words!
If I don't tell them I don't understand, I'm never going to understand the damn thing.
Understanding and learning are more important than feeling smart.
posts.key_insight: Admitting what you don't know and asking questions openly—even about things you feel you should already know—is the fastest path to genuine understanding.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that real-world machine learning work is fundamentally different from Kaggle competitions. While Kaggle focuses on optimizing algorithms with clean data and pre-defined metrics, actual ML work involves understanding business problems, choosing appropriate metrics, collecting and cleaning messy data, deploying models to production, and monitoring ongoing performance. She shares her own experience of feeling demoralized by Kaggle leaderboards despite doing ML professionally, and encourages others not to equate Kaggle performance with ML competence.
key_points: Kaggle abstracts away most of the hard parts of real ML work: business understanding, data collection, cleaning, deployment, and monitoring
Data cleaning alone can take 80% of your time when reconciling multiple uncontrolled data sources
Understanding the business problem matters more than model quality — if you don't know how the model fits into business plans, accuracy is irrelevant
Production deployment introduces entirely new challenges, as Netflix demonstrated by never implementing their competition-winning model because it was too complicated
The choice of metric to optimize is itself a substantive decision that requires domain understanding, not just statistical convenience
money_quotes: I still don't think I could win a Kaggle competition. But I have a job where I do (among other things) machine learning! What gives?
I've spent time on projects where I didn't understand at all how the model was going to fit into business plans. If this is you, it doesn't matter how good your model is. At all.
Cleaning up data to the point where you can work with it is a huge amount of work. If you're trying to reconcile a lot of sources of data that you don't control, it can take 80% of your time.
Netflix didn't actually implement the model that won the Netflix competition because it was too complicated.
Getting historical weather data is one thing, but getting that same data in real time to predict flight delays right now is totally different.

So if you look at Kaggle leaderboards and think that you're bad at machine learning because you're not doing well, don't. It's a fun but artificial problem that doesn't reflect real machine learning work.
posts.key_insight: Real machine learning work is dominated by business understanding, data wrangling, and production engineering — the algorithm optimization that Kaggle tests is only a small slice of the job.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces that talk recordings and transcripts from !!Con have been posted online. She highlights the diverse range of topics covered at the conference, from EEG machines to dancing robots to hacking poetry. She credits Erty Seidel for doing the work of producing the talk recordings.
key_points: !!Con talk recordings and transcripts are now available online
The conference covered an eclectic mix of topics including EEG machines, type theory, dancing robots, and hacking poetry
Erty Seidel handled nearly all the work for producing the talk recordings
money_quotes: Go learn about EEG machines, how to stay in love with programming, type theory, dancing robots, hacking poetry, and more!
posts.key_insight: !!Con featured an unusually diverse and playful range of technical talks, reflecting a community that values curiosity and joy in programming.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her approach to Hacker School, a 12-week programming workshop, explaining how she deliberately ignored interesting-but-familiar topics to focus on things that felt impossible to her. Despite many appealing workshops in web development, machine learning, and new languages, she chose to pursue systems programming, networking, operating systems, and security—areas she knew nothing about. She emphasizes that saying no to interesting distractions and focusing on what scared her was key to her experience. She also highlights the value of learning from peers and creating what she wanted to exist.
key_points: She deliberately skipped workshops and topics she already understood or wasn't scared by, even when they seemed interesting
She focused on things that seemed impossible—like writing an operating system from scratch—because that's where the deepest learning happened
She learned to say no to pairing requests that didn't align with her roadmap, even for interesting projects
She discovered that if she wanted something to exist (like a kernel development workshop), she could just make it herself
She found enormous value in learning from peers who had deep knowledge in areas she was exploring
money_quotes: I wanted to work on things that seemed impossible to me, and writing an operating system seemed impossible.
I mostly did not go to these workshops. It turned out that I was interested in all those things, but more interested in learning: systems programming, networking, how to write an operating system.
I also learned that if I wanted something to exist, I could just make it.
This meant sometimes saying no to requests to pair on things that weren't on my roadmap, even if they seemed super interesting!
I don't feel right now like learning more programming languages is going to make me a better programmer.
Other people have different interests than me, and that's okay, and I can make Hacker School what I want it to be.
posts.key_insight: The most productive way to use unstructured learning time is to ignore interesting-but-familiar topics and focus relentlessly on things that feel impossible.

posts.summary: Evans tackles the common question of how to determine appropriate sample sizes for experiments by walking through coin-flipping simulations. She demonstrates that small samples (10 trials) produce wildly unreliable results, while large samples (10,000 trials) cluster tightly around the true probability. She introduces hypothesis testing and p-values through an accessible mustard-preference thought experiment, using computer simulation rather than formulas. The post connects these empirical observations to the central limit theorem and normal distribution, explaining why historical statisticians could work without computers.
key_points: Small sample sizes like 10 trials can produce dramatically misleading results (70% heads from a fair coin), making them unreliable for drawing conclusions
Hypothesis testing works by asking: if the null hypothesis were true, how unlikely would our observed data be? If very unlikely (p-value below 1-5%), reject the null hypothesis
Computer simulation can replace formal probability calculations, making statistics more intuitive and accessible
The central limit theorem explains why sample means follow a normal distribution, which historically enabled statisticians to work with lookup tables instead of computers
With 10,000 samples, results cluster tightly around the true value, making even small deviations (like 52% vs 50%) statistically meaningful
money_quotes: We are not fooled by small sample sizes and we ALREADY KNOW that we can't trust this information.
Your brother is arguing '7/10 seems like a lot! I like mustard! I totally believe this!'. You need to argue with him with MATH.
We could also calculate the exact probabilities, but this is pretty close so we won't.
it looks like a normal distribution, or 'bell curve'. That's not a coincidence! It's because of the central limit theorem! MATH IS AMAZING.
This also helps us understand how people could have calculated these probabilities back when we did not have computers but still needed to do statistics
I was also going to do a Bayesian analysis of this same data but I'm going to go biking instead.
posts.key_insight: Computer simulation makes hypothesis testing and sample size reasoning intuitive: instead of memorizing formulas, you can literally run the experiment thousands of times to see how likely your observed results would be under the null hypothesis.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her appearance on the Ruby Rogues podcast, where she discussed systems programming tools like strace for debugging everyday code, building an OS in Rust, and the value of asking questions to learn. She expresses pleasant surprise at how the conversation ranged well beyond Ruby into topics like Rust, statistics, and strace. She notes the podcast referenced 13 of her blog posts, and reflects on the fun of cross-pollinating ideas across language communities.
key_points: Systems programming tools like strace are useful for debugging regular application code, not just low-level systems work
Asking questions you think are 'stupid' is a powerful learning strategy
Technical conversations benefit from crossing community boundaries — a Ruby podcast can productively discuss Rust, statistics, and Linux tools
Evans's prolific blogging had generated enough material that 13 posts were referenced in a single podcast episode
money_quotes: We talked about using systems programming tools (like strace) to debug your regular pedestrian code.
How asking stupid questions is an amazing way to learn.
It is totally okay to spend a Ruby podcast discussing Rust, statistics, strace, and, well… not Ruby :)
posts.key_insight: Systems programming tools and a willingness to ask naive questions are valuable across all programming communities, not just in low-level work.

posts.summary: Julia Evans advocates for open-sourcing talk and workshop materials so others can reuse and build on them. Inspired by Sumana Harihareshwara releasing a talk under CC-BY, she shares how her own pandas cookbook was reused for a workshop in the Philippines. She encourages others to freely reuse her talk materials and points to Software Carpentry's MIT-licensed lessons as another example of this approach.
key_points: Open-sourcing talk materials lets more people benefit from the work that went into creating them
Real-world reuse is already happening — Evans' pandas cookbook was adapted for a workshop in the Philippines
Software Carpentry provides MIT-licensed lesson materials that accept pull requests, modeling collaborative educational content
money_quotes: After all, if you've put in a ton of work to put a talk or workshop together, it's wonderful if other people can benefit from that as much as possible.
And none of us have an unlimited amount of time to give talks.
Just attribute me, and maybe tell me about it because THAT WOULD BE COOL :)
posts.key_insight: Open-sourcing talk and workshop materials multiplies their impact far beyond what any single speaker could achieve on their own.

posts.summary: Julia Evans revisits her enthusiasm for pair programming by collecting and categorizing reasons people find it difficult. After soliciting responses on Twitter, she received about 160 thoughtful replies describing challenges including exhaustion, anxiety, partner compatibility issues, and cognitive style mismatches. She organizes the feedback into clear categories: mental drain, typing anxiety, partner dynamics, thinking styles, dominance problems, and the preference for pair debugging over pair programming. The post concludes that pair programming is a useful tool but not universally beneficial, and understanding its difficulties leads to better pairing sessions.
key_points: Pair programming is mentally exhausting for nearly everyone, even those who find it valuable, with most people unable to sustain it for more than a few hours
Anxiety about being watched while coding is extremely common and doesn't always improve with practice — some people's discomfort should be respected rather than pushed through
Pairing requires trust and respect between partners; without psychological safety, it becomes counterproductive
People have fundamentally different cognitive styles — some think by talking, others need silence, and pairing assumes the former
Many developers prefer pair debugging or pair learning over pair programming for code production, suggesting pairing works best for specific activities rather than as a blanket practice
money_quotes: Once you're pairing with an asshole, you might as well stop. There's no point.
I spend half my day staring into space letting solutions pop into my head. Hard to do that with a partner there.
It can be good to encourage someone to try something, but if someone's tried and it just makes them super-anxious, respect that!
Not only do i not understand by talking, but trying to talk through something before i think = more confused
It really has to be a safe space. Done among people who trust and respect one another. It also builds trust and respect.

My first pairing experience was with someone who was a much better programmer than me, and basically bulldozed through the problem and left me no room to contribute. This really undermined my confidence and was awful.
One good trick is to always pair on the less experienced person's project and/or let the newer person drive.
posts.key_insight: Pair programming is a powerful tool when used selectively, but it fails when people ignore the real challenges of exhaustion, anxiety, cognitive style differences, and power dynamics between partners.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her Hacker School project of writing a minimal TCP stack in Python using the scapy library, with the goal of fetching a webpage from Google. She walks through the TCP handshake, discovers that her kernel's existing TCP stack interferes by sending RST packets, and solves this with ARP spoofing to use a fake IP address. After getting it working, she finds that Python is too slow to send ACKs fast enough, causing Google's server to reset the connection. Despite only working 25% of the time, she considers the project more fun and instructive than writing a proper TCP stack in C.
key_points: Writing a TCP stack in userspace conflicts with the kernel's own TCP stack, which sends RST packets for connections it didn't initiate
ARP spoofing to claim a different IP address is a clever hack to bypass the kernel's interference with raw socket TCP
Python's performance is too slow for the millisecond-level timing TCP expects for ACK responses, causing servers to assume connection problems
The TCP spec is extremely complicated, but servers will respond to you even if your implementation is extremely sketchy
Building a broken version of something from scratch can be more instructive than building a correct version in the right language
money_quotes: I didn't care much about proper error handling or anything; I just wanted to get one webpage and declare victory :)
my kernel: lol wtf I never asked for this! RST!
This is when I remembered that I *already* have a TCP stack on my computer, in my kernel.
Google was sending me packets way faster than my program could keep up and send ACKs.
If you're actually writing a production TCP stack, don't use Python.
The TCP spec is really complicated, but you can get servers to reply to you even if your implementation is extremely sketchy.
I think this was actually way more fun and instructive than trying to write a TCP stack in an appropriate language like C :)
posts.key_insight: Building a deliberately impractical TCP stack in Python reveals how networking actually works — from kernel interference to timing constraints — in ways that reading specs or using proper tools never would.

posts.summary: Julia Evans demystifies binary executables by walking through the structure of a simple compiled C program on Linux. She explains the three key organizational concepts of ELF binaries: symbols (function names used for linking), sections (logical groupings like .text for code and .rodata for read-only data), and segments (runtime memory layout). Using tools like readelf, objdump, and nm, she shows how to inspect each layer, from raw bytes to disassembled instructions. The post emphasizes that executables are regular file formats that anyone can learn to read, not impenetrable magic.
key_points: ELF binaries are organized into three hierarchical layers: symbols enable linking by naming functions, sections group code and data at link time, and segments control memory layout at execution time
Programs don't start at main — they start at _start, which performs initialization before calling main
The compiler may silently optimize your code, such as replacing printf with puts when the format string is simple
Sections have different permissions: .text is read/execute, .rodata is read-only, and .data is read-write, enforced through segment mappings
Standard tools like readelf, objdump, and nm make binary inspection accessible without any special knowledge
money_quotes: I used to think that executables were totally impenetrable. I'd compile a C program, and then that was it! I had a Magical Binary Executable that I could no longer read.
It is not so! Executable file formats are regular file formats that you can understand.
When the program starts running, you might think it starts at main. It doesn't! It actually goes to _start.
Those are bytes that our CPU interprets as code! And runs! However we are not magicians (I don't know what 31 ed means!) and so we will use a disassembler instead.
Executables aren't magic. ELF is a file format like any other!
posts.key_insight: Binary executables aren't opaque magic — they're structured file formats with symbols, sections, and segments that anyone can inspect using standard Linux tools.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that kernel programming is not an exclusive domain for wizards — anyone can learn it and benefit from the knowledge. She shares how she discovered during Hacker School that understanding Linux internals, particularly system calls, made her a better programmer even for high-level work. The post presents practical tools like strace, /proc, ftrace, and perf as reasons to care about the kernel, then offers six concrete strategies for learning: stracing programs, reading kernel source, writing kernel modules, building a toy OS, doing the Eudyptula challenge, and internships. She emphasizes that the kernel is fundamentally just C code you interact with through system calls, and demystifying it is both fun and practically useful.
key_points: The Linux kernel is just C code that manages hardware interaction, and system calls are the API between your programs and the kernel
Tools like strace, /proc, ftrace, and perf give you powerful debugging superpowers that come from understanding the OS layer
Kernel modules are surprisingly approachable — they just need an init function and a cleanup function, and you can write fun ones like a rickroll hijacker
You don't need to be an expert to start: writing a toy OS, stracing random programs, or reading kernel source code are all valid entry points
Understanding your operating system makes you a better programmer even if you write Python or do web development
money_quotes: kernel programming is not just for wizards, it can also be for me!
A kernel is a bunch of code that knows how to interact with your hardware.
learning about the interface between your operating system and your programs will make you a better programmer.
strace is literally my favorite program in the universe.
I no longer think that they're wizards. I now think those are people who worked really hard on becoming better at kernel programming, and did it for a long time!
Kamal is pretty great, but he had never written kernel code before that weekend. I understand that he googled things like 'how to hijack system call table linux'. You could do the same!
writing a full-featured operating system from scratch is a ton of work. But the great thing about operating systems is that yours don't need to be full-featured!
posts.key_insight: The Linux kernel is not magic reserved for wizards — it's approachable C code, and understanding it through tools like strace and simple kernel modules will make you a better programmer at any level.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recounts her experience at Strange Loop 2014, where she gave a well-received talk on kernel hacking despite worrying it would be too entry-level. She reflects on her rapid growth from a nervous first lightning talk to speaking at a major conference. She highlights several standout talks, praises the conference's serious inclusivity efforts (implementing all 58 items on their inclusivity checklist), and critiques the static vs. dynamic typing debate for lacking empirical rigor.
key_points: Making technical talks accessible and entry-level can be more impactful than pitching to experts, since even conference attendees at top events have knowledge gaps
Strange Loop implemented all 58 items on an inclusivity checklist rather than cherry-picking, resulting in meaningfully better representation
The static vs. dynamic typing debate at the conference lacked empirical evidence and nuance, with advocates talking past each other
Personal growth trajectory from scared lightning talk speaker to packed-room conference favorite happened in just 18 months
A conference combining academics and industry practitioners should leverage that mix for more rigorous, evidence-based discussions
money_quotes: I always imagine cool developer conferences like Strange Loop as being full of wizard developers who know everything about programming.
It turned out tons of people there didn't know a lot about systems programming / how kernels work, and my choice to make the talk accessible made a lot of people happy.
So far I've given a bunch of talks and I've never regretted trying to make it easy to follow for as many people as possible.
I love the idea of taking a huge list of ideas to make a conference more inclusive and just doing every single one.
This class of argument really bothers me, because it doesn't really hold up in court by itself.

It basically seemed like there were talks by people who really liked static languages, and talks by people who really liked dynamic languages, and they were just talking past each other. I didn't learn anything new from these talks.
I'd love to see some analysis of case studies about organization that have made a big switch, by someone without an agenda to push.
posts.key_insight: Making technical content more accessible rather than more advanced is almost always the right choice, because even expert audiences have surprising knowledge gaps.

posts.summary: Julia Evans and Kamal explore how SQLite stores data by creating a test database and examining its binary file format. They discover that SQLite databases are divided into fixed-size pages, with the page size encoded in the file header. By downloading the SQLite source code and adding a print statement to the btree page-reading function, they can trace exactly which pages are accessed during queries. The experiment reveals how B-tree indexing works in practice: nearby rows share page traversal paths, while distant rows diverge at different levels of the tree. The post demonstrates learning database internals through hands-on experimentation rather than just reading documentation.
key_points: SQLite databases are split into fixed-size pages, with the page size stored in bytes 16-17 of the file header
Adding a single print statement to SQLite's btreePageFromDbPage function reveals the entire page traversal path for any query
B-tree indexes work by traversing interior nodes (containing only pointers) to reach leaf nodes (containing actual data), requiring only a few page reads instead of scanning the whole file
Rows that are close together in the index share the same traversal path and end up on the same leaf page
SQLite's 140,000-line codebase is well-commented and approachable enough to modify for learning purposes
money_quotes: My favorite first thing to do with binary files is to cat them.

I've always understood indexes in a pretty vague way – I know that they're 'some kind of tree', which lets you access data in O(log n), and in particular that databases use something called a btree. I still do not really know what a btree is.
There's about 140,000 lines of SQLite source code, which is a bit intimidating!
All page reads need to go through this function, so we can just add some logging to it :)
Those two rows (1 and 20) are in the same page, so it traverses the same path to get to both of them!
Modifying open source programs to print out debug information to understand their internals better: SO FUN.
posts.key_insight: You can demystify database internals by adding a single print statement to the source code and watching which pages get accessed during queries.

posts.summary: This second part of Julia Evans' SQLite series explains how btrees minimize disk reads when querying a database. She demonstrates that SQLite organizes data into btree structures where each node has many children, keeping tree depth small and reducing the number of page reads needed. Using her 100,000-row test database, she shows that a btree of depth 3 requires only 3 page reads versus 16 for a binary tree. She also reveals that tables and indexes each have their own separate btrees, and walks through a concrete query showing binary searches happening within each page.
key_points: Disk I/O is the dominant cost in database queries — seeking to new file locations is far more expensive than CPU time spent searching data already in memory
Btrees keep their depth small by giving each node many children, so a 100,000-row database needs only 3 page reads versus 16 for a binary tree
Each table has its own btree for data, and each index has a separate btree for lookups, which is why maintaining many indexes slows down inserts
SQLite performs binary search within each page to find the next node to visit, combining tree traversal with intra-page searching
A single query like 'select * from fun where id = 1000' actually triggers two separate btree searches: one in the index btree to find the rowid, then one in the table btree to fetch the data
money_quotes: One of the most important things in database optimization is disk I/O. Reading more data than you absolutely need to read is really expensive, because seeking to a new location in a file takes a long time.
It takes way less CPU time to search through your data than it does to read the data into memory.
btrees are organized so that each node has lots of children, to keep the depth small, and so that we won't have to read too many pages to find a row.
My 100,000 row SQLite database has a btree with depth 3, so to fetch a node I only need to read 3 pages. If I'd used a binary tree I would have needed to do log(100000) / log(2) = 16 seeks!
This is why maintaining lots of indexes is slow – every time you insert a row you need to update as many trees as you have indexes.
So for a simple database scan, reading more data than you need is a huge problem.
posts.key_insight: Btrees minimize expensive disk seeks by keeping tree depth small through high branching factors, turning what would be 16 page reads in a binary tree into just 3 for a 100,000-row database.

posts.summary: Julia Evans provides a quick-start guide for setting up a blog using Jekyll, Octopress, and GitHub Pages. She walks through the steps from installing Octopress to pushing a skeleton site to GitHub, emphasizing that the hardest part is getting Ruby working. The post's real message is that people overthink blog setup and design when the content is what actually matters. She admits the '5 minutes' claim is a lie but argues the low barrier to entry means there's no excuse not to start blogging.
key_points: Blog design doesn't matter nearly as much as having interesting content — most readers use RSS and never see the site directly
The biggest actual barrier to setting up a Jekyll blog is getting a working Ruby environment, not the blog itself
Anyone claiming a technical setup takes '5 minutes' is lying, but the process is still straightforward enough to do in under an hour
The recommended stack (Jekyll + Octopress + GitHub Pages) lets you write in Markdown, version with Git, and deploy with git push
money_quotes: I used to worry a lot about what my website looked like, and then I realized if I wrote interesting blog posts basically nobody cared!
All of my internal monologues about whether I should make it orange or not were for nothing. (the answer to orange is always yes, though).
If you have something to say, even if it is only one blog post's worth of things, you can totally set up a site!
At this point you have probably discovered that if anyone on the internet tells you something takes 5 minutes they are definitely lying.
I have spent months being confused about how to make Ruby work, so If you also need to set up Ruby it will take more than 5 minutes and this will be a total lie.
Not too much! Remember, you only have 5 minutes.
posts.key_insight: Stop worrying about how your blog looks and just start writing — interesting content matters far more than design.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her experience working remotely after 8 months, identifying feeling alienated as the biggest challenge. She connects this to self-determination theory, specifically the 'relatedness' component about feeling connected to others. She discovered that going 4 months without visiting the office was a mistake that led to negative feelings, and that in-person visits dramatically improved her wellbeing. She shares practical strategies like regular 1:1 meetings, frequent office visits, and daily routines to combat isolation.
key_points: The worst part of remote work is feeling alienated, which maps to the 'relatedness' need in self-determination theory — the need to feel connected to others
Going 4 months without visiting the office was a significant mistake that caused feelings of isolation and self-doubt
Timezone differences that initially felt hard became a non-issue over time, turning into an advantage for uninterrupted morning work
Regular 1:1 meetings without scheduled agendas help maintain human connection that remote workers miss
Finding what works in remote work is trial and error — prescribed routines from others may or may not help
money_quotes: The worst thing about working remotely so far has just been feeling generally alienated.

Turns out that if I don't see a group of people for way too long then it's easy to feel like nobody cares about me and everything I do is terrible, and it has all kinds of strange negative unforeseen consequences.
It's basically impossible to learn about how the office 'feels' while remote.
Jobs just get easier after doing them for a while. Now I know more things so lots of things are easier and better.
As with anything, finding things that work is just trial and error. Lots of error :)
posts.key_insight: Remote work's biggest challenge isn't productivity or communication tools — it's maintaining the human connection and sense of relatedness that comes naturally from being physically present with colleagues.

posts.summary: Evans shares results from a survey of 150+ women in technology, highlighting the wide range of experiences from deeply positive to deeply troubling. She emphasizes that women in tech are not a monolith — some feel valued and supported while others face harassment, ageism, and subtle career sabotage. The most popular reasons women cited for liking tech were enjoying the actual work and solving difficult problems. Evans presents extensive excerpts without editorializing, letting the diversity of voices speak for itself. She concludes with hope that workplaces can move toward making everyone feel valued, while acknowledging the heartbreaking gap between the best and worst experiences.
key_points: Women in tech have vastly different experiences — the survey reveals everything from enthusiastic satisfaction to exhaustion and discrimination, sometimes within the same workplace
The top reasons women love working in tech are the same as anyone's: enjoying the work itself and solving hard problems
Several respondents noted that companies differentiate themselves not by preventing all problems but by how they handle problems when they arise
Age compounds gender discrimination — multiple respondents over 40 described feeling increasingly vulnerable and sidelined
Some women expressed frustration that surveys about women in tech focus on negative experiences rather than the cool work they're actually doing
money_quotes: EVERYONE IS DIFFERENT AND PEOPLE THINK LOTS OF DIFFERENT THINGS
I love this industry and I doubt I'll ever leave it, but it has a long way to go in order for it to grow up.
I realized recently that feeling 'lucky' because I've only been sexually harassed by an executive once and denied access to a job once (that I know of) is insane.
What differentiates companies is how they handle it.
Where's the option to say I feel great, valued, treated equally, competent and successful?
I'm tired of people asking when I'm going to leave the field as if it's a given. I'm afraid that it is a given.
Over the years, it was so subtle, but while men were promoted, I was sidelined and told I didn't need additional leadership training.

A lot of the women younger than I are behaving as if the problems they're facing are new ones, and don't look to the past to see what works/doesn't work, thereby losing traction by repeating some of our mistakes!
posts.key_insight: Women in tech have enormously varied experiences, and acknowledging both the genuinely positive and genuinely terrible ones — without collapsing them into a single narrative — is essential to making progress.

posts.summary: Julia Evans previews a talk she's preparing for PyData NYC about the practical challenges of evaluating machine learning models over time. She argues that the hardest part of machine learning isn't training models with scikit-learn, but answering 'how well does it work?' She references Cathy O'Neil's critiques of supposedly objective models to emphasize that measurement itself is fraught. The post introduces ROC curves and confusion matrices as evaluation tools, but pivots to her real interest: tracking model performance historically and designing systems that let you compare results across months and tools.
key_points: Training ML models is easy with modern tools; evaluating whether they actually work is the hard part
You can't trust a model if you can't define and measure success, especially when the model affects people's lives
ROC curves show the tradeoffs between true positives and false positives, and are a practical evaluation tool
The deeper challenge is building systems to track model performance over time so you can tell if things are improving
Practical concerns like changing metrics, multiple tools, and ease of use make historical evaluation surprisingly hard
money_quotes: I often feel like the only thing I've ever learned about machine learning is how important it is to be able to answer this question, and how hard it is.
We should never trust a so-called 'objective mathematical model' when we can't even decide on a definition of success.

If your model is actually doing something important (deciding whether teachers should lose their jobs, or how risky a stock portfolio is, or what the weather will be tomorrow), you have to measure if it's working.
There's no fixed answer to how to do this – if it were easy, statisticians wouldn't have jobs.
I've seen a lot of 'training a model with scikit-learn for beginners' talks. They are not the talk I'm going to give.
But honestly how to use scikit-learn is not something I struggle with, and I wanted to talk about something harder.
posts.key_insight: The real challenge in machine learning isn't building models but designing systems to rigorously measure whether those models actually work, and tracking that over time.

posts.summary: Julia Evans walks through logistic regression from first principles, explaining how it fits a linear model and passes the output through a logistic function to produce probabilities. She demonstrates the technique by generating synthetic data that deliberately fits the model's assumptions, then fits a classifier using sklearn and evaluates it. Her key insight is that model evaluation should go beyond classification accuracy to check whether the predicted probabilities are calibrated—meaning a data point assigned 80% probability should actually be True about 80% of the time. She closes with open questions she cannot yet answer, modeling intellectual honesty about her own learning process.
key_points: Logistic regression fits a linear combination of features, then normalizes the result through the logistic function to produce a probability between 0 and 1.
Generating synthetic data that fits a model's assumptions is a more rigorous way to understand the model than using real-world data, because you control ground truth.
Standard confusion matrix accuracy is not enough—the predicted probabilities themselves should match actual class proportions to confirm the model is well-calibrated.
When data truly fits the model's assumptions, plotting predicted score vs. actual proportion yields a nearly perfect straight line, which real-world data rarely produces.
The post closes with genuinely open questions about convergence of maximum likelihood estimation, data scaling, and mathematical justifications for the model.
money_quotes: understanding the models I'm using helps me sleep a lot better at night
Do those probabilities make sense?
This graph is BEAUTIFUL. It is basically a perfect straight line.
actual data doesn't care what you think the model is :(
not that the data lets you easily perfectly separate the two classes (it doesn't!), but that it fits the assumptions of the model
let's say I have a data point which is assigned a probability of 0.8. Then I'd expect 80% of those points to be Trues
I'm still working some of these things out, and if I've gotten anything terribly wrong I'd love to know about it
posts.key_insight: Model evaluation should include checking whether predicted probabilities are calibrated against actual class proportions, not just whether the binary classification is correct.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her experience discovering LD_PRELOAD at Hacker School with a fellow participant. She shows how surprisingly easy it is to override arbitrary function calls in dynamically linked programs by writing a tiny shared library and setting an environment variable. The post walks through a minimal example of replacing rand() to always return 42, then briefly explains the underlying mechanism of how dynamic linkers resolve function names. She also touches on the security implications, noting that LD_PRELOAD is useful for spying on program internals, and mentions defenses like static linking.
key_points: LD_PRELOAD lets you override any function in a dynamically linked program with just a few lines of C and a single environment variable
The dynamic linker resolves function names at runtime, and LD_PRELOAD simply says 'look in me first' when searching for functions
You can call the original overridden function using dlsym, allowing transparent interception
Defenses include static linking or checking for the LD_PRELOAD variable, though even the latter can be circumvented via LD_PRELOAD itself
money_quotes: I'd been hearing for a while that you can override arbitrary function calls in a program using an environment variable called LD_PRELOAD. But I didn't realize how easy it was!
That is LITERALLY ALL THE CODE YOU HAVE TO WRITE.
LD_PRELOAD is an environment variable that says 'Whenever you look for a function name, look in me first!'
Somebody explained to me at some point that if you work for the NSA and you're trying to spy on what information a program is using internally, tools like LD_PRELOAD are VERY USEFUL.
the attacker could also LD_PRELOAD the function that lets you read environment variables…
posts.key_insight: LD_PRELOAD is a powerful and surprisingly easy-to-use mechanism for overriding function calls in dynamically linked programs, requiring only a few lines of C code.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at PyData NYC 2014, where she gave her first machine learning talk about the importance of evaluating models and tracking experimental results over time. Her talk introduced Stripe's open-source tool 'topmodel' for storing and visualizing training results. She also highlights other talks she enjoyed, including Sasha Laundy's talk on data auditing with csvkit and Dan Blanchard's SKLL framework for streamlined scikit-learn experimentation. The post captures the energy of attending a conference and learning from people working in different domains.
key_points: The most important lesson in machine learning is rigorously evaluating whether your models are actually improving, not just building more models
Tracking experiment results permanently is easier said than done, but lightweight systems like storing results in S3 and visualizing them with a webapp can help
Good data auditing and communication about data requirements are underappreciated challenges in data science work
Frameworks that simplify model training workflows (like SKLL) can be valuable when they don't try to fully automate away human judgment
money_quotes: I often feel like the main (only?) thing I've learned about machine learning so far is how important it is to evaluate your models and make sure they're actually getting better.
"Just remember everything forever" is much easier said than done.
People asked lots of questions, so I think it may have been useful. It's hard to tell, with talks :)
It turns out that one of the nice things about having a blog is that people occasionally come up to me at conferences and tell me GREAT THINGS.
Communication is *hard*.
Good documentation makes me so happy.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable skill in machine learning isn't building models—it's systematically tracking and evaluating whether your experiments are actually making things better.

posts.summary: Julia Evans demonstrates how to use strace to observe the internal workings of Hadoop's distributed filesystem (HDFS). She sets up a small Hadoop cluster and traces the system calls made when reading a file, revealing the two-phase process: first contacting the namenode to get block locations, then connecting to datanodes to retrieve the actual data. She decodes the Protocol Buffer messages in the strace output and locates the physical block files on the datanode's filesystem. The post culminates in a live demo discovery where she directly edits a block file on the datanode and finds the changes reflected when reading through HDFS, revealing that checksums weren't enforced on reads.
key_points: HDFS splits files into 128MB blocks distributed across datanodes, and strace reveals the exact sequence of network calls to locate and retrieve them
The client first contacts the namenode (port 8020) for block locations, then connects to individual datanodes (port 50010) to read data, and strace shows this protocol clearly
The raw strace output of Hadoop's Protocol Buffer messages can be decoded to reveal readable block IDs, pool IDs, and client names
Block data is stored as plain files on the datanode filesystem and can be found and even edited directly, with changes visible through HDFS when replication is set to 1
money_quotes: So when the people at Big Data Montreal asked if I wanted to give a talk about stracing Hadoop, the answer was YES OBVIOUSLY.
This sequence matches up exactly with the order of the blocks in the table up at the top! So fun.
And suddenly I thought… WAIT WHAT IF WE EDITED IT GUYS?!
And someone commented 'No, it won't work, there's metadata, the checksum will fail!'. So, of course, we tried it, because toy clusters are for breaking.
And it worked! Which wasn't perhaps super surprising because replication was set to 1 and maybe a 128MB file is too big to take a checksum of every time you want to read from it, but REALLY FUN.
live demos are GREAT
writing a blog is great because then people ask me to give talks about fun things I write about like stracing Hadoop
posts.key_insight: Strace can demystify even complex distributed systems like Hadoop by revealing the actual network calls and data flow at the system call level, turning opaque infrastructure into something you can observe, understand, and even tamper with.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her first experience writing threaded programs, starting with a deliberately buggy race condition in C and then fixing it using mutexes and atomics in both C and Rust. She discovers that mutexes carry a significant performance penalty (0.08s to 3.1s) and that Rust's mutex implementation was even slower due to its channel-based approach. She then explores atomic operations as a faster alternative, using perf and flame graphs to understand performance differences. The post traces the journey from abstract knowledge of concurrency concepts to hands-on understanding of the actual CPU instructions (the x86 LOCK prefix) that make thread-safe operations work.
key_points: Race conditions produce wildly wrong results — a counter expected to reach 20,000,000 only hit 2.8-5.7 million due to unsynchronized increments
Mutexes give correct results but at enormous cost: 40x slower than the racy version, because futex system calls involve expensive kernel interactions
Rust's mutex implementation was nearly 3x slower than C's because it was built on channels, though both ultimately used the same futex syscall
Atomic operations (lock-free) were dramatically faster than mutexes while still being correct, taking 0.44-0.56 seconds versus 3-8 seconds
The x86 LOCK instruction prefix is what actually provides thread safety at the hardware level, giving a processor exclusive access to shared memory
money_quotes: Actually understanding a Thing is hard if I've never done it.
This is a really big performance hit, even if it means that we have a program that works instead of a program that doesn't!
It's sort of refreshing to hear the people who write GCC (who I think of as MAGICAL WIZARDS WHO KNOW EVERYTHING) say that they read some Intel documentation and it was not clear what it meant!
My reaction at the time was WHAT ARE YOU EVEN ASKING ME HELP. But these concurrency explorations make me feel like that question is a lot more reasonable!
Also when I see a process waiting in a futex(... system call when I strace it, I understand what's going on a little better! This is wonderful.
I did this without actually understanding what an atomic even is, so I'm not going to explain anything yet.
posts.key_insight: Hands-on experimentation with threading reveals that atomic operations can be orders of magnitude faster than mutexes because they use hardware-level CPU instructions instead of expensive kernel system calls.

posts.summary: Evans reflects on how to think about people we admire, arguing that instead of putting them on a pedestal, we should identify specific qualities we respect and ask whether we can develop those same qualities ourselves. She gives examples like admiring Terence Tao's public writing about math without needing to become a mathematician, and learning from a friend's skill at giving thoughtful feedback. She concludes that idolizing people is both unfair to ourselves and awkward for them, and adds an important caveat about the privilege that makes it easier for some people to pursue what they admire.
key_points: Instead of treating admired people as untouchable geniuses, break down what specifically you admire about them and ask if you can develop those traits
You don't have to replicate everything about someone—you can selectively adopt specific qualities that matter to you
Idolizing people is counterproductive: it discourages you from trying and makes the admired person uncomfortable
Privilege plays a significant role in who gets to easily pursue the things they find interesting or important
money_quotes: Instead of being all 'wow that person is so great they must be a genius and a wizard', it's better to ask 'okay, what specifically do I admire about that person? Is that a way I want to be? How did they get like that? Can I do that too?'
Demystifying and writing about things I find interesting in public are things I can do without trying to win the Fields medal
Saying WOW THAT PERSON IS SO GREAT I COULD NOT POSSIBLY is kind of… being unfair. Because I could probably be a little more like them if I think it's important!
It's kind of awkward for people to be idolized like that, and it's better to just figure out what parts of them I'd like to be more like, and try some things out, and see what works.
There's a lot of privilege involved in saying 'hey that awesome thing? I can just go ahead and do that!'
posts.key_insight: Instead of idolizing people as unreachable geniuses, identify the specific qualities you admire and deliberately practice developing those qualities yourself.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores how fear in programming leads to overly conservative decisions and worse software. She distinguishes between healthy caution and paralyzing fear that prevents necessary changes like big refactors or deploys. The post argues that better tools and processes—version control, testing, CI, canary deploys—reduce fear by providing safety nets. Beyond tooling, she emphasizes that blameless postmortems and a culture that doesn't punish mistakes are essential for enabling bold improvements. She also notes that fears can be irrational and contagious, persisting even after objective risks are mitigated.
key_points: Fear creates local maximums—being afraid to make changes prevents dramatic improvements and leads to stagnation around mediocre solutions
Tools like version control, testing, CI, canary deploys, and rollback capability are concrete ways to convert fear into manageable caution
Blameless postmortems are critical because blaming people for mistakes makes them more afraid to make future changes, producing worse software over time
Some fears are irrational and can infect other people, persisting even when objective evidence shows things are usually fine
There is an important distinction between caution (careful, excellent) and fear (which holds you back from doing necessary work)
money_quotes: Fear creates local maximums.
If you're scared of making changes, you can't make something dramatically better, or do that big code cleanup.
Maybe you can't even deploy the code that you already wrote and tested, because it feels too scary.
You just want to stick what's sort-of-working, even if it's not great.
So if you blame people for breaking things, they'll be more scared to make changes in the future, and you'll end up with worse programs.
Even with good design, w/o tests you will fear change and so the code will rot. With good tests, there's no fear, so you'll clean the code.
I feel like there's this really important line between caution (a++ excellent) and fear (which holds you back from doing necessary work)
Some fears are irrational, and they can infect other people.
posts.key_insight: Fear of breaking things creates local maximums in software quality—investing in safety nets like tests, deploys, and blameless culture converts paralyzing fear into productive caution.

posts.summary: Julia Evans addresses the recurring experience of being misgendered in online comments about her technical blog posts, where readers default to 'he' when discussing her work. She reflects on how she never internalized the idea that programming was a male domain, finding the assumption that technical content must be written by a man simply silly. She asserts her sense of belonging in the programming community and pushes back against gatekeeping of all kinds. The post concludes with a broad, inclusive declaration that programming belongs to anyone who writes programs.
key_points: Online commenters consistently default to male pronouns when discussing her technical writing, revealing deep assumptions about who produces technical content
Evans rejects the premise that programming is a male domain, noting she never grew up believing she was worse at math or programming than men around her
Gatekeeping in tech is framed as an illegitimate power grab rather than a natural state of affairs
Programming community membership should be defined by the act of programming itself, not by identity, background, or credentials
money_quotes: I didn't grow up with the idea that I was worse at math or programming than the men around me (because, well, I wasn't!)
I feel like the community belongs to me, and like I'm a part of it.
And when people who have more power push marginalized people out of tech, I think, who do you think you are?
Why do you think this belongs to you, and that you have the right to say who can come and who can't?

Programming doesn't belong to men, or to people who went to MIT, or to white people, or to English-speakers, or to Linux users, or to C programmers, or to people with CS degrees, or to people who are self-taught, or to people who can see, or to people who had easy access to computers when they were young.
If you write programs, it belongs to you.
posts.key_insight: The programming community belongs to everyone who programs, and the default assumption that technical work is produced by men reveals an illegitimate sense of ownership over who gets to participate.

posts.summary: Evans reflects on learning to read source code directly instead of relying solely on documentation, a skill she picked up at her first programming job working with Drupal. She describes how reading source code works well for specific detail questions but breaks down when you're missing fundamental conceptual understanding. She proposes a hierarchy of bug difficulty ranging from immediately obvious problems to ones requiring bigger-picture knowledge you don't even know you're missing. For that hardest category, she advocates asking knowledgeable colleagues as the best strategy.
key_points: Reading source code directly is a powerful technique for answering specific implementation questions that aren't covered in documentation
Source code reading works best when you understand the general system but need specific details; it fails when you lack fundamental conceptual understanding
Bug difficulty exists on a spectrum from immediately obvious to requiring knowledge you don't know you're missing
Asking colleagues remains the best strategy for the hardest category of bugs where you lack the conceptual framework to even know where to look
money_quotes: I like to think of this as a 'oh right I actually need to know how to program' moment – nobody's going to tell me what to do, I just need to figure it out.
I originally felt like Drupal was too complicated to understand (it's a big codebase!), but it turned out that if I tried I could usually figure out what I needed to know.
There are always details about code that aren't exhaustively documented, and using grep to find answers is incredibly helpful.
You're missing some bigger-picture of knowledge about the code you're running that you need to understand the bug.
I still struggle with approaching problems in #4 (especially if I don't know that I don't know the Thing That I'm Missing).
posts.key_insight: Reading source code is a powerful debugging skill, but it only works when you have enough conceptual understanding of the system — without that bigger picture, you need to ask people who have it.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at Data Day Texas 2015, a one-day conference where she attended talks on database upgrades, human-in-the-loop machine learning, and generating fake datasets. Her favorite talk was Charity Majors' presentation on upgrading MongoDB at scale at Parse, which gave her a new appreciation for the planning and testing required for database operations. She also highlights Robert Munro's talk on using human input to improve ML classifiers, particularly the psychological aspects of keeping human labelers engaged. The post reflects her enthusiasm for talks that genuinely advance her understanding of a topic.
key_points: Database upgrades at scale require enormous planning — Parse spent about a year testing MongoDB upgrades, including replaying production workloads against snapshots
Human-in-the-loop machine learning can dramatically outperform pure model tuning, but you need to keep the human labelers engaged and show them their impact
Generating fake datasets has practical applications for debugging, proving statistical intuitions, and creating ML tutorials
Upgrades that seem routine can cause catastrophic performance regressions — one query type got 100x slower in the worst case
money_quotes: I understood before seeing this talk that doing database upgrades was hard, and that it's appropriate to be incredibly skeptical, but I didn't have any clue how to plan to reduce your uncertainty so that you can actually do the upgrade.
How much time is it appropriate to spend planning and testing a database upgrade? (they spend about a year)
I pretty rarely see talks that I feel really advance my understanding of a topic, and this was one of them.
He talked about how judiciously incorporating human input to get a better training set can give you much, much higher accuracy than just messing with your model parameters.
If you're writing a cat classifier and every single thing you show the human is not a cat, they'll get bored and exhausted.
It gave me a little more appreciation for what it means to do ops at scale and to keep services running.
posts.key_insight: Reducing uncertainty in risky operations like database upgrades requires systematic, patient testing against real production workloads — not just hoping for the best.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains A/A testing, a technique she learned at a Data Science meetup from an Airbnb talk about common experimentation mistakes. The concept involves comparing two control groups (instead of a treatment and control) to gauge how much natural variation exists in your data. By seeing how much difference appears between two identical groups, you can get an intuitive sense of whether your experimental results are meaningful or just noise. She walks through a widget store example showing how a promising 2% sales increase looks less impressive when the control-vs-control comparison shows similar variation. The technique is formally called A/A/B testing when combined with an experimental group.
key_points: A/A testing compares two control groups to reveal how much random variation naturally exists in your experiment's metrics
If the variation between two control groups is as large as your experimental effect, your results are likely just noise
A/A testing provides an intuitive sanity check for experimental significance without requiring a formal statistical model
The formal version with an experimental group included is called A/A/B testing
money_quotes: I'd never heard of A/A testing before, didn't really understand what she meant at the time, and didn't manage to ask. (pro tip: ask questions when you have them :))
I now think a) that I get it and b) it's really simple and c) that it's SUPER COOL AND MAYBE USEFUL TO ME.
But let's say you want to get a rough sense for whether or not your results might be significant without doing statistics.
The idea here is to compare two sets of users in the same experimental group and see how high the variation is.
Suddenly, the group we're experimenting on doesn't look so good anymore. It looks like any difference is likely to be just because of random noise.
I like this because it seems like it can give you a rough sense for how significant your results are without having to decide on a statistical model.
A question like "if we compare two groups of this size with the same characteristics, do we naturally see a lot of variation?" is a great smoke test.
posts.key_insight: Comparing two control groups against each other reveals the natural noise level in your data, giving you an intuitive baseline to judge whether your experimental results are real or just random variation.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her decade-long journey learning to program, pushing back against the notion that programming can be learned quickly. She traces her path from writing BASIC on a TI-83 calculator in 2004 through university and into professional work, noting that progress was slow and nonlinear.
