Looking back at her old code, she was surprised not by its quality but by how little she had actually written in those early years. The post serves as reassurance to beginners that learning to program is a long process, and that even experienced developers spent years feeling uncertain about their abilities.
key_points: Learning to program took roughly 10 years of mostly low-speed progress before she felt confident, contradicting narratives about learning in weeks or months
Looking back at early code, the most surprising thing wasn't its quality but how little of it there was — she simply didn't write many programs in those first years
Working 40 hours a week as a professional programmer taught her more about software engineering than years of casual scripting
She didn't consider herself good at programming even after completing math/CS degrees, and never thought to apply to prestigious tech companies
money_quotes: I don't generally like giving advice, because I think it's way too easy to generalize your own experience into advice and do it wrong.
I spent about 10 years learning to program before going to Hacker School, mostly at fairly low speeds.
I was amazed to find out you could write subroutines and define your own variable names because on the TI-83 you only have about 26 variables (A-Z)
At this point (2011) I still basically don't believe I'm any good at programming. I do not in any way consider applying to fancy jobs at Google or Microsoft or whatever.
What was the most surprising was how little of it I found.
If you're programming and just starting out, and worrying that people say you can learn to program in 24 hours or 12 weeks… whatever. It's taken me 11 years so far, and I'm not done :)
posts.key_insight: Learning to program is a years-long journey of slow, uneven progress, and the pressure to learn quickly in weeks or months doesn't match the reality of how most programmers actually develop their skills.

posts.summary: This post explains the second half of how gzip compression works: Huffman coding for individual characters. After gzip uses LZ77 to eliminate repetitions in text, it uses Huffman codes to represent frequently-occurring characters with fewer bits. Evans walks through how prefix-free codes work, demonstrates reading real Huffman tables from compressed files using the infgen tool, and shows how the gzip spec converts code lengths into actual Huffman codes. The post connects theoretical computer science concepts to their practical application in everyday tools.
key_points: Gzip compression is a two-step process: LZ77 removes repetitions, then Huffman coding compresses individual characters by assigning shorter bit sequences to more frequent characters
Huffman codes work because no code is a prefix of any other code, eliminating ambiguity when decoding a stream of bits
The infgen tool reveals how gzip internally represents its Huffman tables, showing only code lengths rather than the codes themselves
The gzip spec defines an algorithm to reconstruct full Huffman codes from just the code lengths, starting at 000000 and shifting left when lengths increase
Examining real Huffman tables reveals patterns about the content — English text compresses common letters like space, a, e, i most efficiently, while source code prioritizes parentheses and quotes
money_quotes: none of these codes is a prefix of any other code. So if we write down 010001001011 we can see that it's 010 00 1001 011 or baec! There wasn't any ambiguity
You might ALSO notice that these are all less than 8 bits! That means we're doing COMPRESSION.
Which initially seems totally unhelpful! Like, who cares if it's represented with a code of length 6 if I DON'T KNOW WHAT THAT CODE IS?!!
these are some of the most common letters in the English language, so it TOTALLY MAKES SENSE that these would be encoded most efficiently. Yay!
I tried it out on the compressed source code infgen.c.gz, and you can totally see it's source code and not a novel!
It's kind of my favorite when things I learned about in math class show up in the programs I use every day.
posts.key_insight: Gzip's Huffman coding assigns shorter bit sequences to more frequent characters, and you can reconstruct the full coding table from just the code lengths using the algorithm in the gzip specification.

posts.summary: Julia Evans investigates how the locate command works by examining its database file, discovering it's essentially a compressed recursive directory listing. She builds a homegrown alternative by piping find's output to a text file and searching it with grep, and finds that this naive approach is actually faster than locate despite using 3x more disk space. She traces this back to a 1983 paper that justified locate's compressed format based on constraints—slow grep over 20,000 files, limited disk space—that no longer apply with modern hardware. The post questions whether other Unix tools carry similar outdated design assumptions.
key_points: The locate database is fundamentally just a compressed recursive directory listing (ls -R /), not the Complicated Thing it might seem
A homegrown locate using find piped to a text file and searched with grep is actually 3x faster than locate, despite using 3x more disk space
The original 1983 locate design optimized for constraints (30-second grep times, limited disk) that are irrelevant on modern hardware with SSDs and 700,000+ files searchable in 0.05 seconds
Standard Unix tools may carry design assumptions from decades ago that no longer hold true
money_quotes: But I always somehow thought of the locate database as this Complicated Thing. Until today I started looking at it!
COINCIDENCE THAT ALL OF THESE WORDS ARE THE SAME? I THINK NOT!
Whoa, our homegrown locate using grep is actually way faster! That is surprising to me.
This kind of makes me wonder if our database format which doesn't use any clever compression tricks might actually be a better format if you're not worried about the extra space it takes up.
The 1983 paper specifically calls out 'Why not simply build a list of static files and search with grep?', and says that grepping a list of 20,000 files took 30 seconds.
I have 700,000 files on my hard disk, and it takes about 0.05 seconds. It seems to me like the locate authors' original issues are really not a problem anymore, 30 years later.
This really makes me wonder what other standard unix programs make design assumptions that aren't true in 2015.
posts.key_insight: The locate command's compressed database format was optimized for 1983 hardware constraints that no longer exist, and a naive grep over a plain text file list is actually faster on modern systems—raising the question of what other Unix tools carry outdated design assumptions.

posts.summary: Julia Evans compiles crowd-sourced advice from Twitter on what to discuss during one-on-one meetings with your manager. The post lists specific questions covering promotions, team dynamics, learning, career trajectory, and project satisfaction. Beyond topic ideas, she shares meta-advice on how to approach 1:1s more effectively, like keeping running lists of discussion items throughout the week. The post emphasizes that 1:1s should be reserved for things you wouldn't say in front of all your coworkers.
key_points: Good 1:1 questions cover promotions, team tensions, learning gaps, project satisfaction, and career direction
1:1s should be reserved for topics you wouldn't discuss in front of all coworkers — that's what makes them valuable
Keeping a running shared list throughout the week prevents forgetting important topics when the meeting arrives
Turning questions around to ask your manager what they're thinking about creates a more productive two-way conversation
money_quotes: What's happening now that I would like to not be happening in a month?
If I'd be comfortable talking about it in front of all my coworkers, don't talk about that.
It is SO EASY to forget everything that was bothering me when I get to a 1:1.
Turn it around: what are *you* thinking about right now? what's your top priority? what's worrying you about this team?
Do I feel like I'm learning? Are there things I feel like I'm *not* learning that I would like to?
posts.key_insight: The most valuable 1:1 discussions are the ones you wouldn't have in front of the whole team, and keeping a running list throughout the week ensures you don't forget them.

posts.summary: Evans challenges conference organizers who claim they have no control over the diversity of their talk submissions. She argues that active outreach to underrepresented groups, combined with helping people improve their proposals, directly increases both the quantity and quality of diverse submissions. She cites PyCon's dramatic increase in women speakers (from 1% to 33% over four years) as proof that deliberate outreach works. The post frames diversity in conference lineups as a choice organizers actively make or fail to make.
key_points: Conference organizers choose who submits proposals through active outreach and encouragement
Helping speakers refine their proposals dramatically improves submission quality, not just quantity
PyCon's women speaker ratio went from 1% in 2011 to 33% in 2014 through deliberate diversity outreach
Personal invitations to speak are far more effective than passive open calls for proposals
money_quotes: We have a choice about who submits talk proposals, and also about who submits the *best* talk proposals.
If you ask someone specifically to consider speaking at your conference, they're WAY more likely to consider submitting a talk than if you don't.
If you then actively work with some talk submitters to help them focus and improve the talk they submit, their proposals will get better!
% PyCon talks by women: (2011: 1%), (2012: 7%), (2013: 15%), (2014: 33%). Outreach works.
Telling people that I like their work and that I'd like for them to talk about it is super fun! (and true!)
posts.key_insight: Conference diversity isn't a matter of luck or pipeline — it's the direct result of organizers choosing to do personal outreach and help underrepresented speakers craft strong proposals.

posts.summary: Julia Evans presents a detective-style investigation into diagnosing why programs are slow, using three mystery programs as case studies. She demonstrates how to distinguish between CPU-bound programs, those waiting on network responses, and those doing heavy disk I/O using language-independent OS tools. The first mystery is solved using `ps` with `wchan` to reveal network socket waiting, the second is identified as CPU-bound via `time`, and the third reveals surprising filesystem cache behavior where writes appear fast but are deferred by the kernel. The disk I/O case is the most interesting, showing how consecutive runs take dramatically different times due to the kernel's write caching filling up. She also compiles a comprehensive list of Linux performance tools recommended by her Twitter followers.
key_points: The `time` command alone can immediately tell you whether a program is CPU-bound or waiting on something else by comparing elapsed time to CPU time
The `ps -eo wchan` trick reveals what kernel function each process is currently blocked on, quickly identifying network vs disk waits
Filesystem write caching means programs can appear to finish writing before data hits disk, causing confusing performance variations between consecutive runs
Knowing when NOT to use OS-level tools matters too — a CPU-bound Python program is best debugged with a language-specific profiler
Tools like `dstat` provide real-time snapshots of disk, network, and CPU activity that reveal what the OS is doing behind the scenes
money_quotes: I specifically wanted programming-language-independent ways to investigate questions like this, and I guess people who follow me on twitter get me because I got SO MANY GREAT ANSWERS.
It's important to know when you *shouldn't* use fancy operating systems tools to debug performance, and I think this is one of those cases.
Writing to disks is super slow, and the kernel loves you and wants you to not have to wait. So it says 'okay, got it!', but doesn't actually write the data to disk until a little later.
When the filesystem cache runs out of space, it says 'okay you need to stop writing now' and actually writes the data to disk and makes you wait until it's done.
The whole point of this exercise is to debug using our operating system, so we need to get a better picture of what the OS is doing!
I actually originally thought I understood this one but I TOTALLY DIDN'T.
posts.key_insight: A handful of OS-level tools like `time`, `ps -eo wchan`, and `dstat` can quickly diagnose whether a slow program is CPU-bound, network-bound, or IO-bound without ever reading the source code.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes different archetypes of senior engineers using fantasy hero metaphors. She identifies four distinct roles: the fortune teller who predicts engineering problems, the cattle wrangler who builds consensus, the spring of knowledge who holds deep technical expertise, and the gardener who helps manage technical debt. The post argues that senior engineering excellence takes many different forms, and that these specializations don't necessarily overlap. She references Rent the Runway's engineering ladder as a related framework that maps engineering qualities to RPG attributes.
key_points: Senior engineering skill is not monolithic — different people excel at fundamentally different things like prediction, consensus-building, deep knowledge, or code stewardship
A fortune teller who predicts project pitfalls doesn't necessarily need to also be a cattle wrangler who drives consensus — specialization is valid
The gardener archetype highlights that managing technical debt is a distinct and valuable senior engineering skill
The spring of knowledge often provides what you need to know rather than the answer to the question you asked
money_quotes: It's about how fortune tellers do not necessarily also need to be cattle wranglers.
You tell her a design decision you're making; she tells you the problems you're going to run into in 3 months.
She saves you an incredible amount of engineering effort in bad directions.
Everyone wants to standardize, and nobody can agree on what the standard should be.
What you need to know is not always the answer to the question you asked.
They show you where the nastiest weeds are, suggest code that you could delete, and help you get to a better architecture in a reasonable amount of time.
posts.key_insight: Senior engineering excellence comes in distinct archetypes — prediction, consensus-building, deep expertise, and code stewardship — and no one person needs to embody all of them.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores using perf as an alternative to strace for observing system calls, motivated by strace's significant performance overhead. She demonstrates that strace slowed du by 65x due to 260,000 system calls, while perf record can capture the same information with minimal impact. She walks through the basic perf record and perf script commands but notes a limitation: perf shows memory addresses for filenames rather than the actual strings. The post ends with a call for help from readers on resolving this limitation.
key_points: strace can slow programs dramatically (65x in this example) when they make many system calls, making it unsuitable for production or performance-sensitive profiling
perf record with syscall tracepoints captures system call data with much less overhead than strace's ptrace-based approach
perf script output shows system call arguments but only as raw addresses, not resolved strings like filenames
perf's scripting capabilities (including Python script generation) open the door to building strace-like tools without the performance penalty
The practical threshold matters: strace is fine for programs with few syscalls but problematic for syscall-heavy workloads like filesystem traversal
money_quotes: You all know by now that I'm pretty into strace, but – strace is not always a good choice! If your program runs too many system calls, strace will slow it down. A lot.
That's 65 times slower! This is because du needed to use 260,000 system calls, which is uh a lot.
But what if we still want to know what du is doing, and du is actually a Really Important Program like a database or something?
But it doesn't give us the filename, just… the address of the filename? I don't know how to get the actual filename out and that makes me sad.
Like maybe you could pretty it up and have a script that's like strace but doesn't slow your program down so much.
posts.key_insight: When strace's performance overhead is unacceptable for syscall-heavy programs, perf's tracepoint-based recording offers a low-overhead alternative for observing system calls, though with less convenient output.

posts.summary: Julia Evans surveys several Linux tools beyond strace for understanding what your operating system is doing. She covers netstat for checking open ports, dstat for real-time disk and network stats, lsof for tracking open files, ngrep/tcpdump for network packet inspection, opensnoop/ftrace for file access tracing, and atop for comprehensive process monitoring. Each tool is presented as a way to discover 'The Truth' about what's happening on your system. She emphasizes that these tools are valuable because they give you direct, unmediated answers to specific questions about your computer's behavior.
key_points: Simple single-purpose tools like netstat, dstat, and lsof are powerful precisely because they answer one specific question directly
For network debugging, tcpdump output is best analyzed in Wireshark's GUI rather than reading raw packet dumps
Brendan Gregg's perf-tools and ftrace provide deep kernel-level tracing but require more effort to learn and may need modification for specific kernel versions
Man pages explain individual words and options but can't substitute for real stories of how people used tools to solve actual problems
money_quotes: netstat tells me the truth about whether it is running.
Want to know how much data is actually being written to your physical hard drive right this second? YEAH YOU DO.
as always with these systems tools, ngrep / tcpdump will tell you The Truth™ about what's going on on your network.
I'm mostly including this as an example to show that a lot of stuff is possible to know.

you can read the man page for tcpdump and understand individually all the words, but it's not a substitute for someone telling you an Awesome Story about how they used tcpdump to debug an intermittent certificate problem or a DNS problem and then they Saved the Day.
I actually have no idea why I had redis installed so uh yeah I uninstalled it.
It's neat and a little terrifying to look at at first (SO MANY NUMBERS).
posts.key_insight: Linux provides a rich ecosystem of simple, focused tools that tell you the truth about what your system is actually doing, and learning them is best driven by real debugging stories rather than man pages alone.

posts.summary: Julia Evans proposes reframing open source sprints as learning opportunities rather than contribution obligations. She observes that contributing to a complex project for the first time is genuinely hard, and the pressure to produce a contribution in a few days can make sprints unproductive. Instead, she suggests picking a project, deciding what you want to learn about it, and digging in without worrying about making a contribution. This approach might actually lead to better contributions later, once you understand the project's internals.
key_points: Contributing to an open source project for the first time is hard because there's a lot of context you need, and the area you're interested in might not need contributions right now
Reframing sprint goals from 'contribute code' to 'learn something' removes pressure and can be more productive
Exploring a project without contribution pressure might naturally lead to discovering changes that need making
Being near the team who works on a project is valuable even if they don't have time to help you directly
money_quotes: Contributing to a project for the first time is hard. There's a lot you need to know!
The area of the project you're interested in might not necessarily need contributions right now!
Instead of trying to write a contribution, change the goals!
And maybe, while exploring with no particular goal, I'd find a change that needs making!
Or if I wanted to contribute to the project 6 months down the road, I'd be a little more prepared to do that.
posts.key_insight: Open source sprints become more valuable when you reframe them as learning opportunities rather than feeling obligated to produce a contribution.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces the release of her strace zine, a printable guide teaching people how to debug programs using strace. Originally planned as a mailed zine, she instead made it available as a free PDF download. The post provides practical printing instructions for making physical copies and encourages readers to share it with friends, colleagues, and students.
key_points: The zine teaches debugging with strace in a physical, printable format
Available as both portrait and landscape PDFs, plus a screen-readable version
Designed for grassroots distribution — print it yourself and share it
Represents an early example of Evans' zine-based approach to technical education
money_quotes: I wrote a zine to teach people about how to debug their programs using strace
I was originally going to mail it out to people, but it turns out I'm too lazy to mail anything.
So instead, you can download, print, fold, and staple it yourself today!
Give it to your friends/colleagues/students to teach them about strace!
You can also read it on your computer. But printing it is better!
posts.key_insight: Technical knowledge about debugging tools like strace can be made accessible and shareable through simple, printable zine formats.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at AdaCamp Montreal 2015, an unconference for women in open technology and culture. She highlights discovering the specialized role of software testers who don't write code, meeting a diverse range of fascinating people, having productive discussions about codes of conduct, and learning about effective moderation techniques for group discussions. The post reflects on how unconference formats enable unexpected, valuable conversations and how voicing your specific interests often reveals others share them.
key_points: Software testing is a specialized discipline distinct from development, including user acceptance testing that focuses on whether software makes sense to users rather than just technical correctness
Unconference formats allow attendees to shape sessions around their specific interests, which often turns out to resonate with others
Good moderation and facilitation skills are crucial for inclusive discussions, especially ensuring quieter participants get space to contribute
When you think you're the only person with a particular concern or question, you're usually wrong
money_quotes: I didn't know there were people who specialized in testing software and were really awesome at it who don't write programs!
You can have a QA team that checks that the software makes sense to users and, like, talks to them and stuff, not just software that's theoretically correct.
When I think that I'm the only person who has a given concern or question or experience, I'm usually wrong.

People take up really incredibly different amounts of space in discussions, and I really really want to get better at making sure people who are quieter get a chance to say their super interesting things.
Interrupting people is hard for me!
After AdaCamp I felt like there are a lot of great people in the world who are trying their best to do what's right and have a lot of good ideas about how to do that.
posts.key_insight: Voicing your specific interests and concerns in collaborative settings usually reveals that others share them, and unconference formats create the space for those unexpected connections.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains bootstrap confidence intervals as a practical, non-parametric approach to quantifying uncertainty in statistical estimates. Using a playful airline overbooking scenario, she demonstrates how sampling with replacement from your data thousands of times can produce useful confidence intervals without needing to know the underlying distribution. She shows that with only 8 data points, bootstrapping reveals wide uncertainty, while 1000 data points yield much tighter estimates. She acknowledges skipping the mathematical justification for why bootstrapping works but emphasizes its practical value as a go-to method for getting error bars.
key_points: Bootstrap confidence intervals let you quantify uncertainty without knowing your data's underlying distribution (nonparametric statistics)
The method works by sampling with replacement from your data many times and computing your target statistic on each resample
A single point estimate like '0.35 people' is meaningless without a confidence interval showing how much to trust it
More data dramatically tightens your bootstrap confidence interval, making the difference between 'could be 0-2' and 'pretty confident it's 2'
Bootstrapping can't save you from unrepresentative samples — if your data doesn't capture important real-world variation, no statistical method will fix that
money_quotes: I analyze data, and I rarely think about what the underlying distribution of my data is.
the technical term for not knowing your distributions is 'nonparametric statistics' :D
Maybe I shouldn't have switched to CS so I didn't have to take statistics (true story). Or alternatively maybe I can BOOTSTRAP MY WAY TO A CONFIDENCE INTERVAL WITH COMPUTERS.
This is actually way more useful! It's telling me I can oversell by 0 - 2 people, and I don't have enough data to decide which one.
I have not explained ANY of the math behind why you should believe this is a reasonable approach, which if you are like me then you are super uncomfortable right now.
If occasionally 100 people don't make the flight because they're all from the same group and that's important and not represented in your sample, bootstrapping can't save you.
This method is the bomb though. It is basically the only way I know to get error bars on my estimates and it works great.
posts.key_insight: Bootstrap confidence intervals give you a practical, distribution-free way to understand how much you should trust a statistical estimate, using nothing but resampling and computation.

posts.summary: Evans reflects on the safety and responsibility implications of using machine learning in production systems. She distinguishes between simple, interpretable models and complex ones like random forests, noting that complex models can perform well but make unpredictable mistakes. She argues that being responsible for a system you don't fully understand is both scary and interesting. The post offers practical mitigation strategies like human review, matching model complexity to error tolerance, and using ML insights to improve simpler systems.
key_points: Model interpretability exists on a spectrum — a 10-variable regression is understandable, a large random forest is not
Complex ML models are simultaneously powerful and dangerous because their mistakes are unpredictable and embarrassing
ML models are only as good as their training data, and bad data leads to bad decisions
Practical safety strategies include human oversight, complexity-appropriate model selection, and distilling ML insights into simpler systems
money_quotes: Being responsible for the accuracy of a system that you don't fully understand is scary/ very interesting.
A model you don't understand is awesome. It can perform really well, and you can save time at first by ignoring the details.
A model you don't understand is scary. It will make unpredictable and sometimes embarrassing mistakes. You're responsible for them.
Often when I train a new model I think at some point 'NO PLZ DON'T USE THAT DATA TO MAKE DECISIONS OH NOOOOO'
Use complicated models when it's okay to make unpredictable mistakes, simple models when it's less okay.
Use ML for research, learn why it's doing better, incorporate your findings into a less complex system.
posts.key_insight: The responsible use of machine learning requires matching model complexity to your tolerance for unpredictable mistakes, and using human oversight and interpretability as safety nets.

posts.summary: Evans describes profiling a slow machine learning pipeline at Stripe where processing 6 million records took 1.5 hours. Using VisualVM, she and colleagues traced the bottleneck to a single Math.pow() call inside a HyperLogLog estimation loop in Twitter's Algebird library. By replacing the expensive floating-point power function with a bit shift operation or lookup table, they achieved a 100x speedup. The post emphasizes building intuition for when code is unreasonably slow by knowing baseline performance expectations for simple operations.
key_points: A loop doing ~10,000 simple floating point operations should take microseconds, not milliseconds — knowing baseline latency numbers helps identify unreasonable slowness
Math.pow() was the hidden bottleneck: replacing it with bit shifting (1.0 / (1 << mj)) gave a 47x speedup, and a 256-entry lookup table gave 100x
Profiling with VisualVM identified the exact code block consuming all the time, turning a vague 'it's slow' into a targeted one-line fix
Building intuition for expected performance is a learnable skill — being able to say 'this should not take this long' is the first step to fixing it
money_quotes: I used to think a millisecond was fast.

This meant that processing 6 million things would take 1.5 hours, which is Slow. The code is kind of complicated, so originally we all thought this was a reasonable amount of time. But my heart was sad.
Since this loop is hardly doing anything omg, the obvious target is java.lang.Math.pow(2.0, -mj), because that looks like the hardest thing.
Many CPU instructions take a nanosecond or something. so 10K of them should be on the order of 10 microseconds or so. Definitely not a millisecond.
So we can literally make this code 100 times faster by just changing one line.
I'm learning intuitions for when code is slower than it should be and it is THE BEST.
Being able to say 'this code should not take 10s to process 10,000 records' is amazing. It is even more amazing when you can actually fix it.
posts.key_insight: Developing intuition for expected performance baselines — knowing that 10,000 simple CPU operations should take microseconds, not milliseconds — is the key skill that turns vague slowness into targeted, dramatic fixes.

posts.summary: Julia Evans enthusiastically reviews the COZ paper on causal profiling, a technique for profiling multithreaded programs. Traditional profiling breaks down with threads because speeding up one thread may not help if another thread is the bottleneck. COZ's core insight is to simulate speeding up a line of code by slowing down all other threads, using Linux perf without modifying the program. The technique was validated on real programs like SQLite and Memcached, finding issues like hash table collisions and unnecessary locking. Evans also speculates about applying causal profiling to single-threaded async applications.
key_points: Profiling single-threaded programs is straightforward, but threads break traditional profiling because parallel work means speeding up one part may not help overall performance
COZ's key innovation is simulating a speedup by slowing down all other threads, implemented using Linux perf without requiring code modifications
The profiler found real optimizations in SQLite and Memcached including hash collisions, unnecessary locking, and thread rebalancing opportunities
COZ can also detect cases where speeding up code would paradoxically cause slowdowns due to increased contention, which helps identify locking problems
The technique might extend to profiling single-threaded async applications by slowing down other work in the event loop
money_quotes: The core idea in this paper is – if you have a line of code in a thread, and you want to know if it's making your program slow, speed up that line of code to see if it makes the whole program faster!
Of course, you can't actually speed up a thread. But you can slow down all other threads!
They also find out places where speeding up a line of code would introduce a slowdown (because of increased contention around some resource).
This seems like literally magic except that THEY EXPLAIN HOW IT WORKS. Papers. Wow.
Speeding up one of a() or b() won't help you, because they both need to finish in order for the program to finish.

the implemention here is super super super interesting – they use the perf Linux system to do this, and in particular they can do it without modifying the program's code. So this is a) wizardry, and b) uses perf
posts.key_insight: Causal profiling solves the hard problem of profiling threaded programs by simulating speedups through slowing down all other threads, revealing which code actually matters for overall performance.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her sudden appreciation for Docker after struggling to set up a complex C++ environment for neural network experiments. She needed Ubuntu 14.04 with many dependencies and was dreading reinstalling her OS until she remembered Docker could give her an isolated environment instantly. She highlights Docker's speed compared to VMs, the reproducibility of Dockerfiles, and the freedom to make messy configurations without risking her main system. The post captures the 'aha moment' of understanding Docker's practical value through a real problem.
key_points: Docker's killer feature is creating isolated environments for messy dependency chains without risking your host system
Docker containers start as fast as regular programs, unlike virtual machines which take forever
Dockerfiles make even chaotic environment setups reproducible — it's a 'reproducible mess'
The practical use case that clicked was needing Ubuntu 14.04 with C++ dependencies for neural network experiments
Docker's value becomes obvious when you have a real problem it solves, not from abstract explanations about containers
money_quotes: I didn't really understand what Docker was *for* until yesterday. I mean, containers. Cool. Whatever.
I am apparently past the age when that was fun.
I downloaded an Ubuntu 14.04 image, and suddenly I just had Ubuntu 14.04! And I could INSTALL THINGS ON IT. Without worrying!
It's not like a virtual machine which takes forever to start – starting a Docker container is just like, well, starting a program.
I don't have to worry about screwing up my computer while doing it, and then even if it's a mess, it's a *reproducible* mess!
The next time I need to compile a thing with horrible dependencies that I don't have on my computer and that conflict with everything, I'm totally using Docker.
posts.key_insight: Docker's real value clicks when you need an isolated, reproducible environment for messy dependency chains — it turns an unreproducible mess into a reproducible one.

posts.summary: Evans argues that understanding TCP basics is practically essential for any developer, not just systems programmers. She illustrates this through a real debugging story where HTTP requests to a local queue were inexplicably taking 40ms each. The root cause turned out to be the interaction between Nagle's algorithm and TCP delayed ACKs, which creates passive waiting between sender and receiver. By setting TCP_NODELAY, the delays instantly disappeared. She references John Nagle himself calling delayed ACKs a bad idea from 1985 that TCP continues to use even when it consistently loses its bet. The post demonstrates how a small amount of TCP knowledge can turn a mysterious week-long debugging puzzle into a simple fix.
key_points: Nagle's algorithm and TCP delayed ACKs interact badly: when HTTP libraries split requests into multiple packets, both sides wait passively, adding hundreds of milliseconds of latency
This isn't an edge case—delayed ACKs are enabled by default on every Linux system, so anyone sending data in multiple TCP packets can hit this
John Nagle himself considers the 200ms delayed ACK timer a bad idea from 1985 Berkeley that TCP continues using even when it consistently loses its bet
A week of debugging was solved instantly by enabling TCP_NODELAY, but only because Evans had previously read about the same TCP interaction in a different context
You don't need deep TCP expertise—just enough surface knowledge to recognize when a problem might be TCP-related
money_quotes: It really should not take 40 milliseconds to send a HTTP request to localhost. Something was terribly wrong.
That period where the application and HAProxy are both passive-aggressively waiting for the other to send information? That's the extra 200ms.
So I committed a change turning on TCP_NODELAY for our application, and BOOM. All of the 40ms delays instantly disappeared. Everything was fixed. I was a wizard.
The 200ms 'ACK delay' timer is a bad idea that someone at Berkeley stuck into BSD around 1985 because they didn't really understand the problem.
A delayed ACK is a bet that there will be a reply from the application level within 200ms. TCP continues to use delayed ACKs even if it's losing that bet every time.

I used to think that TCP was really low-level and that I did not need to understand it. Which is mostly true! But sometimes in real life you have a bug and that bug is because of something in the TCP algorithm.
You don't have to be a systems programming wizard to run into this.
posts.key_insight: A small amount of TCP knowledge can turn mysterious performance bugs into instant fixes, because protocol-level issues like Nagle's algorithm interacting with delayed ACKs affect anyone making HTTP requests, not just systems programmers.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on how she became good at debugging after receiving positive feedback in a performance review. She identifies several key factors: maintaining confidence that bugs have logical causes, building broad systems knowledge, talking through problems with others, and mastering observability tools like strace and tcpdump. She notes that debugging skill came not from being systematic and organized, but from persistence, knowledge accumulation, and learning to enjoy the challenge. The post concludes with her realization that difficult bugs are now more exciting to her than writing new code.
key_points: Bugs always happen for logical reasons, and reminding yourself of that prevents giving up prematurely on hard-to-reproduce issues
Deep systems knowledge (TCP, garbage collection, OS internals) is prerequisite for debugging certain classes of problems — you can't fix what you can't conceptualize
Talking through problems out loud, even just rambling in Slack, often leads to breakthroughs through rubber-ducking or drawing in helpful colleagues
Observability tools like strace and tcpdump that show what a program is actually doing are indispensable for cutting through confusion
Debugging skill creates a positive feedback loop: getting better at it makes it more enjoyable, which makes you seek out harder bugs
money_quotes: Everything on a computer does in fact happen for a logical reason (however much the computer may try to convince you otherwise).
Also known as 'OK JULIA IT IS NOT FAIRIES WHAT ACTUAL REASON COULD BE CAUSING THIS?'

If you don't know that a common source of latency issues on the JVM is garbage collection pauses (or worse, if you don't know that garbage collection pauses are even a thing that happen), then you're going to have a really bad day trying to figure that out.
Understanding the structure of the system I'm trying to debug and what some of the common failure modes are has been really indispensable to me.
I've gotten way better at figuring out what I don't understand, articulating it, and asking about it.
Seriously I could not fix bugs without strace.
Being able to observe directly what a program is actually doing is incredibly valuable.
A difficult bug is way more likely to teach me something I didn't know before about how computers can break.
posts.key_insight: Debugging skill comes from combining confidence that bugs have logical causes, deep systems knowledge, willingness to talk through problems, and mastery of observability tools — and getting better at it makes it genuinely enjoyable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains why Ruby's Timeout module is dangerous after encountering a difficult bug at work caused by it. The core issue is that Timeout uses Thread.raise to inject an exception into a running thread at any arbitrary point in execution, meaning the exception can fire during cleanup code, rescue blocks, or any line of code whatsoever. She surveys how other languages handle the same problem—Java deprecated and then disabled Thread.stop, C# discourages Thread.Abort, and C++ threads aren't interruptible at all. The fundamental flaw isn't Ruby-specific: there is no way to safely interrupt an arbitrary block of code. Java's Thread.interrupt offers a better model by only allowing interruption at specific safe points.
key_points: Ruby's Timeout works by spawning a thread that calls Thread.raise on the original thread after a delay, injecting an exception at whatever line happens to be executing
Thread.raise can fire during rescue blocks, cleanup code, or object creation—nobody writes code to defend against exceptions on literally any line
This is a fundamental design flaw, not just an implementation bug: safely interrupting arbitrary code blocks is impossible
Java deprecated Thread.stop in 1998 and disabled it entirely in Java 8; C# similarly discourages Thread.Abort
Java's Thread.interrupt is a safer alternative because InterruptedException can only be thrown at specific safe points
money_quotes: Nobody writes code to defend against an exception being raised on literally any line. That's not even possible.
Thread.raise is basically like a sneak attack on your code that could result in almost anything.
There is no way to safely interrupt an arbitrary block of code. Anything could be happening at the end of that 5 seconds.
Timeout uses Thread.raise, so it is not safe to use.
You know all the times we say Ruby is more fun than Java? TODAY JAVA IS MORE FUN BECAUSE IT MAKES MORE SENSE.
I felt awesome about tracking it down, and upset with myself about creating it in the first place.
posts.key_insight: There is no way to safely interrupt an arbitrary block of code because an exception injected at any random line can corrupt state during cleanup, rescue blocks, or any other operation.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at the Women in Machine Learning 2015 conference (part of NIPS), expressing enthusiasm for attending academic conferences outside her usual domain. She highlights three talks: Lillian Lee's research on how tweet phrasing affects retweets, Been Kim's work on interpretable ML and human-machine interaction, and Corinna Cortes' talk on web scraping for fact discovery at Google. A recurring theme is her appreciation for interpretable, debuggable ML and for speakers who are honest about how hard ML really is rather than pretending it's magic.
key_points: Academic conferences in unfamiliar fields can be more educational than industry conferences in your own field because everything is novel and practitioners have deep domain expertise.
ML applied to intrinsically hard tasks where even humans can't reach 100% accuracy is more interesting than ML on tasks humans find trivial.
Interpretable and debuggable ML systems that help humans perform better—rather than replacing them—represent an underappreciated area of research.
Honest discussion about how time-intensive and difficult building good ML models actually is counters the harmful narrative that ML is easy or magical.
Making technical talks accessible and interesting without sacrificing rigor is a deliberate and valuable presentation strategy.
money_quotes: I actually liked it a lot more than an industry conference because everything was so alien & unfamiliar to me and everyone knew way more about their field than me.
Doing ML on tasks that have something intrinsically hard about them – where you maybe can't ever get to 100% accuracy – is really interesting to me.
Convince people to be interested and that your work is obvious, not that you're smart and your work is difficult.
All I want is to help humans do better work & save them time so this made me super happy.
I think there's a lot of room in ML to enable people and not just replace them.
Instead of saying 'well at Google we do magic', the impression I got was 'dude this stuff is really hard and involves a lot of really boring HTML and tables'. BUT IN THE END WE WON.
IF YOU'RE NOT LOOKING AT YOUR DATA WHAT ARE YOU EVEN DOING.
I get really mad when people pretend that ML is easy or that if you're smart you can just get good results like magic.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable machine learning work focuses on interpretability and helping humans do better work, not on replacing them or pretending the results come easily.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her article published in Code Words, the Recurse Centre's quarterly publication, about tricking neural networks into misclassifying images. The post is essentially a brief announcement linking to the full article, which includes reproducible code. She also recommends several other Code Words articles on topics like DDoS, Git internals, and floating point equality.
key_points: Evans published an article about adversarial examples in neural networks in the Recurse Centre's Code Words publication
The article includes reproducible code so readers can experiment themselves
She highlights other notable Code Words articles on DDoS, Git internals, and floating point curiosities
money_quotes: It comes with code so that you can reproduce everything in it yourself!
Code Words is overall pretty excellent
posts.key_insight: Adversarial examples in neural networks are an accessible topic that anyone can explore with hands-on code.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her 2015, highlighting the creation of her first strace zine, giving talks at PyCon, blogging about systems programming and performance, and a successful machine learning project at work. She also candidly discusses dealing with wrist pain from typing all year, which forced her to pivot to drawing comics and be more intentional about what she spends her limited typing energy on. The post balances celebration of creative output with honest acknowledgment of physical limitations, concluding that constraints can sharpen focus on what truly matters.
key_points: Her strace zine was a breakout success, handed out at PyCon and adopted by an OS class, validating the format of teaching systems programming through zines
Blogging serves as both a learning tool and a feedback mechanism — writing things down prevents forgetting, and public discussion surfaces corrections and new ideas
A from-scratch ML project at work converted her lingering skepticism about machine learning into genuine belief that it works when the problem is well-suited
Wrist pain from typing forced a creative pivot to drawing comics and taught her that ignoring physical limitations is unsustainable
Constraints can be focusing — if typing hurts, you stop wasting time on things that don't matter
money_quotes: I was thinking of trying to sell it but it felt like too much work. Instead I spent maybe $300 on printing and gave it away for free, since I have a job.
Giving awesome talks is hard!
Blogging is pretty amazing. Writing down what I'm learning helps me not forget.
I don't know how I would have gotten such useful ideas without blogging.
Now I really believe when you have a problem that's well-suited to machine learning, machine learning REALLY WORKS and can make your life awesome.
I've also decided that black box machine learning models that I can't debug are NOT OKAY.
what did not work: pretending that I do not have a body.
If spending 3 hours typing hurts, it makes no sense to work on something in that time that doesn't matter.
posts.key_insight: Physical constraints can sharpen creative focus — when every hour of typing costs something, you stop wasting time on work that doesn't matter.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her personal experience of accepting a job offer with stock options she didn't understand, and walks through the math that reveals how expensive and risky exercising options can be. She explains that when you leave a private company, you typically have only 3 months to exercise your options, which can cost hundreds of thousands of dollars in exercise prices plus taxes on unrealized gains. Using both a hypothetical example and her own situation at Stripe, she shows how the total cost of exercising can exceed an employee's entire savings. The post provides a concrete list of basic and advanced questions to ask before accepting an offer, covering everything from exercise prices to liquidation preferences and early exercise options.
key_points: 65% of people surveyed accepted job offers without understanding how their stock options work, leaving significant money decisions uninformed
When you leave a private company, you typically have only 3 months to exercise your ISOs, and the combined cost of exercise price plus taxes on the spread can be unaffordable
Tax agencies may treat the difference between exercise price and fair market value as income you owe taxes on, even though you can't actually sell the stock yet
VCs often have liquidation preferences guaranteeing they get their money back first, which can make employee shares worthless even in an acquisition
The company making you an offer has lawyers who've thought carefully about all of this — you should be at least somewhat informed too
money_quotes: Stock options are BORING AND COMPLICATED AND AWFUL. They are full of taxes, which we all know are awful.
Surprise! Now you need to pay hundreds of thousands of dollars to invest in an uncertain outcome.
Your local tax agency thinks you have made 3.3 million dollars. Well, you haven't actually made that, since you're buying stock you can't sell.
The total amount it would cost to exercise + pay taxes on my options is more than all of the money I have.
By definition it is impossible for me to exercise my options without borrowing money.
Your employer has all the power.
Being informed isn't disloyal. The company you work for is informed.
The company making you an offer has lawyers and they should know the answers to all the questions I suggested. They've thought very carefully about these things already.
posts.key_insight: Stock options at private companies can be effectively worthless to employees who can't afford the exercise price and tax bill when they leave, so you should do the math before accepting an offer rather than after.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares practical strategies for participating in meetings as a remote worker. She identifies two key scenarios: all-remote meetings (which work fine) and mixed meetings with mostly local participants (which are much harder for the remote person). The core problem is that remote participants can't easily interrupt or signal they want to speak. She describes learning to be deliberately loud and explicit about wanting to talk, and also notes that local participants can actively help by soliciting input from remote attendees.
key_points: All-remote meetings work well because everyone faces the same communication constraints, but mixed meetings with one remote person are a disaster
The fundamental problem for remote participants is the inability to visually signal a desire to speak, making natural turn-taking nearly impossible
Explicit, slightly awkward communication protocols (announcing when you want to speak and when you're done) are effective workarounds
Local participants bear responsibility too — actively asking remote people for input reveals they often have valuable things to say but face too high a barrier to volunteer
Learning to interrupt effectively as a remote participant is a skill that takes significant time to develop
money_quotes: Scenario #2 is a disaster. It took me a long time to understand why, but I think the primary reason is that it's very hard to interrupt and indicate that I want to speak when I'm remote.
if I just wait for a break in the conversation to say something… well, it will never happen :)
Now if I have something to say I just say "HI I HAVE A THING TO SAY". And people say "yes julia?" and then I say the thing. It feels awkward but it works for me.
So my interactions are awkwardly delimited by HI I HAVE A THING TO SAY – I AM SAYING THE THING - I AM DONE SPEAKING NOW YOU MAY PROCEED. This is great and it works.
Normally, this means that the local people do all the talking and the remotes are totally silent (because the barrier to entry is high).
they frequently did have something really interesting to say! They were just not saying it.
posts.key_insight: Mixed local-remote meetings systematically silence remote participants, and the fix requires both remote people learning to be deliberately explicit about turn-taking and local people actively soliciting remote input.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains the bias-variance decomposition, a mathematical framework that clarifies why machine learning techniques like ensembles and regularization actually work. Coming from a pure math background, she was frustrated by machine learning's reliance on heuristics rather than theory. The bias-variance decomposition theorem shows that prediction error decomposes into bias squared plus variance plus irreducible error, which provides actionable insight for model building. She carefully labels which parts of her explanation are rigorous math versus practical heuristics. The post demonstrates how ensembles reduce variance by averaging correlated models, and how regularization trades increased bias for decreased variance. Throughout, she maintains intellectual honesty about where the math ends and the hand-waving begins.
key_points: The bias-variance decomposition theorem proves that prediction error equals bias squared plus variance plus irreducible error, giving a mathematical foundation for understanding model performance
Ensembles work because averaging multiple high-variance, low-bias models reduces variance while preserving the same bias, with variance reduction depending on correlation between models
The bias-variance 'tradeoff' is actually a heuristic observation, not a mathematical necessity — there's no proven reason you must trade one for the other
Regularization increases bias but lowers variance, and can win overall if the variance reduction exceeds the bias increase, as demonstrated by the James-Stein estimator
Most practical claims in machine learning (like 'complex models have lower bias') lack mathematical backing and are empirical observations that happen to work
money_quotes: Everything was 'well it works in practice' and so little of it was math. I was a pure math major at the time, so arguments like 'well it works in practice' made me REALLY MAD.
I've annotated each section by whether it's math or whether it a 'works in practice' heuristic (aka bullshit, but useful bullshit).
This is a theorem that you can find in An Introduction to Statistical Learning. This is math so it will never let us down.
There's no math reason I know of you have to have a tradeoff between bias and variance.
Not knowing how machine learning methods work means I can't debug them, and that means I can't be amazing. which obviously isn't acceptable.
The James-Stein estimator is a way to estimate the mean that gives you lower error. It's a biased estimator, but its error is lower. This is amazing.

Understanding why ensembles work is awesome – it means that if an ensemble method isn't helping you, you can understand why! It's probably because the correlation between the models in your ensemble is too high.
posts.key_insight: The bias-variance decomposition is one of the few pieces of actual math in machine learning that explains why techniques like ensembles and regularization reduce error, turning a supposed tradeoff into something you can win.

posts.summary: Julia Evans challenges the common misconception that Java is inherently slow, arguing that the language itself is capable of extremely high performance. She points to examples like the LMAX Disruptor processing 6 million events per second and databases like Cassandra written in Java. She acknowledges her own Java code was slow due to excessive object allocation and garbage collection pressure, not the language itself. The post highlights a thriving ecosystem of high-performance Java libraries and recommends VisualVM as a free, easy first step to profiling slow JVM code.
key_points: Java's performance is comparable to C for many workloads — the language isn't slow, but naive code patterns like excessive object allocation are
There's a meaningful performance gap between Java and interpreted languages like Python/Ruby, with Java capable of millions of operations per second
A rich ecosystem of high-performance Java libraries exists (LMAX Disruptor, Netty, HikariCP, Chronicle) proving Java's performance credentials
The JVM ships with VisualVM, a free profiler that's an accessible first step to finding and fixing performance bottlenecks
Understanding that your tools are capable of high performance should raise your expectations for what your code can achieve
money_quotes: I used to write Java at work, and my Java programs were really slow. I thought (and people sometimes told me) that this was because Java is a slow programming language.
Java is fast like C is fast (really fast). People write databases (Cassandra) in Java!
So if your Java code is doing something easier than processing 6 million events a second, and it's slow, you can maybe make it faster!
I find it inspiring to think about when I'm having trouble processing like.. 10 things per second :)
My Java code was probably slow because I was creating like a bajillion objects all the time, and destroying them, and doing a bajillion allocations takes time.
Did you know the JVM ships with a free profiler that can tell you which part of your code is the slowest?
Computers are fast, and you should expect a lot out of your computer programs.
posts.key_insight: Java isn't a slow language — slow Java code is usually caused by patterns like excessive object allocation, and profiling with free tools like VisualVM is the first step to fixing it.

posts.summary: After writing a blog post about misunderstanding stock options, Julia Evans started asking questions about equity compensation in a public Slack channel at work. The discussion became so popular that a quarter of the company joined a dedicated #equity channel within days. She discovered that open discussion of confusing compensation topics benefits everyone because it reveals that nobody fully understands these issues. The post advocates for transparency around equity compensation in the workplace.
key_points: Public discussion of equity compensation at work benefits everyone and reveals shared confusion
A quarter of the company joined the #equity Slack channel immediately, showing massive pent-up demand for this information
Fear of being perceived badly for asking about stock options (especially questions implying you might quit) turned out to be unfounded
Even experts like lawyers and finance people were willing to engage openly with employee questions
money_quotes: It turns out that talking about confusing equity / compensation issues in public channels at work is awesome.
Everyone realizes that nobody else fully understands what's going on.
People with more experience can share what they know, and people with less experience can ask all their questions!
A quarter of the company joined the channel immediately.

I was originally worried that asking questions that amount to 'what can I do with my stock options if I quit?' would somehow be a bad idea or perceived badly. But mostly everyone seems really delighted to talk about it.
posts.key_insight: Openly discussing confusing compensation topics at work helps everyone because it surfaces shared ignorance and creates space for collective learning.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares slides from a talk she gave about why she likes the Rust programming language. The post is extremely brief, serving mainly as a pointer to her Speaker Deck slides rather than containing substantive written content. She notes that while she normally doesn't post her slides separately, she feels these stand well on their own.
key_points: Evans gave a talk about her enthusiasm for the Rust programming language
She considers these particular slides unusually self-explanatory compared to her typical presentations
The post functions as a link to external slides rather than a standalone article
money_quotes: Normally my talk slides are unremarkable and I don't post them, but I think these mostly stand on their own and I'm happy with how they turned out.
posts.key_insight: This is a brief pointer to talk slides about Rust rather than a substantive blog post, notable mainly as an early signal of Evans's interest in systems programming languages.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares follow-up notes and resources from her CUSEC conference talk. The post covers two main topics from the talk: debugging techniques and understanding computer performance. She recommends specific tools like strace, dtrace, wireshark, and mitmproxy for debugging, and profilers like visualvm and YourKit for performance analysis. The post serves as a curated reference list linking to her own blog posts and external resources.
key_points: strace on Linux and dtrace on OS X are essential debugging tools that every developer should learn
Network analysis tools like ngrep, mitmproxy, and wireshark make debugging network issues accessible and even fun
Understanding computer performance requires hands-on tools and profiling, not just assumptions about what's fast or slow
A collection of real debugging stories and performance investigations makes these concepts concrete and learnable
money_quotes: on Linux, learn strace. It's the best.
wireshark gives you a really nice graphical interface for network spying. It's fun.
A millisecond isn't fast
posts.key_insight: Debugging and performance analysis become approachable when you know the right tools — strace, dtrace, wireshark, and profilers — and have concrete stories showing how they solve real problems.

posts.summary: Julia documents her experience learning to call C functions from Rust, working through compiler errors with help from a friend. She shows a minimal example of using Rust's FFI to call strcpy and puts from C, wrapping them in an unsafe block. She also notes how easy cargo makes it to start new Rust projects, and that Rust installation has improved since 2013 though error messages remain challenging.
key_points: Calling C from Rust requires declaring extern functions with proper pointer types and wrapping calls in unsafe blocks
Rust's cargo tool makes project scaffolding and building remarkably simple
Rust has gotten easier to install since 2013, but compiler error messages are still hard to understand
Even a simple task like copying and printing a string requires navigating Rust's pointer and safety system
money_quotes: calling C code is definitely unsafe. it could be doing ANYTHING
It turned out to be a little harder than I expected! Largely because I don't know Rust well, and fixing compiler errors is nontrivial.
Rust is way easier to install than it was in 2013, though I still find the error messages hard to understand.
30 minutes and some number of inscrutable-to-me compiler errors later, we figured it out.
I'm mostly writing this down so that I don't forget, but maybe it will be useful for you too!
posts.key_insight: Calling C from Rust is straightforward once you understand extern declarations and unsafe blocks, but Rust's strict type system makes even simple FFI tasks a learning experience.

posts.summary: Julia explores ftrace, a Linux kernel tracing tool, by trying to figure out which CPU registers hold system call arguments. Starting with Brendan Gregg's kprobe tool, she traces the read system call but initially can't see which file is being read. By printing all register values and cross-referencing with strace output, she deduces which registers hold the file descriptor and read length. She discovers this detective work makes ftrace usable for low-overhead system call tracing, and later learns there's actually a standard table mapping registers to system call arguments.
key_points: Ftrace has much lower overhead than strace (strace makes 'find' 20x slower, ftrace doesn't), making it valuable for production tracing
You can deduce which CPU registers hold system call arguments by cross-referencing kprobe register dumps with strace output
On x86, there are only about 7 general-purpose registers to worry about, making the guessing game tractable
The registers used for Linux system calls are actually standardized and documented in a lookup table, eliminating the need to guess
money_quotes: But WHAT FILE DID IT READ? This is not good at all.
This was wizardry. How could he *guess* that the mode of a file was in the register %cx? What even are the registers? This makes no sense.
So that leaves me with only 7 registers to worry about (eax, ebx, ecx, edx, esi, edi, and ebp). That's way better.
I can label those now and be a register-guessing-wizard like Brendan Gregg!
The advantage of using ftrace instead of strace is that the overhead is way lower: when I strace find it makes it 20x slower, but with ftrace it's totally okay.
Guessing registers is really tedious but it seems to be totally possible!
turns out you don't have to guess at all! the registers used for system calls are always the same :D.
posts.key_insight: You can figure out Linux kernel internals through experimentation and cross-referencing tools, turning seemingly wizardly knowledge into a systematic process of elimination.

posts.summary: Evans recounts a Papers We Love meetup talk by Daniel Lemire about roaring bitmaps, a data structure for fast integer set operations. She explains why integer sets matter for databases (row ID intersections for queries), introduces bitmaps as a compact alternative to hash sets, and dives into how modern CPUs can execute multiple instructions per cycle. The post connects CPU architecture concepts like instruction pipelines and SIMD to why roaring bitmaps are designed the way they are. She uses perf stat to demonstrate instructions-per-cycle differences between programs, showing that simple bitwise operations parallelize well at the CPU level.
key_points: Hash sets are not fast for large integer set operations; bitmaps offer dramatically better performance for intersections and unions
Modern CPUs can execute multiple instructions per cycle through instruction pipelines, not just one per cycle like older CPUs
Parallelization isn't just about multiple cores or machines — a single core can do more than one instruction per cycle for simple operations like bitwise AND
Roaring bitmaps compress sparse bitmaps efficiently (2-3 bits per integer) while maintaining fast set operations by leveraging SIMD instructions
Database queries like WHERE clauses with AND conditions are fundamentally set intersection problems on row IDs
money_quotes: One of the big takeaways for me in this talk was 'whoa, hashsets are not fast. okay.'
Taking 'AND' or 'OR' of two bitmaps corresponds to set intersection or union, and is extremely wizard fast.
This is about doing more than one instruction per CPU cycle, on a single core, in a single thread.
I have this idea in my head that my CPU can do 2.7 billion instructions a second (that's what 2.7 GHz means, right?). But I learned this is not actually really true!
Old CPUs used to do only one instruction per cycle, and it was easy to reason about. New CPUs have 'instruction pipelines' and can do LOTS.
The whole goal of the Papers We Love meetup is to show programmers work academics are doing that can actually help them write programs. This was a fantastic example of that.
posts.key_insight: Fast integer set operations matter for real systems like databases, and designing data structures that align with how modern CPUs actually work (instruction-level parallelism, SIMD) can yield massive performance gains beyond what naive implementations achieve.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the sendfile system call, explaining how it optimizes the common pattern of reading a file and writing it to a network socket by eliminating unnecessary data copies and context switches. She presents impressive performance benchmarks, including a Python FTP library doubling throughput and Netflix going from 6Gbps to 40Gbps on FreeBSD. However, she also covers the pitfalls, including a serious OS X bug that caused 5-second delays. The post connects sendfile to related system calls like splice and tee, and discusses practical configuration options in web servers like nginx and Apache.
key_points: sendfile eliminates the need to copy data from kernel to user space and back, reducing both CPU overhead and context switches when serving files over a network
Real-world benchmarks show dramatic improvements: 800MB/s to 1.5GB/s for Python FTP, and 6Gbps to 40Gbps for Netflix on FreeBSD
sendfile isn't universally safe — an OS X bug caused it to not send any data until the socket closed, which is why web servers let you toggle it on and off
sendfile is now a wrapper around the more general splice system call, which copies data between file descriptors without passing through user space
Understanding low-level system calls has practical implications for anyone configuring or building web servers that serve large files
money_quotes: So the idea is – this pattern of reading a file and writing to a socket is really common! So they made a system call to just do that!
That post describes how on OS X, sendfile wouldn't send any data until the socket was closed, causing up to 5 second delays. That's TERRIBLE.
So sendfile isn't some kind of universal panacea, and that's why webservers let you turn it on and off.
It's super interesting to me how much abstractions break down when you're trying to really optimize performance.
It makes me really happy when learning a new system call helps me understand how to do something really practical.
I straced cp on my machine just now, and it seems like it does not use sendfile.
posts.key_insight: The sendfile system call can dramatically improve file-serving performance by keeping data in kernel space, but its platform-specific bugs remind us that even performance optimizations need to be tested and configurable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains CPU load averages by connecting them to practical web server performance analysis through Little's Law from queueing theory. She shows that dividing the load average by request throughput gives you the average CPU time per request, a formula she found useful for real-world debugging. The post walks through what load averages actually measure (processes in running or runnable state), then derives the formula and lists important caveats, including Linux's inclusion of uninterruptible sleep processes. She also proposes an extension for estimating actual on-CPU time versus waiting time when CPUs are overloaded.
key_points: Load average is the average number of processes in a running or runnable state over 1/5/15 minutes, not a mysterious opaque number
Little's Law from queueing theory lets you calculate CPU time per request as load average divided by throughput (requests per second)
Linux load averages include processes in uninterruptible sleep (usually disk I/O), which can distort the calculation
The formula breaks down when load is fluctuating, when processes handle multiple requests per thread, or when non-HTTP work contributes to load
When CPUs are overloaded, you can estimate actual on-CPU time (excluding wait time) by substituting the number of cores for the load average
money_quotes: CPU load averages have long been a little mysterious to me. I understood that low is good, and high is bad, but I thought of them as a mostly inscrutable number.
100ms is a bajillion years of CPU time, and I was super concerned.
Being able to calculate that number so quickly was SUPER USEFUL to me for understanding the server's performance.
This is pretty intuitive: if 10 people per hour arrive at your store, and they spend 30 minutes each there, then on average there will be 5 people at a time in your store.
As far as I understand, 'runnable' means "I'd be running if you'd let me". Processes that are asleep don't count.
If there are 3 processes that want to run on a CPU at the same time, and your computer has 4 CPU cores, then you're totally okay!
posts.key_insight: Little's Law from queueing theory turns the seemingly opaque CPU load average into a practical debugging tool: divide it by your request throughput to get average CPU time per request.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her learning experience about clock skew in distributed systems, prompted by reading the debate between Martin Kleppmann and antirez about Redis's Redlock distributed locking system. She walks through Kleppmann's arguments about why distributed locks can fail—process pauses, network delays, and unreliable clocks—and admits she initially found clock skew hard to believe since she'd never personally encountered it. After asking distributed systems experts on Twitter, she learned that clocks running at wrong speeds is a real and well-documented problem, not just a theoretical concern.
key_points: The Redlock debate between Kleppmann and antirez illustrates fundamental challenges in distributed locking: process pauses, network delays, and clock unreliability
Clock skew—where a computer's clock runs at the wrong speed—is a real operational problem, not just a theoretical edge case for researchers
Distributed systems researchers worry about uncommon failures because they're proving theorems and operating at massive scale where every possible failure will occur
Google invested heavily in their Spanner system specifically because knowing the correct time at scale is genuinely difficult
Reasoning through distributed systems debates yourself, even as a non-expert, is a valuable learning exercise
money_quotes: These are very interesting to read as a distributed systems n00b – the authors of the articles are making significantly different arguments, and so it's a useful exercise to try to reason through the arguments and figure out who you think is right.
Here's the thing – I haven't seen a lot of systems, but I've never seen a system with a clock running too fast.
They were basically both totally 'julia systems with clocks that go at bogus speeds totally exist. 2x clock skew is real'.
if you have 20 million computers everything that can go wrong with a computer will go wrong.
if you prove a theorem that your system is safe, then it'll be safe even if you have 20 million computers. Theorems are the best.
So my laptop's clock is probably mostly okay, but at Google scale I'd imagine you have computers with broken clocks all the time.
People I know have experienced it! It is not just a weird theoretical thing to give researchers jobs. Huh.
posts.key_insight: Clock skew is a real operational problem in distributed systems, not just a theoretical edge case, and reasoning through expert debates is a valuable way to build understanding of distributed systems challenges.

posts.summary: Evans argues that developers should build intuition about how fast computers actually are, rather than accepting slow performance as inevitable. She walks through concrete benchmarks showing nginx can handle 1M QPS, Java servers can do 50K QPS, and even toy Python servers can manage 1K QPS. She then catalogs common reasons servers underperform (CPU saturation, worker starvation, network limits, database load, disk I/O) and argues that understanding these bottlenecks requires investigation, not assumptions. Her core message is that developing an 'absolute sense' of computer speed helps you identify when something is unreasonably slow and gives you confidence to invest time in profiling and optimization.
key_points: Concrete benchmarks show computers are far faster than most developers assume: nginx can do 1M QPS, Netty 50K QPS, even Python 1K QPS
It's easy to be wrong about why something is slow — assumptions about bottlenecks are frequently incorrect and need to be verified through profiling
Five common performance bottlenecks: CPU saturation, worker starvation, network bandwidth, database load, and disk I/O each require different fixes
Developing an absolute sense of computer speed (not just relative 'X is faster than Y') is a powerful debugging skill
Spending time setting up profiling feels terrible but pays off enormously when you actually find and fix the real bottleneck
money_quotes: When I first read that I thought it was a typo. Surely nothing can do a million anythings per second? It wasn't a typo.
Instead of 'Go is faster than Python', I want to be able to know things like 'I should be able to easily do 100 QPS in Python'.
I've found that it's really easy to be wrong about why something is slow.
Spending a day setting up profiling feels terrible but then is AWESOME when you make everything faster and everybody loves you.
Raising my expectations about how fast computers can and should be has been super useful to me. Now when I see that it takes a hour to process a million records, I get mad.
I try to restrict my anger to cases where it actually matters – if the processing job is in my way, or if web performance is actually a problem.
I have this mission to develop an absolute sense of how fast computers are.
posts.key_insight: Building concrete intuition about how fast computers actually are — not just relative comparisons — gives you the confidence and ability to diagnose and fix real performance problems.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that log files are an underrated debugging tool, especially for diagnosing tail latency problems that sampling profilers and dashboards miss. Inspired by Dan Luu's post on the limitations of sampling profilers, she shares how looking at raw logs for slow requests immediately revealed the source of a performance problem that graphs couldn't surface. She explains that while graphs are great for overall trends and correlations, logs excel at tracing individual slow requests to pinpoint exactly where time is being spent. She connects this to the broader insight that tracing tools, from simple print statements to sophisticated distributed tracing systems like Zipkin, all share the same fundamental advantage over aggregated metrics.
key_points: Sampling profilers aggregate events into averages, making them bad at diagnosing tail latency problems which are by definition not average
Looking at log files for the 10 slowest requests immediately revealed the performance bottleneck that dashboard graphs couldn't surface
Graphs are best for overall system pictures and correlations, while logs are best for digging into specific slow requests and short spikes
