Strategic placement of log statements at key points in code creates a lightweight tracing system that makes debugging straightforward
Advanced tracing tools described as 'from the future' are fundamentally doing the same thing as log files, just with more sophistication
money_quotes: Sampling profilers, the most common performance debugging tool, are notoriously bad at debugging problems caused by tail latency because they aggregate events into averages. But tail latency is, by definition, not average.

if you have 1000 events that were unusually slow, they probably failed because of a relatively uncommon event. And uncommon events do not always show up in graphs. But know where they show up? LOG FILES. WHICH ARE THE BEST.
I stopped trying to look at graphs, took Nelson's advice and looked at the log files for the 10 slowest requests.
graphs are a great way to get an overall general picture of the system

logs are amazing for digging into short spikes of timeouts or slow requests. Often I'll look at the logs for a few requests, they'll all exhibit the same behavior, and I'll have a much clearer understanding of what happened there.

these performance tools from the future that Dan describes are basically like.. magical wizard log files. And they're great for the same reason that log files are great. Because they are both tracing tools and tracing is important!
posts.key_insight: Log files beat dashboards and profilers for diagnosing tail latency because they preserve the individual event traces that aggregated metrics hide.

posts.summary: The post explains how to use Linux's clock_gettime system call to measure CPU time spent by a process or thread. Evans discovered this after a colleague mentioned measuring CPU time per HTTP request at work. The key insight is that clock_gettime with the CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID flag returns actual CPU time consumed, not wall-clock time, letting you distinguish between time spent computing and time spent waiting. She walks through the different clock flags available and shows how to bracket code with clock_gettime calls to measure CPU consumption.
key_points: clock_gettime is a Linux system call that does much more than tell wall-clock time — it can report per-process and per-thread CPU time
Using CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID lets you measure exactly how much CPU time a specific operation consumes, which is critical for distinguishing CPU-bound from I/O-bound slowness
System calls like clock_gettime are accessible from high-level languages like Ruby, not just C
CLOCK_MONOTONIC provides a time source that never goes backward, which is useful for avoiding clock skew issues
money_quotes: If you have a slow program, the first thing you want to know is whether your program is spending that time calculating things on the CPU, or whether it's waiting for something else (a disk, a network, user input).
It turns out that if you're a HTTP server, and you want to know exactly how much CPU time your HTTP requests are taking, you can just ask the Linux kernel!
I'd seen this system call before, but I thought it was only for getting the time, like 5:03pm. Not so!
System calls aren't just for C hackers, they're for everyone.
It makes sense to me that Linux keeps track of the CPU time spent per process.
posts.key_insight: Linux's clock_gettime system call with CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID lets you measure actual CPU time consumed by your code, distinguishing computation from waiting — and it's accessible from any language, not just C.

posts.summary: Julia Evans introduces perf top as a simple but powerful tool for understanding CPU usage on Linux systems. She walks through three real-world debugging stories: identifying garbage collection issues in a Node program, discovering that encrypted swap was causing high kernel CPU usage, and finding that HTTPS connection overhead dominated a Python program's CPU time. The post demonstrates how perf top can quickly triage CPU problems even for programs written in high-level languages like Node, Python, or Java. She positions perf top as an essential addition to any developer's debugging toolbox alongside strace.
key_points: perf top shows which functions are consuming CPU in real-time, like top does for processes but at the function level
Even for high-level languages like Node and Python, perf top reveals useful information about C-level functions consuming CPU time
The tool can quickly distinguish between very different root causes: garbage collection, kernel swapping, and crypto overhead all look distinct in perf top output
perf can be extended to understand Java and Node.js functions through additional configuration, making it even more powerful
perf top is safe to run in production and requires just one command: sudo perf top
money_quotes: strace has not ever been able to solve all my problems – it only tells me about system calls, it can slow down my code up to 50x. Not ideal!
Since my new hobby is to run perf top any time I have a question about CPU usage, I ran perf!
I didn't know what all of it meant, but it seemed clear that the program was spending most of its time doing garbage collection.

It turns out that the computer was swapping its memory to disk, and that the swap partition was encrypted. So every time it saved memory to disk or read it back, it needed to encrypt and decrypt all that memory.
It turns out that opening a HTTPS connection is pretty slow! You need to spend a bunch of time in crypto functions in order to be secure.
perf is even more magical than you might expect, though! You can tell perf about your Java and node functions.
I'm slowly building a toolbox of easy-to-use tools that will help me understand what my programs are doing.
But perf top is so simple (just one command!), and so straightforward, and I think it deserves to go in your toolbox.
posts.key_insight: perf top is a simple one-command tool that can immediately reveal what's consuming CPU at the function level, making it invaluable for quickly triaging performance problems even in high-level language programs.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores why 'the stack' is treated as a fundamental, almost revered concept in programming. Starting from basic call stacks in any language, she traces how C's call stack became the canonical One True Stack, examining the x86 assembly instructions (push, pop, callq, retq) that are built around specific assumptions about memory layout and the stack pointer register. She shows how deeply the stack format is baked into hardware instructions, then explores what happens when languages like Rust use different calling conventions. The post concludes that the stack really is fundamental because assembly instructions themselves encode assumptions about how it's organized.
key_points: The x86 assembly instruction set has built-in assumptions about how the stack is organized, making the C stack format effectively canonical
Instructions like callq and retq directly manipulate the stack pointer, meaning the hardware itself enforces a particular stack convention
Languages like Rust that use different calling conventions must do extra work (like implementing libbacktrace) to interoperate with tools that assume the C stack format
There's a chicken-and-egg question about whether x86 instructions shaped C's stack layout or vice versa
Having a non-standard stack format has real consequences: Rust's libbacktrace only works with ELF binaries, limiting stack traces on some platforms
money_quotes: The basic question I want to answer here is – why do people sometimes discuss 'the stack' like it's some kind of revered fundamental object?
As in most cases with the One True Anything on Linux, the One True Call Stack is the C call stack.
I found it pretty surprising that the x86 assembly instruction set knows about the C stack pointer and how the stack in C is laid out.
Of course, you could invent your own new smart stack format, but you wouldn't be able to use the callq and retq instructions to call functions.
There are consequences to having an unusual stack format, and real work you need to do to make the rest of the world understand you.
This explains why people talk about The Stack like it's this fundamental data structure instead of just one choice about how you could organize your program. It kind of is!
posts.key_insight: The stack isn't just a software abstraction — it's baked into hardware instructions, which is why it's treated as fundamental rather than as one of many possible choices.

posts.summary: Julia Evans investigates Ruby HTTP client libraries and discovers surprising default behaviors hidden in commonly used dependencies. She finds that Net::HTTP automatically retries idempotent requests (GET, PUT, DELETE) without any way to disable it, following an HTTP/1.1 RFC recommendation that may not be appropriate for programmatic API clients. She also discovers that Excon splits HTTP request headers and body into separate TCP packets, causing performance problems related to Nagle's algorithm. The post advocates for actually reading the source code of your dependencies, noting that Net::HTTP is only about 2000 lines and reviewable. Evans approaches the discoveries with curiosity rather than blame, tracing each decision back to its rationale through open source archaeology.
key_points: Ruby's Net::HTTP automatically retries idempotent HTTP requests on failure with no way to disable it, which can be dangerous for operations like executing trades
Excon splits headers and body into separate socket writes, creating two TCP packets per request and triggering Nagle's algorithm delays
The real taxonomy of Ruby HTTP libraries is just 4 base implementations (Net::HTTP, Excon, httpclient, libcurl bindings) despite there being 15-20 libraries total
Reading the actual source code of dependencies is feasible and valuable — Net::HTTP is only ~2000 lines
Open source projects make surprising decisions for understandable reasons, and the response should be to critique code rather than people
money_quotes: A HTTP library is conceptually a really simple thing – all HTTP is, for the most part, is you send some text to a server, and they send you some other text back.
Instead of having 15 or 20 libraries to think about, now there are just 4!
I was really surprised and kind of taken aback to learn this.
It makes a lot of sense for a web browser to retry, and perhaps less sense for a library you're calling from your code to automatically retry, without the ability configure it.
The scariest thing about introducing dependencies to me is – how do I know if it will surprise me unpleasantly a year from now, in production?
At work, I'd consider a 2000-line pull request somewhat arduous to review, but perhaps not totally outside the bounds of reasonableness! I can read 2000 lines of code, mostly!

It turns out that open source projects are just like all software projects – they do unexpected stuff, and then when I go find out why a person decided to do that I'm usually like 'oh, I see why you made that decision!'
Critique code instead of people – be kind to the coder, not to the code.
posts.key_insight: Dependencies hide surprising default behaviors that can cause real production problems, and the best defense is to actually read their source code and trace their design decisions back to their rationale.

posts.summary: Julia Evans frames women-only tech spaces as a programming 'hack' — a quick fix that alleviates symptoms without addressing the root architectural problem. She explains that the core bug is women being treated badly at tech meetups, and excluding men is a simple way to prevent harassment. She acknowledges the downsides, including excluding allies and creating complexity around inclusion of trans and nonbinary people. She draws on her experience organizing Montreal All-Girl Hack Night and contrasts women-only spaces with alternative approaches like the Recurse Center's community-building model. The post argues these spaces aren't ideal but are pragmatically effective at making people feel safer.
key_points: Women-only spaces are a pragmatic workaround for the deeper problem of women being treated badly at tech events
The hack has real downsides: excluding allies, requiring careful decisions about trans and nonbinary inclusion
Alternative approaches exist, like the Recurse Center's strong community norms and selective admission, or Boston Python Workshop's invitation model
The decision to keep Montreal All-Girl Hack Night women-only was driven by attendees who said they felt safer that way
money_quotes: Imagine you have a program, and it has a pretty serious issue. It needs some deep architectural changes to fix it, but you can alleviate some of the symptoms by just changing a few lines of code.
If there are no men, nobody can get harassed by men. That's it. That's the entire hack.
This has the, to me, unpleasant side effect of excluding lots of people who I would like to hang out with.
The goal is to have a fun technical meetup where nobody gets asked if they're 'really a programmer' because they're wearing makeup.
We're not trying to change the world. We're just trying to learn some things, meet some people, and have a good time.
Women-only events aren't a perfect solution, but they can be effective at making people feel safer. It's a hack and it doesn't fix the root cause of the bug, but sometimes it's good enough for now.
posts.key_insight: Women-only tech spaces are an imperfect but pragmatically effective workaround for harassment — a hack that alleviates symptoms while the deeper architectural problem remains unsolved.

posts.summary: Julia Evans investigates how the Linux perf profiling tool actually works by reading the kernel source code on a Saturday night. She discovers that hardware counters use the CPU's rdpmc instruction to provide exact counts of events like CPU instructions, with low enough overhead that the kernel runs them every context switch. For software event sampling, perf writes data to a mmap'd ring buffer in userspace, copying CPU register states at sample points. She traces the code path from the perf_event_open system call through core.c to the actual register-copying code in perf_regs.c, while honestly admitting she only understood about 10% of what she read.
key_points: Hardware performance counters use the CPU's rdpmc instruction and provide exact counts, not samples or approximations, with low enough overhead to run on every context switch
Perf's software event sampling writes data to a mmap'd ring buffer, copying all ~15 x86 registers at each sample point
The core perf implementation lives in kernel/events/core.c, a 10,000-line C file containing the perf_event_open system call
Reading kernel source code is approachable even without deep expertise — skimming 4,000 lines in 3 hours and understanding only 10% still yielded real insights
money_quotes: So it turns out I don't really know how perf works. And I like knowing how stuff works.
To answer some of these questions, we're going to read the Linux kernel source code. Because it's Saturday night.

Hardware counters are exact counters, and they're low enough overhead that the kernel can just always run rdpmc every time it's done running a piece of code. There's no sampling or approximations involved.
Files with 10,000 lines of C code are not my specialty, but I'm going to try to make something of this.
I probably skimmed like 4000 lines of Linux kernel code (the perf parts!) to write this post, in 3 hours.
I only understood probably 10% of what I looked at, but I still learned some things about how perf works internally! This is neat.
posts.key_insight: Reading kernel source code is surprisingly approachable — even understanding only 10% of what you read can teach you meaningful things about how your tools actually work.

posts.summary: Julia Evans makes the case that tcpdump is a powerful and approachable network debugging tool, despite its intimidating default output. She demonstrates a practical workflow: capture packets with tcpdump, then analyze them with Wireshark's friendly UI to diagnose problems like slow HTTP requests. She introduces pcap filter syntax for narrowing captures, discusses the overhead question for production use, and reveals tshark as a command-line tool that can decode application-layer protocols like HTTP and MongoDB. The post frames tcpdump mastery as achievable with just a few key techniques rather than deep TCP knowledge.
key_points: tcpdump's raw output is intimidating, but you don't need to read it directly — capture to a pcap file and analyze with Wireshark instead
pcap filter rules let you narrow captures to specific ports or IPs, and you only need to know a few basics to be productive
tshark extends tcpdump's usefulness by understanding application-layer protocols like HTTP and MongoDB, letting you inspect request details directly
tcpdump gives you ground truth about network traffic, which can disprove misleading application-level metrics
Production overhead is manageable for most use cases if you filter appropriately and aren't capturing massive volumes
money_quotes: The first time I ran tcpdump I took one look at some output like that, went WELP NOPE NOPE NOPE NOPE NOPE NOPE and gave up on tcpdump.
The secret here is that we can use tcpdump to record network traffic, and then use a tool that we're less scared of (Wireshark) to analyze it on our laptop after.
With tcpdump I feel really confident that it's telling me the truth about what my network traffic is up to, because that's literally its whole job.
pcap files are like freshly baked chocolate chip cookies. Everybody loves them.
Wireshark was like 'yeah those are all taking 3ms. Your metrics are wrong, or at least counting something other than just the network request!' This was a very helpful fact to know.
tshark doesn't just know HTTP; it knows like EVERYTHING. Everything that Wireshark knows.
since tshark totally understands the Mongo protocol, it immediately started printing out Mongo collection names, and I could see exactly what was going on.
posts.key_insight: tcpdump becomes a powerful and approachable debugging tool once you stop trying to read its raw output and instead capture packets to pcap files for analysis with friendlier tools like Wireshark and tshark.

posts.summary: Evans explores the fundamentals of capacity planning, starting from identifying which resources (CPU, network, disk, databases) are bottlenecks during traffic spikes. She walks through a practical approach: observe failures, check graphs, identify the constrained resource, then measure usage. She discusses the subtlety of locks as a non-scalable resource and demonstrates through simulation how random load balancing creates uneven distribution across servers. The post concludes that measuring resource usage and reasoning about capacity is more effective than blindly adding servers.
key_points: Capacity planning starts with identifying your most constrained resource—CPU, disk I/O, network, or database—usually by observing what breaks first under load
Locks are a fundamentally different kind of resource that can't be scaled by adding more servers, since they're about synchronization rather than throughput
Random load balancing creates surprisingly uneven distribution—a simulation shows one server getting half the load of another across just 7 machines
The 'power of two choices' algorithm (pick 2 random servers, send to the less loaded one) is a simple but highly effective load balancing strategy
Measuring actual resource usage and reasoning about capacity is far more effective than reactively throwing more machines at problems
money_quotes: The first step when capacity planning is to figure out which resource(s) you're the most limited by, how much of that resource you're using right now, and how much capacity have you there.
This sucks because your application has to explode but it seems pretty easy otherwise.
Locks are not a kind of resource you can just get more of, since they're about synchronization!
db7 has half as much work to do as db1! That's not fair at all!
So you're paying for machines that aren't helping you solve your problem!

I'm very slowly starting to realize that, when you're trying to make computers do a lot of stuff, there are maybe easier strategies than 'just throw more computers at it until you are no longer on fire'.
My job got quite a bit less confusing when I realized I could actually measure how much CPU I was using, and scale CPU resources accordingly.
posts.key_insight: Capacity planning becomes far less mysterious when you systematically measure which specific resource is your bottleneck and reason about usage rather than reactively adding servers.

posts.summary: Evans discovered why a Redshift query had been slow for months: a string column was taking up ~100GB on disk, requiring massive I/O on every query. Her coworker fixed it by enabling compression and changing the column from varchar(65536) to varchar(255). The post uses this as a reminder that cloud services are still bound by physical hardware constraints. Even 'magical' database-as-a-service products are just real computers with real disks and real I/O limits.
key_points: Columnar databases like Redshift only read the columns you query, but column size still matters enormously
A varchar(65536) column storing short strings wastes massive disk space and I/O compared to varchar(255) with compression
Cloud services abstract away hardware details but don't eliminate physical I/O constraints
Performance problems that persist for months are worth investigating rather than accepting as normal
money_quotes: I'd been querying this table, for months and it had been slow for months. Finally I was like "well, there has to be a reason why it's slow".
With these Magical Cloud Services it can sometimes be hard to remember that your data is just living on real computers somewhere, with real disks, and you're still bound by the normal limits of I/O.
Having less data on disk will mean you have to do less I/O, and make your queries faster! That's how disks work =D
This is one of these super obvious things (of course Magical Cloud Things are just computers and are bound by the normal laws of computers!), but I'm happy every time I'm reminded of it.
If you have a 900GB table, but the column you want is only 1GB of data, then you only need to read 1GB.
posts.key_insight: Cloud services don't eliminate physical I/O constraints — understanding how your data is stored on disk matters just as much in the cloud as anywhere else.

posts.summary: Evans describes her struggle to parallelize a computation across 32 CPU cores in Scala/JVM, expecting it to be simple but encountering a cascade of concurrency problems. She discovered threads were blocked by Scala's lazy vals using synchronized internally and Java 7's synchronized Double.parseDouble method. After fixing blocking, she found the main thread couldn't feed work fast enough, then hit OutOfMemoryError from an unbounded work queue. The post uses this experience to reflect on how thread pool abstractions are leaky compared to truly great abstractions like TCP/IP or Unix pipes, which can be used for years without understanding their internals.
key_points: Scala's lazy vals use synchronized internally, creating unexpected thread contention in parallel code
Java 7's Double.parseDouble is synchronized, meaning only one thread can parse doubles at a time — fixed in Java 8
Moving parsing into the thread pool solved the bottleneck of the main thread being too slow to feed workers, but created an unbounded queue that consumed all memory
Thread pool abstractions are leaky — unlike TCP/IP or Unix pipes, you quickly need to understand implementation details like work queues to use them effectively
Not knowing something isn't a personal failing; it's just an opportunity to learn it today
money_quotes: Double.parseDouble in Java 7 is a synchronized method. So only one thread can parse doubles from strings at a time. Really? Really.
This is like if you get a 5 year old to mix the batter for the cake when you're doing a Complicated Kitchen Thing and they're like OK OK OK OK OK OK WHAT NEXT and you're like JUST A MINUTE.
The best abstractions I work with (the TCP/IP network layer! unix pipes!) let me use them for years without understanding in the slightest how they worked.
When working with these concurrency abstractions I end up having to worry almost immediately about what's underneath because the underlying queue has filled up and crashed my program.
This kind of thing makes me feel dumb, but in a really good and awesome way. I now know a bunch of things I didn't know before about Java concurrency!
I used to feel bad when I realized I didn't know how to work with stuff like this. Now I don't really feel bad usually I'm just like WELL TODAY IS THE DAY WHEN I LEARN IT.
posts.key_insight: Thread pool abstractions are fundamentally leaky — unlike great abstractions like TCP/IP, you cannot use them effectively without understanding the underlying implementation details like synchronization, work queues, and backpressure.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her journey to solve a seemingly simple concurrency problem: submitting work to a thread pool with backpressure so it blocks when overwhelmed, like Unix pipes. After discovering that Java's standard library doesn't offer this directly, she finds an elegant solution in the book 'Java Concurrency in Practice' using a semaphore-based BoundedExecutor pattern. The post captures her excitement at discovering semaphores as a concurrency primitive and celebrates the satisfaction of finally getting parallelization working correctly.
key_points: Unix pipes provide an ideal concurrency model: an internal buffer that blocks the producer when full, preventing overload
Java's standard library surprisingly lacks a built-in way to submit to a thread pool with blocking when the pool is busy
A semaphore-based BoundedExecutor from 'Java Concurrency in Practice' elegantly solves the backpressure problem by blocking submissions when workers are all busy
Semaphores are a powerful concurrency primitive: a shared counter where acquire() blocks at zero until someone calls release()
Python's ThreadPoolExecutor is even more limited, not allowing configuration of the internal submission queue
money_quotes: I wanted to send a bunch of work to some worker threads, in parallel, have them do the work without anything exploding. That's it.
I thought a bit about how Unix pipes are one of my favorite abstractions. You pipe stuff from thing1 to thing2, it works, nothing explodes.
Pipes have an internal buffer, and when the buffer is full, thing1 is not allowed to write to the pipe any more. It blocks.
So, is there something in Java's standard library that lets you submit to a thread pool and block if the thread pool is too busy? Spoiler: no, there is not.
I didn't know what a semaphore was until I read this code and I was like OH THIS IS AMAZING AND SO USEFUL AND WOW.

A semaphore is just a shared int that you can decrement and increment! acquire() decrements, release() increments. And if it gets to 0, semaphore.acquire() will block until someone else has released it.
posts.key_insight: The best concurrency model mirrors Unix pipes — a bounded buffer with backpressure — and a simple semaphore wrapper around a thread pool achieves exactly that.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares some of her favorite technical blogs and explains what makes each one special. She highlights Rachel by the Bay for sysadmin war stories, Dan Luu for hardware writing, Aphyr for distributed systems analysis, and Camille Fournier for engineering management reflections. The common thread she identifies is that all these bloggers write about what they're currently working on or learning, taking readers along for the ride. She closes with a wish for more people to write blogs sharing their ongoing work and thinking.
key_points: The best technical blogs share the author's current work and learning process rather than polished retrospectives
Great technical bloggers cover topics at a depth that creates learning opportunities readers wouldn't find elsewhere
Following a challenging blog over time can gradually build understanding of difficult concepts like distributed systems
Linking to specific posts rather than homepages is a more useful way to recommend blogs
money_quotes: What I love about all of these blogs (and what I strive for in my own writing) is that they all (at least sometimes) write about what they're currently working on or learning about.
They're all by people who are very knowledgeable, and take you along for the ride as they're learning and experimenting.
My constant wish with blogging is for more people to write blogs where they tell me what they're working on and what they're thinking about.

I honestly struggled with reading it – the concepts he talks about are difficult to understand if you don't have a lot of experience with distributed systems. But having followed him for a couple of years now, I've learned a ton.
This post about whether or not you should use error-correcting memory is a topic I would have no idea where to start learning about if it were not for his blog.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable technical blogs are written by knowledgeable people who share what they're actively working on and learning, inviting readers along for the journey.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her discomfort with using machine learning models she doesn't understand and shares practical techniques for looking inside them. She contrasts simple logistic regression models (where you can read the coefficients) with large random forests that feel like black boxes. A colleague built a simple tool to show exactly which conditions each decision tree used for a given prediction, which immediately helped debug data quality issues and encoding mistakes. She argues that the common tradeoff framing of 'interpretability vs. performance' is a false choice, and that understanding what your models are doing is essential for making them better.
key_points: Random forests are conceptually simple despite being large — each prediction is explainable by a set of conditions and probabilities from individual trees
A simple tool showing why a random forest made a specific choice immediately revealed training data problems and feature encoding bugs like improper handling of categorical variables
The 'interpretability vs. performance tradeoff' framing is unsatisfying — you need interpretability to debug and improve model performance
One practical approach is using ML to guide domain experts rather than replacing them: experts inspect ML outputs and incorporate the smart patterns into hand-built systems
Scikit-learn ships with very few model debugging tools despite debugging being essential for building good models
money_quotes: Not understanding what my models are doing made me very uncomfortable.

People told me a lot 'well, you have a tradeoff between interpretability and how well the model performs!' But I didn't feel satisfied with that. I wanted interpretability *and* a model that performs well.
If you have a model making important decisions, and you don't clearly understand what it's doing, how do you know it's not going to blow up in your face tomorrow?

When you train a model and it doesn't perform well, how do you know how to fix it? Is it missing features? Did you use the wrong hyperparameters? You have to sort of magically intuit why it's not performing well. Magical intuition is Very Hard.
It turns out that if you just do the simplest possible thing (get the random forest to report exactly why it made the choice that it did), it's actually surprisingly helpful in helping debug!
You fundamentally don't know whether the ML is doing a thing because it's smart and correct and better than you, or because there's a bug in the data.
How can you make your models better if you have no idea what they're doing? It makes no sense to me.
posts.key_insight: You don't have to accept machine learning models as black boxes — even simple tools that show why a model made a specific decision can reveal bugs and dramatically improve your ability to build better models.

posts.summary: Evans recounts her first encounter with Java garbage collection performance problems, where a program slowed dramatically when processing 8 million lines instead of one million. Using the -XX:+PrintGCDetails JVM flag, she discovered that full GC collections were taking 1.5 seconds after only 0.2 seconds of work, effectively bringing the program to a halt. She explains the difference between fast young generation collections and slow full mark-and-sweep collections. The post introduces the concept of memory pressure: when an application uses memory close to its limit, constant garbage collection devastates performance. She concludes that sometimes the only fix is reducing memory allocation in the application itself.
key_points: The -XX:+PrintGCDetails JVM flag reveals exactly how much time garbage collection is consuming, making it an essential debugging tool
Young generation collections are fast (0.09s) while full mark-and-sweep collections can be devastatingly slow (1.5s), creating a massive performance cliff
Memory pressure occurs when an application uses memory close to its limit, forcing constant garbage collection over the same still-live objects
Increasing memory limits can speed up a program even if actual memory usage stays the same, simply by reducing GC pressure
Sometimes the only real fix is changing the application to allocate less memory, not tuning GC parameters
money_quotes: Yes, friends, I know this is news to absolutely nobody. But today I had up-close-and-personal problems with Java garbage collection for the first time so I am going to tell you about it.
PROGRAM. You can't just stop for 1.5 seconds after working for 0.2 seconds! That makes no sense
I knew GC was important but did not know that it could bring a program to a screeching halt. But apparently it can!
If your application is using 3.5GB of memory normally, and has a limit of 4G, then it may constantly need to garbage collect and slow your program waaaaaay down.
I do not have a story of redemption for you here yet.
GC tuning can become a highly skilled exercise that often requires application changes to reduce object allocation rates or object lifetimes.
Now we know that garbage collection can ruin your program's day, what memory pressure is, and that memory pressure is bad.
posts.key_insight: When a JVM application uses memory close to its limit, garbage collection can consume more time than actual computation, and the only real fix may be reducing how much memory the application allocates.

posts.summary: Julia Evans compiles resources and insights about Java garbage collection that readers shared after her previous post on the topic. She highlights blog series, official Oracle docs, books, and informative Hacker News comments as valuable learning resources. A lengthy quoted comment provides practical advice on reducing GC pressure through mindful allocation patterns and library design. Evans also notes the existence of pauseless garbage collectors and the option to switch between different GC implementations in Java.
key_points: Blogging about 'obvious' topics generates a flood of expert responses that serve as valuable learning material worth cataloging
Practical GC optimization starts with reducing unnecessary heap allocations rather than tuning collector parameters
Language and library design choices have enormous impact on GC pressure — Java's pointer-heavy object model creates more work than Go's contiguous struct arrays
Official documentation (like Oracle's GC tuning guide) is surprisingly useful and often overlooked
There are multiple garbage collector implementations available in Java, including experimental pauseless options like Shenandoah and commercial ones like Zing
money_quotes: Basically every time I write a blog post, people reply with a huge amount of information about the topic; way more than I can take in immediately. This is great. Please do not stop doing that.
No wonder people can spend their whole career just learning about Java and how to make it work well!
I love it when the awesome people I know write blog posts and it teaches me stuff. Please do this more everyone.
I often forget to check official documentation so this was a great reminder.
Writing about stuff that is pretty well-known but not obvious to newcomers is one of my favorite things.
If you already know everything I'm talking about, maybe tell me something new I don't know and make my day!
Modern GCs are incredibly fast, but that is not a magic property.
posts.key_insight: Blogging about topics you're learning creates a feedback loop where readers contribute expert knowledge far beyond what you could gather alone, making the act of public learning itself a powerful research method.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores how regular expressions are actually implemented on computers, sparked by an email about Google's RE2 and redgrep libraries. She contrasts the university textbook approach (converting regex to DFAs) with what actually happens in practice: Perl and friends use backtracking with exponential worst-case complexity, while Ken Thompson's 1960s algorithm avoids this blowup. She then introduces the surprising concept of 'regular expression derivatives' from Brzozowski's paper, where you can strip leading characters from a regex to produce a simpler regex. The redgrep library uses this derivative approach to compile regular expressions into LLVM bytecode via DFAs.
key_points: Most popular regex engines (Perl, Python, Ruby, Java) use backtracking with exponential worst-case complexity, not the theoretically clean DFA approach taught in university
Ken Thompson wrote a regex algorithm in the 1960s that avoids exponential blowup, and Google's RE2 library uses this approach because they need guaranteed performance
You can take the 'derivative' of a regular expression by stripping off a leading character, producing a simpler regex — a formal operation from Brzozowski's 1964 paper
Google's redgrep library uses repeated regex derivatives to build a DFA, then compiles it to LLVM bytecode in just a few thousand lines of C++
Creating a full DFA upfront can also cause exponential blowup, which is why Thompson's approach avoids constructing it explicitly
money_quotes: I thought I knew how regular expressions worked on computers because I took a class on them in university, but it turns out that no, I did not know.
the important thing to know is: DFAs are simple and fast and very nice. they are state machines!
at google they care about worst-case complexity because they would like their regular expressions to never explode exponentially in complexity.

When Kamal told me about taking the derivative of a string I FREAKED OUT for like 10 seconds and I was like 'omg you can't take the derivative of a string! WHERE EVEN IS THE LIMIT. OMG.' But now I have calmed down and it seems cool.
perl & friends use a different algorithm (backtracking) that has worse worst-case complexity (exponential)
I wanted to write this up because I got this email, was like 'this will take me a while to read', and writing up my understanding has helped me understand it better!
posts.key_insight: Regular expression implementations in popular languages don't use the theoretically elegant DFA approach from textbooks, and there's a fascinating alternative using 'derivatives' of regular expressions that can compile them into efficient machine code.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the non-caching benefits of CDNs after joining her company's infrastructure team. She explains how CDNs speed up TLS handshakes by terminating connections at edge servers closer to users, use private network infrastructure for smarter routing, and provide DDoS mitigation. She also discusses the security tradeoffs of handing SSL certificates to a CDN, including government surveillance concerns. The post acknowledges downsides like configuration complexity and reduced reliability from adding another dependency, concluding that CDNs are commonly used even without caching.
key_points: CDNs dramatically speed up TLS handshakes by terminating connections at edge servers, avoiding multiple speed-of-light round trips to the origin
CDNs can route traffic through private network infrastructure rather than the public internet, using peering agreements and optimized paths
Giving a CDN your SSL certificate creates real security tradeoffs, including potential government data access through the CDN provider
Performance and reliability are inseparable — a site that takes 3 minutes to respond isn't meaningfully 'up'
Adding a CDN as a dependency can actually reduce reliability: two 99.9% uptime services chained together yield only 99.8% uptime
money_quotes: You are in New York, and the speed of light is slow (really!), and you are trying to send the javascript file to Sydney.
No public internet for you! You can buy MAGICAL FANCY INTERNET for your users to get to your server on.
As an extreme example, if your site takes 3 minutes to respond, you cannot truly fairly say that it is 'up'.
I used to think this was weird paranoia but I'm pretty sure by now we all know it's a real thing to worry about.
Nothing with computers is all butterflies & flowers and now we get to talk about a couple more disadvantages.
The more I learn about computers the more I learn to be skeptical of services other people are selling you.
It is apparently a pretty normal thing to put a site behind a CDN without caching it! Who knew? Not me, before today.
posts.key_insight: CDNs provide significant performance and reliability benefits beyond caching — particularly TLS termination and smart routing — but introduce real tradeoffs in security, configuration complexity, and dependency risk.

posts.summary: Evans explores why knowledge gets trapped in individuals' heads at work and how teams can spread expertise more broadly. She identifies discovery as a major problem — knowing who knows what is surprisingly difficult even among close colleagues. She examines why system builders become permanent experts and why others struggle to develop the same depth, pointing to historical context and a sense of entitlement to understanding. She proposes several practical solutions: directing questions to new people to build them into 'caches' of knowledge, apprenticeship models, internal talks, and the magical tactic of asking 'how did you find that out?' after someone solves a problem.
key_points: Discovery is a major barrier: people often have no idea what expertise their coworkers hold, from previous professional experience to open source projects to self-study
The 'person builds system, person is expert forever' pattern is the default and it's harmful — it creates bus factors and prevents others from developing expertise
Answering questions is itself an investment in expertise — putting someone in a question-answering rotation forces them to build and verify their understanding
The One True Expert must sometimes deliberately hold back from answering, even when it would be faster, to let others develop
Asking 'how did you find that out?' immediately after someone solves a problem is one of the most effective ways to extract tacit knowledge
money_quotes: There are a bajillion people who know amazing things, and often that knowledge gets stuck in their head and other people don't end up learning it. For years, sometimes!
I kind of feel like telling someone that they can't understand a system or that it's 'too hard' is like… a crime. We should all expect to be able to understand the software systems that we work with!
You can build people who are 'caches' for information – put them in charge of answering questions, they slowly start to accumulate knowledge and eventually become experts.
If everyone always asks Person A questions about the System, then that is itself an organizational investment in Person A's knowledge.
The One True Expert has a role here too – they need to sometimes hold off on answering questions and doing debugging work, even if it would be faster for them to do it.
This is a MAGICAL TACTIC: 'how did you find that out?' Often experts don't realize what they know, but if you ask them what they did FIVE MINUTES AFTER THEY DID IT they can usually remember.
I basically think this is a travesty. People occasionally run internal talks about topics they know about which is THE BEST.
posts.key_insight: Knowledge gets locked in people's heads not because they're unwilling to share, but because discovery of who knows what is broken, and organizations default to single experts instead of deliberately investing in building new ones through question-routing, apprenticeships, and extracting tacit knowledge.

posts.summary: Julia Evans highlights Etsy's quarterly site performance reports published on their Code as Craft blog, praising them as a model of transparency and engineering rigor. She values their public commitment to performance, detailed graphs, explanations of what internal changes caused specific improvements, and honest discussion of measurement tool tradeoffs. She compiles an index of all historical performance reports dating back to 2011. She also notes that the Etsy performance team is mostly women, with Lara Hogan as Senior Engineering Manager, and links to related talks and resources on web performance culture.
key_points: Etsy's quarterly public performance reports represent a valuable model of transparent engineering culture, combining graphs, root cause analysis, and measurement methodology discussion
Both backend and frontend performance are covered, with Lara Hogan's work on frontend performance specifically highlighted
Public commitments to performance create accountability and drive continuous improvement
The Etsy performance team being majority-women and women-led is presented as both noteworthy and a normal, reasonable thing
money_quotes: It's a public commitment to having good site performance.
They explain what internal changes caused that 25ms drop, and they talk in depth about their measurement tools and what the advantages & disadvantages are of each one.
I would love to make a performance report like this at work.
Of course having majority-women teams and teams led by women is a totally normal & reasonable thing but it made me happy to see.
posts.key_insight: Publishing regular, transparent performance reports with detailed graphs and root cause analysis creates a powerful public accountability mechanism for engineering teams.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her notes from running a workshop teaching coworkers how to use tcpdump and strace for debugging. The tcpdump section progresses from basic packet capture on localhost to analyzing production traffic in Wireshark for performance debugging. The strace section covers reading system call output, finding configuration files, attaching to running processes, and diagnosing performance problems. She reflects that the hour-long format worked for experienced users but was too fast for beginners, and plans to iterate on the workshop format.
key_points: tcpdump is a powerful general-purpose debugging tool for web services since almost all services communicate via network requests
Wireshark complements tcpdump by making packet analysis much more accessible - pcap files are a universal format that every packet analysis tool understands
strace reveals how programs interact with the OS, making it useful for discovering configuration files, understanding performance problems, and debugging running processes
Production use of these tools requires caution: tcpdump can fill disks and capture customer data, while strace will slow down running programs
Preparing good workshop material is hard, and giving the same talk multiple times is valuable because you learn so much each time
money_quotes: We do a lot of web development; almost all of our services talk to each other with network requests. So a tool that can spy on network requests can be a really good general-purpose debugging tool.
pcap files are like chocolate cookies – every packet analysis tool understands how to read them.

as you've seen, tcpdump output is a little difficult to interpret and search. some people are really good at that but IMO it's easier to stick with a basic knowledge of tcpdump and do more advanced stuff in Wireshark
if the client sends a packet and then the server takes 5 seconds to reply to it, that's AWESOME EVIDENCE
we have no idea how it's configured! How will we ever find out? STRACE OBVIOUSLY
STRACING PRODUCTION PROGRAMS WILL SLOW THEM DOWN AND YOU NEED TO BE VERY CAREFUL. Sometimes you do it anyway if the process is a mess
I want to get better at doing workshops and talks at least 2-3 times because preparing good material is so hard, and I always learn so much about the talk/workshop the first time I give it.
posts.key_insight: tcpdump and strace are essential debugging tools that let you spy on network traffic and system calls respectively, turning opaque production problems into visible, diagnosable evidence.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares how a coworker's lunchtime explanation corrected her misconception that RSA is used to directly encrypt messages in SSL/TLS. She learned that RSA is too slow for bulk encryption, so in practice it's used only to exchange session keys for faster symmetric ciphers like AES. This distinction between key exchange and bulk encryption is the core of how SSL cipher suites work. Understanding this made conversations about insecure TLS ciphers suddenly click for her.
key_points: RSA is not used to encrypt messages directly in practice — it's too slow for bulk encryption and is instead used for key exchange or authentication
SSL/TLS uses a fast symmetric cipher (like AES) with a randomly generated session key for actual message encryption
Cipher suite names encode the full negotiation: key exchange method, authentication, cipher, and hash function
A secure key exchange is worthless if the chosen symmetric cipher is weak — attackers can just break the cipher directly
money_quotes: It turns out that no, you do not use RSA to encrypt messages in practice, which is what Cory told me and what surprised me so much.
If you're developing the SSL protocol, you want encryption to be pretty fast. RSA is super secure but not very fast. So what do you do?
If your server chooses a bad cipher to communicate with, it doesn't matter that the way you decided on that key is really good and totally secure!
They can just break the cipher and read your SSL traffic.
I made some offhand remark like 'I know basically nothing about security', which is true. Then at lunch Cory was like 'hey, you said you knew nothing about security! I have some facts for you!'
posts.key_insight: RSA isn't used to encrypt your actual messages in SSL — it's only used to securely exchange a session key for a faster symmetric cipher, which is why the choice of that cipher matters so much for security.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her second attempt at learning Rust, finding it dramatically easier than her first try in 2013. She credits both the language's maturation and a new learning strategy of pair programming with an experienced Rust developer, letting him handle the parts she didn't understand. She argues Rust is easier to learn than C for systems programming, noting that common tasks like hashmaps and string formatting are surprisingly approachable. She highlights Rust's helpful error messages, good documentation, and compatibility with tools like valgrind and perf.
key_points: Rust has matured significantly since 2013 and now feels closer to writing Python than struggling with a systems language
Pair programming with an expert and selectively ignoring complexity you don't yet understand is an effective learning strategy
Common programming tasks (hashmaps, regex, string formatting) are far easier in Rust than in C, making it a better entry point for systems programming
Rust's error messages, official documentation, and investment in developer experience (like paying Steve Klabnik to write docs) make a real difference
Rust programs work seamlessly with systems tools like valgrind and perf, with debug info available for profiling
money_quotes: I think he's right that learning Rust is easier than learning C.
Those things would have been really hard in C (how do you even make a hashmap??? I think you have to write the data structure yourself or something.).

I kind of enjoy the experience of having a Magical Oracle to fix my programming problems for me – having someone elide away the harder stuff so I can focus on what's easy feels to me like a good way to learn.
Of course, you can't let someone else fix all your hard programs forever.
But for today I only understand like 6 things and that's fine.
Sometimes they link to GitHub issues, and someone on the GitHub issue will have a workaround for your problem!
posts.key_insight: You don't need to understand everything about a language to start being productive in it — selectively ignoring complexity and relying on experts for the hard parts is a valid and effective learning strategy.

posts.summary: Julia Evans investigates the Erlang virtual machine by comparing the system calls of 'hello world' programs written in C, Python, Java, and Erlang. She discovers that Erlang generates roughly 15,000 system calls compared to Java's 2,700, Python's 1,550, and C's 38. The types of system calls differ dramatically too: while other languages mostly do file operations and memory allocation, Erlang's top calls are futex and sched_yield, reflecting its concurrent nature. She also notices mysterious behaviors like Erlang binding to a TCP socket just to print hello world. The post demonstrates how system call analysis can reveal fundamental differences in language runtime design.
key_points: Erlang's hello world generates 15,043 system calls compared to Java's 2,699, Python's 1,550, and C's 38
Erlang's dominant system calls (futex, sched_yield, ppoll) reflect concurrency primitives rather than the file I/O and memory allocation calls typical of other languages
Erlang inexplicably binds to a TCP socket and probes NUMA node directories just to run hello world, suggesting deep runtime infrastructure
System call analysis can reveal fundamental architectural differences between language runtimes without knowing the language itself
Familiarity with Linux debugging tools (strace) provides leverage even when investigating unfamiliar programming languages
money_quotes: I often use strace to see what system calls it runs. In my few experiments so far, the Erlang virtual machine runs a TON of system calls and I'm not sure exactly what it's doing.
I was surprised that the Erlang VM runs 6 times as many system calls as Java – I think of Java as already being pretty heavyweight.

The top 2 syscalls for the Erlang process are futex and sched_yield. So there's a lot of synchronization happening, and the operating system threads Erlang starts up keep scheduling themselves off the CPU.
So Erlang seems like a programming language with really significantly different primitives.
Why does it need to listen on a TCP socket to run hello world?
I think it's interesting to take an operating systems-level approach to understanding what a program is doing and I thought this was a cool example of that.
I was looking at this Erlang problem with Victor and David and they had OS X machines and I was like 'dude I can't debug anything on OS X'.
posts.key_insight: Examining a program's system calls can reveal the fundamental design philosophy of its runtime, even if you've never written a line of code in that language.

posts.summary: Evans describes her delightful experience submitting a pull request to the Rust libc project, where two bots made the process smooth and welcoming. The highfive bot automatically greeted her and assigned a reviewer, while homu (bors) handled test coordination and merging after reviewer approval. She highlights how these bots reduce maintainer burden, especially for repositories with long or flaky test suites. The post is a brief appreciation of how automation can make open source contribution more pleasant for everyone involved.
key_points: The rust-highfive bot automatically welcomes contributors and assigns reviewers, creating a friendly first impression
Homu (bors) automates the test-and-merge workflow so maintainers don't have to babysit CI, especially valuable when tests take an hour or are flaky
Simple automation like greeting bots and merge bots can dramatically improve the open source contribution experience
Homu is the second iteration of bors, originally created by Graydon Hoare for the Rust project
money_quotes: Someone described my approach to blogging as 'fanfiction' recently, a description that I kind of loved.
A lot of the time I write about things that I find in the world that love and my take on them.
Basically he's saying 'this looks reasonable; fine with me as long as the tests pass!'
Homu's job is to make it so that you don't have to keep checking to see if the tests pass!
This is a huge blessing on this particular repository because the tests take like an hour.
posts.key_insight: Simple bots that greet contributors and automate test-and-merge workflows can transform the open source contribution experience from tedious to delightful.

posts.summary: Evans argues that despite machine learning being powerful and exciting, people should maintain healthy skepticism about its outputs. She identifies three key reasons for skepticism: models are limited by their training data and its biases, ML programs can contain ordinary software bugs that get mistaken for sophisticated behavior, and even well-built models frequently produce wrong results. She uses relatable examples like predicting nationality from names and Google Photos misclassifying images to illustrate these points. The post encourages non-specialists to question ML results rather than deferring to the technology's perceived authority.
key_points: Machine learning models inherit the biases and gaps of their training data, so underrepresented populations get poor results
ML programs are still software and have regular bugs, which people sometimes excuse as the model being 'cool and smart'
Even bug-free models trained on good data will produce confidently wrong individual predictions
Non-specialists questioning ML outputs are often right that something is wrong, and their skepticism should be encouraged
ML systems can appear objective but are not immune to producing absurd or biased results
money_quotes: machine learning models are only as good as the data you put into them
if you don't include any data from China when training your computer to recognize names, it's not going to get any Chinese names right!

someone's said to me 'hey julia, why did the machine learning make this decision? it seems wrong?' and I've said 'oh, you know, machine learning! it's probably doing something cool and smart'. But then I look into it anyway. And then, rather than something cool and smart, the program I work on just had a bug.
I was really happy that the people asking the questions didn't assume that the machine learning was Correct and Smart, but that instead told us 'hey, this looks wrong'
just because something came out of a statistical model it isn't 'objective' or even necessarily correct
Machine learning is super powerful and you can build models that do amazing things, but they can also tell you that a donald trump pinata is a picture of a baby, and that makes no sense.
posts.key_insight: Machine learning outputs deserve the same skepticism as any other software output — they can reflect biased data, contain bugs, or simply be wrong, and non-experts questioning results are often right to do so.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares notes and resources from her PyData Berlin keynote about tricking neural networks and the dangers of black box models. She covers adversarial examples that fool neural networks, fun art projects using neural nets, and strategies for making ML models more interpretable. She emphasizes that reproducing cool ML research results is achievable with investment of time. The talk also addressed unintended consequences of ML systems and technical debt in production ML.
key_points: Adversarial examples can trick neural networks using surprisingly simple techniques, and the underlying math is accessible through papers like 'Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples'
Cool ML research results like training models to play games are actually reproducible if you invest time reading and understanding the papers
Black box ML models can have dangerous unintended effects, and the problem of interpretability is far from solved
Several practical tools and approaches exist for making black box models more interpretable, including LIME and Bayesian rule list classifiers
money_quotes: I became more convinced at this conference that sometimes people do really cool things (like training a machine learning model to play space invaders), and those things are actually possible to reproduce for yourself if you read the paper and invest a bunch of time!
I think it can often take like 2 months to fully read and understand the paper and get it to work, but it's not impossible, which is cool.
This problem of how to make a complicated machine learning model more interpretable definitely isn't solved, and I'd love to have more conversations about it.
Machine Learning: the high interest credit card of technical debt
We talked about how machine learning sometimes doesn't work or can have unintended effects.
posts.key_insight: Impressive ML research results are more reproducible than people assume, but the field still lacks good solutions for making black box models interpretable and safe.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her approach to tech blogging, explaining her motivations evolved from job-hunting strategy to learning tool and community builder. She advocates for writing short posts quickly (1-3 hours), publishing frequently without excessive editing, and maintaining a low bar for what counts as 'interesting enough' to write about. She addresses the fear of admitting ignorance publicly, arguing it actually accelerates learning. Her core philosophy treats blog posts as small lottery tickets — produce many, keep them short, and let volume do the work.
key_points: Writing short posts (under 500 words) with high frequency is more effective than polishing long-form pieces, and the volume increases the odds of producing something great
Admitting what you don't know in public writing accelerates learning because people help fill your gaps, and the fear of looking ignorant is mostly unfounded
The litmus test for whether something is worth writing about is simple: would this have helped me a year ago, or last week, or be interesting to at least two other people?
Maintaining an aggressively positive tone serves both as genuine expression and as a defensive tactic against internet negativity
Blogging compounds over time — 200 posts exist permanently and continue providing value long after they're written
money_quotes: I sit down and write something and don't stop until I've published it.
Producing things that you think are good is like buying a lottery ticket. So I guess I try to make good things and buy many small lottery tickets.
Basically I have a pretty low bar for interestingness and it mostly works out fine.
Nobody ever thought I was a magical wizard who knows everything in the first place.
Telling people what I don't know means that they can help me learn it sooner!
On the balance I think I'd happy be known for being curious and knowledgeable and a good source of information & questions and that I don't need to be a magical wizard oracle.
I also think it's extremely fun and interesting when regular people (not Programming Wizards) just write about their regular work and what they're learning right now.
posts.key_insight: Prolific, honest blogging that embraces ignorance and keeps a low bar for interestingness compounds into career capital and genuine learning far more effectively than polished, infrequent writing.

posts.summary: Evans presents a simple framework for tackling hard programming problems, breaking them into three approaches: repurposing existing powerful software, stealing ideas from academic papers and implementing them yourself, and coming up with genuinely new ideas (academic research). She argues that the first approach is the most accessible and fun, using examples like integer linear programming and dtrace. She also highlights the special value of research labs that both invent and ship usable software, combining the best of all approaches.
key_points: Many 'hard' problems become easy when you discover existing powerful software that can be repurposed for your use case
Academic papers contain implementable ideas that often don't require much code — the hard part is knowing they exist
Research labs that publish both ideas and usable software (like vowpal wabbit) provide outsized value by collapsing the idea-to-implementation gap
The skills needed to do research and the skills needed to write usable software are different, which is why this combination is rare and valuable
money_quotes: I sorta think of this blog as an extended fanzine for existing software.
HEY DID YOU KNOW THAT SOMEONE WROTE EXTREMELY POWERFUL SOFTWARE???? I wrote a cool small wrapper to make that software do a new thing!
Sometimes big ideas don't take that much code to go implement!
Bad hackers copy, great hackers steal.
They're like 'we do not have enough great ideas yet! TIME TO GENERATE MORE IDEAS'.
The people who think of the idea and the people who implement it can be in the same office which is more efficient.
posts.key_insight: Most hard programming problems don't require inventing something new — they require discovering that powerful existing software or well-documented ideas already solve your problem.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her evolving relationship with design documents, moving from grumpiness about writing them to finding them genuinely useful. She identifies common frustrations — nobody reads them, feedback can be unconstructive bikeshedding, and they can't predict everything. Her key shift was reframing design docs as personal thinking tools and conversation starters rather than formal artifacts requiring thorough review. She also values them as archaeological records that explain past decisions when revisited months later.
key_points: Design documents work better as personal preparation for productive conversations than as formal review artifacts people are expected to read thoroughly
Getting useful feedback requires targeted asks — directing specific questions to specific people rather than broadcasting the whole document
The inability to predict everything in advance doesn't invalidate design docs; they're about doing your best thinking upfront, not achieving perfection
Design documents serve as valuable archaeological artifacts that explain why decisions were made when revisited months or years later
Asking more people for feedback isn't always better — sometimes one or two reviewers is enough for a small project
money_quotes: I have written extensive notes so that I can make sure to ask you all the most important questions I have and we can have a SUPER PRODUCTIVE MEETING.
This idea that you can predict all the flaws in advance and design the perfect system is actually a total lie.
The reason you write them is because your requirements & ideas of how things should work will change over time, and it's useful to track that.
Often I'll make a design decision, forget about it, and then 8 months later be like 'ugh who made that decision and why???'. And then it was me.
Design documents are cool as archaeological artifacts so that people from the future can understand you and why you decided what you did.
Asking more people for feedback isn't always better. Sometimes asking just one or two people is ok if it's a small thing.
Hey just read this for 10 minutes and see if anything makes you EXTREMELY TERRIFIED AND WORRIED.
posts.key_insight: Design documents are most valuable not as formal review artifacts but as personal thinking tools, conversation starters, and archaeological records of why decisions were made.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores how individual speakers can give better conference talks and how organizers can build better conferences. She argues that good talks must teach the audience something new in an understandable way, and offers concrete strategies: thinking deeply about your audience, keeping talks short, getting honest feedback, and talking about things you didn't build. She advocates for 'hard talks' that push audience knowledge while remaining accessible to multiple skill levels. For organizers, she suggests lightning talk tracks, pairing speakers for practice, writing desired talk ideas on websites, and actively recruiting speakers from other communities.
key_points: A good talk is one where the audience learns something new or gains a change in perspective — clarity matters more than polish
Short talks (10 minutes) set speakers up for success by forcing them to cut everything that isn't essential
Talks about things you didn't build are more credible and give you a wider range of interesting topics to choose from
Hard, advanced talks are risky but proportionally rewarding — they tell newer audience members what concepts exist and what experts think is important
Conference organizers should actively recruit speakers, suggest talk topics, and provide institutional support rather than passively accepting submissions
money_quotes: After you go once or twice you don't really go to the talks any more – you won't learn anything. Just hang out in the hallway!
I don't mind too much how many times you say 'um' or how nice your slides are.
If you tell someone 'hey, pick an extremely interesting topic and prepare 10 minutes of material on it', they're set up for success. You can't put in irrelevant or boring stuff! There's no time!
Talking about someone else's work is kinda magical because instead of discussing the small subset of software I've built, I get to pick from ALL THE SOFTWARE IN THE UNIVERSE.
It's super important to be able to watch talks that are somewhat above your current level – they tell you what other concepts exist, and what people with more expertise think is important.

People are terrible at figuring out what cool stuff they know. Awful. I often tell someone 'hey you know this thing that is awesome' and they're like 'wait really? doesn't everyone know that? are you sure???'
If you accept truly difficult talks by speakers who can make the content accessible to enough people, then I think you can end up with a conference that people love going to every year.
posts.key_insight: The best conference talks teach the audience something genuinely new in an understandable way, and both speakers and organizers should actively optimize for learning rather than polish.

posts.summary: Julia Evans discovers the new -y flag in strace after upgrading from Ubuntu 12.04 to 16.04. The flag resolves file descriptor numbers into human-readable filenames in strace output, making it much easier to understand what files a program is reading from and writing to. She demonstrates this by tracing cp and mv commands, showing how cp reads and writes 128KB chunks between files while mv is just a single rename system call. She also highlights the -yy flag which resolves network socket file descriptors into IP addresses and ports.
key_points: The strace -y flag resolves numeric file descriptors into human-readable filenames, eliminating a major usability pain point
Tracing cp reveals it reads and writes 128KB chunks at a time, while mv is just a single rename() system call - explaining the speed difference
The -yy flag goes further by resolving network socket descriptors into source/destination IP addresses and ports
Usability improvements to established developer tools are valuable and worth celebrating
money_quotes: It's important to know about file descriptors, but when I tell people about strace, I always feel like it's kind of annoying! Why can't strace just tell me which file it wrote to, instead of hiding it behind some number?
That's just one system call! No wonder moving is so fast and copying is so slow :)
I love seeing programs like strace get usability improvements!
posts.key_insight: The strace -y flag is a significant usability improvement that replaces opaque file descriptor numbers with human-readable filenames, making system call tracing far more accessible.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores how to build a 'jstack for Ruby' — a tool that can inspect a running Ruby program's stack trace without pre-instrumentation. She walks through using gdb to read Ruby's internal data structures and extract stack frames from memory, then explains why gdb's ptrace-based approach is too heavyweight. She discovers the process_vm_readv system call, which can read another process's memory with minimal impact, and builds a prototype Rust program that samples Ruby stack traces every 10 milliseconds to generate flame graphs.
key_points: Java and C have built-in tools (jstack, perf top) to inspect running programs without pre-instrumentation, but Ruby and Python lack equivalent tooling
gdb can extract Ruby stack traces by traversing internal structs (rb_thread_t, rb_control_frame_t, rb_iseq_t), but uses ptrace which is slow and stops the target process
The process_vm_readv system call can read another process's memory directly without passing through kernel space, with much less impact on the running program
Building a debugger requires two things: debugging info (DWARF) to understand struct layouts, and the ability to read target process memory
She built a working Rust prototype that samples Ruby stack traces every 10ms and generates flame graphs, though it has hardcoded struct types
money_quotes: One of the most frustrating things to me is – when I run a Ruby or Python program, I can't find out what that program is doing RIGHT NOW.
IF JAVA CAN HAVE NICE THINGS THEN WHY NOT RUBY AND PYTHON??
When you attach to a program with gdb, it seems like basically black magic, but as usual it's actually not magic at all.
This is amazing, because we started with practically nothing – just an address of the current thread! And we finished with a file and a place we are in that file.

The reason this is awesome and better than what gdb does is – as far as I can tell, the impact of process_vm_readv on the running process is WAY SMALLER. You just spy on the memory and get out of the way!
Instead of feeling bad that I haven't actually gotten the software to work yet on other peoples' computers, I thought I would take a couple of hours and tell you how it works!
posts.key_insight: You can build lightweight debugging tools for any language runtime by combining DWARF debug info with process_vm_readv to read process memory without stopping the target program.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores whether Unix signals should be feared, after being told by experienced developers that they shouldn't be. She explains how signal handlers work at the kernel level, then catalogs the specific dangers: interrupted system calls returning EINTR, concurrency issues since signal handlers can't safely use locks or malloc, and the restrictions on what functions are safe to call. She surveys important programs that use signals successfully (init, terminals, the JVM, unicorn) and examines signalfd as an alternative approach. She concludes that signals are not trivial but are manageable with care, and advocates replacing vague fear with specific knowledge of what can go wrong.
key_points: Signal handlers create implicit concurrency that's harder to reason about than threads, since the handler interrupts paused code and can't even wait on locks that code holds
Many critical programs rely heavily on signals: init uses SIGINT for restart, terminals use SIGWINCH for resizing, shells use SIGCHLD, and the JVM uses signals for stop-the-world GC
Linux's signalfd offers an alternative that makes signal handling more explicit by turning signals into file descriptor events, though it has its own problems
Profiling tools like stackprof receive hundreds of signals per second via setitimer, which raises concerns about IO interruption overhead
The correct response to scary technical topics is replacing vague fear with specific knowledge of what can actually go wrong
money_quotes: When you get sent SIGKILL nobody communicates with you, you just die immediately.
Surprise! now you have a concurrent program! you need to be very careful if you modify anything in your signal handler code because WHO KNOWS what state that thing was in before you changed it.
It is perhaps telling that functions are by default unsafe to call from signal handlers.

I like this because it means you have a concurrent program, but it's much more explicit that you're running a concurrent program instead of having a weird signal handler that exists outside of space and time.
It's unreasonable to say 'threads are hard! never use threads! it is scary'. Threads are important! Sometimes you need to use them! It is possible to write correct threaded programs!
Instead it is better to say 'this code is not thread safe because of SPECIFIC REASON! it will not work!'.
I don't like having Fears and Uncertainties about things that I can't base in fact.
posts.key_insight: The right response to a scary technical concept like Unix signals isn't avoidance but understanding the specific things that can go wrong, turning vague fear into actionable knowledge.

posts.summary: Julia describes using ltrace for the first time to debug a memory leak in a Rust async library that was leaking 500 bytes per HTTP request. She explains that ltrace traces library calls like malloc and free, which don't go through the kernel. After valgrind proved unhelpful with its complex stack traces, ltrace revealed allocations that were never freed. By combining ltrace output with strategic print statements, they narrowed the leak to specific .boxed() calls in the Rust code that allocated memory on the heap.
key_points: Memory allocation with malloc and free happens in userspace, not through the kernel, making ltrace the right tool to observe them
Valgrind produced overwhelming stack traces that weren't actionable, while ltrace gave a simple, readable view of allocations and frees
The debugging strategy combined ltrace's allocation tracking with print statements to narrow down exactly which code was leaking
In Rust, .boxed() and Box::new allocate on the heap, and even Rust's ownership model doesn't prevent leaks when references are accidentally kept alive
money_quotes: ltrace traces library calls. This is cool because there are a lot of important things that happen that don't go through the kernel!
One thing that took me a while to learn is that memory allocation with malloc and free aren't something that your operating system handles.

We tried using valgrind to find the memory leak, but that was not super successful. It was like 'dude here are some huge stack traces in this library you don't really understand' and we were like 'I don't know what that means'.
Then I thought – ltrace! I know what ltrace is!
We used grep to find out that 0xf4d010… never got freed! A MEMORY LEAK! WE WIN.
