There is probably a smart and clever way to figure what the 32 bytes are. Instead, we added print statements to our program to try to find the leak.
I'm always really happy when I make progress a bug using a thing in my toolkit I haven't used properly before. Yay ltrace!
posts.key_insight: ltrace provides a simple, readable view of memory allocations that can make finding memory leaks straightforward when more sophisticated tools like valgrind produce overwhelming output.

posts.summary: Evans explores why high-performance networking projects bypass the Linux kernel's TCP stack in favor of userspace implementations. Inspired by a talk about the Lwan web server and Seastar framework, she investigates through Twitter crowd-sourcing how companies like Google (Maglev) and high-frequency trading firms use userspace networking for massive speed gains. The post covers DPDK's polling approach, the locking overhead that makes kernel TCP slow, and the surprising insight that performance isn't the only reason — evolving TCP standards can't wait for kernel updates. She catalogs numerous open-source userspace TCP stacks while noting none are truly general-purpose yet.
key_points: Seastar's custom TCP stack, built on Intel's DPDK, achieves several times the performance of the kernel's TCP stack by polling the network card instead of using interrupts
The kernel's TCP stack is slow primarily due to locking and shared data contention, as Van Jacobsen explained — pushing work toward applications follows the end-to-end principle
Performance isn't the only motivation: Google needs userspace TCP to deploy new features like TCP Fast Open without waiting for Android vendors to update kernels
High-frequency trading firms buy specialized hardware and userspace TCP stacks (like Solarflare's OpenOnload) for maximum networking speed
No general-purpose open-source userspace TCP/IP stack exists yet, though many specialized ones (mtcp, lwIP, Seastar, libuinet) are available
money_quotes: Most of the time is spent talking to the Linux kernel and copying things back and forth.
The TCP standard is evolving, and if you have to always use your kernel's TCP stack, that means you can NEVER EVOLVE.

The key to better networking scalability, says Van, is to get rid of locking and shared data as much as possible, and to make sure that as much processing work as possible is done on the CPU where the application is running.
I think with DPDK you can write networking applications that work entirely in userspace with no system calls.

Of course practically nobody needs to do 2 million requests per second. I sure don't. But thinking about high performance computing is a really awesome way to understand the limits of computers better!
There are like 100 interesting rabbit holes which I have zero time to investigate right now!
posts.key_insight: The Linux kernel's TCP stack trades raw performance for generality, and bypassing it with userspace networking unlocks massive speed gains by eliminating locking, context switches, and memory copies — though no general-purpose alternative exists yet.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her five favorite Linux debugging tools: strace, dstat, tcpdump/wireshark, perf, and opensnoop. She frames them as a swiss army knife that works across any programming language when something on your system is wrong or slow. The post gives brief descriptions of each tool's purpose and links to deeper resources, including her own prior writing. She emphasizes practical utility over theoretical completeness, noting she's used nearly all of them to debug actual problems.
key_points: Five core debugging tools (strace, dstat, tcpdump/wireshark, perf, opensnoop) form a versatile toolkit applicable across any programming language
strace is her favorite tool overall but comes with the caveat that it can significantly slow down programs
perf top provides a live sampling profiler view that's useful even for non-C programs, and can generate flame graphs
opensnoop represents a new generation of eBPF-based tools that are faster than strace for specific tasks
Having basic networking knowledge (IP vs TCP vs HTTP, what's a packet) is achievable and makes tcpdump/wireshark much more useful
money_quotes: I think of these tools like a swiss army knife – if something on your system is wrong or slow, in any programming language, knowing how to use them can really help you out.
strace is pretty broadly useful, but keep in mind it can really slow down your programs.
I like to use perf top no matter what language my programs are written in, just to see if I can understand anything from it. Sometimes it works!
Luckily the basics ('what's the difference between IP and TCP and HTTP? what's a network packet?') are totally possible to pick up :D.
It just shows you which files are being opened right now on your computer. And it's fast, unlike strace!
You don't need to know how it works to use it, though :).
posts.key_insight: A small set of system-level debugging tools — strace, dstat, tcpdump, perf, and opensnoop — forms a universal toolkit that works across any programming language to diagnose what's wrong or slow on a Linux system.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at PolyConf 2016, a multilingual programming conference in Poland. She highlights talks on Elm, Erlang, Datomic, high-performance HTTP servers, and Guile Scheme, while appreciating both the enthusiastic and critical perspectives presented. She shares her own talk on systems programming and proposes 'rules of programming experiments' that encourage learning through unfinished, imperfect projects. The post celebrates the value of conferences for meeting people and encountering unfamiliar ideas across programming language boundaries.
key_points: Erlang's process model—where each green thread has dedicated memory and supervisors kill misbehaving processes—represents a fundamentally different approach to error handling
Critical talks about technology failures (like Datomic in production) are as valuable as enthusiastic ones because they save others from repeating mistakes
Small, purpose-built tools can unlock unexpected creative possibilities, as shown by a choose-your-own-adventure project that grew from 8 to 200+ videos
Programming experiments should prioritize learning over completion or correctness
Elm's beginner-friendly design was validated by Julia successfully writing a working program during the talk itself
money_quotes: Talking about it in public means that other people can learn from your experience instead of always having to start from scratch.

I think it's incredibly important to talk critically about databases since it's so hard to change your database, we all have a limited amount of time to experiment, and database integrity is so important.
Rules of programming experiments: 1. it doesn't have to work 2. you don't have to finish it 3. you have to learn something

There was a lot of 'hey! look at this technology! it is amazing!' at this conference. This is always nice because it's great to be excited. But with programming things inevitably go badly sometimes, and sometimes I really like to hear about that.
I think Erlang is more like an operating system than most programming languages are.

I think about how to say negative things in a constructive way a lot – it's appropriate to say 'hey this is cool and I liked it!' about practically anything, but, when should you say that something didn't work well?
posts.key_insight: The best conference talks balance enthusiasm for new technology with honest, critical accounts of what doesn't work, because sharing failures publicly saves everyone from learning the hard way.

posts.summary: Evans explains how web servers route HTTP requests to the right site using the Host header, then builds up to the more complex problem of TLS/SNI. She starts by showing how to make raw HTTP requests with netcat, demonstrating that the Host header is the simple mechanism behind virtual hosting. She then explains why HTTPS breaks this model—the Host header is encrypted, so the server can't read it before choosing a certificate—and how SNI solves this by including the hostname in the unencrypted TLS Client Hello packet. She verifies her understanding by reading nginx source code, discovering that virtual server lookup happens twice per TLS request.
key_points: The HTTP Host header is the mechanism that lets a single web server host multiple sites—it's the only part of the request that identifies which site you want
With HTTPS, the Host header is encrypted inside the TLS session, creating a chicken-and-egg problem: the server needs to know which certificate to use before it can read the Host header
SNI (Server Name Indication) solves this by putting the hostname in the unencrypted TLS Client Hello packet, letting the server pick the right certificate before decryption
In nginx, virtual server lookup happens twice per TLS request: once for SNI (to pick the certificate) and once for the Host header (to pick the response)
Understanding the fundamentals of HTTP made previously opaque web server configuration suddenly obvious
money_quotes: The difference between 17-year old Julia and 25-year-old Julia is that 25-year-old Julia knew how HTTP works.
If you remove the Host: part from this request, it does not work. Zero. Apache is all '400 Bad request'. 400 means it is your fault.

I like this because once you know what a HTTP request looks like – it becomes kind of obvious how a web server would decide where to send the request. There are only 4 things in it and only one of them is julia.com!
So the problem is that you need to choose which certificate you're going to use before you see the Host header.

SNI is a standard where, when getting a website, you say 'hey I'm gonna want julia.com' and the server is like 'ok encryption time! I will use the cert for julia.com!' and then you say, secretly: 'Host: julia.com. here's more about that.'
Learning how HTTP works was an a+ move. It would be totally impossible for me to configure webservers at work if I didn't know how it worked!
posts.key_insight: Understanding what an HTTP request actually looks like makes web server configuration intuitive rather than magical, and SNI extends that same routing concept into the encrypted TLS handshake.

posts.summary: Evans discusses the challenges of correct benchmarking, inspired by a paper on rigorous benchmarking and a floating-point printing paper that had incorrect performance claims. She highlights that many performance improvements are small (5-10%), which falls within typical measurement error, making confidence intervals essential. The post explores the concept of independence in benchmark samples, explaining that sequential runs may not be independent due to JIT warmup, periodic patterns, or other effects. She presents a visual smoke test for checking independence: randomly shuffling benchmark results and comparing them to the original sequence. The core message is that understanding your benchmark data matters more than blindly computing averages.
key_points: A paper on printing floating-point numbers initially claimed 2x faster performance but was actually 2x slower, illustrating how hard benchmarking is even for researchers
The median performance improvement in surveyed papers was only 10%, yet more than half didn't publish confidence intervals
Benchmark samples may not be independent over time due to JIT warmup, caching effects, or periodic patterns, and you should graph results over time to check
A simple smoke test for independence: randomly shuffle your benchmark results and see if the shuffled version looks the same as the original
Understanding the properties of your benchmark data is more valuable than relying on a smart framework to solve problems for you
money_quotes: I love learning about programming problems (that are solved on my computer every day, when serializing numbers into JSON!) that I didn't even know existed.
I don't point this out to make fun of the researchers for coming up with an incorrect result. I'm pretty sure they're way better at performance analysis than I am.
Benchmarking programs and figuring out which one is faster is really hard – much much harder than you might think.
A great way to make your stuff WAY FASTER is to make many small 5% improvements. So you need to actually be able to detect a 5% improvement.
A 10% improvement is well within the bounds of error on a lot of experiments.
Graphing the speed of a benchmark over time is certainly something I've never done (usually I just blindly take an average or something).

The idea that you should look at the data that you're using to make an important decision (more than just calculating a single point estimate of the median / average) seems extremely reasonable to me.
posts.key_insight: Benchmarking correctly requires understanding statistical properties of your data—checking for independence, using confidence intervals, and visualizing results over time—rather than blindly averaging numbers.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains the internal mechanisms gdb uses to debug programs, walking through concrete examples with a Ruby process. She shows how gdb finds global variables by combining symbol table offsets with memory-mapped addresses from /proc/pid/maps, how it reads memory using the ptrace system call, and how it interprets raw bytes as meaningful struct fields using DWARF debugging data. She also touches on gdb's stack unwinding capabilities and notes that understanding these internals gives her a better mental model for using gdb effectively.
key_points: Global variable addresses are found by looking up the symbol table offset and adding it to the process's memory-mapped base address from /proc/pid/maps
gdb reads process memory using the ptrace system call (PTRACE_PEEKTEXT), which is the fundamental mechanism behind most of gdb's operations
DWARF debugging data provides the metadata (struct layouts, field offsets, types) that lets gdb interpret raw bytes as meaningful named fields
DWARF is a large, complicated standard with confusing libraries, but it's what makes the difference between seeing raw bytes and seeing structured data
Understanding gdb's internals builds a better mental model that prevents confusion about gdb's capabilities and limitations
money_quotes: If we want to find the address of a global variable in our program, all we need to do is look up the name of the variable in the symbol table, and then add that to the start of the range in /proc/whatever/maps, and we're done!
gdb is asking 'hey, what's in that address exactly'? It's using the ptrace system call which is how gdb does everything.
If you are a human like me and want to know what it MEANS, you need more.

This makes it a lot more obvious what debugging info is for – if we didn't have all this extra metadata about what all these variables meant, we would have no idea what the bytes at address 0x5598ab3235b0 meant.
This is also why you can install debug info for a program separately from your program – gdb doesn't care where it gets the extra debug info from.
gdb kind of acts like a C REPL sometimes – you type ruby_current_thread->cfp->iseq, and it feels like writing C code! But you're not really writing C at all.
Knowing that it's using DWARF to figure out the contents of the structs gives me a better mental model and have more correct expectations!
posts.key_insight: gdb works by combining symbol tables, ptrace system calls, and DWARF debugging data to translate raw process memory into the meaningful variable names and struct fields that developers see.

posts.summary: Evans reflects on the challenge of deciding what to work on as a software engineer, arguing that choosing the right projects matters enormously because meaningful work takes months and you can only do a few things per year. She advocates for having a clear, explainable focus area while acknowledging there's no single 'right thing' to work on. The post emphasizes that believing a project is possible is essential for finishing ambitious work, and that the transition from school to employment means you must grade your own tests. She candidly admits she still struggles with this and that many of her best contributions came from getting excited about other people's ideas.
key_points: Meaningful engineering projects take 2-6+ months, so choosing what to work on has outsized importance since a failed project can waste a quarter of your year
Having a clear, explainable focus area makes it dramatically easier to decide what to do and make progress toward it
There is no globally optimal project to work on — the goal is reliably finding useful things within your capabilities, not finding the perfect thing
Ambitious projects require genuine belief that you can finish them, and mentors play a critical role in helping junior engineers develop that belief
Professional work requires you to set your own standards and grade your own tests, unlike school where assignments are handed to you
money_quotes: The reason programmers sometimes get paid a lot of money, I think, is because we can build systems that leverage computers to do an unreasonable amount of work.
The more time I spend programming, the more time I see that it's actually super hard to figure out what would be important to work on.
I think of this as kind of the 'can I explain my job to someone at a party?' test. When I can't pass this test I feel uncomfortable.
If I only wrote globally optimal blog posts I would literally never publish anything.
If you give up when you have a bad week or three weeks or somebody doesn't believe that what you're doing is right, you will never finish.

Sometimes you need to build a big thing, and you can write design docs and prototypes, but ultimately you need to decide that damnit, it's going to work, and commit to spending a long time building it.
It's the difference between people who are still trying to do well on a test administered by someone else, and the people who have found in themselves the ability to grade their own test.
So far the best things I've worked on have been other people's ideas that I got excited about.
posts.key_insight: Choosing what to work on is one of the hardest and most consequential skills in engineering — harder than the actual building — and it requires developing a clear focus, believing ambitious projects are possible, and learning to grade your own work.

posts.summary: Evans describes a technique where instead of writing abstract project goals in a design document, she writes a mock release email announcing the finished feature. She found this approach more fun and motivating than traditional goal-setting, and it forced her to think about narrative, audience, and value proposition. The technique also generated immediate helpful feedback from colleagues. She acknowledges this is similar to 'documentation driven development' but finds personal value in the specific framing.
key_points: Writing a fake release announcement before building a feature can replace abstract goal-setting in design documents
Framing goals as a narrative for an audience forces you to articulate why the work matters and how people will use it
The technique works partly as a motivation hack — imagining the release makes the project feel more exciting
Sharing the mock announcement early generates useful feedback on both the idea and implementation challenges
money_quotes: Instead of writing down a list of goals, instead I wrote a release email
I sort of hate writing down lists of project goals, but I love writing emails and blog posts that explain cool new features to people
It forces you to write down a narrative around your end goals, consider your target audience, and explain why what you're doing is important
It helps me feel more energetic about the project ('oh wow this is a good useful thing!')
I think this is kind of a weird brain hack
posts.key_insight: Writing a mock release announcement before building a feature is a motivating alternative to abstract goal-setting that forces you to articulate narrative, audience, and value.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains the three places where UDP packets can be dropped: on the way out (socket send buffer overflow), in transit across the internet, and on the way in (socket receive buffer overflow). She walks through Linux sysctls that control buffer sizes and shows how to use netstat to monitor dropped packets. The post demystifies packet loss by emphasizing that packets don't disappear by magic — they get dropped because buffers get full. She concludes by noting that everything in networking is buffers, and TCP packets get dropped just as easily but are retried automatically.
key_points: UDP packets are dropped at three stages: outgoing socket buffer, in transit, and incoming socket receive buffer, each with different causes
Linux provides sysctls like net.core.rmem_max and net.ipv4.udp_mem to control buffer sizes, and netstat -suna reveals exactly how many packets have been dropped
Most packet loss comes from full buffers somewhere in the chain — network cards, OS socket buffers, and intermediate routers all have buffers that can overflow
TCP packets get dropped just as easily as UDP packets, but TCP handles retries automatically so you're less likely to notice
money_quotes: When you're losing UDP packets, it's sort of tempting to say 'well, whatever, that's what happens when you use UDP!' But UDP packets don't get lost by magic.
EVERYTHING IS BUFFERS.
Your network card has a buffer that can get full! There are a bunch of intermediate routers between your computer and my computer. All of those have buffers! Those buffers can get full!
My current understanding is that most packet loss is because of full buffers one way or another.
Also everything here I said about UDP packets applies just as well to any kind of IP packet – TCP packets can get dropped just as easily, but they'll get retried, so you're not as likely to notice.
There's a joke about UDP. it goes like this: 'Never mind, you probably wouldn't get it.'
posts.key_insight: UDP packet loss isn't mysterious — packets get dropped at specific, observable points where buffers overflow, and Linux gives you tools to monitor exactly where and how often it happens.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes the process of creating her programming zines, from initial inspiration through tools, printing, and distribution. She traces her motivation to feminist publishing culture and riot grrrl zines, combined with a desire to make intimidating technical topics like strace and tcpdump accessible. She details her evolution from hand-drawn sharpie-on-paper to a Samsung tablet with stylus, and explains the PDF toolchain for turning images into printable booklets. The post argues that fun, approachable formats lower barriers to learning topics traditionally considered advanced.
key_points: Zines work as teaching tools because their fun, informal format makes people willing to engage with topics they'd otherwise find intimidating
The technical barrier to making zines is low — sharpie on paper works, though a tablet with stylus improves the workflow significantly
Print shops already know how to produce booklets, making physical zine production surprisingly accessible
Zines occupy a sweet spot between blog posts and books — more tangible than posts but far less commitment than a book
Many technical concepts considered 'advanced' are only hard because nobody presents them accessibly, not because they're inherently difficult
money_quotes: Fun, accessible content works. People understand it.

Some of the things I want to explain are traditionally considered kind of advanced! I didn't learn about any of these things until I'd been programming for 10 years. But there's no reason I couldn't have learned them earlier! It's just that nobody told me.
I spent years being scared of tcpdump. But it's not really that scary, and if I can help a few people be a little less intimidated by it, then I've done my job.

People will read them even if they're new to programming or new to Linux! And they'll often learn something and tell me 'yeah, sure, I didn't understand 100% of it but a lot of it made sense!' To me this is a HUGE WIN.
HELLO HAVE YOU HEARD THE GOOD WORD ABOUT STRACE???

I kind of want to write a book sometimes, but writing a book takes like a YEAR or MORE and is a huge commitment. Writing a zine is a lot easier and lower stakes and I get some of the fun rewards without spending months editing a book.
I filled out a form and now my strace zine is part of the national archives of Quebec.
posts.key_insight: Making technical content fun and visually approachable removes the intimidation factor that keeps people from learning tools they could have learned years earlier.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that asking good questions is a learnable skill that takes practice and deliberate effort. She shares strategies for formulating better questions, including summarizing your current understanding, asking about history and edge cases, and providing partial answers to prompt deeper responses. She emphasizes that being humble about knowledge gaps is essential, and that good questions serve not just the asker but everyone around them. The post catalogs specific question templates that reliably surface interesting knowledge.
key_points: Asking good questions is a skill you can practice and improve, not an innate talent
Summarizing your current understanding before asking helps reveal misunderstandings and gives the answerer context
Specific question templates like 'Why is X hard?' and 'What did X look like when it first existed?' reliably produce interesting answers
Providing partial answers when asking questions prompts others to fill in gaps you didn't know existed
Asking questions is a service to others — it helps frame problems, improve talks, and move conversations forward
money_quotes: One thing about asking questions that I think doesn't always get enough appreciation is that asking good questions is a skill. It's something that you can practice and get better at.
Sometimes if there's a topic I'm really confused about, I need to concentrate for like 10 minutes to actually come up with a more coherent question that "what? I don't get it?".
Finding questions to ask that get people to tell you AMAZING THINGS isn't always easy. But sometimes I stumble upon MAGICAL QUESTIONS that unearth hidden huge amounts of knowledge.
When I discover yet another new thing that I need to know that I know practically nothing about, sometimes it feels embarrassing!
When I find out that I don't know an Important Thing that apparently everybody except me knew I just take a deep breath and am like WELL I GUESS TODAY IS THE DAY I'M GOING TO LEARN IT THEN.
Creating weird edge cases on systems that aren't important gives you a better sense of the edges of your system and how the underlying things work.
The more questions I ask, the better I get at asking questions that move the conversation forward, that teach me (and other people!) something new, or that help someone clarify what they're saying.
posts.key_insight: Asking good questions is a practicable skill, and specific question templates — like summarizing your understanding, asking why something is hard, or providing partial answers — reliably surface deeper knowledge than open-ended questions.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces a new zine about Linux debugging tools that she thinks deserve more attention. The zine covers strace, opensnoop/eBPF, dstat, netcat, netstat, tcpdump, wireshark, ngrep, and perf. It's designed to be accessible to both beginners and experienced Linux users, and she's been pleased to see people at all experience levels learning from it. The zine is available in multiple formats including printable versions.
key_points: The zine focuses on Linux debugging tools that are underappreciated relative to their usefulness, including strace, eBPF tools, and perf
The target audience is explicitly people who don't already know these tools, making advanced debugging accessible to newcomers
The zine has resonated with both new Linux users and veterans with 10+ years of experience, suggesting these tools have broad knowledge gaps
Making the zine freely printable and shareable reflects a philosophy of lowering barriers to technical education
money_quotes: This zine is about some of my favorite Linux debugging tools, especially tools that I don't think are as well-known as they should be.
If you don't know what any of those tools I just mentioned are – PERFECT. You are who this zine is for!!!
And there's a whole section on perf because perf is the best.
I've been really delighted to see that a ton of people have enjoyed & learned something new from this zine, whether they just started using Linux or have been debugging on Linux for 10 years.
Give them to your friends! Teach them about tcpdump!
It turns out that a zine project takes a long time! I do not even know how people write books.
posts.key_insight: Powerful Linux debugging tools like strace, perf, and tcpdump are underappreciated, and making them accessible through approachable formats can help developers at every experience level.

posts.summary: Julia Evans delivered the closing keynote at the first RustConf about how Rust empowers people who don't consider themselves systems programmers to learn and experiment with systems concepts. She shares her journey from not knowing what a system call was to writing an operating system keyboard driver and a Ruby profiling tool in Rust. She argues that Rust's memory safety lets programmers tackle projects that would be impractical in C, and that you don't need to be an expert to teach others — beginners writing about what they learn can be enormously valuable. The talk emphasizes that systems programming concepts aren't as hard as people think, and that the Rust community is uniquely positioned to help experienced developers level up.
key_points: Rust enables programming experiments that would be impractical in C/C++ because it eliminates the need to manually manage memory, letting you focus on the actual systems concepts you're trying to learn.
You don't need to be an expert to teach — writing about what you learn as a beginner can be more valuable than expert documentation, because beginners know what's confusing.
Contributing to open source communities doesn't require writing code; telling people about useful tools and writing about concepts are meaningful contributions.
There's a gap in programming education: plenty of beginner resources exist, but intermediate-to-advanced developers who want to level up in systems programming are underserved.
Systems programming concepts like system calls, concurrency primitives, and networking fundamentals aren't inherently hard — they just need clear explanations.
money_quotes: I spend a lot of time being frustrated with the Rust compiler, but I still like it because it lets me do things I probably wouldn't get done otherwise.
To teach people it turns out you don't have to be an expert at all. Maybe it's actually even better to be a beginner!
If it's not documented, it might as well not exist.
I like to skip the whole first step of writing code, and just tell people about awesome things that already exist. I'm like the most productive software developer ever.
I do not really know how to free memory in C. Like, I technically know that there is a free function, but I don't have a lot of experience with it.
Remember that a lot of systems things aren't really that hard. People can learn harder things than you think they can if you explain it in a way that makes sense.
One delightful thing about systems is that there's always SO MUCH MORE TO LEARN.
My favorite way to start out exploring an idea is to write a program that doesn't work.
posts.key_insight: Rust empowers people who aren't traditional systems programmers to experiment with and learn systems concepts that would be impractical to tackle in C, and sharing what you learn as a non-expert is one of the most valuable contributions you can make.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains how she earned two CS degrees without ever learning what a system call is, not as a failure of education but as a reflection of the breadth possible within CS programs. She lists all her undergrad and grad courses, showing she focused heavily on pure math, algorithms, and CS theory rather than systems programming. Her main point is that 'a CS degree' can represent very different educations depending on what you choose to study, and that's perfectly fine. She emphasizes that it's okay to not know things, and that people learn what they need when they need it.
key_points: A CS degree can represent radically different educations depending on course selection — Julia's was heavily math and theory focused with minimal systems content
Not knowing fundamental practical concepts like system calls despite having advanced degrees isn't a failure — it's a natural consequence of specialization
People without CS degrees sometimes feel inadequate about gaps in theory knowledge, just as CS grads can have gaps in practical knowledge — both groups learn what they need over time
The math and CS theory classes were excellent, so she took all of them, leaving little room for systems-level courses
money_quotes: I have 2 CS degrees but didn't know what a system call was when I graduated.
This is just to say that "a CS degree" can represent a lot of different educations, and personally I think that's totally fine.
But more importantly – it's ok to not know things.
I knew practically nothing about a lot of really important programming concepts when I got out of grad school. Even though I'd started learning to program 8 years before!
Now I know those things! I learned them.

I have friends who are amazing programmers who sometimes feel bad because they don't have a CS degree and sometimes don't know algorithms/CS theory stuff that other people know. They've learned the things they needed to know! They are great.
posts.key_insight: A CS degree represents just one particular path through a vast field, and the gaps it leaves are not failures but opportunities for continued learning.

posts.summary: Evans explores the landscape of running containers in production, comparing Docker and rkt while candidly admitting she has many unanswered questions. She identifies packaging as the most compelling reason to use containers, since installing dependencies on servers is error-prone and painful. She raises concerns about Docker's daemon architecture, where bugs can deadlock and kill all containers, versus rkt's simpler process model. The post also surfaces subtle gotchas like PID 1 behavior, networking complexity, and secrets management. Her overall assessment from talking to practitioners is that containers are genuinely useful despite the hype, but full of sharp edges.
key_points: Packaging is the killer feature of containers — installing dependencies correctly on servers is hard and scary, and containers solve this cleanly
Docker's daemon architecture is a liability: if the daemon deadlocks, all containers die, whereas rkt runs containers as regular processes
Containers violate normal Unix assumptions, such as your process unexpectedly becoming PID 1 and inheriting zombie processes
Container networking adds complexity that may not always be necessary — sometimes you just want normal ports in a normal network namespace
The practitioner consensus is that containers are useful despite the hype, but have many sharp edges to navigate
money_quotes: Installing stuff on computers so you can run your program on them really sucks. It's easy to get wrong! It's scary when you make changes!
I don't want to have to trust that the Docker daemon has no bugs that will affect me. All software has bugs!
I think 'violates a lot of normal Unix assumptions about what normally happens to normal processes' is basically the whole story about containers.
So far reading about containers mostly feels like this hilarious article.
You don't want your process to get random zombie processes as children. Like that might be fine, but it violates a lot of normal Unix assumptions.
Do you really need to use this complicated container networking stuff? I kind of just want to run container processes and run them on normal ports in my normal network namespace and leave it at that.
posts.key_insight: Containers solve the real pain of packaging and deployment, but their abstractions violate familiar Unix assumptions in ways that create surprising sharp edges in production.

posts.summary: Julia Evans presents a talk from Strange Loop about using systems debugging tools to investigate programs without reading source code or even knowing what language they're written in. She demonstrates strace for tracing system calls, time and /proc for diagnosing slow programs, perf top for identifying CPU-intensive functions, dstat for monitoring disk and network activity, and ngrep for inspecting network traffic. Through a series of mysteries (wrong config files, slow programs, French websites), she shows how these tools let you ask questions of the operating system to get concrete answers. Her core message is that debugging doesn't require genius-level intuition — it requires the right tools and a willingness to not be intimidated by systems-level concepts.
key_points: You can debug programs without source code or knowing the language, because all programs use the same OS primitives (system calls, TCP, files)
strace reveals every system call a program makes, but can slow programs 50x — eBPF tools like opensnoop provide similar insights without the overhead
The 'time' command breaks down CPU vs wall-clock time, and combining it with /proc call stacks, dstat, and perf top can quickly categorize why a program is slow
ngrep lets you search network traffic the way grep searches text files, making it easy to spot differences in HTTP requests between tools
Sampling profilers give statistical overviews but tracing tools capture everything, which matters when debugging rare events in high-volume systems
money_quotes: I used to think to debug things, I had to be really really smart. I thought I had to stare at the code, and think really hard, and magically intuit what the bug was.
It turns out that this isn't true at all! If you have the right tools, fixing bugs can be really easy.
But if you're not scared, you can usually figure out almost anything!
You can totally debug programs without having their source code or even knowing what language they're written in at all.
When I use strace, I ignore practically everything, and just grep to find the one thing I'm interested in.
We can peer into the Linux kernel's soul and figure out what it was doing when the program was waiting.
Computer programs aren't always deterministic, but they're always logical. If you're seeing a difference where you think there shouldn't be any, there's always a reason.
I was so excited when I learned about strace, and I couldn't believe that I'd been programming for 9 years without knowing about it.
posts.key_insight: You don't need to be a genius to debug complex problems — you just need the right systems tools to ask your operating system what's actually happening.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares personal productivity strategies that help her get things done as an individual software engineer on a small team. She emphasizes having a clear vision for your work, focusing on one or two things at a time, and breaking large goals into smaller steps. The post covers practical tactics like making daily progress on important work, asking for help when stuck, not being afraid of unexpected problems, and being willing to do unfamiliar work across team boundaries. She frames deadlines as useful scope-management tools rather than threats, and closes by noting that believing in your own ability to deliver is both reasonable and productive.
key_points: Having a clear vision (even an imperfect one) provides a framework for daily decisions and prevents getting stuck in the weeds
Focusing on one or two things at a time dramatically increases throughput compared to juggling many tasks
Unexpected problems are a normal part of every project, and learning to stay optimistic through them is a skill worth developing
Getting things done often means doing unfamiliar work outside your team's codebase rather than waiting for others to do it for you
Deadlines serve as useful scope-management tools that reveal when something is growing uncontrollably
money_quotes: He suggested just picking a vision that seemed mostly reasonable and then moving that way until I learned more. This was actually really helpful.
When I actually focus on a thing, I'm always amazed by how much I can get done. If I do more than about 2 things, then all of the things take forever to get finished.
I've noticed that when I work on things, there is usually a weird maze between the Start Point and the Thing Actually Being Done.

I used to get a lot more scared when something unexpected happened like OH NO WHAT IF MY PROJECT WILL FAIL EVERYTHING IS TERRIBLE. I'm trying to learn to be more optimistic and just think 'ok, well, something weird happened, there are always weird things, it will be fine'.
So it turns out to get things done I have to do a bunch of things that I do not know how to do and am not necessarily qualified to do. Usually I can just do these things and it is fine.
I think deadlines are helpful to see when something's growing in scope uncontrollably or is taking a lot longer than you thought.
It is pretty useful for me to believe that I can actually do the thing I'm setting out to do. Often when I try to do a thing it turns out that I can do it, so this is basically reasonable.
posts.key_insight: Productivity as an individual engineer comes from having a clear (even imperfect) vision, ruthlessly focusing on one thing at a time, and staying optimistic through the inevitable maze of unexpected problems.

posts.summary: Julia Evans catalogs the container software ecosystem to make sense of the overwhelming number of tools available. She lists 17 pieces of software from major organizations including Docker, CoreOS, HashiCorp, Google, Mesosphere, and OCI. By writing everything down and summarizing each tool in three words or less, she finds the landscape less intimidating than it initially seemed. The post serves as a reference for anyone confused by the proliferation of container-related projects.
key_points: The container ecosystem involves only about 17 key pieces of software from roughly 6-8 organizations, which is less overwhelming than it feels
Container software spans multiple concerns: running containers, orchestration, networking, service discovery, secrets management, and init processes
Organizing tools by the organization that produces them helps make the landscape more understandable
Writing things down and enumerating them explicitly is a useful strategy for overcoming the feeling of being overwhelmed
money_quotes: I have been confused about the plethora of container software in the world.
Having written this down, I feel a bit better – there are only 17 pieces of software on this list, from 6 different organizations.
That's actually less than I felt like it was and I kinda sorta know what all of these things do.
I've tried to summarize each one in 3 words or less which is hard because a lot of this software has a lot of different jobs.
My rough heuristic for this is just 'software someone has told me about more than once, and is not experimental'.
posts.key_insight: The container ecosystem feels less overwhelming when you enumerate every tool explicitly — the actual list is smaller and more organized than the vague sense of chaos suggests.

posts.summary: Evans walks through the hidden complexity behind 'just running a container' in production, starting from the simplest case (runC) and progressively discovering requirements like overlay filesystems, image verification, and process supervision. She compares Docker and rkt architectures, explaining why Docker's monolithic approach is being broken apart into components like containerd. The post traces her learning journey through each layer of the container stack, showing that what sounds simple actually involves kernel version requirements, filesystem drivers, security signing, and process management. She concludes that running containers without Docker is possible but far from simple, and understanding the components helps demystify the ecosystem.
key_points: Running containers in production requires overlay filesystems to avoid duplicating shared libraries across dozens of containers, which in turn requires specific kernel versions
runC intentionally excludes overlay filesystem support to stay simple, forcing users toward rkt or Docker for that functionality
Docker's historical problem in production was coupling process supervision with many other features; containerd represents a move toward separating these concerns
Container image verification is a real gap: rkt can run Docker images but cannot check their signatures, creating a security blind spot
The OCI standard and tools like skopeo are working toward modular container infrastructure, but the ecosystem was still evolving
money_quotes: The concerns when you run containers in production are pretty different from running it on a laptop.
If I'm running 50 containers I don't want to have 50 copies of all my shared libraries in memory. That's why we invented dynamic linking!

If you want to run containers using as few new things as possible, you can start out by just running your containers as normal programs that run on normal ports, like any other program on your computer.
No matter what tools you use it's definitely not as simple as 'just run a container'.
Either way going through all these steps helps me understand what the actual components of running a container are and what all these different pieces of software are trying to do.
This landscape is pretty confusing but I think it's not impossible to understand! There are only a finite number of different pieces of software to figure out the role of.
posts.key_insight: What appears to be a simple request—'just run a container'—actually involves overlay filesystems, kernel requirements, image verification, and process supervision, and understanding each layer demystifies the entire container ecosystem.

posts.summary: The post explains how Linux starts new processes using the fork and exec system calls rather than a single 'start process' call. Fork creates a clone of the current process, then exec replaces that clone's memory and program with the new one. Evans uses the memorable metaphor of a child clone getting its 'brain eaten' to explain how exec works. She covers the zombie process state, copy-on-write optimization for fork, and which process attributes survive across exec. The post concludes by showing why understanding this mechanism matters for debugging real bugs, like inherited signal handlers causing unexpected behavior.
key_points: Linux starts processes via two system calls (fork + exec) rather than a single 'start process' call, with fork creating a clone and exec replacing its program
When exec runs, most process attributes (environment variables, open files, signal handlers) are preserved — only memory, registers, and the executable change
Fork uses copy-on-write so the parent's memory isn't actually duplicated until one process modifies it, making fork cheap
After a child exits it becomes a zombie process, waiting for the parent to read its return value via the wait system call
Understanding fork/exec inheritance matters for debugging — inherited signal handlers or environment variables can cause programs to behave differently depending on their parent process
money_quotes: Instead of having children, what I do is you have a child that is a clone of myself, and then that child gets its brain eaten and turns into ls.
At this point ls is actually a zombie process! That means it's dead, but it's waiting around for me in case I want to check on its return value.
The only thing that changes is, well, all of your memory and registers and the program that you're running. Which is a pretty big deal.
It turns out that Linux implements 'copy on write' for fork() calls, so that for all the 2GB of memory in the new process it's just like 'look at the old process! it's the same!'.
This means that the program will behave differently depending on whether you started it from a Python script or from your shell!
Your program's environment (environment, signal handlers, etc.) can matter! It inherits its environment from its parent process, whatever that was!
posts.key_insight: Linux starts processes not by creating them from scratch but by cloning the parent with fork and then replacing the clone's program with exec, which means new processes inherit most of their parent's attributes — and understanding this inheritance is key to debugging subtle bugs.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her migration from Jekyll to Hugo for her blog's static site generation. The switch was motivated by Jekyll's slow 10-second build times and frustrating Ruby dependency management. She completed the migration in about 5 hours using a pragmatic approach: rather than understanding her Jekyll theme, she copied the rendered HTML output and worked backwards. The result was a 0.4-second build time and a simpler setup she actually understands. A 2023 update confirms she's still happily using Hugo seven years later, still on version 0.40 from 2018.
key_points: Hugo reduced site generation from 10 seconds to 0.4 seconds, and eliminated the need for a working Ruby development environment
The pragmatic migration strategy was to copy the rendered HTML output rather than try to understand and port Jekyll theme files
The 80/20 rule applied perfectly: 80% of the migration took 1 hour, the remaining 20% took 4 more hours
The hardest part of static site generation is writing posts, not the tooling itself
Hugo's static binary distribution means you can keep using an old version indefinitely without dependency rot
money_quotes: problem: Jekyll generated my site slowly. It took, like, 10 seconds! Who has time to wait for that!
I couldn't edit my site once for six months because I was too frustrated to get Ruby to work again.
Working backwards from the HTML that Jekyll had generated to come up with a new working theme was way faster than trying to understand what my Jekyll theme files were doing.
Also I got to delete a bunch of cruft in my website that I didn't understand. Now I never need to learn to understand it!
My overall review of Hugo: Jekyll was fine. Hugo is also fine.
I really appreciate that Hugo is a static binary so that I can just keep using an old version forever if I want.
7 years later, I'm still using Hugo and it's still fine.
posts.key_insight: The best migration strategy is often working backwards from what you have rather than trying to understand and port the system you're replacing.

posts.summary: Julia Evans demystifies containers by breaking them down into their underlying Linux kernel primitives: namespaces, cgroups, and seccomp-bpf. She demonstrates that containers aren't virtual machines but rather isolated processes using these kernel features. Through hands-on examples, she shows how to create a PID namespace with unshare, limit memory with cgroups, and explains how seccomp-bpf restricts system calls. The post concludes that Docker is essentially tooling built on top of these basic primitives.
key_points: The word 'container' doesn't refer to a precise technology — it's shorthand for using Linux namespaces and cgroups to isolate processes
Namespaces (pid, networking, mount) let you create isolated views of system resources, and creating them is surprisingly easy with the unshare command
Cgroups provide resource limits (memory, CPU) for groups of processes, evolved from the concept of nice but with far more control
Seccomp-bpf adds security by filtering which system calls a process can execute
Docker containers are built on these basic Linux kernel primitives — there's no magic involved
money_quotes: The word "container" doesn't mean anything super precise. Basically there are a few new Linux kernel features ("namespaces" and "cgroups") that let you isolate processes from each other. When you use those features, you call it "containers".
Basically these features let you pretend you have something like a virtual machine, except it's not a virtual machine at all, it's just processes running in the same Linux kernel.
Wow, we're in a whole new world! There are only 2 processes running – bash and ps. Cool, that was easy!
So they're two views on the same thing, but one is a lot more restricted.
10 megabytes should be enough for anyone!
That's all Docker is! Of course Docker has a lot of features these days, but a lot of it is built on these basic Linux kernel primitives.
posts.key_insight: Containers are not virtual machines or magic — they're just ordinary Linux processes isolated using namespaces, cgroups, and seccomp-bpf, and you can create them yourself with simple command-line tools.

posts.summary: Julia Evans walks through her personal journey of understanding operations as a software developer, from not knowing what it was to recognizing it as a learnable skill. She describes three stages: thinking software just works, being terrified that anything can break, and realizing there's a body of knowledge for keeping systems running. She argues that learning to be appropriately scared of dangerous changes is important, but you can't stop there—you need to learn the precautionary steps to make changes confidently. The post advocates for developers taking on operations work as a way to become better programmers and debuggers.
key_points: Operations knowledge progresses through stages: ignorance, terror, and competence—and each stage is a necessary part of learning
Learning to be scared of dangerous changes like database upgrades is an important skill, but staying scared without acting leads to stagnation like running a super-old Ruby version for years
Operations is a learnable skillset, not a magical innate quality—there are people who treat 99.9% uptime on thousands of machines as a normal, solvable problem
Small, incremental improvements to reliability (dashboards, profiling, isolating risky code) are a practical way for developers to get started with operations
The devops model means developers get to learn how to deploy safely, observe their code in production, and systematically prevent failures
money_quotes: I definitely went from 'operations is trivial, whatever, how hard can keeping a site up be?' to 'OMG THIS IS IMPOSSIBLE HOW DOES ANYONE EVER DO THIS'.
I think learning to be scared is a really important skill – you should be worried about upgrading a database safely, or about upgrading the version of Ruby you're using in production.

But you can't just stop at being scared – you need to learn to have a healthy concern about complex parts of your system, and then learn how to take the appropriate precautionary steps and then confidently make the big change.
Like all things, it's a skillset that you can learn, not a magical innate quality.
If you stop here then you just end up using a super-old Ruby version for 4 years because you were too scared to upgrade it. That is no good either!
It's really helped me to realize that you don't turn into a Magical Operations Person overnight.
The best software engineers I know are the ones who consistently value the impact and lifecycle of the code they ship, and value deployment and instrumentation and observability.
posts.key_insight: Operations is a learnable skillset that progresses from ignorance through healthy fear to competence, and developers who embrace it become significantly better engineers.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores what 'devops' actually means after reading 'Effective DevOps' by Jennifer Davis and Ryn Daniels. She clarifies that devops is fundamentally about communication and collaboration between development and operations teams, not about specific tools like Chef or Puppet. The post distinguishes devops from job titles, automation tooling, and the idea that operations should be made so easy no expertise is needed. Evans reflects on how many devops practices were invisible to her because her organization already followed them, making her realize these were deliberate choices rather than defaults. She concludes that devops is an evolving set of practices the industry is learning together.
key_points: DevOps is about communication and collaboration between development and operations, not about specific tools or job titles
Operations expertise remains essential — devops doesn't mean making operations so easy nobody needs to be an expert
Many devops practices (continuous integration, blameless postmortems) are organizational choices that become invisible when you've never seen the alternative
DevOps is a large, fuzzy, evolving set of practices rather than a fixed manifesto, and organizations can always improve
Operations experts should participate in systems design and architecture, not just be handed finished systems to run
money_quotes: Puppet is software, communication is about how humans work together.
It's not like 'make operations so easy that nobody has to be an expert at it'. Of course you need people who know a ton about operations!
I hadn't realized that this was a choice my organization was making, I thought that was just how things were!
Any organizational choice (like continuous integration, blameless postmortems, having a separate operations team) is a choice, and it's useful to understand why you're making it.

Even if we ostensibly practice 'devops' and do continuous integration and use jenkins and deploy 100 times a day or whatever, that doesn't actually mean that we're the best and awesome and that we can stop.
Devops seems to be less about a 'manifesto' and more of a large and fuzzy set of practices that we're all learning together as an industry over time.
posts.key_insight: DevOps is fundamentally about how people with different expertise collaborate effectively, not about any particular set of tools, and recognizing your organizational practices as deliberate choices opens the door to continuous improvement.

posts.summary: Evans proposes a simple test for job descriptions: would everyone currently on the team have met the listed requirements when they joined? She uses her own experience switching to infrastructure work with zero prior experience in her team's specific tools as evidence that many listed requirements aren't actual requirements. She argues that while overly broad 'anyone can learn anything' claims aren't helpful either, teams should be honest about what they actually need rather than listing aspirational or inflated requirements.
key_points: A good litmus test for job requirements is whether every current team member would have met them when they were hired
Listing specific technologies as requirements is often misleading when current team members learned those technologies on the job
Being vague and saying 'no requirements' is also dishonest — there are always real baseline expectations worth stating explicitly
Inflated requirements can alienate current team members who wouldn't have qualified under those criteria
money_quotes: Would everyone on your team today have met those requirements when they joined?
I had experience with 0 of the software my team uses before I joined it, and I'm totally fine. So those obviously are not requirements for joining my team!
You could cop out and say 'oh, there are no requirements, anyone can learn anything', but that's often not true either.
To save everyone's time it's good to be explicit about what you actually want.
When people write job descriptions for teams I'm on that I wouldn't have met the requirements for, I feel kind of.. alienated?
Usually they just haven't thought through the requirements that carefully and are totally happy to change them to be more realistic.
posts.key_insight: Job descriptions should list only requirements that every current team member actually met when they were hired, forcing honesty about what's truly needed versus what's aspirational.

posts.summary: Evans explains the tension between consistency and availability in distributed systems through a personal story about a database that kept going down at work. Rather than diving into the formal CAP theorem, she builds intuition: consistent systems require machines to communicate and agree, which is hard when networks are unreliable, while available systems can simply serve data from any machine without coordination. The key revelation is that her team's system stopped going down once they dropped consistency as a requirement, accepting slightly stale data in exchange for reliability. She argues that good distributed systems engineering means being extremely careful about a small number of consistent systems and making everything else as simple as possible.
key_points: Consistency and availability conflict because machines must communicate to agree on state, and networks are unreliable
Building consistent distributed systems requires complex algorithms like Raft, while building available ones is comparatively simple if you accept stale data
Changing requirements (dropping consistency) can solve operational problems more effectively than fixing bugs
Good distributed systems engineering means isolating the few places that truly need consistency and keeping everything else as dumb as possible
Having fuzzy intuitions about formal concepts like consistency and availability is valuable even without understanding the full theory
money_quotes: We changed our mind about what we wanted out of the system, and that helped us operate it better!
Like "the best distributed system is the one you deleted."
I feel like really good distributed systems engineering is being like extremely careful about a small number of consistent systems and then making everything else as dumb as possible.
These days when I see a distributed systems problem I'm more likely to think OH NO CAN WE NOT.
There are LOTS of systems that don't need to be consistent, it turns out!
If you don't care if the data you're reading is necessarily totally right, then there's no problem! Do whatever you want!
It wasn't because we fixed bugs, or because we were really smart. We changed our mind about what we wanted out of the system.
posts.key_insight: Many systems don't actually need consistency, and recognizing that lets you trade it for availability — solving operational problems not through better engineering but through better requirements.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores a series of practical questions about open source software from the perspective of a tech company employee. She examines when to use open source vs building your own tools, why companies pay to develop open source, how to evaluate unknown software, and whether promoting software that doesn't work well is harmful. Rather than providing definitive answers, she lays out the trade-offs and complexities honestly, acknowledging that open source is both critically important and surprisingly confusing in practice.
key_points: Using open source doesn't always save time — sometimes the available tools are bad fits for your specific problem, and evaluation is genuinely hard
Companies pay for open source development for practical business reasons: shared infrastructure needs, social pressure, developer retention, and long-term community investment
Understanding who maintains open source software and what their priorities are is essential context for using it effectively
There's a real question of responsibility when open source projects are promoted but don't actually work well, yet most software in the wild is probably unmaintained
Evaluating unknown software is hard but the alternative — writing everything from scratch — is often worse
money_quotes: I do not contribute to open source in my spare time, I write this blog instead.
using nginx instead of writing a web server myself probably saves me like 2 years of developer time or something. But using open source I think doesn't always save you time!
I really do believe in the RAINBOWS PEOPLE WORKING TOGETHER TOWARDS THE IMPROVEMENT OF HUMANKIND version of open source but I am trying to understand some of the more practical reasons right now.
I kinda feel like working on open source is like being part of a really big company and you have to know what the other people who are paying to develop the software care about.
I feel like my current default assumption about software I find in the wild is that it is unmaintained and possibly nobody has ever used it except the people who wrote it.
Ideally people would write on the tin 'we have no idea if anyone other than us has ever used this software successfully' though.
Evaluating software is hard and I still don't really know how to do this well.
posts.key_insight: Open source software is critically important to the tech industry, but the practical questions of when to use it, how to evaluate it, and understanding the incentives behind it are surprisingly complex and under-discussed.

posts.summary: Julia Evans proposes a pragmatic migration plan for adopting containers: start by running containers without Docker, Kubernetes, or any orchestration layer. She argues for horizontal, incremental changes over building a 'Cool New World' cluster, suggesting teams use minimal tools like rkt to run container images in their existing infrastructure. The key insight is that containerizing applications is work you'd have to do anyway, so it can be separated into a smaller, less risky project. She outlines concrete benefits like reducing fragile Puppet configuration and eliminating hidden host dependencies, while acknowledging containers are worth learning about because that's where the ecosystem is heading.
key_points: You can run containers with minimal tooling (like rkt) without adopting Docker, Kubernetes, or any orchestration platform
Horizontal incremental changes across infrastructure are less risky than building a parallel 'Cool New World' and migrating into it
Containers enforce discipline around packaging and running services by isolating them from host configuration, eliminating silent dependencies
Containerizing applications is prerequisite work for any orchestration platform, so doing it first is useful regardless of what you adopt later
Learning to operate new software in production takes time, so minimizing the amount of new moving parts in each step is valuable
money_quotes: You can run containers without using Docker or Kubernetes or Mesos or anything like that.
rkt just runs containers, the same way you run a program. I know how to run programs! This is awesome.
There's no way for it to silently depend on the host configuration, because its filesystem is totally separate from the host's filesystem.
Lots of cool orchestration features like with Kubernetes or Mesos or Docker Swarm mean that I need to learn how the software works and how it operates and exactly how it can fail in production.
In the short term, if I want to deploy changes that I can confidently run in production, using all kinds of exciting features like this just slows me down.
This is just about separating work into smaller useful chunks.
I used to be really annoyed about containers because it seemed like a buzzword.
posts.key_insight: Containerizing your applications is valuable work on its own that you'd have to do anyway, so you can separate it from the much harder problem of adopting orchestration platforms.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Julia Evans shares that she published her first blog post on the official Stripe engineering blog after working there for nearly three years. The post is about service discovery at Stripe and includes a comic she drew. She credits her colleague nelhage for explaining the technical details and expresses gratitude for working with knowledgeable, helpful people. The post primarily serves as a pointer to the full article on Stripe's blog.
key_points: Julia published her first post on the Stripe engineering blog after nearly 3 years at the company
The post covers service discovery at Stripe and includes one of her characteristic comics
She credits colleague nelhage for explaining the technical content to her
She emphasizes the value of working with people willing to answer questions and share knowledge
money_quotes: I've been writing on this blog the whole time I've worked there (going on 3 years), but today for the first time I published a blog post on the Stripe blog.
I'm happy I work with so many people who will answer my questions and explain things. I've learned a lot.
posts.key_insight: Working with generous, knowledgeable colleagues who willingly share their expertise is one of the most valuable aspects of a job.

posts.summary: Julia Evans traces the complete lifecycle of running a rkt container, from fetching and storing images to preparing pods and finally executing the containerized process. She examines how rkt maintains a local image store backed by a SQL database, then pre-renders container filesystems into a 'tree store' (doubling disk usage) due to ACI's DAG-based dependency format. The post walks through overlay filesystem mounting for pods and shows the actual system calls that culminate in a chroot and execve. She deliberately chose the simplest possible options (disabling security, using the lightweight 'fly' stage1) to make the internals tractable, and argues that understanding your infrastructure at this level is valuable when you're the person responsible for operating it.
key_points: rkt stores container images in a content-addressed blob store backed by a 'ql' embedded SQL database, then pre-renders them into a separate tree store, effectively doubling disk usage
The ACI format uses a directed acyclic graph for dependencies (unlike Docker's linked list of layers), requiring a more complex rendering step before containers can run
Running a container ultimately comes down to a chroot, setting uid/gid, and execve — the ceremony is in the preparation, not the execution
rkt's 'stage1' architecture separates container setup logic into swappable programs, from heavyweight (systemd-based) to lightweight ('fly' using just chroot)
Understanding the storage model and system calls of your container runtime lets you make accurate capacity planning decisions in production
money_quotes: I've claimed previously on this blog that rkt 'just runs programs, it's not that complicated, that's why it's cool'. But if you look at the source for rkt, excluding tests, there are about 34,000 lines of Go code today.
If you think this takes extra disk space, you are right! When you use rkt to run a container you get 2 copies of every file in that container.
Simplest does not mean best – the simplest way includes turning off all security features :)
Everyone should not need to know all the details of how everything they use works. That would take too much time. Imagine if every Linux user needed to read Linux kernel code to do their job!

If I know rkt's storage model, then I can make guesses at exactly how much disk space rkt is going to use in production, then I can plan to give my machines an appropriate amount of storage, and then nobody will run out of disk space!
When you work with complex systems, a lot of the time someone needs to understand how they work, so they can plan to operate the system correctly.
Right now I get to be that someone, for some things. Yay!
posts.key_insight: Understanding the full lifecycle of your container runtime — from image storage to system calls — transforms capacity planning from guesswork into informed engineering.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a series of hand-drawn illustrations about Linux concepts that she's been posting daily on Twitter. The drawings cover topics like /proc and other Linux fundamentals, presented in her characteristic accessible visual style. She describes the creative constraints of her drawing ability and how she works within them to communicate technical concepts. The post reflects on the positive response from people discovering Linux features they didn't know about, and her goal to continue the daily drawing practice through November.
key_points: Daily Linux drawings posted to Twitter became a popular series, with readers excited to learn new things about familiar tools like /proc
Creative constraints in drawing ability become a useful puzzle—figuring out which simple visual elements (lightning bolts, stars, hearts) can effectively communicate technical concepts
The drawings are made as vector illustrations using an Android app called 'infinite design', which produces cleaner results than freehand
money_quotes: It makes me really happy to hear 'wow, I didn't know that! That's really cool!'
Drawing these is a fun puzzle – I can't draw most things (a cat? forget it!) so I need to figure out which things are within my capabilities and will communicate what I want.
You will probably also notice that I struggle to keep a consistent font weight :) I'm learning what I think looks good slowly.
posts.key_insight: Creative constraints in visual ability can be turned into a productive puzzle, forcing you to find the simplest possible way to communicate complex technical concepts.

posts.summary: Evans responds to criticism of her simple technical drawings by explaining why they're effective despite not being artistically sophisticated. She argues that comics excel at summarizing complex topics, lowering barriers to learning, and introducing people to concepts they might otherwise overlook. She cites concrete examples: a comic that condensed a 15-minute blog post into 30 seconds of understanding, and a /proc drawing that reached 180,000 people on Twitter. She originally started drawing due to wrist pain but continues because the format demonstrably works for teaching.
key_points: Comics compress complex technical content into quickly digestible summaries that help readers decide whether to invest more time
Simple, approachable drawings can trick people into engaging with topics they'd otherwise dismiss as too advanced or not useful
The effectiveness of the format doesn't depend on artistic skill — it works because it lowers the barrier to encountering new ideas
The drawings serve as entry points that lead people to deeper learning through traditional resources like man pages and Wikipedia
money_quotes: I've found they're, in some situations, such a great way to communicate that I feel silly not taking advantage of it
wow, the blog post took me 15 minutes to understand, but after reading the comic I understood the main ideas in 30 seconds
I can trick people into reading about useful concepts that they might otherwise think are too hard or not useful or not fun
They will probably learn more about it by reading the man page or Wikipedia or some more traditional means. But they learned about it from a small drawing :)
I don't do it because I'm a visual thinker or because I'm a good artist or because I think comics are always the best way to learn. I just do it because it works, and it's fun.
I started drawing things because my wrists hurt a lot and I was too stressed out about it to use my computer after work.
posts.key_insight: The value of simple technical drawings isn't artistic quality but their ability to summarize, lower barriers, and introduce people to concepts they'd otherwise never encounter.

posts.summary: Evans explores what she learned by examining Skyliner, a platform that deploys code to AWS instances, treating it as a case study in good architecture decisions. She discovers that new AWS instances start up faster than expected (under 5 minutes), that Docker can be used simply by avoiding its complex features like container networking and orchestration, and that AWS Application Load Balancers have useful APIs for managing deploys. The post advocates for studying other people's architecture choices as a way to learn, even if you don't use their product directly.
key_points: New AWS instances can start in under 5 minutes, enabling a deploy strategy of launching fresh instances each time, which provides security updates and prevents state leakage
Docker can be used simply by sticking to host networking, syslog logging, and S3-based image distribution — avoiding registries, Swarm, and container orchestration entirely
AWS Application Load Balancers have APIs that let you query instance health and manage connection draining, enabling zero-downtime deploys
Studying how others architect their AWS infrastructure is valuable even if you don't use their product, because good architecture decisions are transferable
Complexity in tools like Docker and AWS comes from using too many features — constraining yourself to a small subset can make them manageable
money_quotes: I often think 'why can't we be as easy to use as Heroku?' and then try to make us a little more like that.
Use containers. Not too much. Mostly for packaging.
--net host means 'use the host networking namespace, don't do any weird container networking stuff'. GREAT.
They don't use a Docker registry. They package the Docker image into a tarball, encrypt it, put it in S3, and then download it onto your machine. Super simple.
It seems like if you're very careful with which features you use, maybe it will work fine!
This week I am weirdly extremely excited about load balancers with APIs, probably more than a normal person should be.
AWS is a super complicated platform. They have like 30 bajillion services you can use.
If someone can write down in a document how a system should work, and that description is workable, then it can save a ton of missteps even if you still have to write all the code.
posts.key_insight: Studying how others have assembled complex platforms like AWS into coherent products is a powerful way to learn which features actually matter and which complexity you can safely avoid.

posts.summary: Evans reviews Martin Kleppmann's paper critiquing the CAP theorem, arguing that CAP is less profound and less useful than its reputation suggests. The theorem's proof is surprisingly simple, and it only applies to linearizable systems, leaving it with nothing to say about the vast majority of distributed systems people actually use. The paper proposes a better alternative that frames the consistency-availability tradeoff in terms of network latency rather than binary partition tolerance. This alternative maps different consistency levels (linearizable, sequential, causal) to how fast reads and writes can be under adverse network conditions, which is far more practical.
key_points: The CAP theorem's proof is extremely simple — it boils down to two computers that can't communicate being unable to stay consistent and available simultaneously
CAP only applies to linearizable systems and has nothing useful to say about common setups like replicated databases, making 'AP' a near-useless label

Kleppmann proposes an alternative framing where consistency levels map to read/write latency under slow networks: linearizable means both are slow, sequential means slow writes but fast reads, causal means both fast
The FLP impossibility theorem is a far more profound result, showing distributed consensus algorithms can get stuck in infinite loops
