Feeling bad about not understanding CAP turned out to be unwarranted — the theorem is genuinely limited in scope and applicability
money_quotes: This system is not linearizable. So the CAP theorem has… uh… nothing to say about this system.
I thought that the CAP theorem was like this complicated deep result in distributed systems theory.
CAP is a useful shorthand for a useful idea, but it's not really that profound.
It turns out that the CAP theorem is actually a relatively small theorem which is only useful in a limited number of cases!
Sometimes my network is not totally down, but communication is SLOW! I would love my distributed systems theory to explain what will happen!
So it was less that I didn't understand it and more that it's actually just not thaaaaaaat useful.
'AP' is not a very useful description for this system since it has some interesting consistency properties, and is quite different from some other 'AP' systems.
posts.key_insight: The CAP theorem is far less useful than its reputation suggests because it only addresses linearizable systems; framing consistency tradeoffs in terms of network latency gives practitioners much better tools for reasoning about the systems they actually use.

posts.summary: Julia Evans compiled a crowdsourced list of Linux topics people want to learn more about, gathered from Twitter responses. The list spans networking, kernel internals, process management, filesystems, security, and more. She reflects on how the list serves multiple purposes: giving her ideas for drawings, reminding her how much there is still to learn, and showing how far she's come. She encourages others to pick a topic and write about it, noting that many of these topics are accessible with a bit of research.
key_points: Crowdsourced lists of what people want to learn reveal both the breadth of Linux knowledge and common gaps in understanding
Even experienced practitioners have significant gaps in their Linux knowledge, and that's normal and exciting rather than shameful
Looking at what you don't know can simultaneously remind you how far you've come and how much is left to explore
Many seemingly intimidating Linux topics are actually accessible if you just start reading about them
Anyone can contribute to technical education by picking an interesting topic and writing about it
money_quotes: there are many things I don't know on this list and it's a cool reminder of how much interesting stuff there still is to learn!

it's also a cool reminder of how far I've come – I at least know where to *start* with most of the things on this list, even if I definitely could not explain a lot of them in detail without looking some stuff up.
you too could write interesting blog posts / drawings on the internet
"what is dmesg even doing" is an interesting topic, and totally possible to learn about!
I just read dmesg on Wikipedia and now I know more!
posts.key_insight: A crowdsourced list of unknown Linux topics is both a roadmap for learning and a reminder that gaps in knowledge are normal, exciting, and fillable by anyone willing to start reading.

posts.summary: This is a brief index post where Julia Evans shares a collection of computer-related drawings she made during November 2016. The post serves as a gallery linking to her daily drawing practice, covering topics including networking. The actual content is primarily visual (images that aren't captured in the markdown), with minimal text. She points readers to drawings.jvns.ca as the permanent home for the collection.
key_points: Julia Evans committed to making daily drawings about computers throughout November 2016
The drawings cover general computing topics and networking specifically
She established a dedicated site (drawings.jvns.ca) as a permanent archive for her visual explanations
money_quotes: I've been making drawings about computers every day in November.
posts.key_insight: Daily practice of creating visual explanations about technical topics is a valuable way to learn and teach computing concepts.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains why the question 'how much memory is my process using?' doesn't have a simple answer, by walking through how virtual memory works on Linux. She covers page tables, the TLB cache, shared memory via copy-on-write, swap, and mmap to show why memory accounting is fundamentally approximate. She then maps this understanding back to the VIRT, RSS, SHR, and %MEM columns in top, explaining what each actually measures. The post resolves a years-long confusion by showing that the complexity isn't arbitrary—it reflects how virtual memory systems actually work.
key_points: Virtual memory addresses are per-process and mapped to physical RAM via page tables, with the CPU itself performing lookups (cached by the TLB)
Shared memory through copy-on-write means any 4KB page could be shared by an arbitrary number of processes, making clean per-process accounting impossible
Not all virtual memory maps to physical RAM—swap and mmap mean some addresses point to disk, which is why VIRT can be vastly larger than RSS
RSS (resident set size) is the closest approximation to 'how much RAM am I using' but still doesn't account for shared memory properly
PSS (proportional share size) via /proc/$PID/smaps could give a fairer count by dividing shared pages among processes, but top doesn't use it
money_quotes: The way memory works on Linux is just fundamentally more complicated than 'process Y is using memory X', so asking 'how much memory is my process using' can only ever have approximate answers.
You have RAM in your computer (i have touched mine with my hands!).
When the CPU comes across this, it'll be like 'what the hell is this, operating system, please fix it'. (the technical term is a 'page fault')
There's no clean way to logically split up shared and non-shared memory. Basically any 4 kilobyte page could be shared or not shared.
VIRT means 'this is how much virtual address space this program has'. This number is not really that useful if you want to know how much RAM you're using.
I've been confused about this for years so it feels nice to actually understand what these numbers mean and why there are so many of them and why it's so confusing.
posts.key_insight: Linux memory accounting is inherently approximate because virtual memory, shared pages, swap, and mmap mean there's no single number that accurately captures 'how much memory a process uses.'

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her 2016 accomplishments in programming, writing, and career growth. She published a new debugging zine, drew daily programming comics throughout November, and gave several conference talks including a RustConf keynote. She switched to an infrastructure team at Stripe where she could dig into networking and systems internals, and wrote her first work blog post. She wrote 87 blog posts and roughly 70,000 words, and distills her year into lessons about asking questions, working with knowledgeable people, and bringing personal passions into work.
key_points: Drawing daily programming comics in November was unexpectedly successful, revealing huge demand for accessible explanations of basic operating system concepts
Switching to infrastructure engineering at Stripe aligned her curiosity about how things work with her day job, letting her understand and improve internal systems
Writing 87 blog posts and producing zines showed that consistent public output compounds into real impact and community recognition
Having flexibility at work to write blog posts, improve job descriptions, and organize events created a virtuous cycle between personal interests and professional contribution
The most basic technical topics often generated the strongest audience reactions, suggesting widespread knowledge gaps in fundamentals
money_quotes: Often the things I go over that I think are relatively basic are the ones where people respond 'wow, I had no idea how that worked, that's really cool!'
It's somewhat shocking to me that some people now refer to me as an 'artist' given my sorta nonexistant drawing skills, but, hey, whatever works.
I was maybe hoping to do even more, but that did not seem to be what the company needed. That's okay!
It's now my job to understand a lot of the nitty-gritty details about how all of our infrastructure works.
At work, figuring out what's important to do and then doing the work to get it done, especially if that work is boring / tedious
I'm trying to get better at designing and iterating on systems
Working on a team of people who know more stuff than me, and listening to what they have to say
posts.key_insight: The most effective career strategy is aligning your genuine curiosity with your work, then sharing what you learn publicly — even the basics — because fundamental knowledge gaps are far more widespread than experts assume.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores what makes UDP interesting by crowdsourcing knowledge from Twitter. She covers UDP's key use cases including DNS, statsd metrics, WebRTC, gaming, and video streaming, while explaining the practical tradeoffs between UDP and TCP. The post highlights that UDP's lack of reliability guarantees makes it useful when speed matters more than delivery certainty, but warns against reimplementing TCP on top of UDP. She notes that the common wisdom about UDP use cases (gaming, video) is more nuanced than it appears, with many applications actually using TCP instead. The post ends with open questions about when UDP truly makes sense versus defaulting to TCP.
key_points: DNS is probably the most important protocol built on UDP, because most DNS requests fit in a single packet making retry logic simple
UDP's lack of connection overhead makes it ideal for high-volume, low-stakes data like metrics (statsd) where losing some packets is acceptable
The common claim that games and video streaming use UDP is more nuanced than expected — many actually use TCP, and the decision depends on whether dropped frames are preferable to delayed frames
UDP packet size limits are practically important because unlike TCP, there's no automatic combining of packets — the 13 root DNS servers exist because that's how many fit in one UDP packet
The general advice is to default to TCP unless you genuinely don't care about packet delivery, ordering, or reliability
money_quotes: Technically speaking it stands for 'user datagram protocol' but I think 'unreliable data protocol' is better because it's funny and probably more accurate anyway.
Another fact about UDP is that if you mention UDP there will be 1000 jokes about dropping packets.
The reason there are only 13 root DNS servers is that DNS uses UDP and that is how many fit inside a single UDP packet!
The idea here is that statsd uses UDP because metrics reporting should be as cheap as possible (sending UDP packets is really fast, there is no connection to manage).
It's possible that, most of the time, the answer to 'when should I use UDP' is 'don't, just use TCP instead, it'll be easier'.
If you really actually do not care if your packets arrive or not and they are all basically independent of each other and the order does not matter at all, maybe that is a good time to use UDP.
UDP is used for video and some games because with TCP you can get huge delays for one dropped packet.
posts.key_insight: UDP's value comes from its simplicity and lack of overhead, but the real-world cases where you should actually use it instead of TCP are narrower than commonly believed.

posts.summary: Julia Evans breaks down the confusing world of container networking into understandable pieces, focusing on the 'every container gets an IP' model used by Kubernetes. She explains how route tables, encapsulation (vxlan and ip-in-ip), and bridge networking work together to make container-to-container communication possible. The post walks through the progression from simple same-network routing to cross-availability-zone encapsulation, and how daemons distribute routes via etcd or BGP. She concludes by mapping these fundamentals to real tools like Flannel and Calico, showing they're just combinations of these basic building blocks.
key_points: Container networking boils down to two core mechanisms: route table entries for same-network communication and encapsulation (vxlan or ip-in-ip) for cross-network communication
Kubernetes requires every container to have its own IP address reachable by any other container in the cluster, which sounds magical but relies on well-understood networking primitives
Route distribution happens either through an etcd cluster or BGP gossip protocol, with daemons on every box keeping route tables updated
Flannel and Calico offer similar capabilities with different implementations — both support encapsulated and non-encapsulated modes, and their limitations on AWS are essentially the same
AWS VPC route tables can solve this problem trivially but hit a hard limit of 50 rules, forcing larger clusters to implement their own routing
money_quotes: One of the hardest things to understand about all this newfangled container stuff is – what is even going on with the networking?!
At first I thought 'how complicated can that be? connecting programs together is simple, right?' Like, there's probably only one way to do it?
It turns out this actually easy peasy! You just add an entry to another table. It's all tables.
Luckily, in AWS you have basically no way to configure the routers, so it doesn't matter if we don't know how they work!

We want to send a packet to the container IP 10.4.4.4, and it is on the computer 172.9.9.9. But because the computer is far away, we have to address the packet to the IP address 172.9.9.9. Woe is us! All is lost! Where are we going to put the IP address 10.4.4.4?

Most of these container networking things will set up all these routes and tunnels and stuff for you, but I think it's important to understand what's going on behind the scenes, so that if something goes wrong I can debug it and fix it.
I don't know what software defined networking. All of this helps you do networking differently, and it's all software, so maybe it's software defined networking?
posts.key_insight: Container networking isn't magic — it's built from just a few well-understood primitives: route tables, encapsulation, and bridge interfaces, combined in different ways by tools like Flannel and Calico.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at Systems We Love 2016, highlighting her favorite talks from the conference. She shares brief reviews of five talks covering topics from Go's race detector to NTP assumptions to DNS protocol design. Each mini-review focuses on what she learned or found interesting, emphasizing clarity of explanation as what makes a talk great. The post serves as a curated guide to the conference's recorded talks.
key_points: Kavya Joshi's talk on Go's race detector stood out for making an unfamiliar topic immediately understandable
The NTP talk revealed a key assumption most people don't think about: that network latency is symmetric between two computers
DNS was deliberately designed so that unknown record types must be passed on verbatim, enabling gradual protocol upgrades
Mainframes still play a role in US government technology infrastructure, with ongoing integration challenges
money_quotes: She explains how the Go race detector works super super clearly. I didn't know anything about the topic, and now I do!
It made me want to learn more about NTP!
The DNS spec goes out of its way to say 'hey, if you don't understand a record, you have to pass it on verbatim'.
This is a great example of a design decision that helps you upgrade a protocol gradually over time!
I've been confused by awk for a long time and this helped me understand a little better how it's used.
posts.key_insight: The best systems talks make unfamiliar topics immediately understandable by clearly laying out the assumptions and design decisions behind the systems we use every day.

posts.summary: Julia Evans defines her three rules for programming experiments: they don't have to be good, they don't have to work, and you have to learn something. She emphasizes that these experiments are typically short (a few hours), require no maintenance, and are driven by curiosity rather than producing usable software. This makes them far more tractable than contributing to open source projects. The program itself is incidental—what matters is exploring questions and understanding new concepts.
key_points: Programming experiments have three rules: they don't have to be good, don't have to work, but you must learn something
Short time commitment and zero maintenance burden make experiments more sustainable than open source contributions
The program is incidental—the real output is understanding, not software
Curiosity-driven questions like 'does this work?' and 'what happens if I do this?' are the motivation
money_quotes: Rules of programming experiments: it doesn't have to be good, it doesn't have to work, you have to learn something.
This makes it a lot more tractable for me than stuff like 'work on actual real open source projects' which I have exactly 0 energy for after programming at work.
It's an interesting reason to write programs because the program itself is totally incidental.
I usually put them on the internet, but certainly not for anyone to *use* or anything.
Often the experiment doesn't take that much time (maybe 4 hours on a Wednesday evening), and when I'm done I don't have to maintain it or anything.
posts.key_insight: The most sustainable way to learn through programming is to treat code as disposable—freeing yourself from quality and maintenance obligations lets you focus entirely on understanding.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares insights from a conversation with her friend Amy Hanlon about how individual team members (not just managers) can actively make their teams better. The key practices include communicating work publicly in shared channels, asking questions openly, scheduling small brainstorming meetings, and approaching discussions with genuine openness to being wrong. Evans emphasizes that teams aren't static — members have real power to shape team dynamics. The post reframes team quality as something every individual can influence through deliberate communication habits.
key_points: Communicating work publicly (e.g., in shared Slack channels) creates opportunities for others to contribute key information and helps everyone understand decision-making processes
Asking questions openly — about priorities, approaches, and tradeoffs — is a powerful team-building practice that anyone can do
Scheduling small brainstorming meetings with an open mind leads to better ideas, since initial ideas are often wrong
Approaching discussions with genuine willingness to be wrong ('I don't know if I'm right, I'm ready to change my mind') is more productive than defending a position
Team members have real power to shape team dynamics — teams aren't static things that just happen to you
money_quotes: I thought the idea that you can make your team awesome as a team member (not as a manager!) was really awesome.
If you write down what you're struggling with and then write down how you fixed it (even if you fix it yourself) then people can learn from what you found!
We agreed that we don't think that our initial ideas about a thing are usually right ('I'm probably wrong 70% of the time').
Coming into a discussion with the attitude 'I don't know if I'm right, I'm ready to change my mind' (and really honestly believing that) has been really really helpful to me.
I sometimes think of teams as kind of a static thing, like either 'this team is working well together' or 'this team isn't working well together, I don't know how to fix it'.
I tried to argue to my manager a while ago 'I can't make my team more awesome, I don't know how!' I think I was probably wrong to argue that.
Making teams more awesome is not trivial but I think it is something I have done before, at least a little, and something I would like be even better at in the future.
posts.key_insight: You don't need to be a manager to make your team better — deliberate practices like public communication, open questions, and genuine willingness to be wrong give every team member power to shape team dynamics.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares an idea for a programming book that would combine her comic-style drawings with short written explanations. Each pair of pages would focus on one concept, with a comic on one side and signposting to further resources on the other. She wants something that inspires curiosity rather than serving as a comprehensive textbook, more like 'Thing Explainer' than a technical reference. The book would likely focus on Linux topics.
key_points: The book format pairs a comic drawing with a short written page that signposts readers toward deeper learning resources
The goal is inspiration and delight rather than comprehensive coverage of a topic
Writing a book that explains one big complicated concept end-to-end is really hard, so a modular one-idea-per-spread format is more appealing
The concept sits between a comic book and a textbook, combining visual appeal with practical pointers
money_quotes: It would be less of 'this is a textbook you use to learn everything about Linux' and more of 'this is a book you can open to be inspired and delighted and find a new interesting thing to learn about'.
Writing a book which explains a big complicated concept ('here is all of networking explained') is really hard.
On the other side of the page there are a few words about why the thing is cool and ways you could learn more.
So more like 'thing explainer' than 'why's poignant guide to ruby'.
Here is the way to more delightful things about this topic!
posts.key_insight: A programming book doesn't have to be a comprehensive textbook — it can be a modular collection of self-contained spreads designed to spark curiosity and point readers toward deeper exploration.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes an experiment in giving a tech talk about machine learning in production using a printed zine instead of slides. She created an 8-page zine based on Google's 'Rules of Machine Learning' document, printed 70 copies at home, and distributed them at a local meetup. She found the format worked well and was comparable in preparation time to slides. The post shares both digital and printable versions of the zine and reflects on the experience of combining physical media with technical presentations.
key_points: Giving a talk without slides is possible by substituting a physical zine, though it felt slightly unusual
A short sketchy zine takes about 2 hours to create, roughly the same as equivalent-quality slides
Physical handouts at meetups create a more engaging and fun experience than directing people to a website for slides
The zine distills concepts from Google's 'Rules of Machine Learning: Best Practices for ML Engineering' into an accessible illustrated format
money_quotes: Instead of making slides, I wrote a short 8-page zine illustrating some concepts about machine learning, printed 70 copies on my home printer, and gave them out to everyone who came!
giving a talk without using slides at all is possible. It was a little weird, but I will probably experiment with it again in the future.
I can write a short, sketchy zine in 2 hours (which is the same amount of time it takes me to write similar-quality slides!)
giving away zines at a meetup is really fun, way more fun than being like 'uh, the slides are on my website'
posts.key_insight: Physical zines can replace slides as a talk format, taking comparable preparation time while creating a more memorable and enjoyable experience for both speaker and audience.

posts.summary: Evans argues that obsessing over choosing the 'best' software tool is paralyzing and largely unproductive. She proposes an alternative framework borrowed from capacity planning: only change something if it's too expensive, too hard to operate, or not doing its job. If a system passes those three tests, it doesn't matter whether something theoretically better exists. She suggests that after confirming something will work, you should just pick whatever the team feels best about, since building something that works well enough is already hard enough.
key_points: The 'what is the Best Thing' framework for technical decisions is often paralyzing rather than helpful
Three practical criteria for evaluating systems: cost, operational burden, and whether it does its job
If a system meets all three criteria, searching for something 'better' is wasted effort when real problems exist elsewhere
When multiple options will work, just pick the one people feel best about — team sentiment matters
What counts as 'the system works' can be redefined when someone has a genuinely better idea
money_quotes: I am going to make an argument that 'it literally does not matter what is the best'.
I find these 3 criteria a lot easier to reason about than the 'Choose The Best Thing' framework.
So, it literally does not matter at all if they are the Best System!
Saying that there's a 'tradeoff' doesn't help me at all!
Nobody has ever told me 'you should make technical decision by just figuring out what will work and then just pick whatever is the most popular', but it seems kind of reasonable to me?
I find the idea that 'it doesn't matter what the best thing is' kind of freeing.

Building a system that works well enough within the constraints that you have is already REALLY HARD. So it seems unreasonable to additionally require that that system also be the Best Possible Thing.
posts.key_insight: Instead of searching for the theoretically best tool, evaluate whether your current system is too expensive, too hard to operate, or failing at its job — if it passes all three, move on to real problems.

posts.summary: Julia Evans dissects an SSL/TLS certificate by downloading one from mail.google.com using openssl and walking through its components. She explains the key fields including domain names, expiry dates, public keys, and signatures, then covers how certificate signing chains work and why certificate authorities matter. The post also introduces certificate transparency as a mechanism to detect rogue certificates. She concludes by acknowledging that SSL configuration is complex but that tools like Let's Encrypt make it accessible.
key_points: SSL certificates can be downloaded and parsed from the command line using openssl, making them less mysterious than they seem
Certificate signing creates a chain of trust from your computer's pre-installed root certificates down to the specific domain certificate
Certificate transparency is a public logging system designed to catch rogue or unauthorized certificates that CAs might issue
The 'common name' field in certificates is counterintuitively not the one you should trust — the Subject Alternative Name field is what matters
Certificate authorities are trusted by default on your computer, but that trust can be abused, which is why systems like certificate transparency exist
money_quotes: I want to make sure that I'm actually talking to mail.google.com and not some other random server on the internet owned by EVIL PEOPLE.
I am not a security person and I am not going to give you security advice for your website (want to know what TLS ciphers you should use? I have no idea!!!).
Basically anyone could make a certificate for mail.google.com. I could make one right now! But if I gave you that certificate, you would have no reason to believe that it is a real certificate.
I have /etc/ssl/certs/Staat_der_Nederlanden_EV_Root_CA.pem on my laptop. Some certificate from the Netherlands! Who knows!
If some random person across the street signed a certificate, my computer would be like 'I have no idea who you are', and reject the certificate.
There is all this stuff like OLD_TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256 on that page (for real, that is a real thing.).
posts.key_insight: SSL certificates become far less mysterious when you download one, parse it with openssl, and understand the chain of trust from root CAs to the domain you're visiting.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her excitement about PaintsChainer, a machine learning tool that automatically colors black and white images. She experiments with it on her zine artwork and traces photos, exploring how ML art tools can augment creative ability. The post reflects on what makes certain AI art tools compelling versus others, concluding that the best tools are ones where the output still feels like something you personally made. She contrasts PaintsChainer favorably with deepart.io, noting that creative control over the process matters more than raw impressiveness.
key_points: PaintsChainer uses machine learning to automatically color black and white drawings, producing surprisingly compelling results
The most exciting ML art tools are ones where the output feels like something you made yourself, not just algorithmic transformation
Combining multiple accessible tools (tablet tracing + ML coloring) can produce results far beyond one's unaided skill level
Deepart.io's style transfer is impressive but less personally satisfying because you have less creative control over the result
Neural network applications that augment human creativity are more exciting than ones that replace it
money_quotes: I want to use magical art tools to make things that feel like I made them.
I find this really cool because I can make an image with my hands with a lot less skills than I would have needed without this magical technology.
It feel more like a thing that I made because I have a lot of creative control over what it looks like.
I spent a bunch of time playing with deepart.io but couldn't make anything I really was excited about.
This kind of thing is probably the application of neural networks that makes me the most excited.
posts.key_insight: The most compelling ML art tools are ones that augment your creative process while preserving your sense of authorship, letting you make things that still feel like yours.

posts.summary: The post explores the question of maximum packet sizes on the internet, starting from UDP's theoretical 65535-byte limit and explaining why real-world packets are constrained to 1500 bytes by Ethernet's Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU). Evans walks through what happens when packets exceed the MTU (they get dropped or fragmented), how Path MTU Discovery works using ICMP packets and the 'don't fragment' flag, and notes that jumbo frames allow larger packets in controlled environments like datacenters.
key_points: UDP packets have a theoretical maximum of 65535 bytes (2^16 - 1), but Ethernet frames limit actual transmission to 1500 bytes (the MTU)
Packets exceeding the MTU will either be dropped entirely or fragmented into smaller pieces, with fragmentation being more problematic for UDP than TCP
Path MTU Discovery works by sending packets with a 'don't fragment' flag and listening for ICMP responses that reveal where fragmentation would occur
Jumbo frames allow larger packets in controlled environments like datacenters where you own all the network infrastructure
The Linux tool tracepath can discover the path MTU between two points on a network
money_quotes: So, if I try to send a 30,000 UDP packet using an Ethernet protocol with an MTU of 1500 bytes, what will happen? We can't send 30,000 bytes in 1500 bytes. It doesn't work.
So one of two things will happen: either the packet will get dropped (not sent at all) or fragmented.
Ethernet frames can only contain 1500 bytes of data. This is called the 'maximum transmission unit' or 'MTU'!
On the internet in general, you can't expect to be able to send packets above 1500 bytes, but if you own the whole datacenter and all of your network infrastructure? Why not!
I'm definitely still confused about how packet fragmentation works and in what cases it makes you lose packets.
posts.key_insight: While UDP theoretically allows packets up to 65535 bytes, Ethernet's 1500-byte MTU is the practical limit on the internet, and packets exceeding it will be dropped or fragmented.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores a quirky Unix behavior where 'cd //' preserves the double slash in the path while 'cd ///' collapses to a single slash. She discovers through the POSIX specification that three or more leading slashes are collapsed to one, but two leading slashes are explicitly left as implementation-defined. The post uses stat to confirm that / and // point to the same inode, deepening the mystery of why the distinction exists in the spec at all. She ends with an open question about the historical reason, later updated with a Stack Overflow link explaining the behavior.
key_points: In bash, 'cd //tmp' preserves the double slash in $PWD, while 'cd ///tmp' collapses to '/tmp' — but fish normalizes both to single slash
The POSIX cd specification explicitly says to replace three or more leading slashes with one, but is silent on two leading slashes
A separate POSIX spec states that pathnames beginning with two successive slashes 'may be interpreted in an implementation-defined manner'
Despite the different path representations, stat confirms / and // have the same inode number and are the same file
money_quotes: What is //tmp? What is happening? Why is cd ///tmp different from cd //tmp?
So! We can replace "three or more leading / characters with a single slash". That does not say anything about what to do when there are 2 / characters though.
So you can define //tmp to mean whatever you want? Like it could be different than /tmp? Why?
A pathname that begins with two successive slashes may be interpreted in an implementation-defined manner
They both have the same inode number (256) so they are the same file.
posts.key_insight: The POSIX specification deliberately treats paths with exactly two leading slashes as a special case with implementation-defined behavior, creating a surprising inconsistency where 'cd //' and 'cd ///' behave differently.

posts.summary: Evans breaks down the paper 'A Neural Algorithm of Artistic Style' to understand how neural network style transfer actually works mathematically. She explains that the technique repurposes an object recognition network (VGG) by defining 'content' as the activation vector at a deep layer and 'style' as the Gram matrix of feature maps at earlier layers. The process then uses gradient descent to find an image that minimizes the difference between the target content and target style. She's honest about not fully understanding the Gram matrix intuition but finds the approach exciting because it suggests other weird art ideas might also work if formulated mathematically.
key_points: Style transfer repurposes an image recognition network (VGG) that was designed for object classification, not art generation
Content is defined as the activation vector at a deep network layer (conv4_2), while style is defined as the Gram matrix of pairwise inner products between feature vectors at earlier layers
The major innovation of the paper was defining 'style' mathematically via the Gram matrix rather than just picking a single layer
The actual image generation works by starting from white noise and using gradient descent to minimize a loss function combining content and style differences
The mathematical formulation of style was non-obvious and surprising, suggesting that other creative neural network applications might work if properly formulated
money_quotes: I started telling this idea to my partner, and he was like 'julia, that sounds like you just read some hacker news headlines about generative neural networks and made this up'. Which was essentially correct!
Neural networks stuff is all math, so what is the mathematical definition of 'style'?
The purpose of this network was not to generate art stuff at all! This network's job is to do image recognition.
If we want to, we could pick one of those vectors arbitrarily and declare 'You know, I think that vector represents the content' of the image.
The exciting thing to me about this is – this is a weird thing that I would not have thought would work.
Maybe some of my other weird ideas about neural networks and art would also work, if I tried them out, as long as I find a reasonable mathematical way to formulate them!
They had to do a surprising-to-me mathematical operation to define the style/texture of a painting (this weird Gram matrix thing where you take all these inner products).
posts.key_insight: Neural style transfer works by mathematically defining 'content' and 'style' as different representations within an image recognition network, then using gradient descent to find an image matching both targets.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on what makes job opportunities exciting to candidates, arguing that the best recruiting strategy is finding people for whom your specific job would be a genuinely exciting opportunity. She observes that generic recruiting emails about roles similar to what she's already done are far less compelling than opportunities to do something new and different. She challenges the notion that hiring is about finding 'the best engineers' for 'the best companies,' arguing instead that great hiring is about matching people with roles that represent genuine growth opportunities. She references Dan Luu's observation that competing for the same talent pool as everyone else is expensive and ineffective.
key_points: Recruiting emails fall into two categories: jobs that sound like what you've already done, and exciting opportunities to do something different — only the latter gets a response
The narrative of 'hiring the best engineers' and 'finding the best company' is a false framework — hiring is about matching people with opportunities that excite them
People get excited about jobs for wildly different reasons: open source work, new technical domains, scale challenges, leadership opportunities, or entirely different fields
If you're competing for the same candidates as everyone else, you need to ask why they'd pick you — instead, find people for whom your specific work is uniquely compelling
Opportunity isn't just for junior developers — experienced engineers constantly encounter new domains that represent genuine growth
money_quotes: Sometimes I see this narrative around hiring which is like 'we are trying to hire the best engineers, the best engineers are trying to find the best companies' — But this idea of the 'best engineer' and the 'best company' don't really.. make any sense to me at all?
If you're trying to hire the same people as everyone else, why would they work for you? They have so many interesting opportunities!
It feels like it makes more sense to figure out 'hmm, who would be excited about this kind of project and where would we find them?'
And then it is more like.. a fun puzzle where you try to figure out who is great who would be excited about the thing?

This doesn't just mean 'find junior developers and train them' – like, I'm not a 'junior developer', and there are all kinds of things that I have not done yet that are interesting opportunities for me!
I'd been doing machine learning before, but at a company where none of my code actually got used for anything by anyone, which was demoralizing
posts.key_insight: The most effective recruiting isn't about finding 'the best' people — it's about finding people for whom your specific job represents a genuinely exciting opportunity to grow.

posts.summary: Julia investigates a puzzling production issue where machines with plenty of free memory were swapping and processes were being OOM killed. After trying swappiness tuning and disabling swap without success, she discovers that cgroup memory limits were the root cause — processes were constrained to less memory than the machine had available. The post walks through her debugging process of elimination and the key insight from reading dmesg output. She reflects on whether swap makes sense on production servers at all, given its unpredictable interactions with cgroups.
key_points: Cgroup memory limits can cause OOM kills even when the host machine has plenty of free memory available
The vm.swappiness sysctl controls how aggressively Linux swaps versus reclaiming filesystem cache, but it won't help when cgroup limits are the real constraint
Processes in a cgroup that exceed their memory limit can still use swap rather than being killed immediately, which contradicts a common mental model
The dmesg output from OOM kills contains detailed memory usage information for everything in the cgroup, making it a critical debugging tool
Swap on production servers introduces unpredictable behavior and may not provide meaningful benefits
money_quotes: In my head I had an axiom 'if a computer has free memory, there is no reason the processes on it should get OOM killed'. Obviously there was something I did not understand.

My model of memory limits on cgroups was always 'if you use more than X memory, you will get killed right away'. It turns out that that assumption was wrong! If you use more than X memory, you can still use swap!
I think the reason that swap is considered bad in production systems is that it has weird/unpredictable effects and predictability is good.
It's really important to me to understand what's happening on the computers that I work with – when something happens like 'this computer is swapping and I don't know WHY' it bothers me a lot.
I finally looked at the output of dmesg and then there was this magic word: cgroups. Everything became clear pretty quickly.
posts.key_insight: Cgroup memory limits create per-container memory boundaries that can cause swapping and OOM kills even when the host machine has free memory, breaking the intuitive assumption that available RAM prevents memory pressure.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her experiment of writing down everything she accomplished over three years at her job. She used a structured format covering projects, design work, collaboration, and non-job tasks, spending half a day producing 13 pages of details. The exercise revealed patterns in her growth, like the shift from guided work to independence and the late adoption of design documents. She found the fact-based approach more useful than self-evaluation, and recommends the practice for career reflection.
key_points: A structured written inventory of career accomplishments reveals patterns invisible in day-to-day work
Fact-based reflection ('we did X, here's why, here are the results') is more productive than evaluative reflection ('I did a good job')
The exercise surfaced a gap between stated values and actual behavior — she valued documentation but wasn't writing much
The written record served practical purposes: onboarding a new manager and providing evidence for advocacy
money_quotes: what have I really done here? Am I doing things that are worthwhile? What should I be doing differently?
I was pretty honest because, well, it was for me and I wanted to understand what was working and what wasn't.
I really do not write a lot of documentation at work, even though it's something I thought I valued.
It was really useful to look at projects that failed / didn't work out the way I had hoped and think about whether I could learn anything.
Sometimes I worry that I haven't accomplished enough good things ("3 years? what have I even been doing?").
I really like the fact-based approach – "we did X! here is the reason we did it! here were the results!" instead of trying to evaluate everything ("I did a good job").
posts.key_insight: Writing a fact-based inventory of your career accomplishments reveals growth patterns, exposes gaps between values and actions, and combats the feeling that you haven't done enough.

posts.summary: The post introduces ftrace, a Linux kernel feature for tracing kernel function calls that has been available since kernel 2.6. Evans walks through practical examples using trace-cmd, a friendlier frontend to ftrace's filesystem-based interface, demonstrating how to trace individual functions like do_page_fault, all functions for a specific process, and kernel events like scheduling switches. She explains that ftrace works by dynamically inserting tracing instructions into kernel functions at runtime using pre-compiled NOP slots, meaning zero overhead when not in use. The post also points readers to Brendan Gregg's perf-tools and kernelshark for more accessible ways to leverage ftrace.
key_points: ftrace traces Linux kernel function calls and has been available since kernel 2.6 (~2008), making it usable even on older systems
The raw filesystem interface at /sys/kernel/debug/tracing is cumbersome, but trace-cmd provides a much more usable command-line frontend
Function graph tracing instruments both entry and exit of functions, showing call hierarchies and timing for each function
ftrace works by dynamically modifying function code at runtime, inserting tracing instructions into pre-compiled NOP slots, so there's zero performance impact when not tracing
Beyond function tracing, ftrace supports kernel events like sched_switch that don't map cleanly to function calls
money_quotes: Unlike strace and perf, ftrace isn't a program exactly – you don't just run ftrace my_cool_function. That would be too easy!
When Linux allocates memory, it often does it lazily ("you weren't really planning to use that memory, right?").
Reading the function trace was definitely an easier way to find that out than by reading the kernel code, and it's nice to see how long all of the functions took.
The kernel will insert some extra instructions in the assembly for that function to notify the tracing system every time that function gets called.
I think ftrace is a bit of a niche tool (it's definitely less broadly useful and harder to use than strace) but that it's worth knowing about.
Being able to see all these function calls is pretty cool, even if I don't quite understand them.
The tradeoff here is that they're easier to use, but you're limited to things that Brendan Gregg thought of & decided to make a tool for. Which is a lot of things!
posts.key_insight: ftrace lets you trace Linux kernel function calls with zero overhead when disabled, and trace-cmd makes it practical to use for understanding what the kernel is actually doing when debugging system-level problems.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares the blogging principles that help her consistently publish content on the internet. She advocates for honesty about knowledge gaps, writing for a past version of yourself, staying positive, sticking to personal experience rather than prescriptive advice, and writing about things right after learning them. She argues that admitting what you don't know actually builds trust rather than undermining credibility. The post emphasizes that not every post needs to be popular and that frequently posting small interesting things matters more than optimizing for page views.
key_points: Being honest about knowledge gaps builds credibility rather than undermining it — readers appreciate someone who knows a lot but isn't afraid to admit they don't understand everything
Writing for a past version of yourself is the easiest way to find an audience and create genuinely useful content
Writing about things right after learning them captures the freshness of confusion and makes it easier to communicate what's difficult for beginners
Making minor mistakes in public isn't a big deal — people send corrections and are nice about it
Resisting the temptation to optimize for page views and instead frequently posting small interesting things is more sustainable
money_quotes: I like doing this because it lets me write about things a little out of my comfort zone without worrying about misleading someone.
The surprising and delightful thing to me about being honest on this blog is – a lot of the time people love it.
It turns out that making minor mistakes in public isn't that big of a deal – people sometimes send me corrections, and they're always really nice about it.

I don't usually like to give advice ('you should do X'). Instead I usually just tell stories about my own experience with something, and then people can decide for themselves if that will work for them.
At this point I'm pretty good at learning a thing and then turning around after 2 days and teaching it to someone else.
I follow all kinds of blogs where I am only interested in like 30% of the posts. I LOVE those blogs.
It's always tempting to try to optimize my posts to get the most possible page views, but it's more important to me to just be able to frequently post small things that I find interesting.
posts.key_insight: Being honest about what you don't know lets you write outside your comfort zone and paradoxically builds more trust with readers than projecting false expertise.

posts.summary: Julia Evans catalogs the quirks and safety tips every bash scripter should know, from variable assignment pitfalls to the critical importance of `set -eu` flags. She covers basic constructs like for loops, if statements, and functions, but focuses on the non-obvious gotchas that trip people up even after years of use. The post emphasizes defensive practices: always quoting variables, using `set -e` to stop on errors, `set -u` to catch unset variables, and running shellcheck to lint scripts. She advocates for keeping bash scripts simple rather than fighting the language's weirdness.
key_points: Unquoted bash variables cause subtle, hard-to-debug failures—always quote them to avoid word splitting surprises
The `set -eu` flags transform bash from a language that silently continues past errors into something safer, and `set -o pipefail` catches failures in pipelines
Shellcheck is a linter that catches bash quirks automatically and knows more best practices than most scripters
Bash syntax has genuinely confusing design choices, like `[` being a program while `[[` is syntax, and spaces around `=` in assignment changing meaning entirely
The pragmatic approach is to keep bash scripts simple, use defensive practices, and not fight the language's inherent weirdness
money_quotes: It's very important that you don't put spaces around the = sign – VARIABLE= 2, VARIABLE = 2, and VARIABLE =2 are not syntax errors, but will all do different unwanted things.
I put quotes because i know bash will betray me otherwise.
There are cases where it's okay to just use $X instead of "$X", but can you really keep track of when it's okay and when it's not okay? I sure can't.
If one of the programs in my script fails, I do not want it to just merrily keep going and do possibly undefined / questionable things! That is terrifying.
rm -rf "$DIRECTORY/*" will actually run rm -rf /* if $DIRECTORY is unset.
We get truly into bash quirk territory: [ is a program (/usr/bin/[) but [[ is bash syntax. [[ is better.
Shellcheck knows about way way more bash scripting best practices than I do.
I just try to not write very complicated bash scripts, stick to some of the best practices here, and don't worry too much about it.
posts.key_insight: Bash is full of non-obvious gotchas that bite even experienced users, but a few defensive habits—quoting variables, using set -eu, and running shellcheck—make it manageable without needing to master every quirk.

posts.summary: Julia Evans introduces tc (traffic control), a Linux tool for deliberately adding network latency to test how applications behave under slow conditions. She demonstrates adding 500ms delay to her laptop's wireless interface and discovers something surprising: HTTPS pages degraded far less than HTTP pages under high latency, because HTTP/2 (which requires HTTPS) enables parallel requests. She also notes tc's usefulness for testing server behavior, such as observing how a DNS client handles a slow DNS server, while warning about the side effects of slowing down all packets on a machine you're SSHed into.
key_points: tc (traffic control) is a simple Linux command that can add artificial latency to all outgoing packets on a network interface
Under 500ms added latency, HTTP page loads degraded 10x while HTTPS/HTTP2 loads degraded far less, demonstrating HTTP/2's parallel request advantage
TLS connection reuse dramatically reduces latency impact — an HTTPS page loaded in 1.1s with a reused connection vs 5s with a fresh one, compared to 33s for HTTP
tc is useful for testing how services handle network latency in controlled environments, like observing DNS client timeout behavior
Adding latency to all packets on a remote machine also affects your SSH connection, which is an easy gotcha to forget
money_quotes: This week I learned how to make my internet slower on purpose!
That's weird, right? The HTTP version got 10x slower, but the https version didn't slow down as much.
HTTP/2 lets you do a lot more requests in parallel, so you're not slowed down as much.
I might conceivably wait 5 seconds for a page to load but I feel like I'd get too bored in 30 seconds.
So it's really neat to see that HTTPS and HTTP/2 are really possibly making things faster for people with high latency!
You probably don't want to add a delay to every packet on a machine in production, you might have a bad day.
If you add a delay to every packet on a machine you're SSHed into, your SSH connection is also sending packets, so your SSH connection also gets slow.
I think it's cool that you can pretend to be on a machine that is Really Far Away by just running one command on your laptop!
posts.key_insight: Deliberately adding network latency with tc reveals that HTTPS with HTTP/2 handles high-latency conditions dramatically better than plain HTTP, making a compelling performance case for HTTPS adoption.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her stream-of-consciousness notes from day 1 of netdev, a Linux networking conference. The talks covered XDP (eXpress Data Path) usage at Cloudflare and Facebook for DDoS mitigation and load balancing, showing how XDP provides dramatic performance improvements over iptables and IPVS. She documents her learning process openly, including things she didn't understand, conversations at lunch about RX queues and tc capabilities, and a networking performance workshop covering memory allocation bottlenecks and AF_PACKETv4. The post captures how much new material she absorbed despite much of it going over her head.
key_points: XDP enables packet filtering much earlier in the Linux networking stack, giving Cloudflare and Facebook dramatic performance gains (10x faster than IPVS at Facebook) for DDoS mitigation and load balancing
Network cards can have multiple RX queues that can be split between the kernel and userspace programs, enabling kernel bypass for specific traffic
The tc tool can do far more than rate limiting — it can program network cards to do hardware offload of packet processing like checksums
AF_PACKETv4 is a work-in-progress effort to make tools like tcpdump faster by eliminating packet copying into userspace memory
There's a sharp throughput cliff in latency-vs-throughput graphs where systems simply refuse to process more requests and latency spikes
money_quotes: If you want to filter out packets very quickly, you don't want to go through all those steps! You want to just read packets off the network card, be like 'NOPE not that one!', and move on really quickly.

I think I'm learning more at this conference than most conferences I've been at in the last few years. A lot of it goes above my head, but I kind of like that! It means there are a lot of new ideas and terminology.
I thought it was interesting that they talked about it like it's a normal thing because I think of it as like.. something totally magical that doesn't exist in real life.
He said that XDP is 10x faster.
I like tcpdump a lot so anything that could make tcpdump faster is something I am excited about!
I know that there's a tradeoff between latency and throughput for any system, and it was nice to see graphs of that tradeoff. And it was interesting to see such an abrupt throughput cliff.
posts.key_insight: XDP represents a fundamental shift in Linux networking by allowing packet filtering to happen as early as possible in the stack, and companies like Cloudflare and Facebook are already using it in production for massive performance gains in DDoS mitigation and load balancing.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her notes from the second day of the netdev conference, covering several networking topics including Google's BBR TCP congestion control algorithm, running the Linux kernel networking stack in userspace, XDP/eBPF capabilities and myths, and the surprisingly powerful tc tool. She explains how BBR differs from traditional TCP congestion algorithms by estimating bandwidth rather than just reacting to packet loss, and documents interesting benchmarking challenges encountered when running BBR in the Linux Kernel Library project. The post also covers practical details about programming network cards with tc and the importance of rigorous network benchmarking.
key_points: BBR is a new TCP congestion control algorithm from Google that estimates bandwidth instead of interpreting all packet loss as congestion, potentially replacing CUBIC
The Linux Kernel Library project lets you use the Linux networking stack as a userspace library on any OS, but performance tuning (timing resolution, buffer sizes) matters enormously
XDP is a powerful packet processing framework that runs at the device driver level, is stateless and allocation-free, with use cases from DDoS prevention to high frequency trading
tc can not only manipulate packets in software but also program network cards to perform those operations in hardware, which is surprisingly accessible from the command line
Network benchmarking is unreliable — the same test under the same conditions can produce wildly different results, making repetition essential
money_quotes: TCP algorithms today all fundamentally interpret packet loss as 'congestion'. The idea is that – if packets are being lost along the way, then there must be too much data being sent, so you should slow down.
So basically instead of using Linux, you take all the Linux networking code and just use it as a library!
Being able to program your network card to do stuff for you just by running a thing on the command line seems pretty magical.
He was like 'is that a serious question' but he answered me!
He tested this 25 times, and 3 of those 25 times, the results were REALLY BAD. Like the second TCP stream only got to 100Mbps bandwidth even though there was a lot more available.
I'm still pretty vague about all of this but I know more than yesterday so that's cool
high frequency trading ('but they won't tell us what they did, it's their secret sauce :)')
posts.key_insight: Modern Linux networking is evolving rapidly with tools like BBR, XDP, and tc that challenge fundamental assumptions about congestion control, packet processing, and hardware programmability, but benchmarking these advances reliably remains surprisingly difficult.

posts.summary: Evans recaps a netdev conference tutorial on building a DDoS prevention filter using eBPF and XDP. The tutorial walked through a complete working example: an XDP program running in the kernel that filters packets by IP address, a userspace command-line tool for managing the blacklist, and per-CPU counters tracking blocked packets. She explains the key concepts—eBPF as a kernel virtual machine without loops, XDP as an early-stage packet filter running at the network driver level, and BPF maps as the communication mechanism between kernel and userspace. The XDP approach achieved 9.7 million packets/second compared to iptables' 4.5 million, demonstrating both the performance and flexibility advantages.
key_points: XDP programs run at the network driver level before normal kernel networking, making them extremely fast for packet filtering—more than twice as fast as iptables in their benchmark
eBPF programs interact with userspace through key-value maps, and the BPF map files in sysfs mean the command-line tool doesn't need root access
The kernel performs static analysis on eBPF code to verify bounds checking and memory safety before allowing it to run, rejecting programs that could access data they shouldn't
The entire XDP packet-parsing and filtering program is only 270 lines of C, partly because kernel data structures like ethernet headers can be reused directly
eBPF's lack of loops guarantees bounded execution time, which also makes programs easier to understand and reason about
money_quotes: eBPF is a virtual machine in the kernel.
eBPF doesn't have loops, so every eBPF program will finish within some bounded execution time.
In particular eBPF isn't turing complete :) :)
If you don't do your bounds checking properly, the kernel will reject your code.
This whole program that parses the packet, finds the IP address in the IP header, looks it up in the BPF map, and returns is only 270 lines of C! That seems really reasonable!!
It's just an ELF file, it's not magic.
This XDP program really was more than twice as fast as iptables!
This made the idea of writing eBPF programs to make my kernel filter packets seem WAY more concrete/feasible.
posts.key_insight: XDP and eBPF make custom kernel-level packet filtering both fast and accessible, with a complete DDoS filter implementable in just 270 lines of C that outperforms iptables by more than 2x.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recaps the final day of the netdev 2.1 conference, covering nftables (the replacement for iptables), IoT mesh networking, and a networking daemon for data center reliability. She highlights nftables' unified approach to packet classification and its nearly 2x performance improvement over iptables. The post also explores low-power networking protocols like 6LoWPAN and the surprising constraints of devices that can only send 100 bytes per day. She closes with reflections on the conference culture, appreciating its deep specialization and friendly community while noting the lack of women's t-shirt sizes.
key_points: nftables unifies the fragmented iptables/ip6tables/arptables/ebtables infrastructure into a single packet classification framework with nearly 2x performance gains
There are 2.5 million lines of network device driver code in the Linux kernel, and bandwidth demands may grow 1000x in the next decade
Low-power lossy networks (LLNs) operate at extreme constraints, with some wide-area networks limited to 100 bytes per day
The conference culture of requiring original research talks and filling slides with code rather than cat gifs created a uniquely valuable learning environment
A question about integrating eBPF into nftables highlighted the challenge of users needing to learn too many different networking tools (tc, nftables, etc.)
money_quotes: my main practical takeaway from today was 'oh man, maybe I don't ever have to learn iptables, I should maybe learn nftables instead?'
there are some wide-area networks where you might only be able to send 100 bytes per day
a thing that exists in the world is 'kinetically powered lightswitches' – they only get power when you flip them, and only enough to maybe send a packet before they die
the wifi in this room isn't working well because there are too many access points which are transmitting at too high power, and they're interfering with each other
on the balance I think I prefer some text/code to cat gifs if the person is trying to explain a code thing to me
they don't accept recycled talks, your talk is supposed to be kinda original research / a new development in linux kernel networking
the conference was super-specialized and focused (not for a general developer audience at all!) and that was really awesome because I got to learn a lot about linux kernel networking
Blogging this conference has been fun! It made me pay closer attention than I probably would have otherwise, and now I can remember what I learned about!
posts.key_insight: Deep specialization at a conference — where every attendee knows far more than you about the topic — creates an exceptionally valuable learning environment precisely because it exposes you to things you've never heard of.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that individual software engineers can make meaningful culture changes at work, even without management authority. She shares several examples from her own experience: creating interview rubrics that became company-wide standards, publishing 'what to expect' guides for candidates, writing more inclusive job descriptions, and improving team documentation. Her partner's experience shows how writing one detailed postmortem can seed an entire postmortem culture. She acknowledges that some changes require top-down authority, but maintains that leading by example on focused, small efforts can compound into significant improvements.
key_points: Individual engineers have real power to change workplace culture by leading by example rather than waiting for management directives
Starting with personal practice (writing your own rubric, your own postmortem) creates templates that make it easier for others to follow
The right approach to change depends on timing and context — introducing standards when none exist is different from changing established processes
Focused, one-at-a-time efforts work better than scattered attempts to improve everything at once
Some systemic changes genuinely require top-down authority and can't be driven from the bottom up
money_quotes: It's also true though that individual software engineers have some power!
Doing the work myself to start is easier than asking somebody else to change how they're doing something.
It's way easier to write a postmortem for an incident if you have an existing template to work with that your coworker wrote last week.
We basically wanted to write down the same tips we'd give to a friend about how to prepare for the interview, except, well, just put them on the website and give them to everyone.
Lots of the best people I know didn't have any experience with the specific technologies we work with before they started here (including me!) so why should it be a requirement?
All companies are a work in progress and things cannot be good unless we make them that way together.

Even if what I want is for all of our interviews to have standard evaluation guidelines, I can't make that happen all at once, it's easier to say 'ok, I'm just going to make some guidelines for myself to use'.
posts.key_insight: The most effective way for individual engineers to change workplace culture is to start by doing the thing themselves — creating a template, writing a document, setting a standard — which makes it far easier for others to follow.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that traditional statistics education focused on formulas and tests like chi-squared is less useful for programmers than computational, nonparametric methods. She points out that classical statistical tests make assumptions (like normal distribution) that often don't hold for real-world data. Instead, methods like bootstrapping and shuffling let you get statistically valid answers by writing programs rather than applying formulas. The post curates a list of recommended resources for learning statistics through programming, including talks, free textbooks, and books by Allen Downey and Larry Wasserman.
key_points: Classical statistical tests make assumptions about data distribution that often don't hold in practice, making them unreliable for everyday programming questions
Nonparametric statistics is a subfield that requires fewer assumptions and is actually easier to use than traditional parametric methods
Computational methods like bootstrapping and shuffling replace formulas with programs, giving statistically valid answers that are more intuitive for programmers
The historical reliance on formulas was a product of limited computational resources — modern computers make fundamentally different statistical approaches possible
money_quotes: One of the biggest problems with tests like the chi-squared test is that they make a lot of assumptions about how your data was generated.
There's a whole subfield of statistics devoted making less assumptions ('nonparametric statistics'). And even better – that subfield is actually easier to use than regular statistics.
Even though I know a lot of math, I often find programs more intuitive than formulas.

A lot of the formulas you can use to do statistics make a lot of assumptions, and then you can quickly use a formula to calculate the statistical thing you want. This was necessary when people had really limited computational resources.
Today we have computers! So we can use really dramatically different statistical methods than people used in the 19th century.
posts.key_insight: Nonparametric statistical methods like bootstrapping and shuffling are both more practical and more intuitive for programmers because they replace assumption-laden formulas with computation.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains why she switched to the fish shell three years ago and why she'll never go back to bash. The post highlights fish's killer feature: context-aware autosuggestions from shell history that adapt based on your current directory. She also praises fish's syntax highlighting for catching typos, its more readable loop syntax, and its simpler history search. The core appeal is that fish provides excellent defaults with zero configuration, unlike zsh which requires extensive setup to match. She acknowledges fish isn't POSIX compliant but considers it a minor inconvenience since you can just type 'bash' when needed.
key_points: Fish's autosuggestions from shell history are contextual, changing suggestions based on your current directory, which makes them dramatically more useful
Fish requires no configuration to be great, unlike zsh+oh-my-zsh which can match features but requires extensive setup
Fish's syntax highlighting catches typos in real-time by coloring invalid commands red and valid ones blue
Fish's control flow syntax is more readable than bash, using 'end' consistently instead of 'done', 'fi', or 'esac'
The lack of POSIX compliance is a minor tradeoff since switching to bash temporarily takes one second
money_quotes: The thing I like personally about fish is that I don't have to configure it! I just install it on all my computers, it comes with a lot of awesome features out of the box, and I don't need to do any configuration.
My fish configuration file literally just sets some environment variables and that's it.
I LOVE that it takes the directory I'm in into account and it means that the autocompletions work so much better.
It's so READABLE. All the control flow statements just end with end, not done or fi or esac.
also apparently it automatically generates completions by parsing man pages? that's so smart and amazing.
If I ever need to run a bash script or something I just switch to bash for a couple minutes, it takes like 1 second to type bash.
posts.key_insight: The best developer tools are the ones that work brilliantly out of the box without requiring configuration, and fish's context-aware autosuggestions from shell history are a killer feature worth switching shells for.

posts.summary: Evans champions the 'no feigning surprise' social rule from the Recurse Center, which says you shouldn't act shocked when someone doesn't know something. She argues this applies even when you're genuinely surprised, because the negative effect on the other person is the same regardless of intent. She suggests transmuting surprise into excitement as a positive alternative. The post uses personal anecdotes to illustrate both sides—being the teacher who doesn't judge and the learner who isn't embarrassed by gaps in knowledge.
key_points: Feigning surprise when someone doesn't know something serves no educational purpose and only makes people feel worse
Even genuine surprise should be suppressed because the negative effect on the learner is the same whether the surprise is performed or real
Surprise can be transmuted into excitement, turning a potential shame moment into an enthusiastic teaching opportunity
Practicing this rule makes you both a better teacher and a more confident learner who isn't embarrassed by knowledge gaps
money_quotes: Feigning surprise has absolutely no social or educational benefit: When people feign surprise, it's usually to make them feel better about themselves and others feel worse.
A key trick here is to not act surprised even if you're actually surprised.
Whether you're performing surprise or just genuinely a bit surprised, it has the same negative effect!
And I can sometimes transmute surprise into excitement, like 'what's the stack?' 'oh it's really interesting let me tell you about it!!'
I'm less likely to be embarrassed these days when someone is surprised that I don't know something.
posts.key_insight: Acting surprised when someone doesn't know something—whether the surprise is genuine or performed—shuts down learning, but redirecting that energy into excitement creates a moment of connection and teaching.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces a new 12-page zine about tcpdump, a network packet analysis tool she once found intimidating but now uses comfortably. She describes her journey from being confused by tcpdump's output and man page to feeling confident using it for networking problems. The zine is offered as an early-access purchase for $10, with a free release planned later. She also cross-promotes her earlier networking fundamentals zine and shares preview pages showing that only a few command line arguments are really essential.
key_points: tcpdump went from intimidating and incomprehensible to being a go-to tool once Julia learned the basics
The zine distills tcpdump down to just 12 pages, emphasizing that only a few command line arguments really matter
She's experimenting with a paid early-access model followed by a free release to support her zine-making work
Networking fundamentals are a prerequisite — she points readers to her earlier Networking! ACK! zine for background
money_quotes: I'd run tcpdump, it would print a bunch of incomprehensible output, I'd look at the man page, and I'd run away.
I'll see a networking problem, think "oh, no big deal, I'll just fire up tcpdump!", and be one step closer to figuring it out.
I decided to write a short 12-page zine to explain tcpdump basics so that you too can realize "hey, this isn't so bad!!".
It turns out there are only a few that you really need to know about!
I'm doing an experiment with this one where – you can buy it today for $10 (early access!), and then I'll release it for free on this blog a little later on.
posts.key_insight: tcpdump seems overwhelming at first, but the core command line arguments you actually need fit on a couple of pages, making it far more approachable than its man page suggests.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains the Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header after enabling it on her own site. She walks through why simply redirecting HTTP to HTTPS isn't sufficient—browsers can still leak cookies or be tricked by malicious networks on that first HTTP request. She demonstrates how GitHub uses the HSTS header and the browser preload list to ensure HTTPS is always used. She also covers the practical tradeoff: once you enable HSTS, you're committing to serving HTTPS indefinitely, since browsers will refuse to load the HTTP version.
key_points: Redirecting HTTP to HTTPS isn't enough because the browser still makes one insecure request first, potentially leaking cookies or being intercepted
The HSTS header tells browsers to never visit the HTTP version of a site again after the first secure visit, and the preload list eliminates even that first insecure visit
Enabling HSTS is a one-way commitment—if you stop serving HTTPS, your site becomes inaccessible to visitors until the max-age expires
Mixed HTTP/HTTPS content is dangerous because any HTTP content on a page can be tampered with, even if secure elements like payment forms use HTTPS
ISPs can inject ads into HTTP sites, so HTTPS ensures visitors see the site exactly as intended
money_quotes: If I go to http://github.com, by default my browser will send my GitHub cookies unencrypted, which is bad! Somebody could steal them!
If a sketchy free wifi portal starts serving a fake 'github.com' site, then I don't want my browser to be tricked.
Someone could replace the secure payments thing with an Evil Bad Payments Thing. That would be no good!
Sometimes ISPs will inject ads into sites. I don't want ads injected into my site! I want people to see my site exactly how I intended them to see it.
Basically browsers will really refuse to visit the HTTP version of your site after you turn on HSTS. So you'd better make sure that you can keep having a HTTPS version of your site forever.

Technically the fact that my site uses HTTPS doesn't mean that everything on the site is from me – Cloudflare actually owns the TLS certificate for my site, and they can (and do!) add stuff to my website.
posts.key_insight: HSTS and the preload list exist because redirecting HTTP to HTTPS still leaves a window where the first request is insecure, and closing that window requires a permanent commitment to serving HTTPS.

posts.summary: Julia Evans recommends Bubblesort Zines by Amy Wibowo, a collection of computer science zines aimed at teenage girls that cover topics like sorting algorithms, networking, cryptography, and image processing. She highlights the 'How does the internet' zine for its impressive range of topics and the newly released 'Pixel Perfect' zine on image processing. Evans emphasizes that these zines explain complex concepts clearly without dumbing them down, making CS accessible to audiences who might not see themselves in traditional CS materials. She draws from her own experience of wanting to learn programming as a teenager but not knowing where to start.
key_points: Bubblesort Zines by Amy Wibowo fills a gap for CS education aimed at teenage girls, covering topics from sorting algorithms to cryptography to image processing
The zines explain complex concepts clearly without dumbing them down, respecting the intelligence of young readers
The mission is to make computer science accessible and inclusive to people who might not think there's a place for them in CS
Evans personally vouches for the quality, noting the 'How does the internet' zine covers an impressive range from carrier pigeons to TCP routing
money_quotes: Teenage girls are smart and curious people who are interested in learning complicated things about science & technology!
She explains all these things in a clear and fun way without dumbing down the ideas.
I definitely didn't understand the basics of how TCP worked when I was teenager learning HTML (or even when I started working as a professional web developer!)

Often material that's written for young people gets dumbed down or doesn't go into all the interesting details, but of course 13-14 year olds can understand a lot of complex concepts, as long as you explain them clearly!
Let's make sure that these tools & skills are accessible to everyone.
The goal of these zines is to show people that interesting computer science concepts like encryption, image processing, and computer networking can be for them.
posts.key_insight: Complex computer science concepts don't need to be dumbed down for young or non-traditional audiences — they just need to be explained clearly and presented in an inclusive, welcoming way.
