posts.summary: Julia Evans debugs why her website was displaying garbled content by tracing the issue through HTTP headers. She discovers the response was gzipped but missing the Content-Encoding: gzip header, which meant browsers couldn't decode it. The root cause was an old Apache .htaccess rule she'd written years ago to always serve gzipped content regardless of what the client requested, which interacted badly with Cloudflare's caching. She fixed it by removing the .htaccess rule and clearing the Cloudflare cache, though she never fully determined why the Content-Encoding header was being stripped.
key_points: The website was serving gzipped content without the Content-Encoding: gzip header, causing browsers to display garbled data
An old Apache rewrite rule was unconditionally serving pre-gzipped files regardless of whether clients requested gzip encoding
The interaction between the misconfigured Apache backend and Cloudflare's caching layer compounded the problem
Enabling HSTS the previous week prevented her from simply bypassing Cloudflare to diagnose the issue, since her backend didn't have HTTPS
Even after fixing the issue, the exact mechanism that stripped the Content-Encoding header remained unknown
money_quotes: How do you check if a file is gzipped? The easiest way is probably to try to unzip it and see if it works.
I happen to know that those are the 2 bytes every gzip stream starts with!
When a site sends gzipped data, it's meant to send a Content-Encoding: gzip header to say 'hey, this content is gzipped, unzip it before displaying it!'
This tells Apache 'hey, always send gzipped replies no matter what!!'

Hilariously, I turned on Strict-Transport-Security last week, which means my site only works if it's served over HTTPS. And my normal webhost isn't set up with HTTPS yet, so I can't just turn off Cloudflare.
I still don't know where the Content-Encoding: gzip header went! Did Cloudflare remove it? Did my webhost stop serving it for some reason? I have no idea!
posts.key_insight: Small configuration decisions made years apart—pre-gzipping files for bandwidth savings, adding Cloudflare, enabling HSTS—can interact in unexpected ways that create debugging puzzles where no single component is clearly at fault.

posts.summary: Julia Evans compiled a collection of handwritten books after asking Twitter whether it would be too weird to write a fully handwritten book. She gathered examples spanning technical topics like electronics, computing, and math, as well as cookbooks, self-help, and hiking guides. The examples share a common thread: handwriting enables seamless integration of diagrams, illustrations, and visual elements without fighting typesetting tools. The collection ranges from Scott McCloud's Understanding Comics to Dijkstra's EWD manuscripts to the Moosewood Cookbook, demonstrating that handwritten books have a rich history across many genres.
key_points: Handwritten books share a common trait: they naturally integrate visual elements like diagrams, illustrations, and comics alongside text
The ease of including diagrams is a major advantage of handwriting over typeset systems — you can just draw one instantly without fighting your tools
Handwritten technical books have a long history, from Forrest Mims' electronics guides to Dijkstra's thousand-plus EWD manuscripts
The format spans far beyond technical writing — cookbooks, self-help, hiking guides, and art books all use handwriting effectively
money_quotes: One of my favorite things about writing by hand is exactly this – if you want to include a diagram, you can just do it instantly!! You don't need to fight with your typesetting system for days.
Dr. Kaufman wrote the book. I mean, he wrote it, the actual words you see, with a pen. He also drew the pictures, diagrams, flow charts, and things.
The right way to read a FORTRAN book is by a series of skimming passes. Each time through you pick up a bit more of the nitty-gritty detail.

Like most of us, Dijkstra always believed it a scientist's duty to maintain a lively correspondence with his scientific colleagues. To a greater extent than most of us, he put that conviction into practice.
They consist entirely of reproductions of Wainwright's manuscript, hand-produced in pen and ink with no typeset material.
posts.key_insight: Handwritten books have a rich tradition across many fields because the format naturally encourages the seamless integration of visual elements like diagrams and illustrations that would be difficult to achieve with conventional typesetting.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces the public release of her 'Let's learn tcpdump' zine, which was previously available as a $10 early access experiment. She shares the results of the paid early access model: 190 people bought it for a total of $1,970, including one enterprise license at $100. The positive response and revenue validated that people value her educational content enough to pay for it. She credits collaborators who helped with lettering, review, and illustration.
key_points: The tcpdump zine is now freely available online with a pay-what-you-can print version
The early access experiment generated $1,970 from 190 buyers, validating paid educational content
Revenue from zine sales enables paying illustrators and other collaborators
One buyer purchased a $100 enterprise license to distribute at a conference
money_quotes: It feels really cool that people actually think the stuff I make is worth money, and it makes me feel motivated to make more zines like this.
And it makes it easier for me to do things like pay illustrators to make awesome illustrations!
Paying artists is really cool.
190 people bought it in all, for a total of $1970.
1 person bought an 'enterprise license' ($100) so that they could print it out and give it out to a lot of people at their conference.
posts.key_insight: Charging for early access to educational technical content can validate its value and fund future creative work, while still making it freely available to everyone.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the Linux system calls select, poll, and epoll for monitoring multiple file descriptors, drawing from The Linux Programming Interface book. She explains that select and poll share the same underlying kernel code but differ in their interfaces, with poll performing better for sparse file descriptor sets. The key insight is that epoll dramatically outperforms both by having the kernel remember which file descriptors to monitor, avoiding redundant work on each call. She walks through epoll's three-step API (create, ctl, wait) and shows real strace output from a Go program using epoll for DNS queries. The post also covers level-triggered vs edge-triggered notifications and links to libraries like libuv, libev, and Go's runtime that use epoll under the hood.
key_points: poll and select use fundamentally the same kernel code, but poll can perform better with sparse file descriptor sets because select must iterate through all descriptors up to the highest numbered one
epoll dramatically outperforms select/poll at scale because the kernel remembers the list of monitored file descriptors between calls, avoiding redundant setup work
epoll works with pipes, sockets, and other I/O primitives but notably not regular files, because files lack the simple readable/writable semantics of streams
Event loops and green thread runtimes (Node.js via libuv, Go's runtime, Python's gevent via libev) all use epoll on Linux for non-blocking I/O
The performance difference is stark: at 10,000 file descriptors, poll/select take ~930-990 units vs epoll's 0.66
money_quotes: Instead of spending all CPU time to ask "are there updates now? how about now? how about now? how about now?", instead we'd rather just ask the Linux kernel "hey, here are 100 file descriptors. Tell me when one of them is updated!"
The first surprising thing I learned from this chapter are that poll and select fundamentally use the same code.

Every time you call select or poll, the kernel needs to check from scratch whether your file descriptors are available for writing. The kernel doesn't remember the list of file descriptors it's supposed to be monitoring!
Files are weird! You can write to the middle of a file! So it doesn't really make sense to say "there's new data available for reading in this file".
Writing test programs is important: quite a few kernel and glibc bugs have been uncovered while writing test programs during the preparation of man pages.
I learned quite a few new things about select & epoll by writing this post!

One small thing I like about my job is that I can expense programming books! This is cool because sometimes it causes me to buy and read books that teach me things that I might not have learned otherwise.
posts.key_insight: epoll outperforms select and poll because it asks the kernel to persistently track file descriptors rather than re-specifying them on every call, making it the foundation of modern event-driven servers on Linux.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her journey learning Kubernetes over six months, moving from confused reader to someone actually setting up a cluster. She explains the architecture by breaking it down into components: the kubelet runs containers on nodes, the API server writes to etcd, the scheduler assigns pods to machines, and the controller manager handles higher-level abstractions. The central insight is that etcd is Kubernetes' brain — every component watches etcd for work, does it, and writes state back. She emphasizes that understanding fundamental networking concepts is essential for debugging Kubernetes issues.
key_points: Every Kubernetes component is stateless; all state lives in etcd, and components communicate by watching and writing to etcd
The architecture becomes understandable when you learn components individually: kubelet, API server, scheduler, and controller manager each have focused responsibilities
Kubernetes networking requires genuine understanding of overlay networks, DNS, iptables, and namespaces — you can't just google error messages
Kubernetes components like DNS and overlay networking can themselves run inside Kubernetes, which only needs five core pieces to schedule containers
When something breaks, identifying which controller is responsible and checking its logs is the key debugging strategy
money_quotes: Every component in Kubernetes (the API server, the scheduler, the kubelet, the controller manager, whatever) is stateless. All of the state is stored in a key-value store called etcd, and communication between components often happens via etcd.

When I understood that basically everything in Kubernetes works by watching etcd for stuff it has to do, doing it, and then writing the new state back into etcd, Kubernetes made a lot more sense to me.
One thing that kind of blew my mind was that relatively core Kubernetes components (like the DNS system, and the overlay networking system) can run inside of Kubernetes!

If I had to debug networking issues in a Kubernetes cluster without understanding a lot of the networking fundamentals, I think I would just be googling error messages and trying random things to fix them and it would be miserable.

I found it a lot easier to understand when presented like this — look, you can run the kubelet by itself! And if you have a kubelet, you can add the API server and just run those two things by themselves!

I thought about it for a minute, thought 'hmm, the scheduler is in charge of scheduling pods', and went to look at the scheduler's logs. It turned out that I'd reconfigured the scheduler wrong so it wasn't starting anymore!
posts.key_insight: Kubernetes becomes comprehensible once you understand that every component is stateless and all coordination happens through watching etcd for work, doing it, and writing the result back.

posts.summary: Julia Evans breaks down iptables basics after being motivated to learn them through Kubernetes work. She explains how to view all iptables rules (requiring four separate commands or using iptables-save), walks through the nat and filter tables, and dissects a specific MASQUERADE rule from the Kubernetes source code. She highlights that the iptables man page is incomplete and points to better resources like iptables.info for understanding concepts like SNAT and MASQUERADE. The post demystifies how network address translation works for container networking, drawing an analogy to home networks.
key_points: Viewing all iptables rules requires running four separate commands across different tables (filter, nat, mangle, raw), or using iptables-save for a complete dump
The iptables man page is insufficient for understanding key concepts like MASQUERADE — external resources like iptables.info fill critical gaps
MASQUERADE solves the same problem as SNAT (rewriting source IPs for packets leaving a private network) but automatically uses the host's IP address instead of requiring a hardcoded one
Kubernetes creates large numbers of iptables rules for container networking, making understanding iptables practically necessary for Kubernetes work
money_quotes: I don't like being scared of things and understanding a few of the basics of iptables seems like it shouldn't be scary!
The first surprising thing I learned about iptables is that to look at all the iptables rules you have to run 4 commands.
I googled iptables masquerade like 5 times and was like 'why doesn't the iptables man page even say the word masquerade at all?!'
iptables is kind of frustrating because if you don't have the right rules you can end up in situations like 'oh well, no networking works at all, oops'.
I am a little less intimidated by iptables than I was last week so that's good!
I like being able to see everything by running one command!
posts.key_insight: iptables is less intimidating once you know how to dump all rules with iptables-save and find better documentation than the man page for understanding concepts like MASQUERADE.

posts.summary: Evans shares her excitement about reading Martin Kleppmann's 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' and explains the concept of log-structured storage she learned from it. She breaks down two fundamental approaches to database storage: log-structured engines (which append writes sequentially for faster writes) and page-oriented engines like B-trees (which update data in place). She illustrates the concept with a two-line bash script that functions as the 'world's simplest database.' She also clarifies the distinction between log-structured storage and write-ahead logs, explaining how WALs protect against data loss during crashes.
key_points: Database storage fundamentally divides into two approaches: log-structured (append-only, faster writes) and page-oriented (B-trees, faster reads)
A functional database can be built with just two bash functions that append to and grep a text file, illustrating the core idea of log-structured storage
Write-ahead logs are distinct from log-structured storage — both types of storage engines can use WALs to prevent data loss on crash
The tradeoff between LSM and B-tree storage shows up clearly in benchmarks: LSM has higher write throughput, B-tree has higher read throughput
The word 'consistency' has at least five completely unrelated meanings in database contexts, and ACID's 'C' is about application-level invariants, not linearizability
money_quotes: I seriously thought atomicity in databases was the same idea as atomicity in concurrent programs like an 'atomic instruction' and it's a TOTALLY DIFFERENT THING.
It's really helpful to know that there are actually 5 completely unrelated uses of the word 'consistency' when talking about databases.

Basically this appends to a text file (called `database`) every time you write, and greps that text file for the latest update every time you read. I tried it! This is a totally functional database :)
The idea here is that this way you don't need to make random disk writes – you can just write to the end, and so writes can be a lot faster.
Writing to disk is expensive. Because of that, databases will often just do writes to memory, and then save their state to disk later. This is smart and efficient!
But if you crash, then you can lose data. Losing data is bad. To avoid losing data, you can just log all your writes to disk by appending to a file.
posts.key_insight: Database storage engines split into two fundamental approaches — log-structured (fast sequential writes) and page-oriented (fast random reads) — and understanding this distinction unlocks comprehension of real-world database design tradeoffs.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her excitement about NLP tutorials written by Allison Parrish, a creator of computer-generated language art. The tutorials cover text generation with Tracery, text parsing with spaCy, and understanding word vectors. Evans walks through each tutorial, highlighting practical examples like identifying characters in Pride and Prejudice and averaging word vectors. She frames these three tools as accessible building blocks for anyone wanting to experiment with text programming.
key_points: Tracery is a library that solves practical text generation problems like handling articles ('a' vs 'an') in generated sentences
spaCy can parse text to identify sentence boundaries, parts of speech, and grammatical constructs like prepositional phrases
Word vectors allow programmatic operations on words, like averaging 'day' and 'night' to get 'evening'
These three tools (Tracery, spaCy parsing, spaCy word vectors) form accessible building blocks for text experimentation
money_quotes: NLTK is a fantastic library, but it's also a writhing behemoth: large and slippery and difficult to understand.
The cool thing I learned from this is that you can programmatically 'average' words like 'day' and 'night' to end up with 'evening'!

I think these 3 things (tracery for generating sentences, spacy for parsing text, and spacy (again) for seeing which words are similar to each other) seem like a super awesome way to get started with playing with text!
You could do this by writing like "I'm a %s, not a %s", but you'll run into problems fast because it's "I'm an apple", not "I'm a apple".
So it seems to have done a good job of identifying the characters in Pride and Prejudice! Neat!
posts.key_insight: Text generation, parsing, and word vectors are three accessible building blocks that make it easy to start experimenting with natural language programming.

posts.summary: Evans reframes on-call duty from a dreaded obligation into a powerful learning opportunity for software engineers. Drawing from a Charity Majors Twitter thread, she catalogs the specific skills being on call teaches: understanding real failure modes, building monitoring instincts, learning system limitations, developing judgment about severity, and designing for reliability. She defines 'on call' broadly as being responsible for your code when it breaks, not necessarily midnight pages. The post argues that the feedback loop of writing code and watching it fail in production is uniquely valuable for building engineering intuition that carries forward to every future system you design.
key_points: On-call experience teaches you which failure modes actually matter in practice, replacing the unhelpful mental model of 'anything could go wrong' with calibrated intuition about real risks
The pain of debugging without visibility is the strongest motivator to build monitoring and diagnostics into systems from the start
Being responsible for production failures forces you to understand adjacent systems you'd otherwise treat as black boxes
On-call builds judgment about severity triage — knowing which problems are urgent versus which can wait — a skill that otherwise seems mysterious
Seeing real distributed systems failures (like S3 eventual consistency) teaches lessons that reading documentation alone often doesn't
money_quotes: It's important to have a better model of system failure than 'anything could go wrong, protect against everything!' because often you have to prioritize where to spend your time.

There's nothing quite like a system breaking, being in charge of fixing it, and having no way of seeing what's wrong to convince you of the value of building monitoring and logging into your application.
It used to be really mysterious to me how some engineers could just tell you 'yeah, that's not a big deal' and be.. right about it?
A huge part of our jobs as software engineers is to design systems that continues working for your customers even when things don't happen quite as your expected.
Being on call means I can't pick and choose favorite/comfortable subjects avoiding hard/unhappy ones. I'm forced to stretch and learn.
I feel like it's a great privilege to be able to write software and see how it holds up in practice over the course of months/years.
Seeing issues like this in practice helps me understand what 'eventually consistent' systems look like when they fail and remember to write my code with that in mind next time.
posts.key_insight: Being on call creates an irreplaceable feedback loop between writing code and seeing how it fails, building engineering intuition about real failure modes, monitoring needs, and system design that you can't get any other way.

posts.summary: Julia Evans experiments with recording short screencasts as an alternative to traveling for conference talks. She records three 2-minute demos covering /proc, strace (applied to Python's asyncio library), and tcpdump (for DNS queries), all done in a single take with no editing. The post is a brief announcement sharing the results and noting how surprisingly easy the process was. She hints she may make more in the future.
key_points: Short screencasts can replace travel-heavy conference talks for sharing technical knowledge
The three demos cover core Linux debugging tools: /proc, strace, and tcpdump
Low production effort (one take, no editing) can still produce useful technical content
Making technical content accessible doesn't require polish — just doing it is enough
money_quotes: I like giving short talks, but travelling to give talks is so much work. What if I could give talks from my house?
I recorded 3 2-minute screencasts (nothing fancy, 1 take, no editing) showing demos of /proc, strace, and tcpdump.
These were surprisingly easy to do and some people seemed to like them so maybe I'll do more.
posts.key_insight: You don't need travel or production polish to share technical knowledge — quick, unedited screencasts from home can be just as effective as conference talks.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a tiny whack-a-mole game she built while learning Vue.js. The game uses a simple data structure (a grid of 1s and 0s) with Vue.js handling the DOM updates automatically. She contrasts this with her usual jQuery approach, which she describes as producing disastrous callback-heavy code. She finds Vue.js appealing for small interactive programs because it's simpler than React for her needs.
key_points: Vue.js provides a cleaner model for small interactive programs compared to jQuery's callback-heavy approach
Defining a simple data structure and letting the framework handle DOM updates makes code easier to reason about
Vue.js feels less complicated than React for simple interactive projects
Learning by building tiny, fun projects is an effective way to explore new tools
money_quotes: Usually when I write Javascript I use jQuery and make a lot of callbacks and the code I write is sort of a disaster.
I'm excited about vue.js right now because it feels like an easier way to make tiny interactive javascript programs like this one.
I just need to define a data structure for my game state. Vue.js automatically takes care of updating the HTML when my game state updates.
This is also how React works but vue seems less complicated to me than React and I only want to do simple things anyway.
posts.key_insight: Reactive frameworks like Vue.js simplify small interactive programs by letting you focus on data structures rather than manually wiring up DOM updates.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares notes from a Papers We Love talk about the original BPF paper and modern eBPF. She explains how BPF solved packet filtering by moving filtering logic into the kernel and using control flow graphs instead of inefficient tree evaluation. The post traces BPF's evolution from packet filtering to eBPF, which can attach programs to kernel functions, userspace functions, network cards, and more. She walks through how tcpdump converts filter expressions into BPF bytecode that gets JIT-compiled to native code. The piece highlights eBPF's ability to hook into virtually any kernel or userspace function through kprobes and uprobes, making it a powerful tracing and monitoring tool.
key_points: BPF solved two problems: eliminating expensive userspace packet copying by filtering in the kernel, and replacing inefficient tree-based filtering with control flow graphs
BPF programs are compiled to bytecode, verified by the kernel, then JIT-compiled to native code that often runs only 100-200 CPU instructions per packet
eBPF extends BPF far beyond packet filtering — programs can attach to kernel functions (kprobes), userspace functions (uprobes), network cards (XDP), tracepoints, and more
eBPF programs can't access arbitrary kernel memory but can communicate with userspace through BPF maps, maintaining a safety model while enabling powerful tracing
money_quotes: if you filter in userspace, it means you have to copy all the packets into userspace, copying data is expensive
BPF programs run on these arrays of bytes. They're not allowed to have loops but they can have smart stuff to figure out the length of the IP header
It'll often run only 100-200 CPU instructions for each packet that needs to be processed, which is super fast!
You can pick a function (any function!) in the Linux kernel and execute a program that you write every time that function happens. This seems really amazing and magical.
I sort of knew you could attach eBPF programs to kernel functions, but I didn't realize you could attach eBPF programs to userspace functions! That's really exciting.
But BPF has been around for a long time! Now we live in the EXCITING FUTURE which is eBPF.
posts.key_insight: eBPF transforms BPF from a packet filtering mechanism into a general-purpose framework for safely running custom programs in the kernel, enabling tracing and monitoring of virtually any kernel or userspace function.

posts.summary: Julia Evans breaks down the confusing landscape of Linux tracing systems into three clear categories: data sources (kprobes, uprobes, tracepoints, USDT probes), mechanisms for collecting data (ftrace, perf_events, eBPF, systemtap, LTTng), and user-facing frontends (perf, bcc, trace-cmd). She explains how probes dynamically modify assembly at runtime while tracepoints are compiled into programs, and walks through how each mechanism extracts data from the kernel. The post concludes that eBPF is the most promising direction for newer kernels, while perf trace remains the simplest tool for tracing system calls.
key_points: Linux tracing systems become understandable when split into three layers: data sources, collection mechanisms, and frontends
Probes (kprobes/uprobes) dynamically modify assembly at runtime, while tracepoints are compiled in and have near-zero overhead when inactive
eBPF is the most powerful and exciting collection mechanism because it's built into Linux, requires no kernel modules, and lets you define custom aggregation programs
The tracing landscape is fragmented but each piece serves a specific role — knowing which layer a tool operates at helps you choose the right one
For practical use, perf trace handles system calls simply, while kprobes via eBPF or ftrace give deeper kernel visibility
money_quotes: You can split linux tracing systems into data sources, mechanisms for collecting data for those sources, and tracing frontends.
A probe is when the kernel dynamically modifies your assembly program at runtime (like, it changes the instructions) in order to enable tracing. This is super powerful (and kind of scary!).
Basically kprobes let you dynamically change the Linux kernel's assembly code at runtime (like, insert extra assembly instructions) to trace when a given instruction is called.
eBPF is cool because it's part of Linux (you don't have to install any kernel modules) and you can define your own programs to do any fancy aggregation you want so it's really powerful.
Being able to know what's going on in the kernel is a good superpower.
Ftrace is a kind of janky interface which is a pain to use directly.
posts.key_insight: The confusing Linux tracing ecosystem becomes navigable once you understand its three layers: data sources (where tracing data originates), collection mechanisms (how data leaves the kernel), and frontends (the tools you actually use).

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces a new zine about Linux tracing tools, created as a companion to a previous blog post on the topic. The zine is 12 pages and available in both a digital reading version and a print version. It's a characteristically quick, visual format for making Linux tracing concepts accessible.
key_points: The zine distills the content from her longer Linux tracing tools blog post into a concise visual format
Available in two formats: a screen-readable PDF and a print-ready version
Demonstrates her iterative approach to technical education — blog post first, then zine
money_quotes: I wrote a really quick zine out of the linux tracing tools post from yesterday.
posts.key_insight: Complex technical topics like Linux tracing can be distilled into short, visual zine formats to make them more accessible.

posts.summary: Evans investigates how the Kubernetes scheduler works internally after encountering a bug where pods got stuck in Pending state forever. She traces through the scheduler's source code to discover it uses an event-driven queue system rather than a simple polling loop, which is an optimization for performance at scale. The bug turned out to be a missing error handler call that prevented failed pods from being re-queued. She explores the concept of Kubernetes 'informers' and the deliberate design decision not to resync the scheduler, since maintainers prefer correctness bugs to be surfaced rather than hidden. The post doubles as a demonstration of how to reverse-engineer a complex system's internals by reading source code with specific questions in mind.
key_points: The Kubernetes scheduler uses an event-driven queue rather than polling all pods, primarily for performance reasons at scale (scheduling 30,000 pods went from 2 hours to 10 minutes).
Kubernetes 'informers' are a key abstraction that provide add/update/delete notifications and shared caches, used by most controllers for efficiency.
The scheduler deliberately never resyncs because maintainers believe resync hides correctness bugs that should be found and fixed instead.
When reading unfamiliar code, concatenating files together and having specific questions to answer makes the process much more productive.
The event-driven design introduces failure modes that simpler polling doesn't have — if error handling misses a case, pods can be permanently lost from the queue.
money_quotes: It's also an illustration of how to go from 'how is this system even designed I don't know anything about it?' to 'okay I think I understand the basic design decisions here and why they were made' without actually.. asking anyone.

If for whatever reason a pod fails to get scheduled, there's nothing in here yet that would make the scheduler retry. It'd get taken off the queue, it fails scheduling, and that's it. It lost its only chance!
If resync ever fixes a problem, it means there is an underlying correctness bug that we are hiding. I do not think resync is the right solution.
So it makes sense to me that it would be too slow to query for all 30,000 pods in your system every time you want to schedule a new pod, and that you'd actually want to do something more complicated.
I've definitely had to practice 'read the documentation and if it's not there read the code' more than usual. But that's a good skill to get better at anyway!
Be careful when you do this. If you ever skip requeuing your item on failures, you could fail, not requeue, and then never retry that item again.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing distributed systems for performance often introduces subtle failure modes that simpler but slower designs would avoid, and understanding these tradeoffs requires reading the actual source code.

posts.summary: Julia Evans discusses three software development practices she's encountered while working with Kubernetes that originate from Google's internal culture. She covers cherry-picking commits for release management, writing shell scripts in Go instead of bash, and using services instead of scripts for infrastructure automation. While she finds the first two practices genuinely useful at any scale, she's more skeptical about the service-oriented approach for smaller teams. She concludes with a broader point about being critical of practices from large companies, noting that what works for 50,000 engineers may not suit a team of 20.
key_points: Kubernetes release branches receive hundreds of cherry-picked bugfixes after release, raising questions about when it's safe to upgrade
Writing shell scripts in Go gives you a compiler, proper argument handling, static compilation, and safer contributions from inexperienced programmers
Google's SRE book describes replacing ad-hoc shell scripts with peer-reviewed RPC servers for infrastructure management
Practices from large companies like Google should be evaluated critically rather than adopted wholesale, since scale changes what works
Building software from source gives teams the flexibility to apply their own patches on top of official releases
money_quotes: The practices you need to work with 50,000 other engineers effectively don't necessarily have any relationship to the practices that work with like 20 or 100 or 200 engineers.
I'd much rather have an inexperienced Go programmer contribute to a Go program than an inexperienced bash programmer contribute to a Bash script.
you get a COMPILER so it can tell you if you make typos (this is such a big deal to me)
SREs moved from writing shell scripts in their home directories to building peer-reviewed RPC servers with fine-grained ACLs.
you can't edit the script with vim in production (you could also say this is a 'con' but i'm gonna go with 'pro' for now :))
This idea of 'admin servers are in charge of installing packages' instead of 'scripts are in charge of installing packages' is new to me!
if they can convince people to adopt their operations practices (and software, like kubernetes!), then it makes it much more natural for those people to switch to using Google-managed infrastructure.
posts.key_insight: Practices from large tech companies can contain genuinely useful ideas, but they need to be evaluated critically against your own team's scale rather than adopted as gospel.

posts.summary: This post demystifies the overwhelming number of certificate and key command line arguments in Kubernetes by breaking them down into distinct, independent certificate authorities. Evans explains that contrary to common documentation, a Kubernetes cluster does not require a single root CA—you can use multiple independent CAs for different purposes. She walks through five main CA categories: the API server's TLS certificate, client certificate authentication, authenticating proxy certificates, service account keys (which uniquely don't use CAs at all), and kubelet certificates. The post aims to help readers map their own PKI requirements onto Kubernetes' flexible certificate infrastructure.
key_points: Kubernetes does not require a single root certificate authority per cluster—you can use certificates from multiple independent CAs, despite what much documentation implies
The API server alone has over 16 certificate-related command line arguments, and there are at least 5 independent CA contexts across the cluster components
Service account keys are a special case that don't support CA verification, meaning you must distribute the exact same private key to every controller manager
Having too many authentication methods (client certs, authenticating proxy, tokens) on the API server makes debugging harder—stick to 1 or 2
Unlike most TLS-using software, Kubernetes doesn't trust system CAs from /etc/ssl—you must explicitly specify which CA issued each certificate
money_quotes: The various Kubernetes components have a TON of different places where you can put in a certificate/certificate authority.
If you just had thousands of unrelated independent keys that would be chaos. Chaos is not great for security.

It turns out that this sentence ('every Kubernetes cluster has a [single] cluster root Certificate Authority') is not true – you can actually use certificates issued by several completely different CAs to manage your Kubernetes cluster and it's fine.
Almost everything else in the universe that uses TLS will look in /etc/ssl to find a list of certificate authorities the computer trusts by default. But Kubernetes doesn't do that.
It's useful to authenticate requests to the kubelet because the kubelet can execute arbitrary code on your machines :) (in fact that's its job)
We learned pretty quickly that having too many auth methods in your API server makes things confusing.
Unlike every other private key that Kubernetes supports, the serviceaccount key doesn't support 'hey use a CA to check if this is the right key'.
posts.key_insight: Kubernetes' certificate infrastructure is more flexible than documentation suggests—its many CA settings are independent of each other, so you can map your own PKI requirements onto a cluster rather than being forced into a single-root-CA model.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her evolving approach to contributing to open source projects at work, moving from passively accepting bugs to actively fixing them. She breaks down why open source contributions feel harder than regular work despite requiring the same core engineering skills—it's the social dynamics, unfamiliar conventions, and lack of context that create friction. She offers practical tactics like starting with tiny PRs, writing detailed descriptions, reading lots of code, and breaking changes into smaller pieces. She concludes that open source contribution is a valuable work skill but not something she'd do strictly for fun in her spare time.
key_points: Contributing to open source requires the same technical skills as regular work; the real challenges are social—sending code to strangers, navigating unfamiliar conventions, and not being able to DM maintainers
Breaking large PRs into smaller ones dramatically improves review speed and likelihood of getting merged
Reading code extensively without asking anyone questions is an underutilized skill that can get you surprisingly far in unfamiliar codebases
Being respectful of maintainers' time is essential since maintaining a project is much harder and more thankless than contributing to one
It's okay to close a PR that isn't getting responses and move on rather than spending indefinite time on it
money_quotes: I am trying to be more like I AM A PROGRAMMER I CAN MAKE THIS HAPPEN. Which is true! I can!!

It takes us from 'open source is hard and scary, how do I do it' from 'there are a bunch of specific challenges when contributing to open source projects that I don't usually have to deal with, but that's fine, I just need to deal with them!'
While contributing to an OSS project is definitely work, maintaining a project is often pretty thankless and is way more work.
Code-reading is a muscle that maybe I don't always exercise as much as I could and so this was a good reminder of how far I can get without asking any questions at all.
Turns out making your code simpler gets you more reviewers!
I used to want to / think I should contribute to open source in my spare time. I have mostly decided/realized that this is not going to happen.
I think 'being able to make improvements to open source projects' is a super good work skill and it's something I'm excited about getting better at.
posts.key_insight: Contributing to open source isn't technically harder than regular work—it's the social and contextual challenges that make it difficult, and those can be addressed with specific tactics like smaller PRs, detailed descriptions, and extensive code reading.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores how programmers can invest in learning without spending significant personal time outside of work. She argues that great programmers don't necessarily code after hours, and shares practical strategies gathered from Twitter for learning on the job. The strategies range from choosing projects for their learning potential, to reading pull requests and source code, to watching how senior colleagues operate. She makes a strong case that learning during work hours is not only acceptable but is actually part of the job, and that spending time understanding things deeply pays dividends over time.
key_points: You don't need to program outside of work to become a great programmer — learning languages and frameworks can happen entirely on the job through writing code, reading others' code, and code review
Strategically choosing work projects based on learning opportunities is one of the most effective ways to grow, since you learn new skills while delivering value
Reading pull requests, source code, and following up on bugs you couldn't solve are underrated ways to deepen technical understanding during work hours
Watching how senior people operate and emulating their approaches teaches skills that aren't purely technical, like how to introduce new technology effectively
Taking time during the workday to learn is part of the job, not a luxury — and investing in understanding upfront often makes you more productive overall
money_quotes: I know some great programmers who don't program outside of work at all!
I think it's silly when people are like 'hey, we work with X technologies, you need to have experience with them to work here'.
My view is that it's my job to take time out of my workday to learn things.
I actually think I would probably be more productive if I took a little more time to read in advance.
Reading source of what I use is a big one for me. Understand what it does internally but mainly why it does it a certain way.
Whenever I have to look something up for troubleshooting, I go a little deeper or broader than strictly necessary.
It's not good to go too far down every rabbit hole, but consistently reaching a little further than I have to pays dividends for sure.
posts.key_insight: The most effective professional learning happens during work hours through deliberate choices — picking projects for their learning potential, reading source code and pull requests, and treating time spent understanding systems as a core part of the job rather than extra work.

posts.summary: Evans walks through debugging a Kubernetes networking problem where a container network backend couldn't delete routes. She discovers netlink, the Linux kernel interface for managing routes and network configuration, and demonstrates how to use strace to capture netlink messages, pyroute2 to decode them, and tcpdump/wireshark as alternatives. The root cause turned out to be an invalid RTA_OIF field set to 0 (not a valid network interface ID) in the netlink message. She also covers other debugging tools like ip monitor and nltrace, evaluating their usefulness for this specific problem.
key_points: Netlink is a Linux kernel socket interface used for IPC between kernel and userspace, commonly used for managing routes and network interfaces
Strace can capture raw netlink messages, and pyroute2's decoder tool can parse those hex bytes into human-readable route attributes like destination, gateway, and interface ID
The bug was that the program set RTA_OIF (output interface) to 0, which is not a valid network interface ID, causing route deletion to fail
Multiple tools exist for debugging netlink (strace, tcpdump+wireshark, ip monitor, nltrace) but strace combined with pyroute2 decoding proved most practical
Running Kubernetes networking in production means learning low-level Linux networking details through painful debugging sessions
money_quotes: Not super understandable, right? Well, luckily there's a Python tool that can help us understand it!
But in our errant program, the RTA_OIF field was set to 0! 0 is not even a valid value for this field, I don't think!
It always makes me happy when I learn about a NEW LINUX THING during the course of my job.
kubernetes is cool but definitely not easy, my experience is definitely like 'learn how all the networking works in excruciating detail'

it's less like 'set up networking and it works' and more like 'pick a networking backend, wait a month, discover weird problems, strace it, learn things about netlink and what a RTA_OIF is, fix the bugs, eventually it works'
I actually found it harder to work with than the strace method – it captured too many packets and I found it hard to filter them in Wireshark.
posts.key_insight: Debugging opaque kernel networking problems becomes tractable when you combine strace to capture raw netlink messages with pyroute2 to decode them into human-readable route attributes.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores what network interfaces actually are by running hands-on experiments with Linux network namespaces. She creates a namespace with no interfaces to show that servers can bind to ports but can't receive packets without at least one interface. She then investigates why packets appear multiple times in tcpdump when containers are involved, tracing the answer to how routing assigns packets to specific network devices. The post builds up a working mental model of interfaces through experimentation rather than definitions.
key_points: A network namespace with no interfaces can bind servers to 0.0.0.0 but cannot actually send or receive packets
Adding the loopback interface to an empty namespace immediately makes networking work without restarting servers
Packets appear multiple times in tcpdump because they pass through multiple virtual interfaces (veth, bridge, eth0) on the way out
The route table determines which network interface a packet gets assigned to, and this assignment happens early in the network stack
Packets to local IP addresses like 172.17.0.1 may route through 'lo' instead of the expected interface due to 'local' route table entries
money_quotes: When learning about new unfamiliar stuff, I often realize that I don't fully understand something much more fundamental.
This is a thing I thought I understood but it turns out there are at least 2 things I didn't know about them.
I'm not going to try to give you a crisp definition, instead we're going to make some observations, do some experiments, ask some questions, and make some guesses.
If you don't have any network interfaces, you can't do any networking (but you can start servers on 0.0.0.0 and netstat shows those servers).
Routing a packet means 'assigning it a network device'.
If you don't have any network interfaces, your packets don't enter the linux network stack at all really.
posts.key_insight: Network interfaces are the gateway into the Linux network stack — without one, packets never enter the stack at all, and the route table determines which interface each packet gets assigned to.

posts.summary: Evans argues that publicly announcing work plans and projects, while scary, is far more effective than hiding until things are finished. She identifies the fear of failure and changing plans as reasons people avoid sharing, but counters that transparency helps teams coordinate, surfaces problems early, and creates organizational momentum. She acknowledges legitimate reasons to keep early-stage ideas private, particularly in cultures with excessive 'stop energy.' The post concludes that the benefits of open communication about plans far outweigh the risks of occasional failure or plan changes.
key_points: Announcing plans helps teams coordinate and allocate resources to related projects, making shared planning the most important benefit
The instinct to hide until work is finished is analogous to not pushing code until it's perfect — sharing works in progress is better
Assuming your plans will succeed makes it easier to communicate them and execute on them confidently
Early-stage ideas are fragile and sometimes genuinely benefit from privacy before being shared widely
In cultures with excessive 'stop energy,' people rationally respond by hiding their plans, which points to a cultural problem
money_quotes: It seems much safer to: start working on a thing, hide in a corner until it's done, announce 'look, we did it, it worked'
This pattern of 'let me hide in a corner until I'm done this project' sounds suspiciously like 'I won't push my code to github until it's perfect'
I think announcing ambitious plans can help kind of.. inspire the people around you?
If everyone hides in a corner and doesn't tell people what they're doing until they're done… well, that wouldn't really be a very effective way to work together.
Remember it's my job/responsibility to talk about what I'm doing. We're all trying to build something together, and as a team we can't do that if we don't talk about our plans.
I think if I start with the assumption that the thing I'm embarking on will probably work, it'll be easier to execute and tell people about.
Early-stage ideas are fragile and need to be sheltered.
In some work cultures, sometimes people can put out a lot of stop energy!
posts.key_insight: Sharing your work plans openly is scary because failure becomes public, but the coordination benefits and feedback you get far outweigh the comfort of hiding until you're done.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores how to detect and diagnose packet drops on a Linux server. She starts with the basic diagnostic of using `netstat -i` to see if packets are being dropped on any interface, then introduces `dropwatch`, a tool that identifies which kernel function is responsible for dropping packets. She also covers using `perf` to monitor the `kfree_skb` kernel event for packet drop debugging. The post is framed as personal notes for future reference rather than a battle-tested guide.
key_points: Use `netstat -i` as a first step to check RX-DRP and TX-DRP columns for evidence of packet loss on specific interfaces
The `dropwatch` tool can pinpoint which kernel function is dropping packets, giving you a specific location to investigate
The `perf` tool can monitor the `kfree_skb` kernel event to trace packet drops with full kernel stack traces
Packet drops happen for a reason and the diagnostic approach differs between packets dropped locally vs. somewhere on the internet
money_quotes: When packets are being dropped on a computer, they're being dropped for a reason. How do you find out whether/why packets are being dropped?
I'm not interested here in 'packets are being dropped somewhere else on the internet, let's diagnose it with traceroute / by counting TCP retransmits'
I asked on Twitter and got the very useful answer 'look at netstat -i!'
It tells me at which kernel function I'm losing packets. Cool!
I still haven't ever used these tools to seriously debug a packet loss problem yet, just wanted to write this down so that I have the notes if I do want to in the future!
posts.key_insight: Linux provides layered tools for packet drop diagnosis: `netstat -i` tells you if drops are happening, `dropwatch` tells you which kernel function is responsible, and `perf` gives you full stack traces.

posts.summary: Julia Evans introduces the treap, a randomized data structure that combines a binary search tree with a heap to create a balanced BST. She explains it's useful for implementing ordered maps (like C++'s std::map) and notes that while std::map typically uses red-black trees, treaps perform equivalently in expected value with much simpler insert/delete algorithms. The post is a quick visual sketch format with hand-drawn illustrations explaining how treaps work.
key_points: A treap is a randomized data structure that provides a simple way to implement a balanced binary search tree
Treaps perform as well as red-black trees in expected value but have significantly simpler insert/delete algorithms
Treaps are practical for implementing ordered maps like C++'s std::map
money_quotes: It's basically a really cool way to implement a balanced binary search tree.
C++'s std::map is usually a red-black tree, but a treap performs just as well (in expected value) and the algorithms for insert/delete are way simpler.
posts.key_insight: Treaps offer the same performance as red-black trees for balanced BST operations but with dramatically simpler algorithms for insertion and deletion.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her discovery of vim's built-in session feature, which lets you save and restore all open files and editor state. The feature requires no plugins—just :mksession to save and :source to restore. She drew a comic explaining the workflow and links to three plugins that extend session functionality.
key_points: Vim has a built-in :mksession command that saves your current editor state including open files
Sessions can be restored with :source or by launching vim with the -S flag
Several plugins like vim-obsession and vim-startify add extra session management features
money_quotes: It's a builtin feature, no fancy plugins needed.
Basically you can save all your open files and current state with :mksession
posts.key_insight: Vim's built-in :mksession command lets you save and restore your entire editing session without any plugins.

posts.summary: Julia Evans revisits how she spent her 46 days at the Recurse Center four years earlier, breaking down her time across projects like a TCP stack in Python, a mini OS in Rust, and a gzip implementation in Julia. She averaged 50-100 lines of code per day and deliberately avoided comfortable topics like web development, focusing instead on things that seemed hard or scary like networking, operating systems, and security. She spent 1-2 hours daily blogging as a deliberate 'media strategy' to land a good job afterward, which worked. The post reflects on what made the experience valuable: the people, the debugging culture, and discovering unknown-unknowns like strace and netcat that still excite her years later.
key_points: She deliberately avoided comfortable skills (web dev, ML) and focused on scary/hard topics like networking, OS internals, and security — and those interests persisted for years afterward
Short 2-3 day explorations (kernel modules, buffer overflows) were surprisingly high-value learning experiences despite their brevity
Daily blogging was a conscious career strategy that consumed 1-2 hours per day but paid off with a job, though she acknowledges it's not an approach that works for most people

The most lasting lessons were attitudinal — that debugging is fun, that systems topics aren't as hard to start with as they seem, and that unknown-unknowns (strace, netcat, system calls) are everywhere
Almost all her time was spent on demoable projects, which helped maintain focus and motivation even if nothing was polished
money_quotes: I focused on stuff I thought seemed hard/scary (networking/security/operating systems/writing a shell/compression).
The stuff I learned about at RC 4 years ago is still a lot of the same stuff I'm excited about today.
There were a lot of things I worked on for only 2-3 days (like 'write kernel modules'). Some of those things I learned a LOT from.
I think this media strategy approach ('everything I do has to be a cool thing I can demo and write about') thing is pretty weird and I don't know that I recommend it. It worked for me though.
Today I have a really positive attitude about debugging (what are we going to learn TODAY?!). I think I learned that from the RC facilitators.
systems/networking/linux are extremely cool and not really that hard to get started with even if you only know a little bit of C.
I learned some about unknown-unknowns – things that I didn't know existed and wouldn't really have thought to ask about (strace! netcat! system calls!)
posts.key_insight: Deliberately pursuing topics that feel hard or scary, combined with daily writing and demoable projects, can produce lasting learning and career momentum in a compressed timeframe.

posts.summary: Evans explores Go's pprof profiling tool, focusing on heap profiling for debugging memory problems. She walks through the basics of setting up pprof, collecting profiles, and analyzing them with go tool pprof. The core of the post deconstructs the actual protobuf format of a pprof file to understand what data it contains. She explains key distinctions like alloc_space vs inuse_space and how the heap profiler tracks both allocations and frees. She also discovers through strace that pprof resolves function names by reading the original binary from disk, not from the profile file itself.
key_points: A pprof profile is fundamentally a collection of stack traces with metadata, stored as protobuf, where each sample records the call sequence that led to a particular event like allocation
Heap profile stack traces show where memory was allocated, not where the leak is — the misbehaving function that should be freeing memory may be entirely different from what appears in the profile
The distinction between inuse_space and alloc_space matters: inuse helps debug memory usage while alloc_space helps debug garbage collection pressure
Pprof files don't always contain function names — go tool pprof resolves them by opening the original binary from the filesystem path stored in the profile
The trace endpoint (/debug/pprof/trace) produces a completely different file format from all other pprof endpoints and requires go tool trace instead of go tool pprof
money_quotes: When understanding how things work I like to start at the beginning. What is a "profile" exactly?
The 7th time I looked at the runtime/pprof docs, I read this very useful sentence.
The stack traces in the heap profile are the stack trace at time of allocation.
So the function to blame for the memory leak might be totally different than the function listed in the heap profile.
I was originally confused about this works – the profiles have already be collected! How can I make this choice after the fact?
I answered this with strace, obviously – I straced go tool pprof and this is what I saw.
There are probably also talks about pprof but I am too impatient to watch talks, that's part of why I write lots of blog posts and give few talks.
Flamegraphs are the best thing in the universe so I'm very excited for that to be available.
posts.key_insight: Understanding what a pprof file actually contains — stack traces stored as protobuf with metadata — demystifies the tool and makes it easier to reason about what profiling data means and how to use it effectively.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains why Kubernetes is interesting after three months of working with it full-time, deliberately avoiding buzzwords. She highlights that Kubernetes lets developers deploy services without setting up servers, provides unified visibility into all running applications through etcd, and continues running existing code even when its own components fail. She argues that Kubernetes' stateless design makes it resilient to bugs in its own core components, and that its constrained model makes it a good platform for building custom distributed systems. She concludes that while Kubernetes enables amazing things, it requires deep understanding of underlying systems like Linux networking and isn't easy to operate.
key_points: Kubernetes dramatically simplifies deploying new services once a cluster is set up, reducing what used to take hours of server provisioning to a single configuration file
All cluster state lives in etcd, giving operators a single unified API to query and control everything running in production
Kubernetes components are stateless and can be restarted at any time without consistency issues, making the system resilient even to bugs in its own core
The constrained resource-and-controller model makes Kubernetes a good platform for building custom distributed systems, not just a system you use as-is
Operating Kubernetes requires deep understanding of underlying infrastructure like Linux networking and certificate management — the abstractions don't fully hide the complexity
money_quotes: With Kubernetes you can set up a new service with a single command... There must be a trap.
There kind of is a trap, setting up a production Kubernetes cluster is (in my experience) definitely not easy.

Imagine that I asked you today 'hey, tell me every application you have running in production, what host it's running on, whether it's healthy or not, and whether or not it has a DNS name attached to it'. I don't know about you but I would need to go look in a bunch of different places to answer this question.
If you restart any component, what happens is: it reads all its relevant state from etcd. It starts doing the necessary things it's supposed to be doing based on that state.
Because all the components don't keep any state in memory, you can just restart them at any time and that can help mitigate a variety of bugs.

Kamal introduced me to this idea of 'Kubernetes is a good platform for writing your own distributed systems' instead of just 'Kubernetes is a distributed system you can use' and I think it's really interesting.
I find that with a lot of the abstractions it gives me, I need to understand what is underneath those abstractions in order to debug issues and configure things properly.
posts.key_insight: Kubernetes' real power comes from its stateless, etcd-backed architecture that provides a unified API for all cluster state, making it both a useful platform and a foundation for building custom distributed systems — but operating it well requires understanding the layers beneath its abstractions.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the operational challenges of running Kubernetes networking in production, arguing that while there's plenty of guidance on setup, there's far less on ongoing operation. She walks through the key networking components—overlay networks, kube-proxy, kube-dns, and ingress—detailing specific failure modes she's encountered or anticipates. She emphasizes that networking software is inherently hard to operate because it depends heavily on Linux kernel configuration, requirements change over time, and debugging requires deep expertise. Her core advice is to minimize moving parts, think critically about architecture rather than blindly following what Google does, and invest heavily in understanding each component.
key_points: Kubernetes networking has many components (overlay network, kube-dns, kube-proxy, ingress) and each introduces distinct failure modes that operators need to understand
Operating networking software is hard because it depends on correct Linux kernel configuration, requirements shift over time, and debugging demands deep expertise
kube-proxy's iptables-based load balancing creates scalability problems—adding a rule can take 11 minutes with 5,000 services
It may be worth using familiar tools like HAProxy or Envoy instead of adopting entirely new load balancing approaches just because Kubernetes ships with them
The Kubernetes networking ecosystem is still immature and operators need to invest significantly in understanding every piece to handle production incidents
money_quotes: It's worth thinking critically about major changes to your networking infrastructure and the impact that will have on your reliability, even if very fancy Googlers say "this is what we do at Google".
A misconfigured sysctl can easily be the difference between "everything is 100% fine" and "everything is on fire".

If you already have a lot of experience operating one kind of load balancer (like HAProxy), it might make sense to do some extra work to use that instead of starting to use an entirely new kind of load balancer.
My sense of all this Kubernetes networking software is that it's all still quite new and I'm not sure we (as a community) really know how to operate all of it well.
Avoiding networking complexity seems like a good idea to me if you can.
My plan right now is just to keep learning about how things work and reduce the number of moving parts I need to worry about as much as possible.
If you use host networking for literally every pod you don't need kube-dns or kube-proxy. You don't even need a working overlay network.
posts.key_insight: Kubernetes networking is operationally complex with many failure modes, and operators should minimize moving parts and think critically about each component rather than adopting the full stack uncritically.

posts.summary: Julia Evans discovers Binder, a free tool that lets anyone host interactive Jupyter notebooks online. She explains the progression from static notebook sharing (GitHub, nbviewer) to fully interactive environments, drawing on her experience running Python data science workshops where installation issues consumed valuable time. She highlights how Binder, built on JupyterHub and Kubernetes, eliminates the setup barrier entirely by letting users run notebooks directly in the browser.
key_points: Binder lets anyone launch interactive copies of Jupyter notebooks hosted on GitHub with zero installation
Static notebook sharing via GitHub and nbviewer has existed for years but doesn't let users edit or experiment with code
Installation friction is a major barrier at workshops—Binder and JupyterHub solve this by moving the environment to the server
Binder uses Kubernetes and Docker under the hood, making it expensive to run but enormously useful for education and sharing
money_quotes: Binder is a tool that lets other people easily launch an interactive copy of your Jupyter notebooks.
Having to install a bunch of software really sucks when you're trying to get started with a new thing.
I especially love them for doing data analysis – it's basically life changing.
nbviewer isn't interactive – it shows you a read-only version. So you can't edit the code and experiment!
It must be much much more expensive to run than the read-only services, but it's such a useful and cool thing! I hope it continues to exist.
posts.key_insight: The biggest barrier to learning programming tools isn't the concepts—it's the installation, and Binder eliminates that friction entirely by making notebooks instantly interactive in the browser.

posts.summary: Julia Evans enthusiastically introduces Glitch as a platform for building small JavaScript web projects with zero setup. The platform auto-reloads your backend code as you type, making it feel like the old days of editing PHP directly on a server. She highlights the 'remix' feature that lets you fork and modify example apps in clicks, and shares her own app for turning off Twitter retweets. The post argues that removing friction from deployment makes programming feel fun again, even if it's not a 'serious development practice.'
key_points: Glitch auto-deploys JavaScript code as you type, removing the entire setup and deployment cycle for small projects
The 'remix' feature lets you fork and modify someone else's web app in just a few clicks, making experimentation accessible
The joy of instant deployment echoes the old practice of editing PHP on a server, which modern deployment practices replaced but which was genuinely fun
Removing friction like local dev environments, dependency installation, and hosting setup makes the difference between actually building something and not bothering
money_quotes: Coding like this (you type, and the code runs on the public internet immediately) just feels really fun to me.

Now we have "better deployment practices" than "just edit the code and it is instantly on the internet" but we are not talking about Serious Development Practices, we are talking about writing tiny programs for fun.
Using Glitch feels really fun and democratic.
I think tasks like installing node.js dependencies used to be interesting, like "cool i am learning something new" and now I just find them tedious.
So I love being able to just click "remix this!" and have my version on the internet instantly.
I liked that I didn't have to set up a local development environment, I could just start typing and go!
posts.key_insight: The biggest barrier to building fun small web projects isn't coding skill but deployment friction, and tools that eliminate setup make programming feel joyful and democratic again.

posts.summary: Julia Evans discovers that Linux group membership checks don't work the way most people assume. Rather than checking /etc/group at runtime, Linux stores group IDs as attributes directly on each process. This means adding a user to a group doesn't affect already-running processes — you need to start a new login session for the changes to take effect. The post walks through the discovery, demonstrates the behavior with concrete examples, and explains how the login process initializes groups via initgroups().
key_points: Linux checks group membership by looking at group IDs stored as process attributes, not by reading /etc/group at runtime
Adding a user to a new group doesn't affect existing processes — the shell and its children retain their original group IDs
The login program (/bin/login) runs as root and calls initgroups() to set up a user's groups from /etc/group when starting a login session
You can use 'newgrp' or 'sg' to start a new shell with updated group membership without a full re-login
Setuid programs like passwd work by setting the effective user ID to root while keeping the real user ID as the original user
money_quotes: Linux does not check which groups a process's user belongs to every time that process tries to access a file.
The key new insight for me was pretty simple! User and group IDs are attributes of the process.
Adding a user to a group doesn't put existing processes in that group.
There needs to be some 'first' process that has your groups set on it, and all the other processes you set inherit their groups from that.
If I really want the new panda group everywhere, I need to restart my login session completely, which means quitting my window manager and logging in again.
Reading 17 pages about how users and groups work is really approachable, self-contained, super useful, and I don't have to tackle all 1300 pages of it at once to learn helpful things.
posts.key_insight: Linux group permissions are checked against group IDs baked into each process at creation time, not looked up dynamically from /etc/group, which is why adding a user to a group doesn't affect already-running processes.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces a small zine compiling 24 Linux comics she drew the previous November. She formatted them into a printable booklet after people kept asking her to write a book, deciding to release something imperfect rather than keep it unpublished. She also promotes her new 'So You Want to Be a Wizard' zine about learning hard things, and shares the Python script she uses to format zines into printable booklets.
key_points: She compiled 24 of her daily Linux comics from November 2016 into a printable zine format
She chose to release the zine despite noticing factual errors, prioritizing availability over perfection
Her booklet-generating script is only 50 lines of Python, mostly argument parsing, built on top of pdftk
She also released a separate zine called 'So You Want to Be a Wizard' about learning hard things in programming
money_quotes: I figure better to have it out in the world and imperfect than on my laptop where nobody can enjoy it.
Since then, people have been asking me to write a book. I have not yet written a book. (one day!).
Well, what would happen if I just formatted all those comics from last year into a small zine that folks could print?
It's just 50 lines of Python (mostly argument parsing), nothing fancy.
One day I have to write a love letter to pdftk – I've used pdftk to do so much pdf wrangling and I do not know how I would make zines without it.
posts.key_insight: Releasing imperfect work into the world is better than keeping it polished but unseen on your laptop.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores what references mean in Rust by starting from a simple struct that won't compile due to a missing lifetime parameter. She explains that Rust needs to know at compile time where memory lives (heap, stack, or data segment) to determine when to free it. The post walks through two solutions: using Box<T> for heap-allocated data, or adding lifetime parameters for references to existing memory. She identifies the relationship between owned heap types (Vec<T>, String, Box<T>) and their reference counterparts (&[T], &str, &T) as a key insight for understanding Rust. The post concludes by observing that most real-world Rust structs don't use lifetimes, based on examining ripgrep's source code.
key_points: A Rust reference (&T) points to memory that could be on the heap, stack, or data segment, and Rust needs to know at compile time when to free it
Box<T> tells the compiler memory is heap-allocated, so it knows to free it when the value goes out of scope
The three main heap types (Vec<T>, String, Box<T>) each have corresponding reference types (&[T], &str, &T), and understanding this mapping is essential
Converting from owned to reference is easy (as_ref()), but going back requires cloning and new heap allocation
Most real-world Rust structs own their data rather than using lifetime parameters
money_quotes: The most important thing about Rust (and the thing that makes programming in Rust confusing) is that it needs to decide at compile time when all the memory in the program needs to be freed.
You might be thinking 'julia, this is boring, I KNOW what a reference is, it is like a pointer in C, I know what that is'. BUT DO YOU REALLY??
There is a straightforward answer ('it's missing a lifetime parameter'), but that answer is not that useful.

I think these 3 types (Vec<T>, String, and Box<T>) are very important in Rust and understanding the relationship between them and their reference version is extremely important when writing Rust programs.
The reason you can do this conversion is that you're just 'forgetting' that that variable is allocated on the heap and saying 'who cares, this is just a reference'.
You can't as easily convert back from a &[T] to a Vec<T> though (because a &[T] could be a pointer to memory on the stack, so it doesn't make sense to just say 'this is something on the heap')!
posts.key_insight: Understanding Rust references requires knowing that memory can live in three places, and the distinction between owned heap types and their reference counterparts is the key mental model for writing Rust structs.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new free zine 'So you want to be a wizard,' which covers strategies for getting really good at programming. The zine includes topics like asking great questions, reading source code, debugging skills, and software design. She thanks the 200 people who paid for early access and notes that people have been giving it to newer developers. She also mentions redesigning her zines page and reflecting that she's now published 8 zines total, with 4 made in 2017 alone.
key_points: The zine focuses on meta-skills for becoming a better programmer: asking questions, reading source code, debugging, and software design
The zine is available for free in both online and printable formats, after an early-access paid period
Evans abandoned Gumroad's pay-what-you-want model for zine downloads in favor of direct PDF links for simplicity
She published 4 zines in 2017, bringing her total to 8
money_quotes: Do you want to get really good at your job? Me too. This zine is about how I learn new things about programming!
A bunch of folks have told me that they've given this one to newer developers they work with which makes me happy!
Did you know I've published 8 zines???? I didn't! I was delighted to learn this.
I've decided that was a failed experiment so now all the links on the page are just normal links to PDFs. Much easier to use!
This year I made 4 zines, it turns out. Maybe next year I will make 4 zines too!
posts.key_insight: Getting really good at programming is less about mastering specific technologies and more about developing meta-skills like asking great questions, reading source code, and systematic debugging.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces a three-month sabbatical from Stripe to build better Ruby profiling tools through the Segment Open Source Fellowship. She's frustrated that Ruby and Python profiling requires choosing libraries, adding gems, and writing setup code, unlike C and Java where you can just attach to a running process. Her goal is to build a tool where you simply point at a PID and get useful profiling data immediately. She acknowledges this is new territory for her—she's used many profilers but never built one—and is both excited and nervous about the challenge.
key_points: Ruby and Python profiling tools require too much setup compared to C and Java, where you can attach to any running process instantly
The usability of profiling tools matters as much as their capability—tools people give up on aren't useful at all
Three core debugging capabilities should be easy to get from any program: stack traces, memory profiles, and CPU profiles/flamegraphs
Open source sabbaticals are a valuable model—you get to work on something meaningful and return to your job afterward
money_quotes: Profiling tools are not useful if they are so confusing to use that people give up!
There's so much low-hanging fruit in performance – maybe you accidentally wrote a quadratic function, maybe you wrote a small hacky thing and someone just needs to spend 30 minutes optimizing it.
Without a profiler, it's basically impossible to diagnose these performance problems!

I've often had a performance problem, thought 'oh, let me just get a memory profile of this node.js program, that will help', and 3 hours of frustration later been unable to get the information I wanted and given up.
I've never tried to make an open source project that other people actually use!
Anyway, how am I going to learn & get better if I don't do things that are a bit scary?
posts.key_insight: The biggest barrier to fixing performance problems isn't the complexity of the fix—it's that profiling tools are too hard to set up, so people never get the data they need to diagnose the problem.

posts.summary: Julia Evans surveys the internals of 14 Ruby and Python CPU profilers to understand how they actually work under the hood. She identifies two fundamental categories—tracing profilers that record every function call via interpreter hooks, and sampling profilers that periodically snapshot the stack using setitimer signals. She demonstrates that all these profilers are built from a surprisingly small set of fundamental capabilities (setitimer, PyEval_SetProfile, rb_add_event_hook, ptrace).
