The post includes code examples showing how simple stack sampling can be, highlights tradeoffs like tracing overhead and setitimer's signal interference with syscalls, and notes her particular interest in pyflame's out-of-process ptrace approach for her own Ruby profiler project.
key_points: All CPU profilers fall into two categories: tracing profilers (record every function call via interpreter hooks) and sampling profilers (periodically snapshot the stack, typically via setitimer)
Almost all Ruby/Python profilers run inside the target process, where accessing the call stack is trivially easy—Python's sys._current_frames() or Ruby's caller method

The setitimer system call is the dominant mechanism for sampling profilers, letting you receive periodic signals to trigger stack collection, but it can interfere with system calls if sampling too frequently
Tracing profilers add fixed overhead per function call, which can distort results by making function-call-heavy code appear disproportionately slow
Pyflame stands out by profiling from outside the process using ptrace, avoiding in-process overhead but introducing tradeoffs around consistent snapshots
money_quotes: The interesting thing here is that all profilers are implemented using a pretty small set of fundamental capabilities.
If you're having a serious performance problem (like 80% of your time is being spent in 1 slow function), 200 samples a second will often be enough to figure out which function to blame!
It's a useful, working, profiler, and it's only about 100 lines of Python. So cool!

If you have 2 implementations of something – one with a lot of function calls and one without, which take the same amount of time, the one with a lot of function calls will appear to be slower when profiled.

One INTERESTING downside of setitimer-based profilers is that the timers cause signals! Signals sometimes interrupt system calls! System calls sometimes take a few milliseconds! If you sample too frequently, you can make your program keep retrying the same syscall forever!
process_vm_readv is lower overhead because it doesn't stop the process, but it also can give you an inconsistent snapshot because it doesn't stop the process :).
posts.key_insight: CPU profilers are built from a surprisingly small set of OS and interpreter primitives—setitimer for sampling, interpreter hooks for tracing, and ptrace for out-of-process observation—and understanding these building blocks demystifies how any profiler works.

posts.summary: Evans investigates whether Ruby's internal struct definitions change frequently enough to complicate building a sampling profiler. She compares two approaches: compiling against Ruby header files at build time (like pyflame does for Python) versus parsing DWARF debug symbols at runtime (like gdb). After examining struct layouts across Ruby 2.1 through 2.5, she finds that unlike Python which only has three variations, Ruby's internal structs change with every major version release. She concludes that either DWARF-based decoding or committing headers for all ~40 minor Ruby versions are the viable paths forward.
key_points: A sampling profiler needs to decode Ruby's internal C structs to read the current stack, requiring knowledge of exact struct layouts
Python's internal structs only have 3 variations, making compile-time header inclusion practical, but Ruby's structs change every major version
Ruby 2.5 introduced a major refactor replacing ruby_current_thread with ruby_current_execution_context_ptr, completely changing the thread struct
Conditional compilation via #ifdefs adds another dimension of variation to Ruby struct layouts
The two viable approaches are using DWARF debug symbols at runtime or pre-generating bindings for all ~40 Ruby minor versions
money_quotes: The main question a sampling profiler needs to answer (over and over and over… :)) is "what's the stack right now?".
Dealing with DWARF is a pain and not every Ruby process has debug symbols, so I'd prefer to be able to use approach #2.

It seems like the situation with the Ruby structs defining the Ruby stack is very different from the situation in Python – instead of only having 3 versions to worry about, the struct layouts of the structs I care about change every major Ruby version.
There are maybe.. 40 minor ruby versions? That is a lot but not an infinite amount.
In Rust I could use bindgen to generate Rust bindings for all those ruby versions and just like.. commit all the versions into my repository.
posts.key_insight: Ruby's internal struct layouts change with every major version, making compile-time profiler approaches much harder than equivalent Python profilers and potentially requiring either DWARF parsing or pre-generated bindings for dozens of Ruby versions.

posts.summary: Evans compares the public C API header files exposed by Ruby and Python, motivated by her work building a Ruby profiler similar to pyflame. She discovers that Python exposes far more structs and detailed type information than Ruby, whose API relies heavily on an opaque VALUE type. Both languages are technically embeddable, though Ruby's embedding story is less documented. The key finding is that Ruby's public headers don't expose the internal VM structs needed for external process profiling, unlike Python's headers which include things like PyThreadState.
key_points: Python's public C headers expose ~60 structs with detailed fields, while Ruby exposes only ~12, making Python's internals more accessible to external tools
Ruby's C API uses an opaque VALUE type for nearly everything, whereas Python uses distinct typed structs like PyObject
Ruby's rb_profile_frames API requires being in the same process, unlike pyflame's approach of profiling Python from an external process
Both CRuby and Python are embeddable, but Ruby's embedding documentation is sparse compared to Python's
The stackprof profiler was built within 3 days of Ruby 2.1's new C profiling API being committed
money_quotes: pyflame uses Python's public headers. Can my profiler use the corresponding Ruby headers? I don't think so!
One surprising/interesting thing to me about the Ruby headers – the type returned by almost every function is VALUE.
Python gives you a lot more structs than Ruby, and those structs in general I think have more fields.
The structs I want access to are like PyThreadState, and they're not in Ruby's public header files.
This is a function though so to call it you need to be in the same Ruby process, not in a separate process.
When I said on twitter that ruby wasn't embeddable, Matz replied and said 'CRuby is also embeddable. But mruby has better API for embedding.'
posts.key_insight: Python's C API exposes far more internal VM structure than Ruby's, which relies on opaque types — making it fundamentally harder to build external profiling tools for Ruby.

posts.summary: Julia Evans introduces bindgen, a Rust tool that automatically generates Rust bindings from C header files, framing it as essentially '#include for Rust.' She explains how bindgen fits into her vision of Rust as offering all the power of C with better tooling, and demonstrates its practical use by generating bindings for 33 different Ruby versions' internal header files for her Ruby profiler project. She highlights useful features like type whitelisting and automatic Debug derive annotations, and notes that while committing generated bindings works, the recommended approach is generating them at build time via build.rs.
key_points: Bindgen automatically generates Rust struct definitions and function declarations from C header files, making C interop nearly as simple as #include
The tool handles complex real-world header files including #ifdefs and can whitelist specific types to keep output manageable
For the Ruby profiler project, bindgen generated bindings for 33 different Ruby versions to handle constantly-changing internal structs
The recommended approach is generating bindings at build time using build.rs rather than committing generated code, though both work
cbindgen exists as the reverse tool, generating C bindings from Rust code
money_quotes: To me the dream of Rust is "it gives you all the power of C, but with a more powerful compiler, better documentation, better tools (cargo), and a great community". I think bindgen is a really important part of "great tools"!
You can run `bindgen cool_header_file.h -o rust-bindings.rs` and it'll automatically generate Rust struct definitions or function declarations that will let you link in the C library. So simple!

I wanted to do something weird: #include vm_core.h, which is an internal header file in Ruby. And I didn't actually want to just include one vm_core_h, I wanted to generate bindings for 33 different Ruby versions.
Generate #[derive(Debug)] annotations for C structs so you can easily print them out. This is SO USEFUL when debugging!!!!

I'm still not sure if this approach of "let's generate bindings for 33 different ruby versions and commit them into my repository" will work. But I figure the best way to find out it to keep trying it and see how it goes!
I think I like the approach of having a build.rs + Cargo.toml instead of a Makefile a lot – it seems easier to maintain.
posts.key_insight: Rust's bindgen tool makes C interop nearly effortless by auto-generating bindings from header files, enabling practical use cases like supporting dozens of Ruby versions in a profiler.

posts.summary: Evans documents debugging a segfault in her Rust program that grabbed stack traces from Ruby processes. The bug stemmed from unsafe code that incorrectly used Vec::from_raw_parts, passing the wrong capacity and violating alignment requirements when casting a byte vector into a vector of structs. The segfault only occurred with jemalloc (not the system allocator), which made debugging confusing but ultimately revealed that jemalloc's stricter memory management was actually catching the bug earlier. She fixed it by creating a slice view of the byte vector instead of trying to transmute the vector itself, letting Rust handle deallocation of the original bytes.
key_points: Segfaults in Rust almost always come from incorrect unsafe code rather than compiler bugs, and the fix requires carefully reading the invariants that unsafe functions expect
Different allocators (jemalloc vs system malloc) can surface or hide memory bugs — jemalloc's thread-local caching and size-bucketed approach made it stricter, which was actually a feature

The debugging process followed a systematic narrowing: print statements to locate the crash, valgrind to confirm it was during deallocation, mem::forget to identify the variable, then reducing to a minimal reproduction
Creating a minimal reproduction (under 50 lines) both enabled asking for help and led to self-discovery of the bug while writing up the question
Leaking memory is considered safe in Rust, and safe Rust code can still segfault (e.g., stack overflow guard pages) — undefined behavior and unsafe are related but not synonymous
money_quotes: It was a new class of bug for me and so it was a good learning opportunity!

So, as far as I understand it, there are 2 possible reasons your Rust program can segfault: you wrote unsafe code in a way that violates Rust's memory safety guarantees, or the Rust compiler has a bug.
step 5 was not a constructive step but instead just a lot of confusion.
As often happens when I try to explain what's going on in writing, halfway through writing the question I figured it out for myself.
The program that segfaulted was better than the program that didn't, because it was picking up a subtle problem that could bite me later if I didn't fix it.
I was kind of surprised to learn that leaking memory is safe in Rust.
Using unsafe functions can be safe! You just need to be careful to make sure to call those functions in a way that maintains the invariants that Rust expects.
posts.key_insight: Debugging unfamiliar bug classes systematically — narrowing scope, creating minimal reproductions, and writing up the problem for others — often leads you to the answer yourself while teaching you about the underlying systems.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes writing her first Rust macro using macro_rules! to solve a practical problem: generating the same get_stack_trace function for 30 different Ruby versions in her Ruby profiling tool. Each Ruby version had structurally similar but distinct bindings, so she needed the same code compiled against different type definitions. She found the macro system surprisingly easy to use, going from zero knowledge to a working solution in under an hour. The macro let her avoid massive code duplication by parameterizing the Ruby version identifier.
key_points: Rust's macro_rules! system can be surprisingly approachable for straightforward code generation tasks, even for first-time users
The specific use case was generating version-specific functions for ~30 Ruby versions where structs had the same names but different layouts
Macros solved the problem of needing identical logic compiled against different type bindings without manual code duplication
The pattern was simple substitution: parameterize the version identifier and let the compiler generate different code for each version
money_quotes: The most striking thing to me about this was how easy it was – I kind of expected it to be a weird hard finicky thing, and instead I found that I could go from "I don't know how macros work but I think I could do this with a macro" to "wow I'm done" in less than an hour.
I basically just needed to put my code in and insert $ruby_version in the places I wanted it to go in. So simple!
I literally just looked at an example, tried the first thing I thought would work, and it worked pretty much right away.
I was SO HAPPY about this because I'd been worried getting this to work would be hard but instead it was so easy!!
I'm not actually going to try to explain what a macro is, instead I will just show you what I used a macro for yesterday and hopefully that will be interesting.
posts.key_insight: Rust's macro_rules! system is more approachable than its reputation suggests, and even simple textual substitution macros can solve real code generation problems like compiling the same logic against dozens of different type definitions.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a one-page perf cheat sheet she created as part of her upcoming perf zine. After struggling to explain perf concisely, she decided to make a centerfold reference covering basic and advanced command line arguments. The examples are sourced from Brendan Gregg's perf reference page. She highlights surprising capabilities like counting system calls by process with live updates and tracking sent network packets by process.
key_points: A concise cheat sheet format can be an effective way to cover a tool's command line arguments comprehensively
perf can do more than basic profiling — it can count system calls and network packets by process in real time
Brendan Gregg's perf reference page is a canonical resource for perf examples
money_quotes: I've been struggling with how to explain all there is to say about perf in a concise way.
Yesterday I finally hit on the idea of making a 1-page cheat sheet reference which covers all of the basic perf command line arguments.
sudo perf top -e raw_syscalls:sys_enter -ns comm -d 1 counts system calls by process and shows you live updates
stdbuf -oL perf top -e net:net_dev_xmit -ns comm | strings counts sent network packets by process and prints an update every second.
I didn't realize you could do either of those things!
posts.key_insight: A well-designed one-page cheat sheet can make a powerful but complex tool like perf immediately accessible, revealing capabilities that even experienced users might not know about.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes how she set up an automatic weekly email digest of her blog using Mailchimp's RSS-to-email feature. After initially dismissing the idea as too time-consuming, she realized it could be fully automated. She walks through the simple setup process, shares her custom HTML template, and discusses bugs she encountered. The newsletter grew to over 1,050 subscribers in about six months with zero ongoing effort from her.
key_points: An automatic RSS-to-email newsletter removes the burden of manually sending updates while serving readers who don't use RSS
Mailchimp's built-in RSS campaign feature makes setup trivial — just provide a feed URL, set a schedule, and create a template
Writing a custom HTML template was worth the effort because Mailchimp's fancy templates added unnecessary complexity and rendering issues
Including full post content in the email is important because truncated feeds discourage actual reading
Self-subscribing to your own newsletter is a practical way to catch bugs, though it took six weeks to notice a sending outage
money_quotes: My instinctive reaction was "I don't have time to do that every week!! I won't remember!". But then I realized that I could automatically generate a weekly blog digest!
Lots of people do not use RSS readers, but they still want to keep up with the blogs they read! That makes sense!
The newsletter gets sent on Thursday every week and I don't have to do anything! Nice!
I got frustrated with these templates last week – it was displaying in a weird way in my email, and I didn't think all the fancy CSS they were using in their template was really adding anything.
One of my pet peeves is blogs which don't include the full content of their posts in their RSS feeds – I end up subscribing to those blogs but then not actually reading them.
I subscribe to the newsletter myself which helps me notice/debug problems like this – it took me 6 weeks to notice that I wasn't getting newsletters anymore, but eventually I noticed and fixed it!
I really like that it's a simple transparent opt-in thing (nobody gets email who doesn't want it!) and that people seem to find it useful!
posts.key_insight: Automating a blog newsletter via RSS-to-email removes all ongoing effort while serving the large audience of readers who prefer email over RSS.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her 2017 professional year, covering her work shipping Kubernetes at Stripe, her growth as a tech lead, and her inclusion-focused organizational projects. She details revamping Stripe's engineering career ladder to be more objective and promoting 'Brag Documents' for tracking accomplishments. She also reviews her prolific output of 4 zines, 77 blog posts, and multiple keynotes, while planning a sabbatical to build Ruby profiling tools in 2018.
key_points: Inclusion work means finding concrete organizational projects that people agree are good ideas but nobody has found time to do, like making engineering ladders more objective
Being a tech lead doesn't mean making all decisions — it means ensuring information flow, manageable scope, and cross-team communication
The 'Brag Document' concept helps ensure accomplishments are recognized, especially across manager changes and during performance reviews
She attended netdev without speaking and learned more there than at the four conferences where she keynoted, suggesting passive learning at deep technical events can outweigh speaking
Cool opportunities don't just happen — they require deliberate planning, as demonstrated by her self-organized sabbatical
money_quotes: I've come to realize that there's a lot of inclusion work that people generally agree is a good idea, but that nobody has found the time to actually do yet.
It gave me a lot of sympathy for why this kind of change is hard to make, but also made me feel like I was empowered to make changes if I was willing to put the work in.
It definitely doesn't mean I'm in charge of making all of the decisions – everyone on the team makes decisions, and I certainly don't have all the answers!
I think I still have a lot to learn about this role, but those 3 things are all things that I think are important, that take time, and are things that I can do.
Cool interesting opportunities don't just happen, I need to do work to plan them and figure out how to make them work in my life.
I attended netdev, on linux kernel networking. I didn't speak, but mentioning it just because I think I learned more from it than from any of the 4 conferences I spoke at.
Maybe in 2018 I will talk about how to build new things that did not exist before.
posts.key_insight: Meaningful inclusion work often isn't about inventing new ideas but about finding the time and willingness to execute on changes everyone already agrees should happen.

posts.summary: Julia Evans investigates how gdb can call C functions in a running program, a capability she found surprisingly non-obvious. She walks through the mechanics: finding function addresses via the symbol table, creating stack frames, changing the instruction pointer, and setting trap instructions. Through experiments with strace and register inspection, she pieces together that gdb builds new stack frames on top of the existing stack, modifies the instruction pointer via ptrace, and places trap instructions in libc functions like longjmp to regain control. She concludes that the process is more complicated than it first appears and embraces having unanswered questions.
key_points: Calling a function from gdb requires pausing the program, finding the function address, setting up a new stack frame, changing registers, and restoring state afterward
You can't simply jump to a function address — gdb must carefully manage the stack pointer, instruction pointer, and return mechanism using trap instructions
GDB creates new stack frames right on top of the current stack rather than allocating a separate pseudo-stack
Breakpoints work by replacing an instruction with 0xcc (int3/SIGTRAP) via PTRACE_POKEDATA, then restoring the original instruction after the trap fires
GDB places trap instructions in libc functions like __libc_siglongjmp and __longjmp, likely as a mechanism to regain control when the called function returns
money_quotes: I thought this was cool because I'd previously thought of gdb as mostly a read-only debugging tool.
You can't just find the address of the function you want to run and then jump to that address. I tried that in gdb (jump foo) and the program segfaulted. Makes sense!
If you're going to run a C function, most likely it needs a stack to store variables on! You definitely don't want it to clobber your current stack.
I stared at the strace output and I still don't understand how it changes, but that's okay.
It seems like the answer to 'how does gdb call a function?' is definitely not that simple.

I still have a lot of unanswered questions about how exactly gdb does all of these things, but that's okay. I don't really need to know the details of how this works and I'm happy to have a slightly improved understanding.
posts.key_insight: Calling C functions from gdb is far more complex than just jumping to an address — it requires carefully orchestrating stack frames, registers, instruction pointers, and trap instructions via ptrace.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her first weekly progress update on building a Ruby profiler during her open source sabbatical. The main challenges involve reliably extracting stack traces from Ruby processes across different OS and Ruby version combinations, especially when debug symbols are stripped. She set up Docker-based integration tests across multiple Linux distributions and made partial progress on profiling system Ruby without debug symbols by using educated guessing to find key memory addresses. She also discusses adopting the C4 open source contribution model after advice from a more experienced maintainer.
key_points: Integration testing across multiple axes (Ruby version, symbol availability, OS) is essential for a profiler that reads process memory directly
System-packaged Ruby binaries often strip debug symbols, requiring creative workarounds like educated guessing of memory addresses
The profiler was rewritten to use bindgen-generated Ruby bindings instead of DWARF, making it possible to work without debug symbols
Open source project governance matters early on — she's considering the C4 (Collective Code Construction Contract) model from ZeroMQ
money_quotes: My profiler interact with the Ruby binaries in a pretty opaque way – it doesn't have much insight into Ruby's internals, it just looks at the process's memory and figures out its stack from there.
I managed to find the address of the ruby_current_thread symbol through some educated guessing!!
I could deal with this by just asking people to install the libruby2.3-dbg package (which contains debug symbols). But it seemed more fun to just try to get it to work without debug symbols.
Right now all the rbenv-installed Ruby tests pass and all the system Ruby tests fail.
This is a 'partial success' instead of a 'total success' because it worked great on my laptop, but then when I tried it in the Docker container it didn't work.
posts.key_insight: Building a reliable profiler requires testing across a combinatorial explosion of environments, and the hardest part is making it work on stripped binaries where key symbols are missing.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents three specific problems she encountered when trying to resolve symbol addresses in memory while building a Ruby profiler. She explains how ASLR randomizes where binaries are loaded, how symbols may live in dynamically linked libraries rather than the main binary, and how the ELF program header's vaddr offset must be accounted for. She walks through each problem using concrete examples with readelf, /proc/PID/maps, and musl's dlsym source code. The post illustrates how a seemingly simple task — finding a symbol's memory address — requires navigating multiple layers of OS and binary format complexity.
key_points: ASLR means you can't just read the symbol table offset directly — you must find the binary's random base address via /proc/PID/maps
Symbols may not be in the main binary at all; they can live in dynamically loaded libraries like libruby, requiring you to search all memory-mapped libraries
The ELF program header's vaddr field introduces yet another offset that must be subtracted to get the correct address
Reading musl's dlsym source code revealed the correct formula: map_base - ph->p_vaddr + sym->st_value
There are two symbol table sections (.symtab and .dynsym), and readelf -a is the easiest way to inspect both
money_quotes: Unsurprisingly, it turns out that figuring out the address in memory corresponding to a given symbol is actually not really that straightforward.

readelf -a is the same in both cases because readelf -a just shows you everything in an ELF file. It's my favorite because I don't need to guess where the information I want is, I can just print out everything and then use grep.
Here we're meeting our good friend /proc/$PID/maps – this file contains a list of memory maps for a process.
It turns out that virtual memory isn't contiguous! Instead process get a bunch of possibly-disjoint memory maps!
So I decided to, instead of randomly guessing, go read the code for dlsym.
It's interesting that such a seemingly simple thing ('what's the address of this symbol?') is so complicated!
It would be nice to be able to just call dlsym and have it do all the right calculations for me, but I think I can't because the symbol is in a different process.
posts.key_insight: Resolving a symbol's runtime memory address requires navigating three layers of indirection — ASLR base addresses, dynamically loaded libraries, and ELF program header offsets — turning a seemingly simple lookup into a surprisingly complex task.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on how Rust has become significantly easier to use since she first tried it in 2013, attributing the improvement to deliberate compiler ergonomics work rather than her own skill growth. She demonstrates this with real compiler error messages that now suggest fixes directly, and highlights features like .clone() as pragmatic escape hatches for beginners. She praises Rust's crate ecosystem, Cargo, bindgen, and macros as tools that enabled her to build a Ruby profiler supporting 35 Ruby versions. For Rust's 2018 goals, she proposes a marketing push to tell people Rust is easier now, and clearer messaging on rust-lang.org about who Rust is for.
key_points: Rust's compiler error messages have improved dramatically since 2016, often telling you exactly how to fix the problem rather than just reporting it
Pragmatic escape hatches like .clone() let beginners make explicit performance tradeoffs to avoid fighting the borrow checker while learning
The crate ecosystem exposes concepts without unnecessary abstraction, using the same naming conventions as the underlying C structures
Rust has a reputation problem: even people close to the community didn't realize how much easier it had become until they tried it again
Being specific about who Rust is for ('people who wish they could write C but found it unapproachable') is more useful than generic inclusive statements
money_quotes: I don't spend as much time being frustrated with the Rust compiler anymore. But it's not because I've learned a lot more about Rust (I haven't, yet!) I think it's mostly because the compiler is more helpful.
I've tried to be careful to say 'easier' instead of 'easy' – I think probably there's some limit to how 'easy' Rust can be!
I just do what the compiler tells me to do, and it works!
I think being able to make tradeoffs like this (where you make the program a little easier to write and sacrifice a little bit of performance) is great when starting out with Rust.
The thing I love the most about this particular tradeoff is that it's explicit. I can search for every place I use .clone() in my program and audit them.
I feel kind of like Rust is my superhero language! If I want to do some weird systemsy magical thing, I know that it'll be possible in Rust. Maybe not easy, but possible!
Rust is great about being inclusive ('Rust could be for you!') but IMO 'here are 10 specific groups of people Rust is for' is dramatically more useful than a generic inclusive statement.
posts.key_insight: Rust's deliberate investment in compiler ergonomics has dramatically lowered the barrier to entry without compromising its core guarantees of safety and performance, but the community needs to actively market this improvement to overcome its reputation as a hard language.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reports on the second week of building a Ruby profiler in Rust. She expanded version support to cover Ruby 1.9.1 through 2.5.0, added line number tracking, and improved error handling using Rust's failure crate. The highlight was successfully profiling Rubocop, a popular Ruby linter, which produced interesting flamegraph visualizations showing how Rubocop's call stacks mirror the AST structure of the code it analyzes. She plans to release a first experimental version in the coming week.
key_points: The profiler now supports every Ruby version from 1.9.1 to 2.5.0, covering essentially all versions in active use
Profiling Rubocop revealed that its cached mode spends 40% of time on file checksums, while uncached mode shows call stacks that mirror the AST structure of the linted program
Practical compromises were made: C extension calls are handled by simply ignoring them rather than crashing
The next milestone is a first experimental release with line number support, flamegraph generation, and a name for the profiler
money_quotes: I profiled Rubocop, and it worked!!!!! Rubocop is a popular linter/static analysis tool for Ruby.
the call stack of Rubocop basically reflects the structure of the program it's linting, which is kind of interesting.
I added "support" for calling into C extensions (by which I mean: the profiler doesn't crash, it just ignores all calls into C extensions)
did you know Rubocop has a cache? I didn't!
there are lots of problems but it's producing useful output!!
lots of bugs that I don't know about yet =)
posts.key_insight: Building a profiler that works across many Ruby versions reveals surprising insights about the tools you profile, like how Rubocop's call stacks mirror the AST structure of the code it analyzes.

posts.summary: Evans explores whether her Ruby profiler should use ptrace to pause a Ruby process before collecting stack traces. She finds that not pausing yields about 1 error per 1000 traces, while pausing reduces errors to about 1 per 10,000 — but doesn't eliminate them entirely. The remaining errors occur when the Ruby interpreter is paused mid-execution of internal functions like vm_call_iseq_setup, where the stack is temporarily in an invalid state. She demonstrates how easy ptrace is to use from Rust, leveraging RAII to ensure processes are always restarted. She concludes that since it's a sampling profiler, dropping a few invalid traces is acceptable.
key_points: Pausing a Ruby process with ptrace reduces stack trace errors from ~1/1000 to ~1/10,000 but doesn't eliminate them entirely
Errors during paused collection occur when the Ruby interpreter is mid-execution of internal functions like vm_call_iseq_setup where the stack is temporarily invalid
Rust's RAII pattern (Drop trait) makes it easy to guarantee a ptraced process is always restarted, even on errors
Ruby's relative slowness compared to a Rust profiler makes unpauseed profiling surprisingly viable — the profiler usually wins the race
For a sampling profiler, losing a small fraction of traces is acceptable and doesn't meaningfully affect results
money_quotes: Ruby programs are relatively slow! Like, trying to collect stack traces from a C program without pausing it at all I think would be a losing battle.
It's still a race, but the profiler has a pretty good chance of winning.
An error rate of 1 in 1000 isn't nothing, but it's also maybe not the end of the world!
I went and took a shower to think about it, and then came up with a way to figure out why!
I wanted to make absolutely sure that after I stopped the Ruby process, I restarted it again.

It does definitely seem like there are points during execution of the Ruby interpreter when the stack is not valid though. That's fine! I can just drop a few stack traces, say 'whoops, those didn't work', and move on.
That is the joy of having a sampling profiler.
posts.key_insight: Even pausing a process with ptrace doesn't guarantee valid stack traces because the interpreter's internal stack can be in a transitional state, but for a sampling profiler, dropping the rare invalid trace is perfectly acceptable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reports on week 3 of building rbspy, a Ruby profiler written in Rust. She released an alpha version, refactored the codebase from a single 1000-line file into well-organized modules, and implemented a clever testing strategy using core dumps. The refactoring was guided by writing an architecture document, which helped identify parts of the code that didn't make sense. She highlights contributions from collaborator Kamal and early community engagement, with plans for Mac support and container support next.
key_points: Writing an architecture document was the most useful refactoring strategy — explaining the project to an outsider revealed what didn't make sense
Core dump-based testing allows verifying stack trace reading against real Ruby processes without needing live processes, giving high confidence in correctness
Keeping test data (core dumps) in a separate repository prevents bloating the main repo while enabling comprehensive testing
Early community engagement through an alpha release yielded bug reports, issues, and a merged pull request within days
The project was refactored from one monolithic file into clearly separated concerns: initialization, OS-specific code, Ruby-version-specific code, and UI
money_quotes: My most useful strategy for refactoring was to write an architecture document. Basically I tried to explain to an outsider how the project was put together, found parts that really didn't make sense, and then refactored until those parts were easier to explain.
I'm very happy to have these tests and they make me feel a lot more confident that the project is actually doing the right thing.
I wanted something that was a little bit fun (profiling is fun!), but not too clever – I want people to be able to actually tell what the project does from the name.
This is exciting because one of my major goals is to get more people contributing to rbspy so it's a sustainable project and not just me.

And they let me make improvements! For example – right now I have a core dump of a process where rbspy gives me an error if I try to get a stack trace out of it. Once I fix the issue, I can check that core dump into rbspy-testdata, add a test, and make sure it stays fixed!
posts.key_insight: Writing an architecture document for others is one of the most effective refactoring strategies because explaining your code to an outsider forces you to confront the parts that don't make sense.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her multi-day journey to read memory maps from a Mac process, something she needs for her Ruby profiler rbspy. On Linux this is trivial via /proc/PID/maps, but on Mac it requires diving into Mach kernel APIs. She first tried the vmmap command-line tool but found it unacceptably slow (2 seconds). She then wrote her own partial vmmap clone in Rust using the mach crate, achieving 15x faster performance in about 100 lines of code. She's still working on handling dynamically linked libraries stored in the 'dyld shared cache' and shares useful resources for others tackling the same problem.
key_points: Linux's /proc filesystem makes reading memory maps trivial compared to Mac's Mach kernel API approach, which took days instead of hours
The vmmap tool works but is impractically slow at 2 seconds per invocation, likely because it pauses the target process
A custom Rust implementation using the mach crate achieved the same result in ~80ms, about 15x faster than vmmap
Mac's 'dyld shared cache' for dynamically linked libraries adds another layer of complexity not yet solved
ASLR means you can't just look up symbol addresses—you need the memory maps to find where binaries are actually loaded
money_quotes: Trying to do this on Mac gave me a lot of appreciation for Linux's "everything is a text file in /proc" philosophy – it feels a little janky sometimes to be parsing a text file, but getting the memory maps on Linux took me like 2 hours and I was done, and trying to figure it out on Mac has taken me like 3 days and I'm still not finished.
My Rust program did what I hoped – it runs in like 80ms or something, about 15x faster than vmmap.
I still don't know exactly what vmmap is doing that's slow (dtruss didn't tell me anything terribly helpful), but whatever it is, my Rust program isn't doing that thing.
It made me happy that I could write a reasonable first approximation of a vmmap clone in 100ish lines of Rust!
I've never done any Mac or BSD systems programming before, and I'm still trying to understand basic concepts like "what is a port?" but I feel like I'm making good progress :D
if vmmap really is pausing the process, that's not good – I'd really prefer that my profiling tool not interfere with the process it's profiling at all.
posts.key_insight: Linux's /proc filesystem approach to exposing process information, while seemingly janky, is dramatically more accessible than Mac's Mach kernel APIs for the same task—a difference measured in hours versus days of development effort.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains how she added Linux container support to rbspy, her Ruby profiler, so it could profile Ruby programs running inside containers from the host machine. The core problem was that containerized processes have different mount namespaces, meaning the Ruby binaries visible in /proc/PID/maps don't exist on the host filesystem. The fix involved using setns() to temporarily switch to the target process's mount namespace to read the binaries, then switching back. After publishing, someone pointed out an even simpler approach: reading files through /proc/PID/root/ which provides the process's filesystem view directly. The whole change was about 50 lines of code, illustrating how understanding container fundamentals makes container support straightforward.
key_points: Programs in containers are just regular processes visible via ps; the only complication is they have different mount namespaces, so filesystem paths inside the container don't exist on the host
You can switch mount namespaces using setns() with the file descriptor from /proc/PID/ns/mnt, but you can't do this from a multithreaded process
An even simpler alternative exists: /proc/PID/root/ provides access to a process's filesystem view without switching namespaces at all
Understanding how containers actually work (Linux namespaces) makes adding container support trivial — no Docker API or container runtime interaction needed
money_quotes: Programs running in containers are just programs, like any other program! You can see them by running ps.
The precise issue here is that the target process (in the container) has a different mount namespace than rbspy.
A process may not be reassociated with a new mount namespace if it is multithreaded.
To read the file /usr/bin/ruby1.9.1 from a process's mount namespace you can just read /proc/PID/root/usr/bin/ruby1.9.1. That's way easier than switching mount namespaces!
We didn't need to care about Docker or anything like that – it's irrelevant what container runtime our containers are using, and we certainly don't interact with Docker at all.
Understanding the fundamentals of how containers work helped us add container support really easily.
posts.key_insight: Container support often reduces to understanding Linux namespaces — once you know the mount namespace is the issue, the fix is a few system calls, no container runtime interaction required.

posts.summary: While building Mac support for rbspy (her Ruby profiler), Julia Evans accidentally discovered what appears to be a Mac kernel bug in High Sierra. Early adopters reported that rbspy was freezing their computers, which was mystifying since the program wasn't running as root. With help from the community, she narrowed it down to a race condition in the `task_for_pid` Mach function and reproduced it with a 17-line C program. The bug causes `ps` and Activity Monitor to stop working when `task_for_pid` is called on a process immediately after exec'ing it. The same bug was independently affecting htop users. Her workaround is simply to sleep for a few milliseconds before calling `task_for_pid`.
key_points: A race condition in macOS High Sierra's task_for_pid Mach function can freeze system tools like ps and Activity Monitor, reproducible with just 17 lines of C
The bug only affects High Sierra (10.13.2), not Sierra, suggesting it was introduced as a regression
Community collaboration was essential — early adopters filed detailed bug reports, @parkr helped narrow the cause, and Twitter users connected it to a known htop issue
The workaround is simple: add a small delay before calling task_for_pid on newly exec'd processes
A non-root user program can effectively freeze parts of macOS without allocating extra memory or doing anything obviously dangerous
money_quotes: I accidentally found something that looks like a Mac kernel bug!
I managed to write a very short (17 line) C program that reliably causes ps to stop working. Without allocating any memory or anything like that!
I was really mystified by this – how could a program not running as root freeze someone's computer?
If I exec a new program and then immediately run task_for_pid on that program, then the process I ran task_for_pid from will hang and ps will stop working.
This is weird and I don't really understand the underlying kernel issue.
Systems programming is weird and exciting though!
posts.key_insight: A race condition in macOS High Sierra's task_for_pid can be triggered by a trivial 17-line C program, freezing system tools — demonstrating how subtle kernel bugs lurk in common system call sequences.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a week 4 progress update on rbspy, her Ruby profiler written in Rust. The project received external contributions including new flags and callgrind output format support, and she added container profiling support. Mac support proved harder than expected due to kernel bugs and memory map difficulties. She's considering building a reusable Rust visualization library and is excited about prototyping memory profiling capabilities.
key_points: External contributors are successfully submitting PRs to the Rust codebase despite not being Rust programmers, validating the language's accessibility
Mac systems programming turned out to be significantly harder than expected, with kernel bugs and difficult memory map APIs
Container profiling support was added, expanding rbspy's usefulness for production environments
She's thinking ahead to memory profiling as a natural extension, with two approaches: object summaries and allocation tracking
money_quotes: it turns out that Mac systems programming is more complicated than I thought
So far it seems like Rust is easy enough that some non-Rust-programmers can come in and start contributing PRs to rbspy, which is really nice to see!

I think it could be cool to build a Rust crate with support for different visualization formats, so that if people build other profilers then they can kinda have access to a consistent library of visualization formats.
I don't really know if I have time for that though! For now I'm going to stay focused on lower level concerns.
I'm not done with CPU profiling (there are still lots of rough edges to sort out!) but once I get things slightly more feature complete I think I kinda want to let the CPU profiler rest for a bit
posts.key_insight: Building developer tools in Rust can lower the barrier to contribution enough that non-Rust programmers can meaningfully contribute, while the real complexity lies in platform-specific systems programming.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes a one-day experiment building a tool that can spy on a running Ruby process's memory allocations using eBPF and uprobes, without modifying the target process. She attaches a small BPF program to Ruby's internal newobj_slowpath function to count allocations per class, using bcc's Python/C toolkit. To resolve class pointers into human-readable names, she writes a Rust program that copies the target process's memory maps into its own address space and calls rb_class2name directly. The result is a live-updating view of which Ruby classes are being allocated most frequently, achieved in a single day of hacking.
key_points: eBPF uprobes let you attach instrumentation code to any userspace function from outside the process, without modifying it
bcc makes writing BPF programs accessible by combining C code that runs in the kernel with Python code that reads results in userspace
To resolve class pointers to names, she copied the Ruby process's entire memory maps into her profiler process and called rb_class2name directly
She handled intermittent segfaults by forking before each potentially crashing call and ignoring child process crashes
The guiding design principle is building tools that feel magical — streaming live memory allocation data from an unmodified process
money_quotes: My idea at the beginning of the day was – what if you could take an arbitrary Ruby process's PID (that was already running!) and start tracking its memory allocations?

You can't ask the kernel to run just any code, though (at least not with eBPF) – you ask it to run "eBPF bytecode" which is basically C code where you're restricted in what memory you can access. And it can't have loops.
But I wasn't inside the Ruby process, so I couldn't exactly call the function. OR COULD I?!
My (terrible/delightful) plan for calling rb_class2name was basically – copy all the memory maps from the target process into my profiler process, and then just call rb_class2name and hope it works.
Of course all of this (copying the memory maps, casting essentially a random address into a function pointer, calling the resulting function) is unsafe in Rust, but I can still do it!
When I finally got this to work at like 9pm today I was so delighted.
The main design principle I'm using right now is – how can I build tools that just feel really magical?
posts.key_insight: eBPF uprobes enable building magical debugging tools that can observe a running process's internals from the outside, without any code changes to the target program.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces an experimental Rust library (rust-bcc) for writing BPF tracing tools, porting the Go BCC library to Rust. She explains how the BPF Compiler Collection works — compiling C to eBPF bytecode, attaching it to kernel/userspace functions, and communicating results back via data structures like hashmaps and perf buffers. She argues that while Python BCC bindings work well for simple tools, Rust is better suited for more complex tracing experiments like streaming Ruby memory allocations. The post walks through two working examples (strlen counting and opensnoop) and demonstrates practical Ruby allocation tracking.
key_points: BCC lets you compile C to eBPF bytecode, attach it to kernel/userspace functions, and communicate results back through shared data structures like hashmaps and perf ring buffers
Python BCC bindings are great for simple tools but fall short for complex experiments requiring high-performance data processing and rich type representations
Rust's #[repr(C)] attribute makes sharing data structures between Rust and C straightforward, and its byteorder crate handles binary decoding naturally
The rust-bcc library enables streaming perf events via Rust closures as callbacks, opening the door to more sophisticated tracing tools
A practical application is already working: tracking Ruby memory allocations in real-time using BPF probes from Rust
money_quotes: It turns out that writing BPF-based tracing tools in Rust is really easy (in some ways easier than doing the same things in Python).
I think it's exciting that you can say 'hey, run this tiny bit of code every time a Ruby object is allocated' and get data back about ongoing allocations!

Once you want to start doing more complicated experiments like 'stream every memory allocation from this Ruby program, calculate some metadata about it, query the original process to find out the class name for that address, and display a useful summary', Python doesn't really cut it.
The really exciting thing about eBPF is what comes next – there's no use keeping a hashmap of string counts if you can't access it!

Basically all the data that comes out of a BPF program is an opaque Vec<u8> right now, so you need to figure out how to decode them yourself. Luckily decoding binary data is something that Rust is quite good at.
This crate is still extremely sketchy and there are bugs & missing features but I wanted to put it on the internet because I think the examples of what you can do with it are really exciting!!
posts.key_insight: Rust's type system and performance make it a natural fit for building complex BPF tracing tools that outgrow Python's capabilities, while remaining just as approachable for simple cases.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reports on week 5 of building rbspy, a sampling CPU profiler for Ruby. The week's highlights include completing Mac support and experimenting with memory allocation profiling. She demonstrates a tool that tracks every Ruby memory allocation by function, filename, and line number, showing how it can reveal surprising allocation sources like Range object creation. She outlines upcoming work including Debian packaging, UI improvements beyond flamegraphs, and continued memory profiling experiments.
key_points: Mac support for rbspy is complete, already being used for real performance work on CocoaPods despite Macs being primarily laptops rather than production servers
A new memory profiling experiment tracks every Ruby allocation's function, filename, and line number from outside the process using just a PID
The eBPF-based allocation tracker revealed that a Range object creation was responsible for over 1200 allocations in rubocop's parser
Flamegraphs don't work well for recursive programs, motivating exploration of alternative visualization styles like pprof's graph output
Core rbspy functionality is mostly complete, shifting focus toward UX, packaging, and experimental profiling capabilities
money_quotes: I'm curious about what memory profiling tools I can build that work from outside of the Ruby process (just give the tool a PID and it tells you about the internals of your Ruby program!)
It's not immediately obvious what the allocation is here, but it turns out that it's self.begin_pos...self.end_pos – that allocates a new Range object.

This particular example doesn't let us make any actual performance improvements to rubocop (1000 allocations over 10 seconds is actually not a lot at all), but it's interesting to be able to see which lines are allocating the most.
Supporting Mac is kind of an interesting thing because in my mind rbspy is a production profiler, and Macs are basically all laptops.
Flamegraphs are a nice way to present profiler data, but they don't work well for recursive programs.
posts.key_insight: Profiling tools that work from outside a process—requiring only a PID—can reveal surprising allocation patterns like implicit Range object creation that would otherwise go unnoticed.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her approach to writing useful performance reviews, focusing on positive constructive feedback rather than generic praise. She outlines three main strategies: highlighting specific strengths, articulating the person's impact on the organization, and discussing forward-looking goals. She emphasizes that positive feedback should be specific and actionable, not just complimentary. She also argues that negative feedback should never appear for the first time in a performance review, and that investing significant time in writing thoughtful reviews is worthwhile.
key_points: Generic positive feedback like 'X is so great!' is easy but useless — specific positive feedback explaining exactly what makes someone's work great is far more valuable
When highlighting strengths, be careful to focus on skills central to the person's role and appropriate to their level, to avoid accidentally implying they lack more important skills
Documenting someone's impact makes invisible work visible, especially 1:1 mentoring which is incredibly important but often unrecognized
The 'what could be better?' section is a great place for forward-looking positive feedback — affirming goals the person already has and suggesting ambitious new directions
Poorly-considered negative feedback is much more destructive than poorly-considered positive feedback, so err on the side of omitting negative feedback rather than including something unhelpful
money_quotes: It's very easy to write positive feedback that isn't useful – 'X is so great!' 'I love working with X!' 'X is one of the best developers I know!'. But positive feedback can (and should!) be useful.
1:1 mentoring is incredibly important but often not that visible to other people, so bringing it up in a perf review helps makes sure it's recognized.

My view right now is that poorly-considered negative feedback is much more destructive than poorly-considered positive feedback, so I'm much more likely to include positive observations that I'm not 100% sure of than negative ones.
I've written a bunch of performance reviews which contain no negative feedback, which I'm fine with.
Negative feedback shouldn't show up for the first time in a perf review.
X is great at proactively working on systems that aren't currently on fire, but are going to be on fire in 3-6 months if we don't intervene.
I really appreciate it when I get really thoughtful + insightful feedback about my work from other people, and if I can do that at all for someone else I think that's a really good thing.
posts.key_insight: The most useful performance reviews replace generic praise with specific positive feedback about strengths, impact, and forward-looking goals — making invisible contributions visible and giving people actionable information about what they're doing well.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on four years of working remotely for a San Francisco company (Stripe) while living in Montreal. She addresses common concerns about remote work across three categories: mental health/productivity, basic job effectiveness, and career growth. She's been promoted multiple times, led major projects, and learned extensively from colleagues, attributing her success to working in a remote-friendly organization and being intentional about communication. She emphasizes that remote work requires deliberate relationship-building and overcommunication, but that it hasn't limited her career growth or ability to do meaningful work.
key_points: Career development (promotions, leadership, mentoring) is fully possible remotely, but requires working in an organization with strong remote support and a critical mass of remote workers
Remote work demands intentional relationship-building since there are no serendipitous encounters — you must explicitly decide to talk to people or you may never interact with them
Having a team that's at least 50% remote forces the whole team to adopt remote-first communication, which makes it work for everyone
Overcommunication is essential: documenting progress, having regular 1:1s, and actively sharing what you're working on prevents work from getting lost or unrecognized
The adjustment period is real — it took at least a year before the loneliness and disconnection started to feel manageable
money_quotes: If I don't explicitly decide to talk to someone, it's very possible that I'll literally never talk to them.
Working as the only remote on a team of people who are all in person seems like hard mode – I have never done it and I'm not that interested in trying that.
I think it can actually be really useful to be somewhat extroverted when working remotely.
It can be really alienating to be in a meeting where people can't hear you well and you have to keep repeating yourself.
I really struggled with feeling lonely/disconnected for at least a year after I started.
I don't feel like working remote ever gets in the way of anything I want to accomplish.
It's way more useful to get advice from someone who's familiar with everything I've been doing for the last year than from someone just swooping in with their thoughts.
posts.key_insight: Remote work succeeds not through isolation management but through deliberate overcommunication and intentional relationship-building within an organization that has genuine remote-first infrastructure.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reports on weeks 6 and 7 of her sabbatical building rbspy, a Ruby profiler. The major milestone is that rbspy appears to actually work reliably, with no new bug reports in weeks. She added several new features including a 'report' subcommand for generating visualizations from raw data, a live summary display during profiling, and C function tracking in Ruby stacks. Her next goals are releasing a proper beta version and creating documentation.
key_points: rbspy has reached a stability milestone where it works reliably for other users with no new bug reports in weeks
The new 'rbspy report' subcommand lets users record raw profiling data first and decide how to visualize it later
A live summary feature shows the top 20 functions by self-time, updating every second during recording
She deliberately paused exciting eBPF experiments to focus on finishing and documenting what already works
money_quotes: When I started out on this sabbatical, I was pretty worried that I wouldn't be able to stabilize it and that it would always be kind of buggy and unstable.
I think it works, though!
It's surprising and exciting to me that this program actually seems to be working for other people!! They can actually use it to profile their code!
Mac systems programming is hard and I'm not planning to invest more time in it.
They're on pause for now because I decided it was better to actually finish some stuff and write a documentation site.
Also next week I'm giving a talk about rbspy! Looking forward to that, but first I need to write the talk :)
posts.key_insight: The transition from 'will this ever work?' to 'people are actually using this' is a surprising and gratifying milestone in building developer tools.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that blog success should be measured by meaningful conversations and relationships rather than page views. She explains that blogging has been rewarding because it connects her with people working on similar problems who share tips and insights. She advocates for writing posts that genuinely help people, treating readers as real humans rather than an 'audience' consuming 'content.' She recommends tracking subscriber counts as a more meaningful metric than page views, and emphasizes that even small blogs with engaged readers can be deeply successful.
key_points: Page views are a poor measure of blog success; conversations and relationships with readers are far more meaningful
Writing publicly about your work invites helpful feedback and connections from others in the same field
Subscriber counts are a better metric than page views because they reflect ongoing interest rather than one-time visits
Blog posts that genuinely help people are more rewarding than posts that merely get high traffic
Letting people subscribe via RSS, email, and Twitter builds a sustained readership without obnoxious marketing tactics
money_quotes: I find it much more useful to ask myself – am I having interesting conversations / learning things with as a result of blogging?
It's very easy to write blog posts that are widely read but don't make a big difference to the people who read them.

I'd much rather have a few hundred people who are keeping up with what I'm working on and sometimes chip in suggestions when they have something to add than 100,000 people who read 1 blog post and make some snarky comments.
A result of this attitude is that I don't write about topics I'm not interested in having discussions with people about!
Because I know that all these people I like read my blog, it makes me want to write posts that are kind and interesting, not just 'content' that I think my 'audience' will 'engage' with.
People keep periodically claiming that RSS is dead but thousands of people subscribe to my RSS feed so that's obviously not true.
Talking about what I'm working on in public means that other people working in the same field can tell me useful things and help me out, which is awesome.
posts.key_insight: A blog's real success is measured not by page views but by the meaningful conversations, genuine help, and human connections it creates between writer and readers.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her weekend project building an ltrace clone using eBPF and bcc, written in Rust. The original ltrace tool traces library calls but has problems: it can't reliably attach to running processes and introduces significant performance overhead via ptrace. Her prototype solves both issues by using eBPF uprobes instead, parsing ELF files to find dynamically linked functions, then attaching eBPF probes to trace them. The whole thing is about 200 lines of Rust and successfully traces library calls on a running Firefox process.
key_points: ltrace's two main problems are inability to attach to running processes and high performance overhead from ptrace
eBPF uprobes offer a faster, lower-overhead alternative to ptrace for tracing library calls in production
Finding dynamically linked functions requires parsing ELF relocations (rela.dyn and rela.plt sections), which is straightforward with the goblin Rust crate
The prototype generates per-function eBPF tracing code from a template and attaches each as a uprobe to the target library
The entire implementation is about 200 lines of Rust, demonstrating how accessible systems-level tooling has become
money_quotes: This is neat because it's a way to spy on what a program is doing that's different from spying on its system calls!
I don't fully understand why ltrace can't attach to processes, but that's not what this post is about.
It turns out it's not hard!!!!!!!! Basically if we run readelf -a we can find a bunch of function names in the sections rela.dyn and rela.plt, and that lists the functions.
I thought it was cool that it was possible to implement such a cool thing in so little code so I wanted to explain how it works!

Using eBPF + bcc to do tracing is in general faster than using ptrace to do tracing (which is what ltrace does) so this approach should also be lower overhead and maybe more suitable for production use.

I like that Rust crates generally don't try to make things simpler or harder than they are – like they don't introduce unnecessary abstractions, they just make it relatively easy for you get the information you need.
posts.key_insight: eBPF uprobes combined with ELF parsing make it possible to build a working ltrace alternative in about 200 lines of Rust that solves the original tool's inability to attach to running processes and its performance overhead.

posts.summary: Julia Evans appeared on the CodeNewbie podcast to discuss how the internet works, covering TCP, UDP, netcat, and other networking fundamentals. She notes that many programmers go years without learning about computer networking, despite these concepts being both interesting and approachable. The post promotes making systems-level knowledge accessible to beginners and shares related resources including her own zines and a recommended networking book.
key_points: Many programmers don't learn about networking fundamentals like TCP for years into their careers, even though these concepts are approachable
A segment of programmers are motivated by wanting to understand how things actually work under the hood
Networking knowledge is especially satisfying because networking is everywhere in computing
Systems-level concepts can and should be introduced to beginning programmers rather than treated as advanced topics
money_quotes: I didn't learn anything about how TCP worked until I'd been programming for 10 years!
I feel like there's a certain segment of people who get into programming partly because they just want to know how things work
I have a special love for networking because it's EVERYWHERE and I find it very satisfying to know how it works
I feel like often people don't learn about things like computer networking for a long time but I think a lot of systems-y concepts are both really interesting and pretty approachable
posts.key_insight: Computer networking fundamentals like TCP are both more approachable and more universally relevant than most programmers realize, and there's no reason to wait years before learning them.

posts.summary: Julia Evans catalogs the specific networking concepts and skills she's learned over roughly eight years, from basic HTTP requests to container networking. Rather than prescribing a learning path, she presents her knowledge as an accumulation of small, concrete skills picked up one at a time, mostly on the job. She lists tools she's found useful for networking tasks and describes her learning process as identifying small gaps, filling them, and repeating. The post argues that broad competence in networking comes from patient, incremental learning rather than any single course or resource.
key_points: Computer networking competence is built from dozens of discrete, concrete skills learned individually over years, not from a single learning resource
Practical tools like tcpdump, wireshark, curl, and dig are essential for hands-on networking understanding
The learning process is iterative: identify something small you don't know, figure it out, repeat
Most networking knowledge was acquired on the job when needed, not through formal study
Even with broad competence, there are always remaining mysteries (like wifi)
money_quotes: Like anything else, "computer networking" involves a large number of different concepts and skills and tools and I've learned them all one at a time.
"can reliably use tcpdump without reading the man page"
It's really fun to see how learning a bunch of tiny things adds up over time.
Today I feel like I can handle most things about computer networking that I run into in my job, and I don't feel like there are that many Big New Ideas about networking I don't know about.
I've found it really useful to take it one step at a time.
I've learned a lot of the things I know at work, and I think picking up new things when I need them has been fine.
posts.key_insight: Broad networking competence comes not from mastering a textbook but from accumulating dozens of small, concrete skills over time through curiosity and on-the-job problem solving.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her wishlist for RustConf talks as a program committee member, focusing on talks that help people become better systems programmers. She suggests topics ranging from Rust compiler internals and library deep-dives to systems concepts like databases and filesystems, C interop best practices, and emerging Rust patterns. She emphasizes that speakers don't need to be expert Rust programmers, noting that intermediate-level speakers often give the most useful talks because they remember what learning felt like.
key_points: Rust's main value proposition for many developers is making systems programming accessible to people who couldn't do it before
The Rust community is still discovering its own programming patterns and best practices since the language is relatively new
Intermediate-level programmers often give better conference talks than experts because they remember the learning experience
C interop and cross-platform code are critically important but under-documented areas in the Rust ecosystem
Studying architecture choices in large, high-quality C/C++ codebases could teach valuable lessons for structuring complex Rust programs
money_quotes: My main interest in Rust is that it lets me to do systems programming, which I couldn't really do before!
Often people think that you need to be a wizard expert to give a talk at a programming conference. This isn't true!
People who are at an intermediate level give extremely useful talks, because they remember what it was like to learn the material for the first time and so can explain it well.
I feel like it's very easy to write sketchy Rust bindings for a C library and I'd love to see some best practices here!
I feel like since Rust is so new, we're still learning what works and what doesn't when writing Rust code.
When I started writing Rust this year, I got the advice to make my structs own all of their data, and then almost always make my functions take references to structs.
posts.key_insight: Rust's greatest potential is democratizing systems programming, and the community benefits most from talks by intermediate learners who can bridge the gap between beginners and experts.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces the documentation site for rbspy, her Ruby profiler. She emphasizes her belief that undocumented software might as well not exist, and describes the extra effort she put into making the docs useful. Beyond standard usage docs, she included a comparison with stackprof and a general guide to profiling concepts like flamegraphs and benchmarking. She built the site with Hugo using a minimalist theme and added a responsive hamburger menu for mobile.
key_points: Good documentation is essential — undocumented software might as well not exist
The docs include a comparison between rbspy and stackprof to help users choose the right tool for their situation
A general guide to profiling basics (flamegraphs, benchmarks vs profilers) was included because many developers are confused about fundamentals
The site was built with Hugo using a minimalist theme, with manual CSS tweaks for mobile responsiveness
money_quotes: My view about documentation is – if software isn't well documented, it might as well not exist.
I've spent SO MUCH time explaining other people's software on this blog, so I figured it was extra important to make sure the software I write is well explained.
This is not because rbspy is awesome and stackprof is bad (stackprof is very useful!), but because rbspy and stackprof are useful in different situations.
I've heard from a lot of people who want to make their programs faster that they're confused about some of the basics of profiling.
I think documentation is really important, and if you have a question probably other people do too.
posts.key_insight: Software documentation should go beyond basic usage to include conceptual guides and tool comparisons that help users understand the broader domain, not just the specific tool.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her experience taking a 3-month open source sabbatical funded by Segment's Open Fellowship to build rbspy, a Ruby profiler. She catalogs various open source funding models and explains how flexibility from both Segment and her employer Stripe made the sabbatical possible. She emphasizes that having a working prototype beforehand gave her confidence, and that applying even without a clear plan led to a great outcome. The post encourages others to consider sabbaticals and to apply for opportunities even when logistics seem uncertain.
key_points: Open source funding comes in many forms—corporate employment, foundations, crowdfunding, stipends, and fellowships—and the 'sprint of activity' model worked well for a focused project
Flexibility from both the fellowship sponsor (Segment) and employer (Stripe) was essential to making the sabbatical happen, including a 6-month delay
Having a working prototype beforehand built confidence that a full project was achievable during the limited sabbatical time
Accepting that you don't program after work is fine—sabbaticals create dedicated space for projects you wouldn't otherwise tackle
Applying to opportunities without a clear plan for how you'd accept them can still work out well
money_quotes: I've been at Stripe for 4 years, so Stripe's sabbatical policy said that I was allowed to take a 3-month unpaid break and come back after.
I really like my job, and I didn't want to quit just to be able to dedicate some time to a project that I was interested in.

It's been useful for me to accept that I don't really want to program after work or on the weekends. But of course there are projects I'm excited about building, so this sabbatical was a great opportunity to do programming work that I wouldn't otherwise do.
I think the biggest thing that helped me be confident in my ability to do the project was – I had a prototype!!
I don't really believe in 'github is your resume' (lots of great programmers don't do any open source work! that's fine!) but it does feel good to have.
So I think it was a great idea to apply with no clear plan of how I'd handle it if they accepted me, because we figured it out and it was fine!
If you're not getting rejections you're not being ambitious enough, julia!
posts.key_insight: Sabbaticals and fellowships can unlock meaningful open source work that wouldn't happen otherwise, but they require flexibility from sponsors and employers, a prototype to build confidence, and the willingness to apply before you have all the logistics figured out.

posts.summary: Julia reverse engineered the Notability app's proprietary .note file format to convert her existing SVG drawings from another app. She discovered the format is a zip file containing Apple plist files with drawing data stored as arrays of 32-bit floats. The entire process took about a day and resulted in less than 200 lines of Python. She walks through each step from unzipping the file to decoding curve points, demonstrating that reverse engineering is more approachable than it seems because developers tend to reuse standard formats rather than inventing custom ones.
key_points: The Notability .note format is just a zip file containing Apple plist files, with drawing data stored as arrays of 32-bit floats for curve points, colors, and widths
Reverse engineering file formats is approachable because developers reuse standard formats (zip, plist, float arrays) rather than inventing custom ones
The decoding process involved trial and error - trying msgpack, bencode, and other formats before realizing the data was simply an array of 32-bit floats
Proprietary file formats in mobile drawing apps prevent interoperability, but reverse engineering makes conversion possible
A useful debugging tactic was regenerating an existing plist file from decoded data and verifying it matched, then turning that into a unit test
money_quotes: I thought I'd write up the process of reverse engineering this file format because it wasn't really THAT hard, and reverse engineering often seems kind of unapproachable and scary.
I think the thing that makes reverse engineering not that hard is – developers reuse code! People don't usually invent totally custom file formats!
Nothing in here was really complicated – it was just some existing standard formats (zip! apple plist! an array of floats!) combined together in a pretty simple way.
It's really fun to start with a seemingly intractable or very manual task, think 'wait, I'm a programmer!!' and manage to use the power of programming to (at least sort of) do what I want!
This is going to sound pretty straightforward but in real life I was more confused and complained more.
I do find it kind of frustrating that all these mobile drawing apps use proprietary file formats and you can't convert between them without reverse engineering. But reverse engineering is possible!
posts.key_insight: Reverse engineering proprietary file formats is more approachable than it seems because developers reuse standard formats like zip, plist, and simple float arrays rather than inventing custom encodings.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces the release of her new zine about profiling and tracing with perf on Linux. She highlights that perf is an underappreciated tool that many developers don't know about, and the zine aims to help people get started with it. She also explains her funding model: selling early access for $10, then releasing for free after a few weeks. 250 people bought early access to this zine.
key_points: perf is a powerful but underused Linux profiling and tracing tool that many developers don't know about
The zine follows a timed-release funding model: paid early access ($10) followed by free release after 2-3 weeks
Donation-based funding didn't work — switching to early-access sales proved more sustainable with 250 buyers
The goal is making perf approachable and showing people why it's worth learning
money_quotes: perf is one of my favorite programs for profiling / tracing Linux programs and SO MANY people don't know about it and don't know how to get started.
I decided a while back that giving away zines 100% for free wasn't working for me. Making them is a lot of work!
I used to have donation links on my zines page but nobody really used them so I don't do that anymore.
250 of you bought early access to the zine (thank you!!) and I feel great about that.
