And today I'm releasing it for free so that everyone else can read it too!
posts.key_insight: A timed-release model — paid early access followed by free release — can sustainably fund educational technical content where pure donations fail.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post about a talk Julia Evans gave at Localhost, the Recurse Center's monthly talk series. The talk covers the technical core of rbspy, her Ruby profiler tool, focusing on how it reads memory from the Ruby interpreter to determine what function a process is running. She highlights the challenges of supporting multiple Ruby versions and whether the Ruby process needs to be stopped during profiling. The post is essentially a pointer to the 30-minute video talk followed by 30 minutes of Q&A.
key_points: rbspy works by reading memory directly out of the Ruby interpreter to determine what function is currently running
Supporting multiple Ruby versions is a key technical challenge for the profiler
The profiler aims to work without stopping the Ruby process being profiled
The Recurse Center's Localhost format encourages deep technical talks with extensive audience Q&A
money_quotes: How do we read memory out of the Ruby interpreter to figure out what function a Ruby process is running?
My favourite thing about Localhost's talk format is that speakers give relatively in depth talks about technical topics, and then people ask lots of questions at the end.
posts.key_insight: Building a Ruby profiler requires reading memory directly from the Ruby interpreter across multiple versions without stopping the target process.

posts.summary: Evans walks through the practical steps of debugging a segfault on Linux, from getting a core dump to extracting a useful stack trace with gdb. She explains the two key settings that often prevent core dumps from being written: ulimit and kernel.core_pattern. The post covers how Ubuntu's Apport system intercepts core dumps by default and how to override it. She also briefly mentions valgrind and ASAN as alternative approaches. The post distills what took her a full week of frustration into a clear, reproducible five-step process.
key_points: Core dumps are often silently disabled because ulimit -c defaults to 0, meaning the kernel won't write them at all
Ubuntu's Apport system intercepts core dumps via kernel.core_pattern and ignores crashes from non-package binaries, which silently eats your core dumps
The practical debugging flow is: try valgrind first, then if needed set ulimit and core_pattern, get a core dump, and open it in gdb with debugging symbols
Loading both the binary's symbols and shared library symbols in gdb is essential to get meaningful stack traces instead of question marks
ASAN can change program behavior enough to make bugs disappear, since it may use a different memory allocator
money_quotes: I'd never done this before, and some of the basic things involved (get a core dump! find the line number that segfaulted!) took me a long time to figure out.
When I originally tried to get a core dump, I was pretty frustrated for a long time because – Linux wasn't writing a core dump!! Where was my core dump????
This caused me a lot of confusion (what is this apport thing and what is it doing with my core dumps??)
If you have a core dump & debugging symbols and gdb, you are in an amazing situation!! You can go up and down the call stack, print out variables, and poke around in memory to see what happened.

I ended up just overriding this Apport business and setting kernel.core_pattern because I was on a dev machine, I didn't care whether Apport was working on not, and I didn't feel like trying to convince Apport to give me my core dumps.
in my case the segfault disappeared with ASAN turned on for some reason, possibly because the ASAN build used a different memory allocator
posts.key_insight: Getting a useful stack trace from a segfault on Linux is straightforward once you know the two settings that silently prevent core dumps: ulimit -c and kernel.core_pattern.

posts.summary: Julia Evans discovered a practical use for the ed text editor when she needed to batch edit 200 files at work. After reading Michael W Lucas's 'Ed Mastery' book (published as a semi-joke on April Fools' Day), she learned that any ed session can be replayed as a script, making it useful for applying vim-macro-like edits to multiple files. She walks through how she translated a simple Vim workflow into an ed script to insert lines after a pattern match. The post reframes ed from a terrifying relic into an occasionally useful tool for batch file editing.
key_points: Any ed editing session corresponds to a script that can be replayed, making it a practical tool for batch file editing
Ed commands map fairly directly to Vim commands, so translating a Vim macro into an ed script is straightforward
The name 'grep' originates from ed syntax (g/re/p), which is a fun piece of Unix history
Ed's default error message is just '?' with no explanation, which makes it intimidating but can be configured to give better messages
money_quotes: Basically if you do something wrong, ed will just print out a single, unhelpful, ?.
I'd basically dismissed ed as an old arcane Unix tool that had no practical use today.
The other neat thing about Ed is that any Ed session corresponds to a script that you can replay!
So if I know Ed, then I can use Ed basically as a way to easily apply vim-macro-like programs to my files.
It was super surprising and delightful to me to find a practical use for Ed!
The most compelling thing about Ed is that I use simple Vim macros a lot, and it's a pretty direct way to translate a Vim macro into a way to batch edit a bunch of files.
posts.key_insight: The ed text editor, often dismissed as an obsolete Unix relic, becomes genuinely useful when you realize that any ed session is a replayable script — making it a simple way to batch-apply Vim-macro-like edits across many files.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her practical Wireshark workflow for debugging networking problems, focusing on the small number of features she actually uses regularly. She describes her typical process of capturing packets with tcpdump in production, then analyzing them locally in Wireshark. The post covers five main techniques: analyzing pcap files, isolating TCP connections, decoding protocols on unusual ports, inspecting packet contents, and searching with Wireshark's query language. She emphasizes that knowing just a few Wireshark features covers 95% of her use cases, and that the tool doubles as a way to learn networking protocols by making them concrete and inspectable.
key_points: Wireshark is most useful for determining whether a client or server is to blame in networking issues, by examining the lifecycle of TCP connections
A practical Wireshark workflow starts with tcpdump capture in production, then local analysis — separating packet capture from packet analysis
Wireshark's query language is much more powerful than tcpdump's, making it effective to capture broadly with tcpdump then filter precisely in Wireshark
The Statistics > Conversations view lets you find slow TCP connections by sorting on duration across thousands of packets
Wireshark can serve as a learning tool for networking protocols by making abstract concepts like TLS handshakes visible and concrete
money_quotes: Wireshark is one of my very favourite networking tools.
I only know how to do a very small number of things with it, and those things are really useful!

Usually there's a client and a server, and there's a bug or configuration error on either the client or the server. Wireshark is invaluable for helping me figure out whether I should blame the client or the server.

Wireshark's packet search language is much more powerful than tcpdump's (and it has tab completion!!), so I'll often capture a large amount of packets with tcpdump and then do some more in depth searching using Wireshark.

I can see that the first two packets are 'client hello' and 'server hello', and it makes the protocol seem less like a scary mystery and more like a concrete thing that I can easily look at the details of.
The 5 tricks I've described here are probably 95% of what I use Wireshark for – you only need to know a little Wireshark to start using it to debug networking issues!
posts.key_insight: You only need a small number of Wireshark techniques to effectively debug most networking problems, and the tool's real power lies in making abstract protocols concrete and helping you quickly determine which side of a connection is misbehaving.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her notes from the first day of the netdev conference, where she attended an IPsec workshop. She explains that IPsec is a kernel-level protocol for encrypting IP packets, commonly used for VPNs but not exclusively. The post breaks down how IPsec works with its userspace key exchange (IKE) and kernel encryption components, and contrasts it with TLS to explain why IPsec lives in the kernel. She covers the two modes of IPsec (transport and tunnel), the concept of opportunistic IPsec using DNS records, and notes that IPsec and TLS have historically influenced each other's design.
key_points: IPsec has two parts: userspace handles key exchange via IKE, while the kernel handles actual packet encryption, managed through the unintuitive 'ip xfrm' command
IPsec is easier to implement in the kernel than TLS because key exchanges happen infrequently and sessions are long-lived, unlike TLS's constant per-connection handshakes
IPsec has two modes: transport mode (encrypts packet contents, similar to TLS) and tunnel mode (encrypts entire packet including headers, used for VPNs)
Opportunistic IPsec allows encrypted connections to any supporting host by looking up public keys via DNS IPSECKEY records, encrypting even plain HTTP connections
money_quotes: VPN is just a general term meaning 'your IP packets get encrypted and sent through another server' and VPNs can be implemented using a bunch of different protocols.
The way you inspect these databases is, extremely unintuitively, using a command called `ip xfrm`. What does xfrm mean? I don't know!
For some reason that might be obvious but that I don't understand yet (??) people don't want to do key exchange in the kernel.
With TLS, there isn't a natural protocol boundary where you need to start doing encryption – with IPsec, you just encrypt every IP packet in a given IP range.

Somewhat confusingly, even though Libreswan and Strongswan are different software packages, they both install a binary called `ipsec` for managing IPsec connections, and the two `ipsec` binaries are not the same program.
Your connection is now encrypted! Even though it's a HTTP connection! so interesting!
The IPsec and TLS protocols have actually learned from each other over time – like they said IPsec's IKE protocol had perfect forward secrecy before TLS.
posts.key_insight: IPsec's split architecture—userspace for infrequent key exchange, kernel for packet encryption—is what makes it practical to implement in the Linux kernel, unlike TLS which requires constant per-connection negotiation.

posts.summary: Julia Evans summarizes a keynote by Van Jacobson at netdev about the future of congestion control on the internet. The talk argued that the internet has fundamentally changed since 1988: switches are no longer dramatically faster than servers, so clients can easily saturate links in the middle of the network. Counter-intuitively, sending packets more slowly at the bottleneck rate actually improves performance because it avoids tail-of-stream packet drops that cause expensive timeouts. The proposed solution involves adding an 'earliest departure time' timestamp to Linux kernel packet data structures and replacing queues with timing wheels for scheduling. Since Linux runs on Android phones, wifi routers, and most servers, changing the Linux networking stack alone could meaningfully improve internet congestion.
key_points: The internet's architecture has shifted: switches in routers are no longer dramatically faster than server NICs, making it easier for clients to saturate links and cause congestion
Sending packets more slowly at the bottleneck rate is better even selfishly, because packet loss at the end of a stream causes expensive timeouts while mid-stream loss is cheap to recover from
Google's BBR algorithm can measure bottleneck speeds, but the Linux networking stack's queue-based design makes it hard to send at controlled rates
Adding an 'earliest departure time' timestamp to Linux's skb packet data structure and using timing wheels instead of queues would enable more sophisticated traffic shaping
Linux's dominance across servers, Android phones, and consumer routers means improving just the Linux networking stack could have outsized impact on internet congestion
money_quotes: It turns out that if you implemented TCP that way, the internet would collapse and grind to a halt. We know that it would collapse because it did kinda collapse, in 1986.
Sending packets more slowly often actually results in better performance (even if you are the only one doing it).
The key observation is that packet loss in the middle of your stream is much better than packet loss at the end of your stream.
Even if everybody else is sending all their packets really fast, it's still more advantageous for you to send your packets at the correct rate.
Networking software isn't really designed to send data at a controlled rate!

He said that timing wheels actually perform better than queues for scheduling work – they both offer constant time operations, but the timing wheels constant is smaller because of some stuff to do with cache performance.

I usually think of TCP/IP as something that we figured out in the 80s, so it was really fascinating to hear that folks think that there are still serious issues with how we're designing our networking protocols.
posts.key_insight: Counter-intuitively, sending network packets more slowly at the bottleneck rate produces better performance than sending as fast as possible, and a small change to Linux's packet data structure could enable this across much of the internet.

posts.summary: Julia Evans breaks down how IP addresses and routing work on Linux, covering three systems that affect packet delivery: route tables, iptables, and IPsec. She explains subnets and CIDR notation, then walks through how Linux decides where to send packets using the route table. The bulk of the post covers iptables NAT — both destination NAT (used to redirect packets to different addresses, as in container networking tools like kube2iam) and source NAT (used to masquerade container IPs as host IPs). She closes by mapping these Linux networking concepts to their AWS VPC equivalents, showing how the same principles apply at cloud infrastructure scale.
key_points: IP headers contain source/destination addresses and TTL but not ports — ports live at the TCP/UDP layer, which is why TCP port 8080 and UDP port 8080 are different
Linux route tables map subnets to network devices, which becomes especially important when working with container networking and virtual devices like docker0
iptables can intercept packets at various stages and rewrite source or destination IP addresses through NAT, enabling powerful tricks like making a local service impersonate a remote IP
Source NAT (MASQUERADE) is essential for containers because the outside world has no idea about internal container IP addresses and can't reply to them
AWS VPC networking concepts like route tables, NAT gateways, and VPC peering map directly to their Linux counterparts
money_quotes: There are 14 fields in the IP header. The only 3 important ones for most people to know about are: the source IP address, the destination IP address, the TTL.
One thing you'll notice isn't in the IP header is a port! The TCP and UDP protocols both have ports, but that lives at a different network layer.
Usually iptables rules make me want to hide under the couch but in the last year I've become a little less afraid of them.

Linux won't just rewrite packets to 169.254.169.254:80 to go to $LOCAL_IP:8081, it'll also modify the reply packets from $LOCAL_IP:8081 to make them appear as if they came from 169.254.169.254. So from the perspective of the application receiving the reply, it has no idea that it's not talking to the IP 169.254.169.254. Lies!!!
MASQUERADE is a confusing way of saying 'use source NAT and pretend the packet is coming from this host's IP address'.

If you have a bunch of containers with weird virtual container IP addresses sending packets to the outside world, you can't just let them use those IP addresses!! The outside world has no idea about your container IPs and will not be able to reply to those packets.
Not understanding what those things mean hasn't hurt me yet.
posts.key_insight: IP routing on Linux involves three layered systems — route tables, iptables NAT, and IPsec — and understanding how they intercept and rewrite packets is essential for anyone working with containers or cloud networking.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine 'Bite Size Command Line', a sequel to 'Bite Size Linux', which explains the basics of Unix command line tools in an approachable format. Despite having used Linux for 15 years, she learned several new tricks while writing it. The zine aims to help beginners by curating the most important command line arguments and options, since man pages often overwhelm with too many options. She positions the zine as a substitute for the verbal knowledge transfer that typically happens between experienced and newer command line users.
key_points: Even after 15 years of Linux use, writing the zine revealed useful new tricks like bash process substitution and human-readable sorting
Command line tools have too many options, and man pages don't distinguish crucial flags from rarely-used ones
Much command line knowledge gets passed down verbally, creating a barrier for people without experienced colleagues to learn from
The zine aims to be a curated guide that acts as a knowledgeable friend pointing out what matters most
money_quotes: I kind of didn't think it was that exciting – I thought 'whatever, I know command line tools, I've been using Linux for 15 years'.
Most command line tools have a TON of command line arguments, and it's often hard to tell by reading the man page which ones are crucial to know and which ones hardly anyone uses.
I think a lot of this knowledge often gets passed down verbally, which makes it harder to learn if you don't know many command line users.
The goal of this zine is basically to be your helpful, more experienced friend who's been using these tools for a while and can tell you which bits are the most important.
It turns out that I learned quite a few fun new tricks!
posts.key_insight: Command line mastery is often gatekept by verbal tradition rather than documentation, and curated guides that highlight what actually matters can democratize that knowledge.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares four recent podcast appearances covering topics from rbspy and profiling to zines, teaching, and computer networking. She reflects on the differences between podcasting and her primary medium of writing, noting that podcasts don't allow the same editing control. She finds podcasts easier to organize than conference talks since they require no travel, and notes the unexpected importance of recording equipment quality. She sees podcasting as useful practice for extemporaneous speaking and mentions aspirations for future recorded content like animated videos.
key_points: Podcasts cover a range of her interests: profiling tools (rbspy), teaching through zines, and computer networking fundamentals
Writing allows editing out confusion in ways that live conversation does not, making podcasts feel scarier
Podcasts are much easier to organize than conference talks because they eliminate travel
Sound quality and recording equipment matter more than she expected
money_quotes: They told me I'm the guest that asked them the most questions, which I took as a compliment :)
I find podcasts a little scary – my main medium is blogging/writing, which lets me edit it out when I say something in a confusing way.
I think practicing talking extemporaneously on a subject is useful :)
Podcasts are WAY easier to organize than talks (no travel!) and now I have a fancy podcasting microphone.
I have this dream of making fun animated videos but that probably won't happen any time soon.
posts.key_insight: Podcasts offer a lower-friction alternative to conference talks for sharing technical knowledge, though they trade away the editing control that makes writing feel safer.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces she started a Canadian corporation to manage the business side of her increasingly successful zine sales. After Bite Size Linux sold 2,600 copies—far exceeding expectations—she needed proper infrastructure for taxes, expenses, and paying collaborators. The corporation makes it easier to hire illustrators, collaborate with other creators, and potentially expand into print shipping. She also introduced corporate pricing tiers for workplace use. The post ends with thoughtful open questions about the tension between selling creative work and keeping it accessible.
key_points: Unexpected commercial success of her zines (2,600 copies of Bite Size Linux) created enough financial complexity to justify incorporating
A corporation simplifies paying collaborators, hiring illustrators, expensing equipment, and separating business from personal finances
She introduced corporate pricing ($100/$500 tiers) for workplace use, with 47 companies purchasing so far
She raises genuine questions about whether selling zines changes incentives—toward general-interest topics, away from weird niche ones
She's wrestling with the philosophical tension between keeping work accessible and normalizing the expectation that creative work should be free
money_quotes: The only downside of selling things is – making money is complicated!
Reframing writing zines as a 'small side business' instead of just a 'fun thing' raises all kinds of interesting questions.
Does selling zines make it harder to make weird niche zines because there's an incentive to make things that are more general-interest?
Does giving away really good work for free normalize the idea that work 'should' be given away for free? Does it undermine people who sell their work?
$10 is a lot of money for many people (both in the US and outside the US). How do I keep them accessible?
Do people value work more if it's not free?
posts.key_insight: When a creative side project unexpectedly becomes commercially successful, the resulting business complexity is worth embracing because it enables better collaborations and higher-quality work—but it also forces hard questions about accessibility and incentives.

posts.summary: Evans breaks down the meta-skill of learning into four concrete, practicable sub-skills: identifying what you don't understand, having confidence in what you do know, asking good questions, and doing effective research. She argues that a growth mindset alone isn't sufficient—learning is itself a skill that requires deliberate practice. Drawing on her experience with self-directed math education, a pure math degree, and the Recurse Center, she shows how translating vague confusion into specific questions is the most critical skill. She reframes confusion as a positive signal that you're about to learn something new.
key_points: The most important learning skill is translating 'I'm confused' into specific, answerable questions—without this, you depend on others to structure your learning for you
Confidence in what you already know is just as important as identifying gaps—upgrading from 97% sure to 100% sure prevents wasted debugging time
Learning to learn is a skill developed through practice over years, from childhood math programs to PhD research to self-directed programming
Doing research effectively means knowing where information lives in your domain—man pages, books, mailing lists, source code—not just Googling
Confusion should be recognized as a positive signal that you're about to learn something, not a source of shame
money_quotes: Having a positive attitude is really important, but IMO by itself it's not enough to learn hard things. Learning is a skill which takes a lot of work to get better at.
This is the skill of translating 'I'm confused, I don't get it' to 'I have a specific question about X'.
There is a big difference between 'I'm 97% sure this is true' and 'I am 100% sure about this and I never need to question it again'.
I still find breaking down 'I'm confused about X' into specific questions about X takes work.

It seems a bit weird to put 'learn some math independently when you're 8' alongside 'do a PhD' but to me figuring out fractions on your own feels like the same kind of mental discipline that's useful as an adult researcher exploring new ideas.
Almost everything I do in my job day-to-day is something I learned on my own, and most of it is stuff that I learned recently (in the last 2-3 years).
If I'm confused, that's usually a good thing because it means I'm not stagnating.
posts.key_insight: Learning hard things requires the concrete skill of decomposing vague confusion into specific, answerable questions—a skill that itself improves with deliberate practice.

posts.summary: Julia Evans examines the economics of developer education, mapping out who actually pays for the learning resources that professional developers rely on. She identifies several categories: companies with products to sell (developer advocates), individuals investing in exposure (bloggers and speakers), recruiting-funded education (Recurse Center), companies and individuals selling education directly, and corporate training. She reflects on the fact that most of what she reads is free, funded either by people building their reputation or companies marketing products, and questions whether those incentive structures produce the best learning materials. The post is prompted partly by her own transition from giving everything away for free to selling her zines.
key_points: Developer education is funded through a surprisingly diverse set of economic models, from corporate developer advocacy to individual reputation-building to direct sales

Most free developer education exists because either the creator wants exposure/reputation or a company wants to market a product, and neither incentive is perfectly aligned with producing excellent learning materials
Blogging and speaking in tech is a long-term career investment that pays off through networking, job opportunities, and industry recognition
Self-publishing appears to be a more viable way to make money from teaching than traditional publishing, where authors may earn only around $23/hour
Corporate training is a logical model because companies will pay $2000/person for training that individuals wouldn't buy themselves
money_quotes: All of these things (tweets, blog posts, conference talks, etc) take time to make, and a lot of it is given away for free. So who pays for all of this work?
A lot of companies have realized that a good way to sell tech products is to explain the tech concepts behind their products to people in a way that they can actually understand.
Blogging / speaking in tech is a long-term investment in your future job opportunities and it can pay off!

As much as I ADORE all the bloggers I read I feel like it's kind of weird that I mostly learn from free sources, and the incentive structures there aren't that well aligned with producing really excellent learning materials.
It's a MUCH more reliable and useful and efficient source than reading Stack Overflow answers about Linux.
In 2018, it seems like a much more viable way to actually make money from teaching than traditional publishing.
posts.key_insight: Most free developer education is funded by misaligned incentives—reputation-building or product marketing—which raises the question of whether developers should invest more in paid learning materials that are directly incentivized to be excellent.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes a six-month intermittent bug where her blog would display raw HTML instead of rendered pages due to a missing Content-Type HTTP header. Despite extensive debugging attempts, she couldn't reliably reproduce the issue or determine whether her web host or Cloudflare's CDN was dropping the header. She ultimately solved it using Cloudflare Workers, a paid feature that lets you run custom JavaScript on every HTTP request, to detect and add the missing Content-Type header. She acknowledges it's a hack but finds it preferable to migrating web hosts.
key_points: Intermittent bugs that can't be reliably reproduced are extremely difficult to report and debug, especially when they involve infrastructure you don't fully control
Sometimes the pragmatic fix is a workaround rather than finding the root cause — Cloudflare Workers let her patch the symptom without understanding the underlying server configuration issue
Cloudflare Workers provide a powerful mechanism for modifying HTTP responses with custom JavaScript at the CDN edge, useful for fixing problems in infrastructure you can't directly control
Adding a custom debug header (x-julia-test) is a practical technique for verifying that your fix is actually being applied in production
money_quotes: People do not want to read raw HTML. I would like my pages to render!

I think debugging weird computer networking bugs is super fun, what I've been doing at work for the last while has been debugging computer networking bugs and so I'm not that motivated to do it at home too.
Even though I don't know *why* this is happening and I have no visibility into it, I can still try to fix it!
The most annoying thing about this issue is that I couldn't reliably reproduce it, so it seemed hard to report to my web host.
Running custom javascript on every single HTTP request is an extremely silly way to fix what is probably some kind of server configuration issue somewhere.
It's definitely a hack... But it helps me fix my problem until I decide to spend the time to migrate web hosts or whatever, so I'm happy with that.
Paying $60/year is definitely worth it to me to fix the problem & not have to spend the time to migrate to a different host right now.
posts.key_insight: When you can't find or fix the root cause of an intermittent infrastructure bug, a well-placed workaround that patches the symptom at the right layer can be the most practical solution.

posts.summary: Julia Evans highlights py-spy, a new Python profiler written in Rust by Ben Frederickson that takes a similar approach to her own rbspy project. She explains how py-spy can profile any running Python program using process_vm_readv to read memory, and praises its top-like default UI and pip-based installation. She notes that py-spy is likely more stable long-term than rbspy because it relies on Python's public header files rather than unstable internal interpreter structures. Evans expresses excitement about an emerging ecosystem of Rust-based sampling profilers that share code and could eventually cover many programming languages with consistent interfaces.
key_points: py-spy profiles any running Python program without modification by reading process memory with process_vm_readv, similar to rbspy's approach for Ruby

py-spy uses Python's public bindings (Python.h) rather than internal interpreter headers, making it significantly easier to maintain than rbspy which depends on 42 unstable Ruby binding files versus py-spy's 6
The tool is installable via pip despite being a Rust program, meeting Python developers where they are
rbspy and py-spy share code through extracted crates like proc-maps, enabling an ecosystem of similar profilers for other languages
Evans envisions a suite of open source sampling profilers for many languages with consistent user interfaces, noting phpspy already exists for PHP
money_quotes: It can profile any running Python program, it uses process_vm_readv to read memory, and it by default displays profiling information in a really easy-to-use way.
Even though py-spy is a Rust program, obviously Python programmers are used to installing software with pip and not cargo.
Since rbspy depends on unstable internal Ruby interfaces, even though it works relatively well today, future versions of Ruby could break it at any time.
If someone wants to make a py-spy/rbspy-like profiler in Rust for another language like Perl or Javascript or something, it's even easier!
I have this secret dream that we could eventually have a suite of open source profilers for lots of different programming languages that all have similar user interfaces.
Today every single profiling tool is different and it's a pain.
posts.key_insight: Rust-based sampling profilers like py-spy and rbspy are converging on a shared ecosystem of code and consistent interfaces that could make profiling accessible across many programming languages.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post sharing Julia Evans' talk 'Build Impossible Programs' from the Deconstruct 2018 conference. The talk draws on her experience building a Ruby profiler (rbspy) and focuses on debunking myths about doing ambitious programming work. Unlike her previous technical deep-dive on the same project, this talk takes a 'squishier' approach examining beliefs that hold people back from attempting difficult projects. She also mentions her hand-drawn slide style and notes that 2300 zines were printed and distributed at the conference.
key_points: The talk challenges myths people believe about doing ambitious programming work, arguing many of those myths turn out not to be true
Building a Ruby profiler served as the concrete example for exploring what makes 'impossible' projects achievable
Evans distinguishes between technical deep-dives and 'squishier' talks about mindset and approach to ambitious work
Hand-drawing slides is described as easier, faster, and more fun than traditional slide tools
money_quotes: This one is a squishier talk about myths I believed about doing ambitious work and how a lot of those myths turn out not to be true.
As usual these days I drew the slides by hand. It's way easier/faster, and it's more fun.
One extremely awesome thing that happened at Deconstruct was that Gary agreed to print 2300 zines to give away to folks at the conference.
posts.key_insight: The myths we believe about what makes programming projects 'impossible' often turn out not to be true, and ambitious work is more accessible than we assume.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine 'Help! I have a manager!' which covers how to work effectively with your manager. The zine focuses on communication skills she wishes she'd known at the start of her career. She emphasizes that even with a great manager, there are proactive steps you can take to improve the working relationship. The zine covers practical topics like what to discuss in 1:1 meetings and how to ask for feedback.
key_points: Working well with a manager requires proactive effort even when the manager is great
Clear communication is the foundation of a good manager-employee relationship
Knowing what to talk about in 1:1s and how to ask for feedback are key skills that aren't taught explicitly
money_quotes: This zine is everything I wish somebody had told me when I started out in my career and had no idea how I was supposed to work with my manager.
Even when I have a great manager, there are still a lot of things I can do to make sure that we work well together, mostly around communicating clearly!
posts.key_insight: Working effectively with your manager is a learnable skill built on clear communication, not something that just happens naturally even with a great manager.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains her decision to switch from giving away her programming zines for free to selling them for $10 each. She weighs the tradeoff between free distribution (which maximizes reach) and paid distribution (which funds higher quality work and signals seriousness). Using her own data, she argues that 3,000 paid sales may result in more actual readers than 5,000 free downloads, since buyers are more likely to read what they paid for. She discusses corporate pricing experiments, the advantages of posting drafts on Twitter as a free complement, and various friction points she's encountered with selling digital products.
key_points: Free distribution maximizes reach, but paid distribution funds investment in quality and sustains motivation to do the work well
3,000 sales may represent more actual readers than 5,000 free downloads because buyers are far more likely to engage with what they paid for
Charging money signals quality and seriousness — her zine became a required university text and companies buy them for employees
She prices at $10 for individuals as a 'reasonable/fair' price rather than tying to business outcomes, while experimenting with higher corporate pricing
Posting draft pages on Twitter serves as both free access for those who can't pay and as feedback/marketing for the paid product
money_quotes: If you decide to sell your work instead of giving it away for free, you don't need to justify that.
I think 'this costs money' is a nice way to signal 'I actually spent time on this, this is good'.
Someone who bought a zine is probably like 4x more likely to read it than someone who just clicked on a PDF.
The fact that I'm making money from it makes it way easier to spend time on the project.
If I could figure out how to charge more, I could share that with other people and make a world where smaller creators could be more successful.
I worry a lot about making mistakes when selling things to folks, but once I write down the issues so far they all feel very resolvable.
$10/zine is not actually a lot of money, it's only sustainable for julia because julia has a big audience.
posts.key_insight: Selling creative educational work rather than giving it away can actually increase genuine readership while funding higher quality output, because people engage more seriously with things they pay for.

posts.summary: Evans catalogs a wide range of possible career goals for software developers, organized into categories like technical, job, entrepreneurship, product, people/leadership, communication, and work environment. The list was crowdsourced from Twitter and intentionally mixes big and small goals pointing in very different directions. She emphasizes that no one can tell you what your goals should be, and that achieving a goal doesn't always feel as satisfying as expected. The post was inspired by the idea that company career ladders don't necessarily align with personal goals, and encourages readers to reflect on which milestones would actually feel meaningful to them.
key_points: Career goals extend far beyond promotions and titles — they span technical mastery, product impact, people leadership, communication, entrepreneurship, and work environment preferences
Your company's career ladder is not the same as your personal goals, and chasing promotions may not be the best path to fulfillment
Achieving a goal doesn't always feel satisfying — sometimes unexpected accomplishments feel more meaningful than planned ones
Writing down possible goals and honestly evaluating which ones matter to you is a useful exercise for career planning
money_quotes: This list intentionally has big goals and small goals, and goals in very different directions. It definitely does not attempt to tell you what sorts of goals you should have.
remain as curious and in love with programming as the first time I did it
be a blessing to others you wished someone else was to you

Sometimes I will achieve a new goal and find that it doesn't feel very satisfying to have done it. And other times I will do something that I didn't think was a huge deal to me, but feel really proud of it afterwards.
your company's career ladder is probably not the same as your goals and chasing the next promotion may not be the best way to achieve them
do I really want to work at FANCY_COMPANY? would that feel good? do I care about working at a nonprofit? do I want to learn how to build software products that lots of people use?
posts.key_insight: Writing down a broad inventory of possible career goals — then honestly asking which ones would actually feel meaningful — is more useful than defaulting to whatever your company's career ladder rewards.

posts.summary: This post introduces Envoy, a network proxy in the same space as HAProxy and nginx, focusing on practical configuration basics. Evans walks through a minimal working example, explaining Envoy's core concepts: listeners (bind to ports), filters (process requests), routes (match and direct traffic), and clusters (backend server groups). She then covers Envoy's distinctive feature of dynamic configuration via discovery services (CDS, EDS, RDS, LDS), which allow centralized control of network routing without config file restarts. The post concludes that discovery services are what make Envoy genuinely exciting, enabling circuit breaking, traffic shaping, and encrypted communication without application code changes, while acknowledging the significant operational complexity involved.
key_points: Envoy's core configuration model consists of four concepts: listeners (ports), filters (request processing), routes (traffic matching), and clusters (backend groups)
Unlike HAProxy or nginx, Envoy can be configured dynamically via discovery services (XDS protocol) rather than static config files, enabling runtime changes to routing, backends, and listeners
The service mesh pattern puts Envoy on every machine to proxy all network traffic, giving centralized control over how applications communicate

Envoy's HTTP timeout and retry headers let you control retry/timeout behavior without implementing it in application code, which Evans considers particularly valuable since people get timeout logic wrong constantly
Envoy is both very powerful and very complex, similar to Kubernetes, and the operational overhead of discovery services may not be appropriate for most use cases
money_quotes: Even after using Envoy for a year this basic configuration actually took me 45 minutes to get right.
One thing that makes Envoy really different from HAProxy or nginx is that Envoy often isn't configured with a configuration file.
I think these discovery services are really the exciting thing about Envoy.
Implementing timeout / retry logic correctly works differently in every programming language and people get it wrong ALL THE TIME.
Like Kubernetes, it's both very powerful and very complicated.

Obviously setting up a bunch of discovery services and operating them and using them to configure your internal network infrastructure in complicated ways is a lot more work than just 'write an nginx configuration file and leave it alone'.
Basically it's a very powerful/flexible decentralized load balancer.
posts.key_insight: Envoy's real power over traditional proxies like nginx and HAProxy lies in its dynamic configuration via discovery services, which enable centralized control of distributed network infrastructure without application code changes, though this power comes with significant operational complexity.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces a new zine collaboration with Katie Sylor-Miller called 'Oh shit, Git!' that helps people fix common git mistakes. The zine was inspired by Katie's website ohshitgit.com, which explains how to recover from git mishaps. About 50% of the content is new, including fundamental explanations of how git works internally. Julia reflects on the challenge of writing about git because she had forgotten what it felt like to be confused by it, and notes this is her fifth zine of 2018.
key_points: The zine was a collaboration born from Katie Sylor-Miller's ohshitgit.com website, making it Julia's first collaborative zine project
About half the zine is new material including fundamentals about how git commits and branches work, because effective git usage requires understanding the internals
Julia struggled to write about git on her own because she'd forgotten what it was like to be confused by it, highlighting how expertise can make it harder to teach beginners
The zine format serves as a paper reference for common git recovery scenarios that feel scary to less experienced users
Julia produced five zines in 2018 and acknowledges a tradeoff between zine-writing and blogging
money_quotes: I was in this weird place with git where, even though I know that git is really confusing, I felt like I'd forgotten what it was like to be confused/scared by Git.
I write most things from a place of 'I was super confused by this thing just recently, let me explain it!!'
To really work with Git effectively you need to understand at least a little bit about how commits and branches work!
A lot of the things on that site ('oh shit, i committed to the wrong branch!') are things I remember being really scary when I was less comfortable with git!
There's a definite tradeoff between writing zines and blogging, and writing blog posts is really fun.
posts.key_insight: Expertise can paradoxically make it harder to teach a topic because you forget what it was like to be confused, and collaboration with someone who remembers that confusion can bridge the gap.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the question of when teaching with comics is most effective, pushing back on simplistic 'learning styles' explanations while acknowledging that learning preferences still matter for self-directed learners. She identifies four specific situations where comics excel: creating focused architectural diagrams that highlight key decisions rather than exhaustively mapping components, illustrating real-world scenarios from complex systems, summarizing structured lists like command-line arguments, and explaining simple ideas where mixing text and small diagrams aids comprehension. Her central insight is that good teaching diagrams should be opinionated, emphasizing the most important architectural decisions rather than trying to be comprehensive.
key_points: Learning styles as a framework lacks strong evidence, but learning preferences still matter because self-directed learners simply won't engage with formats they dislike
Effective diagrams should highlight key architectural decisions rather than exhaustively mapping all components and their connections
The 'scenes from' approach of zooming into specific real-world scenarios is more effective than starting with generalities when teaching complex topics
Comics work well for structured lists because they make reference material colorful and visually appealing while remaining concise
Mixing text and small diagrams is a natural strength of comics for explaining concepts like pipes that benefit from visual representation
money_quotes: I don't teach in a classroom, I teach whoever feels like reading what I'm writing on the internet! And if people don't feel like learning the things I'm teaching because of the way they're presented, they won't!
Diagrams like this often don't highlight the most important/unusual architectural decisions!

Figure out what the key architecture decision(s) are that folks need to understand to use it, draw a diagram that illustrates those architecture decisions, leave out parts that aren't key to understanding the architecture.
Often when trying to explain a huge topic, people start with generalities. I have another approach that I prefer, which I think of as the 'scenes from' approach, or 'get specific!'

A really common situation when using a linearizable distributed system is that it'll periodically become unavailable due to a leader election. I didn't know that that was common when I started working with distributed systems!!

It literally includes every grep command line argument I've ever used, as well as a few I haven't but that I think seem useful. And I've been using grep for 15 years! I think it's amazing that it's possible to usefully summarize grep in such a small space.
posts.key_insight: The best teaching diagrams and comics are opinionated — they highlight the few key architectural decisions or scenarios that matter most, rather than trying to comprehensively map every component.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her experience using Tailwind CSS to build a website for her zines, despite having self-described mediocre CSS skills. She contrasts the old CSS Zen Garden philosophy of semantic HTML with Tailwind's utility-class approach, where you add many small CSS classes directly to HTML elements instead of writing custom CSS. She finds Tailwind's approach to media queries particularly useful, as prefixes like 'md:' make responsive design trivially easy. The post argues that this utility-first approach works well because HTML templates reduce duplication, and the constrained set of options actually helps non-experts make better design decisions.
key_points: The CSS Zen Garden ideal of semantic HTML styled by separate CSS doesn't work well for people who only write CSS occasionally
Tailwind's utility classes differ from inline styles because they provide media query prefixes and a constrained set of design options
Having limited choices (30 width options, a fixed color palette) paradoxically makes it easier to produce good-looking results
Modern HTML generation via templates reduces the duplication concern, since absurd class strings only need to be written once
The approach is utility-first, not utility-only — Tailwind doesn't require you to never write CSS
money_quotes: I've been writing mediocre HTML/CSS for probably like 12 years now, and since I don't do it at all in my job and am making no efforts to improve, the chances of my mediocre CSS skills magically improving are… not good.
In practice I always end up with some media query problem sadly googling 'how do I center div' for the 500th time.
It's basically the opposite of the CSS zen garden – you have a single CSS file that you don't change, and then you use 10 billion classes in your HTML to style your site.
It feels incredibly freeing to not have to give any of my divs styles or IDs at all and just focus on thinking about how they should look.
With normal CSS, I can make any element any width I want. For me, this is not a good thing!
No more 'width: 300px; /* i hope this looks okay i don't know help */'
I have no position on the One True Way to write (or not write) CSS. I'm not a frontend developer and you definitely should not take advice from me.
posts.key_insight: Constrained utility-class CSS frameworks like Tailwind can be more effective than traditional CSS for occasional web developers because limited options and inline-style composition eliminate the cognitive overhead of naming classes and managing stylesheets.

posts.summary: Evans discusses the significant investment required to run new software in production safely, particularly at high availability targets like 99.99% uptime. She presents a practical playbook: start with non-critical use, let it bake, run into problems, and fix them iteratively. Using Envoy and Kubernetes as concrete examples, she catalogs the kinds of issues teams encounter—mostly configuration surprises rather than bugs—and argues for treating each incident as a one-time learning opportunity. She emphasizes categorizing components into 'ok to break' and 'not ok to break' lists to focus attention where it matters most.
key_points: At 99.99% SLO, a single serious incident discovered in production can consume half your annual downtime budget, making pre-understanding critical
Most issues with new software aren't bugs but configuration surprises where defaults don't match expectations, and figuring out correct configuration takes significant time
The playbook is iterative: deploy to non-critical systems, encounter problems, fix them thoroughly so each incident only happens once
Categorizing failure modes into 'ok to break' vs 'not ok' helps teams focus their attention on what actually matters for production reliability
Knowledge-sharing about incidents and failure modes across teams prevents the same incident from recurring through different people
money_quotes: That's 45 minutes / year of downtime, and if you find out about a serious issue for the first time in production it could easily take you 20 minutes or to revert the change. That's half your uptime budget for the year!
Just because something is great software doesn't mean you won't also run into 10 or 20 relatively serious issues along the way that need to be addressed in one way or another.
My view is that running new software in production inevitably results in incidents.
My experience so far has been that it's actually relatively possible to pull off 'have every incident only once'.
A lot of these aren't bugs – they're just cases where what we expected the default configuration to do one thing, and it did another thing.

Figuring out all the failure modes of a new piece of software and how they apply to your situation can take months. (this is why when you ask your database team 'hey can we just use NEW DATABASE' they look at you in such a pained way).

Understanding which parts are OK to break and which aren't lets me focus: I can put big boxes around some components and decide 'ok, if this breaks it doesn't matter, so I won't pay super close attention to it'.
posts.key_insight: Running new software in production inevitably causes incidents, so the strategy is to encounter them in non-critical contexts first, ensure each incident only happens once through thorough investigation, and categorize failure modes to focus attention where it matters.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains how she came to understand RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) through working with Envoy's C++ codebase. She walks through a concrete example: Envoy's circuit breakers need to track open TCP connection counts accurately, and destructors solve this by automatically decrementing the counter when a connection object is destroyed. The post demonstrates how tying resource cleanup to object lifetime prevents bugs that would arise from manually tracking cleanup across many code paths. She notes this pattern works because C++ guarantees destructors run when objects leave scope, are removed from collections, or when exceptions occur.
key_points: Envoy uses circuit breakers to limit TCP connections, requiring an accurate count of open connections at all times
By incrementing a counter in the constructor and decrementing in the destructor, cleanup happens automatically regardless of how a connection is closed
RAII provides prompt, deterministic, and hard-to-forget resource cleanup — unlike garbage collected languages where finalization timing is unpredictable
The pattern extends beyond obvious resources like memory and file descriptors to subtler cases like decrementing counters
Python context managers offer a related but more limited approach to deterministic cleanup
money_quotes: there are 10 million things to know about C++ and that is not what we're doing today, we are just talking about one thing.

Connections can be destroyed in a lot of ways (they can time out! they can be closed by Envoy! they can be closed by the server! maybe something else I haven't thought of could happen!) and it seems very easy to accidentally miss a way of closing them.

The beauty of this is that now you don't need to make sure that the counter gets decremented in all the right places, you now just need to organize your code so that the ActiveClient object's destructor gets called when the connection has closed.

And there's no chance of forgetting to decrement the counter!! It will definitely always happen unless you accidentally leave the object on one of these lists, which would be a bug anyway because the connection is closed.

Previously I knew about using this pattern for kind of obvious things (make sure all the memory gets freed in the destructor, or make sure file descriptors get closed). But I didn't realize it was also useful for cases that are slightly less obviously a resource like 'decrement a counter'.

In a garbage collected language you can often set up destructors to be run when the object is GC'd. But often you want things to be cleaned up right away when the object is no longer in use, not some indeterminate period later whenever GC happens to run.
posts.key_insight: RAII isn't just for freeing memory — tying any cleanup action to object lifetime via destructors makes resource management automatic and nearly impossible to forget, even for non-obvious resources like reference counters.

posts.summary: Julia Evans addresses the frequent requests she gets to create comics for open source projects, and instead offers a practical guide for anyone wanting to document tech projects with comics. She clarifies that a zine is a printed booklet, not a single drawing, and emphasizes that the information matters far more than the art. She outlines three formats—a single graphic, a series of comics, or a printed zine—and explains when each is appropriate. She also covers three approaches to hiring: finding a tech-savvy illustrator, collaborating with an illustrator while providing clear explanations, or drawing it yourself with stick figures. The core message is that most people underinvest in the explanation and overinvest in the art.
key_points: Comics without clear information to convey are useless—figuring out what to say is 80% of the work
Comics take roughly 5x longer than writing to convey the same information, so focus on core concepts that won't change
Most people who collaborate with illustrators underinvest in writing clear technical explanations, producing adorable but unhelpful results
There are three viable formats: a single graphic distilling a project to ~50 words, a series of targeted comics for a specific audience, or a printed zine for in-person distribution
A zine is a printed booklet, not a single drawing—an individual drawing is just a drawing
money_quotes: You should think about comics the same way as any kind of documentation – start with the information you want to convey, who your target audience is, and how you want to distribute it, and figure out how to illustrate it after.
The information is the main thing, not the art!
Figuring out what information would be useful to tell people is very hard!! It's 80% of what I spend my time on when making comics.
Drawing comics is a much bigger investment than writing documentation (it takes me like 5x longer to convey the same information in a comic than in writing). So use it wisely!
What's the point of having an adorable illustration if it doesn't teach anyone anything?
Most folks underinvest in writing clear explanations for their illustrators.
You wouldn't call a photo of a model in Vogue a magazine! The magazine has like a million pages!
posts.key_insight: Tech comics succeed or fail based on the clarity of the information they convey, not the quality of the art—most people underinvest in the explanation and overinvest in the illustration.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her KubeCon talk about making major infrastructure changes while maintaining high availability (99.99%+ uptime), drawing on her experience with Kubernetes and Envoy at Stripe. She provides a toolbox for derisking migrations and making infrastructure changes with confidence. She advocates for ignoring most Kubernetes ecosystem software to focus on core components, and notes that many companies are still figuring out how to use this new software reliably. The talk represents a personal milestone as her first work talk she's genuinely happy with, reflecting two years of learning how to make big infrastructure changes safely.
key_points: High availability infrastructure migrations are possible if you build a systematic toolbox for derisking changes
Her team ignores almost all Kubernetes ecosystem software to focus on core pieces like Kubernetes, Envoy, and a few small tools
The Kubernetes community creates pressure to adopt Cool New Things, but it's okay to stay focused on the basics
Stripe developed skycfg, a Starlark-based tool for configuring Kubernetes with functions instead of YAML
Learning to make big infrastructure changes safely is a multi-year process that she's still working through
money_quotes: For companies with high availability requirements (99.99% uptime or higher), running new software in production comes with a lot of risks.
On my team we ignore almost all software in the Kubernetes ecosystem so that we can focus on a few core pieces.
I think often in Kubernetes land it can seem like everyone is using Cool New Things (helm! istio! knative! eep!).
I find it much simpler to stay focused on the basics and I wanted people to know that it's okay to do that if that's what works for your company.
I think the reality is that actually a lot of folks are still trying to work out how to use this new software in a reliable and secure way.
Figuring out how to make big infrastructure changes safely took me a long time (and I'm not done!), and so I hope this talk helps other folks do the same thing.
posts.key_insight: When operating high-reliability infrastructure, deliberately ignoring most of the ecosystem to focus on a few core, well-understood components is a valid and often superior strategy to chasing every new tool.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her 2018 side projects and professional growth. She built rbspy, a Ruby profiler that inspired similar tools for Python and PHP. She launched a surprisingly successful zine business at wizardzines.com that could have sustained her financially. She shifted her conference talks from 'cool tools' to substantive work topics, gave her first podcasts, and wrote 54 blog posts. She also describes small but meaningful workplace inclusion projects and her ongoing struggle to define what a senior engineer's job actually is.
key_points: rbspy inspired py-spy and phpspy, demonstrating how one innovative tool can seed an ecosystem of better developer tools
Her zine business generated enough revenue to live on, challenging her assumption that teaching couldn't compete financially with software development
She shifted from giving 'here are cool tools' talks to talks about her actual work, finding it harder but more valuable
She learned that sales and marketing don't have to be gross — it's possible to run a business by being honest and building good things
Small inclusion projects at work, like writing an unofficial guide to performance reviews, can have outsized impact relative to time invested
money_quotes: Writing a program that does something innovative and inspiring other people to make amazing new tools is something I'm really proud of.
I always thought that it was impossible to make anywhere near as much money teaching people useful things as I can as a software developer, and now I think that's not true.

I used to think that sales / marketing had to be gross, but reading some of these business books made me think that it's actually possible to run a business by being honest & just building good things.
Previously when I gave talks about my work I think I fell into the trap of just describing what we do ('we do X Y Z' … 'okay, so what?').
I struggled a bit with understanding/defining my job this year. I wrote 'What's a senior engineer's job?' about that, but I have not yet reached enlightenment.

I like that I work at a place where it's possible/encouraged to do projects like this. I spend a relatively small amount of time on them but it feels good to be able to make tiny steps towards building a better workplace from time to time.
posts.key_insight: Building innovative tools that inspire others, teaching through honest business practices, and making small workplace improvements are all deeply satisfying forms of impact that compound over time.

posts.summary: Julia Evans works through the early chapters of 'All of Nonparametric Statistics' by Larry Wasserman, trying to build intuition for nonparametric methods and the bootstrap. She explores how nonparametric statistics avoids strong distributional assumptions, walks through Hoeffding's inequality to show how confidence scales with dataset size, and attempts to understand the formal conditions under which bootstrap estimators are consistent. She finds that the math behind bootstrap consistency is surprisingly difficult and that even the textbook doesn't fully prove general consistency. The post is explicitly a work-in-progress learning journal rather than a polished explanation.
key_points: Nonparametric statistics makes minimal assumptions about the underlying distribution, though it still requires independence and some smoothness conditions
Hoeffding's inequality shows that confidence in empirical estimates scales exponentially with dataset size — 100,000 points is not 10x better than 10,000, it's astronomically better
The bootstrap method estimates statistics and their variance by resampling with replacement, but requires regularity conditions to be valid
Understanding when and why bootstrap estimators are consistent turns out to be mathematically very hard, even for simple cases like the median
The textbook warns against treating the bootstrap as a panacea — it should not be applied blindly
money_quotes: since these notes are from reading a math book and math books are extremely dense this is basically going to be 'I read 7 pages of this math book and here are some points I'm confused about'
100,000 data points is AMAZING, 10,000 data points is pretty good, and 1,000 is much less useful.
So 100,000 data points isn't 10x better than 10,000 data points, it's actually like 10000000000000x better.
If I'm estimating some parameter (like the median), I would DEFINITELY LIKE IT TO BE TRUE that if I do it with an infinite amount of data then my estimate works.
An estimator that is not consistent does not sound very useful!
there is a tendency to treat the bootstrap as a panacea for all problems. But the bootstrap requires regularity conditions to yield valid answers. It should not be applied blindly.
understanding when/why nonparametric bootstrap estimators are consistent in general might be very hard
posts.key_insight: The guarantees of nonparametric methods like the bootstrap rest on surprisingly deep mathematics, and having more data points provides exponentially — not linearly — better statistical confidence.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her early thinking about marketing for her zines business, exploring how to promote her work in a way that feels authentic and not annoying. She examines common internet marketing techniques like mailing lists, social proof, and advertising, evaluating each against her personal comfort level. Her current approach relies heavily on Twitter, where she posts free comics that naturally attract followers who then buy her zines. She considers whether to invest in email marketing and conversion tracking but remains skeptical of aggressive optimization, preferring to focus on making good things and letting people know where to find them.
key_points: Good marketing is fundamentally about clearly explaining what something is and why it's good, then letting people decide
Twitter drives roughly 70% of her zine sales through a natural cycle: post free comics, gain followers, announce paid zines
She's skeptical of conversion rate optimization, arguing it creates a rabbit hole that distracts from making great work
The key marketing insight she discovered is that people actually want to buy good things — you just need to tell them where
She prefers marketing approaches that balance discoverability with not being annoying, rejecting aggressive popups and social proof tactics
money_quotes: So basically as far as I can tell good marketing is just explaining what the thing is and why it is good in a clear way.
I think a marketing secret is 'people actually want to buy things that are good, it is useful to tell people where they can buy things that are good'.

Doing a bunch of work to track conversion rates feels bad to me: it seems like it would be really easy to go down a dumb rabbit hole of 'oh, let's try to increase conversion by 5%' instead of just focusing on making really good and cool things.
Obviously the most important thing in all of this is to make cool things that are useful to people, but I think cool useful writing does not actually sell itself!
Maybe marketing is all about 'make your things discoverable without being annoying'?
I don't try to hack/optimize this really: I just post comics when I make them and I try to make them good.
Whenever people refer to my writing as 'content' I get grumpy.
posts.key_insight: The best marketing strategy for independent creators is making genuinely good work, sharing it freely, and simply telling people where they can buy more — no aggressive optimization needed.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares work-in-progress comics from her upcoming zine about Linux networking tools. She explains that while she loves networking, getting started with the tools is tricky because their man pages are enormous and the most useful tricks are often learned from coworkers. The zine aims to be that helpful coworker who shares practical tips about tools like curl, ssh, openssl, netcat, nmap, and ethtool. She advocates mixing basic and advanced tools so beginners learn what exists.
key_points: Most networking tool knowledge is passed informally between coworkers rather than learned from documentation
Man pages for tools like openssl contain 60,000+ words but you only need about 5 command line options for 90% of tasks
Mixing basic tools (ssh) with advanced ones (tc) helps beginners discover what's available even if they don't need it yet
Practical examples like curl --resolve solve real problems that are hard to discover on your own
money_quotes: So the goal with this zine is basically to be "your very helpful coworker who gives you tips about how to use networking tools" in case you don't have that person.
A lot of these tools have VERY LONG man pages and you only usually need to know like 5 command line options to do 90% of what you want to do.
I only ever do maybe 4 things with openssl even though the openssl man pages together have more than 60,000 words.
I know how to use curl --resolve because my coworker told me how.

I'm a big fan of mixing more advanced things (like tc) with basic things (like ssh) because then even if you're learning the basic things for the first time, you can learn that the advanced thing exists!
posts.key_insight: The most useful networking tool knowledge is typically passed between coworkers informally, not found in man pages, which is exactly the gap a good zine can fill.

posts.summary: Julia Evans promotes !!Con 2019, a two-day conference in NYC celebrating the joy, excitement, and surprise of computing. She explains the conference's inclusive philosophy, including open captioning, paid speakers, anonymous talk review, and active diversity outreach. The post encourages submissions by showcasing past talk titles that range from Islamic geometry to making mushrooms glow. New this year, non-traditional formats like plays and songs are welcome. She emphasizes that anonymous review helps surface great first-time speakers and that detailed outlines matter more than reputation.
key_points: !!Con is built around celebrating what's joyful and surprising about computing, not just what's practical or career-advancing
Anonymous talk review levels the playing field between first-time speakers and well-known figures, and many of the best talks come from unknown speakers
The conference invests heavily in inclusion through open captioning, paid speakers, travel funding, and active outreach for diverse speakers
This year expands the format to include non-talk performances like plays and songs
The pay-what-you-can model makes the conference accessible but depends on corporate sponsorship to sustain
money_quotes: Computers are AMAZING. You can make programs that seem like magic, computer science has all kind of fun and surprising tidbits, there are all kinds of ways to make really cool art with computers.
The only rule of !!Con talks is that the talk has to have an exclamation mark in the title :)
We originally considered calling !!Con ExclamationMarkCon but that was too unwieldy so we went with !!Con :).
Many of our best talks are from first time speakers or people who I'd never heard of before, and I think anonymous review makes it easier to find great people who aren't well known.
We can't rely on someone's reputation to determine if they'll give a good talk, but we do need a way to see that people have a plan for how to present their material in an engaging way.
If you can't afford a $300 conference ticket, we're the conference for you!
posts.key_insight: Anonymous review and a focus on joy over prestige creates a conference where the best talks come from unexpected people sharing genuine excitement about computing.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes reorganizing her blog's front page from a chronological list into 28 categories, after six years and 390 posts. She traces the evolution from a standard 'show 5 recent posts' layout to a full chronological list inspired by Dan Luu, and finally to categorized sections. Her method involved dumping all post titles into a text file and using a Marie Kondo-style approach to discover natural groupings, then implementing them as a Hugo taxonomy. She argues that while reverse chronology works well for years, adding structure eventually becomes necessary for readers to navigate a large body of work.
key_points: Showing only recent posts makes it impossible for readers to understand the breadth of a blogger's work or find older posts
A chronological list of all posts works better but breaks down at scale — 390 posts and 360,000 words is too much to scan
Discovering categories emergently by organizing all titles in a single text file worked far better than trying to define categories in advance
Each post gets exactly one category, accepting imperfect classification as a worthwhile tradeoff for simplicity
Personal websites allow this kind of iterative evolution in a way platform-hosted blogs don't
money_quotes: it's SO HARD to get an overall sense for the body of a person's work by reading 1 blog post at a time
This is objectively a lot of writing and I would like people new to the blog to be able to navigate it and actually have some idea what's going on.
it's totally obvious to me what other posts that post is related to, but obviously to a new person it's not at all clear what the trains of thought are in this blog
I knew immediately that coming up with categories in advance would be impossible (how was I supposed to know that 'fan posts about awesome work by other people' was a substantial category?)
Seeing everything in one place (a la marie kondo) really helped me see the patterns and figure out what some categories were.
I think it was nice for the first 6 years or whatever to be able to just write things that I think are cool without thinking about where they 'fit'.
today, 360,000 words in, I think it makes sense to add a little more structure
what I wanted in 2013 is not the same as 2016 is not the same as 2019! This is okay!
posts.key_insight: Blog organization should evolve with the size of the archive — reverse chronology works early on, but at scale, emergent categorization helps readers understand the trains of thought behind a body of work.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her third 'Bite Size' zine focused on networking, explaining that it covers practical skills like inspecting SSL certificates, making DNS queries, and capturing network traffic. She frames the zine as the resource she wished she had when she started doing networking work, noting that these tasks aren't inherently hard but are obscured by unhelpful man pages. She also reflects on the positive experience of switching from free to paid zines, discusses wanting better feedback from readers, and teases potential future topics.
key_points: Networking tools like tcpdump aren't inherently difficult, but their man pages fail to distinguish essential options from obscure ones, making them intimidating
The zine targets people who need to debug networking problems (SSL errors, DNS updates, firewall issues) even though networking isn't their full-time job
Switching from free to paid zines has been entirely positive: better sales, people take the work more seriously, and quality has improved
She wants more specific, actionable feedback beyond general praise to improve future zines
Practice is the key differentiator in networking skill — spending time debugging weird networking things is how you get better
money_quotes: As usual this zine is the resource I wish I had 4 years ago.
As often happens with computers none of them are really that hard!!

The man pages for the tools you need to do these things are Very Long and as usual don't differentiate between 'everybody always uses this option and you 10000% need to know it' and 'you will never use this option it does not matter'.
So I spent a long time staring sadly at the tcpdump man page.
It's Thursday afternoon and your users are reporting SSL errors in production and you don't know why.
When you practice something you get better at it :)
Sales have been really good, people take the work more seriously, I can spend more time on them, and I think the quality has gone up.

When I made the switch about a year ago from 'every zine I release is free' to 'the old zines are free but all the new ones are not free' it felt scary! It's been startlingly totally fine and a very positive thing.
posts.key_insight: Networking tools aren't inherently hard — the real barrier is that documentation doesn't distinguish the essential from the obscure, making practical knowledge feel more inaccessible than it actually is.

posts.summary: Evans explains why monoidal (tensor) categories are interesting, focusing on how they let you prove and define mathematical concepts using pictures/diagrams. She walks through examples from the paper 'Seven Sketches in Compositionality,' showing how inequalities can be proved by arranging boxes and lines in diagrams, and how the trace of a matrix can be defined by simply drawing a circle around a function. She connects this to her own master's thesis on topological quantum computing and acknowledges she's not sure it relates to programming, though others have drawn connections to software design.
key_points: Tensor categories formalize the idea of proving things with pictures by giving specific meaning to spatial arrangements in diagrams — stacking means tensor product, loops mean duality
The trace of a matrix can be elegantly defined by drawing a circle around a function in a string diagram, which is more intuitive than 'sum up the numbers on the diagonal'
String diagrams differ from regular category theory diagrams: lines are objects, boxes are functions, adjacency means tensor product, and half circles mean duality
This is explicitly not about monads or Haskell — it's about visual, compositional reasoning in mathematics
Evans connects this to her master's thesis on topological quantum computing and notes others have related these diagrammatic methods to software design
