money_quotes: The first time I saw something like this in a math class I felt like – what? what is happening? you can't just draw PICTURES to prove things?!!

This is a really outrageous thing! We've said, hey, we have a function and we want to get a number in return right? Okay, let's just… draw a circle around it so that there are no lines left coming out of it, and then that will be a number!
That seems a lot more natural and prettier than the usual way of defining the trace of a matrix ('sum up the numbers on the diagonal')!
When I first saw this I thought it was super cool that just drawing a circle is actually a legitimate way of defining a mathematical concept!
Even though this is usually a programming blog I don't know whether this particular thing really has anything to do with programming, I just remembered I thought it was cool.
I thought it was really cool that you could formally define / prove things with pictures. And useful things, like the trace of a matrix!
posts.key_insight: Tensor categories formalize the intuition that you can prove and define mathematical concepts through pictures, where spatial arrangement in diagrams carries precise algebraic meaning.

posts.summary: Julia Evans aggregates community wisdom on debugging into a practical framework after realizing there aren't many good debugging guides. She breaks down the process into reproducible steps: reproduce the bug, reproduce it quickly, check your assumptions, and experiment methodically. The post emphasizes that good error handling and informative error messages are as important as debugging technique itself. She highlights creative debugging approaches like using sounds and LEDs when traditional tools aren't available. The post acknowledges that debugging skill is hard to teach because the hardest parts vary by experience level, and she's still trying to understand where newer programmers actually get stuck.
key_points: Debugging follows a loop: make a guess about what's happening, design an experiment to check it, repeat until you understand the system
Most bugs are in your own code, not in libraries or infrastructure, and accepting this quickly saves time
Writing code that produces clear, chained error messages (not silent failures) is a form of pre-debugging that pays off enormously
Checking assumptions is critical because many bugs stem from things you were sure were true but aren't, like editing the wrong file or misunderstanding which server handles a request
The skill of interpreting error messages is domain-specific and non-transferable, making it a recurring challenge even for experienced programmers in new areas
money_quotes: A lot of debugging is realizing that something you were sure was true is actually… not true.
In general between an established library and my code that I wrote last month, usually it's my code that I wrote last month that's the problem.
The point here is that information is the most important thing and you need to do whatever's necessary to get information.
Debuggable code isn't necessarily clean, and code that's littered with checks or error handling rarely makes for pleasant reading.
One interpretation of 'easy to debug' is 'every single time there's an error, the program reports to you exactly what happened in an easy to understand way'.
The skill of understanding what error messages mean is often not transferable when you switch to a new area.
It's easy to say 'reproduce the problem, then start coming up with guesses and verifying them' but where are people actually getting stuck in practice?
posts.key_insight: Debugging is fundamentally a cycle of questioning your own assumptions, designing experiments to test them, and building better mental models of the system — and the hardest part to teach is knowing where you're stuck.

posts.summary: Julia Evans presents 21 curl exercises designed as a deliberate practice experiment for learning HTTP requests from the command line. Inspired by Kathy Sierra's concept of micro-skills that can be learned in three 45-minute sessions, she identifies curl fluency as one such skill. The exercises use httpbin.org as a mirror to reflect requests back, progressively covering GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, headers, JSON bodies, authentication, and content negotiation. The post argues that fluency with command-line tools like curl is genuinely valuable for day-to-day development work like testing APIs.
key_points: Deliberate practice works best when focused on a small micro-skill learnable in roughly three 45-minute sessions
curl fluency lets you quickly test APIs and servers without needing a separate tool or GUI
httpbin.org is a useful practice target because it echoes back your request, making the invisible visible
The exercises are intentionally repetitive, covering the full range of common curl operations: methods, headers, JSON bodies, query parameters, auth, and content types
money_quotes: I was trying to think of a computer skill that I thought could be learned in 3 45-minute sessions.
With any command line tool, I think having fluency is really helpful. It's really nice to be able to just type in the thing you need.
curl is a command line tool for making HTTP requests. I like it because it's an easy way to test that servers or APIs are doing what I think, but it's a little confusing at first!
httpbin.org/anything will look at the request you made, parse it, and echo back to you what you requested.
They're a little repetitive on purpose. They exercise basically everything I do with curl.
posts.key_insight: curl is a micro-skill that can be learned through deliberate practice in a few focused sessions, and the resulting fluency pays off every time you need to quickly test an API.

posts.summary: Julia Evans presents a set of 18 hands-on Git exercises focused on navigating a real repository (Ruby's source code) rather than a toy example. The exercises use only five git subcommands (checkout, log, diff, show, status) to build comfort with SHAs, tags, branches, and viewing file history. She emphasizes that git SHAs always refer to the same code, making them a reliable anchor for understanding repository state. The post includes complete solutions for each exercise, making it self-contained for self-study. The approach breaks down Git learning into a manageable chunk by focusing purely on reading and navigating rather than writing or modifying.
key_points: Learning Git can be broken down by starting with navigation before tackling modification, making it less overwhelming
Using a real repository (Ruby) instead of a toy example makes exercises more engaging and realistic
Tags and branches are both just pointers to commits — they differ in how you change them, not how you look at them
A git SHA always refers to the same code, which is the foundational concept for understanding git navigation
Only five git subcommands (checkout, log, diff, show, status) are needed to navigate any repository effectively
money_quotes: Git is a big thing to learn, probably too big to learn in a few hours, so my first idea for how to break it down was by starting by navigating a repository.
I was originally going to use a toy test repository, but then I thought – why not a real repository? That's way more fun!
The most important thing to keep in mind while doing these exercises is that a git SHA always refers to the same code.
The way you change tags and branches are very different, but the way you look at tags and branches is exactly the same.
All of these exercises only use 5 git subcommands.
posts.key_insight: Git becomes much less intimidating when you start by learning to navigate and read a repository using just five commands, rather than trying to learn everything at once.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains practical, accessible ways to create zines without expensive tools. She realized that when people asked about her tools, they really wanted to know how to get started themselves, and 'buy a $500 iPad' wasn't a helpful answer. She walks through five methods for creating zine content—paper, Google Docs, iPad, single-sheet folding, and Adobe Illustrator—then covers how to assemble pages into booklets using either a photocopier or computer-based methods like pdflatex. The core message is that zine-making is fundamentally a low-tech, accessible activity.
key_points: The real question behind 'what tools do you use?' is often 'how can I do this myself?', which requires a different kind of answer
Zines can be made with nothing more than paper, a Sharpie, and a few dollars at a print shop photocopier
When using Google Docs for zines, 'less is more'—since pages print at half letter size, everything needs to be twice as big
The pdfpages LaTeX package provides precise control over booklet assembly without requiring actual LaTeX knowledge
Multiple methods exist at every skill and budget level, from hand-drawing to computer layout
money_quotes: I finally realized last week that instead of 'what tools do you use to write zines?' some people may have actually wanted to know 'how can I do this myself?'!
And 'buy a $500 iPad' is not a terribly useful answer to that question – it's not how I got started, iPads are kind of a weird fancy way to write zines, and most people don't have them.
They key thing about this Google doc approach is to apply some 'less is more'.

I like using this relatively complicated method because there are always small tweaks I want to make like 'oh, the right margin is too big, crop it a little bit' and the pdfpages package has tons of options that let me make those tweaks.
You definitely 100% do not need to use any fancy expensive tools to do it, you can literally use some sheets of paper, a Sharpie, a pen, and spend $3 at your local print shop to use the photocopier.
posts.key_insight: Zine-making is fundamentally low-tech and accessible—the barrier to entry is paper and a pen, not expensive software or hardware.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains the simplest possible way to put a static HTML page on the internet, walking through creating a single index.html file and hosting it on Neocities. She advocates for keeping web projects simple, noting that all her own sites are just static HTML and CSS. She mentions alternatives like GitHub Pages, GitLab Pages, and Netlify, but recommends Neocities as the lowest-friction starting point for beginners. The post serves as an encouraging entry point for people who want to learn HTML/CSS by actually publishing something.
key_points: Putting a static HTML page on the internet can be as simple as three steps: sign up for Neocities, paste your HTML, done
All of Julia's sites are static HTML and CSS, which keeps them maintainable despite minimal web design skills
Git-based hosting (GitHub Pages, Netlify) is better for real projects, but Neocities is ideal for experimentation and first-timers
Starting with a single file removes all the magic and complexity that can obstruct understanding
money_quotes: One thing I love about the internet is that it's SO EASY to put static HTML websites on the internet.
keeping all my internet sites relatively simple means that I have some hope of being able to make changes / fix things without spending a billion hours on it.
I also like that Neocities (like glitch, which I also love) is about experimentation and learning and having fun.
not having things in Git makes me nervous – I like to know what changes to my website I'm actually pushing.
there's literally just one file, so there's no fancy extra magic to get in the way of understanding what's going on.
it's always nice to start with the basics.
posts.key_insight: The simplest path to learning web development is publishing a single HTML file on the internet, stripping away all the tooling and complexity that usually obscures what's actually happening.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine 'HTTP: Learn your browser's language!' which teaches web developers the fundamentals of the HTTP protocol. She explains that understanding HTTP requests, responses, and headers demystifies caching, cookies, and web security. The zine was inspired by a conversation with Marco Rogers about how new developers would benefit from understanding HTTP basics. Evans describes her iterative creative process of posting individual pages to Twitter and incorporating feedback before assembling the final zine. She also introduces examplecat.com, a whimsical domain she registered for the zine's HTTP examples.
key_points: HTTP is fundamentally simple at its core, and understanding the basics makes the complex details much easier to look up and grasp when needed
Evans developed an iterative process of publishing individual zine pages on Twitter first, using community feedback to improve clarity and decide what to write next
Caching, cookies, and web security are all implemented through HTTP headers, so without understanding headers these concepts feel like impenetrable magic
The zine targets new web and mobile developers who would benefit from understanding HTTP fundamentals for debugging
money_quotes: HTTP is important to understand because it runs the entire web – if you understand how HTTP requests and responses work, then it makes it WAY EASIER to debug why your web application isn't working properly.
Caching, cookies, and a lot of web security are implemented using HTTP headers, so if you don't understand HTTP headers those things seem kind of like impenetrable magic.
Actually the HTTP protocol is fundamentally pretty simple – there are a lot of complicated details but the basics are pretty easy to understand.
The goal of this zine is to teach you the basics so you can easily look up and understand the details when you need them.

Instead of writing everything and then releasing it, instead I write a page at a time, post the page to Twitter, and then improve it and decide what page to write next based on the questions/comments I get on Twitter.
Writing zines one tweet at a time has been really fun. I think it improves the quality a lot, because I get a ton of feedback along the way that I can use to make the zine better.
posts.key_insight: HTTP is fundamentally simple, and understanding its basics transforms caching, cookies, and web security from impenetrable magic into straightforward concepts you can debug and reason about.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces she's leaving her job to spend a full year working on explaining computer things. After six years of blogging, 11 zines, 370,000 words, and 20 talks, she's turning her passion for technical education into a full-time endeavor. She plans to write more zines and build interactive learning tools. She'll run it as a solo business, which is already generating enough revenue to live on, without plans to hire employees or take on consulting work.
key_points: After 6 years of blogging and creating zines as a side project, she's going full-time on explaining computer concepts
Her plan includes writing up to 10 zines in a year and building interactive learning tools that let people learn by breaking things
Her existing zine business already earns more than her first programming job, making the leap financially viable
She's deliberately keeping the operation small — no employees, no consulting, no world domination plans
She expects the blog to shift from 'here's what I'm learning at work' to 'here are explanations of things I mostly already know'
money_quotes: Sometimes there are things you just feel compelled to do.
I've written 11 zines, 370,000 words on this blog, and given 20 or so talks. So it seems like I like explaining things a lot.
I learn things best by trying things out and breaking them, so I want to see if I can facilitate that a little bit for other people.
This business has been making more than I made in my first programming job (that is, definitely enough money to live on!), which has been really surprising and great.
I also don't have any specific plans for world domination or to work 80-hour weeks.
It's been pretty interesting to learn more about running a small business and so far I like it more than I thought I would. (except for taxes, which I like exactly as much as I thought I would)
posts.key_insight: After years of explaining computer things as a side project, turning it into a sustainable solo business feels like a natural next step when the work is something you feel compelled to do.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her process of building interactive SQL exercises designed to help people improve their practical SQL skills. She explains that SQL is exciting because distributed SQL engines let you query billions of rows quickly, and that exercises are needed because knowledge differs from skills. Her key design insight was to start each section with a challenge rather than easy exercises, so experienced users aren't bored. She tracks anonymous query data to understand where people get stuck and iterates on the exercises based on real usage patterns.
key_points: Distributed SQL engines like BigQuery and Presto make SQL exciting because they let you query billions of rows across hundreds of computers
Knowledge is different from skills — SQL requires practice, not just explanation, which motivated building interactive exercises instead of more written content
Starting with a challenge rather than easy exercises prevents experienced users from going through everything without learning anything new
Anonymously tracking user queries reveals where people actually get stuck, like column name matching, result ordering, and unfamiliar syntax like COUNT(DISTINCT col)
The entire SQL engine runs in the browser via sql.js, so there's no server to take down with expensive queries
money_quotes: SQL is a really nice way to go from 'I have this sort of complicated question about billions of rows of data' to 'ok, that's the answer, great, I can move on'.
She defines a 'skill' as 'something you have to practice'. And SQL is definitely something that you have to practice if you want to learn it!
A lot of people already know some SQL, and sometimes they would go through all the exercises without learning anything at all! That's no fun!
I think I need to spend more time cataloguing where/how people are getting stuck in practice and helping make sure fewer people get stuck.

The person I have in mind is sort of (as usual) myself 6 years ago, when I'd heard of SQL and could write a basic query but if you gave me a table of VERY INTERESTING DATA I couldn't really effectively use SQL to answer the questions I had about it.
posts.key_insight: Effective programming exercises should start with a challenge that tests the target skill, then offer easier exercises only if needed, because many learners already have partial knowledge and need to be pushed rather than walked through basics.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares revenue data from her zine business (wizardzines.com) for 2019, revealing $87,858 in revenue through September, on track to match 2018's $101,558. Revenue spikes dramatically in months when new zines are released, with September's $30,000 driven mostly by individual sales of the HTTP zine. She breaks down expenses at roughly 10% of revenue and highlights her buy-one-give-one program that has distributed 1,358 free copies. The post aims to demonstrate that making money by teaching technical topics directly to developers is viable without relying on ads or sponsorships.
key_points: Zine revenue for 2019 was ~$88K through September, with massive spikes in months when new zines launched
September's $30K broke down to 85% individual sales and 15% corporate licenses, showing strong direct-to-consumer demand
Expenses (illustrators, accountant, mailing list) are roughly 10% of revenue, making it a high-margin business
A buy-one-give-one-free model has distributed over 1,358 free copies while maintaining strong revenue
The core lesson is that developers will pay directly for quality educational materials, challenging the assumption that internet writing only monetizes through ads or sponsorships
money_quotes: The most surprising thing I've learned is that it's possible to make money by teaching people computer things on the internet.
I thought for a long time that you could only really make money from writing on the internet with ads or sponsorships, but it's not true!
This September was the month with the most sales ever, which is mostly because of individual humans who find the zines useful.
Many developers do really value good educational materials and are happy to pay for them.
Writing about money on the internet is weird, so this will probably be the first and last zine revenue post.
I've been giving away one free copy for every copy that people buy, so that people can get it even if $12 is hard for them to afford.
posts.key_insight: Teaching developers technical concepts through self-published zines can generate nearly six figures in annual revenue, proving that quality educational content can be monetized directly without ads or sponsorships.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains that SQL SELECT queries don't conceptually execute starting with SELECT — they follow a specific semantic order: FROM/JOIN, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, SELECT, ORDER BY, LIMIT. This ordering explains why certain operations are valid or invalid, like why you can't filter on window functions in WHERE. While database engines optimize and reorder operations for performance, understanding the semantic order helps reason about query correctness. She notes that other query tools like LINQ and pandas naturally follow this more logical order, and expresses surprise that this foundational concept isn't more widely taught.
key_points: SQL queries semantically execute in the order FROM/JOIN → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT, not starting with SELECT
This ordering explains common SQL puzzles like why you can't use window functions in WHERE clauses or filter GROUP BY results with WHERE
Database engines don't literally follow this order — they apply optimizations like filtering before joining — but the semantic order determines what's valid
Column aliases in SELECT can be used in GROUP BY because the engine rewrites the query, not because GROUP BY runs after SELECT
Other query tools like LINQ and pandas naturally use this more logical FROM-first ordering
money_quotes: SELECT isn't the first thing, it's like the 5th thing!

This was something that I felt like I knew intuitively ('I've written at least 10,000 SQL queries, some of them were really complicated! I must know this!') but I struggled to actually articulate what the order was.
You can use this diagram when you just want to understand which queries are valid and how to reason about what results of a given query will be.
You shouldn't use this diagram to reason about query performance or anything involving indexes, that's a much more complicated thing with a lot more variables.
It would be silly to do the whole left join and match up all the rows in the 2 tables if you just need to look up the 3 cats named 'mr darcy'.
I was SO SURPRISED that I'd never seen it written down that way before – it explains basically everything that I knew intuitively about why some queries are allowed and others aren't.
posts.key_insight: Understanding that SQL queries semantically execute in the order FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT demystifies why certain queries are valid and others aren't, even though database engines optimize the actual execution order.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces a print box set of her first seven free Linux zines, published through No Starch Press. The collection includes zines on strace, networking, tcpdump, perf, debugging tools, and Bite Size Linux, with new color covers. She negotiated to keep all the zines freely available online despite the print deal. The project was motivated more by curiosity about print publishing than profit, as she earns only 10% per sale compared to 97% from her online store.
key_points: No Starch Press published a physical box set of Evans' first seven free Linux zines with professional color illustrations
She contractually ensured all zines in the box set remain free to download online despite the print deal
The economics are heavily skewed: 10% royalty from print vs 97% revenue from self-published online sales
Her primary motivation was learning how traditional print publishing works, not maximizing revenue
She's exploring how to offer print copies of her newer paid zines independently
money_quotes: I asked them to write it into my publishing contract that I could still give them away, and they did :)
I get 10% or less of each sale, vs 97% for sales of my other zines online
I did it because I love the free zines I wrote, I wanted to make a really nice print version of them, and I wanted to learn about how print works and how traditional publishing works!
I don't necessarily expect to make a lot of money from this box set
I've already learned a lot about how publishing works and it's been super interesting
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best reason to pursue a publishing deal isn't the money but the learning opportunity, especially when you can negotiate to keep your work freely accessible.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her research into logistics for shipping printed zines to customers worldwide. She evaluates several options including Amazon's various programs (KDP, Advantage, Fulfilled By Amazon), and third-party fulfillment services like Blackbox, Shipbob, and Whiplash. She discovers that letter mail won't work for items with monetary value, and that shipping costs are charged in weight increments that make single-zine orders impractical. She concludes that bundling 5-10 zines per shipment is the most economically viable approach, since a pack of lightweight zines costs roughly the same to ship as a single one.
key_points: USPS letter mail can't be used for items with monetary value, eliminating the cheapest international shipping option

Amazon's various seller programs each have significant drawbacks: KDP doesn't support saddle stitching, Advantage takes 55% and requires frequent small shipments, and FBA involves complex international tax considerations
Third-party fulfillment services like Blackbox, Shipbob, and Whiplash offer domestic US shipping around $6 and international shipping around $10-12
Since zines weigh only 40g each and shipping is charged in half-pound increments, shipping 5 zines costs about the same as shipping 1, making bundles far more practical
Self-shipping from Canada is impractical due to cost and availability constraints
money_quotes: I thought I'd write down what I've learned so far because it turns out shipping is pretty complicated!
Value of contents can not exceed $0.00
Amazon Advantage takes 55% and they decide how to price your book.
You have to ship them your books every week or two depending on how many copies they want, you can't just ship them a big shipment to keep in a warehouse
Shipbob advertises a 99.8% fulfillment accuracy rate which is pretty interesting – it means they expect 2 in 1000 orders to have a problem.
Zines are pretty light – I just weighed some of my zines printed on high quality paper on my kitchen scale and they're 40g each on average.
Shipping 1 thing costs the same as shipping 5 things
posts.key_insight: Because shipping costs are based on weight tiers rather than per-item, bundling multiple lightweight zines into packs of 5-10 makes print distribution economically viable where single-zine orders would not be.

posts.summary: Julia Evans needed a newer version of SQLite to support window functions for a SQL examples website, but upgrading turned into a surprisingly educational adventure. After failing to use a pre-built binary (wrong loader for the build server), breaking her system trying to install a mismatched Debian package, and hitting shared library version mismatches, she finally tried compiling from source. SQLite's compilation process turned out to be remarkably simple: just download the amalgamation tarball and run a single gcc command. The experience highlighted how SQLite's design for embedded contexts makes it uniquely easy to build from source.
key_points: A binary can fail with 'File not found' not because it's missing, but because its loader (visible via ldd) doesn't exist on the system
Installing packages from a different Debian version than you're running is reliably a bad idea and can break critical dependencies like git
SQLite's entire codebase compiles from a single amalgamation file with no weird dependencies, making it far easier to build than most software
The simplicity of SQLite's build process reflects its design philosophy for embedded and restricted environments
Sometimes compiling from source is actually easier than finding the right pre-built binary
money_quotes: I straced it, and sure enough execve was returning the error code ENOENT, which means "File not found". This was kind of maddening because the file was DEFINITELY there and it had the correct permissions and everything.
To run a binary, you don't just need the binary to exist! You also need its loader to exist.
Trying to install a package from a different Debian version that I'm not using is literally never a good idea, but for some reason I decided to try it anyway.
Doing this completely unsurprisingly broke the sqlite installation on my computer (which also broke git).
All the code is in one file (sqlite.c), and there are no weird dependencies! It's amazing.

Often compiling things feels like this: run ./configure, realize i'm missing a dependency, run ./configure again, run make, the compiler fails because actually i have the wrong version of some dependency, go do something else and try to find a binary.
posts.key_insight: SQLite's single-file amalgamation build is so simple that compiling from source is actually easier than hunting for compatible pre-built binaries.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains how overlay filesystems (overlayfs) work as a key building block of container technology. She demonstrates that containers use overlays instead of copying entire images to save disk space and time, with a read-only lower directory and a writable upper directory. Through hands-on terminal examples, she shows how reads, writes, and deletes work in an overlay, including the surprising character device trick used to represent deleted files. She also explains how Docker layers map to multiple lower directories and briefly covers alternative storage drivers like btrfs snapshots.
key_points: Container images can be huge (up to 1.5GB), so Docker uses overlay filesystems instead of copying images for each container, saving disk space and startup time
Overlayfs combines a read-only lower directory with a writable upper directory — reads check upper first then fall back to lower, while writes always go to upper
Deleting a file in an overlay creates a special character device file in the upper directory as a 'whiteout' marker, rather than actually removing the lower file
Docker's multi-layer images work by mounting multiple lower directories in overlayfs, with each layer unpacked into its own directory
Docker's storage driver depends on the underlying filesystem — ext4 uses overlayfs, btrfs uses copy-on-write snapshots, each with different tradeoffs
money_quotes: Every container you start with an image starts out with the same blank slate, as if it made a copy of the image just for that container to use. But for big container images, like that 800MB Anaconda image, making a copy would be both a waste of disk space and pretty slow.

There's was an extremely annoying error message I kept getting while doing this, that said 'mount: special device overlay does not exist.' This message is a lie, and actually just means that one of the directories I specified was missing.
But what happened in upper is a little strange: there's a file called upper/in_both.txt, but it's a… character device? I guess this is how the overlayfs driver represents a file being deleted.
I think containers often seem like they're doing 'complicated' things and I think it's fun to break them down like this.
You can just run one mount incantation without actually doing anything else related to containers at all and see how overlays work!
posts.key_insight: Overlay filesystems are a surprisingly simple mechanism at the heart of containers — you can understand them completely by running a single mount command and experimenting with reads, writes, and deletes.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience switching from Notability to Affinity Designer for drawing her programming zines on iPad. After making four zines with Notability's simple interface, she hit limitations around mixing text boxes with pencil drawings. Affinity Designer offers dramatically more features including custom paper sizes, color palettes, object grouping, and flexible text handling. She notes that importing existing work via PDF is surprisingly smooth. The post also reflects on how iPad creative apps offer great value and capability, even for someone who primarily uses Linux.
key_points: Notability's extreme simplicity was initially a strength but became limiting as zine-making needs grew more complex
Affinity Designer's complexity means that most frustrations have a solution somewhere in its feature set
PDF import/export provides a reliable bridge between drawing apps, making switching tools much less painful than expected
Specific features like two kinds of text boxes (art text vs frame text) become meaningful once you understand your workflow needs
iPad creative apps offer professional-grade tools at surprisingly reasonable prices ($12-$30)
money_quotes: I still don't understand what all the tools do (what's the difference between Pencil and Vector Brush? I don't know!).
Unlike with Notability, there are so many options that if I'm frustrated about something, 90% of the time there's a way to do the thing I want!
This is the kind of thing that I wouldn't have understood 2 years ago but that now I LOVE.
It feels like an app that I won't grow out of.
I've been exclusively using Linux for the last 15 years where the image editing/media tools aren't always great.
It doesn't make me want a Mac (I like the Linux desktop experience!), but it's nice to have access to a bunch of these great tools.
posts.key_insight: Growing beyond a simple tool's limitations is a sign of progress, and the right upgrade is one complex enough that you won't outgrow it.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores what qualities make programming exercises effective for learning. She identifies criteria including being fun, teaching relevant skills, providing appropriate challenge, having clear success criteria, and being completable in a reasonable time. She draws an analogy to Julia Child's cookbook testing process, noting that exercises require more rigorous testing than blog posts because learners invest more time and expect to develop actual skills. The post also emphasizes practical concerns like avoiding setup friction, making help accessible, and ensuring learners won't get stuck on irrelevant details. She concludes with a consolidated list of nine criteria that good exercises should meet.
key_points: Good exercises must be challenging enough to teach something new — boredom from exercises that are too easy is a major failure mode
Exercises need far more testing than blog posts because readers expect to develop real skills, not just learn a fact or two
Setup friction and hidden gotchas are among the biggest exercise killers — running code in browsers or Docker can eliminate this
The Scratch philosophy of low floors, wide walls, and high ceilings applies to exercises: learners should see many possibilities for extending what they've built
Clear success criteria distinguish good exercises from vague prompts like 'write a toy TCP stack' that leave learners uncertain whether they've accomplished anything
money_quotes: Programming is fun and learning is fun so I can't see why programming exercises would need to be boring.

I often start programming exercises and get bored quickly ("oh, I know how to do this, this is boring"). For me it's really important for the exercise to teach me something I really don't know how to do and that's a little bit hard for me.
It's easy to write exercises that are too vaguely specified ("write a toy tcp stack!"). But what does that mean?
People have much higher expectations for exercises than for blog posts – with the blog posts you maybe expect to learn 1-2 new facts, but with exercises you expect to actually develop a new skill!
It's SO EASY to get stuck on some random irrelevant point in a programming exercise that's totally unrelated to the skill you're trying to learn.

It sucks when you start learning something and then learn that what you can do with the Thing is very limited! It's exciting when you learn something and see "oh, wow, there are SO MANY POSSIBILITIES, what if I did X instead?"
Installing software is boring, and a lot of programming projects require installing software!
posts.key_insight: Good programming exercises require rigorous testing and thoughtful design — clear success criteria, appropriate challenge, minimal setup friction, and room to grow — because learners invest far more time and expect to develop real skills, not just absorb facts.

posts.summary: Julia Evans presents a programming challenge to build a deliberately silly Linux window manager that bounces windows around the screen. She recommends starting from tinywm, a 50-line C window manager, and provides tooling instructions using Xephyr for safe development. She completed the challenge herself in about 5 hours with 50 lines of code, despite having never looked at window manager source code before. The post frames what sounds intimidating—writing a window manager—as surprisingly approachable when you strip away all the real requirements.
key_points: Writing a toy window manager is surprisingly approachable when you remove all serious requirements like resizing, switching, and minimizing windows
tinywm (50 lines of C) serves as an excellent starting point that demystifies window manager internals
Xephyr allows safe development by embedding an X session inside your existing desktop, and xtrace helps debug X protocol interactions
The exercise is a practical way to learn about the X window system protocol through hands-on experimentation rather than reading documentation
money_quotes: The goal here is to make a very silly Linux window manager that bounces its windows around the screen.
It turns out implementing this kind of toy window manager is surprisingly approachable!
Writing a window manager from scratch seems intimidating (at first I didn't even know how to start!).
I've been using window managers for 15 years, today I got to write one!
As usual when working with a new library I spent most of that time being confused about various basic things about how X works.
posts.key_insight: Intimidating-sounding systems programming tasks like writing a window manager become approachable when you define a deliberately minimal scope and build on existing tiny implementations.

posts.summary: Evans shares the results and her experience from a programming challenge to write a tiny window manager that bounces windows around the screen. She explains what an X window manager is and how it communicates with the X server via messages. The core technical lesson involves discovering that X batches XMoveWindow calls, which prevented sequential moves from working as expected. The solution was using XSync to force X to process events immediately. She also showcases several community solutions that went beyond the basic challenge requirements.
key_points: An X window manager is just a program that sends messages to the X server telling it where to display windows, and you can trace these messages with xtrace
You can run graphical programs without a window manager at all, though you lose the ability to resize, move, or switch between windows
X batches XMoveWindow calls by default, so multiple sequential moves only apply the last one unless you force processing with XSync
The xtrace debugging tool was key to understanding that ConfigureWindow events were being sent all at once rather than sequentially
money_quotes: An X window manager is a program that sends messages to the X server (which is in charge of drawing your windows) to tell it which windows to display and where.
You technically don't need a window manager to run graphical programs.
It has some major usability problems, for example I don't think you can resize or move or switch between windows. Which is where the window manager comes in!
I ran into a problem (which I got stuck on for a couple of hours) where when I ran XMoveWindow twice, it would only apply the last move.
I used xtrace to trace the events and found out that my ConfigureWindow events that XMoveWindow was sending were all being sent at the same time. So it seemed like X was batching the events.
Sure enough, I used XSync and everything worked beautifully.
posts.key_insight: X batches window management events by default, and understanding this through tracing tools like xtrace reveals how debugging at the protocol level can quickly resolve seemingly mysterious behavior.

posts.summary: Evans explores why the seemingly simple question 'what's a server?' turns out to be surprisingly hard to answer. She walks through multiple valid meanings: a server can be a program listening on a port, a virtual machine you SSH into, a container with cgroup limits, a physical 1U rack-mounted computer, or an entire distributed system of thousands of machines. Using Gmail search as a concrete example, she shows how 'it happens on the server' can mean a request touches 20 computers across 3 countries. The conclusion is that 'server' is context-dependent and genuinely ambiguous, which is fine until something goes wrong.
key_points: The word 'server' can refer to a program, a virtual machine, a container, a physical computer, or a distributed system depending on context
A Gmail search for 'boarding pass' might involve many computers across multiple datacenters, but we casually say it happens 'on the server'
The ambiguity of 'server' is usually harmless — the abstraction lets us avoid caring about what happens behind the scenes until something breaks
Physical servers look nothing like home computers — they're short, wide, rack-mounted machines measured in 'rack units'
Containers add another layer where 'the server is running out of memory' really means a cgroup limit was exceeded and the kernel OOM-killed processes
money_quotes: Somebody asked me recently what a server was, and I had a harder time explaining it than I expected!
You can't ssh to a program, though the ssh server that runs on the VM is itself a program!

'The server is running out of memory' could mean 'we set a cgroup memory limit on this container and the programs in the container with that cgroup exceeded the limit so the Linux kernel OOM killed them'.

There's not just one computer or program or virtual machine that searches your Gmail, there are probably lots of computers and programs at Google that are responsible for that and they're probably distributed across many datacenters all over the world.
Even if we're just talking about doing 1 search, there could easily be 20 different computers in 3 different countries involved in just running that 1 search.
We can just say 'it happens on the server' and not worry too much about the ambiguity of what exactly that means (until something weird goes wrong).
At first when I thought about the question 'what's a server?' I really thought there was going to be a single simple answer!
posts.key_insight: The word 'server' is genuinely ambiguous — it can mean a program, a VM, a container, a physical machine, or a distributed system — and this ambiguity is usually a useful abstraction rather than a problem.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her journey from lightweight window managers through tiling window managers like xmonad back to Gnome, and then discovering PaperWM, a Gnome extension that brings tiling window management to the Gnome desktop. PaperWM arranges all windows in a line rather than arbitrary tiles, which she finds appealingly simple. She highlights that she only uses a handful of keyboard shortcuts. The post emphasizes her growing appreciation for tools that work well out of the box without requiring configuration.
key_points: PaperWM is a Gnome extension that adds tiling window management without leaving the Gnome ecosystem where things Just Work
Its 'paper' metaphor arranges windows in a linear sequence rather than arbitrary tiling layouts, trading flexibility for simplicity
Evans's tool preferences have shifted over time from enjoying deep configurability to valuing sensible defaults
She uses only four keyboard shortcuts despite PaperWM having many more features
money_quotes: About 6 years ago I decided configuring my tiling window manager wasn't fun for me anymore and switched to using the Ubuntu stock desktop environment: Gnome.
The basic idea of PaperWM is: you want to keep using Gnome (because all kinds of things Just Work in Gnome) but you also kinda wish you were using a tiling window manager.
I'd never heard of this way of organizing windows before but I like the simplicity of it – if I'm looking for a specific window I just move left/right until I find it.
I really appreciate that even though it's configurable (by writing a Javascript configuration file), it does the things I want out of the box without me having to research how to configure it.
I could configure xmonad by writing a Haskell program! I could customize everything in all kinds of fun ways!
posts.key_insight: The best tools are ones that deliver what you want out of the box, without requiring you to invest time in configuration.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine about SQL called 'Become a SELECT Star!' She explains her motivation for writing it, drawing on her experience writing SQL queries for machine learning at her previous job. The zine focuses on SQL basics with carefully crafted examples showing queries, input tables, and outputs. She also built an interactive SQL playground using SQLite compiled to WebAssembly so readers can run the examples in their browser. She teases that her next zine will likely cover containers.
key_points: SQL has many non-intuitive behaviors (like query execution order not starting with SELECT, and NULL behavior) that are worth explaining clearly
SQL is a high-leverage skill because of the prevalence of powerful SQL databases like BigQuery and Redshift that can process billions of rows
The zine uses a visual format showing query, input table, and output together to make examples concrete and understandable
She built an interactive SQL playground using SQLite compiled to WebAssembly, letting readers experiment with real queries in the browser
money_quotes: SQL queries don't actually start with SELECT.
The way NULL behaves isn't really intuitive at first.
I think SQL is a really nice thing to know because there are SO MANY SQL databases out there, and some of them are super powerful!
If you know SQL and have access to one of these big data warehouses you can write queries that crunch like 10 billion rows of data really quickly.
I ended up spending a lot of time on the examples in this zine, more than in any previous zine.
All the examples in the zine are real queries that you can run.
posts.key_insight: SQL is a high-leverage skill worth learning well because it unlocks the ability to query massive datasets across the many powerful SQL databases that exist, but its non-intuitive behaviors need clear, example-driven explanation.

posts.summary: Julia Evans walks through her process of learning to write GPU shaders, starting from the default Shadertoy example and building up to a shiny spinning octahedron animation. She introduces the core concept of signed distance functions (SDFs) as a powerful alternative to hardcoded if-statements for defining shapes, explaining how they're simple to define, easy to compose, and easy to transform. The post demonstrates a step-by-step learning approach: start with the basics, iterate quickly with better tooling, draw simple shapes with math, then adopt SDFs and raytracing for 3D rendering. She emphasizes that shaders are more approachable than they appear, especially when you copy working tutorial code and modify it experimentally.
key_points: Shaders map coordinates and time to colors, making them trivially parallelizable and perfect for GPUs
Signed distance functions define shapes by how far any point is from the surface, replacing brittle if-statement approaches and enabling easy composition, rotation, and transformation
The three steps to rendering a 3D shape are: define an SDF, raytrace/raymarch it into 2D, then texture and light the surface
Shaders provide instant visual feedback on math, making them a fun playground for building intuition about trigonometry and geometry
The 'copy code and change things chaotically' approach is a legitimate and effective way to learn shader programming
money_quotes: The neat thing about this programming model is that it's extremely trivially parallelizable.
I love that you get instant visual feedback about your math code – if you multiply something by 2, things get bigger! or smaller! or faster! or slower! or more red!
But the problem with this is that it's not clear how it generalizes to more complicated shapes! Writing a bajillion if statements doesn't seem like it would work well.
Signed distance functions are awesome because they're: simple to define, easy to compose, easy to rotate / stretch / bend!
I used the time honoured programming practice here of 'copy the code and change things in a chaotic way until I get the result I want'.
Basically I just changed parameters to the noise function mostly at random until I got an effect I liked.
My shader skills are still extremely basic, but this fun spinning thing turned out to be a lot easier to make than I thought it would be.
posts.key_insight: Signed distance functions make shader programming surprisingly approachable by replacing complex conditional geometry with simple, composable mathematical functions that define shapes by distance.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine 'How Containers Work!' which explains the Linux kernel features that make containers possible. She describes how containers initially confused her despite her Linux knowledge, and explains they're actually six different features glued together. The zine covers cgroups, namespaces, pivot_root, seccomp-bpf, and related fundamentals. She demonstrates that containers can be implemented in just 15 lines of bash, demystifying them. The zine is deliberately container-runtime-agnostic, focusing on kernel primitals rather than Docker or Kubernetes. She also announces a redesign of wizardzines.com done with designer Melody Starling.
key_points: Containers are not a single feature but six different Linux kernel features glued together, which is why they seem confusing at first
Understanding the fundamentals (cgroups, namespaces, pivot_root, seccomp-bpf) makes debugging container problems in production much easier
A basic container can be run with just 15 lines of bash, showing containers aren't magic — the complexity comes from security features like dropping capabilities and blocking syscalls
Working with a designer improved not just the visual appearance of wizardzines.com but the information architecture and organization
money_quotes: It turns out that containers ARE actually pretty weird. They're not just one thing, they're what you get when you glue together 6 different features that were mostly designed to work together but have a bunch of confusing edge cases.
As usual, the thing that helped me the most in my container adventures is a good understanding of the fundamentals – what exactly is actually happening on my server when I run a container?
Once I understood those ideas, it got a lot easier to debug when my containers were doing surprising things in production.
If you understand the fundamentals you can figure all those things out!
One cool thing about working with a designer is – they don't just make things look better, they help organize the information better so the website makes more sense and it's easier to find things!
posts.key_insight: Containers stop being confusing once you understand they're just six Linux kernel features glued together, and that understanding of fundamentals is what makes debugging production container issues tractable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans investigates why strace fails inside Docker containers with a 'ptrace not permitted' error. She systematically tests and rejects two hypotheses — missing CAP_SYS_PTRACE capability and user namespace differences — before discovering the real cause: Docker's default seccomp-bpf profile blocks the ptrace system call entirely. She then digs into Docker's source code to explain why --cap-add=SYS_PTRACE fixes it, finding that the flag secretly also whitelists related system calls in the seccomp profile. The post demonstrates how containers are built from multiple interacting security mechanisms that can produce surprising behavior.
key_points: The real reason strace fails in Docker is not missing capabilities but Docker's seccomp-bpf profile blocking the ptrace system call
CAP_SYS_PTRACE is for tracing arbitrary processes across users — regular users can already ptrace their own processes without it
Docker's --cap-add flag does more than advertised: it also whitelists related system calls in the seccomp profile
The root user inside a Docker container (without user namespaces) is the same root user as on the host
Newer Docker versions (19.03+) allow ptrace by default for kernels newer than 4.8
money_quotes: I wasn't interested in fixing it, I wanted to know why it happens.
The point of CAP_SYS_PTRACE is to let you ptrace arbitrary processes owned by any user, the way that root usually can. You shouldn't need it to just ptrace a regular process owned by your user.
I hadn't realized that the root user in a Docker container is the same as the root user on the host, so I thought that was interesting.
--cap-add doesn't do exactly what it says it does in the man page, it's more like --cap-add-and-also-whitelist-some-extra-system-calls-if-required.
When the documentation doesn't help, the only thing to do is go look at the source.
This was a fun small thing to investigate, and I think it's a nice example of how containers are made of lots of moving pieces that work together in not-completely-obvious ways.
posts.key_insight: Docker's --cap-add flag secretly does double duty, adding both Linux capabilities and seccomp-bpf whitelist entries, which is why it fixes strace even though the root cause is a seccomp rule blocking ptrace, not a missing capability.

posts.summary: Inspired by Maggie Appleton's talk on metaphors and the book 'Metaphors We Live By,' Julia Evans explores the pervasive use of cognitive metaphors in Unix man pages. She greps through man pages on her computer looking for examples of common metaphor categories like processes as people, data as food, and limits as buildings. The post catalogs dozens of examples organized by metaphor type, revealing that technical documentation is saturated with figurative language we rarely notice. She finds metaphors treating processes as living beings (death, birth, orphaned), data as physical objects or food, resources as consumable goods, and system limits as architectural features like floors and ceilings.
key_points: Cognitive metaphors differ from figurative metaphors — they're embedded in everyday language so deeply we don't notice them
Unix man pages are rich with metaphors: processes are people (birth, death, orphaned, zombie), data is food (raw, swallow, feed), operations are money (expensive, cost, budget)
The 'processes as people' category is the richest, with man pages consistently treating processes as alive — they die, have parents and children, listen, observe, and shake hands
Container metaphors are everywhere in computing: files 'contain' data, caches get 'full', buffers 'overflow', and you can 'get out' of a chroot
Technical writing uses far more metaphorical language than we typically recognize, even in supposedly precise documentation
money_quotes: I went through some of the examples of metaphors in Metaphors To Live By and grepped all the man pages on my computer for them.
I found a lot more metaphors than I expected, and most of them are just part of how I'd normally talk about a program.
I was surprised that there were so many different metaphors for ideas, and that we're using metaphors like this all the time in normal language.
Apparently some digital cameras get indigestion if you feed them a CF card.
debugfs is a debugging tool. It has rough edges!
the maximum value will easily eat up three extra gigabytes or so of memory
posts.key_insight: Technical documentation like Unix man pages is permeated with cognitive metaphors — processes are people, data is food, resources are wealth — revealing that even our most precise technical language is fundamentally figurative.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her iterative process of building a formative assessment tool to help self-directed learners identify what to learn next. She started with flashcards, discovered problems with how questions interact with readers' mental models, and evolved toward a side-by-side question/answer format inspired by The Little Schemer. A key design insight was that framing matters enormously: being told what you don't know feels bad, but being helped to learn something new feels good. She added an 'I learned something' button to shift focus from scoring to discovery.
key_points: Formative assessment (helping learners identify gaps) is far more useful for self-directed learning than summative assessment (grades)

Questions are held to a higher standard than statements for matching the reader's mental model — the same content phrased as a question can feel wrong when phrased as a statement it would be unobjectionable
Framing matters: an 'I learned something' button shifted user behavior away from score-keeping toward focusing on new knowledge
The Little Schemer's side-by-side question/answer format gives readers more control, which matters for self-directed learning
Coming up with the right questions is the hardest part of learning when you don't know much about a topic
money_quotes: One thing I learned from this is that being told you don't know something is a bad experience for a lot of people.
So instead of it being something that tells you what you don't know (which, so what?), it helps you learn something new!
People dislike questions that don't match their mental model.
If I'm asking a question, it gets held to a higher standard for how well it matches with the reader's mental model than when making the same statement.

Coming up with the right questions on your own is hard when you don't know a lot, so I'm hopeful that providing folks with a bunch of questions and answers to think about will help you decide what you want to learn next.
I see more people commenting 'I learned something' and less 'I got 9/10'.
Building small simple things takes more time than I'd expect!
posts.key_insight: How you frame a learning tool matters as much as its content — shifting from 'what don't you know' to 'what did you learn' fundamentally changes whether people engage productively with their own knowledge gaps.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience building a small interactive website using plain JavaScript without any frameworks or libraries. After years of relying on jQuery and Vue.js for side projects, she discovered that modern browser APIs like classList, querySelectorAll, scrollIntoView, and innerHTML made it surprisingly easy to build what she needed in about 50 lines of code. The post walks through specific patterns she used, like toggling CSS classes for UI state changes and using CSS transitions for animations. She positions the advice as practical tips for fellow non-professional frontend developers who want to build small, functional websites without framework overhead.
key_points: Modern browser APIs like querySelectorAll, classList, and scrollIntoView have eliminated many of the reasons people originally reached for jQuery
Implementing UI changes primarily through adding and removing CSS classes keeps JavaScript simple and leverages CSS transitions for animations
querySelectorAll has been available in all major browsers since around 2011, meaning the jQuery-style selector convenience has been built into browsers for over a decade
For small projects under 200 lines of JavaScript, plain JS without a framework can be simpler and more appropriate than pulling in a library
money_quotes: I've never worked as a professional frontend developer, so even though I've been writing HTML/CSS/JS for 15 years for little side projects, all of the projects have been pretty small, sometimes I don't write any Javascript for years in between, and I often don't quite feel like I know what I'm doing.
I decided to implement almost all of the UI by just adding & removing CSS classes, and using CSS transitions if I want to animate a transition.
When I started learning jQuery I remember thinking that if you wanted to easily find something in the DOM you had to use jQuery.
I was pretty surprised by how much I could get done with just plain JS. I ended up writing about 50 lines of JS to do everything I wanted to do.
As usual with my frontend posts, this isn't meant to be Serious Frontend Engineering Advice – my goal is to be able to write little websites with less than 200 lines of Javascript that mostly work.
If you are also flailing around in frontend land I hope this helps a bit!
posts.key_insight: Modern browser APIs have made plain JavaScript surprisingly capable for small projects, often eliminating the need for frameworks that add complexity without proportional benefit.

posts.summary: Julia Evans introduces entr, a command line tool that reruns arbitrary commands whenever specified files change. She explains how it fills a gap when custom build processes lack built-in file watching, unlike frameworks like Hugo or Flask. The post covers key flags like -r for restarting servers, -c for clearing the screen, and -d for detecting new files. She demonstrates practical patterns combining entr with git ls-files to watch tracked and untracked files. The post briefly explains that entr uses inotify on Linux to watch for filesystem events.
key_points: entr watches a set of files passed via stdin and reruns any command when they change, providing instant feedback for custom build processes
Key flags include -r (restart servers), -c (clear screen), and -d (exit when new files are added to watched directories)
Combining entr with git ls-files is a natural pattern since tracked files usually match what you want to watch
A while loop wrapping entr -d with git ls-files handles the case of newly added files that aren't yet tracked
Under the hood on Linux, entr uses inotify with inotify_add_watch system calls for each watched file
money_quotes: I found out about entr relatively recently and I felt like WHY DID NOBODY TELL ME ABOUT THIS BEFORE?!?!
Like possibly every single programmer in the universe, I find it Very Annoying to have to manually rerun my build / tests every time I make a change to my code.
entr lets me have a magical build experience where I get instant feedback on whether my change fixed the weird bug with just one line of bash.

A lot of tools (like hugo and flask) have a built in system to automatically rebuild when you change your files, which is great! But often I have some hacked together custom build process that I wrote myself.
Usually the set of files I want to track is about the same list of files I have in git, so git ls-files is a natural thing to pipe to entr.
posts.key_insight: entr gives any custom build process the same instant-feedback file-watching experience that frameworks provide built-in, with just one line of bash.

posts.summary: Julia Evans advocates for companies to write documents explaining their interview process to candidates. She describes how at her previous job, candidates were sometimes caught off guard by unexpected interview formats, wasting everyone's time. The solution was simple: write a short document covering what to expect, how to prepare, and what candidates would be evaluated on. She emphasizes that documenting an existing process is far easier than changing it, and that keeping the document updated over time is even more important than the initial writing. The post frames this as a small, achievable culture change that levels the playing field for all candidates.
key_points: Different companies run interviews very differently, and expecting candidates to intuit your process is unfair and wasteful
Telling candidates what they'll be measured on lets them prepare effectively instead of wasting time on irrelevant skills
Documenting an existing interview process is far easier than changing one, since it's just factual information about what you already do
Small, concrete cultural changes like this are more achievable and rewarding than trying to overhaul everything at once
Keeping the document updated over time matters more than the initial writing
money_quotes: I think it's silly to expect candidates to magically intuit how your company's interview process works.
It sucks for everyone when a candidate is surprised with an unexpected interview.
The point of the interview wasn't to watch people install bundler!
Telling people in advance what they'll be measured on makes it way easier for them to prepare.
Documenting an existing interview process (without changing it!!) is WAY EASIER.
Basically nobody objected to documenting the interview process the company already had – it was just factual information about what we were already doing!
Making the companies I work at a better place for everyone to work is important to me. It's a huge project, and I've tried a lot of things that haven't worked.
posts.key_insight: Documenting your existing interview process for candidates is a low-effort, low-controversy change that levels the playing field and makes interviews less wasteful for everyone.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her 'Saturday Comics' mailing list, which sends subscribers one programming comic per week drawn from her best past work rather than her latest output. She created it to solve three problems: not everyone follows Twitter, she couldn't maintain a traditional weekly newsletter, and her best comics were hard to discover. The list uses ConvertKit's sequence feature so each subscriber starts from the beginning regardless of when they join. After a year, 5000 people have subscribed and the response has been positive. She frames the list as a trust-building marketing tool rather than a conversion-optimization exercise.
key_points: A mailing list of curated past work is more sustainable than one requiring fresh content every week
New subscribers start from the beginning of a fixed sequence rather than joining mid-stream, ensuring everyone sees the best material
Previous attempts at weekly newsletters failed because she could never maintain the cadence of writing new emails
Marketing for her zine business works best as trust-building through genuinely helpful content rather than conversion optimization
money_quotes: If you want to have a mailing list that's useful to people, but don't have the discipline to write new email all the time, consider just making a mailing list of your best past work!
I've tried to set up a "weekly mailing list" of sorts a few times, and inevitably what happens is: I announce the mailing list, people subscribe, I literally never email the list.
If someone isn't familiar with my work and wants to learn more, they're more likely to find something interesting to them in my "best" work than just whatever I happen to be working on at the time.
It's maybe not ideal, but I think it's okay, and it's definitely better than my previous mailing list practices of "literally never email the mailing list ever".
I've learned to think of marketing (at least for my business) as just building trust by helping people learn new things.
Instead of worrying about optimizing conversion rates or whatever (which has never helped me at all), I just try to send emails to the list that will be helpful.
posts.key_insight: A sustainable newsletter doesn't require constant new content — curating and sequencing your best existing work can be more valuable to subscribers and more maintainable for the creator.

posts.summary: Julia Evans discovers scanimage, a command line tool for scanning documents on Linux. After struggling with a GUI scanning application, she finds that scanimage from the sane-utils package works easily from the terminal. She walks through discovering her scanner, listing its options, and writing a simple 5-line shell script to scan documents to PDF. The post captures her surprise that Linux scanning turned out to be straightforward rather than the nightmare she expected.
key_points: scanimage is a command line scanning tool in the sane-utils Debian package that uses the SANE libraries
Each scanner has different options discoverable via --help, including duplex scanning and resolution settings
scanimage doesn't output PDFs directly, but a simple 5-line shell script combining scanimage and convert solves this
Linux printer/scanner support has a reputation for difficulty but scanimage worked with minimal friction
money_quotes: I always expect using printers/scanners on Linux to be a nightmare and I was really surprised how scanimage Just Worked.
At first I couldn't get this to work and I was a bit frustrated but it turned out that I'd connected the scanner to my computer, but not plugged it into the wall. Oops.
I started by using gscan2pdf and had trouble figuring out the user interface – I wanted to scan both sides of the page at the same time but couldn't get it to work.
I think all Linux scanning tools use the sane libraries ("scanner access now easy") so my guess is that it has similar abilities to any other scanning software.
I could just run my script with scan-single-sided receipts.pdf and it would scan a document and save it to receipts.pdf!
posts.key_insight: Command line tools like scanimage can be simpler and more effective than GUI alternatives for tasks like scanning, turning a dreaded Linux hardware interaction into a quick shell script.

posts.summary: Evans advocates for telling your coworkers' managers when they're doing great work, framing it as a concrete way to support colleagues—especially those from underrepresented groups. She emphasizes always asking the person first, since well-intentioned praise can backfire if it conflicts with someone's goals or focus areas. The post details when this matters most: highlighting invisible work like documentation, cross-team help, or problem prevention that managers may not see. She explains the mechanics—be specific, explain impact, highlight when someone exceeds expectations for their level. The practice directly supports promotion cases by providing third-party evidence of valuable contributions. Evans positions this as something anyone at any level can do to lift up their coworkers.
key_points: Always ask before telling someone's manager about their work—people have legitimate reasons to want control over what feedback reaches their manager
The most valuable time to do this is when important work is going unrecognized: cross-team contributions, private help, prevention work, documentation, and work by people from underestimated groups
Specific, impact-focused praise helps managers build promotion cases with concrete evidence
Even junior employees can meaningfully advocate for coworkers by describing how someone's help made a difference to their own work
Well-intentioned praise can backfire if it highlights the wrong skills, undersells someone's level, or draws attention to work outside their assigned focus
money_quotes: Managers don't always see the work their reports are doing, and if someone is doing really amazing work that their manager isn't seeing, they won't get promoted as quickly.
A lot of people (for very good reasons!) want to have control over the kind of feedback their manager hears about them.
For someone to get promoted, they need evidence that they've been doing valuable work, and managers don't always have the time to put together all that evidence.

Even if you're really new and don't feel that qualified to comment on how effective someone more senior is at their job, you can still point out things like 'this person helped me do a project that was really out of my comfort zone!'
I've tried to expand the set of people I do this for over time — I think it's important to keep an eye out for coworkers who are really excelling and to make sure their work is recognized.
One person mentioned that they got reprimanded by their manager for getting a spot peer bonus for helping someone on another team.
posts.key_insight: Telling someone's manager about their great work is a simple, high-impact practice anyone can do to help colleagues get recognized—but always ask first, because even positive feedback can backfire.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a series of CSS comics she's been creating while deliberately learning CSS. She draws a parallel between CSS and systems programming, arguing both have counterintuitive facts that become manageable once learned. She advocates reading the CSS specs directly as an authoritative source, finding that intimidating topics like selector specificity are simpler than expected. The post includes links to several comics covering the box model, units, selectors, position absolute, and inline vs block elements.
key_points: CSS is similar to systems programming in that mastering counterintuitive facts makes it much more manageable
Reading the CSS specs directly can be more reliable than learning from websites with conflicting information
The CSS2 spec is easier to digest for understanding the big picture since it's smaller and more unified than the modular CSS3 specs
Deliberately deciding to learn CSS rather than flailing around and giving up is a key mindset shift
money_quotes: CSS feels a little like systems programming / Linux to me – there are a lot of counterintuitive facts that you need to learn to be effective with it, but I think once you learn those facts it gets a lot easier.
I've decided to actually take some time to learn CSS instead of just flailing around and deciding 'oh no, this is impossible'.
I think reading (parts of) the CSS specs is fun because I'm so used to learning CSS by reading a lot of websites which sometimes have conflicting information.
So it's fun to read a more authoritative source!
Some things about CSS that I was intimidated by (like selector specificity) aren't as complicated as I thought.
posts.key_insight: CSS, like systems programming, has counterintuitive rules that become manageable once you commit to learning them systematically rather than avoiding them.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experiment using OpenType font features to make her handwritten font look more like actual handwriting by introducing character variation. She used iFontMaker to create the original font, then learned from Tristan Hume's !!Con talk that OpenType rules can replace characters based on context. Using the fonttools Python library, she wrote about 33 lines of code to merge multiple handwriting fonts and apply substitution rules. While she found the process surprisingly easy and fun, she wasn't fully satisfied with the results, describing them as 'uncanny valley' — still obviously a font but with slightly more variation.
key_points: iFontMaker lets you create a TTF font of your handwriting in about 15 minutes with an iPad and Apple Pencil
OpenType substitution rules let you replace glyphs based on surrounding context, introducing variation without randomness
The fonttools Python library makes it trivially easy to add OpenType rules to existing fonts in just 5 lines of code
The results fell into an 'uncanny valley' where the font had more variation but still didn't feel like real handwriting
money_quotes: It feels a little uncanny valley to me, like it's obviously still a font but it's pretending to be something else.

I started out being extremely confused about what OpenType even is. I still don't know much, but I learned that you can write extremely simple OpenType rules to change how a font looks, and you don't even have to really understand anything about fonts.
I'm actually not super happy with the results of this experiment, but I wanted to share it anyway because it was very easy and fun to play with fonts.
It's like 33 lines of code and I think it's pretty straightforward.
Mostly what was delightful to me here is that it's so easy to add OpenType rules to change how fonts work, like you can pretty easily make a font where the word 'the' is always replaced with 'teh'.
I'm skeptical of the 'just your fingers' claim – I used an Apple Pencil, which has much better accuracy.
posts.key_insight: OpenType substitution rules and fonttools make it surprisingly easy to programmatically modify font behavior, though making a font truly mimic handwriting remains an unsolved challenge.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares six new CSS comics and reflects on her evolving relationship with CSS debugging. She discusses how handling browser bugs is a normal part of CSS development, citing the flexbugs repository and a specific Safari bug she encountered. She categorizes three distinct ways CSS can go wrong: wrong styles applied, correct styles with unexpected behavior due to spec misunderstanding, and correct styles broken by browser bugs. The post advocates for a calmer, more accepting mindset toward browser quirks rather than frustration.
key_points: Working around browser bugs is a normal, expected part of writing CSS, not an exceptional frustration
There are at least three distinct categories of CSS bugs: wrong styles applied, spec misunderstanding, and browser implementation bugs
Adopting a calm 'oh well, that happens sometimes' mindset toward browser bugs leads to better outcomes than getting angry
Resources like the flexbugs repo help catalog and document known browser implementation issues with workarounds
money_quotes: The fact that finding workarounds for browser bugs is kind of a normal part of writing CSS really surprised me.
In the past I would have reacted to this in a more grumpy way (WHY? NOOOOO? WHAT IS HAPPENING?!?! CSS?!?!?!).
I think this mindset of 'oh, there's a browser bug, oh well, I guess that happens sometimes!' is a lot healthier and more likely to result in success than getting mad about it.
The element has the 'right' styles applied and those styles do the right thing according to the spec, but the browser has a bug and isn't implementing the spec correctly.
I noticed that my site looked weird in Safari on my iPad, figured out after 30 minutes or so that it was a Safari bug, implemented a workaround, and it actually wasn't that big of a deal!
posts.key_insight: Accepting that browser bugs are a normal part of CSS development, rather than a source of frustration, leads to a healthier and more productive debugging mindset.
