posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her process for choosing zine topics, identifying four key criteria: the topic must be fundamental, useful for programming work, stable over time, and possible to learn quickly. She realizes that beyond these objective criteria, she can only write about topics she genuinely finds exciting and wants to share. She notes that her best topics are things she once struggled with but eventually came to love, like tcpdump and containers. The post lists potential future zines and topics she doesn't feel ready to tackle yet.
key_points: Good technical writing topics should be fundamental, useful, stable over 5-10 years, and learnable quickly
Personal passion is a non-negotiable filter beyond objective criteria — she can only write about topics she genuinely cares about
The most compelling topics to write about are ones she once struggled with but eventually came to love
Topics that were immediately easy or obviously great feel less interesting to write about
She maintains honest self-awareness about topics she can't yet write about, either from insufficient knowledge or insufficient love
money_quotes: I only write about topics that I really think are exciting and fun and important and want to share with people.
I think it's often important for me to write about topics which I now love but in the past did not love.
It feels a lot less interesting to write about topics where it was immediately obvious to me why they were great or which were easy for me to learn.
once I got it I felt like I had to tell everyone HELLO I FIGURED IT OUT TCPDUMP IS ACTUALLY AWESOME AND NOT THAT HARD.
I don't think that I could write these today, either because I don't know enough about the topic yet or because I don't really feel enough love for it yet.
I'm still not sure (even after doing this for years!) why it's so hard for me to tell what topics will make for a good zine that I can write.
posts.key_insight: The best technical writing comes from topics you once struggled with but eventually came to love, because that journey from confusion to understanding is exactly what makes the explanation valuable to others.

posts.summary: Evans describes building a 'focus and reply' email feature for Fastmail using their JMAP protocol, inspired by a similar feature in Hey.com. She walks through the process of authenticating with JMAP, querying for emails in a 'Reply Later' folder, and rendering them in a simple Vue.js frontend. The post serves as a practical quickstart guide for JMAP since she found few examples when learning the API. She highlights JMAP's request chaining as a key design feature that reduces latency. The whole project came out to 170 lines of JavaScript running entirely in the browser with no server code.
key_points: JMAP is Fastmail's modern replacement for IMAP that's significantly easier to work with for building email tools
JMAP's request chaining lets you bundle multiple dependent queries into a single API call, reducing round trips
The entire email UI experiment works as a pure frontend app with no server code, authenticating via HTTP Basic with an app password
She started read-only to stay safe, copying replies into webmail rather than sending through the API directly
Fastmail's own web interface uses JMAP differently than the public API, with a different endpoint and Bearer token authentication
money_quotes: I think the idea that anyone can just use JMAP to make fun email UI experiments without dealing with the Hard Parts of email is really fun!
I was a bit too scared to actually send email to start (read-only is safe!), so I decided to start by just making a UI that would show me all the emails I needed to reply to.
The API wasn't that intuitive at first, but I was able to figure how to do what I wanted to by reading the spec.
One of the big ideas in JMAP seems to be this chaining – it really reduces latency if you can do all your work in a single request.
This is a little janky, but I don't mind it for now.
And it's really cool that I could get this to work 100% as a frontend app, without any server code at all!
posts.key_insight: JMAP makes it surprisingly easy for anyone to build custom email UI experiments as pure frontend apps, lowering the barrier that IMAP's complexity previously imposed.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces the first print run of her Wizard Zines business, shipping 400 copies of 'Help! I have a Manager!' from her home in Montreal. She explains her decision to handle printing and shipping herself rather than using a fulfillment company, embracing the concept of 'flintstoning' — starting simple rather than overengineering. The post covers practical details like pricing, shipping logistics, and her experience finding a quality print shop. She frames this as an experiment that could scale up if successful.
key_points: First physical print run of Wizard Zines, with 400 copies printed at a high-quality Montreal print shop
Deliberately chose to handle shipping herself rather than use fulfillment companies, embracing simplicity over scalability
Uses lettermail from Canada to keep costs low enough to offer free worldwide shipping at $3/zine postage
Ships without tracking to reduce costs, comparing the approach to UDP networking
money_quotes: in the spirit of flintstoning, I decided it would be a lot simpler to start out by not overengineering the process
I found out that using a print shop that cost more got me way better quality zines! So that's what I did.

All the zines will be shipped by first class letter mail from Canada, without any tracking. This keeps shipping costs down so that I can do free shipping :). If anything at all goes wrong with your shipment, just let me know and I'll mail you another one. (it's like UDP!)
That time I even wrote all the addresses by hand, which I definitely won't do this time.
doing it myself means I can do some fun things with the envelopes & my laser printer that would be hard to convince a fulfillment company to do
posts.key_insight: Starting a physical product business works best when you resist the urge to overengineer and instead begin by doing everything yourself to learn the process firsthand.

posts.summary: Julia Evans introduces ninja, a build system she finds refreshingly simple compared to make or bazel. She explains that ninja's entire syntax boils down to just two concepts: rules and builds. The key insight is that ninja files are designed to be generated programmatically, so instead of learning a complex build language, you write a Python script to generate the ninja file. She demonstrates her workflow of converting SVG files to PDFs for her zines, showing how ninja handles incremental builds without the complexity of traditional build systems.
key_points: Ninja's syntax is extremely minimal—just 'rule' (defines a command) and 'build' (maps inputs to outputs)—which makes it easy to learn and remember
The design philosophy of ninja is that build files should be generated by code rather than hand-written, letting you use any programming language you already know instead of learning a build DSL
Traditional build systems like make and bazel are off-putting for small projects because their complexity doesn't match the simplicity of the task
Meson is a higher-level build system that generates ninja files as a backend, used for C/C++/Java/Rust/Fortran projects
money_quotes: In the past I've been pretty put off by using a Build System like make or bazel for my small projects because bazel is this Big Complicated Thing and make feels a little arcane to me.

The magic of ninja is that instead of having to use some confusing Build Language that's hard to remember because you use it so infrequently, instead the ninja language is SUPER simple, and if you want to do something complicated then you can generate the build file you want using any programming language you want.
I find this a lot easier because I know Python, but I can never remember anything about how the make language works.
So for a long time I've just written a bash script or something for my builds and resigned myself to just waiting for a minute sometimes.
I've been using ninja for a few months now. I really like it and it's caused me approximately 0 build-related headaches which feels pretty magical to me.
posts.key_insight: Ninja solves the build system complexity problem by making its language so simple that you generate build files with code you already know, rather than learning yet another arcane build DSL.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares what she's learned about email marketing after overcoming her initial aversion to it. She debunks several myths she held: that selling is adversarial, that marketing email is inherently unwanted, and that you need to trick people into subscribing. Her core realization is that marketing works best when it's about building trust by genuinely helping people, rather than pushing products. She credits the 30x500 business course for reshaping her thinking and describes her 'Saturday Comics' newsletter as an example of marketing that people actually love receiving.
key_points: Selling doesn't have to be adversarial — many people actively want to spend money on things that solve real problems for them
Effective marketing is about building trust by helping people, not describing your product — her Saturday Comics newsletter exemplifies this
You don't need to constantly produce new content for a mailing list; a curated drip of your best existing work can be more helpful than fresh weekly writing
Clear, honest communication about what people are signing up for eliminates unwanted email and dissatisfied customers
Email marketing analytics and optimization are less productive than simply focusing on creating genuinely helpful content
money_quotes: I've learned that actually a lot of people are happy (or even actively want) to spend money on something that will help them solve a problem they have.
Marketing is about building trust by helping people.
If you clearly communicate who your mailing list will help, then people can easily filter themselves in, and the only people on the list will be happy to be on the list.

The point is most people in the world probably haven't already read your best articles, and so if you just send them one article a week from that list, it'll probably actually be MORE helpful to them than if you were writing a new article every week.
When someone is dissatisfied with something they bought, it doesn't always mean that the product is bad. But it definitely means that the person's expectations weren't met.
I'd rather spend my energy on just writing more helpful things for people to read.
The big surprise for me was that email marketing is not a monolithic thing. I have a lot of choices about how to do it.
posts.key_insight: Email marketing works best when you treat it as genuinely helping people rather than selling to them, because building trust through useful content naturally leads to happy customers.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces she's starting another virtual batch at the Recurse Center, a self-directed programming retreat. She explains what RC is—a free, self-directed retreat where you choose what to learn and work on fun projects alongside other programmers. She shares her tentative plans including learning Rails, experimenting with generative neural networks, and possibly building a game, while acknowledging she'll probably abandon these plans as she did last time. She also mentions upcoming zine releases on CSS and bash.
key_points: The Recurse Center is a free, self-directed programming retreat with batches of 1, 6, or 12 weeks, funded by a recruiting business model
Evans plans to shift focus from systems/networking to web development and creative projects like Rails, neural networks, and games
She recognizes the pattern of making plans and not following them, but finds value in the planning process anyway
The virtual format has been carefully designed, and the social structure helps during pandemic isolation
She's also releasing two zines—one on CSS and one on bash written during a self-imposed October challenge
money_quotes: The Recurse Center is a self-directed programming retreat. It's free to attend.
Last time I did RC I had a bunch of plans going in and did not do any of them. I think this time it'll probably be the same but I'll list my ideas anyway.

In my first batch I spent a lot of time on systems / networking stuff because that felt like the hardest thing I could do. This time I feel pretty comfortable with my ability to learn about systems stuff, so I think I'll focus on different topics!
I think it might be fun to have the superpower of being able to build websites quickly.
It turns out that blogging every day is a lot of work!
I wrote another entire zine about bash while I was procrastinating on finishing the CSS zine in a self-imposed 'write an entire zine in October' challenge.
posts.key_insight: Growth as a programmer means recognizing when your comfort zone has expanded and deliberately seeking new challenges in unfamiliar territory.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her new process for getting beta reader feedback on her zines before release. She recruits 10-20 readers from her mailing list, sends them detailed instructions about what kind of feedback she wants (things they personally found confusing, questions they have, things they learned), then consolidates and acts on the feedback. The process helped her identify patterns in confusion, make targeted improvements, and feel more confident about the final product. She emphasizes the importance of being specific about what feedback you want and targeting readers who match your intended audience.
key_points: Recruiting 10-30 beta readers from a subset of her mailing list (about 5% response rate) gave a manageable but useful range of feedback
Being explicit about what kind of feedback you want is the most important step — she specifically asked for things readers personally found confusing, not what they think others might find confusing
Knowing each reader's background made the feedback much more useful for resolving contradictory opinions about what was clear vs confusing
Patterns in feedback were the most actionable signal — when multiple readers were confused about the same thing, it clearly needed to change
When something is genuinely too complex to explain in a small space, the fix is to find a simpler related concept you can explain instead
money_quotes: I find that most people (including me, which is why I'm asking for this feedback!) aren't great at simulating what someone else might find confusing.
I always have this moment of anxiety when I release a new zine like 'OH NO MAYBE THIS IS AWFUL AND NOBODY WILL UNDERSTAND ANYTHING'.
I think the root cause here is that stacking contexts might be actually literally impossible to explain in 100 words / 6 panels.
But then I remembered that I'm a programmer, so I went into the network tab and reloaded the page.
I still felt like I wasn't getting quite enough feedback about which parts of the zine were confusing and needed to be made more clear.
There were a few places where everyone would be confused about the exact same thing. This was SO HELPFUL because it was a clear sign that I definitely needed to change it.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable feedback comes from asking readers to report what they personally found confusing, not what they think others might struggle with, because people are bad at simulating confusion they don't actually feel.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her first day at a Recurse Center batch, where she started a Rails project and encountered a confusing error message. She iterated on her Rails setup seven times before settling on a minimal configuration with Postgres. The core debugging story involves a typo in a validates declaration ('user' instead of 'username') that produced a misleading error about an undefined method on save. She reflects on Rails' 'magic' — both its power in providing built-in session management and authentication primitives, and its downside of producing opaque error messages that obscure root causes.
key_points: Rails' default configuration includes many unnecessary components; stripping them down (skipping sprockets and javascript) made setup faster
A typo in a Rails validates declaration produced an error message pointing at a completely different line of code, making debugging confusing
Rails' built-in magic for sessions, cookies, and user management is surprisingly complete but can feel strange when you don't fully understand what's happening underneath
The official Rails getting started guide and DHH's original 2005 talk are effective learning resources for newcomers
money_quotes: I installed and rm -rf'd Rails maybe 7 times before I was satisfied with it.
I was EXTREMELY confused about what was going on here because I hadn't called a method called user. I'd called .save. What????
I still don't understand how I was supposed to debug this though: the stack trace told me the problem was with the @user.save line, and never mentioned that validates :user thing at all.
The whole point of me playing with Rails is to see how the Rails magic plays out in practice so this was a fun bug to hit early on.
It's definitely kind of strange to already have a session management system and cookies and users without quite knowing what's going on exactly, but it's also kind of fun!
posts.key_insight: Rails' convention-over-configuration magic is powerful but produces opaque error messages that point far from the actual problem, making debugging require understanding the framework's implicit behaviors.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her second day of building a refrigerator poetry forum in Rails, a toy project designed to help her evaluate Rails for small web projects. She describes using jQuery UI's draggable library to let users drag words around, and explores Rails' ORM associations feature for efficiently loading related data. She also highlights Rails scaffolding for code generation and database migrations for schema changes, finding both features surprisingly straightforward and somewhat magical.
key_points: A refrigerator poetry forum cleverly solves the moderation problem by letting users interact without typing free text
Rails associations eliminate the N+1 query problem by letting you preload related records in a single query with minimal configuration
Rails scaffolding and database migrations provide a fast starting point for CRUD operations and schema evolution
jQuery UI draggable (with touch punch for mobile) makes drag-and-drop trivially easy without writing custom JavaScript
money_quotes: But of course if people can actually type on a website that creates all kinds of problems (what if they're spammers? or just mean?).
Rails now knows how to join these two tables even though I didn't tell it what columns to join on!
This is no good though – it's doing a separate SQL query for every post! I knew there was a better way.
Instead of having to go figure out where to add all the code, I could just run: rails generate scaffold Topic title:text
I'll probably delete a lot of it, but it feels kinda nice to have a starting point where I can expand on the parts I want and delete the parts I don't want.
That's it – just run a couple of command line incantations!
It feels pretty magical.
posts.key_insight: Rails' conventions like associations, scaffolding, and migrations dramatically reduce boilerplate by trading configuration for sensible defaults, making it appealing for small web projects.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a quick update from Day 3 at the Recurse Center about building a toy refrigerator poetry forum website. She needed to display all content on a fridge image but the content wouldn't fit, so she devised a CSS technique to create an infinitely tall fridge using three images: a top, a repeatable 1px middle line, and a bottom. The solution is simple HTML/CSS shared via CodePen.
key_points: Creative web design sometimes means solving absurd-sounding problems like making a fridge image infinitely tall
The infinite fridge technique uses three images: top, a 1px repeatable middle strip, and bottom
CSS padding-bottom as a percentage is relative to parent width, not height, enabling fixed aspect ratio boxes
money_quotes: I ran into a problem immediately, which was that the entire website could not fit on said fridge image.
So I figured how to make a fridge that was as tall as I wanted it to be.
"a fridge that is as big as required" didn't have quite the same ring
It's relatively simple and I'm very pleased about this.
The main trick is that padding-bottom is a percentage of the parent element's width, not its height, so you can use it to create a box with a fixed aspect ratio.
posts.key_insight: Whimsical creative constraints like fitting a website onto a fridge image can lead to elegant CSS solutions using simple image-repeating techniques.

posts.summary: Evans explores the challenge of writing simple explanations of complex topics without coming across as condescending. She argues that good simple explanations should include some jargon to give readers search terms, strive for accuracy rather than oversimplification, and use stories and fun visual elements only when the underlying content is genuinely clear. She emphasizes having a specific audience in mind and notes that extreme simplification can become entertainment for experts rather than education for learners. Her core philosophy is that simple explanations work because the ideas themselves often aren't fundamentally complicated.
key_points: Including some jargon in simple explanations gives readers search terms to learn more on their own
Simple explanations should still be true — oversimplified explanations that aren't accurate do a disservice to learners
Fun visual elements and stories should only be attached to content that is genuinely well-written and clear, not used as a veneer over confusing explanations
Writing for a specific audience with assumed baseline knowledge avoids the condescension of explaining basics to people who already know them
Extremely simple language can become a joke for experts rather than a learning tool for beginners, as with XKCD's Up Goer Five
money_quotes: I like to include some jargon in my explanations because otherwise it's impossible for the reader to search & learn more about the concept they're trying to learn about.
Sometimes when I'm trying to write down a simple/clear explanation for a concept, I realize that I don't actually understand the concept as well as I thought and that I'm not able to explain it.
Basically to me 'fun' things are a signal that the content itself is really clear/accessible, and I try to not misuse that signal.
If someone is trying to understand some super technical aspect of containers, they probably understand the basics of containers already!

I really don't think they need to feel condescending though – to me the point of writing a clear/simple explanation is usually that I think the idea is not actually fundamentally that complicated and so I'm just explaining it in a way that's exactly as complicated as it needs to be.
posts.key_insight: The best simple explanations work because the writer believes the idea isn't fundamentally complicated, and presents it at exactly the level of complexity it deserves — with enough jargon for searchability and enough accuracy to be genuinely useful.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her exploration of sketch-rnn, a neural network trained on Google's Quickdraw dataset, to generate drawings of faces during generative art week at the Recurse Center. She walks through getting the model working, refactoring callback-heavy code to use async/await, and building a simple gallery website that draws faces in real time. While the generated faces aren't particularly appealing yet, she identifies the next challenge: teaching the model to distinguish between 'interesting' and 'boring' faces. The post captures her iterative, curiosity-driven approach to learning about RNNs through a concrete creative project.
key_points: sketch-rnn is an RNN trained on the Quickdraw dataset that can generate line drawings, and ml5js provides accessible JavaScript bindings for it
Refactoring callback-heavy example code to async/await made the model much easier to experiment with interactively
The generated faces were uniformly uninteresting, raising the question of whether a neural network can learn aesthetic preferences like 'interesting' vs 'boring'
Performance was notably slow on older hardware due to LSTM computations running in JavaScript
Learning about machine learning models is more effective when driven by a concrete creative question rather than abstract study
money_quotes: I don't really like *any* of these faces, but it's a start!
Can I convince the neural network to distinguish between faces that I think are 'interesting' and 'boring' and maybe only generate more 'interesting' faces?
I still don't really know anything about RNNs, but maybe if I can answer this question I will learn something about them.
I didn't spend a lot of time profiling it, but it seems to spend a lot of time in some function with `lstm` in its name – I don't know what an LSTM is exactly but I know it's a component of an RNN.
People wrote some charming poems and I'm happy with it.
posts.key_insight: Pursuing a specific creative question — like why some generated faces are more interesting than others — is an effective way to bootstrap understanding of a complex ML model you know nothing about.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her approach to tackling programming projects outside her comfort zone: find existing code that works, then incrementally modify it until it does what you want. She emphasizes that the starting code must actually run on your machine, and that the technology must support incremental changes. She contrasts her positive experience with Rails (easy to modify step by step) against neural network code (hard to modify without breaking). The post frames good starting points as magical accelerators for learning new technologies.
key_points: The key to hard projects is starting with existing code that actually works on your machine, not just code that looks relevant
Give up quickly on examples you can't get running and look for more recent alternatives
Some technologies (like Rails) are much easier to incrementally modify than others (like neural network notebooks)
Good starting points that are minimal but functional — like tinywm's 50-line window manager — are invaluable for learning
money_quotes: Find some code on the internet that already does something a little like what I want, then incrementally modify that code until it does what I want, often completely changing everything about the original code in the process.
It's been pretty helpful to me to give up relatively quickly on code that I can't get to work right away and look for another example.
rails new gives you a webserver that does almost nothing, but it works! And then you just need to change it one tiny step at a time into the website you want to build.
I find it pretty easy to find somewhat relevant Jupyter notebooks that do RNN things, and pretty hard to modify those examples to do something closer to what I want.
Finding the right starting point can be hard, but when I find a good one it makes everything so much easier!
posts.key_insight: Hard programming projects become manageable when you start with minimal, working code and incrementally transform it, rather than trying to build from scratch or adapt broken examples.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her early attempts to train a character-level RNN to generate text, following Karpathy's char-rnn approach. She describes the learning curve of working with tensors, debugging dimension mismatches, and discovering that PyTorch's cross entropy loss isn't what she expected. Her model currently produces gibberish instead of coherent text, and she shares the broken code openly. The post captures the messy, iterative reality of learning neural networks from scratch by just trying things and getting confused.
key_points: Tensor dimension mismatches are one of the most common and time-consuming debugging problems when building neural networks
PyTorch's cross entropy loss secretly applies softmax first, which can confuse newcomers who expect identical vectors to produce zero loss
GPU training was literally 10x faster than CPU, making it essential for practical experimentation
Her preferred learning style is banging her head against a problem until very confused, then reading to understand — not starting with courses or books
Sharing broken, non-working code is valuable because it honestly represents the learning process
money_quotes: I decided that since I knew literally nothing about neural networks it would be good to start with something simpler.
I tweeted about this and a bunch of people agreed with me that they have also spent a bunch of time debugging dimension mismatches so at least I'm not alone.
It turns out that in PyTorch the 'cross entropy loss' of x and y isn't actually the cross entropy of x and y.
This makes it seem like it's learned something – those letters are common letters in the English language, sure! It's better than generating zxisqqqqxw.
If you're interested in what some code for an RNN that DOES NOT WORK looks like, here's the code I've written so far.
Unlike his code, it does not generate text that looks vaguely Shakespeare after I train it, it generates gibberish! Maybe tomorrow I will find out why.
For whatever reason I prefer to just bang my head against a problem I'm trying to solve until I get VERY CONFUSED and then resort to reading things that might help me understand what's going on.
It's been pretty fun so far, I know a bunch of things about loss functions that I didn't know before at least, and now I kind of know what a softmax is!
posts.key_insight: Learning neural networks by building something broken and debugging it piece by piece — sharing failures openly — is more honest and instructive than pretending to understand everything from the start.

posts.summary: Julia describes her progress learning RNNs at the Recurse Center by dramatically simplifying her task. After failing to train an RNN to generate Shakespeare-like English, she stepped back to train the same RNN on a trivially simple task: generating repeating sequences of numbers like 0 1 2. This worked, proving her RNN implementation was functional. She shares her PyTorch code and notes that longer sequences take longer to train.
key_points: When a complex ML task fails, simplifying to a trivial case helps verify the implementation works
The same RNN architecture that failed on Shakespeare succeeded on simple counting sequences
Longer sequences predictably take more training time to memorize
Having a working simple case builds confidence to tackle the harder problem again
money_quotes: Yesterday I was trying to train an RNN to generate English that sounds kind of like Shakespeare. That was not working, so today I instead tried to do something MUCH simpler: train an RNN to generate sequences like 0 1 2 0 1 2

I used (I think) the exact same RNN that I couldn't get to work yesterday to generate English by training it on Shakespeare, so it was cool to see that I could at least use it for this much simpler task
I'm super happy that I got an RNN to do something that I actually understand!
I feel pretty hopeful that on Monday I'll be able to go back to the character RNN problem of trying to get the RNN to generate English words now that I have this simpler thing working
posts.key_insight: When a complex problem isn't working, simplifying to a trivial case you fully understand is a powerful debugging and learning strategy.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine 'Hell Yes! CSS!' and shares her personal journey from finding CSS impossibly frustrating to developing real confidence with it. After watching a professional designer work with CSS efficiently and without guesswork, she realized CSS isn't arbitrary — it just has counterintuitive rules that can be learned. She uses the example of position: absolute (which positions relative to the closest positioned parent, not the page) as a case where one key fact unlocked understanding. The zine covers CSS fundamentals and emphasizes that attitude matters as much as knowledge. She also built an interactive CSS examples site as a companion learning resource.
key_points: CSS felt impossible for 15 years because Evans never invested in understanding fundamentals, instead relying on Googling and random changes
Watching a professional designer use CSS revealed that the difference was systematic understanding, not magic — they never made random changes
CSS isn't arbitrary, it's counterintuitive — learning single key facts (like how position: absolute actually works) can unlock entire areas of confidence
Attitude shift from 'CSS BAD' to curious investigation made a huge practical difference in productivity
Standards-compliant CSS benefits from browser backwards compatibility, meaning the investment pays off for years
money_quotes: For almost all of that time, CSS really felt impossible to me – every time I did a seemingly simple task (like the classic "center a div"), I had to Google how to do it, then it wouldn't work the way I expected.
They never made random changes.
CSS isn't arbitrary, it's just counterintuitive sometimes.
I thought it positioned things absolutely on the page (isn't that what the absolute means??), but sometimes I'd see things that made that seem wrong, so I was just confused and sad about it.
Actually taking the time to understand how position: absolute worked didn't take that long (I just needed to learn 1 fact!!) and it meant that now I no longer have to blindly copy paste CSS.
No more throwing spaghetti at the wall and hoping my website eventually looks the way I want!
Did you know that if you write standards-compliant CSS, then browser backwards compatibility will ensure that your website keeps working FOREVER?!
I've changed my attitude towards CSS from "UGH WHY! CSS BAD! I AM SAD!" to "oh, interesting! Why doesn't this work? I can probably figure it out!"
posts.key_insight: CSS isn't arbitrary or impossible — it's counterintuitive, and learning a small number of key fundamentals can transform years of frustrated guessing into confident, systematic problem-solving.

posts.summary: Evans documents her experience debugging why her RNN model couldn't learn to generate Shakespeare-like text after successfully training it to count to three. She discovered two problems: the learning rate was set too high (0.01 instead of 0.002), and she was accidentally resetting the Adam optimizer's state in her training loop. After fixing both issues, the model progressed from generating random characters to producing vaguely English-looking text. Along the way, she provides a concise mathematical explanation of how deep learning training and learning rates work.
key_points: A learning rate that's too high prevents the model from learning anything, as shown by a non-decreasing loss function
The optimizer's internal state matters because it gradually adjusts the learning rate during training, so reinitializing it mid-training destroys progress
Training a neural network involves practical debugging skills similar to regular programming, where small configuration mistakes can completely block progress
Gradient descent learning rates are fundamentally hard to choose, requiring heuristics like Adam rather than exact solutions
money_quotes: machine learning model training is a lot like general continuous function optimization in that finding the "right" step size to do gradient descent is basically impossible so there are a lot of heuristics for picking step learning rates that will work.
By "not learning anything", I mean that the value of the loss function was not going down over time.
I'd written the code this way because I didn't realize that the state of the optimizer ("Adam") was important, so I just reset it sometimes because it seemed convenient at the time.
It turns out that the optimizer's state is very important, I think because it slowly reduces the training rate as training progresses.
It's a big improvement! There are some actual English words in there! "Woman steed and oppet!"
posts.key_insight: Debugging machine learning training often comes down to mundane configuration issues like learning rate tuning and accidentally resetting optimizer state, not algorithmic breakthroughs.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her struggles debugging why an RNN model's loss function climbs instead of converging, while experimenting with back-propagation through time (BPTT). She compares training one character at a time versus doing gradient descent periodically, finding that sequence length significantly affects whether the model trains successfully. The post captures the frustration of slow feedback loops in ML experimentation, where each hypothesis takes 30 minutes to test. She catalogs open questions about gradient clipping, exploding/vanishing gradients, and numerical stability, showing the messy reality of hands-on machine learning work.
key_points: BPTT with a sequence length of 40 causes the model to fail (loss improves then worsens), while a sequence length of 10 works fine, and she doesn't yet understand why
Debugging ML training failures is hard because the feedback loop is slow (30 minutes per experiment) and the failure modes are unintuitive
She's learning to identify vanishing and exploding gradients by graphing gradient norms, but isn't confident in interpreting what she sees
The generated text from both BPTT and non-BPTT models looks similarly nonsensical, suggesting the training approach hasn't yet produced meaningfully different results
money_quotes: Basically, it went down for a while, and then it went back up a LOT. I still have no idea why this happened.
If the optimizer was struggling to optimize, I'd expect the loss to get stuck and plateau and maybe wobble around, but not just keep going up forever.
I've spent way too much time over the last couple of days waiting 30 minutes for a model to train so I can tell if it's working or not.
I need either a faster feedback loop or something to do while I'm waiting.
I think the main reason this is frustrating is that I have a lot of hypotheses I want to experiment with, but it takes so long for every one.
It's only one line of code, but I can't tell if it's helping yet.
posts.key_insight: Debugging ML training failures is uniquely frustrating because the feedback loops are slow, the failure modes are unintuitive, and even basic diagnostics like gradient norms are hard to interpret without experience.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her attempt to implement char-rnn (character-level recurrent neural network) using PyTorch, following Karpathy's famous blog post on RNN effectiveness. She walks through the entire process: preparing training data from Hans Christian Andersen fairy tales, defining an LSTM model, writing training code, and generating text output. While her results don't match Karpathy's quality, she produces text that resembles English and shares the complete ~75 lines of code. She reflects on lessons learned, including that letting PyTorch's built-in LSTM handle backpropagation through time worked better than her manual implementation.
key_points: A character-level RNN can be implemented in roughly 75 lines of PyTorch code, training on fairy tales to generate English-like text
Letting nn.LSTM handle backpropagation through time internally produced better results than manually passing one letter at a time
Temperature controls output diversity: high temperature produces varied but nonsensical text, low temperature produces repetitive but more coherent text
She abandoned fastai's training helper class in favor of writing her own training loop because she couldn't understand what the helper was doing
The model learned to produce English-like text from scratch based purely on character sequences, despite having no concept of words or grammar
money_quotes: I'd never trained a neural network before so this seemed like a fun way to start.
I found that kind of confusing because I didn't understand what it was doing, so I ended up writing my own training code.
This kind of worked, but my loss would go down for a while and then kind of spike later in training. I still don't understand why this happened.
I reran this training code over the same data maybe 300 times, until I got bored and it started outputting text that looked vaguely like English.
It doesn't make any sense, but what did we expect really.
It's weirdly obsessed with beetles and confectioners, and the sun, and the sea. Seems fine!
I got bored with training after an hour and didn't have the patience to babysit the Colab notebook for longer.
posts.key_insight: You can learn neural networks by implementing them from scratch, and even imperfect results that generate vaguely English-like nonsense are genuinely impressive when you understand the model learned everything from individual characters.

posts.summary: Julia documents her attempt to use autoencoders to cluster neural network-generated face sketches. She wants to group Quick Draw faces into clusters so she can identify which groups she likes best and train models on those. She discovers autoencoders as a way to handle clustering sequences of vectors (where k-means won't work), learns the basic encoder-decoder pattern, but ends the day still confused about implementation details. She plans to try a simpler toy example next.
key_points: K-means clustering doesn't work for sequences of vectors, which led to discovering autoencoders as an alternative
Autoencoders work by training an encoder to compress input into a lower-dimensional vector and a decoder to reconstruct the original from that compression
Listing specific unanswered questions (like what relu means, or which vector is the encoding) is a deliberate learning strategy
Starting with a complex dataset when you don't understand the technique is counterproductive — simplifying the data helps clarify the concept
money_quotes: The usual way to do clustering is with k-means or something, but these drawings of faces aren't a single vector, they're a sequence of vectors! So k-means wouldn't make any sense.
I'm still pretty confused about how this encoder / decoder pattern actually works, and I didn't get very far on this today.
As always trying to use a technique I don't understand at all with a complicated dataset is really confusing and demoralizing, I think if I simplify the dataset a LOT it should go better.
Both of the examples I'm looking at use a `relu` function as part of their neural networks. What does `relu` mean?
See if I like the faces in some groups more than others
posts.key_insight: When learning a new technique, simplifying your dataset dramatically is more productive than struggling with a complex one you don't yet understand.

posts.summary: Evans shares her experience debugging a specific question about autoencoders during her Recurse Center batch. She was confused about whether the output or hidden vector from a PyTorch Encoder class represents the encoding. After struggling to find answers in the many deep learning blog posts available, she found clarity by searching PyTorch forums directly. The answer—that the hidden vector is the encoding and you discard the output—unblocked her work significantly.
key_points: The hidden vector (not the output) is the encoding in a PyTorch autoencoder using GRU/RNN layers
The abundance of deep learning blog posts doesn't necessarily mean your specific questions get answered
Searching official community forums (like PyTorch forums) can be more effective than blog posts for precise technical questions
Articulating your specific question clearly is a surprisingly powerful debugging and learning strategy
money_quotes: there are 10 million blogs about deep learning, but somehow I feel like they often don't answer my questions.
It's always surprising to me every single time how helpful it is to focus and articulate the questions I have.
posts.key_insight: Having millions of tutorials available doesn't substitute for clearly articulating your specific question and searching the right venue for the answer.

posts.summary: Evans documents her experiment using an RNN autoencoder to cluster hand-drawn faces from the Google Quick Draw dataset. She encodes each face (represented as a sequence of vectors) into a 50-dimensional vector, then decodes it back, training the network to minimize reconstruction error. The autoencoder learns to reproduce face outlines but fails at interior features. When she clusters the encoded vectors with DBSCAN, the clusters separate mostly by face size rather than facial features, which she notes logically follows from what the autoencoder learned to encode.
key_points: RNN autoencoders compress a sequence of vectors into a single fixed-dimension vector, then reconstruct it, learning a compressed representation in the process
The autoencoder successfully learned to reproduce face outlines and approximate sizes but completely failed at reproducing interior features like eyes and mouths
Clustering on the encoded vectors revealed groupings by face size rather than facial features, directly reflecting what the autoencoder actually learned to encode
The quality of clustering is fundamentally limited by what the autoencoder's latent space captures, demonstrating that poor reconstruction leads to poor downstream representations
money_quotes: The idea is to train the Encoder and Decoder networks so that Decoder(Encoder(x)) is as close to x as possible for every x in the training set.

The training didn't go that well – I got the loss to go down a bit (from 0.3 to 0.25), but it definitely didn't seem that well trained. I think it would have maybe done better if I trained it for longer but I got bored.

I'm actually pretty impressed by this – it seems to have figured how to draw a circle of about the right size for the face, and vaguely that it should draw something inside the circle. Way better than nothing!
It seems to have clustered them mostly by the size of the face, and not by any of the interior features.
This makes sense because the decoder seems to be able to reproduce the size of the face pretty effectively but totally fails at reproducing the interior features.
posts.key_insight: Clustering quality from autoencoder embeddings is directly bounded by what the autoencoder actually learns to reconstruct, so if it only captures coarse features like size, the clusters will only reflect coarse features.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her frustrating experience trying to understand Google Cloud IAM after years of working with AWS IAM. She discovers that GCP's IAM terminology maps confusingly to AWS concepts — a GCP 'IAM role' is like an AWS 'permission,' while a GCP 'service account' is like an AWS 'IAM role.' She finds that granting permissions in GCP requires different approaches depending on the resource type, unlike AWS's unified model. After hitting a wall trying to grant SQL database access at 11:30 PM, she resorts to granting overly broad editor permissions just to get things working.
key_points: GCP IAM terminology is confusingly different from AWS — 'IAM role' means completely different things in each platform
GCP lacks a single unified way to assign permissions; each resource type has its own method for granting access
The Terraform GCP provider has dozens of nearly identical IAM binding resources, generated via ERB-templated Go code
Some GCP resources don't follow the expected gcloud pattern for granting access, like SQL instances
The pragmatic fallback when stuck is granting broad project-level permissions and narrowing later
money_quotes: So "IAM role" in GCP and "IAM role" in AWS have completely different meanings. Cool, that's fine, we can work with that.
At this point it was like 11:30PM so I gave up and granted my service account very broad permissions on the project because I just wanted to get something to work.
I feel like there should be a way to define "here are all the things this service account is allowed to do" in a single place, but I haven't found it yet.
As promised in the section heading, it seems to be a bunch of Go code templated with ERB.
I still don't know the correct way to grant access to a SQL instance but that's a fight for another day.
posts.key_insight: GCP IAM's resource-specific permission model is fundamentally different from AWS's unified approach, requiring different commands and Terraform resources for each type of resource you want to grant access to.

posts.summary: Julia Evans breaks down her process for creating programming comics into three categories: surprising/hidden facts, lists of important things, and relatable stories. She argues that drawing skill is not the hard part — the real challenge is identifying what's genuinely surprising or useful to explain. She describes two sources for surprising facts: things she recently learned herself, and things she observes others finding surprising. She emphasizes that she only writes comics about topics she knows well, because distilling a topic to its 3 most important facts requires deep understanding, not surface familiarity.
key_points: The hard part of programming comics isn't drawing — it's identifying what's genuinely surprising or important to explain
Three types of effective programming comics: surprising/hidden facts, curated lists of important things, and relatable stories
Surprising facts come from two sources: things you recently learned yourself, and observing what others find surprising when you explain things to them
Lists work because they're not just any facts about X, but the most important facts — curation is the value
Writing short, distilled explanations is harder than writing long ones and requires deep knowledge of the topic
money_quotes: I'm actually very bad at drawing, and I've made comics that people love with very simple tools.
A key thing about this is that it's not just 'a list of facts about X' (anyone can make a list!) but a list of the most important facts about X.
I think the skill of 'figuring out what people typically find surprising when learning a topic and coming up with clear explanations' is probably called 'teaching'.
I find writing short things a LOT harder than writing long things.

If I'm still a little bit confused about the topic, it's very hard to definitively list 'Here are the 3 most important surprising facts you need to know to understand TOPIC', because very likely I actually don't know those 3 facts yet!

Obviously I don't think everyone needs to have a tech blog, but I do think that noticing surprising computer facts as you learn them and explaining them clearly is a skill that you need to practice if you want to get good at it!
posts.key_insight: The real skill behind effective programming comics isn't drawing but teaching — identifying what people find genuinely surprising and distilling complex topics down to their most important facts.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience setting up a Rails deployment on a Digital Ocean VM after getting frustrated with GCP's IAM complexity. She explores Digital Ocean's app platform and marketplace VM images, sets up push-to-deploy using git post-receive hooks, and learns about systemd's ExecStartPre for running pre-deployment scripts. She encounters a confusing journald logging issue that was fixed by restarting the service. The post captures the pragmatic joy of hacking together a simple deployment system rather than over-engineering it.
key_points: Digital Ocean's app platform is Heroku-compatible but too restrictive for projects needing SSH access to other instances
Git post-receive hooks provide a simple push-to-deploy workflow without needing a full CI/CD platform
systemd's ExecStartPre directive can run pre-deployment scripts like bundle install and database migrations before a service starts
Digital Ocean marketplace images provide reasonable pre-configured stacks (nginx, Rails, Postgres, systemd) as deployment starting points
Sometimes a hacked-together bash script deployment is more fun and practical than a fancy stable system
money_quotes: I got mad because of my experience with IAM on Friday and decided to try Digital Ocean instead.
It turns out that Digital Ocean is MUCH EASIER to use than GCP (at least at first) and so I decided to try it out.

This approach feels more like "hacked together bash script" than "fancy stable system" but after spending some time wrangling systemd I got it to work and it's kind of fun to have a more basic system where I push directly to the VM I'm running my code on.
I spent a LOT of time being confused because systemd-journald didn't have my Rails app's logs, even though it seemed to be running correctly.
Eventually I restarted journald and it fixed everything, but I don't really understand why.
I found setting up a VM in a very non-reproducible way with a hacked together build/deploy system pretty fun.
posts.key_insight: A simple, hacked-together deployment using git hooks and systemd on a plain VM can be more fun and practical than wrestling with complex cloud platforms.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience setting up OAuth login in a Rails project using Devise and OmniAuth. She followed tutorials without fully understanding them, which mostly worked for GitHub login but broke when implementing a custom OAuth provider. She discovered two key bugs: misunderstanding that access_token.get('/me') makes an HTTP request rather than a hashmap lookup, and needing the X-FORWARDED-PROTO header set to https for correct redirect URIs. She ends by questioning whether Devise is even necessary.
key_points: Following tutorials without understanding them can get you surprisingly far in Rails, but breaks down when you need to customize beyond the tutorial's scope
The access_token.get('/me') call in OmniAuth is an HTTP request to the OAuth provider's API, not a hashmap lookup — a misunderstanding that blocked the custom OAuth integration
When behind a reverse proxy like Cloudflare, Rails needs the X-FORWARDED-PROTO header to generate https URLs correctly for OAuth redirect URIs
After getting everything working with Devise, she considers removing it entirely in favor of using OmniAuth directly — a common pattern of simplifying after understanding the problem
money_quotes: But one nice thing about Rails is that there are about 10 billion blog posts & Stack Overflow questions that make it possible to figure out what's going on even if you don't really know anything.
I followed all the steps without really understanding them and it kind of worked! Hooray.
I had this vague notion that once the OAuth server sent my application an access token, it would be "done".
which I thought meant "get the /me field from a hashmap called access_token" but which actually means "make an HTTP request to the OAuth provider's API using the access token, and get the path /me".
And devise seems pretty complicated, so now that I have everything working I'm considering totally removing it and instead just using Omniauth.
posts.key_insight: Following tutorials without understanding them works until you need to customize, at which point the bugs you encounter become the actual learning moments.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience discovering Rails testing tools while building an app to manage virtual machines for programming puzzles. She describes a common pattern of starting without tests, struggling with manual testing, then finally adding automated tests. She highlights several Rails testing features she found helpful: auto-generated test scaffolds, fixtures for test data, Devise authentication helpers, WebMock for HTTP request mocking, and easy integration tests. She appreciates Rails' mature ecosystem and extensive documentation.
key_points: Rails scaffolding auto-generates test templates, lowering the barrier to writing tests
Fixtures, authentication helpers, and HTTP mocking make Rails testing surprisingly approachable for newcomers
Integration tests in Rails require very little code to verify multi-step user flows
Rails' massive community means most testing questions have already been answered many times over
The recurring pattern of skipping tests, struggling with manual testing, then finally automating is a common developer experience
money_quotes: 2 days later, realize I'm spending a lot of time manually testing and fixing the same bug 3 times because I broke it again
This was really nice because it gave me a template to start from
I obviously don't actually want to launch VM instances in my tests (could get expensive!)
This code definitely has a "magical Ruby" feel but I don't really mind and it's fun to write so far.

It really feels like if I'm confused about something, many thousands of people have been confused about the exact same thing and have written about it in 1000 different places, which is a nice change of pace from some of the weirder things I've tried to learn about.
posts.key_insight: Rails' built-in testing infrastructure and massive community documentation make automated testing surprisingly easy to adopt, even for someone who habitually forgets to write tests.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares slides from a 10-minute talk at Recurse Center about common blogging myths that discourage people from writing, including beliefs that you need to be original, expert, or always correct. She also describes a debugging technique of adding print statements directly to library code to understand confusing errors. The post is brief and practical, combining encouragement for aspiring bloggers with a concrete debugging tip.
key_points: Common blogging myths (needing originality, expertise, perfection) unnecessarily discourage people from writing
Adding print statements directly to library source code is an underused but effective debugging technique
90% of the time the bug is in your own code, and reading library internals helps you understand how the library actually works
Languages with accessible dependency directories (node_modules, site-packages) make this technique especially easy
money_quotes: Sometimes when I'm debugging, I'm using a library, and I'm getting an error, and I have NO IDEA why and all the Googling in the world just does not tell me anything useful.
90% of the time the problem is with my code and it just helps me understand how the library is actually supposed to work.

This works really well with Ruby / Node / Python / Go – anywhere where it's normal to have a directory like node_modules with all the code for all your dependencies where you can easily edit & rerun the code.
posts.key_insight: When stuck on a library error, adding print statements directly to the library's source code is a simple but powerful way to understand what's actually happening.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her 2020 accomplishments, which included publishing four zines (CSS, Bash, SQL, and Containers), experimenting with interactive learning tools, shipping physical zines, running !!Con remotely, and growing her business. Despite the pandemic, she found her revenue grew 2.3x, largely because she doubled her zine output. She experimented with companion websites and a questions-based learning tool, discovering that framing matters—rewarding learning rather than correct answers changed how people engaged. She also reflects on what worked well, including beta readers, collaboration, and adopting organizational tools like Trello and Focusmate.
key_points: Published 4 zines with an accidental 'weird programming languages' theme (Bash, CSS, SQL) plus Containers, and created companion interactive websites for two of them
Redesigned the questions/flashcards learning tool to reward 'I learned something!' instead of correct answers, fundamentally changing user engagement
Shipping 400 physical zines was rewarding but labor-intensive, taking 2-3 days of packaging, and letter mail caused delivery issues with no tracking
Business revenue grew 2.3x year-over-year, strongly correlated with doubling zine output
Ran Domain Name Saturday at Recurse Center where writing a DNS server in one day proved impossible, but 'bug presentations' at the end were a hit
money_quotes: you can't really learn about computers without trying things!
So you don't get rewarded for already knowing the thing, you get rewarded for learning something new.
It turns out it's impossible to write a DNS server in 1 day (literally nobody succeeded, but I think it might be possible in 2 days).
I basically wrote the CSS zine because we did this project and I was so amazed and delighted by what Melody could do with CSS that I had to write a zine about CSS.
Revenue is up 2.3x over last year, which is incredible and probably very related to the fact that I published twice as many zines.
I continue to enjoy being able to easily understand what value the work I'm doing brings to the world and having a lot of control over my time. I still miss having coworkers though :)
I think it really worked well as a remote conference because !!Con (unlike a lot of conferences) really is largely about the talks and about the joy of watching a lot of delightful talks together.
I started using Trello to track what needs to be done for my zines instead of literally no organizational system at all and it really helped a lot.
posts.key_insight: The most effective learning tools reward discovery and experimentation rather than testing existing knowledge, and creative output scales surprisingly linearly with effort invested.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her positive experience using Docker Compose to set up a local development environment for a Ruby on Rails project with multiple services. She walks through her docker-compose.yml configuration, explains how Docker Compose networking works (including digging into the internal DNS server and iptables rules), and covers practical tips like persisting database files and Rails console history. She contrasts the simplicity of Docker Compose with her previous failed attempt using Puppet and Vagrant, and briefly discusses its viability for small-scale production use.
key_points: Docker Compose makes it easy to run multiple interconnected services locally with a single short YAML configuration file
Docker Compose networking uses an internal DNS server (run by dockerd) with iptables port forwarding to let containers reference each other by name
Storing Postgres data in a mounted temp directory avoids the hassle of managing a local database installation
Docker Compose is a good fit for simple setups but has limitations with large microservice architectures, huge databases, and Mac performance due to VM overhead
Practical workarounds like persisting IRB history to a mounted volume solve container ephemerality annoyances
money_quotes: I'm also only talking about using Docker Compose for a dev environment here, not using it in production.
I spent two days getting frustrated with trying to set up a dev environment with other tools and Docker Compose was a lot easier and simpler.
If I have a Rails server running in my rails_server container on port 3000, then I can access that with http://rails_server:3000. So simple!

I really do not want to administer a Postgres installation on my laptop (I don't really know how to configure Postgres), and conceptually I like having my dev database literally be in the same directory as the rest of my code.
My first guess for 'this server seems to be running on port X but I'm accessing it on port Y, what's going on?' was 'iptables'.

I spent an entire day before this trying to configure a dev environment by using Puppet to provision a Vagrant virtual machine only to realize that VMs are kind of slow to start and that I don't really like writing Puppet configuration.
The whole point of Docker Compose is that it's super simple and Kubernetes is certainly not simple.
posts.key_insight: Docker Compose provides a surprisingly simple way to set up multi-service dev environments, with built-in DNS-based networking that makes container-to-container communication trivial.

posts.summary: Evans describes two improvements she made to her puzzle game project during a Recurse Center batch. First, she deployed her Docker Compose setup to production on a DigitalOcean droplet, finding it easier than expected and more robust than her previous systemd-based approach. Second, she switched her Puzzle model from database-backed ActiveRecord to ActiveHash, which stores data in a file/hashmap instead of a database table, making it much easier to edit her small set of puzzles.
key_points: Deploying Docker Compose to production on a single droplet was easier than expected and felt more robust than manually managing systemd files
ActiveHash lets you define Rails model data in a file instead of a database table, which works well for small, frequently-edited datasets
Storing puzzle definitions in the database made editing too slow through a web interface and caused dev/prod sync problems
ActiveHash preserves enough ActiveRecord-compatible methods (like Puzzle.find) that switching required minimal code changes
The main risk with ActiveHash is manually managing IDs that other database tables reference via foreign keys
money_quotes: This is pretty far from "just click one thing and you're done" but I feel like if I had to do it again because I lost my production server it wouldn't be too bad.
It's definitely much better than "just create a lot of systemd files by hand" which is what I was doing before.
This really wasn't working for me because I needed to make a lot of updates to the puzzles (at least to start) and having to do it through a web interface felt way too slow.
This feels good for now because I only have like 6 puzzles and so having them all in a file makes it way easier to edit them.
And because I'm using ActiveRecord methods, if I ever want to switch back to using a database to manage them, it should be pretty easy!
posts.key_insight: For small, frequently-edited datasets, storing model data in files rather than a database can dramatically simplify the development workflow while preserving the option to switch back later.

posts.summary: Julia recounts a day at the Recurse Center where she debugged a Rails login issue through pair programming and discovered several useful git tricks along the way. A missing database column was quickly tracked down using 'git log -S', which searches for commits that changed the count of a string. She also discovered the 'delta' tool for beautiful terminal diffs and learned about 'git log --patch'. The post also covers using Python's pathlib module and a working directory context manager for a cloud-init YAML generation script.
key_points: Pair programming can rapidly transform frustrating bugs into quick fixes by bringing fresh perspective
'git log -S' finds commits that changed the count of a string's occurrences, which is different from 'git log -G' that searches diffs directly
The 'delta' tool provides beautiful side-by-side diffs in the terminal as a drop-in git pager
'git log --patch' shows diffs inline with log output, eliminating the need to manually copy commit IDs for 'git show'
Python's pathlib provides a clean interface for path manipulation and file reading
money_quotes: I'm always amazed by how quickly pairing can turn 'UGH THIS IS TERRIBLE' into solving the problem really quickly.
I ran 'git log -S provider' to find every commit that had added/removed the string 'provider' and Mikkel was like WHAT IS THAT??? Pairing is magic!
I learned that 'git log -S' doesn't actually search diffs to find diffs containing the string, instead it just counts occurrences of the string and reports when the count was changed.
I've been copying and pasting commit IDs from 'git log' and doing 'git show COMMIT_ID' to look at a diff for like 7 years.
It always brings me a lot of joy when I think of a little bit of simple code that I want to exist and I can kind of just summon it with a Google search.
posts.key_insight: Pair programming is a superpower for debugging because sharing context with another person can collapse hours of frustration into minutes of productive problem-solving.

posts.summary: Julia documents her day spent learning qemu to automatically test cloud-init.yaml files locally instead of spinning up DigitalOcean VMs. She first tried virt-install and Vagrant's experimental cloud-init support, both of which failed. She eventually succeeded by using qemu directly, finding it surprisingly simple once she bypassed the abstraction layers. She shares a complete, working script for starting an Ubuntu image with cloud-init and qemu, while noting several things she still doesn't understand about the underlying mechanics.
key_points: qemu is like ffmpeg — a powerful CLI tool with a million arguments, created by the same person (Fabrice Bellard), that can accomplish anything but is nontrivial to learn
Abstraction layers like virt-install and Vagrant's experimental cloud-init support were harder to get working than using qemu directly
The working solution was surprisingly simple: download an image, package cloud-init data, create a qcow2 snapshot, and run qemu-system-x86_64
Being transparent about what you still don't understand (like qcow2 internals) is a natural part of the learning process
money_quotes: qemu is a command line tool in a similar style to ffmpeg, with a million command line arguments that you can use to accomplish literally anything (which means it's very nontrivial to learn and is also an incredible tool).
The reason qemu is like ffmpeg is that they're by the same person, Fabrice Bellard.
Here's what I know about virt-install: 1. it's part of a package called libvirt 2. libvirt is some sort of abstraction over qemu and other virtualization providers 3. I can't get it to work

I spent a bunch of time trying to get various things using virt-install to work – I added myself to the libvirt group, installed some more packages, tried some random commands related to bridges that I found on the internet to get the networking to work, and got very frustrated eventually gave up.
This felt way simpler to me than all virt-install examples I found.
how exactly is my qcow2 "snapshot" of the Focal image is only 11MB? I expected the snapshot to make a copy of the data and be like 500MB.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the direct, lower-level tool is easier to use than the abstraction layers built on top of it, because the abstractions introduce their own complexity and failure modes.

posts.summary: Julia Evans posts a brief note explaining that she has been writing daily blog posts about her time at the Recurse Center but has removed them from her main RSS feed. She feels the daily posts are more stream-of-consciousness than her typical polished writing. She provides links to find the posts in a dedicated category and a separate RSS feed for those interested in following along.
key_points: Evans separates her daily RC posts from her main blog feed to maintain quality expectations for regular subscribers
She characterizes the daily posts as more stream-of-consciousness compared to her usual writing
She provides alternative ways to follow the RC content via category page and dedicated RSS feed
money_quotes: I took them out of this RSS feed because I think they're a bit more stream of consciousness than my usual posts.
posts.key_insight: Separating exploratory, unpolished writing from a curated feed lets you share learning-in-progress without diluting your main body of work.

posts.summary: Julia describes debugging an unreliable VM launch process for her programming puzzles. The root cause was that SSH becoming available didn't mean cloud-init had finished running, so her setup scripts would sometimes fail due to missing dependencies. She fixed this by waiting for cloud-init's result.json file before proceeding. She also wrote a local testing script using qemu that made iteration much faster.
key_points: SSH being available on a VM doesn't mean cloud-init has finished running, which was causing intermittent setup failures
cloud-init creates /var/lib/cloud/data/result.json when complete, providing a reliable readiness signal
Using ssh -o UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null suppresses known_hosts warnings when testing with ephemeral VMs
Writing a local qemu testing script reduced the iteration cycle dramatically compared to testing in production
Bash is a good fit for scripts with simple logic that primarily orchestrate other processes
money_quotes: just because ssh is running, it doesn't mean that the cloud-init is done running!
I don't know why it took me so long to remember this, but – just because ssh is running, it doesn't mean that the cloud-init is done running!
I felt like this was a nice example of a good place for a bash script because: the logic is very simple, it needs to run a bunch of processes, and I'm the only person using it
It takes about a minute to boot a VM and it made it WAY WAY WAY faster to iterate on changes.
I've gotten a bit better at bash recently by writing the bash zine and I used some of my newfound bash knowledge here
posts.key_insight: Intermittent infrastructure failures often come from assuming one service being ready means all initialization is complete, when in reality you need explicit completion signals.

posts.summary: Julia Evans discovers server-sent events (SSE) as a simpler alternative to websockets for streaming updates from a server to a client. She explains that SSE is just regular HTTP with a text/event-stream content type and a dead-simple wire protocol. While implementing SSE with Rails behind nginx, she hits two surprising bugs: nginx defaulting to HTTP/1.0 for upstream connections (which breaks long-lived connections), and Rack's ETag middleware buffering the entire response before sending. Both bugs were solved with help from Stack Overflow, reinforcing that weird computer interactions are often well-documented by others who hit the same problems.
key_points: Server-sent events are just HTTP requests with a keep-alive connection and text/event-stream content type, requiring no special server libraries
Unlike websockets, SSE only allows server-to-client communication, but the browser's EventSource API automatically reconnects if the connection drops
Nginx defaults to HTTP/1.0 for upstream proxying, which silently breaks SSE connections after the first event
Rack's ETag middleware buffers entire responses to compute hashes, defeating the purpose of streaming — setting Last-Modified bypasses it
Stack Overflow solutions that seem unrelated to your problem sometimes turn out to be exactly right, but you should understand why before applying them
money_quotes: I was SO DELIGHTED to learn that they're just HTTP requests.
The wire protocol is really simple (just set event: and data: and maybe id: and retry: if you want), so you don't need any fancy server libraries to implement server-sent events.

I think the reason why it worked if I didn't pause between sending the 2 events was basically that the server was racing with the client to send the second part of the response before the connection closed.
I expected it to return 1 event per second for 10 seconds, but instead it waited 10 seconds and returned 10 events all at once. That's not how we want streaming to work!
Sometimes computers interact in weird ways, other people have experienced computers interacting in the exact same weird ways in the past, and Stack Overflow sometimes has answers about why.
I do think it's important to not just randomly try things from Stack Overflow.
It's always such a delight to learn about a new easy-to-use web technology that I'd never heard of.
posts.key_insight: Server-sent events offer a dramatically simpler alternative to websockets for server-to-client streaming, though real-world deployment behind proxies like nginx introduces subtle bugs from protocol version mismatches and middleware buffering.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents setting up a new ThinkPad T14s laptop, sharing her pragmatic approach to OS migrations: tar the entire home directory, reinstall Ubuntu from scratch, and manually curate packages. She also switched from stimulus.js to Vue.js for frontend work and describes ongoing issues with her Go proxy server setup for SSH connections to VMs.
key_points: Her OS migration strategy centers on copying the entire home directory via tar and always reinstalling Ubuntu fresh rather than upgrading in place
She manually curates which packages to reinstall rather than blindly restoring everything, filtering out accumulated cruft
She switched from stimulus.js to Vue.js because she wanted declarative conditional rendering instead of manually toggling CSS classes
Her SSH proxy architecture using gotty processes spawned per-connection is messy and unreliable, motivating a rewrite
money_quotes: I never upgrade Ubuntu, I always reinstall from scratch. (I tried breaking this rule and upgrading Ubuntu this year and it failed very spectacularly so I'm even more personally committed to this rule :) )
The `pv` is important to me because it takes a long time and I get impatient so it's nice to have a progress indicator.
I'm a bit mad that it has soldered RAM and that in general it's harder to service myself but I went with it anyway.
I don't claim that this is the Best Way to do things, it's just what I do and it works ok for me so far.

I find this whole thing pretty messy and it's also quite unreliable, the gotty processes sometimes don't quite start up right and I'm always worried about them dying and I have no real way to manage them.
posts.key_insight: A simple, repeatable OS migration strategy—tar the home directory, reinstall fresh, manually curate packages—beats more sophisticated approaches by being reliable and understandable.

posts.summary: Julia describes modifying gotty, a Go webserver that puts terminals on websites, to handle multiple websocket connections simultaneously instead of running separate processes per session. Her previous setup required coordinating three different pieces (multiple gotty processes, a proxy server, and a Rails server), which was fragile and flaky. By pairing with another Recurser, they forked gotty and wrote a simple HTTP handler that routes requests by session ID to the right command, replacing the complex multi-process architecture with a single Go program. The solution turned out to be much simpler than expected.
key_points: Gotty only supports one session at a time, forcing a fragile multi-process architecture with separate gotty instances, a proxy server, and a Rails coordinator
The fix was surprisingly simple: a single HTTP handler with an if statement that routes websocket connections to different commands based on URL path
Go's http.ResponseWriter and http.Request interfaces make it easy to wrap and modify existing HTTP handlers
They had to fork gotty rather than use it as a library because the needed functions were private (lowercase in Go)
Consolidating three moving pieces into one Go program eliminated the flakiness of the original setup
money_quotes: I was originally worried that it would be really complicated to modify gotty to handle multiple websocket connections but actually it was very straightforward!
