Because all Go HTTP handlers take a http.ResponseWriter and a http.Request, it's super easy to wrap other HTTP request handlers and make them behave slightly differently.

We forked gotty instead of using it as a library because the functions we needed were private, you can tell because they're lowercase and in Go the way you know if a function is private or public is based on whether it starts with an uppercase or lowercase letter.
I paired on this with Chetan and it was SO MUCH easier to do with someone else than on my own.
Having everything coordinated in 1 simple Go program instead of having 3 different moving pieces to coordinate works SO MUCH BETTER.
I really thought this would be super hard and it wasn't – it was really helpful to talk it through with another person and we got the main functionality working in just 1 hour!
posts.key_insight: Complex-looking problems often have simple solutions, especially when you pair with someone else and look at the actual code instead of assuming it will be hard.

posts.summary: Julia documents her process of customizing the terminal colors in gotty, a web-based terminal tool, by finding and integrating HTerm themes from Blink Shell's open source theme collection. She modified gotty's JavaScript to inject Solarized theme preferences, though the result didn't perfectly match her local terminal. She also improved her deployment workflow by switching from building Docker images on the server to pulling pre-built images from GitHub's container registry.
key_points: Gotty uses HTerm as its terminal, and Blink Shell's open source theme collection provides compatible color themes
Customizing gotty's terminal colors required modifying gotty.js directly since the theme variable wasn't available in external HTML
The Solarized theme looked different in gotty than in a native terminal, possibly due to shell differences between fish and bash
Moving Docker image builds from the server to a registry dramatically improved deployment speed and reliability
money_quotes: I couldn't find any explanation on the internet of how to do this, so here's what I did.
I've never really understand shell colors so maybe this will be the reason I finally learn.
This took a long time like things like this always do but it was pretty boring so I don't have much more to say about it.
I also wasted a bunch of hours trying to build the images in CI but in the end I decided to just build them on my laptop because I couldn't get Docker image caching to work with github actions.
It's way faster and also feels like it's going to be more reliable.
posts.key_insight: Customizing web-based terminal tools often requires digging into their JavaScript internals, and the results may not perfectly match native terminal rendering due to subtle differences in how shells handle colors.

posts.summary: Julia investigates whether her new ThinkPad laptop's screen is causing daily headaches, learning about pulse width modulation (PWM) as a backlight dimming technique that some people are sensitive to. She discovers the 'panel lottery' phenomenon where Lenovo ships different display panels in the same laptop model without telling buyers. She takes slow-motion videos of three different laptops to compare flickering behavior and identifies her panel model using Linux commands. Despite finding her panel has a 980 Hz PWM frequency (considered high enough that most sensitive people shouldn't notice), she remains unsure if PWM is the actual cause of her headaches.
key_points: Some laptop screens use pulse width modulation (PWM) to dim the backlight by rapidly flickering on and off, which can cause headaches in sensitive people
Lenovo engages in a 'panel lottery' where the same laptop model may ship with different display panels that have meaningfully different characteristics
You can identify your Linux laptop's display panel by running 'strings /sys/class/drm/card0-eDP-1/edid'
Slow-motion video can reveal screen flickering patterns not visible to the naked eye, though interpreting the results isn't straightforward
money_quotes: I've had a headache every day this week since I got a new laptop which has been pretty unpleasant.
Apparently the way some screens do screen dimming is to rapidly flicker the screen on/off.
Another thing I learned which was surprising to me is that Lenovo won't tell you which exact monitor panel you get when you order a laptop from them.
I thought a screen was called a screen but everyone on the internet talking about laptop/TV screens seem to call them 'panels' so I guess I'll say 'panel' for the rest of this post.

That seems kind of high (900 times a second is fast!) and that site says 'The frequency of 980.4 Hz is quite high, so most users sensitive to PWM should not notice any flickering.' So I'm still not quite sure if this is the reason I have a headache.
posts.key_insight: Debugging a physical discomfort problem follows the same systematic approach as debugging software: gather information, form hypotheses, collect evidence, and iterate when results are ambiguous.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her journey to understand what a network bridge is, motivated by needing to configure Firecracker VM networking. She explains that a bridge is a layer 2 ethernet device that routes packets to attached network interfaces based on MAC addresses, and that containers use ARP to discover MAC addresses just like physical networks. She walks through the specific steps needed to get internet access from inside a VM: creating a tap device, attaching it to a bridge, configuring route tables on both host and guest, and adding SNAT/iptables rules. Despite not fully understanding every detail, she successfully gets her Firecracker VM connected to the internet.
key_points: A bridge is a layer 2 (ethernet) network device that forwards packets to attached interfaces based on MAC address matching, or broadcasts to all interfaces if no match is found
Containers and VMs use ARP to discover MAC addresses even though all the information exists on the same physical computer, essentially pretending to be a physical network

Getting VM internet access requires three key configurations: host route table entries directing subnet traffic to the bridge, a gateway setting inside the VM pointing to the bridge IP, and an SNAT iptables rule on the host
The 'blindly copy and paste' approach to networking configuration doesn't work when you can't debug failures, which forces actual understanding
money_quotes: I've been avoiding understanding what a bridge is for years because it seemed confusing, but I copied and pasted some things from Github issues / various blog posts and none of them did what I wanted.
So I figured the 'blindly copy and paste' approach wasn't really going to work and decided OKAY TODAY IS THE DAY I WILL LEARN WHAT A BRIDGE IS.

I think this is really funny because – all of this information is on the same computer and it doesn't feel like it should need to use ARP to look up MAC addresses, like it has all the information already! But I guess it just literally pretends like it's a physical network.
A bridge is a layer 2 (ethernet) device that can have an arbitrary number of network interfaces attached to it.
I'm still not 100% on bridges but I did manage to configure a bridge so that when I ssh into my Firecracker VM I can access the internet! I am extremely delighted by this.
Some things in this post are almost certainly wrong.
posts.key_insight: Container and VM networking relies on bridges that simulate physical network behavior using layer 2 devices, ARP, route tables, and NAT rules—and understanding these components is necessary when copy-pasting configurations inevitably fails.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her progress building an SSH game project using Firecracker VMs. She shares three accomplishments: understanding why Linux bridges are confusing (they're actually two things bundled together), fixing slow Ubuntu VM boot times caused by systemd entropy generation, and writing a Go HTTP server to manage Firecracker VM lifecycles. The post captures a day of hands-on systems work with honest accounts of confusion and pragmatic workarounds.
key_points: Linux bridges are confusing because they bundle two things: an actual bridge (like a switch) and a network interface with an IP address, which switches don't have
Ubuntu Firecracker VMs were slow to boot due to systemd's random seed service; after six failed fix attempts, setting a 2-second timeout worked
She wrote a simple Go HTTP API to create and destroy Firecracker VMs, largely by adapting code from the firectl command-line tool
The post demonstrates a pragmatic approach to systems work: accepting imperfect solutions and moving forward
money_quotes: Every single time I say I'm confused about bridges someone will tell me "well, you see julia, a bridge is like a virtual switch". This has never made any sense to me because I've never used a switch either.
But switches don't have IP addresses! So if "a bridge is like a switch", what's going on?
When you create a bridge on Linux by default, you actually get 2 things: a bridge (the kind that's "like a switch") and a network interface with the same name as the bridge, which has an IP address.
I feel kind of vindicated in disbelieving this "a bridge is like a switch" explanation because I guess it's technically true but it's definitely missing a key piece of information.

It's extremely possible that I actually need this entropy generation for some reason (maybe to give sshd enough entropy so that it can generate session keys securely?) but I'll cross that bridge when I come to it.
So now I can start an Ubuntu virtual machine in like 5 seconds! It's really amazing.
posts.key_insight: Common technical explanations like 'a bridge is like a switch' often fail because they omit crucial implementation details that would actually help people understand the concept.

posts.summary: Julia documents her struggles building Ubuntu VM images for Firecracker, learning about disk image manipulation, kernel compilation, and base image selection along the way. She discovered that compiling the Linux kernel is surprisingly fast, taking only about 5 minutes. She tried both Ubuntu cloud images and Docker images as bases for her VMs, finding problems with each approach. The cloud image wouldn't boot due to a UEFI-related issue, while the Docker image boots but lacks essential system components.
key_points: Disk image manipulation involves tools like sfdisk, dd, and sparse file handling with fallocate
Compiling a Linux kernel from source takes only about 5 minutes with parallel builds, far less than the expected 2 hours
Ubuntu cloud images and Docker images each have distinct problems when used as Firecracker VM bases
Docker images lack basic system components like init and udev since they aren't designed for full VM use
money_quotes: It only took me like 5 minutes to compile a Linux kernel from scratch (with `make -j12` on an AMD Ryzen 5). I was really surprised by this, I thought it would take like 2 hours.
The Firecracker instructions suggest building your own Linux kernel from source. This seemed intimidating to me because I hadn't done it before but it was actually totally fine!
Some Linux files are "sparse" which means that regions which are filled with 0s are collapsed. Very useful for disk images.
it's not really designed to be a system to log into so I feel like it's missing a lot of things
I spent all day yesterday trying to build Ubuntu VM images that work with Firecracker without too much success.
posts.key_insight: Building Linux kernels from source is surprisingly fast and accessible, but assembling a working VM image from existing Ubuntu artifacts is harder than expected because cloud images and Docker images serve different purposes than a Firecracker VM.

posts.summary: Evans documents her progress building VMs with Firecracker, pivoting from cloud-init to Docker for building VM images after cloud-init proved too slow. She converts Docker container filesystems to ext4 images that boot in Firecracker, which is both simpler and faster than her previous approach. She also figures out Docker Compose networking to put VMs and containers on the same bridge. After getting frustrated building her own VM service, she discovers fly.io, which does essentially the same Docker-to-Firecracker conversion she built, and gets VMs running there using their undocumented Go API.
key_points: Docker container filesystems can be extracted and converted to ext4 images for use as Firecracker VM root filesystems, which is simpler than cloud-init
Docker Compose allows explicit bridge naming via driver_opts, enabling VMs and containers to share the same network bridge
fly.io uses the same Docker-to-Firecracker approach rather than running a container runtime inside the VM like Fargate or Kata Containers
When APIs aren't documented, reading the source code of open-source CLI tools can serve as effective documentation
money_quotes: I really wanted the VM to boot in less than 2 seconds.
So I decided to instead build my containers with Docker, convert the Docker filesystem to an ext4 image, and then start that image in Firecracker.
Actually building my VMs with Docker feels a lot simpler than doing it with cloud-init.yaml, I think they might be easier to develop this way.
It turns out that fly.io supports a Docker container interface, but they actually just unpack the Docker container's files, convert it into an ext4 filesystem, and boot a Firecracker VM with it.

I'd initially assumed it was a container because Fargate and Kata Containers both run containers images in VMs by putting a container runtime inside the Firecracker VM and running the container that way.
Their command line management tool is open source so I could just read the source for flyctl to figure out how it worked.
posts.key_insight: Docker's container image format serves as a surprisingly effective and simple build system for Firecracker VM filesystems, bypassing the complexity and slowness of cloud-init.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience learning to use Firecracker, AWS's open-source microVM technology, for a personal project: a CTF-like game where each player gets their own virtual machine. She explains why she chose VMs over containers (needing full root access for sysctls, iptables, perf), why Firecracker's sub-second boot time solves her problem compared to DigitalOcean VMs (60s) or qemu (20s), and walks through practical setup including hello-world scripts, Docker bridge networking, and building custom images. She shares her Go HTTP service for managing VMs and discusses running Firecracker with nested virtualization on DigitalOcean.
key_points: Firecracker is not just for cloud providers — individuals can use it directly for DIY VM management with relatively straightforward setup
Containers weren't sufficient because the project needed full Linux system access including sysctls, iptables, perf, and nsenter
Building custom Firecracker images involves compiling a Linux kernel (surprisingly easy and fast) and creating ext4 filesystem images from Docker containers
The Go SDK and firectl made it practical to build a custom HTTP service for programmatically launching and stopping VMs
Nested virtualization on DigitalOcean works out of the box, making it feasible to run Firecracker VMs in the cloud on cheap instances
money_quotes: I didn't think that it was something that I could directly use myself.
I wanted to use virtual machines and not containers for this project basically because I wanted to mimic a real production machine that the user has root access to.
I didn't think it was an acceptable user experience for people to have to wait a minute.
I was kind of intimidated by this because I'd somehow never compiled a Linux kernel before, but I followed the instructions and it just worked the first time.
I thought it would be super slow but it actually took less than 10 minutes to compile from scratch.
I still don't totally understand why Firecracker is fast (or alternatively, why qemu is slow).
It does start VMs successfully though which is an important first step!!!
posts.key_insight: Firecracker is accessible enough for individual developers to use directly for personal projects, not just cloud providers, and its sub-second VM boot times enable interactive experiences that were previously impractical.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents debugging a networking issue where pinging the host IP from inside a container worked on her laptop but not on her server. After initially suspecting bridge configuration issues, she discovered through iptables verbose output that Ubuntu's ufw firewall was silently dropping packets. She disabled ufw entirely since the server already had DigitalOcean's external firewall. The post also briefly covers her experiments with Ignite for managing Firecracker VMs.
key_points: Networking problems that work in one environment but not another are often caused by firewall rules rather than fundamental networking misunderstandings
The iptables verbose flag (-L -n -v) showing 'policy DROP 2 packets' was the key clue that revealed ufw was silently dropping traffic
Ubuntu servers often come with ufw enabled by default, which can interfere with container networking in non-obvious ways
When a cloud provider already offers an external firewall, running a host-level firewall may add complexity without meaningful security benefit
money_quotes: Then Kamal said "well, maybe it's iptables" and so I spent some time looking into that. I really didn't think it was iptables because I didn't think there were any special iptables rules except the Docker rules.
policy DROP 2 packets! That is suspicious!
I double checked that this was 100% definitely the problem by running iptables -Z to reset all of iptables' counts and tried again. Same result! Hooray!
DigitalOcean comes with an external firewall anyway and I didn't really see why I also needed an extra iptables firewall running on my machine.

At first I thought that maybe I didn't understand something about how bridges work, and I spent some time reading about bridges. I think I learned a tiny bit more about bridges but this didn't really help.
posts.key_insight: When container networking works locally but fails on a server, the culprit is often a host firewall like ufw silently dropping packets rather than a fundamental networking misconfiguration.

posts.summary: Julia documents her exploration of Linux device mapper as a way to efficiently manage Firecracker VM filesystem images. Rather than copying base images every time a VM launches, she uses device mapper's copy-on-write snapshot feature to overlay writes on top of a read-only base image. She walks through a bash script implementation that uses losetup and dmsetup, following patterns from the Ignite project. The post is written as rough working notes, acknowledging it's not polished but meant as a personal reference.
key_points: Copying VM base images on every launch is slow and inefficient; copy-on-write via device mapper solves this by overlaying writes on a read-only base
Unlike overlayfs which works at the file level, device mapper copy-on-write operates at the block/disk image level, making the overlay data meaningless without the base
The implementation is surprisingly simple — just two calls each to losetup and dmsetup
The losetup tool has a reliability issue where it sometimes enters an infinite loop trying to create a loop device
money_quotes: I can't use the filesystem image directly I launch a VM because if I do, the user will accidentally end up changing the base image themself if they write files.
The solution is to use copy on write! So instead of making a copy, instead we overlay another image on top. Reads come through the bottom, but any writes only go to the top level.

The weird thing I had to wrap my head around was that unlike with overlayfs, this copy-on-write thing is implemented at the disk image level – the overlay on top doesn't contain files, it just contains sort of random blocks of data that would be totally impossible for any program to interpret by themselves.
It was way simpler than I expected – it's just runs both losetup and dmsetup twice.
I noticed that sometimes losetup, instead of finding and creating a loop device, sometimes just ends up in an infinite loop where it tries and fails to create the same loop device forever.
posts.key_insight: Linux device mapper provides a surprisingly simple way to implement copy-on-write for VM images, avoiding expensive full copies by overlaying writes at the block level on top of a shared read-only base image.

posts.summary: Julia describes a low-energy day where she translated a bash script for device mapper setup into Go. She also experimented with the vim-go plugin and found three useful features: auto-formatting, building, and go-to-definition. The go-to-definition feature stood out as genuinely useful compared to her usual approach of grepping through code.
key_points: Translating bash scripts to Go can be a productive low-energy task that still moves a project forward
The vim-go plugin's go-to-definition feature was a significant improvement over manually grepping for function definitions
Auto-formatting and build shortcuts feel marginal when you can just switch to a terminal, but navigation features provide real value
money_quotes: The format and builder things are fine (it doesn't seem that much better to me than just switching to a terminal to do the same thing), but the go to definition thing is GREAT.
Usually I do "go to definition" with a lot of grep so this was a lot faster.
On Wednesday I was feeling tired so I didn't do too much.
posts.key_insight: Editor features that improve code navigation (like go-to-definition) provide more value than ones that merely shortcut terminal commands you already know.

posts.summary: Julia documents her attempts to make Firecracker VMs boot faster, initially planning to replace systemd with a lighter init system. Along the way she encounters loop device allocation bugs, learns about the devpts filesystem for pseudoterminals, and ultimately discovers she can keep systemd by disabling most services. The real revelation comes at the end: after optimizing userspace, she realizes the kernel itself is taking 1 second to boot — 7x slower than Firecracker's benchmark of 150ms — suggesting the kernel compilation is the actual bottleneck.
key_points: Loop device allocation can fail mysteriously when the kernel reports a device as free but it can't actually be used, possibly due to reusing devices for the same file
Replacing systemd with a minimal init reveals how much Linux infrastructure (procfs, devpts) systemd quietly sets up for you
The devpts filesystem is a dedicated filesystem for managing pseudoterminals, required for SSH to allocate a PTY
Disabling most systemd services via a targeted cleanup script got systemd boot time down to 0.3 seconds
The actual bottleneck was kernel boot time (1 second vs Firecracker's expected 150ms), not the init system
money_quotes: My plan for making them boot faster was: 1. replace systemd with a lighter weight init system 2. that's it
I think I actually discovered that the problem wasn't systemd and that I can keep systemd!
I didn't know that Linux had was a devpts filesystem for managing pseudoterminals, that feels like a major missing piece in my "how do terminals even work" model.
Well, if you need to do so little to have an init system, maybe you can just disable most of systemd and then keep using systemd!
I got systemd's start time to about 0.3s on my fast computer, which felt pretty good.
Once I got systemd to be fast, I noticed something new I hadn't noticed before: my kernel was taking 1 second to boot and get into userspace!
I don't know why my kernel is 7x slower, but that's what I'm going to look into next. Probably I compiled it wrong or something.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing the wrong layer first (init system) led to discovering the real bottleneck (kernel boot time), illustrating how performance debugging often requires peeling back assumptions one at a time.

posts.summary: Julia documents a day of progress on her Firecracker VM puzzle platform, tackling three problems: speeding up kernel boot times, building puzzle tarballs using Docker's internal overlayfs, and loading those tarballs into VMs. She found that a kernel boot delay was caused by i8042 AUX port detection and fixed it with a boot option, though a second delay remained unsolved. She built a Python script that exploits Docker's overlay filesystem internals to create tarballs, and a Go function to extract them into VM images.
key_points: A 0.3-second kernel boot delay was fixed by using the i8042.noaux option, which was suggested right in the kernel log output she'd overlooked
She built puzzle tarballs by directly accessing Docker's overlayfs upper directory rather than using Docker's official interfaces, which proved simpler
She chose to shell out to command-line tools like mount/umount/tar in Go rather than reimplementing them, reasoning that known tools beat buggy reimplementations
A second 0.5-second boot delay remained unsolved despite trying six different kernel configurations
money_quotes: I asked about this in the Firecracker Slack and got a reply suggesting to use the i8042.noaux option for the first delay. (which I hadn't noticed, even though it says it right there :))
Basically I took advantage of the fact that I know how Docker uses overlayfs internally and just took a tarball of the upper part of the overlay directly.
I did this because I couldn't find a good way to do it using the normal Docker interfaces
I feel like these command line tools (mount/umount/tar) are really a known quantity and if I tried to reimplement them in Go I would just write a buggy version that I'd then have to maintain.
I spent a while poking at my kernel config and tried compiling with 6 different configurations and didn't get anywhere, so I gave up for the day and moved onto something else.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the pragmatic approach is to leverage existing tools and their internals directly rather than reimplementing them or using official but inadequate interfaces.

posts.summary: Julia describes consolidating her server's scattered logging setup—Rails, a Go proxy, and a Firecracker manager were each logging to different places. She unified them through journald and Docker Compose configuration, then wrote a small bash helper to query logs easily. She also created two beginner strace puzzles for her teaching project, both centered on discovering where programs write their output.
key_points: Having logs scattered across multiple systems (files, Docker defaults, journald) creates confusion and makes debugging harder
Docker Compose can be configured to use journald as its logging driver, enabling unified log viewing
Simple shell functions can smooth over the rough edges of tools like journalctl
Strace puzzles that involve finding where programs write output are effective beginner exercises for learning system tracing
money_quotes: This was very annoying because I was constantly confused about where to look for logs and it was impossible to see everything in one place.
Use strace to find out what it's writing.
It's logging its output to a log file, but you can't find the name of the log file ANYWHERE in its documentation!
These aren't particularly good yet, probably because I spent maybe 5 minutes writing them.
Lots of work to do on this front, but I'll probably do most of it after RC is over.
posts.key_insight: Centralizing logs into a single system like journald eliminates the cognitive overhead of remembering where to look when debugging, and strace is a natural teaching tool for understanding program I/O.

posts.summary: Julia tests how many Firecracker VMs she can run on a small 1GB DigitalOcean droplet and discovers the limit is about 4 VMs before the kernel starts OOM killing them. Each VM uses roughly 100MB of memory, which she considers reasonable given that the kernel alone is 30MB and VMs can't share memory. She reconciles this with Firecracker's impressive demo of 4000 VMs by noting it ran on a machine with 512GB of RAM — simple arithmetic shows the per-VM memory usage is consistent. She concludes by considering fly.io as an alternative.
key_points: Each Firecracker VM uses about 100MB of memory, with the kernel alone accounting for 30MB since it must be fully loaded into memory
A 1GB RAM droplet can only run about 4 VMs before the OOM killer intervenes
The impressive 4000-VM Firecracker demo used a 512GB RAM machine — the per-VM memory usage was actually the same ~125MB
VMs cannot share memory with each other, setting a hard floor on per-VM resource usage
money_quotes: each VM as configured right now seems to use about 100% of memory
I can start about 4 VMs at a time before they start getting OOM killed by the kernel for using too much memory
I think I'd definitely need a bigger machine to use this approach
Then I did the arithmetic and it seems pretty straightforward – they were running the demo on an i3.metal which has 512GB of RAM
512GB divided 4000 gives each VM about 125MB of memory, which is what I'm using
posts.key_insight: Impressive VM density numbers in demos are often just a function of having more RAM — the per-VM memory cost stays roughly the same regardless of scale.

posts.summary: Julia documents a day spent trying to deploy a puzzle application on fly.io, covering three practical discoveries along the way. She learns about IPv6 address notation and when square brackets are needed, discovers that nginx resolves DNS only once at boot unless you use a variable in proxy_pass, and finds that setting up Wireguard is surprisingly simple. The post is a candid learning log where not everything works perfectly—her nginx DNS fix still isn't functioning. It captures the messy reality of deploying applications and the incremental knowledge gained from wrestling with infrastructure.
key_points: IPv6 addresses need square brackets when a port is included to avoid ambiguity, but not always in other contexts like SSH
Nginx resolves DNS for proxy_pass targets only once at boot; using a variable forces re-resolution, and you must explicitly set a resolver
Setting up Wireguard as a client is remarkably simple—just install, drop in a config file, and run one command
Real deployment work involves hitting multiple small but surprising gotchas across different layers of infrastructure
money_quotes: CMD ["rails", "server", "-b", "0.0.0.0"] will only listen on IPv4 addresses!
I think the rule might be that you need to use square brackets if you're going to include a port, because otherwise it would be ambiguous whether the port is part of the IP address or not.
nginx will resolve some-website.com to an IP address once when it boots, and then never update again.
It turns out that if you proxy_pass to something with a variable with it, nginx will resolve DNS.
You also need to set a DNS server to use explicitly, nginx won't use what's in /etc/resolv.conf.
This actually still doesn't work and I don't know why, nginx. Maybe I'll figure that out today.
I set up Wireguard for the first time and it was very straightforward!
posts.key_insight: Deploying real applications reveals surprising default behaviors in familiar tools—like nginx caching DNS forever—that you only discover by running into them.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents a brief foray into WebAssembly during her Recurse Center batch. She paired with others to explore creating art in Rust compiled to WebAssembly and found the setup process surprisingly simple. The post notes the initially confusing use of npm to build a Rust program, and mentions compilation times on different machines.
key_points: Getting started with WebAssembly was easier than expected, requiring just a git clone and npm install
The npm-based build process for a Rust WebAssembly project can be initially confusing since it hides cargo commands behind the scenes
Compilation times varied significantly between machines, from 2 to 10 minutes
money_quotes: I learned that it's easier to get started with WebAssembly than I thought
At first I was confused because the instructions said to use `npm`, but this is a Rust program! What's going on!
posts.key_insight: Getting started with Rust and WebAssembly is more approachable than expected, requiring only standard npm tooling to compile and serve a working example.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her experience trying to migrate rbspy's CI from Travis to GitHub Actions, which turned into a frustrating debugging session. She also mentions an exciting pull request that adds C function identification to rbspy, a feature she'd wanted for years. The CI migration followed a familiar pattern: assuming it would be easy, then spending excessive time debugging. She found some hope in a tmate-based debugging action that allows SSH access to the CI environment.
key_points: A long-wanted rbspy feature for identifying C functions in stack traces was contributed via PR, porting logic from Ruby's .gdbinit
Migrating CI from Travis to GitHub Actions followed the classic pattern of underestimating complexity
Debugging CI pipelines remains universally frustrating because of the slow feedback loops
The tmate GitHub Action for SSH debugging into CI runners offers a potential escape from the pain
money_quotes: think "maybe I'll implement GitHub actions, how hard can it be?"
This was just exactly the same as every other time I've tried to set up CI – it's fine, but it's always kind of frustrating.
spent a billion years trying to debug various things in GitHub actions
when I woke up this morning and opened Twitter I saw a tweet from someone who was also having a sad GitHub Actions day
posts.key_insight: Setting up CI always follows the same deceptively optimistic arc—it seems simple until you're deep in debugging, and the shared frustration is universal among developers.

posts.summary: Julia Evans built a simple web tool for making DNS queries because existing tools like dig and Google's DNS lookup tool produce output that's overwhelming for most people. She wanted something approachable for a zine she's writing about owning a domain name. The tool presents DNS responses in a clean table format, strips out unnecessary technical details like flags and the 'IN' class, and includes a 'GET ME ALL THE RECORDS' button that queries every record type. The backend is a small Go service running on Netlify functions, with a Vue.js frontend. She also explains why ANY queries don't reliably work and why record types in responses aren't actually redundant.
key_points: dig's output includes too much information for most people—flags, message sizes, and cryptic abbreviations make it hard to parse without prior knowledge
Google's DNS lookup tool has a nice query interface but its output is just as complex as dig's command-line output
DNS ANY queries are unreliable because many servers (like Cloudflare) have dropped support for them, so querying each record type individually is more practical
Response record types aren't redundant information—an A record query can return CNAME records, as with www.twitter.com
Lambda-style serverless functions make deploying small tools like this trivially easy
money_quotes: This is great if you're used to reading it and if you know which parts to ignore and which parts to pay attention to, but for many people this is too much information.
Like, what does flags: qr rd ra mean? Why does it say QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 2, AUTHORITY: 0, ADDITIONAL: 1? What is the point of MSG SIZE rcvd: 68? What does IN mean?
I mostly know the answers to these questions because I implemented a toy DNS server one time, but it's kinda confusing!
This is just as bad as dig!
I put the answers in a table and left out a lot of information I thought wasn't necessary for most people like the flags, and the IN (we're all on the internet!)
Using an AWS Lambda-style function was really nice and made this project super easy to deploy. It's fun not to have worry about servers!
posts.key_insight: Most DNS tools expose the full complexity of the protocol when most people just need to see what records exist for a domain, so stripping away unnecessary details makes DNS far more approachable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans introduces dnspeep, a 250-line Rust tool she built that shows DNS queries and responses from your computer in a human-readable format. The tool uses libpcap to capture packets and matches queries with their responses automatically, solving the main usability problem with tcpdump's DNS output. She explains the motivation: DNS seems mysterious to people who can't easily observe it, and existing tools like tcpdump require too much expertise to parse. She walks through several implementation challenges including libpcap compatibility issues, static linking difficulties, and the surprisingly hard problem of naming a DNS tool when every spy-related word is taken.
key_points: DNS becomes less mysterious when you can actually watch the queries and responses your computer makes in real time
tcpdump can show DNS traffic but forces users to manually match requests with responses and parse dense output format
Watching DNS queries reveals which programs on your computer are secretly using the Internet, sometimes surfacing forgotten software
Building small, focused tools that present existing information more clearly can be more valuable than just explaining complex existing tools
Even a 250-line Rust program encounters substantial real-world problems: libpcap version differences across distros, crate compatibility bugs, and Ethernet frame parsing edge cases
money_quotes: I think DNS can seem really mysterious when you don't know a lot about it!
Your browser (and other software on your computer) is making DNS queries all the time, and I think it makes it seem a lot more 'real' when you can actually see the queries and responses.
I think the question 'is this a DNS problem?' is harder to answer than it should be.
A friend of mine actually discovered using this tool that he had some corporate monitoring software installed on his computer from an old job that he'd forgotten to uninstall.
That's the kind of thing computers are good at!
This was one of those bugs which took many hours to figure out and 1 line to fix :)
I'm interested in making more friendly ways to see the same information so that everyone can understand what DNS queries their computer is making instead of just tcpdump wizards :).
posts.key_insight: Building simpler, friendlier interfaces to existing low-level information — like matching DNS queries to responses automatically — can make debugging accessible to everyone instead of just power users.

posts.summary: Evans crowdsourced 200 responses from Twitter about real-world strace use cases and categorized them into 11 problem types. The problems fall into three broad categories: finding files a program depends on, figuring out why a program is stuck or slow, and finding out why a program is failing. The most popular use case is simply finding where a program's config file lives. She highlights that strace's power comes from its simplicity—it just traces system calls—yet this is sufficient to debug an enormous range of problems from permissions errors to network failures to reverse engineering closed-source software. The post serves as both a practical reference and motivation for practice challenges she's building.
key_points: Finding config files is the #1 use case for strace because there's only one way a program can actually open a file: with a system call
Strace can serve as a coarse profiling tool by showing timestamps on system calls, revealing unexpected slowness from things like repeated file reads or blocking network calls
Debugging 'why does this work one way but fail another' (different user, cron job, systemd) is a common pattern where comparing strace outputs is invaluable
Hidden permissions errors are a frequent culprit that strace exposes when programs fail with inscrutable error messages
The same simple mechanism—tracing system calls—solves problems across an enormous range of domains from network debugging to reverse engineering
money_quotes: There are a million ways for a program to document where its config file is (in a man page, on its website, in --help, etc), but there's only one way for it to actually open it (with a system call!)

You have a program, it's just sitting there doing nothing, what's going on? This one is especially easy to answer because a lot of the time you just need to run strace -p PID and look at what system call is currently running.
We straced the J2EE application server and found that it was reading class files one. byte. at. a. time.
In an ideal world programs would report those errors, but of course the world is not perfect, so strace can really help with this!
Server using 100% CPU time randomly with low actual traffic. Turns out it's hitting the number of open files limit accepting a socket, and retrying forever after getting EMFILE and not reporting it.
I think it's really delightful how such a simple tool ('trace system calls!') can be used to solve so many different kinds of problems.
As long as the program makes system calls while it's running, this is super easy to answer with strace – just strace it and see if it's making new system calls!
posts.key_insight: Strace's power comes from the fact that programs can document their behavior in many ways, but they can only interact with the operating system through system calls—making strace a universal debugging tool.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her new project of building choose-your-own-adventure-style debugging puzzles using Twine, where players diagnose specific networking bugs using real tools like tcpdump, dig, and curl. She explains key design decisions: showing realistic command output rather than pre-interpreted results, letting players collect knowledge like an inventory, using free-form text boxes without penalizing wrong answers, and including red herrings to mirror real debugging. The puzzles reproduce actual bugs in VMs to ensure realistic output. Early feedback was overwhelmingly positive, with the main critique being that players wanted more tool options than were available. She frames the experience as simulating debugging alongside a knowledgeable coworker.
key_points: Flowcharts for debugging are too rigid and exhaustive to be useful, but choose-your-own-adventure games capture the exploratory nature of real debugging
Showing raw command output and asking players to interpret it is more realistic and educational than pre-interpreting results for them
Unlike traditional choose-your-own-adventure games where wrong choices kill you, debugging has no permanent failure — you can always keep trying, which the puzzles reflect
Red herrings are valuable because they teach how to prove what the bug ISN'T, which is harder than proving what it is
Reproducing real bugs in VMs makes building puzzles more straightforward than imagining theoretical output
money_quotes: I've don't think I've ever seen a flowchart that I found helpful in solving a problem, so I found it really hard to imagine creating one – there are so many possibilities!
One of the biggest problems with using some of these command line networking tools is that their output is hard to interpret!
When debugging, if you make a 'wrong' choice (for example by making a guess about the bug that isn't correct), there's no cost except your time!
One thing I like about including red herrings is that it lets me show how you can prove what the cause of the bug isn't which is even harder than proving what the cause of the bug is.
I kind of think of my zines as being the experience 'your coworker explains something to you in a really clear way'.
I think the experience here might be 'you're debugging a problem together with your coworker and they're really knowledgeable about the tools you're using'.
I don't learn about how to read all of the output all at once, I learn it in bits and pieces by debugging real problems.
posts.key_insight: The best way to teach debugging is not through flowcharts or documentation but through interactive puzzles that show real tool output and let learners safely make mistakes, mirroring how debugging actually works.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces that she has published all 273 of her programming comics in a searchable online archive at wizardzines.com/comics. Previously, the comics were scattered across Twitter posts, making them hard to find, link to, and update. She built a simple search using list.js with custom word-boundary matching. She explains her initial hesitation about potentially cannibalizing zine sales, but decided to embrace abundance over scarcity since her zine business is going well.
key_points: All 273 programming comics are now searchable in one place at wizardzines.com/comics, solving discoverability problems with Twitter-only distribution
The search implementation uses list.js with a custom function that matches from word beginnings to avoid false positives like 'tar' matching 'start'
Some content is excluded because introductions and conclusions don't work as standalone comics
The technical implementation was straightforward — the real barrier was fear that free availability would hurt zine sales
She chose to operate with a 'spirit of abundance' rather than scarcity, trusting that accessible content supports rather than undermines her business
money_quotes: I'd really like them to just be able to find it at https://wizardzines.com/comics/socat
I wrote a small custom search function that only matches starting at the beginning of the word, so that the search 'tar' doesn't give you 'start'.
This isn't actually that hard of a change to make technically – I just needed to write some Python scripts and write a little search function.
But I felt a bit worried about making all the comics more easily available online because – what if I put them online and then nobody wants to buy the zines anymore?
I decided this week not to worry about that and just do it because I'm really excited about being able to easily link any comic that I want.
The zine business is going really well in general so I think it's a lot nicer to operate with a spirit of abundance instead of a spirit of scarcity.
posts.key_insight: The real barrier to making content freely available is often fear of lost revenue, but operating with a spirit of abundance rather than scarcity can be both more generous and better for business.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains why the OSI model is a poor fit for understanding TCP/IP networking, despite being widely referenced. She maps which OSI layers do correspond to TCP/IP (layers 2, 3, 4, and 7) and highlights that layers 5 and 6 have no clear equivalent. She traces the OSI model's origins to the Protocol Wars of the 70s and 80s, where OSI protocols lost to TCP/IP, explaining the mismatch. She recommends talking about specific protocols like Ethernet or HTTP instead of abstract layer numbers.
key_points: Only OSI layers 2, 3, 4, and 7 map cleanly to TCP/IP; layers 5 and 6 have no clear correspondence
The OSI model originated as a description of specific competing protocols that lost to TCP/IP in the Protocol Wars
Some TCP/IP protocols like ARP don't fit neatly into the OSI layer model at all
Despite its poor fit, understanding layers 2, 3, 4, and 7 is necessary because the terms are used constantly in networking documentation
Referring to specific protocols (Ethernet, HTTP) is clearer and more useful than abstract layer numbers
money_quotes: I don't use the OSI model when explaining networking because when I first started learning about internet networking I found all of the OSI model explanations really confusing.
I've never seen anyone actually refer to layer 5 or 6 in practice when talking about TCP/IP, even though people talk about layers 2, 3, 4, and 7 all the time.

This explains why the OSI model doesn't really correspond that well to TCP/IP, since if the OSI protocols had 'won' then the OSI model would correspond exactly to how internet networking actually works.
Instead of using numbered layers I like to instead just talk about specific networking protocols I mean.
I've written many blog posts talking about MAC addresses and zero posts talking about 'layer 2'.

I wish someone had told me when I started learning networking that I could just learn approximately how layers 2, 3, 4, and 7 of the OSI model relate to TCP/IP and then ignore everything else about it.
posts.key_insight: The OSI model was designed for protocols that lost to TCP/IP, so learners should map layers 2, 3, 4, and 7 to their TCP/IP equivalents and safely ignore the rest.

posts.summary: This post walks through using gdb to inspect the stack of a simple C program, showing exactly how variables are laid out in memory. Evans demonstrates how to find variable addresses, examine raw bytes, and understand the difference between stack-allocated and heap-allocated strings. The walkthrough deliberately uses the unsafe `gets` function to demonstrate a buffer overflow, showing how writing past a buffer's bounds overwrites adjacent memory. The post also covers how stack smashing protection detects overflows using canary values, and how /proc/PID/maps reveals where the stack and heap live in memory. Evans argues that hands-on exploration with gdb makes abstract concepts like the stack, heap, and pointers much more concrete.
key_points: You can use gdb's `x` command to examine raw memory and see exactly how variables like strings, integers, and pointers are laid out on the stack
Stack-allocated strings store their contents directly on the stack, while heap-allocated strings store a pointer on the stack that references data elsewhere in memory
Buffer overflows happen when you write past a buffer's declared size, overwriting adjacent memory—demonstrated here by writing 13 bytes into a 10-byte array
The compiler's stack smashing protector uses canary values to detect when a buffer overflow has corrupted the stack
The stack and heap occupy different address ranges visible in /proc/PID/maps, making it easy to distinguish stack addresses (0x7fffff...) from heap addresses (0x5555...)
money_quotes: We learn more when things go wrong.

I really recommend playing around with gdb like this – even if you don't understand every single thing that you see in memory, I find that actually seeing the data in my program's memory like this makes these abstract concepts like 'the stack' and 'the heap' and 'pointers' a lot easier to understand.
This is a classic buffer overflow, and what's happening is that stack_string wrote over other data from the program.
It can crash your program or, worse, open you up to Very Bad Security Problems.
The weird thing about this is that stack_string was only supposed to be 10 bytes. But now suddenly we've put 13 bytes in it?
These bytes actually get read backwards because x86 is little-endian.
Playing about with gdb like this is really helpful.
posts.key_insight: Actually examining raw memory with gdb transforms abstract concepts like the stack, heap, and buffer overflows into something concrete and understandable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues that the best blogging strategy is to write about things you've personally struggled with, because your struggles likely mirror others' experiences. The key distinction is that posts should focus not on the struggle itself but on what you eventually learned from it. She explains that struggles naturally guide you toward specific, useful topics rather than generic overviews. The process can take years—sometimes you need distance before you can articulate the lesson. She also notes that teaching others who are struggling can reveal gaps in your own understanding, and that vulnerability on the internet can be managed by focusing on technical problems and being selective about what you share.
key_points: Struggles are a natural filter for identifying useful blog topics—if you found something hard, others likely do too
The blog post should focus on what you learned, not the struggle itself; 'I find Rust hard' helps nobody, but explaining references in Rust does
It can take years between struggling with something and being able to articulate what you learned from it
Teaching topics you think are easy but others find hard often reveals gaps in your own understanding
Deliberately identifying what you learned from hard experiences is a practicable skill, not something that happens automatically
money_quotes: I think this approach is effective because if I struggled with something, there's a pretty good chance that other people are struggling with it too, and what I learned is likely to be useful to at least some of them!
I'm still not that great at writing Rust, but I wouldn't write a blog post called 'I find Rust hard' – that wouldn't help anyone!
If I instead think about what I've struggled with Go, it suddenly gets MUCH clearer – one thing I've had trouble with is deadlocks!
It's very easy to misidentify what you learned if you don't remember what it was like to struggle with the topic.
It turned out that when I started I had no idea what was actually challenging about learning SQL.
You can learn things on an intuitive level but never actually identify what exactly it was that you learned.
Putting the writing on the internet really forces me to think about whether the lessons I think I learned actually make sense.
posts.key_insight: Your personal struggles are the best compass for finding blog topics that will genuinely help others, but the post should focus on what you learned, not the struggle itself.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces the launch of a print zine store where readers can buy physical copies of her 'Bite Size' technical zines. She details her decision to work with a quality print company rather than print-on-demand services, and has partnered with a small fulfillment company for shipping. The store includes stickers, offers discounts for existing PDF buyers, and bundles PDF versions with print purchases. She started with a limited run of 350 copies each to avoid waste, with plans to expand the catalog and add bulk rates.
key_points: Print-on-demand services couldn't meet the quality standard she wanted, so she went with a dedicated print company for a 350-copy run of each zine
She outsourced fulfillment to a small artist-focused shipping company rather than handling it herself, citing self-awareness about her own forgetfulness
Existing PDF buyers get 40% off print versions, and print purchases include the PDF, creating a fair pricing model for loyal customers
Initial sales consumed nearly half the print run quickly, validating demand for physical technical zines
money_quotes: I ordered test prints from all the ones I could find and it turns out that none of them could produce the print quality I wanted.
I'm not handling it myself because I am extremely forgetful and I would just get distracted and forget to ship your order, everybody would suffer
I wasn't sure how many to order and didn't want to end up with thousands of zines in a warehouse by accident (think of the trees!)
Sales have been going pretty well so far (we've sold almost half of the initial print run!)
If you order the print version and you don't already have the PDF version – it's included!
posts.key_insight: Building a quality physical product for technical education requires rejecting convenient shortcuts like print-on-demand in favor of dedicated partners who can match your quality standards.

posts.summary: Julia Evans categorizes the reasons why bugs can feel 'impossible' to understand, based on about 400 Twitter responses. She identifies five main categories: the bug is hard to reproduce, you don't understand the overall system, it's hard to get data about the program's internal state, one of your assumptions is wrong, and the bug is genuinely complex. She emphasizes that bugs always have logical explanations but can feel impossible due to combinations of these factors. The post is groundwork for a debugging zine she's writing, and she finds it reassuring that many developers encounter the same types of 'impossible' bugs.
key_points: Impossible-feeling bugs fall into five categories: hard to reproduce, system unfamiliarity, difficulty observing internal state, wrong assumptions, and genuine complexity
Many bugs that seem very difficult are actually simple once understood — the difficulty comes from wrong assumptions or lack of system knowledge, not inherent complexity
Multiple factors often compound to make a single bug feel impossible, such as being intermittent AND in production AND in an unfamiliar system
Debugging tools themselves can change program behavior — debuggers and print statements can make race conditions disappear
The experience of 'impossible' bugs is widely shared, even the weirdest variants like debuggers hiding the bug
money_quotes: Of course, bugs always happen for logical reasons, but I've definitely run into bugs that felt like they might be impossible for me to understand (until I figured them out!)

The ones that make me contemplate a career change are usually bugs that are only happen to a few users, can't be reproduced consistently by users or at all in-house, and have slightly varying descriptions in each bug report (kinda like Bigfoot sightings).
A lot of bugs that are VERY DIFFICULT to understand are actually pretty simple in the end! They're just difficult to understand because of some of the above reasons.

Debugging sometimes feels like a really intense personal struggle (WHY is this happening to ME?!?!) and I thought it was really cool to see that even some of the weirdest reasons for bugs are shared by a lot of people!
It's also easy to drown in too much output – I've turned on debug output and then been totally overwhelmed by how much information there is.
This one is SO frustrating when it happens – you're 100% sure nothing changed but somehow the code is no longer working! (of course, the answer is that something did change, you just can't see it)

It's a lot harder when multiple things are broken at once – maybe there's a bug in your program, and also a bug in a library you're using, and also some unexpected behaviour on the part of your load balancer.
posts.key_insight: Most 'impossible' bugs aren't genuinely complex — they feel impossible because of compounding factors like wrong assumptions, lack of system knowledge, and difficulty observing program state.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains her approach to writing good programming examples: start with real code you've actually written, then distill it down to something minimal and self-contained. She demonstrates this by showing how a contrived Python lambda example (squaring numbers) is less effective than one derived from real usage patterns (sorting events by time). She argues that realistic examples help 'sell' concepts by showing readers tasks they might actually encounter, and acknowledges that distilling real code into clean examples can take hours of work but is worth the effort when many people will read them.
key_points: Good examples should come from real code you've written, not be invented out of thin air, because contrived examples often demonstrate unrealistic or non-idiomatic usage
Grepping your own codebase for patterns reveals what real usage actually looks like, which naturally produces better teaching examples
Realistic examples serve a dual purpose: they teach the concept AND convince the reader the concept is worth learning
Distilling real code into minimal examples can take hours (e.g., turning hundreds of lines of CSS/JS into 5 lines), but the time investment pays off when many readers benefit
There are different types of useful examples: ones that change mental models through surprise, and ones that serve as copy-paste starting points
money_quotes: The basic idea here is to start with real code that you wrote and then remove irrelevant details to make it into a self-contained example instead of coming up with examples out of thin air.
I think the easiest way to make realistic examples is, instead of pulling an example out of thin air, instead just start by looking at real code!

To convince someone that lambdas are useful, it really helps to show someone how lambdas could help them do a task that they could actually imagine themselves doing, and ideally a task that they've done before.
In the end it 'just' took 5 lines of HTML and a tiny bit of CSS to demonstrate the problem and it doesn't really look like it took hours to write.
But originally it was hundreds of lines of JS/CSS/JavaScript, and it takes time to untangle all that and come up with something small that gets at the heart of the issue!
If hundreds of people are reading your example, you're saving them all so much time!
posts.key_insight: The best programming examples come from grepping your own real code for patterns and then distilling those patterns down to minimal, self-contained demonstrations rather than inventing contrived scenarios from scratch.

posts.summary: Evans explains why quadratic-time algorithms are dangerously slow and how hashmaps can convert them to linear time. Using a concrete example of intersecting two lists, she shows that nested loops over 100,000 elements take nearly 3 minutes while a hashset-based approach handles 10 million elements in under 2 seconds. She demonstrates this with runnable Python code and actual timing results, making the performance difference visceral rather than theoretical. The post also notes that 'accidentally quadratic' code is a real-world problem that can sneak up on programmers because it works fine on small inputs.
key_points: Quadratic algorithms seem fine on small inputs but become catastrophically slow at scale — 100,000 elements took 160 seconds vs 0.04 seconds with the linear approach
Converting a list to a hash set enables constant-time lookups, turning a nested-loop O(n²) algorithm into O(n)
Quadratic slowdowns are sneaky because they don't manifest until inputs grow large enough, making them easy to ship accidentally
For very small inputs, the 'slower' algorithm can actually be faster due to overhead of hash structures — context matters
money_quotes: The weird thing about quadratic time algorithms is that when you run them on a small number of elements (like 1000), it doesn't seem so bad! It's not that slow! But then if you throw 1,000,000 elements at it, it can really take hours to run.
I didn't try to run this program on 1,000,000 elements, because I knew it would take 100x longer again – probably about 3 hours. I don't have time for that!

You might think that this check – if y in set1 – would be slower if the set1 contains 10 million elements than it is if set1 contains 1000 elements. But it's not! It always takes basically the same amount of time (SUPER FAST), no matter how big set1 gets.
So I think it's worth being broadly aware of them, so you can avoid writing them by accident. Especially if there's an easy way to write a linear-time algorithm instead (like using a hashmap).

Hashmaps aren't magic of course (you can learn the math behind why hashmap lookups are instant! it's cool!), but it always feels a little magical to me, and every time I use hashmaps in a program to speed things up it makes me happy :)
posts.key_insight: Quadratic algorithms sneak up on you because they work fine on small inputs, but replacing a list scan with a hashmap lookup is often a simple fix that delivers dramatic speedups at scale.

posts.summary: Evans advocates for debugging by dropping into a REPL at a breakpoint, showing how this works in Python (via ipdb), Ruby (via pry), and even partially in C (via gdb). She demonstrates the technique with concrete code examples, showing how you get access to all in-scope variables and functions at the breakpoint. She contrasts this with traditional debuggers, noting that REPL debugging is easier to remember because you just set a breakpoint and start running code interactively. The post positions this as a middle ground between print-statement debugging and full debugger usage.
key_points: Setting a breakpoint that drops you into a REPL gives you both in-scope variables and access to all your program's functions interactively
Python's ipdb.set_trace() and Ruby's binding.pry are the practical ways to get this experience in those languages
GDB provides a surprisingly REPL-like experience for C thanks to extraordinary engineering effort, even though C is compiled
REPL debugging is easier to remember than traditional debugger workflows, especially for people who switch languages frequently
This technique works in interpreted languages but generally not in compiled ones, with GDB as a notable partial exception
money_quotes: I like doing this because it gives me both: all the variables in scope at the breakpoint, so I can print them out interactively, and easy access to all the functions in my program, so I can call them to try to find issues.
This stuff only works in gdb because the gdb developers put in a lot of work doing Very Weird Things to make it easier to get a REPL-like experience.
This is the only way I use gdb when looking at C programs – I never set watchpoints or do anything fancy, I just set a couple of breakpoints in the program and then poke around at those points.
I think part of the reason I like this type of REPL debugging more than using a more traditional debugger is – it's so easy to remember how to do it!

With debuggers, I always forget how to use the debugger (probably partly because I switch programming languages a lot) and I get confused about what features it has and how they work, so I never use it.
posts.key_insight: Dropping into a REPL at a breakpoint combines the simplicity of print-statement debugging with the power of a full debugger, and its low cognitive overhead makes it a technique you'll actually use.

posts.summary: Julia Evans articulates her teaching philosophy: instead of explaining topics from the ground up, she targets specific knowledge gaps that experienced practitioners already have. She argues that most programmers learn 'backwards' — building things first with abstractions, then filling in foundational knowledge later. This mirrors how people actually work: you set up a website before learning HTTP, learn Rails before understanding SQL. Her approach assumes readers already know a lot, identifies the one or two things they're missing, and explains just those gaps. She traces this style to her experience as a drop-in math tutor, where she had 20 minutes to diagnose what someone didn't know and fill in the minimum useful information.
key_points: Most programmers learn 'backwards' — they build with abstractions first and fill in foundational knowledge later, and this is fine
Modern jobs have more abstractions than ever, so knowledge gaps about underlying systems are natural and expected
Effective teaching assumes the learner already knows a lot, identifies specific gaps, and explains just one concept at a time
Bugs are the best natural way to learn what's underneath an abstraction because they break through abstraction boundaries
Ranting about what 'kids these days' should know is boring and ineffective — providing targeted useful information works better
money_quotes: You can make a website and set up nginx or Apache by copying some stuff you found on Stack Overflow and didn't fully understand, and then you have a website on the internet!
I think that learning about HTTP headers after you already have a website is way more fun and a lot easier than learning about them before you have a website.
Abstractions are great, but they're also leaky, and to do great work you sometimes need to learn about what lives underneath the abstraction.
We've had that argument a billion times, and it's ineffective – people are going to keep learning abstractions first! It's fun to learn abstractions first!
One mistake I see people make a lot when they notice someone has a knowledge gap is assume 'wow, you don't know how binary works, you must not know ANYTHING.'

I learned to listen a lot and not jump to conclusions about what the person didn't know – often they already actually understood a lot about the math class they were taking and I just needed to explain one or two small things.
I think 'show people what lives underneath their abstractions' is a big part of what I'm trying to do with my writing.
posts.key_insight: The most effective technical teaching doesn't start from the beginning — it meets people where they are, identifies the specific gap in their existing knowledge, and fills in just that one piece.

posts.summary: Julia Evans built an nginx playground website that lets users paste in nginx configurations and test them with curl commands, getting back either results or error logs. She walks through the technical architecture: a static Vue.js frontend on a CDN paired with a single-endpoint Go backend that runs each nginx config in an isolated network namespace. The security approach combines practical isolation measures (network namespaces, no database, separate frontend hosting) with an honest acknowledgment of remaining risks. She discusses performance tradeoffs of running on a tiny fly.io instance and design decisions borrowed from JSFiddle and CodePen. The project came together quickly because she kept the scope minimal, and she's considering building similar playgrounds for other tools.
key_points: Playgrounds are valuable learning tools because they let you experiment safely without fear of breaking things, and nginx's finicky configuration makes this especially useful
The security approach combines practical measures (network namespaces, no database, CDN-hosted frontend, isolated VM) with honest acceptance of residual risk
Keeping the architecture simple—static frontend plus a single Go API endpoint—made the project buildable in a couple of days
Performance on a tiny instance (1 shared CPU, 256MB RAM) is acceptable at ~400ms per request because the CDN handles all frontend traffic
A SIGKILL vs SIGTERM bug caused nginx worker process leaks and eventual memory exhaustion—a concrete example of why process lifecycle management matters
money_quotes: I find that playgrounds really help me learn – it's incredibly useful to be able to quickly and safely experiment and try out different options without worrying that Something Terrible is going to happen if you make a mistake.
And nginx in particular is EXTREMELY finicky to configure, so I think it's extra important to have a playground for nginx to quickly test things out.
Don't use a database, just browser local storage (can't hack the database if there isn't one!)
Ask people to be nice in the FAQ (that's the "YOLO" part :) )
Someone already showed how to read the /etc/passwd file which is fun, but there's nothing sensitive in there.

I didn't want to use an npm build step to combine them because I'm pretty bad at Javascript and I'm always worried my Javascript build will break and then I'll be too lazy to fix it and then it'll be impossible for me to deploy changes.
I added a little rocket ship gif that plays while the backend is running to make it a little more fun to wait
This was a lot easier to build than I thought it would be!
posts.key_insight: Building useful developer tools doesn't require complex architecture—a static frontend, a single API endpoint, and pragmatic security tradeoffs can produce something genuinely valuable for learning.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces that all her technical zines are now available in print through a preorder system, with shipping in time for Christmas. She explains the logistics of the preorder model, pricing with discounts for PDF buyers and bulk orders, and free shipping tiers. The post highlights her decision to work with small, local businesses near Seattle for both printing and fulfillment rather than cheaper overseas options. She shares testimonials from happy customers about the print quality and explains how the arrangement with nearby small companies provides faster turnaround and more flexibility.
key_points: All zines are available for preorder in print, with an October 12 deadline and November 5 shipping date
PDF buyers get 50% off print versions, and print purchases include the PDF version
She chose local Seattle-area small businesses for printing and fulfillment over cheaper overseas options, valuing speed and flexibility
The preorder model helps her accurately gauge demand so everyone who wants zines can get them
Working with small businesses avoids absurd logistics problems like needing individual barcodes on stickers or accidentally shipping 500 stickers to one person
money_quotes: I don't want to accidentally underestimate demand and then not have enough zines for everyone who wants them.
I never like paying for shipping, so I've set up free shipping for US orders over $30, and international orders over $50.
I decided to use a print company that's in the same area as the company that handles my shipping.
Small businesses in general seem more flexible and easier to work with.
A big fulfillment company I was considering told me that for them to ship stickers for me, every sticker needed to be individually barcoded.
They warned me not to ship them stickers shrink wrapped, because they might accidentally decide that 500 stickers is a single item and ship all 500 to one person.
posts.key_insight: Working with local small businesses for printing and fulfillment trades slightly higher costs for dramatically better turnaround, flexibility, and fewer absurd logistical problems.

posts.summary: Motivated by the 2021 Facebook outage, Julia Evans explores tools that let anyone investigate BGP routing information, despite BGP being a protocol most people can't directly experiment with. She walks through BGP terminology (autonomous systems, routes, announcements) and demonstrates four practical tools: BGPView for looking up AS information, traceroute/mtr with ASN flags, looking glass services like PCH that let you query real routing tables, and BGPlay for visualizing historical BGP events. She uses the Facebook outage as a concrete example throughout, showing how routes disappeared and returned. The post concludes that understanding BGP tools is practically useful even for people who can't publish routes, because it helps diagnose network issues caused by upstream routing problems.
key_points: Unlike most networking protocols (TLS, HTTP, TCP, DNS), BGP is uniquely difficult to experiment with because you need your own ASN to publish routes on the real internet
Tools like BGPView, mtr -z, looking glass services, and BGPlay make BGP surprisingly observable to regular people despite being considered a 'secret network wizard' thing
The mtr tool looks up ASN information via DNS queries to cymru.com, reversing the IP address similar to reverse DNS lookups
BGPlay's visualization of the Facebook outage clearly shows all BGP routes disappearing and then gradually returning, making the abstract concept of route withdrawal concrete
Even though you can't fix someone else's bad BGP routes, understanding BGP tools lets you confidently diagnose that a problem is upstream and out of your control
money_quotes: I got frustrated because none of the articles showed me how I could actually look up information related to BGP on my computer.
With most networking protocols, you can pretty trivially get access to implement the protocol yourself if you want.
But with BGP, I think that unless you own your own ASN, you can't publish routes yourself!

What happened yesterday with Facebook is that they basically made BGP announcements withdrawing all their BGP routes, so every router in the world deleted all of its routes related to Facebook, so no traffic could get there.

I think for most companies all you can do to respond to outages caused by someone else's bad BGP routes is 'do nothing and wait for it to get fixed', but it's nice to be able to confidently do nothing.

I was surprised by how much information I could get about BGP just as a regular person, I always think of it as a 'secret network wizard' thing but apparently there are all kind of public machines anybody can just telnet to and use to look at the route tables!
posts.key_insight: BGP may be a protocol you can't directly experiment with, but there are surprisingly accessible public tools that let anyone observe and understand internet routing, which is practically useful when upstream routing problems affect your network.

posts.summary: Julia Evans expands on her earlier 'how to ask good questions' post with practical tactics for getting useful answers when asking technical questions. She identifies three common failure modes: getting explanations of things you already know, irrelevant information, or jargon-filled responses. Her primary tactics are asking yes/no questions to constrain answers, stating your current understanding so others can correct it, and being willing to interrupt or redirect when explanations go off track. She argues that extracting information from people is a learnable skill that requires confidence, and that most 'how to ask questions' advice unhelpfully undermines the asker's confidence.
key_points: Yes/no questions are the most effective tactic because they constrain the answerer and almost always produce useful information, even when the actual answer includes elaboration beyond yes or no
Stating your current understanding gives the answerer something concrete to confirm or correct, making their job easier and sometimes helping you answer your own question in the process
Being willing to interrupt irrelevant explanations is not rude but actually more efficient for both parties, and usually the fix is asking a more specific follow-up question
Getting useful answers requires confidence that most 'how to ask questions' advice actively undermines by assuming the answerer is a perfect genius whose time you're wasting
Being good at extracting information expands the set of people you can learn from, since you no longer need to find someone who is naturally a great explainer
money_quotes: it often feels condescending when someone embarks on a lengthy explanation of things I already know, even if they had no way of knowing that I already know those things
The answers to yes/no questions usually aren't just 'yes' or 'no' – for all of those questions my friend elaborated on the answer, and the elaborations were always useful to me.
I also find the process of writing down my understanding is really helpful by itself just to clarify my own thoughts – sometimes by the time I'm done I've answered my own question
If someone goes off on a very long explanation that isn't helping me at all, I think it's important to interrupt them. This can feel rude, but ultimately it's more efficient for everyone.

One of the reasons I dislike a lot of 'how to ask questions' advice out there is that it actually tries to undermine the reader's confidence – the assumption is that the people answering the questions are Super Smart Perfect People and you're probably wasting their time with your dumb questions.
instead of being mad that some people aren't great at explaining things, it's more effective for me to get better at asking questions that will get me the answers I need
