instead of finding someone who can easily give a clear explanation, I just need to find someone who has the information I want and then ask them specific questions until I've learned what I want to know
posts.key_insight: Asking yes/no questions and stating your current understanding are the most effective tactics for getting useful answers, because they constrain the responder and require confidence that most question-asking advice actively undermines.

posts.summary: Julia Evans walks through practical tools and techniques for determining whether a server or networking problem is caused by DNS. She covers browser error messages (which are unhelpfully vague), programming language error messages, using dig to confirm DNS resolution, querying multiple DNS servers to catch caching issues, and using tcpdump to spy on DNS traffic. She illustrates each technique with real debugging stories, including a negative caching gotcha and an nginx failure caused by a DNS server bug.
key_points: Browser error messages for DNS failures and TCP connection failures are nearly identical, so you need command-line tools to distinguish them
Querying multiple DNS servers (local vs public like 8.8.8.8) reveals caching discrepancies, including negative caching of missing records
tcpdump on port 53 lets you see exactly what DNS queries are being made and whether they're timing out or returning errors like NXDOMAIN
DNS problems can exist even without visible DNS failures — stale cached records from application-level DNS caching cause issues that don't look DNS-related
Different programming languages produce surprisingly varied error messages for the same DNS failure, making it hard to recognize DNS issues from errors alone
money_quotes: These two error messages ('we can't connect to the server' and 'firefox can't establish a connection to the server') are so similar that I don't try to distinguish them – if I see any kind of 'connection failure' error in the browser, I'll immediately go the command line to investigate.
I'd gone to https://bananas.wizardzines.com in my browser before I'd created the DNS record (huge mistake!), and then my ISP's DNS server cached the absence of a DNS record.
I think it would have been literally impossible for me to guess what was happening here without using tcpdump to see what queries nginx was making.
The NXDOMAIN response made nginx think that that domain didn't exist, so it ignored the IPv6 address it got later.
This is a DNS problem (your program should be requesting DNS updates more often!) but you have to diagnose it by noticing that there are missing DNS queries.
Even if my programming language gives me a clear DNS error, I like to use dig to independently confirm because there are still a lot of different error messages and I find them confusing.
posts.key_insight: Diagnosing DNS problems requires going beyond browser and application error messages to actively probe with dig, compare multiple DNS servers, and watch actual DNS traffic with tcpdump.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her journey from a simple 'pretend it's 2005' frontend approach (plain HTML and script tags) to adopting esbuild as a build tool. She explains how she was frustrated by npm libraries that require imports and complex build pipelines she didn't understand. After discovering esbuild, she hit a problem where Vue's runtime-only build was loaded instead of the full build with template compiler. She traces through the issue using strace and learns about npm build artifacts, eventually fixing it by importing the specific Vue distribution file she needed.
key_points: Frontend build tools like vue-cli-service and webpack are confusing because they do too many things at once, making them hard to debug when they break
esbuild provides a simpler alternative that just resolves imports and bundles files, without the complexity of a full build pipeline
NPM packages can ship multiple build artifacts (dev/prod, runtime/full, ESM/CommonJS) and understanding which one gets loaded is key to debugging import issues
The package.json 'main' field directs which build artifact gets loaded by default, which may not be the one you need
Using strace to debug which files esbuild actually opens is a practical way to understand what's happening under the hood
money_quotes: I stopped using those tools and went back to my old <script src="script.js"> system because I don't understand what those vue-cli-service and vite are doing and I didn't feel confident that I could fix them when they break.
I'm excited about it because it lets me use imports in my code without using a giant build pipeline that I don't understand.
So I'd rather stick to a setup that I actually understand.
I also double checked which version of Vue esbuild is loading using strace, because I love strace.
I don't understand everything esbuild is doing, but it feels a lot more approachable and transparent than the Webpack-based tools that I've used previously.

The other thing I like about esbuild is that it's a static Go binary, so I feel more confident that I'll be able to get it to work in the future than with tool written in Javascript, just because I understand the Javascript ecosystem so poorly.
posts.key_insight: You don't need to adopt a complex build pipeline you don't understand just to use npm imports — simpler tools like esbuild can bridge the gap between 'pretend it's 2005' and modern JavaScript packaging.

posts.summary: Julia Evans debugs a confusing 'file not found' error when running a Go binary inside an Alpine Docker container, even though the binary clearly exists on disk. She traces the problem through strace, readelf, and Google searches to discover that the ELF binary's dynamic linker interpreter (/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2) doesn't exist in Alpine Linux. The root cause is that Go unexpectedly produced a dynamically linked binary due to cgo being enabled by default. She finds two fixes: using the golang:alpine build container, or setting CGO_ENABLED=0 to produce a statically linked binary.
key_points: The 'file not found' error from execve can mean the binary's ELF interpreter is missing, not that the binary itself is missing
Go binaries are not always statically linked by default — cgo can cause dynamic linking even without explicitly enabling it
Setting CGO_ENABLED=0 forces Go to produce a truly static binary that works across different Linux distributions
Tools like readelf, ldd, and file are essential for diagnosing dynamic linking issues in containers
The debugging process involved multiple wrong hypotheses (permissions) and incremental narrowing through strace, Google, and readelf
money_quotes: what do you MEAN file not found, the file is RIGHT THERE???!!??
I'm including this even though it's 'obviously' wrong just because I have a lot of wrong thoughts when debugging, it's part of the process :)
When execve() returns the error ENOENT, it can mean more than one thing: the program doesn't exist; the program itself exists, but it requires an 'interpreter' that doesn't exist.
ELF executables can request to be loaded by another program, in a way very similar to #!/bin/something in shell scripts.
I've run into this 'file not found' error a couple of times, and it feels kind of mind bending because it initially seems impossible (BUT THE FILE IS THERE!!! I SEE IT!!!).
I still don't understand why it's using cgo here, I ran env | grep CGO and I definitely don't have CGO_ENABLED=1 set in my environment, but I don't feel like solving that mystery right now.
posts.key_insight: A 'file not found' error when executing a binary that clearly exists usually means the binary's ELF dynamic linker interpreter is missing from the system, not the binary itself.

posts.summary: Julia Evans provides a practical guide to using the dig command-line tool for DNS queries. She breaks down dig's arguments into two categories: query options (name, type, server) and formatting options. She shows how to tame dig's verbose default output using +noall +answer, +short, and a .digrc configuration file. She also covers reverse DNS lookups with -x and the +trace option for understanding DNS resolution. The post concludes by explaining why she prefers dig over simpler alternatives like dog and host: dig shows all sections of DNS responses when needed.
key_points: dig arguments fall into two categories: what query to make (name, type, server) and how to format the response
The default dig output is overwhelming for beginners, but +noall +answer or +short make it much more readable
You can create a ~/.digrc file to change dig's default output format permanently
dig +trace mimics what a DNS resolver does by starting at root nameservers and working down
dig's verbosity is actually its advantage over simpler tools like dog and host, since it shows all DNS response sections
money_quotes: When I first started using dig I found it a bit intimidating – there are so many options!
If you're not used to reading this, it might take you a while to sift through it and find the IP address you're looking for.

By the way: if you've ever wondered what IN means, it's the "query class" and stands for "internet". It's basically just a relic from the 80s and 90s when there were other networks competing with the internet like "chaosnet".
What I like about dig is actually the same thing I don't like about dig – it shows a lot of detail!
I only learned about .digrc recently and I love using it so much, so I hope it helps some of you spend less time sorting though dig output!
-x isn't magic – dig -x 172.217.13.174 just makes a PTR query for 174.13.217.172.in-addr.arpa.
posts.key_insight: dig's verbose default output becomes manageable with a few formatting flags and a .digrc file, and that same verbosity is actually dig's advantage over simpler DNS tools when you need full response details.

posts.summary: The post argues that the common term 'DNS propagation' is misleading because it implies DNS records are actively pushed out to resolvers, when in reality DNS is a pull-based system where resolvers cache records and you're simply waiting for those caches to expire. Evans demonstrates that new DNS records are actually available instantly, and the delays people experience come from cached records at resolvers, browsers, and operating systems. She walks through four specific reasons DNS updates can be slow: resolvers ignoring TTLs, negative DNS caching, browser/OS caching, and long-lived nameserver record caches. She concludes that while the terminology is likely here to stay, recognizing its misleading nature can help people build more accurate mental models.
key_points: DNS is pull-based, not push-based: authoritative nameservers never push updates to resolvers, they only respond to queries
New DNS records for previously nonexistent names are available instantly since there's nothing cached to expire
Four reasons DNS updates are slow: resolvers ignoring TTLs, negative caching of nonexistent records, browser/OS caching, and long-lived nameserver record caches
The 'propagation' metaphor implies an active push process, leading people to an incorrect mental model when the reality is passive cache expiration
Understanding the caching model lets you skip the common '24-48 hours' advice in many cases
money_quotes: you're not actually waiting for DNS records to 'propagate', you're waiting for cached records to expire
DNS is pull, not push
if you create a DNS record, it's possible that no DNS resolver will ever know about it!
new DNS records are actually available instantly

the reason that so many people have an incorrect mental model of how DNS works isn't because they're dumb – the 'propagation' terminology we use to talk about why DNS updates are slow implies an incorrect mental model!
For most programmers, 'there are a bunch of cached records you have no control over and you need to wait for them to expire' is a pretty normal and approachable concept!

DNS resolvers are much more difficult to work with because there are thousands of different DNS resolvers run by different organizations and running different software and there's no way to control what they do directly
I do think it's important to recognize when terms we use are potentially misleading
posts.key_insight: DNS 'propagation' is actually cache expiration — DNS is a pull-based system where resolvers cache records, and understanding this distinction lets you predict and minimize update delays.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces Mess With DNS, an interactive educational tool that gives users a real subdomain and DNS server to experiment with. The tool addresses three barriers to learning DNS: lack of a safe domain to test with, invisible behind-the-scenes queries, and not knowing what experiments to try. She details the design process, including user testing that significantly improved the UI, and the backend implementation in Go with a custom authoritative DNS server, websocket-based live streaming, and pragmatic security decisions. The post is a thorough technical walkthrough that doubles as a reflection on building educational tools.
key_points: DNS is hard to learn because queries are invisible, people lack safe test domains, and they don't know what experiments would be revealing
User testing with just 5 people produced massive improvements to the sidebar, terminology, and instructions — proving that observing real users is far more valuable than guessing
The backend uses Go channels for pub/sub instead of external services, deliberately trading distributed systems complexity for simpler architecture at the cost of latency
The DNS server intentionally doesn't follow RFCs strictly, taking a pragmatic approach that prioritizes educational value over spec compliance
Design is fundamentally about how things work, not how they look — functionality and clarity matter more than aesthetics
money_quotes: I like to learn in a very hands-on way. And so when I write a zine, I often close the zine by saying something like '… and the best way to learn more about this is to play around and experiment!'.
Instead of viewing these as frustrating, I thought – I'll make these into an interesting experiment with no consequences, that people can learn from!
One of the basic facts about DNS is that people don't have a consistent experience of it. So I think it's better to just be honest about that reality.

I used to think that design is about how things look – that websites should look nice. But every time I talk to someone who's better at design than me, the first question they always ask instead is 'okay, how should this work?'.
Usually my testing strategy for Javascript is 'write a bunch of terrible untested code, manually test it, hope for the best'.
And this means I don't need to deal with any distributed systems, which is amazing. Distributed systems are very annoying.
I spent a few minutes trying to read the IETF draft about what HTTPS queries are and I couldn't really understand it so I gave up.
posts.key_insight: The best way to make a complex system like DNS learnable is to give people a safe, real environment to break things in, with visibility into what's happening behind the scenes.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her second full year working independently on Wizard Zines, articulating a mission focused on explaining established technologies and helping people build foundational confidence with computers. She shifted energy toward building educational websites rather than finishing zines, creating several DNS-related tools and playgrounds. She found success collaborating with Marie on Mess With DNS and opened a print zine store. Despite releasing more free content than paid products, the business remains sustainable through zine sales. She also began relaxing her rule against writing anything negative, finding that slightly more critical posts turned out better.
key_points: Her emerging mission is to help people understand the 'simple' core of how computers work so they gain confidence to handle any new complexity thrown at them
She built seven educational websites and tools in 2021, mostly DNS-related, driven by the belief that people learn best through hands-on experimentation rather than reading
She got stuck finishing several zines because she needs a clear mental model of why people struggle with a topic, and her first guesses about that are often wrong
She began intentionally relaxing her 'never write anything negative' blogging rule, finding that slightly more critical posts about DNS propagation and the OSI model were better for it
Collaboration with Marie on Mess With DNS was a highlight, addressing the loneliness of working independently
money_quotes: I don't know what all this nonsense is but at the end of the day it's all just assembly instructions and memory and system calls, I can figure this out
I think the two hardest things about working for myself are working alone and having to decide what to do.
There's a lot of hidden knowledge about these 'old' tools (what are they used for? what are the common mistakes? which parts can you ignore?). I want to make that knowledge easy to find.

We think about debugging as a technical skill (and it absolutely is!!) but a huge amount of it is managing your feelings so you don't get discouraged and being self-aware so you can recognize your incorrect assumptions
The explanation looks something like 'the reason many people struggle with TOPIC is because they don't understand X, here's what you need to know'. My first guess at what X is often wrong.
I want to figure out how to help other people move towards that confidence of 'ok, I know how this computer stuff works, I can figure out any weird new nonsense you throw at me'.
All of the work in this post continues to be 100% funded by zine sales, which I'm grateful for.
posts.key_insight: Understanding the simple foundations of how computers work gives you the confidence to handle any complex new technology, and the best way to teach that is through hands-on experimentation rather than reading.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the reasons why someone might want to run their own DNS server, covering both authoritative nameservers and resolvers. She first establishes that DNS's decentralized nature gives users control over how their DNS works, then catalogs specific motivations gathered from Twitter responses. For authoritative nameservers, reasons include security, familiar tooling, custom behavior, cost savings, and resilience against shared-infrastructure attacks. For resolvers, reasons include privacy, blocking malicious sites, resolving internal domains, and avoiding ISP manipulation. The post frames DNS's complexity as a feature rather than a bug, arguing that understanding the 'why' behind the system makes learning it more motivating.
key_points: DNS is not a static phone book — query results vary based on location, network, load balancing, and many other factors, which is central to why running your own server is useful
There are two types of DNS servers you can run: authoritative nameservers (for domains you own) and resolvers (for computers you manage), each with distinct motivations
Running your own authoritative server provides security control, support for new record types, custom behavior, cost savings, and isolation from shared-infrastructure DDoS attacks
Running your own resolver gives you privacy, malicious site blocking, internal domain resolution, and protection from ISP manipulation
Understanding why DNS is complex and decentralized is key to staying motivated to learn about it
money_quotes: One of the things that makes DNS difficult to understand is that it's decentralized.

I want to talk about how the fact that it's decentralized means that you can have control of how your DNS works. You can add more servers to the giant complicated mess of DNS servers! Servers that you control!
DNS isn't a static database.
You can write a DNS server that does anything you want, it doesn't have to just return a static set of records.
If someone can see all your DNS lookups, they have a complete list of all the domains you (or everyone from your organization) is visiting!
Some ISPs run DNS resolvers that do bad things like nonexistent domains to an IP they control that shows you ads or a weird search page that they control.

It feels important to me to explore the 'why' of DNS, because it's such a complicated messy system and I think most people find it hard to get motivated to learn about complex topics if they don't understand why all this complexity is useful.
posts.key_insight: DNS's decentralized complexity isn't just an obstacle to understanding — it's what gives you the power to control security, privacy, performance, and custom behavior for your domains and networks.

posts.summary: This post explains how to reliably find a domain's authoritative nameserver, a key debugging step when DNS updates don't seem to be working. Evans walks through a two-step process: querying a root nameserver, then querying the TLD nameserver to get the definitive answer. She explains why this method is guaranteed to be correct—it mirrors exactly what DNS resolvers do behind the scenes. The post also evaluates several alternative methods (dig ns, dig soa, whois, dig +trace) and explains why each can give incorrect results in edge cases.
key_points: When a DNS update doesn't work, the first diagnostic step is determining whether the authoritative nameserver has the wrong record or whether you're seeing a cached version
The guaranteed-correct method walks the DNS hierarchy manually: root nameserver → TLD nameserver → authoritative nameserver, which mirrors how real DNS resolution works
Common shortcuts like 'dig ns DOMAIN' can return stale cached results or NS records from a different source than what resolvers actually use
After updating nameservers with a registrar, the change isn't instant—querying the TLD nameserver directly lets you check whether the update has propagated
money_quotes: If you made a DNS update and it didn't work, there are 2 options: your authoritative nameserver doesn't have the correct record, or your authoritative nameserver does have the correct record, but an old record is cached and you need to wait for the cache to expire.
When I looked up 'how to find a domain's authoritative nameserver' to see what advice was out there, I found a lot of different methods being mentioned, some of which can give you the wrong answer.
Because this method uses the exact same information source as a real DNS query, you're guaranteed to get a correct answer every time.
The reason I like this method is that it mimics what's happening behind the scenes when you make a DNS query.
When I update my nameservers with my domain registrar, they don't actually update the authoritative nameserver right away. It takes a while, maybe an hour.
This does the exact same thing so it will always give you the right answer, but the output is a bit confusing to read so I'm a bit more hesitant to recommend it.
posts.key_insight: The only guaranteed way to find a domain's authoritative nameserver is to walk the DNS hierarchy from the root, because this uses the exact same information source that real DNS resolvers use.

posts.summary: Julia Evans catalogs the many ways DNS can break systems in practice, noting that textbook knowledge of DNS doesn't prepare you for real-world failures. She collected examples from Twitter of DNS problems that didn't initially appear to be DNS problems. The post covers issues ranging from slow resolvers and timeouts to Kubernetes ndots configuration, negative caching, nginx and Java caching DNS forever, musl's lack of TCP DNS support, and round-robin DNS breaking with getaddrinfo. The key theme is that DNS failures are diverse, often subtle, and frequently masquerade as other problems.
key_points: DNS problems in practice are far more varied than textbook descriptions suggest, and many don't initially appear to be DNS problems at all
Caching is a recurring theme: negative caching, nginx resolving once on startup, Java's JVM never refreshing DNS, and forgotten /etc/hosts entries all cause subtle failures
Kubernetes introduces several DNS-specific pitfalls including the ndots:5 configuration that causes 4 unnecessary lookups before the real query, and race conditions between containers
Implementation differences between libraries (musl vs glibc, getaddrinfo vs gethostbyname) can silently break DNS behavior in ways that are extremely hard to debug
Even DNS servers themselves can have bugs, like returning NXDOMAIN instead of NOERROR when a record type doesn't exist but the domain does
money_quotes: With DNS, reading about how it works in a textbook doesn't prepare you for the sheer volume of different ways DNS can break your system in practice.
So every time you make a DNS query, you need to wait for 4 DNS queries to fail before you can get to the actual real DNS query that succeeds.
If you visit a domain before creating a DNS record for it, the absence of the record will be cached.

nginx will resolve some.domain.com once on startup and never again. This is especially dangerous if the IP address changes infrequently, because it might keep happily working for months and then suddenly break at 2am one day.
This is extra confusing because dig ignores /etc/hosts, so everything SEEMS like it should be fine.
I've implemented this bug myself too, so I understand why it happens – it's easy to think 'there aren't any records for this query, I should return an NXDOMAIN error'.

This is especially insidious because you might not realize that you're switching from gethostbyname to getaddrinfo at all – if you're not writing a C program, those function calls are hidden inside some library.
posts.key_insight: DNS failures in production are extraordinarily diverse and often masquerade as unrelated problems, making the 'it's always DNS' meme a reflection of genuine operational pain rather than just a joke.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her experience migrating all her static sites from Netlify to a self-hosted $5/month DigitalOcean droplet running nginx. She walks through her decisions: building a tiny Docker-based build system that clones clean Git repos, using rsync for deployment, Ansible for configuration management, and rewriting a small Lambda function in Go. The result is faster builds (7 seconds vs. a minute on Netlify), cheaper bandwidth (20x less than Netlify's overage pricing), and better visibility into 404 errors through nginx logs. She notes that the setup is simpler than expected and acknowledges she doesn't yet know what long-term maintenance will look like.
key_points: A conversation with a friend convinced her that self-hosting static sites might not be the maintenance burden she assumed, prompting the experiment
She built a minimal Docker-based build system that clones a clean Git repo each time, avoiding the problem of uncommitted local files leaking into production
Local git clones use hard links instead of copying, making even 1GB repo clones fast and rendering --depth 1 unnecessary
Moving from Netlify to a $5/month droplet cut build times from ~60 seconds to ~7 seconds and reduced bandwidth costs by 20x
Running her own nginx gave her direct access to logs, which revealed broken links that had existed for years unnoticed
money_quotes: Originally I thought that running my own servers would require a lot of maintenance and be a huge pain, but I was chatting with Wesley about what kind of maintainance their servers require, and they convinced me that it might not be that bad.
Everything in here is pretty standard but I wanted to write down what I did anyway because there are a surprising number of decisions and I like to see what choices other people make.
I have pretty bad hygiene around files on my laptop – often I have a bunch of uncommitted files that I don't want to go onto the live site.
I didn't use any Ansible plugins because I wanted to maximize the probability that I would actually be able to run this thing in 3 years.

I ran grep 404 /var/log/nginx/access.log to figure out if I'd broken anything during the migration, and I actually ended up finding a lot of links that had been broken for many years, but that I'd just never noticed.
I don't really know what this will be like to maintain, we'll see how it goes – maybe I'll like it! maybe I'll hate it!
posts.key_insight: Self-hosting static sites on a simple nginx server can be faster, cheaper, and more transparent than managed services, with less maintenance overhead than expected.

posts.summary: Evans explores why web servers should support IPv6 despite major sites like Twitter and GitHub not doing so. She explains how ISPs without enough IPv4 addresses use CGNAT to share addresses among customers, which creates performance bottlenecks. The post catalogs reasons gathered from Twitter responses, ranging from reducing CGNAT load and improving performance to enabling IPv6-only servers and making home server networking easier. She also honestly notes that adding IPv6 support can sometimes cause problems when broken network equipment fails IPv6 connections. Evans frames IPv6 support partly as a public commons issue where broad adoption benefits everyone, especially users in regions with limited IPv4 access.
key_points: ISPs without enough IPv4 addresses use CGNAT to share addresses, creating performance bottlenecks and attractive DDoS targets
IPv6 support is partly a public commons issue: broad adoption reduces pressure on shared IPv4 infrastructure, especially benefiting users outside North America
Supporting IPv6 can paradoxically cause problems when broken network equipment on dual-stack networks fails IPv6 connections silently
For many sites behind CDNs or modern hosting, IPv6 support comes essentially for free with just an AAAA record
The IPv6 address space is too large to scan, providing a secondary security benefit for IPv6-only servers
money_quotes: twitter.com does not support IPv6. Presumably it can't be causing them THAT many issues to not support it. So why do websites support IPv6?
CGNAT is a bottleneck and it causes performance issues, and it's going to continue to get worse over time as access to IPv4 addresses becomes more and more restricted.
It's less that supporting IPv6 will make your site specifically work better, and more that if almost everyone supports IPv6 then it'll make the experience of the internet better for everyone.
If your server only has a public IPv6 address, attackers can't easily find it by scanning the whole internet. The IPv6 address space is too big to scan!
Any time I run an IPv4 public server I'm always a tiny bit surprised by how it's constantly being scanned for vulnerabilities.
I thought it was interesting and surprising that supporting IPv6 can actually in some cases make things worse for people on dual stack networks.
One of Facebook's IPv6 addresses is '2a03:2880:f10e:83:face:b00c:0:25de' — it has face:b00c in it.
posts.key_insight: IPv6 adoption is as much a public commons problem as a technical one — supporting it reduces strain on shared IPv4 infrastructure and improves the internet for users in regions with scarce IPv4 addresses.

posts.summary: Julia Evans demonstrates how DNS resolution works by building a toy DNS resolver in 80 lines of Go (with the core function being just 20 lines). She explains the four sections of a DNS response (Question, Answer, Authority, Additional) and shows how a resolver iteratively queries nameservers starting from root servers, following referrals through glue records and authority records until it finds an answer. She compares her toy resolver's behavior to the real resolver 'unbound' using tcpdump, revealing that production resolvers make additional queries to cache nameserver addresses. She also wrote a bash version that, despite being janky, actually helped simplify the Go version's design.
key_points: DNS responses contain four sections (Question, Answer, Authority, Additional) that work together during resolution, not just a simple question-and-answer exchange
The core DNS resolution algorithm is surprisingly simple: start at a root nameserver, then loop checking Answer for results, Additional for glue records, or Authority for the next nameserver to query
Real DNS resolvers like unbound make more queries than strictly necessary, proactively caching nameserver addresses for future use
The number of queries needed varies by domain hierarchy depth — jvns.ca needs 6 queries (3 nameservers) while www.maths.ox.ac.uk needs 7 (4 nameservers)
Writing a constrained bash version first helped debug the Go version by forcing design simplification
money_quotes: I usually find it easier to understand things work when they come in the form of programs that I can run and modify and poke at.
When they start, DNS resolvers don't know any DNS records (except the IP addresses of the root nameservers). But they do know how to find DNS records for you.

I really recommend writing a toy DNS resolver that actually does the parsing of DNS packets if you want to learn about binary protocols though, it's really fun and it's a totally doable to get something basic working in a weekend.
You might think of DNS queries as just being a question and an answer. But actually DNS responses contain 4 sections, and we need to use all 4 sections to write our DNS resolver.
The bash version is even more janky and uses grep in very questionable ways but it did resolve every domain I tried which is cool.
Bash's limitations forced me to simplify the design and simplifying it fixed a bug I was running into.
As usual I always learn something from writing tiny versions of real programs.
posts.key_insight: The entire DNS resolution algorithm is simple enough to fit in 20 lines of code — start at a root nameserver and iteratively follow referrals through the Answer, Additional, and Authority sections until you get your answer.

posts.summary: Evans examines how the common DNS terms 'nameserver' and 'DNS resolver' each have multiple meanings depending on context, which creates confusion for people learning DNS. 'Nameserver' can mean either an authoritative nameserver (storing domain records) or a recursive nameserver (caching and resolving queries). 'DNS resolver' can mean a stub resolver library like getaddrinfo, a stub resolver server like dnsmasq, or a full recursive nameserver like 8.8.8.8. She provides real-world examples for each meaning and a Google results survey showing the ambiguous generic terms vastly outnumber the precise ones. Rather than inventing clearer terminology, she argues it's better to explain the existing messy terminology people actually encounter.
key_points: The term 'nameserver' refers to either authoritative nameservers (in the context of a specific domain) or recursive nameservers (in the context of your computer's DNS settings)
'DNS resolver' has three possible meanings: a stub resolver library (like libc's getaddrinfo), a stub resolver server (like dnsmasq or systemd-resolved), or a full recursive nameserver (like 8.8.8.8)
The generic terms 'nameserver' and 'DNS resolver' are orders of magnitude more common than precise terms like 'authoritative nameserver' or 'stub resolver'
Context clues reliably disambiguate: if it's about a domain, it's authoritative; if it's about your computer's settings, it's recursive; if it's a library, it's a stub resolver
money_quotes: Two of the most common DNS server terms ('nameserver' and 'DNS resolver') have different meanings depending on the situation.
This isn't a problem if you already understand how DNS works – I can easily figure out what type of 'nameserver' is being discussed based on context.
But it can be a problem if you're trying to learn how DNS works and you don't realize that those words might refer to different things depending on the context – it's confusing!
A DNS resolver can either be a library or a server. (I'm sorry, I know I said that a DNS resolver is a server earlier. But sometimes it's a library.)
The terminology is a bit messier than I'd like, but it seems better to me to explain it than to use less-ambiguous language that isn't as commonly used in practice.
The more specific terms like 'recursive nameserver', 'authoritative nameserver', and 'stub resolver' are much less common.
posts.key_insight: DNS terminology is genuinely ambiguous because the most commonly used terms ('nameserver' and 'DNS resolver') each have multiple meanings, and the precise alternatives are rarely used in practice, so learners need to rely on context clues to disambiguate.

posts.summary: Evans shares a crowdsourced list of programming tasks that used to be difficult but have become significantly easier thanks to modern tools and services. The list spans infrastructure (Let's Encrypt for SSL, Terraform for cloud), languages (Go for concurrency, Rust for memory safety), hosted services (GitHub Actions for CI/CD, Firebase for realtime apps), and developer experience improvements (Docker for dev environments, LSP for IDE tooling). The post is intentionally brief and list-focused, serving as a catalog of progress rather than deep analysis. Evans frames it as inspiration for things worth learning now that the barrier to entry has dropped.
key_points: Many previously expert-level tasks are now accessible to regular developers thanks to better tooling and abstractions
The improvements come from multiple sources: open-source tools (Let's Encrypt, Wireguard), language design (async/await, Rust's borrow checker), and hosted services (GitHub Actions, Firebase)
The list serves as a learning guide — if you avoided something because it was hard years ago, it may be worth revisiting
Some improvements eliminate entire categories of difficulty (SSL certificates went from painful multi-step processes to automated free tooling)
money_quotes: You know this thing that used to be super hard? Turns out now it's WAY EASIER and maybe you can do it now!
Not all of them are equally "easy", but I found reading the list really fun and it gave me some ideas for things to learn.
SSL certificates, with Let's Encrypt
Someone pointed out that the joke in this XKCD doesn't make sense anymore
Centering in CSS, with flexbox/grid
posts.key_insight: Many programming tasks that once required deep expertise have become dramatically easier thanks to modern tools and services, making it worth periodically revisiting things you previously considered too hard.

posts.summary: The post explores the surprising behaviors of getaddrinfo, the libc function responsible for most DNS lookups. Evans explains that not all programs use it (dig and Go have their own resolvers), that its implementation differs significantly across platforms (glibc, musl, Mac OS), and that Mac's version doesn't even use /etc/resolv.conf, instead routing through mDNSResponder. These differences explain common DNS mysteries like why dig doesn't check /etc/hosts, why switching Linux distributions can break DNS, and why Macs have DNS caching but Linux doesn't always.
key_points: getaddrinfo is the standard DNS lookup function in libc, but it has at least three major implementations (glibc, musl, Mac OS) with significant behavioral differences
Not all programs use getaddrinfo — dig, Go's pure-Go resolver, and browsers with DNS-over-HTTPS all implement their own DNS resolution
Mac OS getaddrinfo routes through mDNSResponder instead of /etc/resolv.conf, which explains why DNS caching works differently on Macs
musl libc's getaddrinfo doesn't support TCP DNS and handles search domains differently from glibc, which can cause subtle breakage when switching distributions like Alpine Linux
money_quotes: When you resolve a DNS name in a Python program, it checks /etc/hosts, but when you use dig, it doesn't.

Before you learn what getaddrinfo is, it's not at all obvious that socket.gaierror: [Errno -2] Name or service not known means "that domain doesn't exist". It doesn't even say the words "DNS" or "domain" in it anywhere!

I used to use a Mac for work, and I always felt vaguely unsettled by DNS on Mac. I could tell that something was different from how it worked on my Linux machine, but I couldn't figure out what it was.
You might think ok, Mac OS getaddrinfo is different, but the two versions of getaddrinfo in glibc and musl libc must be mostly the same, right?
I found it really surprising that such a widely used library function has such different behaviour on different platforms.
It definitely makes DNS a bit more difficult to reason about.
posts.key_insight: The widely-used getaddrinfo function behaves differently across platforms and implementations, which is the hidden cause of many confusing DNS behaviors developers encounter.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares a collection of small personal programming projects she's written to solve minor problems in her life, from scraping festival calendars to generating envelope labels. She frames these tiny projects not as practical problem-solving (many could have been solved faster without code) but as a joyful way to spend an evening or weekend programming. The projects range from API polling for vaccine appointments to reverse engineering file formats, all united by being low-stakes, fun, and personally useful. She recommends this approach especially for people learning to program, emphasizing that the best part is the combination of creative fun and tangible real-world output.
key_points: Tiny personal programs that solve minor life problems are a great way to have fun with programming, even when they're not the most practical solution
Many of her projects involve discovering and using undocumented APIs by copying cookies from the browser — a recurring pattern she enjoys
The best tiny projects share three qualities: they're just for you (low stakes), finishable in an evening or weekend, and produce tangible output
Several projects turned out to not be useful in the end, but the process of building them was the real value
These low-stakes personal projects are especially valuable for people learning to program
money_quotes: I like to write small programs that have some marginal utility in my life.
Many of the problems I've solved with programming could have been solved in less time in other ways.
As long as your goal is actually to have fun programming and your programs don't hurt anyone else, I think this is a great approach.
One of my favourite things is to use secret undocumented APIs where you need to copy your cookies out of the browser to get access to them.
I don't think this actually helped us at all with buying a condo but it was fun.
They're just for me (if it goes wrong, it doesn't matter!)
I can finish them in an evening or weekend (it's not a Huge Giant Thing hanging over my head).
Presumably you could calculate the same thing with math, but it's easier with code.
posts.key_insight: The most rewarding personal programming projects are low-stakes, completable in a single session, and solve some small real problem in your life — even if the solution isn't strictly necessary.

posts.summary: Evans walks through a practical step-by-step process for using undocumented web APIs by intercepting browser requests with developer tools. She demonstrates copying a request as cURL from the network tab, stripping unnecessary headers through trial and error, and translating the minimal request into a Python script. She discusses practical limitations like expiring session cookies and the importance of being respectful with request volume. The post concludes with the critical security insight that this technique applies to your own APIs too — anyone can inspect and manipulate the requests your frontend makes.
key_points: You can reverse-engineer any web API by copying requests from the browser's network tab as cURL commands, then stripping headers until you find the minimal set needed
The process is mechanical: find a JSON response in dev tools, copy as cURL, remove irrelevant headers by trial and error, translate to Python
Browsers aren't magic — they send standard HTTP requests, so there's no way for a backend to distinguish a Python script sending identical headers from a real browser
This approach has practical limits: session cookies expire, making it unsuitable for long-running programs, and you should be careful not to overwhelm smaller sites
The most important takeaway is defensive: anyone can do this to your own API, so your backend must properly authorize every request
money_quotes: The answer is sort of yes – browsers aren't magic! All the information browsers send to your backend is just HTTP requests.
It's much more important to understand that anyone can do this to your backend API!
I usually just figure out which headers I can delete with trial and error – I keep removing headers until the request starts failing.
So if anyone can just change some parameters to get another user's information, that's no good.
I also stick to downloading data that's either mine or that's intended to be publicly accessible – I'm not searching for vulnerabilities.
Everyone has developer tools and the network tab, and it's pretty easy to see which parameters you're passing to the backend and to change them.
posts.key_insight: Browsers aren't magic — any request your browser makes is just HTTP that anyone can replicate, which is both a useful hack for personal scripts and a critical security lesson for API developers.

posts.summary: Julia Evans argues against traditional goal-setting for learning programming, finding it stressful and unhelpful when goals go unmet. She proposes instead celebrating 'tiny milestones' — small learning achievements that happen naturally through projects and debugging. Using her own experience of setting C/C++ learning goals nine years ago and achieving none of them, she shows how she still made enormous progress in systems programming through a different path. The post reframes 'failed' goals as simply redirected learning, and encourages tracking and celebrating small wins to stay motivated.
key_points: Traditional learning goals can feel depressing when unmet, even when the reason you didn't pursue them is perfectly rational
Tiny milestones — like using a new tool, fixing a new type of bug, or learning a concept — are better markers of progress than big goals
Most meaningful learning happens 'by accident' through projects and bugs rather than explicit planning
Changing goals isn't failure; it reflects that your actual needs and interests evolved
Celebrating milestones somewhere (blog posts, brag documents) creates a tangible sense of progress that sustains motivation
money_quotes: Goals can be useful, but a lot of the time I actually find them stressful and not that helpful.
9 years later, I have done 0 of those things. With the "goal" framing, it's to think of this as a bad thing!
I find this framing depressing and unhelpful. In reality, I didn't have any real reason to learn C++ then and I still don't now.
Every time I solve a bug that I couldn't have solved before, I think of it as a tiny milestone.
The reason I still haven't learned C isn't that I suck or that C is impossible to learn. It's just that learning how to write C well was never actually something I had a real reason to do.
I think it's much more healthy to be flexible about your goals and to celebrate the milestones you do end up getting to instead of feeling bad about goals that you "failed" at.
Most of my tiny milestones came up naturally because I had a project I wanted to do or a bug I needed to solve.
posts.key_insight: Tracking and celebrating small, naturally-occurring learning milestones is more motivating and realistic than setting big goals that may never match your actual needs.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience implementing a toy version of TLS 1.3 in Go, motivated by her habit of learning networking protocols by building working implementations. She dramatically simplifies the task by only supporting one cipher suite, skipping certificate verification, and targeting a single website (her own blog). Using the invaluable resource tls13.ulfheim.net, she walks through each step: the client/server hello exchange, elliptic curve Diffie-Hellman key exchange, the surprisingly complex key derivation process, authenticated encryption with AES-GCM, and finally downloading her blog's homepage. The project reinforced her belief that you should never roll your own crypto while giving her concrete understanding of TLS internals.
key_points: TLS 1.3 involves at least 4 different symmetric keys (client/server handshake and application keys), and the key derivation process using HKDF is surprisingly complex
Elliptic curve Diffie-Hellman with Curve25519 is elegantly simple to use — any random 32-byte string works as a private key, and generating a public key is just one multiplication
TLS data is transmitted in blocks rather than as a continuous stream, with each block requiring a different initialization vector derived by XORing with the message count
You don't technically need to parse or verify the server's certificate to establish a TLS connection, though you obviously should
Unit tests using known-good example data from tls13.ulfheim.net made the implementation roughly 10 times easier by catching parsing and crypto errors quickly
money_quotes: I already 100% believed that I should not invent my own crypto implementations, and seeing how the crypto in TLS 1.3 works gave me even more of an appreciation for why I shouldn't.
I can't stress enough how helpful this was, I looked at probably more than a hundred times and I only looked at the TLS 1.3 RFC for a few small things.
I was surprised to learn that there are at least 4 different symmetric keys involved in TLS.
I was surprised that you don't technically need to look at the server's certificate at all to make a TLS connection (though obviously you should verify it!).
I previously thought that once you established the connection, encrypted TLS data was just a stream. But that's not how it works – instead, it's transmitted in blocks.
Every time I write networking code like this, I forget that unit testing is good, and I thrash around with a bunch of parsing / formatting code that does not work.
My code truly is terrible, it can connect to my site (jvns.ca) and I think literally no other sites.
I won't pretend to understand all the reasons TLS is designed this way, but it was a fun way to spend a couple of days.
posts.key_insight: Implementing a deliberately simplified toy version of a real protocol like TLS 1.3 reveals surprising complexity in key derivation and encryption layering that you'd never appreciate from reading documentation alone.

posts.summary: Julia Evans compiled a crowd-sourced list of modern command line tools after asking on Twitter about newer alternatives to standard Unix utilities. The list covers direct replacements for classic tools like grep, find, ls, and du, as well as entirely new inventions that solve problems the original Unix toolkit didn't address. She organizes them into categories: replacements for standard tools, new inventions, JSON/YAML/CSV tools, grep variants, and lesser-known older utilities. The post serves as a practical reference for anyone looking to modernize their terminal workflow, and she closes by sharing her personal favorites and the tools she's most excited to try.
key_points: Many modern CLI tools are direct replacements for classic Unix utilities, often written in Rust, offering better defaults, speed, and UX (ripgrep, fd, bat, exa, dust)

A second category of tools represents genuinely new ideas that didn't exist in the traditional Unix toolkit, like fzf for fuzzy finding, entr for file watching, and croc for peer-to-peer file transfer
The JSON/YAML/CSV ecosystem has grown significantly, with jq spawning a family of similar tools for different formats (yq, fq, htmlq, xsv)
Some valuable older tools like pv, vidir, and sponge from moreutils remain underappreciated despite being widely useful
The list was crowd-sourced from Twitter, demonstrating how community knowledge-sharing surfaces tools people might otherwise never discover
money_quotes: My favourites of these that I use already are entr, ripgrep, git-delta, httpie, plocate, and jq.
I think the most exciting tool I learned about is vidir.
pbcopy/pbpaste maybe aren't "new" but were mentioned a lot.
posts.key_insight: The Unix command line is experiencing a renaissance of modern replacements and novel tools that improve on decades-old defaults, and crowd-sourcing recommendations is an effective way to discover them.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine about DNS, explaining why she chose the topic: DNS is ubiquitous, has an elegant decentralized design, and is frustratingly confusing without proper understanding. She highlights companion tools she built including Mess With DNS (a playground for safe DNS experimentation) and a simple DNS lookup tool. The zine was a collaborative effort with Marie Claire LeBlanc Flanagan over four months, featuring an innovative custom feedback system that collected ~1200 feedback items from ~60 beta readers. DNS security topics like DoH, DoT, and DNSSEC were deliberately excluded because the landscape is still evolving.
key_points: DNS deserves a clear, comprehensive explanation because it's ubiquitous, elegantly designed, and frustratingly confusing without proper knowledge
Hands-on experimentation through tools like Mess With DNS is the most effective way to learn technical concepts
Building a custom feedback website that collected ~1200 items from ~60 people dramatically improved the zine's quality compared to the usual 10 beta readers
DNS security was intentionally excluded because the landscape is still evolving and wouldn't stay accurate for 5-10 years
Consistent collaborative pairing (an hour every weekday for 4 months) was key to actually finishing the project
money_quotes: DNS is everywhere! You basically can't use the internet without using DNS — nobody is going to memorize all the IP addresses for every domain they use.
I love that it gives you control of your own little corner of the internet (it just costs ~$12/year to own a domain!)
I love that the basic design is relatively straightforward, and I love that it's worked basically the same way for 40 years and scaled relatively well over time
DNS is very frustrating! I've run into some VERY weird DNS problems over the years, and it can feel magical and incomprehensible if you don't know how it works.
But once you learn how DNS works, these problems all become totally possible to understand.
I've never seen a clear explanation of DNS that included all of the technical details I wanted to know, so I decided to write one.
I find the most fun way to learn is by experimenting and breaking things!
posts.key_insight: DNS appears magical and incomprehensible until you learn how it actually works, at which point even the weirdest problems become totally understandable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares draft pages that were cut from her 'How DNS Works' zine. She originally planned a broader zine covering everything needed to own a domain, including domain registration, TLS, and email, but discovered DNS alone required all 28 pages. Rather than let the drafts sit unused, she published them as bonus content on her blog, covering domain registration, email basics, and TLS fundamentals. She acknowledges the pages aren't as polished or cohesive as her usual zine work.
key_points: DNS turned out to be far more complex than initially estimated, expanding from an expected 5 pages to filling all 28 pages of the zine
The cut content covers three adjacent topics to DNS: domain registration, email, and TLS
Publishing draft work that didn't make the final cut is a practical way to share knowledge without letting it go to waste
Even an expert on the topic underestimated how much there was to explain about DNS
money_quotes: At the time I thought "I can just explain DNS in like 5 pages, it's not that complicated, there will be lots of space for other topics about domains". I was extremely wrong about that and it turned out I needed all 28 pages to explain DNS.
Instead of letting all of the old draft pages languish in purgatory on my hard drive, I could post those extra pages here all together on my blog.
Email security is a HUGE topic that honestly I don't know a lot about.
These pages aren't as cohesive as I usually like my zines to be and they definitely do not tell you everything you need to need to know to own a domain.
posts.key_insight: Even seemingly simple technical topics like DNS can turn out to be far more complex than expected once you try to explain them thoroughly.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes sqlite-utils, a tool that makes importing data into SQLite easy for ad-hoc data analysis. She walks through a practical example of importing Shopify order data, showing how the tool auto-generates schemas from JSON, handles schema changes with alter, and deduplicates with upsert. She also covers the command line interface and pairs it with Metabase for visualization. The post highlights how this setup replaced a more complex Flask/plotly/pandas workflow.
key_points: sqlite-utils auto-generates database schemas from JSON data, eliminating tedious manual schema definition
The alter flag and upsert method handle real-world messiness like evolving schemas and duplicate data gracefully
The tool works both as a Python library and a command line tool for importing CSV and JSON data
Pairing sqlite-utils with Metabase provides a simpler alternative to custom Flask/plotly/pandas dashboards
money_quotes: The Shopify data has about a billion fields and I really did not want to type out a schema for it.
But this seemed like a lot of boring work, like I'd have to write a schema and write a Python program.
This whole setup (sqlite-utils + metabase + SQL) feels a lot easier to use than my previous setup, where I had a custom Flask website that used plotly and pandas to draw graphs.
I was really delighted by sqlite-utils, it was super easy to use and it did everything I wanted.
Luckily, sqlite-utils thought of that: there's an alter flag which will update the table's schema to include the new fields.
posts.key_insight: sqlite-utils removes the friction of getting data into SQLite by auto-generating schemas and handling real-world import problems like evolving fields and duplicates, making ad-hoc data analysis much more accessible.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores bubblewrap as a fast alternative to Docker for running containers, motivated by her need for sub-5ms container startup times for an nginx playground project. She benchmarks Docker/podman at ~300ms startup versus bubblewrap at ~8ms, explaining that bubblewrap achieves this by being a much lower-level tool that skips features like overlay filesystems and networking bridges. The post documents several surprising pitfalls she encountered: processes appearing in ps but being unkillable due to mismatched PID namespaces, capabilities issues with privileged ports, and UID mapping problems that break tools like apt. Despite these sharp edges, she concludes that bubblewrap works well for her constrained use cases and is worth the tradeoffs.
key_points: Docker and podman containers take ~300ms to start, while bubblewrap achieves ~8ms by operating at a lower level without overlay filesystems or networking bridges
Bubblewrap runs containers as an unprivileged user using Linux user namespaces, avoiding the need for a root daemon like Docker requires
The lower-level interface exposes Linux namespace details that Docker abstracts away, leading to confusing states like processes visible in ps but unkillable due to mismatched /proc mounts
UID mapping differences between bubblewrap and podman cause tools like apt to fail because unmapped users can't be used in system calls
For simple, constrained use cases like running a single binary in a sandbox, bubblewrap's tradeoffs are worthwhile
money_quotes: I want to stress that, while I'm not sure exactly what the slowest part of Docker and podman startup time is (I spent 5 minutes trying to profile them and gave up), I'm 100% sure it's something important.
The way we're going to run containers faster with bubblewrap has a lot of limitations and it's a lower level interface which is a lot trickier to use.

I was surprised the first time I learned that Docker actually runs containers as root – even though I run docker run ubuntu:20.04 as an unprivileged user, that message is actually sent to a daemon running as root.
I tried to do this 6 months ago and I don't remember most of the details, but basically I spent 8 hours working on it, got frustrated because I couldn't get anything to work, and gave up.
ps doesn't actually list all the processes in your current PID namespace. Instead, it iterates through all the entries in /proc and prints those out.
But that's the price I pay for wanting things to be fast I guess :)
At the end of the day I think it's a VM and it's not going to be anywhere near as low overhead as a process – there's a whole operating system to start!
posts.key_insight: Bubblewrap can start containers 35x faster than Docker by operating at a lower level, but this exposes Linux namespace complexities that Docker deliberately abstracts away.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her approach to monitoring small, low-stakes web services she runs as side projects. She explains how she went from no monitoring (and embarrassing downtime discoveries) to a simple three-step system: uptime checkers that email on failure, end-to-end healthchecks that verify the service actually works, and automatic restarts when healthchecks fail. The key insight is that monitoring doesn't have to be complex or expensive — her entire setup costs about $5/year and achieves 99.95% uptime. She emphasizes that this approach is specifically for tiny services where occasional downtime is acceptable.
key_points: Past oncall experience created an intimidating mental model of server management that initially kept her from running servers at all
A healthcheck that just returns 200 OK is nearly useless — end-to-end checks that exercise actual functionality catch real problems
Automatic restarts on healthcheck failure are a pragmatic way to paper over bugs you haven't fixed yet
Hourly healthchecks at $5/year are sufficient for low-stakes services where immediate response isn't critical
This simple three-step approach (uptime checker, real healthcheck, auto-restart) took her sites from embarrassing downtime to 99.95% uptime
money_quotes: I want the sites to mostly work, but I also want to spend approximately 0% of my time on the ongoing operations.
In my mind "being responsible for servers" meant "get woken up at 2am to fix the servers" and "have lots of complicated dashboards".
So for a while I only made static websites so that I wouldn't have to think about servers.
This had the extremely predictable outcome of – sometimes the site broke, and I didn't find out about it until somebody told me!
"Restart a lot" is a very useful strategy to paper over bugs that I haven't gotten around to fixing yet.
It was nice to have a workaround in place that could keep things running while I was procrastinating fixing the bug.
If there is a problem there's no guarantee I'll get to fixing it all that quickly anyway.
posts.key_insight: Monitoring tiny web services doesn't require sophisticated infrastructure — a cheap uptime checker, an end-to-end healthcheck, and automatic restarts can achieve 99.95% uptime with near-zero operational effort.

posts.summary: Evans explores what actually happens at the byte level when you press keys in a terminal, using a browser-based terminal (xterm.js with goterm) to log bytes sent and received over a websocket. She demonstrates that the client is essentially 'dumb' — it sends raw keystrokes and relies on the server to echo them back. She maps out how Ctrl combinations, special keys, and escape sequences are encoded as bytes, revealing that Alt is literally the same as Escape, and Ctrl+letter simply maps to that letter's position in the alphabet. The post explains how ANSI escape sequences control terminal display, originating from the VT100 era. She acknowledges the encoding scheme is weird by modern standards but exists for decades of backwards compatibility.
key_points: Terminal clients are 'dumb' — they send keystrokes and must be told by the server what to display, even for simple character echoing
Ctrl+letter maps to the letter's position in the alphabet (Ctrl+a=1, Ctrl+c=3, etc.), and Ctrl+Shift does the same thing as Ctrl alone
Alt is functionally identical to Escape, which creates ambiguity that programs resolve with timeouts to distinguish escape sequences from the Escape key itself
ANSI escape sequences starting with \x1b control terminal display (colors, cursor position, formatting) and originate from VT100 terminals in the 1970s-80s
Window resizing is handled differently from normal byte communication — it uses the ioctl system call with TIOCSWINSZ rather than sending bytes
money_quotes: when you press a key on your keyboard in a terminal (like Delete, or Escape, or a), which bytes get sent?

The client sends l and then immediately receives an l sent back. I guess the idea here is that the client is really dumb – it doesn't know that when I type an l, I want an l to be echoed back to the screen.
I thought this was really cool because I've always been a bit confused about how Ctrl+C works – it's good to know that it's just sending an \x03 character.

the Linux kernel on the server side receives this \x03 character, recognizes that it means "interrupt", and then sends a SIGINT to the process that owns the pseudoterminal's process group. So it's handled in the kernel and not in userspace.
Alt is literally the same as "Escape", except that pressing Alt by itself doesn't send any characters to the terminal and pressing Escape by itself does.
all of this was designed in the 70s or 80s or something and then needed to stay the same forever for backwards compatibility, so that's what we get :)

Escape codes are why your terminal can get messed up if you cat a bunch of binary to your screen – usually you'll end up accidentally printing a bunch of random escape codes which will mess up your terminal
posts.key_insight: Terminal communication is fundamentally just bytes sent back and forth — keystrokes encoded as simple byte values and display instructions as escape sequences — a protocol largely unchanged since the 1970s VT100.

posts.summary: Julia Evans builds a toy remote login server (like a tiny SSH without security) to explain how pseudoterminals work in Linux. She walks through the server implementation in Go, showing how to create a pseudoterminal, connect it to bash, and ferry data between a TCP connection and the terminal. Along the way, she demonstrates why you can't just use a raw TCP socket as bash's stdin/stdout (Ctrl+C breaks, top doesn't work), and dives into Linux sessions and process groups to explain how the kernel routes signals like SIGINT. The post includes a working client implementation and a public demo running tetris.
key_points: A pseudoterminal is needed because connecting bash directly to a TCP socket breaks terminal features like Ctrl+C signal handling and programs like top that expect a real terminal
Linux uses sessions, process groups, and foreground process group membership to determine which processes receive SIGINT when Ctrl+C is pressed
Creating a pseudoterminal on Linux involves opening /dev/ptmx, getting the slave device name via ioctl, unlocking it, and connecting it to a child process
The client side requires putting the terminal into raw mode so keystrokes are sent immediately rather than line-buffered
Nearly all terminal configuration in Linux happens through ioctl system calls
money_quotes: The biggest difference between this program and SSH is that there's literally no security (not even a password) – anyone who can make a TCP connection to the server can get a shell and run commands.
A pseudoterminal is a lot like a bidirectional pipe or a socket – you have two ends, and they can both send and receive information.
If the 'terminal' is just a TCP connection, this doesn't work, because when you send 0x04 to a TCP connection, Linux won't magically send SIGINT to any process.
When you press Ctrl+C in a terminal, SIGINT gets sent to all the processes in the foreground process group.
All of the communication with the Linux kernel about terminals seems to be through various ioctl system calls.
I had no idea processes had a session ID!
I figured out how to do this in Go by going to grep.app and typing in syscall.TCSETS to find some other Go code that was doing the same thing.
posts.key_insight: Building a toy remote login server reveals why pseudoterminals exist: the Linux kernel needs a real terminal device to handle signal routing, process groups, and terminal-aware programs, which raw TCP sockets simply cannot provide.

posts.summary: Evans shares a framework for categorizing debugging skills, drawn from academic research on troubleshooting. She reframes five categories from a paper into actionable ways to improve: learn the codebase, learn the broader system, learn your tools, learn strategies, and get experience. Each category represents a different kind of knowledge needed for effective debugging, from specific codebase familiarity to general heuristics. She notes that experience is perhaps the most straightforward to acquire since programmers encounter bugs constantly throughout their careers.
key_points: Debugging skills can be categorized into five types: codebase knowledge, system/domain knowledge, tool knowledge, strategic knowledge, and experiential knowledge
Research suggests experts and novices use similar debugging strategies, but experts form more correct hypotheses due to experience
Debugging is itself one of the best ways to learn how a codebase works, creating a virtuous cycle
System knowledge (HTTP caching, CORS, database transactions) requires more intentional learning than codebase-specific knowledge
money_quotes: Debugging is also one of the best ways to learn how a new codebase works – seeing how something breaks helps you learn a lot about how it works.
Their findings did not show a significant difference in the strategies employed by the novices and experts. Experts simply formed more correct hypotheses and were more efficient at finding the fault.
I've had SO MANY bugs that were really frustrating and difficult the first time I ran into them, and very straightforward the fifth or tenth or 20th time.
All you need to do is investigate a million bugs, which is our whole life as programmers anyway.
I find that I often have to be a bit more intentional about learning systemic things like this – I need to actually take the time to look them up and read about them.
posts.key_insight: Debugging expertise comes from five distinct types of knowledge — codebase, system, tools, strategies, and experience — and recognizing which you're missing helps you improve more deliberately.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains how to send raw network packets in Python using tun/tap virtual network devices. The core problem is that if you try to implement TCP yourself on a normal network interface, the Linux kernel will interfere by closing connections it didn't initiate. Tun/tap solves this by creating a virtual network device that your program controls directly. The post walks through creating a tun interface, setting up NAT with iptables, and writing a minimal Python program that sends a hardcoded SYN packet to example.com and receives a SYN ACK reply. She also demonstrates using tcpdump to observe the packets on both the virtual and real network interfaces.
key_points: The Linux kernel's existing TCP implementation will kill TCP connections it didn't initiate, so you need tun/tap to experiment with your own TCP implementation
A tun device acts like a virtual network interface that a Python program can read from and write to like a regular file
NAT via iptables is required to route packets from the virtual tun device out to the real internet
tcpdump on different interfaces reveals how NAT rewrites IP addresses and shows TCP retry behavior like exponential backoff
This approach is much more reliable than the alternative of ARP spoofing, which is also sketchy to do on networks you don't own
money_quotes: the Linux kernel on your machine gets the SYN ACK, thinks "wtf?? I didn't make this connection??", and closes the connection
imagine I have a tiny computer in my network which is sending and receiving network packets. But instead of it being a real computer, it's just a Python program I wrote.

I'm not going to explain this ip addr add command because I don't understand it, I find ip to be very inscrutable and for now I'm resigned to just copying and pasting ip commands without fully understanding them.
The iptables part is very important because otherwise the packets would only exist inside my computer and wouldn't be sent to the internet, and what fun would that be?
There's actually a type of DDOS attack like this called SYN flooding, but just sending one or two SYN packets isn't a big deal.
And ARP spoofing is kind of a sketchy thing to do on a network you don't own.
posts.key_insight: Tun/tap virtual network devices let you bypass the kernel's TCP stack and send raw packets from userspace programs, making it possible to experiment with your own network protocol implementations.

posts.summary: The post explains why domain names sometimes end with a trailing dot, tracing the answer through DNS internals, zone files, dig output, and search domains. Evans starts by noting that the simple answer ('it means fully qualified') doesn't explain why it matters. She shows that DNS wire format doesn't use dots at all, then explains how zone files use the trailing dot to distinguish absolute from relative names. The key practical insight is that search domains (used in local networks and Kubernetes) can silently append suffixes to domain names, so the trailing dot explicitly signals 'nothing else gets added.' She concludes with practical advice: use trailing dots when configuring DNS, but not in browsers where it often breaks.
key_points: In DNS wire format, domain names contain no dots at all — they're encoded as length/string pairs, so the trailing dot is purely a convention of text representations.
Zone files use the trailing dot to distinguish fully qualified domain names from relative ones that get the zone's domain appended automatically.
Search domains (configured via /etc/resolv.conf) can silently modify lookups — 'grapefruit' becomes 'grapefruit.lan' — and the trailing dot prevents this modification.
dig's output format with semicolons and trailing dots is designed to be paste-able into zone files, which explains its otherwise puzzling formatting choices.
Using a trailing dot in browsers often breaks things because web servers expect Host headers without the dot.
money_quotes: Originally I thought I knew the answer to this ('uh, the dot at the end means the domain is fully qualified?'). And that's true – a fully qualified domain name is a domain with a '.' at the end! But that doesn't explain *why* dots at the end are useful or important.
When a computer sends a DNS request or response, the domain names in it don't have a trailing dot. Actually, the domain names don't have *any* dots.

So because domain names can actually be translated to something else in some cases, people like to put a '.' at the end to communicate 'THIS IS THE DOMAIN NAME, NOTHING GETS ADDED AT THE END, THIS IS THE WHOLE THING'.

I really wish dig had a +human flag that printed out all of this information in a more human readable way, but for now I'm too lazy to put in the work to actually contribute code to do that (and I'm a pretty bad C programmer) so I'll just complain about it on my blog instead :)

I always have to remind myself for the reasons for this because I rarely use zone files or search domains, so I often feel like – 'of course I mean google.com and not google.com.something.else! Why would I mean anything else?? That's silly!'
The technical term for 'THIS IS THE WHOLE THING' is 'fully qualified domain name' or 'FQDN'.
posts.key_insight: The trailing dot in domain names exists because both zone files and search domains can silently modify unqualified names, so the dot explicitly signals that the domain is complete and nothing should be appended.

posts.summary: Julia Evans walks through building a minimal DNS query tool in Ruby from scratch, demonstrating how to generate DNS queries and parse DNS responses in about 120 lines of code. She starts by capturing a real DNS query from Wireshark, then progressively replaces hardcoded bytes with proper encoding functions. The post covers the DNS packet format including headers, domain name encoding, and the tricky DNS compression scheme. She builds a complete working clone of `dig` that can resolve domain names to IP addresses, explaining each step of the binary protocol along the way.
key_points: DNS queries and responses are structured as concatenated binary fields that can be generated and parsed with basic byte manipulation
Wireshark is invaluable for understanding binary protocols—you can capture a working query and reverse-engineer the format from there
DNS compression, where domain names reference earlier positions in the packet, is the most complex part of parsing DNS responses and can even be exploited for infinite loops
The entire DNS query/response cycle is just sending and receiving a single UDP packet, making it one of the simplest network protocols to implement from scratch
Building things from scratch in ~120 lines reveals that protocols that seem mysterious are actually quite approachable when broken into small steps
money_quotes: I left out "how to generate and parse DNS queries" because I thought it was boring, but a few people pointed out that they did not know how to parse and generate DNS queries and they were interested in how to do it.
The easiest way to get a DNS query to play with and make sure our UDP connection is working is to just copy one that already works!

Along the way I'm going to try to explain how you could figure out some of this stuff yourself if you were trying to figure out how DNS queries are formatted from scratch. Mostly that's "poke around in Wireshark" and "read RFC 1035, the DNS RFC".
The hardest part of this (maybe surprisingly) is going to be "parse a DNS name".
All c00c is saying is: The first 2 bits mean "DNS compression ahead!" The remaining 14 bits are an integer.
A malicious actor could exploit this code by sending a DNS response with a DNS compression entry that points to itself, so that read_domain_name would end up in an infinite loop.
posts.key_insight: DNS query generation and parsing, which sounds intimidating, can be implemented from scratch in 120 lines of Ruby by starting with a captured packet from Wireshark and progressively replacing hardcoded bytes with proper encoding logic.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience learning Rust through Advent of Code, focusing on understanding compiler errors rather than just fixing them. She walks through two specific errors: a borrowing issue caused by temporary values being freed too early, and a confusing trait implementation error with Sum. For each error, she creates minimal reproductions and works through the underlying language concepts.
