key_points: There are two fundamentally different kinds of rebase: squashing commits on an ancestor (low risk) vs rebasing onto a diverged branch (merge conflict risk), and conflating them causes confusion
Undoing a bad rebase is significantly harder than undoing a bad merge, and newcomers have permanently lost work because they didn't know recovery was possible
Force pushing to shared branches is the most dangerous rebase-related practice, and even --force-with-lease has a subtle gotcha if you fetch right before pushing
People's arguments about rebase are often really arguments about required commit discipline levels: anything goes, squash-on-merge, or atomic beautiful commits
A merge-based workflow using 'squash and merge' can achieve clean history without rebase's risks
money_quotes: I've found that if many people have a very strong opinion that's different from mine, usually it's because they have different experiences around that thing from me.
I heard from several people that when they were new to rebase, they messed up a rebase and permanently lost a week of work that they then had to redo.
The only worse thing than having to hunt through the reflog is multiple different people having to hunt through the reflog.

I went into this really feeling like 'rebase is fine, what could go wrong?' But many of these problems actually have happened to me in the past, it's just that over the years I've learned how to avoid or fix all of them.
I've been wondering if when people argue about whether people 'should' use rebase or not, they're really arguing about which minimum level of commit discipline should be required.
I've never really seen anyone share best practices for rebase, other than 'never force push to a shared branch'. All of these honestly make me a lot more reluctant to recommend using rebase.
posts.key_insight: The widespread frustration with git rebase stems not from rebase being inherently bad, but from the accumulated hidden knowledge needed to use it safely—knowledge that experienced users have internalized but rarely share with newcomers.

posts.summary: Julia Evans investigates how git cherry-pick actually works under the hood, discovering it's not simply applying a patch as she previously assumed. She demonstrates that naive patch application fails without merge conflict resolution, while cherry-pick uses a 3-way merge internally. The post explains 3-way merge by showing how two changed files plus their common ancestor allow git to intelligently combine changes. She maps this to cherry-pick (using the parent commit as base) and revert (same thing but with commits flipped), and coins the term '3-way patch' for this technique of specifying patches as before/after file pairs rather than diffs.
key_points: Git cherry-pick is not applying a patch file — it performs a 3-way merge using the parent commit as the base, which enables proper merge conflict resolution
A 3-way merge works by comparing two versions against their common ancestor, letting git merge changes rather than files
Git revert is implemented identically to cherry-pick but with the base and target commits swapped to apply a reverse patch
The 'index' line in patch files contains blob IDs that enable git apply --3way to reconstruct the full 3-way merge context
This 3-way merge approach to patching predates git entirely, originating in the late 1970s
money_quotes: I thought that git cherry-pick was basically applying a patch, but when I tried to actually do it that way, it didn't work!
The way I think about the 3-way merge is – git merges changes, not files.
When we talk about git 'applying a patch' (as you do in a rebase or revert or cherry-pick), it's not actually creating a patch file and applying it. Instead, it's doing a 3-way merge.
Revert and cherry-pick are so closely related in git that they're actually implemented in the same file: revert.c.
The point is that the 3-way patch is a much better way to patch than a normal patch, because you have a lot more context for merging when you have both full files.
I was pretty surprised to learn that I didn't actually understand the core way that git applies patches internally – it was really cool to learn about!
posts.key_insight: Git cherry-pick, revert, and rebase all use 3-way merge rather than simple patch application, using the parent commit as a common ancestor to enable intelligent conflict resolution.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience migrating from basic nix-env usage to nix flakes after 9 months of using nix. She walks through creating a flake that consolidates all her installed packages into a single configuration file, drawing an analogy between flakes and Docker containers to make the concept accessible. Along the way, she encounters six distinct problems including git tracking quirks, unfree packages, relative path bugs, and mysterious daemon errors. Despite the friction, she arrives at a simple setup using a flake.nix file and a 6-line bash script, deliberately avoiding more complex tools like home-manager in favor of a configuration she can actually understand.
key_points: Nix flakes can be loosely understood as self-contained environments similar to Docker containers, but with much stronger reproducibility guarantees and native Mac support
Flakes have a surprising behavior where untracked git files are completely invisible to nix evaluation, causing confusing errors for newcomers
The practical advantages of flakes over nix-env are modest for personal use: easier setup replication and simpler uninstallation via config file editing
Keeping nix configuration deliberately simple and avoiding abstraction layers you don't understand is a valid strategy for long-term maintainability
Nix's biggest practical advantage may simply be its large repository of pre-compiled binary packages rather than its theoretical properties like reproducibility
money_quotes: people always say that nix is good because it's "declarative" or "reproducible" or "functional" or whatever but my main motivation for using nix personally is that it has a lot of binary packages
if you're in a git directory and your flake.nix file isn't tracked by git yet (because you just created it and are trying to get it to work), nix will COMPLETELY IGNORE YOUR FILE
I have a long history of fiddling with my computer setup (Arch Linux ftw) and so I decided to have a Day Of Fiddling
it's important to me to keep my configuration as simple as possible without a lot of extra abstraction layers that I don't understand
I still kind of like nix after using it for 9 months despite how confused I am about it all the time, I feel like once I get things working they don't usually break

My solutions to all the nix problems I described are maybe not The Best (tm), but I'm happy that I figured out a way to install stuff that just involves one relatively simple flake.nix file and a 6-line bash script
posts.key_insight: Nix flakes offer stronger reproducibility than alternatives but come with significant papercuts and confusing error messages, making a deliberately simple configuration that you actually understand more valuable than a sophisticated one you don't.

posts.summary: Evans explores the gap between how people intuitively think about git branches (as offshoots from a parent branch) and how git actually implements them (as pointers to commits with full history). She argues that the intuitive model is actually quite useful because it closely matches how rebases, merges, and pull requests work in practice. The key disconnect is that git has no concept of branch hierarchy or parent-child relationships between branches, forcing users to specify these relationships explicitly every time. She also highlights confusing UI choices like the different meanings of '..' and '...' in git log versus git diff.
key_points: Git branches are stored as simple text files containing a commit ID, with no concept of parent-child relationships between branches
The intuitive 'offshoot' model of branches closely matches how rebases, merges, and GitHub PRs actually work, making it more useful than people give it credit for
Git's lack of branch hierarchy means it can't prevent confusing operations like rebasing main onto a feature branch
The '..' and '...' syntax means different things for git log vs git diff, making it hard to inspect just the 'offshoot' commits on a branch
GitHub's concept of a default branch adds back some of the hierarchy that git itself lacks
money_quotes: In git, a branch is the full history of every previous commit, not just the "offshoot" commits.
In general, even if people's intuition about a topic is technically incorrect in some ways, people usually have the intuition they do for very legitimate reasons! "Wrong" models can be super useful.

Git doesn't have any notion of whether a branch is an "offshoot" of another branch, it won't give you any guidance about if/when it's appropriate to rebase branch X on branch Y. You just have to know.
I hear the statement "the main branch is not special" a lot and I've been puzzled about it – in most of the repositories I work in, main is pretty special!
You have to tell git explicitly about the relationship between branches every single time you run a git command like git rebase or git merge, and if you make a mistake things can get really weird.
So to see the 2 commits x and y with git log, you need to use 2 dots (..), but to look at the same commits with git diff, you need to use 3 dots (...).

This all seems extremely obvious in retrospect, but it took me a long time to figure out what a more "intuitive" idea of a branch even might be because I was so used to the technical "a branch is a reference to a commit" definition.
posts.key_insight: People's intuitive model of git branches as offshoots from a parent branch is actually very close to how merges, rebases, and pull requests work in practice—git just refuses to track that relationship, forcing users to re-specify it every time.

posts.summary: Julia Evans built git-commit-folders, a tool that mounts a git repository as a filesystem where every commit appears as a folder, branches appear as symlinks, and you can explore old code by navigating directories. She experimented with FUSE, NFS, and WebDav implementations on Mac OS, ultimately focusing on NFS since FUSE requires kernel extensions that Mac is making harder to install. The project aims to make the concept that 'every git commit is like a folder' feel concrete and intuitive. Along the way she encountered numerous practical problems including handling repositories with millions of commits, stale NFS file handles, inode numbering, and mysterious error messages. She found the tool genuinely useful for tasks like searching for deleted functions and quickly viewing files on other branches without setting up worktrees.
key_points: Git commits really are similar to folders internally — each contains a directory listing — but aren't implemented as actual folders to save disk space
FUSE is increasingly difficult to use on Mac OS due to kernel extension restrictions, so NFS provides a native alternative for mounting custom filesystems
Branches and tags are represented as symlinks to commit folders, directly mirroring how git references work under the hood
Scaling to large repositories required creative solutions like two-level prefix directories (mirroring .git/objects) and caching packed commit hashes in memory
The tool proved genuinely useful for grepping deleted functions across branch history and quickly viewing files on other branches
money_quotes: Every Git commit contains a directory listing of the files in it, and that directory can have subdirectories, etc. It's just that git commits aren't actually implemented as folders to save disk space.
FUSE is pretty annoying to use on Mac – you need to install a kernel extension, and Mac OS seems to be making it harder and harder to install kernel extensions for security reasons.

Branches are symlinks. In the filesystem mounted by git-commit-folders, commits are the only real folders – everything else (branches, tags, etc) is a symlink to a commit. This mirrors how git works under the hood.
None of these are the most efficient way to do this, but personally I always forget the syntax and navigating a filesystem feels easier to me.
git worktree also lets you have multiple branches checked out at the same time, but to me it feels weird to set up an entire worktree just to look at 1 file.
Each commit hash is only 20 bytes so caching 1 million commit hashes isn't a big deal, it's just 20MB.
Eventually I realized that I could use Wireshark to look at all the NFS packets being sent back and forth, which made some of this stuff easier to debug.
posts.key_insight: Git commits are conceptually just folders containing snapshots of your code, and mounting them as an actual filesystem makes this mental model tangible while providing a surprisingly practical way to explore repository history.

posts.summary: Julia Evans reflects on her fourth year running Wizard Zines full-time, covering a productive 2023 that included publishing a zine on integers and floats, creating interactive playgrounds, releasing 'Implement DNS in a Weekend,' giving a Strange Loop keynote, and writing extensively about Git. She hired an operations manager to handle business logistics, migrated her social presence to Mastodon, and started research for a new Git zine. The year's recurring theme is that deep educational projects take longer than expected, and she's learning to accept that pace.
key_points: Released 'Implement DNS in a Weekend' to strong community response, with dozens of implementations appearing in over a dozen programming languages
Her research into why Git is hard challenged her assumption that people just need to learn Git's internal data model — talking to users revealed the problem is more nuanced
She's deliberately releasing software in unfinished states to reduce self-imposed pressure for perfection, favoring exploration over polish
Hiring an operations manager freed her to focus on writing and coding rather than business logistics
She's come to terms with the reality that deep educational projects simply take longer than anticipated
money_quotes: Writing my own working shitty implementations was relatively easy, but writing clear tutorials that other people can follow is not.
I originally thought 'to understand Git, people just need to learn git's internal data model!' But the more I talk to people about their struggles with Git, the less I think that's true.

I've been trying to put a little less pressure on myself to release software that's Amazing and Perfect – sometimes I have an idea that I think is cool but don't really have the time or energy to fully execute on it.
This is one of the hardest projects I've ever worked on, because over the last 10 years of using it I'd completely lost sight of what's hard about Git.
I've started to come to terms with the fact that projects always just take longer than I think they will.
I've been working on this Git zine since September and I still don't completely understand why Git is hard yet.
Things take a long time and that's okay.
posts.key_insight: Creating clear educational material is far harder than building the underlying implementations, because understanding why something is hard requires losing your own expertise bias.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience setting up NixOS on a personal server to replace her chaotic Ansible-managed setup. She walks through the process step by step, from installing NixOS via nixos-infect on Hetzner, to creating a flake, deploying configuration, and setting up a Go service with Caddy. She finds NixOS more reliable than Ansible because it controls the entire OS rather than just parts of it, but acknowledges the Nix language syntax and debugging experience remain confusing. The post is a practical guide mixed with honest reflections on the tradeoffs of using Nix.
key_points: NixOS feels more reliable than Ansible because it controls the entire OS, so ad-hoc changes get blown away on rebuild rather than persisting as drift
Nix flakes have counterintuitive git rules: files must be git-added but don't need to be committed, and unstaged changes are fine
Nix error messages can be deeply unhelpful, with truncated stack traces, missing line numbers, and cached errors hiding the real problem
SystemD's DynamicUser and StateDirectory features provide a clean way to run services with isolated users and persistent storage without boilerplate
Despite finding Nix difficult and confusing to debug, she appreciates the result of having all service configuration defined declaratively in one file
money_quotes: I think the reason NixOS feels more reliable than Ansible to me is that NixOS is the operating system.
Because Nix has so much control over the OS, I think that if I tried to make any ad-hoc changes at all to my Nix system, Nix would just blow them away the next time I ran nixos-rebuild.
These rules feel very counterintuitive to me (why require that you git add files but allow unstaged changes?) but that's how it works.

I definitely skipped Step 1 in nixos-infect's README ('Read and understand the script'), but I didn't feel too worried because I was running it on a new instance and I figured that if something went wrong I'd just delete it.
My plan for now with NixOS is to just keep copying and pasting that my-service.nix file above forever.
I will say that I find using Nix to be very difficult and I really struggle when debugging Nix problems, but I kind of like it anyway.
Maybe because I'm not using Linux on my laptop right now I miss having linux evenings and Nix feels like a replacement for that.
posts.key_insight: NixOS offers more reliable server configuration than Ansible by controlling the entire OS state rather than just the parts you remember to manage, but the learning curve and debugging experience remain genuinely painful.

posts.summary: Evans explores how people mentally model Git commits, finding through a Mastodon poll that opinions are nearly evenly split between thinking of commits as diffs (51%) versus snapshots (42%). She explains that Git internally stores commits as snapshots but uses packfiles for compression, making both mental models partially correct. She argues that 'wrong' mental models (like thinking commits are diffs) can be extremely useful for everyday Git usage. The post examines the practical advantages of each perspective and why the common advice to 'just learn Git internals' often fails as a teaching strategy.
key_points: Git internally stores commits as snapshots, not diffs, but uses packfiles with delta compression to save disk space — making both mental models partially true
The 'just learn the internals' approach to teaching Git hasn't been as successful as expected, because people with correct internal knowledge still struggle with Git
Wrong mental models about Git (commits as diffs, editable commit messages, movable commits) form a coherent and useful set of ideas well-supported by Git's UI
People are reluctant to share their 'wrong' mental models because Git Explainers will correct them, creating a chilling effect on honest discussion about how people actually think about Git
The diff vs snapshot framing maps to different use cases: diffs are better for thinking about changes and rebasing, snapshots are better for understanding checkouts and merge conflicts
money_quotes: I've been considering other options besides 'just learn how Git represents things internally, and everything will make sense.'
I think wrong mental models are often extremely useful, and this one doesn't seem very problematic to me for every day Git usage.
Checking out a Git commit is always fast: it's just as easy for Git to check out a commit from yesterday as it is to check out a commit from 1 million commits ago.
It takes deltas, turns them into a snapshot, and then calculates a diff. It feels a little weird because it starts with a diff-like-thing and ends up with another diff-like-thing.

People get really into policing 'wrong' mental models even though those 'wrong' models are often really useful, so folks are reluctant to share their 'wrong' ideas for fear of some Git Explainer coming out of the woodwork.
I really like that it makes the thing that we deal with the most often (the diff) the most fundamental – it seems really intuitive to me.
Git often gets confused about file moves because git only stores snapshots, so when it says 'moved old.py -> new.py', it's just guessing because the contents are similar.
posts.key_insight: Wrong mental models about Git can be more useful than correct ones for everyday work, and the near-even split between people who think of commits as diffs versus snapshots suggests there's no single 'right' way to conceptualize them.

posts.summary: Julia Evans provides a guided tour of the .git directory, explaining each component with concrete examples. She covers the core data model (HEAD, branches, commits, trees, blobs) and supporting structures (reflog, remote-tracking branches, tags, stash, config, hooks, staging area). Each section shows the actual file contents using cat or git cat-file commands, demystifying what are often treated as opaque internals. She emphasizes that while understanding .git internals is valuable, it doesn't explain everything about git — merges, rebases, collaboration workflows, and conflict resolution require separate understanding.
key_points: The core of git is just 5 things: HEAD, branches, commits, trees, and blobs — all stored as small files in .git
Branches, remote-tracking branches, tags, and stash are all just commit IDs in .git/refs, but git treats each type very differently
The stash is unique among reflogs because entries are deleted when you git stash pop, unlike branch reflogs which keep entries for ~90 days
Understanding the .git directory structure alone doesn't explain merges, rebases, collaboration workflows, or conflict resolution
Tree permissions look like Unix permissions but are restricted to only 644 and 755
money_quotes: HEAD is a tiny file that just contains the name of your current branch.
A branch is stored as a tiny file that just contains 1 commit ID.
When git status says "you're up to date with origin/main", it's just looking at this. It's often out of date, you can update it with git fetch origin main.
Unlike branches, when you make new commits it doesn't update the tag.
The stash is the only reflog in git where things get deleted very soon after they're added.
Every ref is a commit ID, but the different types of refs are treated VERY differently by git, so I find it useful to think about them separately even though they all use the same file format.

One of the most common pieces of advice I hear about git is "just learn how the .git directory is structured and then you'll understand everything!" I love understanding the internals of things more than anyone, but there's a LOT that "how the .git directory is structured" doesn't explain.
posts.key_insight: The .git directory is mostly just small text files containing commit IDs, and understanding this structure is useful but insufficient — git's complexity lives in the operations and workflows built on top of it.

posts.summary: The post explains what it means for local and remote git branches to diverge, how to recognize the situation, and what to do about it. Evans walks through the four possible states of local vs remote branches and focuses on the diverged case. She shows three ways to detect divergence (git status, git push errors, git pull errors) and notes that git's error messages can be confusing for newcomers. She then presents three main resolution strategies: rebasing to keep both sets of changes, force-pushing to overwrite remote changes, or hard resetting to overwrite local changes. The key message is that there's no single best solution—it depends on your workflow and why the divergence happened.
key_points: Git's error messages for diverged branches are intimidating and don't clearly explain what's happening, making the problem harder than it needs to be
There are exactly four states for local vs remote branches (up to date, local behind, remote behind, diverged) and understanding this model makes the problem tractable
The three main solutions map to three situations: keep both changes (rebase), overwrite remote (force push), or overwrite local (hard reset)
Force-with-lease is recommended as a safer alternative to force push, but it's not foolproof—VSCode's autofetch feature can undermine it
When you accidentally committed to main, the pattern of branching first then resetting is a safe way to preserve work while fixing the divergence
money_quotes: If you're not used to interpreting git's error messages, it's nontrivial to even realize that your main has diverged from the remote main.
There's no "best" way to resolve branches that have diverged – it really depends on your workflow for git and why the situation is happening.
I'd rather be warned "hey, these branches have diverged, how do you want to handle it?" and decide for myself if I want to rebase or not.
As always git reset --hard is a dangerous action and you can permanently lose your uncommitted work.
I'd never really thought about how confusing the git push and git pull error messages can be if you're not used to reading them.
posts.key_insight: Diverged branches aren't a single problem with a single fix—recognizing which of three situations you're in (keep both, overwrite remote, or overwrite local) determines the right resolution strategy.

posts.summary: Julia Evans surveyed her Mastodon followers about their favorite git config options and compiled the most popular ones into a ranked guide. The post covers options ranging from pull behavior (pull.rebase, pull.ff) and merge conflict display (zdiff3) to diff algorithms (histogram) and push defaults. She explains each option with practical examples, showing how merge.conflictstyle diff3 adds original code to conflict markers and how diff.algorithm histogram produces clearer diffs when functions are reordered. Evans shares her own minimal config and the three options she added after writing the post, while noting she's learned to be cautious about changing too many settings at once.
key_points: The most popular git config options focus on avoiding accidental merge commits (pull.ff only, pull.rebase true) and making merge conflicts more readable (merge.conflictstyle zdiff3)
diff.algorithm histogram produces much clearer diffs than the default when code blocks are reordered, and is more popular than the patience algorithm
push.autoSetupRemote true is preferable to push.default current because it also enables pulling from the matching remote branch
Crowdsourcing popular options from real users is far more efficient than reading through all 600+ git config options in the documentation
Git's defaults have changed significantly over the years, so some popular config options people set are now actually the default behavior
money_quotes: I always wish that command line tools came with data about how popular their various options are, like: 'basically nobody uses this one' or '80% of people use this, probably take a look'
Having a list of 20 or 30 options to consider feels so much more efficient than combing through a list of all 600 or so git config options
I've learned to be cautious about setting new config options – it takes me a long time to get used to the new behaviour and if I change too many things at once I just get confused.
I'm always amazed by how useful to just ask a lot of people what stuff they like and then list the most commonly mentioned ones
I'm pretty sure it doesn't make sense to set both of them at once, since --ff-only overrides --rebase.
Someone explained it as 'detect data corruption eagerly. Rarely matters but has saved my entire team a couple times'
I usually set all of my options globally because it stresses me out to have different git behaviour in different repositories.
posts.key_insight: Crowdsourcing which git config options real developers actually use is far more valuable than reading documentation, because it surfaces the 20 options that matter from the 600 that exist.

posts.summary: Julia Evans investigates why so many git users feel unconfident about understanding HEAD, discovering it's more complicated than she initially thought. She breaks HEAD into three distinct concepts: the .git/HEAD file, HEAD as a revision parameter in commands, and HEAD as it appears in various git output formats. She explains detached HEAD state, how to interpret git log's parenthetical references, and why HEAD in merge conflicts is particularly confusing because its meaning flips between merge and rebase operations. She concludes that git's inconsistent terminology around HEAD makes it harder to learn than it needs to be.
key_points: HEAD has three closely related but distinct meanings: the .git/HEAD file contents, HEAD as a revision parameter, and HEAD in command output
The .git/HEAD file contains either a branch reference or a raw commit ID, and the latter triggers 'detached HEAD state'
HEAD in merge conflicts is genuinely confusing because during a rebase, HEAD refers to the other commit you're rebasing onto, not what HEAD was when you ran git rebase
Git's terminology is inconsistent: it talks about 'detached HEAD' but never 'attached HEAD', and 'on a branch' but never 'not on a branch', making these concepts harder to connect
The git log output uses 'HEAD -> main' to indicate an active branch versus 'HEAD,' to indicate detached state, a subtle distinction
money_quotes: Usually when people say that a topic is confusing when I think it's not, the reason is that there's actually some hidden complexity that I wasn't considering.
Having no current branch is a little dangerous because if you make new commits, those commits won't be attached to any branch – they'll be orphaned!
The fact that the meaning of HEAD changes depending on whether I'm doing a rebase or merge is really just too confusing for me and I find it much simpler to just ignore HEAD entirely.
How is the user supposed to guess that HEAD detached has anything to do with branches at all, or that 'on branch main' has anything to do with HEAD?
I've honestly never actually heard the term 'revision parameter' before, but that's the term that'll get you to the documentation for this concept.
Git talks about 'detached HEAD state', but never about 'attached HEAD state' – git's documentation never uses the term 'attached' at all to refer to HEAD.
posts.key_insight: HEAD in git is more confusing than it appears because it conflates three related concepts and its meaning changes depending on context, particularly flipping between merge and rebase operations.

posts.summary: Evans explores what 'current branch' actually means in git by testing four different definitions across six scenarios. She compares what .git/HEAD contains, what git status reports, what was most recently checked out, and what the shell prompt shows. Through scenarios like detached HEAD, mid-rebase, bare repositories, and fresh git init, she demonstrates that these definitions frequently disagree with each other. The post concludes that 'current branch' is not as well-defined a concept as the git glossary implies, and that git does significant work behind the scenes to track context beyond what HEAD alone captures.
key_points: Four plausible definitions of 'current branch' (HEAD file, git status, last checkout, shell prompt) diverge in edge cases like rebases, tag checkouts, and bare repos
During a rebase, HEAD points to a detached commit but the 'real' current branch is preserved in .git/rebase-merge/head-name and restored afterward
When checking out a tag, .git/HEAD stores a commit ID while git status and the prompt helpfully resolve it back to the tag name
Git status provides richer context than HEAD alone, distinguishing between detached states, rebases in progress, and bare repositories
The concept of 'current branch' is genuinely ambiguous in git's design, not just confusing to newcomers
money_quotes: I originally thought that these 4 definitions were all more or less the same, but after chatting with some people on Mastodon, I realized that they're more different from each other than I thought.

My original instinct when talking about git was to agree with the git glossary and say that HEAD and the 'current branch' mean the exact same thing. But this doesn't seem as ironclad as I used to think anymore!
I have a lot more sympathy than I used to for the definition 'the current branch is whatever you last checked out'.

Git does a lot of work to remember which branch you last checked out (even if you're currently doing a bisect or a merge or something else that temporarily moves HEAD off of that branch) and it feels weird to ignore that.

it doesn't have the usual implications of being in 'detached HEAD state' – if you make a commit, it won't get orphaned! Instead, assuming you finish the rebase, it'll get absorbed into the rebase and put somewhere in the middle of your branch.
The more I think about git the more I can understand why people get confused.
'current branch' doesn't seem completely well defined
posts.key_insight: Git's 'current branch' is not a single well-defined concept but at least four different things that usually agree but meaningfully diverge during rebases, tag checkouts, and other edge cases.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares results from dozens of Mastodon polls about how people use, understand, and think about Git. The polls cover workflows (merge vs rebase), mental models (commits as diffs vs snapshots), terminology confidence (HEAD, fast-forward, reference), and common problems (merge conflicts, lost work). She acknowledges the polls are unscientific but finds them useful for checking her assumptions against how others actually use Git. The results consistently reveal that many experienced developers have significant gaps in their understanding of core Git terminology.
key_points: Git users are roughly split between rebase-first (41%) and merge-first (29%) workflows, with 24% using both regularly

58% of people handle merge conflicts by editing the text file by hand rather than using dedicated tools, and most merge conflicts are infrequent (52% deal with nontrivial ones only a few times a year)
Large percentages of Git users report having 'literally no idea' what core terms mean: 14% for HEAD, 21% for fast-forward, 29% for reference
50% of people think of commits as diffs rather than snapshots (42%), despite Git internally storing snapshots
80% of respondents use Git on the command line and 71% display their current branch in their shell prompt
money_quotes: Polls on social media that I thought about for approximately 45 seconds before posting are not the most rigorous way of doing user research, so I'm pretty cautious about drawing conclusions from them.

It's helpful for me to know that there are a lot of people out there who rarely/never use rebase, because I use rebase all the time – it's a good reminder that my experiences isn't necessarily representative.

For the poll 'how confident do you feel that you know what HEAD means in git?', 14% of people replied 'literally no idea'. That tells me to be careful about assuming that people know what HEAD means in my writing.

These polls gave me the impression that for a lot of git terms (fast-forward, reference, HEAD), there are a lot of git users who have 'literally no idea' what they mean. That makes me want to be careful about using and defining those terms.
I thought it was interesting in the next one that 'edit the weird text file by hand' was most people's preference.
posts.key_insight: Even experienced Git users have significant gaps in their understanding of core terminology, which means technical writers should never assume familiarity with terms like HEAD, fast-forward, or reference.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience learning to crochet tiny cacti and other amigurumi as a non-computer hobby. She describes her deliberately low-pressure, chaotic approach: modifying patterns on the fly, skipping recommended tools like safety eyes and stitch markers, and embracing mistakes as learning opportunities. The post covers practical notes on yarn types, hook sizes, stitches learned, and strategies for dealing with crochet's counting requirements. She emphasizes keeping the hobby compact and low-investment, repurposing old materials instead of buying new ones. The post is part of April Cools Day, where tech bloggers write about topics outside their usual domain.
key_points: Embracing imperfection and 'wonkiness' as part of the creative process, deliberately avoiding pressure to be good at a new hobby
Modifying patterns chaotically to accommodate mistakes rather than undoing work, treating errors as learning opportunities
Keeping the hobby minimal and compact — avoiding buying too much stuff, repurposing old materials, and fitting everything in a shoebox
Learning new stitches just-in-time by starting patterns without reviewing them fully, then watching videos only when encountering unknown stitches
Crochet is forgiving of mistakes and experimentation, making it a good hobby for someone used to more exacting computer work
money_quotes: I've been trying to do some non-computer hobbies, without putting a lot of pressure on myself to be "good" at them.
They are a little wonky and I like them.
I like working in a pretty chaotic way and I think you need to be a lot more careful when you make clothing so that it will actually fit.

I've been modifying all of the patterns I make in a somewhat chaotic way, often just because I made a mistake somewhere along the way and then decide to move forward and change the pattern to adjust for the mistake instead of undoing my work.
I still don't really know how to choose hook sizes but that doesn't seem to have a lot of consequences when making cacti.
I've been trying to not buy too much stuff, because I never know if I'll get bored with a new hobby, and if I get bored it's annoying to have a bunch of stuff lying around.
There are definitely still a lot of things I'm doing "wrong" but it's fun to learn through trial and error.
posts.key_insight: Approaching a new hobby with low expectations, embracing mistakes, and resisting the urge to optimize or buy more stuff makes learning more enjoyable and sustainable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans examines several confusing Git error messages, explaining why they're misleading and what she does to work around them. She covers messages from git push, git pull, git checkout, git switch, git rebase, and git clean, noting patterns like ambiguous references to HEAD and 'them' that change meaning depending on context. She acknowledges that improving error messages is genuinely difficult due to translation requirements, funding challenges, and the difficulty of knowing whether a new message is actually better. The post reveals that even experienced Git users develop workarounds rather than truly understanding every message, and that much confusion stems from Git's dual-purpose commands and inconsistent terminology.
key_points: Git's error messages often hide critical context: git push gives the same error whether a branch is behind or diverged, requiring git status to distinguish
Git's dual-purpose commands like git checkout cause confusing errors because git can't know if you meant a branch or a path
The meaning of HEAD and 'them' in merge conflict messages changes depending on whether you're merging, rebasing, or cherry-picking, which is a persistent source of confusion
Even experienced users develop coping strategies (mentally replacing 'reference' with 'branch', ignoring parts of messages) rather than fully understanding Git's error output
Improving error messages is harder than it looks due to translation into 19 languages, lack of funding, and difficulty measuring whether changes actually help
money_quotes: Sometimes I actually am confused by the error messages, I'm just used to being confused.
It's very hard to imagine how a beginner to git could easily use this hint to sort through all these options on their own.
90% of the time when a git error message says 'reference' I just mentally replace it with 'branch' in my head.
My impression is that a lot of git users have literally no idea what a 'reference' is in git.
What 'them' refers to depends on whether you did a merge or rebase or cherry-pick.
I'm personally way too lazy to do that so even though I've probably been using git clean for 10 years I still had no idea what -i stood for.
Usually I just chaotically run git clean -f to delete all my untracked files and hope for the best.
posts.key_insight: Git's error messages are confusing not because users lack knowledge, but because the messages omit context, use inconsistent terminology, and overload users with options instead of guiding them toward the most likely fix.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine 'How Git Works,' designed for people who've used git for years but still find it intimidating. She explains that git's confusing terminology, misleading messages, and inconsistent behavior make it genuinely hard to learn from usage alone, but once you understand the internal logic, dealing with its quirks becomes routine. The zine focuses on git's observable behavior rather than deep internals, connecting what users see in the UI to what's happening under the hood. She worked with collaborators for 8 months and incorporated feedback from 66 beta readers to refine the explanations.
key_points: Git is genuinely confusing due to misleading messages, inconsistent behavior, and jargon — not because users are doing something wrong
Once you learn what git actually means by its weird error messages, handling them becomes a 10-second routine rather than a source of fear
The zine focuses on git's UI behavior rather than deep internals, because knowing the internals doesn't automatically clarify the user experience
Git's documentation can be technically accurate but still misleading — like 'up to date with origin/main' not meaning what most people think
Despite its UX problems, git's speed, backwards compatibility, and simple internal file formats make it genuinely lovable
money_quotes: I wrote this zine for people who have been using git for years and are still afraid of it.
I think it sucks to be afraid of the tools that you use in your work every day!
The more I heard from people how about how confusing they find git, the more it became clear that git really does not make it easy to figure out what its internal logic is just by using it.
After I learned what git actually means by all of its weird error messages, dealing with it became pretty routine!
Even if you've read one of these guides to git's internals, it isn't totally obvious how to connect that information to what you actually see in git's user interface.
The stash is one of the places in git where it's easiest to lose your work in a way that's incredibly annoying to recover from.
It's always hard to hear from beta readers that a page I thought made sense is actually extremely confusing, and fixing those problems before the final version makes the zine so much better.
posts.key_insight: Git's real difficulty isn't its internals but the gap between its confusing UI messages and the straightforward logic underneath — once you bridge that gap, the fear disappears.

posts.summary: Evans explores the use cases for shell job control (fg, bg, Ctrl+Z) after polling her Mastodon audience for their habits. She explains the three process states (foreground, background, stopped) and the commands for moving between them. The post catalogs nine practical reasons people use job control, from killing unresponsive processes to suspending CPU-hungry programs. Evans admits she mostly prefers opening new terminal tabs but found several use cases compelling, particularly killing Ctrl+C-resistant processes and running tcpdump in the background alongside other commands.
key_points: Shell job control manages three process states: foreground, background, and stopped, with commands like fg, bg, Ctrl+Z, jobs, kill, and disown to move between them
Ctrl+Z followed by kill is a faster way to deal with processes that don't respond to Ctrl+C than switching tabs to find and kill them
Running tcpdump in the background while executing network commands in the same terminal lets you see interleaved output with matching timestamps, avoiding confusion from tab-switching
Job control becomes essential in constrained environments like single-user mode, restricted machines, or SSH sessions without tmux/screen
Many people's first encounter with job control is accidentally pressing Ctrl+Z and needing to learn fg to recover their process
money_quotes: "Job control" is a set of commands for seeing which processes are running in a terminal and moving processes between these 3 states
I run into processes that don't respond to Ctrl+C pretty regularly, and it's always a little annoying – I usually switch terminal tabs to find and kill and the process.

This really surprised me because I usually think of having the output of lots of different commands interleaved as being a bad thing, but one person said that they like to do this with tcpdump specifically and I think that actually sounds extremely useful.

Many people replied that they didn't use job control intentionally, but that they sometimes accidentally ran Ctrl+Z, which stopped whatever program was running, so they needed to learn how to use fg to bring it back to the foreground.

Probably the most obvious reason to use job control to manage multiple processes is "because you have to" – maybe you're in single-user mode, or on a very restricted computer, or SSH'd into a machine that doesn't have tmux or screen.
I feel like I'd also be likely to switch terminal tabs and end up wondering "wait… where did I put my editor???" and have to go searching for it.
posts.key_insight: Shell job control is an underused but practical tool with specific scenarios where it outperforms the common habit of opening new terminal tabs, especially for killing unresponsive processes and correlating interleaved output from background tools like tcpdump.

posts.summary: The post categorizes terminal text input into four modes: baseline (no input library), readline-based, alternative libraries like libedit, and fully custom systems. Evans explains why entering and editing text at the command line is surprisingly complicated, primarily due to inconsistency between programs. She traces readline's keybindings back to Emacs, explains why some programs don't use readline (licensing, simplicity, minimal interactivity), and recommends rlwrap as a way to add readline support to any program. The core message is that understanding which input mode you're in makes the terminal feel predictable rather than chaotic.
key_points: Terminal text input falls into four categories: no input library, readline, alternative libraries like libedit, and fully custom systems, each with different capabilities
Even baseline terminal mode provides useful shortcuts like Ctrl+W (delete word) and Ctrl+U (delete line) that work everywhere, without any library support
Readline's keybindings originate from Emacs, which explains why they feel arbitrary to vim users
GPL licensing of readline pushes some programs (like macOS Python) toward alternatives like libedit, creating subtle feature gaps
Custom input systems (fish, ipython, atuin) are usually readline-inspired, supporting the same core shortcuts while adding better autocomplete and features
money_quotes: It took me maybe 15 years of using the terminal every single day to get used to using Ctrl+A to go to the beginning of the line.
A big part of what makes entering text in the terminal hard is the inconsistency between how different programs handle entering text.
One of my absolute favourite things is that if you have a program like nc without readline support, you can just run rlwrap nc to turn it into a program with readline support!
It took me a very long time to understand where these keybindings come from (why Ctrl+A to go to the beginning of a line??? so weird!)
I'm extremely ignorant of most of readline's features. I only use maybe 5 keyboard shortcuts, so as long as they support the 5 basic commands I know I feel pretty comfortable.
Being able to diagnose what's going on like this makes the command line feel a more predictable and less chaotic.
I've always thought that vi mode seems really cool, but for some reason even though I'm a vim user it's never stuck for me.
posts.key_insight: Terminal text input is confusing because programs silently use four different input systems with varying capabilities, but learning to identify which one you're in transforms the experience from chaotic to predictable.

posts.summary: Evans describes discovering that Go structs are copied on assignment, a basic language behavior she'd missed despite years of casual Go usage. A bug in a range loop where she returned a pointer to a copied struct rather than the original led her to the '100 Go Mistakes' book, which revealed additional gaps in her understanding. She catalogs several Go pitfalls including slice append side effects and the distinction between value and pointer receivers. The post serves as both a practical Go debugging story and an endorsement of the '100 common mistakes' learning format.
key_points: Go structs are copied on assignment, meaning modifying a copy doesn't affect the original — a subtle difference from Python, JS, and Java where objects are references
Range loops in Go copy each element, so returning a pointer to the loop variable gives you a pointer to a copy, not to the original slice element
Slice sub-expressions share backing arrays, so appending to a sub-slice can silently mutate the original slice
The '100 common mistakes' book format is effective because it lets experienced developers quickly triage which gaps in their knowledge matter most
Years of casual use of a language can leave fundamental misconceptions undetected until the right bug surfaces
money_quotes: I thought I more or less understood the basics of the language, but this week I've been writing a lot more Go than usual while working on some upgrades to Mess with DNS, and ran into a bug that revealed I was missing a very basic concept!

When I learned that I was mistaken about how assignment worked in Go I was really taken aback, like – it's such a basic fact about the language works! If I was wrong about that then what ELSE am I wrong about in Go????

But somehow it never occurred to me that you need to think about the same thing for assignments, perhaps because in most of the other languages I use (Python, JS, Java) I think everything is a reference anyway.
I don't think this has ever actually happened to me, but it's alarming and I'm very happy to know about it.

It made it really easy for me to skim through the mistakes and very quickly mentally classify them into: yep, I know that; not interested in that one right now; WOW WAIT I DID NOT KNOW THAT, THAT IS VERY USEFUL!!!!
posts.key_insight: Years of casual experience with a programming language can leave fundamental misconceptions undetected, and structured 'common mistakes' resources are an efficient way to surface and fill those gaps.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes migrating Mess With DNS, her DNS learning playground, from a custom DNS implementation to PowerDNS after users reported various RFC compliance issues. The migration involved eight distinct challenges including intercepting DNS queries, redesigning the API, managing record IDs, translating error messages, switching from Postgres to SQLite, upgrading Vue.js, improving frontend state management, and sequencing the rollout. She shares practical lessons about API design, particularly that internal APIs should be tailored to the frontend's needs rather than following abstract purity principles. The project was split into phases and deployed incrementally to keep changes manageable.
key_points: Replacing a custom DNS implementation with PowerDNS eliminated a whole class of RFC compliance bugs that would have been impractical to fix individually
Internal APIs can and should duplicate information when it serves the frontend's display needs, rather than adhering to normalization principles
Switching from Postgres to SQLite solved chronic OOM issues on a low-traffic site, with practical choices like single connection limits and separate databases per table
Error messages from underlying APIs need thoughtful translation for end users, combining upfront validation with hacky-but-effective message rewriting
Splitting a large migration into phases with working deployments between each kept the project manageable
money_quotes: I wasn't very careful with the DNS implementation though (to quote the release blog post: "following the DNS RFCs? not exactly"), and people started reporting problems that eventually I decided that I wanted to fix.
I could just swap out my own crappy DNS implementation for PowerDNS.
Writing even more complex code in Javascript didn't feel that appealing to me – I don't really know how to write tests in Javascript and it seemed like it wouldn't end well.
I realized that I could return the same information in the API in 2 different ways! Then the frontend just has to display it.
If I'm making an API that isn't intended for external use, I can tailor it very specifically to the frontend's needs and that's okay.
If I'm building a user-facing application on top of an API, I need to be pretty thoughtful about how I resurface those errors to users.
I made sure that the website was (more or less) 100% working and then deployed it in between phases, so that the amount of changes I was managing at a time stayed somewhat under control.
posts.key_insight: When building on top of an existing API for internal use, pragmatism beats purity — tailor your API to your frontend's needs, duplicate information freely, and translate error messages for your actual users.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains why she still loves the fish shell after 10 years of daily use, expanding on a 2017 post with new reasons. She highlights fish's zero-configuration philosophy, smart history autosuggestions that validate against existing files, safe multiline pasting, terminal repair on every prompt, and sensible history defaults. She acknowledges downsides like occasional missing tool integrations and confusing PATH management with fish_add_path. The post concludes that fish is ideal for people who want great defaults without configuration overhead, noting its growing popularity at 16% in her Mastodon poll despite never being a system default.
key_points: Fish requires zero configuration to be useful — Evans has never needed to customize anything beyond environment variables and aliases in 10 years
Fish's autosuggestions are context-aware, only suggesting history commands containing paths that actually exist in the current directory
Fish automatically repairs terminal state on every prompt, preventing the broken-terminal issues common when accidentally catting binaries in bash
Fish's history system stores 256,000 commands by default and writes immediately rather than waiting for session end, preventing history loss on crashes
Despite not being any system's default shell, fish has reached 16% adoption in Evans's poll, suggesting its defaults-first approach resonates with many users
money_quotes: In 10 years of using fish I have never found a single thing I wanted to configure. It just works the way I want.
I didn't understand exactly how this worked until last week, it just felt like fish was magically able to suggest the right commands. It still feels a little like magic and I love it.

I actually didn't even realize that this was because of fish – I thought that things somehow magically just got better, or maybe I wasn't making as many mistakes. But I think it was mostly fish saving me from myself.
In bash, the maximum history size is 500 by default, presumably because computers used to be slow and not have a lot of disk space.
This is a bit alarming – what if I didn't actually want to run all those commands?
Fish disables Ctrl+S (which freezes your terminal and then you need to remember to press Ctrl+Q to unfreeze it). It's a feature that I've never wanted and I'm happy to not have it.

I think 16% for fish is pretty remarkable, since there isn't any system where fish is the default shell, and my sense is that it's very common to just stick to whatever your system's default shell is.
posts.key_insight: The best developer tools are the ones that just work without configuration, and fish proves that smart defaults can eliminate entire categories of terminal frustrations most people have learned to tolerate.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares practical lessons from building a web application in Go, covering routing improvements in Go 1.22, SQL code generation with sqlc, SQLite configuration tips, and garbage collector tuning. She highlights a trailing-slash gotcha in Go's new router that silently breaks POST requests by redirecting them to GET. She explains why she prefers Go for small web projects: single binary deployment, minimal toolchain setup, and code that's easy to return to after long breaks. She contrasts this with Rails, where the magic that makes initial development fast becomes an obstacle when returning to abandoned projects.
key_points: Go 1.22's improved standard library routing eliminates the need for third-party routers like gorilla/mux, but has a trailing-slash redirect gotcha that can silently break POST requests
sqlc generates Go boilerplate from SQL queries, offering the control of raw SQL without ORM abstraction overhead
Go's default garbage collector can double heap usage, which causes OOM kills on memory-constrained VMs — GOMEMLIMIT (Go 1.19+) fixes this
For SQLite in Go, using a single-connection writer prevents SQLITE_BUSY errors from concurrent writes
Go's simplicity and explicitness make projects easy to abandon for years and resume without confusion, unlike framework-heavy approaches
money_quotes: In general everything about it feels like it makes projects easy to work on for 5 days, abandon for 2 years, and then get back into writing code without a lot of problems.
It feels easier to me to come back to my Go projects that are full of a lot of repetitive boilerplate, because at least I can read the code and figure out how it works.
I've never felt motivated to learn any of the Go routing libraries (gorilla/mux, chi, etc), so I've been doing all my routing by hand.

What I like about this is that if I'm ever unsure about what Go code to write for a given SQL query, I can just write the query I want, read the generated function and it'll tell me exactly what to do to call it.
I ran into an issue with this where sending a POST request to /records redirected to a GET request for /records/, which broke the POST request because it removed the request body.

I've tried to learn Rails a couple of times and I really want to love Rails – I've made a couple of toy websites in Rails and it's always felt like a really magical experience. But ultimately when I come back to those projects I can't remember how anything works.
In general I'm not sure how to implement security-sensitive features so I don't start projects which need login/CSRF/etc.
posts.key_insight: Go's explicit, boilerplate-heavy approach to web development pays off over time because code you can read and understand after a two-year break is more valuable than code that was magical to write initially.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the surprisingly complex world of terminal color configuration, cataloging 11 distinct problems users face from unreadable blue-on-black text to vim colorscheme synchronization issues. She explains how the 16 ANSI colors lack standardization across terminal emulators, how 256-color and 24-bit color modes create additional complexity, and how colorscheme remapping can cause unexpected clashes with programs like ngrok. She shares her personal setup using base16-shell with Solarized Light and discusses the evolution of vim's color support from ANSI-only to 24-bit hex colors in 2016. Her biggest practical takeaway is enabling the 'minimum contrast' feature available in modern terminals like iTerm2, which automatically adjusts colors to ensure readability.
key_points: The 16 ANSI terminal colors have no standard hex values, leading to wildly inconsistent rendering across terminal emulators
Programs using 256-color or 24-bit color codes bypass your carefully chosen terminal colorscheme, creating contrast issues
Vim's 2016 addition of 24-bit color support eliminated the long-standing need to synchronize terminal and vim colorschemes
The 'minimum contrast' feature in modern terminals automatically fixes readability problems by adjusting insufficient color contrast
Remapping ANSI colors for one purpose (like base16-vim integration) can cause unexpected visual breakage in other programs
money_quotes: Yesterday I was thinking about how long it took me to get a colorscheme in my terminal that I was mostly happy with (SO MANY YEARS), and it made me wonder what about terminal colours made it so hard.
The answer seems to be 'there's no standard, terminal emulators just choose colours and it's not very consistent'.
I find it SO FRUSTRATING to deal with it when I just want my colours to work! Being surprised by unreadable text and having to find a workaround is just not my idea of a good day.
Personally I'm a zero-configuration kind of person and it's not that appealing to me to have to put together a lot of custom configuration just to make my colours in the terminal look acceptable.

So this 'you need to synchronize your terminal colorscheme and your vim colorscheme' problem is not an issue anymore for me in 2024, since I don't plan to use terminals without 24-bit color support in the future.
I'd much rather just have some reasonable defaults that I don't have to change.
posts.key_insight: Terminal colors are a mess of unstandardized defaults, competing color modes, and colorscheme remapping conflicts, but modern features like minimum contrast and 24-bit color support are finally making the situation manageable.

posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience upgrading Hugo from version 0.40 (2018) to v0.135 (2024), detailing every breaking change she encountered along the way. The most painful change was switching markdown renderers from Blackfriday to Goldmark, which required manually updating 80 out of 700 markdown files. She developed a systematic process of diffing old and new HTML output to find silent rendering differences. Despite the tedium, she considers the upgrade worthwhile because the new CommonMark-compliant renderer is more futureproof and actually fixed some long-standing rendering bugs she hadn't noticed. She also reflects on static site generator backwards compatibility, noting Jekyll and 11ty are reportedly better in this regard.
key_points: Upgrading Hugo across 6 years of versions required handling template syntax changes, API renames, and a complete markdown renderer swap from Blackfriday to Goldmark
The markdown renderer change was by far the hardest part, requiring a manual diff-every-HTML-file process to find silent rendering breakages across 700 posts
CommonMark compliance introduces subtle but important differences: HTML/markdown mixing requires blank lines, nested list indentation rules change, and smart quotes behave differently in code blocks
The new Goldmark renderer actually fixed several long-standing bugs including unwanted smart quotes in code blocks and broken blockquotes inside lists
Jekyll and 11ty were cited as static site generators with better backwards compatibility track records
money_quotes: But what are blogs for if not documenting one's very boring yakshaves from time to time?
There's no real reason to do this – I've been using Hugo version 0.40 to generate this blog since 2018, it works fine, and I don't have any problems with it.
Almost all of the problems just silently broke the site, they didn't cause any errors or anything. So I had to diff the HTML to hunt them down.
This was very time consuming but it was a little bit fun for some reason so I kept doing it until it seemed like nothing too horrible was left.
I might have spent 10 hours on this upgrade, but I've probably spent 1000+ hours writing blog posts without thinking about Hugo at all so that seems like an extremely reasonable ratio.
I don't think I'd particularly recommend moving 700 blog posts to a new Markdown renderer unless you're really in the mood for a lot of computer suffering for some reason.
Relying on someone else to keep a theme updated feels kind of scary to me, I think if I were using a third-party theme I'd just copy the code into my site's github repo and then maintain it myself.
posts.key_insight: Upgrading a static site generator after years of stability is mostly a markdown renderer migration problem, where the hardest part is finding silent rendering breakages that produce no errors.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her journey to reduce memory usage in Mess With DNS, which was getting OOM killed on a 465MB VM. The main culprit was a 117MB in-memory IP-to-ASN lookup database. She tried three approaches: SQLite (worked but was 500x slower), a trie (used even more memory), and optimizing her existing array-based binary search. The winning solution combined deduplicating ASN name/country strings and switching from net.IP to Go's newer netip.Addr type, cutting memory from 117MB to 46MB. She also honestly describes how the real debugging process was far messier than the linear narrative suggests.
key_points: An IP-to-ASN lookup table consumed 117MB in memory despite the raw data being only 37MB on disk, revealing significant overhead from Go's data structures and string duplication
SQLite eliminated memory usage but was 500x slower than binary search, and couldn't effectively use both indexes for range queries despite various optimization attempts
Deduplicating repeated ASN name/country strings by storing them in a shared pool with index references saved 50MB, and switching to netip.Addr saved another 20MB
Go's newer netip.Addr type (originally from Tailscale) solves real memory overhead problems with the older net.IP type by avoiding unnecessary heap allocations
The author deliberately chooses to work within a 512MB VM constraint rather than throwing more hardware at the problem, treating it as a fun optimization puzzle
money_quotes: This database by itself used about 117MB of memory, and a simple du told me that was too much – the original text files were only 37MB!
Doing manual work like this is the #1 thing I try to avoid with all my web services (who has time for that!) so I really wanted to do something about it.
I always try to take my actual debugging process (total chaos) and make it seem more linear and understandable because the reality is just too annoying to write down.

It's always such a great feeling when I think 'hey, I don't like this, there must be a better way' and then immediately discover that someone has already made the exact thing I want, thought about it a lot more than me, and implemented it much better than I would have.

I could very easily afford to pay for a VM with 1GB of memory, but I feel like 512MB really should be enough (and really that 256MB should be enough!) so I'd rather stay inside that constraint. It's kind of a fun puzzle.
I had fun, I learned a few things about SQLite, I still don't know what to think about tries, and it made me love binary search even more than I already did.
I tried to figure out if there was a way to make SQLite use both indexes, but I couldn't find one and maybe it knows best anyway.
posts.key_insight: Simple data structure optimizations like deduplicating strings and using properly-sized types can dramatically reduce memory usage without sacrificing the simplicity and speed of a basic binary search approach.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the 33 ASCII control characters and how they actually work in modern terminals. She categorizes them into OS-handled codes (like Ctrl-C for SIGINT), literal keypresses (Enter, Tab), readline shortcuts, and application-specific bindings. The post reveals surprising details like how there are only 33 possible control codes, how Ctrl-M is literally identical to Enter, and how the original ASCII names bear little relationship to modern terminal behavior. She discusses the complexity of canonical vs noncanonical mode, the configurable nature of these codes via stty, and the surprisingly deep history behind something as simple as the Backspace key.
key_points: There are only 33 ASCII control codes (A-Z plus 7 more), meaning Ctrl+1 or Ctrl+Shift+C aren't real control codes — they work completely differently from GUI keyboard shortcuts

The codes mix together several unrelated categories: OS terminal driver signals, literal keypresses, readline shortcuts, and application-specific bindings, with no logical structure to the organization
The original ASCII names for control characters are largely meaningless for modern terminal usage since they were designed for telegraph machines, not UNIX terminals
Canonical vs noncanonical mode determines whether the OS or the application handles certain codes like Ctrl-W and Ctrl-U
The Backspace key has a surprisingly contentious history — some systems send byte 127 (DEL) and others send byte 8 (BS), leading to decades of configuration headaches
money_quotes: There's no real structure to which codes are in which categories, they're all just kind of randomly scattered because this evolved organically.
This 'there are only 33 codes' thing is totally different from how keyboard shortcuts work in a GUI where you can have Ctrl+KEY for any key you want.
50% of the time the name in ASCII has no actual relationship to what that code does on UNIX systems today.
I have personally never remapped any of these and I cannot imagine a reason I would (I think it would be a recipe for confusion and disaster for me).
I've used the terminal pretty successfully every day for the last 20 years without knowing literally any of this.
Telegraph machines aren't the same as UNIX terminals so a lot of the codes were repurposed to mean something else.
I think the contents of this post are kind of interesting but I don't think they're necessarily that useful.
posts.key_insight: The 33 ASCII control characters are a messy historical artifact where telegraph-era codes, OS signals, readline shortcuts, and application bindings all collide in the same tiny namespace with no logical organization.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes adding a new TIL (Today I Learned) section to her blog for saving interesting tools and facts she'd otherwise only post on social media. The motivation was that useful links and tips she shared on Mastodon/Bluesky were getting lost because she couldn't remember them later. She set up a lightweight system with custom styling, a Rake task for quick posting, and a separate RSS feed. She also reflects on the POSSE philosophy, concluding she prefers letting most social media posts be ephemeral while selectively archiving specific categories on her own site.
key_points: Short-form useful links and tips deserve a home that's more permanent than social media but doesn't require a full blog post
The TIL section is designed for 2-minute posts — low friction is key to actually using it
She rejects the full POSSE approach, preferring to let most social media be ephemeral while selectively archiving what matters
Public posting works better than bookmarks for her as a personal reference system
She identifies three specific categories worth archiving on her own site: blog posts, comics, and TILs
money_quotes: Usually I don't want to write a whole blog post about those things because I really don't have much more to say than "hey this is useful!"

You might think "julia, why not use bookmarks??" but I have been failing to use bookmarks for my whole life and I don't see that changing ever, putting things in public is for whatever reason much easier for me

For me part of the appeal of social media is that it's a little bit ephemeral. I can post polls or questions or observations or jokes and then they can just kind of fade away as they become less relevant.
I find it a lot easier to identify specific categories of things that I actually want to have on a Real Website That I Own
So far it's been working, often I can actually just make a quick post in 2 minutes which was the goal.
posts.key_insight: Not everything needs to be a full blog post or permanently archived — creating a lightweight TIL section fills the gap between ephemeral social media and polished blog posts.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains how to import frontend JavaScript libraries without using a build system, a task she finds repeatedly frustrating because most library documentation assumes a build system. She identifies three types of JS files libraries provide: 'classic' global variable files, ES modules, and CommonJS modules. She walks through real examples (Chart.js, @atcute/oauth-browser-client, @atproto/oauth-client-browser) showing how to identify each type and use them in the browser. The post covers practical techniques including UMD files with script tags, ES modules with importmaps, and using esm.sh to convert CommonJS modules. She concludes that ES modules being a web standard gives her confidence in their longevity, and frames the post as understanding the JS landscape rather than complaining about it.
key_points: JavaScript libraries provide three types of files: 'classic' global variable scripts, ES modules, and CommonJS modules — and you need to identify which you're dealing with before you can use them
Even without using Node, you're still consuming NPM builds — CDN files all originate from NPM, so npm installing locally to inspect what's available is a useful detective technique
ES modules with dependencies require importmaps in the browser, which are fiddly to set up and can cause issues with many small file downloads in development
CommonJS modules cannot be used in the browser without a build step, but services like esm.sh can convert them to ES modules on the fly
ES modules being a web standard provides long-term confidence — browsers commit to backwards compatibility for web standards forever
money_quotes: I ran into a problem where I needed to figure out how to import a Javascript library in my code without using a build system, and it took FOREVER to figure out how to import it because the library's setup instructions assume that you're using a build system.
I sometimes like to npm install the library even if I'm not planning to use Node to build my library at all — I'll just create a new temp folder, npm install there, and then delete it when I'm done.
Unhelpfully a .js or .min.js file extension could be any of these 3 options, so if the file is something.js you need to do more detective work to figure out what you're dealing with.
If I write some code using ES modules today, I can feel sure that it'll still work the same way in 15 years.
I don't really trust CDNs to keep working forever — usually I like to copy dependencies into my repository so that they don't go away for some reason in the future.
A lot of the time when I talk about this stuff I get responses like 'I hate javascript!!! it's the worst!!!' But my experience is that there are a lot of great tools for Javascript.
The goal of this post is definitely not to complain about Javascript, it's to understand the landscape so I can use the tooling in a way that feels good to me.
posts.key_insight: Importing JavaScript libraries without a build system requires understanding the three types of JS files (classic, ES module, CommonJS) and knowing how to identify and work with each, which is learnable detective work despite poor documentation.

posts.summary: Julia Evans catalogs the unwritten rules that terminal programs follow, despite there being no formal standards beyond POSIX and a few guidelines. She identifies seven conventions: Ctrl-C quits noninteractive programs, q quits TUIs, Ctrl-D quits REPLs, programs stick to 16 ANSI colors, readline keybindings are loosely supported, colors are disabled when piping, and - means stdin/stdout. She emphasizes these are descriptive observations from 20 years of terminal use, not prescriptions, and notes that understanding which rules are the program's responsibility helps explain why implementations vary slightly.
key_points: Terminal program behavior is governed by unwritten conventions rather than formal standards, yet programs behave surprisingly consistently
Many behaviors that feel like 'laws of terminal physics' (like Ctrl-D quitting a REPL) are actually explicitly implemented by each program to mimic OS defaults
Understanding whether a behavior comes from the OS, shell, terminal emulator, or program itself makes exceptions less surprising
Programs adapt their output format and color when writing to a pipe versus a terminal, which is a widely followed but uncodified convention
Learning these rules is a multi-stage process from basic awareness through subconscious pattern recognition to explicit understanding
money_quotes: As far as I know, there are no real standards for how programs in the terminal should behave.
Instead of 'uh, programs could do literally anything', it's 'ok, here are the basic rules I expect, and then I can keep a short mental list of exceptions'.

I actually thought that this one was a 'Law of Terminal Physics' until very recently because I've basically never seen it broken, but you can see that it's just something that each individual input library has to implement.
None of those programs actually uses readline directly, they just sort of mimic emacs/readline keybindings.
A lot of my understanding of the terminal is honestly still in the 'subconscious pattern recognition' stage.
The only reason I've been taking the time to make things explicit at all is because I've been trying to explain how it works to others.

If you stick to the default 16 base colours, you have a much better chance that the user has configured those colours in their terminal emulator so that they work reasonably well with their background color.
posts.key_insight: Terminal programs follow a consistent set of unwritten conventions that feel like laws of nature but are actually explicitly implemented by each program, and making these implicit rules explicit accelerates learning for newcomers.

posts.summary: The post explains why piping commands like 'tail -f file | grep thing1 | grep thing2' often appear to produce no output. The root cause is that programs buffer their output (typically 8KB) when writing to a pipe rather than a terminal, so data never actually reaches the next command in the pipeline. Programs use the isatty function to decide between line buffering (for terminals) and block buffering (for pipes). Evans catalogues which common commands buffer, how various programming languages handle it, and presents five practical solutions including stdbuf, unbuffer, and using line-buffered flags. She also notes that pressing Ctrl-C on a buffered pipeline loses the buffer contents entirely.
