key_points: Programs check isatty to decide buffering strategy: line buffering for terminals, 8KB block buffering for pipes, which is why adding a second grep to a pipeline breaks visible output
Common commands like grep, sed, awk, tcpdump, and jq all buffer output when writing to pipes, each with different flags to disable it
Pressing Ctrl-C on a buffered pipeline loses all buffered data because grep receives SIGINT before the upstream program can flush
Five practical solutions exist: avoid slow pipes, use line-buffer flags, consolidate into awk, use stdbuf (LD_PRELOAD-based), or use unbuffer (forces TTY)
A standard environment variable like NO_BUFFER (analogous to NO_COLOR) could solve this universally but doesn't exist yet
money_quotes: The reason why 'pipes get stuck' sometimes is that it's VERY common for programs to buffer their output before writing it to a pipe or file. So the pipe is working fine, the problem is that the program never even wrote the data to the pipe!
I internalized this as 'uh, I guess pipes just get stuck sometimes and don't show me the output, that's weird'
grep thing1 is saving up all of its matches until it has 8KB of data to write, which might literally never happen.

When you press Ctrl-C, what happens? In a magical perfect world, what I would want to happen is for tcpdump to flush its buffer, grep would search for example.com, and I would see all the output I missed. But in the real world, what happens is that all the programs get killed and the output in tcpdump's buffer is lost.

Programs do not use 8KB output buffers when writing to a terminal isn't, like, a law of terminal physics, a program COULD use an 8KB buffer when writing output to a terminal if it wanted, it would just be extremely weird if it did that.
Does anyone REALLY want to read 3000 words about buffering?
posts.key_insight: Pipes don't get stuck — programs silently buffer their output when they detect they're not writing to a terminal, and understanding the isatty check is the key to diagnosing and fixing the problem.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores what it actually takes to achieve a 'modern' terminal experience, cataloging the specific features she considers essential and which system components are responsible for each. She starts by thinking it's simple, then realizes the list of requirements keeps growing. The post identifies three main obstacles: shell configuration (bash/zsh defaults are inadequate), terminal text editor setup (vim/emacs require years of investment), and individual application quirks. She emphasizes that terminal configuration is an interconnected system where changing one thing can break others, and recommends making changes slowly over time. Her honest conclusion is that getting a modern terminal experience is genuinely hard, despite what experienced users might assume.
key_points: A 'modern' terminal experience requires coordinating multiple components: shell, terminal emulator, text editor, and individual applications, each needing separate configuration
Neither bash nor zsh provide acceptable defaults out of the box — limited history, no git completions, no useful prompt — making fish or oh-my-zsh practically necessary
Terminal configuration is an interconnected system where seemingly small changes (like adding tmux) cascade into unexpected requirements elsewhere (like changing how colors are managed)
The text editor problem remains unsolved: vim/emacs require years of investment, nano is too limited, and newer options like micro/helix are still maturing
Experienced users underestimate the difficulty because their own setup evolved incrementally over 10-20 years
money_quotes: My immediate reaction was 'oh, getting a modern terminal experience isn't that hard, you just need to…', but the more I thought about it, the longer the 'you just need to…' list got.
Figuring out how to configure vim in a way that I was satisfied with really did take me like ten years, which is a long time!
'Using tmux means that I have to change how I manage my colours' sounds unlikely, but that really did happen to me.
Each of these 'not complicated' things really does add up and it's especially tough if you need to keep your config in sync across several systems.

I find that debugging application-specific issues like this is really not easy and often it doesn't feel 'worth it' — often I'll end up just dealing with various minor inconveniences because I don't want to spend hours investigating them.
Trying to explain how 'easy' it is to configure your terminal really just made me think that it's kind of hard and that I still sometimes get confused.
I've found that there's never one perfect way to configure things in the terminal that will be compatible with every single other thing.
posts.key_insight: Getting a 'modern' terminal experience is genuinely hard because it requires coordinating many interdependent components, each with its own configuration, and experienced users underestimate this because their own setup evolved incrementally over decades.

posts.summary: Julia Evans surveyed 1600 terminal users about their biggest frustrations and spent days categorizing the qualitative responses. The top complaints include remembering syntax, difficulties switching between systems, color configuration problems, keyboard shortcut inconsistencies, and copy/paste issues. Notably, 95% of respondents had 4+ years of terminal experience and 40% had 21+ years, meaning these frustrations persist even for experts. The results reveal that discoverability, steep learning curves, and legacy design decisions continue to plague the terminal experience decades into people's careers. Evans connects several categories as related problems: syntax recall ties to history search, discoverability drives the learning curve, and the outdated feel of terminals is precisely what makes them ubiquitous.
key_points: Remembering syntax for tools like awk, jq, sed, and even basic redirects remains the #1 frustration even among 20+ year veterans
Switching between systems exposes painful inconsistencies in keyboard shortcuts, tool versions, shell differences, and missing configurations
Color, keyboard shortcuts, and copy/paste each represent distinct but overlapping usability failures that the terminal has never properly solved
The terminal's legacy design creates a paradox: tools that haven't changed in 30-40 years are frustratingly outdated but reliably available everywhere
Discoverability is a fundamental problem — most terminal knowledge comes from random people over years, not from the tools themselves
money_quotes: There are just so many little "trivia" details to remember for full functionality. Even after all these years I'll sometimes forget where it's 2 or 1 for stderr, or forget which is which for > and >>.
How difficult it is to learn independently. Most of what I know is an assorted collection of stuff I've been told by random people over the years.
After 15 years of using it, I'm not much faster than using it than I was 5 or maybe even 10 years ago.
I dread touching any of that configuration ever again.
The debilitating fear that I'm going to do some mysterious Bad Thing with a command and I will have absolutely no idea how to fix or undo it or even really figure out what happened.
Shell scripting. My tolerance to ditch a shell script and go to a scripting language is pretty low. It's just too messy and powerful. Screwing up can be costly so I don't even bother.
Tools that haven't really changed in 30 or 40 years have many problems but they do tend to be always there no matter what system you're on, which is very useful and makes them hard to stop using.
posts.key_insight: Terminal frustrations aren't beginner problems — even decades-long users struggle with syntax recall, system inconsistencies, and poor discoverability, revealing fundamental UX failures baked into the terminal's legacy design.

posts.summary: Julia Evans writes a comprehensive guide to adding a directory to your PATH, a task that seems obvious to experienced terminal users but has many undocumented gotchas. She walks through identifying your shell, finding the correct config file, determining which directory to add, and restarting the shell. The post also covers common problems like running the wrong program version, duplicate PATH entries, and losing shell history when reloading config. She includes shell-specific syntax for bash, zsh, and fish, and discusses tools like cargo and Homebrew that use source scripts instead of explicit PATH additions.
key_points: Bash has three possible config files (~/.bashrc, ~/.bash_profile, ~/.profile), and the simplest way to figure out which one your system uses is to test empirically rather than memorize flowcharts
Finding which directory to add is often the hardest part — installers print PATH directions during setup that are easy to miss, and there's no universal way to discover where tools install binaries
Adding directories to the beginning vs. end of PATH matters when multiple versions of a program are installed, since the shell uses the first match
PATH modifications only apply to programs run from your shell — cron jobs, IDEs, and GUIs require different approaches
Running 'bash' to reload config can cause duplicate PATH entries and lost command history, making 'source ~/.bashrc' a better alternative
money_quotes: It's something that feels 'obvious' to me since I've been using the terminal for a long time, but when I searched for instructions for how to do it, I actually couldn't find something that explained all of the steps.
There are a lot of elaborate flow charts out there that explain how bash decides which config file to use but IMO it's not worth it to internalize them and just testing is the fastest way to be sure.
How do you find what directory http-server is in? Honestly in general this is not that easy – often the answer is something like 'it depends on how npm is configured'.
An extremely important step: updating your shell's config won't take effect if you don't restart it!
Personally I usually use the second approach because I prefer knowing exactly what configuration I'm changing.

Sometimes fish_add_path will update the PATH for every session in the future (with a 'universal variable') and sometimes it will update the PATH just for the current session and it's hard for me to tell which one it will do.
posts.key_insight: Adding a directory to your PATH involves multiple shell-specific decisions and subtle gotchas that are rarely documented in one place, making a seemingly simple task genuinely confusing for newcomers.

posts.summary: Julia Evans surveys the landscape of ANSI escape code standards, from the 1976 ECMA-48 specification through xterm control sequences and the terminfo database. She identifies two competing approaches programs take: using terminfo to adapt to different terminals, or hardcoding a 'common set' of widely-supported escape codes. She draws a parallel between terminfo's TERM variable and browser user agent detection, noting both led to similar workaround patterns. The post concludes that clearer standards could make terminals feel less 'outdated' and enable richer features, drawing hope from how web standards eventually improved from a similarly fragmented state.
key_points: ANSI escape codes handle both input (keypresses, mouse) and output (colors, cursor, clipboard) but lack a single comprehensive standard
Programs are split between two approaches: consulting the terminfo database via TERM, or hardcoding a common set of escape codes that work in most emulators
The TERM environment variable functions like browser user agent detection, with terminal emulators misreporting identity to work around broken detection, mirroring Safari's bloated user agent string
There is no 'Can I Use' equivalent for terminal escape codes, and terminfo takes 10+ years to add new features, limiting its usefulness for modern terminal development
The terminal landscape is more fragmented and less well-funded than the web, making standardization harder but potentially just as valuable
money_quotes: I just had a kind of vague 'there be dragons' feeling around them.
Did you know there's a way to copy to your system clipboard when SSHed into a remote machine?? It's an escape code called OSC 52!
They aren't completely standardized, and because of that they don't always work reliably. And because they're also invisible, it's extremely frustrating to troubleshoot escape code issues.

The way iTerm2 reports itself as being 'xterm-256color' feels similar to how Safari's user agent is 'Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 14_7_4) AppleWebKit/605.1.15 (KHTML, like Gecko) Version/18.3 Safari/605.1.15'.
On the web we ended up deciding that user agent detection was not a good practice and to instead focus on standardization so we can serve the same HTML/CSS to all browsers.

Maybe if we had a clearer standards landscape (like we do on the web!) it would be easier for terminal emulator developers to build new features and for authors of terminal applications to more confidently adopt those features.
Obviously standardizing ANSI escape codes is not easy (ECMA-48 was first published almost 50 years ago and we're still not there!).
posts.key_insight: The terminal's escape code ecosystem suffers from the same fragmentation that plagued early web browsers, and could benefit from the same shift toward clear, shared standards rather than per-terminal detection.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explains how non-C-programmers can compile C programs from source using make, walking through the full process from installing a compiler to dealing with dependency errors. She uses real examples (paperjam, sqlite, qf) to demonstrate common problems and solutions, focusing on how environment variables like CPPFLAGS and LDLIBS pass flags to the compiler and linker. The post demystifies the configure/make workflow and offers practical tips like looking at how Linux distributions build the same package to find clues for fixing errors. She concludes that understanding C compilation basics is valuable even for people who never plan to write C.
key_points: C has no dependency manager, so you must hunt down dependencies yourself, but this forces C programmers to keep dependencies minimal

Most compiler errors during builds are dependency problems — knowing the difference between compiler flags (-I for headers) and linker flags (-L for libraries, -l for linking) is the key to fixing them
Make has implicit environment variables like CPPFLAGS that get automatically passed to the compiler, and there are two ways to pass env vars to make with different override behavior
Looking at how packaging systems (Nix, Homebrew, Debian) build the same C program is a powerful debugging technique when you're stuck
You can run 'make filename' without a Makefile and make will automatically expand it to a basic compile command
money_quotes: For a long time, my approach was basically "install the dependencies, run `make`, if it doesn't work, either try to find a binary someone has compiled or give up".
Unlike some newer programming languages, C doesn't have a dependency manager. So if a program has any dependencies, you need to hunt them down yourself.
It usually prints out a million compiler warnings when compiling the program. I always just ignore them. I didn't write the software! The compiler warnings are not my problem.

Over the years I've learned it's usually best not to overthink problems like this: if it's talking about `qpdf`, there's a good chance it just means that I've done something wrong with how I'm including the `qpdf` dependency.
I don't want to pretend that I "magically" knew that those were the right arguments to pass, figuring them out involved a bunch of confused Googling that I skipped over in this post.

Every Linux distribution has build files for every package that they build, so even if you can't install packages from that distribution directly, maybe you can get tips from that Linux distro for how to build the package.
It can be useful to understand some basics of how C programs work even if you're never planning to write a nontrivial C program in your life.
posts.key_insight: Compiling C programs from source is demystified once you understand that most build errors are dependency problems solvable by passing the right flags to the compiler and linker via make's environment variables.

posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine 'The Secret Rules of the Terminal,' the product of months of deep-dive research into how terminals actually work. Despite using the terminal daily for 20 years, she discovered she had many misunderstandings about its inner workings. The zine explains how the four pieces of the terminal (shell, terminal emulator, programs, and TTY driver) fit together, along with core conventions and practical tips. She argues that while terminal internals are a mess of decades-old decisions, understanding select parts can meaningfully improve your daily experience. The project involved extensive collaboration with technical reviewers and was accompanied by numerous blog posts exploring specific terminal topics.
key_points: The terminal is actually composed of four distinct pieces of software (shell, terminal emulator, programs, TTY driver) written by different people with different ideas, which explains its many inconsistencies
Having an intuition for terminal behavior from years of use is not the same as understanding why things happen, and even experienced users carry significant misunderstandings

Terminal internals are largely a mess of irreversible decisions from the 1980s, but selectively understanding parts like escape codes, shell responsibilities, and color can meaningfully improve your experience
Understanding the terminal's structure makes debugging weird terminal behavior possible by helping you identify which piece of software is causing problems
The 'rules' for how the terminal works aren't written down anywhere because the system emerged from many independent pieces of software rather than a unified design
money_quotes: I've been using the terminal every day for 20 years but even though I'm very confident in the terminal, I've always had a bit of an uneasy feeling about it.
Having an intuition for them isn't the same as understanding why they happen.

The 'rules' for how the terminal works are extremely hard to fully understand, because 'the terminal' is actually made of many different pieces of software which are written by different people with different ideas about how things should work.
Terminal internals are a mess. A lot of it is just the way it is because someone made a decision in the 80s and now it's impossible to change.
Even though I feel totally confident in the terminal and even though I've used it every day for 20 years, I had a lot of misunderstandings about how the terminal works.
Identifying exactly which piece of software is causing a weird thing to happen in my terminal still isn't easy but I'm a lot better at it now.
posts.key_insight: Even after 20 years of daily terminal use, the terminal's behavior remains poorly understood because it's not one system but many independent pieces of software with unwritten rules, and selectively learning its internals transforms vague intuition into genuine debugging ability.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience switching from Vim to the Helix text editor after 20 years of Vim usage. The primary motivation was getting language server support without extensive configuration. She finds Helix's built-in LSP support, search with full context, and inline keybinding help significantly better than her Vim setup. While she notes several annoyances including poor Markdown list handling and occasional crashes, the switch was easier than expected. Her Helix configuration is remarkably minimal compared to her hundreds-of-lines Neovim config.
key_points: Built-in language server support was the killer feature that motivated the switch, eliminating the configuration burden that plagued both custom and pre-built Vim/Neovim setups
Multiple cursors replaced macros as her primary batch-editing workflow, and she finds them preferable
Relearning 20 years of muscle memory was easier than expected, especially by embracing the 'Helix way' rather than trying to force Vim keybindings
The configuration went from hundreds of lines in Neovim to essentially 4 keyboard shortcuts
Several annoyances remain, particularly around Markdown editing (list continuation, text reflowing), but none severe enough to drive her back to Vim
money_quotes: After using Vim/Neovim for 20 years, I've tried both "build my own custom configuration from scratch" and "use someone else's pre-buld configuration system" and even though I love Vim I was excited about having things just work without having to work on my configuration at all.
The first time I tried Helix I tried to force it to use keybindings that were more similar to Vim and that did not work for me. Just learning the "Helix way" was a lot easier.
I appreciate that my configuration is really simple, compared to my neovim configuration which is hundreds of lines. It's mostly just 4 keyboard shortcuts.
I like multiple cursors a lot more than writing macros all the time.
I was worried that relearning 20 years of Vim muscle memory would be really hard. It turned out to be easier than I expected.
The "markdown list" and reflowing issues come up a lot for me because I spend a lot of time editing Markdown lists, but I keep using Helix anyway so I guess they can't be making me that mad.
posts.key_insight: The best tool configuration is the one you don't have to maintain — built-in sensible defaults can outweigh decades of customized muscle memory.

posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her effort to improve Git's official documentation, including writing a new 'data model' document explaining core concepts like objects, references, and the index. Rather than relying on expert intuition about what's clear, she recruited ~80 test readers on Mastodon to identify confusing terminology and sentences in existing man pages. This evidence-based approach helped her update man pages for git add, checkout, push, and pull. She reflects on the difficulty of writing documentation that is both accurate and clear, and shares notes on Git's contribution process including using GitGitGadget to avoid learning email-patch workflows.
key_points: Git's documentation used core terms like 'object', 'reference', and 'index' extensively without adequately explaining them, prompting a new data model document
Expert users arguing about clarity is unproductive because experts are notoriously bad at predicting what non-experts will find confusing
Recruiting 80 test readers to comment on existing docs provided evidence-based feedback that made it easier to argue for specific changes
Writing documentation that is both clear and technically accurate requires real compromises, sometimes leaving things intentionally vague
Git's contribution process can be navigated using GitGitGadget to convert GitHub PRs into the mailing-list patch workflow
money_quotes: Usually if I think the documentation for something could be improved, I'll write a blog post or a zine or something. But this time I wondered: could I instead make a few improvements to the official documentation?
Expert users of a piece of software are notoriously bad at being able to tell if an explanation will be clear to non-experts.

If a group of test readers who have been using Git regularly for 5+ years find a sentence or term impossible to understand, it makes it easy to argue that the documentation should be updated to make it clearer.
I thought this 'get users of the software to comment on the existing documentation and then fix the problems they find' pattern worked really well.
It's not easy to write things that are both clear and true, and sometimes we had to make compromises.
The 'accurate' part turned out to not be that easy: I knew the basics of how Git's data model worked, but during the review process I learned some new details.
posts.key_insight: The most effective way to improve open source documentation is not expert debate about clarity, but gathering evidence from real users about what actually confuses them.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience starting to learn Django after years of building websites with Go binaries and static sites. She compares it favorably to Rails, appreciating Django's explicitness which makes it easier to return to projects after months away. She highlights several features she's enjoying: the built-in admin interface, the ORM's elegant JOIN syntax using double underscores, automatic migrations, and the batteries-included approach to email, CSRF, and other web features. She's running Django with SQLite in production and finding the documentation excellent, though she remains slightly uneasy about the settings file's lack of typo protection.
key_points: Django's explicitness over Rails' convention-based magic makes it easier to return to projects after long breaks
Django's ORM uses __ syntax for JOINs across multiple tables, making complex queries far more concise than raw SQL
Automatic migration generation from model changes removes significant friction during iterative development
SQLite works well for small Django sites in production, with simple backup via VACUUM INTO
Django's batteries-included approach (admin, email, CSRF, CSP) reduces the need for external dependencies
money_quotes: One of my favourite things is starting to learn an Old Boring Technology that I've never tried before but that has been around for 20+ years.
It feels really good when every problem I'm ever going to have has been solved already 1000 times and I can just get stuff done easily.

Being able to abandon a project for months or years and then come back to it is really important to me (that's how all my projects work!), and Django feels easier to me because things are more explicit.
In the past my attitude has been "ORMs? Who needs them? I can just write my own SQL queries!". I've been enjoying Django's ORM so far though.
I have zero concern about the performance of my ORM-generated queries so I'm pretty excited about ORMs for now, though I'm sure I'll find things to be frustrated with eventually.
I'm realizing that being able to do migrations easily is important for me right now because I'm changing my data model fairly often as I figure out how I want it to work.
I'm still a bit intimidated by the settings.py file... what if I make a typo in the name of one of those variables? How will I know?
posts.key_insight: Mature, 'boring' frameworks like Django reward beginners with explicitness and batteries-included features that let you get things done without fighting the tooling.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores what makes man pages effective and easier to navigate, drawing on examples from rsync, strace, curl, Perl, and the ASCII man page. She crowdsourced favorite man pages on Mastodon and catalogs specific techniques: options summaries, category-based organization, embedded cheat sheets, tables of contents with hyperlinks, per-option examples, and table formatting. She also touches on the GNU project's preference for info manuals over man pages and notes tools like tldr and Dash that supplement man pages. The post frames man page improvement as a design challenge within a constrained format.
key_points: An OPTIONS SUMMARY section (as in rsync) provides a scannable one-line description of each flag, separate from the full OPTIONS section
Organizing options by category rather than alphabetically (as strace does) helps users find related flags faster
Embedding examples for every option (as curl does) and including cheat sheets (as Perl does) makes man pages more immediately useful
HTML versions of man pages can add tables of contents and hyperlinks without maintaining separate documentation
The GNU project's preference for info manuals over man pages means many coreutils man pages lack examples, though the info manuals themselves are more comprehensive
money_quotes: could the man page itself have an amazing cheat sheet in it? What might make a man page easier to use?
once you're listing almost the entire alphabet, it's hard
I can never remember the name of the -l grep option. It always takes me what feels like forever to find it in the man page

I think adding a table of contents and adding internal hyperlinks is kind of a nice middle ground where we can make some improvements to the man page format without maintaining a totally different form of documentation.
I've heard from some Emacs users that they like the Emacs info browser. I don't think I've ever talked to anyone who uses the standalone info tool.
After a certain level of complexity a man page gets really hard to navigate
Man pages are such a constrained format and it's fun to think about what you can do with such limited formatting options.
posts.key_insight: Man pages can be dramatically improved through design techniques like options summaries, categorical organization, embedded examples, and cheat sheets — all within the existing constrained format.

posts.summary: Julia Evans contributed examples sections to the official man pages for tcpdump and dig, motivated by her earlier writing about how examples make man pages more useful. Her goal was to provide the most basic, beginner-friendly examples for infrequent users who don't remember how the tools work. She found the process rewarding and collaborative, learning new things from maintainers along the way. The experience shifted her perspective on official documentation, making her cautiously optimistic that it could be as useful as blog posts while being more accurate. She also wrote a custom markdown-to-roff converter to avoid learning the roff language directly.
key_points: Contributing beginner-focused examples to official man pages is a compelling pitch to maintainers and aligns with what users actually want
Official documentation has a key advantage over blog posts: it goes through a review process that ensures accuracy
Working with maintainers surfaces useful features you'd never discover yourself, like tcpdump's -v flag when writing to a file
Writing a custom markdown-to-roff converter let her contribute without learning roff, using a Markdown AST parser with custom output methods
There's a technical and cultural divide between BSD and Linux documentation ecosystems worth exploring further
money_quotes: Maybe the documentation doesn't have to be bad? Maybe it could be just as good as reading a really great blog post, but with the benefit of also being actually correct?
Man pages can actually have close to 100% accurate information! Going through a review process to make sure that the information is actually true has a lot of value.
I always kind of assume documentation is going to be hard to read, and I usually just skip it and read a blog post or Stack Overflow comment or ask a friend instead.
Even with basic questions 'what are the most commonly used tcpdump flags', often maintainers are aware of useful features that I'm not!
I went on a whole rabbit hole learning about the history of roff, how it's evolved since the 70s, and who's working on it today.
posts.key_insight: Contributing examples to official man pages is a high-leverage way to improve documentation accuracy and accessibility, because the review process guarantees correctness in a way blog posts never can.

posts.summary: Julia Evans explores how to write integration tests for Vue components that run directly in the browser, without using Node.js or any server-side JavaScript runtime. She uses QUnit as a lightweight test framework and creates a simple mountComponent helper to render Vue components in temporary DOM elements. The post walks through practical challenges like waiting for async rendering, filling out forms programmatically, and identifying elements for testing. She discovers that many testing pain points (like elements appearing before they're ready) point to genuine UX improvements. The approach aligns with her ongoing project of doing frontend development without Node, and she finds the experience genuinely enjoyable despite its rough edges.
key_points: Vue components can be tested directly in a browser tab using QUnit and a simple mountComponent helper, without any Node.js tooling
Waiting for async DOM updates requires polling-based waitFor functions rather than brittle sleep calls, and figuring out the right condition to wait for is often the hardest part
Form interactions in tests require dispatching the correct DOM events (input for textareas, change for checkboxes) to notify Vue of changes
Testing pain points often reveal real UX issues — if an element isn't ready for tests to interact with, it probably isn't ready for users either
Chrome's built-in code coverage tool can provide test coverage data without any additional tooling
money_quotes: I just don't test my frontend code which doesn't feel great.
I think we all learned a long time ago that putting random sleep() calls in your tests and hoping that the timings are right is slow and flaky and extremely frustrating.
If there's an element in the DOM that isn't actually ready for the user to interact with, maybe I shouldn't be displaying it yet!
Feels like there's a way to make the app more accessible and easier to test at the same time.
My frontend projects always feel so fragile because they're untested, and maybe one day I'll have a test suite I'm confident in!
I feel like I'm constantly learning how to do super basic tasks.
I really had a blast doing this.
posts.key_insight: You can write meaningful integration tests for Vue components directly in the browser without any build tools or Node.js, and the friction points you encounter often reveal genuine improvements to make in your application's UX.

posts.summary: Julia Evans shares CSS colour palette resources after deciding to move away from Tailwind for new projects. She missed Tailwind's convenient colour palette system and asked on Mastodon for alternatives. The post collects her favourites including uchū, flexoki, and reasonable colours, along with other palettes and generators. It's a practical resource post born from a real workflow need rather than a deep technical dive.
key_points: Moving from Tailwind to vanilla CSS means losing convenient built-in colour palettes
Having a pre-made colour palette matters most for people who aren't confident with colour choices
Accessibility-focused palettes like 'reasonable colours' exist as alternatives to Tailwind's defaults
Colour palette generators exist but can be hard to use for people who aren't strong with colour
money_quotes: A while back I decided to stop using Tailwind for new projects and to just write vanilla CSS instead.
I'm not very good with colours so it makes a big difference to me to have a reasonable colour palette that somebody who is better at colour than me has thought about.
But I'm also a little tired of those Tailwind colours, so I asked on Mastodon today what other colour palettes were out there.
I've always found these types of generators too hard to use but maybe one day I will get better enough at colour that I'm able to use a colour palette generator successfully.
posts.key_insight: When moving away from CSS frameworks like Tailwind, curated colour palettes remain valuable for developers who aren't confident with colour design.
